
# section 1: --not of use


==================================================================================

# section 2: The Basics of Nest

1
00:00:00,690 --> 00:00:04,290
Now that we've got some administrative stuff out of the way, we're going to start working on our first

2
00:00:04,290 --> 00:00:05,940
application almost immediately.

3
00:00:05,970 --> 00:00:08,490
First, just 1 or 2 quick disclaimers.

4
00:00:08,490 --> 00:00:11,850
When we work with Nestjs, we usually write TypeScript code.

5
00:00:11,850 --> 00:00:16,220
So in this course I'm going to assume that in general you are familiar with the basics of TypeScript.

6
00:00:16,230 --> 00:00:19,740
If you're not familiar with TypeScript at all, or if you just need a quick refresher.

7
00:00:19,740 --> 00:00:24,690
I've put in a little review section at the very end of the course, so go and take that appendix very

8
00:00:24,690 --> 00:00:25,170
quickly.

9
00:00:25,170 --> 00:00:27,920
It will be titled something like TypeScript Appendix.

10
00:00:27,930 --> 00:00:32,820
Just go through those videos very quickly and it'll give you a quick refresher or hopefully just a quick

11
00:00:32,820 --> 00:00:34,680
reminder on how TypeScript works.

12
00:00:34,860 --> 00:00:39,810
Now, besides knowledge of TypeScript, I'm also going to assume that you've got some general expertise

13
00:00:39,810 --> 00:00:41,700
or I don't really mean to say expertise.

14
00:00:41,700 --> 00:00:46,320
I don't expect you to be an expert, but I'm going to assume you understand the basics of NodeJS.

15
00:00:47,040 --> 00:00:47,400
All right.

16
00:00:47,400 --> 00:00:49,440
So with that in mind, our first application.

17
00:00:49,440 --> 00:00:50,610
What are we going to do here?

18
00:00:50,640 --> 00:00:55,710
Well, usually whenever we create a Nest application, we make use of a tool called the nest cli.

19
00:00:55,980 --> 00:01:00,540
This is a tool that we use from our terminal to generate a new project and run it.

20
00:01:00,570 --> 00:01:05,010
We are going to use this tool inside this course, but for our first project that we're going to work

21
00:01:05,010 --> 00:01:08,120
on, we're going to create a Nest project from scratch.

22
00:01:08,130 --> 00:01:12,780
We're going to write out everything required and add in all the tooling required to create a project

23
00:01:12,780 --> 00:01:13,620
on our own.

24
00:01:13,900 --> 00:01:15,930
There's a very good reason we're doing this.

25
00:01:16,230 --> 00:01:20,570
The first project that you and I are going to work on is going to be just a little bit challenging and

26
00:01:20,580 --> 00:01:22,500
want you to understand why that is.

27
00:01:22,800 --> 00:01:28,440
For this very first project, you and I are going to focus on how some internals inside Nestjs work.

28
00:01:28,470 --> 00:01:32,880
Now, this will be just a little bit challenging, but the reason we're doing this is that as soon as

29
00:01:32,880 --> 00:01:38,550
you understand how Nest internally works, everything else around Nest starts to get really easy and

30
00:01:38,550 --> 00:01:39,700
really straightforward.

31
00:01:39,720 --> 00:01:44,100
So at the very start of this course, just right away, I want to tackle the hard stuff so that we can

32
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:46,020
spend the rest of the course building.

33
00:01:46,020 --> 00:01:48,010
Really interesting applications.

34
00:01:48,620 --> 00:01:48,920
All right.

35
00:01:48,920 --> 00:01:50,060
So that's the general game plan.

36
00:01:50,060 --> 00:01:56,030
We're going to create a very small project from scratch to focus on some behind the scenes aspect aspects

37
00:01:56,030 --> 00:01:56,810
of Nest.

38
00:01:57,110 --> 00:02:00,650
Now, to get started on this, we're going to go directly back over to our terminal.

39
00:02:01,790 --> 00:02:06,170
You'll notice I've already got my terminal open and I'm inside of a workspace directory of sorts, so

40
00:02:06,170 --> 00:02:08,420
this can be any folder on your computer.

41
00:02:08,720 --> 00:02:13,140
Then inside of this terminal, I'm going to create a new folder and I'm going to call it scratch.

42
00:02:13,160 --> 00:02:14,870
So that's going to be the name of our project.

43
00:02:14,870 --> 00:02:18,140
We're just going to call it scratch because we're creating a project from scratch.

44
00:02:18,530 --> 00:02:23,870
I'll then change inside there, and then I'm going to generate a new Package.json file, which we'll

45
00:02:23,870 --> 00:02:27,280
use to install a couple of different dependencies to generate this file.

46
00:02:27,290 --> 00:02:30,260
I will run npm init dash y.

47
00:02:31,710 --> 00:02:34,280
And that will generate our package.json file.

48
00:02:34,300 --> 00:02:35,550
I can now see it right there.

49
00:02:36,000 --> 00:02:40,350
We will then install a couple of different dependencies that are required to run nest.

50
00:02:40,380 --> 00:02:45,180
Now usually when you generate a nestjs project, you're going to get a big list of different dependencies.

51
00:02:45,180 --> 00:02:49,650
But for us, because we're going to wire everything up from scratch, we only need a handful of very

52
00:02:49,650 --> 00:02:50,900
small dependencies.

53
00:02:50,910 --> 00:02:52,280
So let's do the install right now.

54
00:02:52,290 --> 00:02:53,850
I'm going to run NPM install.

55
00:02:54,660 --> 00:02:59,370
And then we're going to install a total of five dependencies and we're going to specify a very specific

56
00:02:59,370 --> 00:03:04,080
version for each one just to make sure that your code and my code is 100% equivalent.

57
00:03:04,110 --> 00:03:12,660
So we're going to add in at Nestjs slash common at 7617.

58
00:03:14,120 --> 00:03:16,880
Then Nestjs core.

59
00:03:17,870 --> 00:03:20,420
At 7617.

60
00:03:22,200 --> 00:03:23,670
Nestjs.

61
00:03:25,280 --> 00:03:30,560
Platform Dash Express at 7.6, 0.17.

62
00:03:30,560 --> 00:03:32,510
And then just two more very quickly here.

63
00:03:32,540 --> 00:03:38,780
Reflect dash metadata at 0.1, .13.

64
00:03:38,780 --> 00:03:44,120
And then finally, very last one, TypeScript at 4.3, 0.2.

65
00:03:44,300 --> 00:03:45,230
And that's it.

66
00:03:45,260 --> 00:03:49,370
Now, I would really encourage you to double check your spelling on all these different dependencies,

67
00:03:49,370 --> 00:03:53,530
make sure you're specifying the exact same dependencies at the same versions that I am.

68
00:03:53,540 --> 00:03:56,390
Once you've double checked that, we'll go ahead and run this command.

69
00:03:56,690 --> 00:04:00,530
The installation might take a moment or two, so let's take a quick pause right now and come back in

70
00:04:00,530 --> 00:04:03,860
the next video and start writing up some code for our first project.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1
00:00:00,730 --> 00:00:05,710
In between the last video and this one, I opened up my code editor inside of my scratch project directory

2
00:00:05,710 --> 00:00:10,540
so I can see that I've got my package.json file which we just generated, and then all my dependencies

3
00:00:10,540 --> 00:00:12,430
are inside that nodemodules directory.

4
00:00:12,460 --> 00:00:15,790
Let's open up our package.json file inside of here.

5
00:00:15,790 --> 00:00:19,690
We've got the dependencies section and there's all the dependencies we just installed.

6
00:00:19,720 --> 00:00:24,190
Now believe it or not, we can actually understand a little bit around Nest by taking a look at these

7
00:00:24,190 --> 00:00:27,310
dependencies and just understand what they do for us.

8
00:00:27,310 --> 00:00:29,770
So very quickly, what are all these things about?

9
00:00:30,220 --> 00:00:30,550
All right.

10
00:00:30,550 --> 00:00:34,510
So I took that section, put it in this diagram right here, and we're going to take a look at four

11
00:00:34,510 --> 00:00:35,980
of these different dependencies.

12
00:00:36,070 --> 00:00:39,880
So the first one on that list is Nestjs Slash common.

13
00:00:39,910 --> 00:00:43,720
The nest library itself is split out into several different packages.

14
00:00:43,750 --> 00:00:49,180
The vast majority of functions, classes and whatnot that we're going to use to build a Nest application

15
00:00:49,180 --> 00:00:51,220
are coming from the common package.

16
00:00:51,220 --> 00:00:54,850
So we're going to be importing that package quite a bit throughout this entire course.

17
00:00:55,610 --> 00:01:00,170
Next up, we're going to skip over the core one and go to Platform Express.

18
00:01:00,290 --> 00:01:05,060
This one is a little bit more interesting, and this is what is going to start to really help us understand

19
00:01:05,060 --> 00:01:07,040
what Nest is doing internally.

20
00:01:07,160 --> 00:01:11,960
So internally, Nest itself does not handle incoming requests.

21
00:01:11,990 --> 00:01:15,620
Instead, here's a quick diagram of what's going on behind the scenes.

22
00:01:15,650 --> 00:01:21,110
Nest relies upon some outside implementation to handle Http requests for it.

23
00:01:21,200 --> 00:01:26,300
So inside of our Nest server that you and I are going to eventually create, we are going to have some

24
00:01:26,300 --> 00:01:31,310
place inside of our code where we have to plug in some kind of Http implementation.

25
00:01:31,310 --> 00:01:36,350
We have to provide some kind of server that says here is something that's going to handle incoming requests

26
00:01:36,350 --> 00:01:39,560
and then eventually respond to them with an outgoing response.

27
00:01:40,230 --> 00:01:45,460
In the world of Nest, we currently have two options for Http server implementations.

28
00:01:45,480 --> 00:01:51,930
We can either make use of the very popular library express.js or another one called Fastify.

29
00:01:51,960 --> 00:01:57,930
So we need to make a choice between both of these Http server implementations and essentially drop one

30
00:01:57,930 --> 00:02:00,150
in for nest to use.

31
00:02:01,380 --> 00:02:04,260
Right now the default is to make use of express.

32
00:02:04,260 --> 00:02:10,260
So if you ever generate a new project right now as it stands, you are going to get express by default.

33
00:02:10,290 --> 00:02:15,900
But we can easily say, you know what, instead of using express, I want to use Fastify instead, which

34
00:02:15,900 --> 00:02:19,470
is just another Http server implementation.

35
00:02:19,590 --> 00:02:23,910
Now, as to why you would use one or the other, it comes down to the kind of application you are building

36
00:02:23,910 --> 00:02:27,510
for you and I inside this course we're going to stick with the default of Express.

37
00:02:28,580 --> 00:02:34,310
So this dependency that we have right here of platform Dash Express says that we want to install an

38
00:02:34,310 --> 00:02:36,930
adapter between Express and Nest.

39
00:02:36,950 --> 00:02:39,860
It's going to make the two of them work together and behind the scenes.

40
00:02:39,890 --> 00:02:45,020
Nest is going to use Express for handling all incoming Http requests.

41
00:02:46,250 --> 00:02:46,550
All right.

42
00:02:46,550 --> 00:02:50,330
So now on to our next dependency reflect metadata.

43
00:02:50,360 --> 00:02:53,090
This is a library tied to decorators.

44
00:02:53,120 --> 00:02:55,190
If you're not familiar with decorators, don't sweat it.

45
00:02:55,190 --> 00:02:58,610
We're going to go into more detail on decorators in just a moment.

46
00:02:58,820 --> 00:03:01,410
And then finally, our last dependency there is TypeScript.

47
00:03:01,430 --> 00:03:04,490
So usually we write nest applications with TypeScript.

48
00:03:04,490 --> 00:03:05,900
That is not strictly required.

49
00:03:05,900 --> 00:03:10,970
You can write nest apps with JavaScript, but by far the most popular way to do this and probably what

50
00:03:10,970 --> 00:03:15,800
you're going to see on any job or serious project you work on is the use of TypeScript with Nest.

51
00:03:15,830 --> 00:03:18,470
So we are installing the TypeScript compiler here.

52
00:03:19,360 --> 00:03:19,630
All right.

53
00:03:19,630 --> 00:03:20,850
So now back on track.

54
00:03:20,860 --> 00:03:22,360
Let's continue with our application.

55
00:03:22,360 --> 00:03:25,960
We still haven't really said what this app is going to do, but for right now, let's just continue

56
00:03:25,960 --> 00:03:29,440
doing a little bit of setup and then we'll eventually decide on what kind of application we want to

57
00:03:29,440 --> 00:03:30,680
build in just a moment.

58
00:03:30,700 --> 00:03:33,270
So at this point in time, we have installed dependencies.

59
00:03:33,280 --> 00:03:38,020
The next thing we're going to do is set up a TypeScript compiler config file.

60
00:03:38,020 --> 00:03:41,110
So this is going to be a classic tsconfig.json file.

61
00:03:41,110 --> 00:03:45,340
We're going to write out a couple of settings inside of it just to tell TypeScript how to compile our

62
00:03:45,340 --> 00:03:46,120
project.

63
00:03:46,420 --> 00:03:49,780
Now, usually we do not have to do steps one and two.

64
00:03:49,780 --> 00:03:53,740
If we are using the CLI to generate our project for us.

65
00:03:53,740 --> 00:03:57,790
So these are just steps that we are doing because we are setting up our project from scratch.

66
00:03:58,330 --> 00:04:03,430
All right, so let's go create our config file and set up our TypeScript compiler very quickly.

67
00:04:04,950 --> 00:04:06,440
Tobacco inside my editor.

68
00:04:06,450 --> 00:04:10,560
I'm going to create a tsconfig.json file.

69
00:04:11,540 --> 00:04:16,550
I'm going to put in a set of curly braces, a string of compiler options.

70
00:04:17,930 --> 00:04:19,730
And then I'll assign to that an object.

71
00:04:21,210 --> 00:04:23,160
Opening there a couple of different key value pairs.

72
00:04:23,160 --> 00:04:27,060
The first one is module of Commonjs.

73
00:04:27,630 --> 00:04:31,140
Then a target of 2017.

74
00:04:32,430 --> 00:04:42,720
And then experimental decorators of true and finally emit decorator metadata of true.

75
00:04:43,140 --> 00:04:47,220
Once again, I got to encourage you to double check your spelling on each of these words.

76
00:04:47,250 --> 00:04:48,730
There are some tricky words inside of here.

77
00:04:48,750 --> 00:04:50,400
Make sure you've got the same spelling as I.

78
00:04:50,430 --> 00:04:53,360
Also, make sure you've got the same capitalization as well.

79
00:04:53,370 --> 00:04:58,020
So decorators should be capitalized, decorators should be capitalized and metadata should be capitalized

80
00:04:58,020 --> 00:04:58,800
as well.

81
00:04:58,950 --> 00:05:03,660
So once again, these are just a couple of settings to get TypeScript to properly transpile our project

82
00:05:03,660 --> 00:05:06,660
into normal JavaScript code before we eventually run it.

83
00:05:06,810 --> 00:05:11,160
The two settings that are very relevant for us and we're going to learn a lot more about very quickly

84
00:05:11,160 --> 00:05:14,930
our experimental decorators and emit decorator metadata.

85
00:05:14,940 --> 00:05:19,590
So we are going to come back to this file and go into more detail on what those are all about.

86
00:05:19,620 --> 00:05:24,780
These two features right here, believe it or not, are at the absolute core of what makes Nest really

87
00:05:24,780 --> 00:05:25,230
work.

88
00:05:25,230 --> 00:05:28,470
So it's super important for us to understand those two settings.

89
00:05:29,810 --> 00:05:30,080
All right.

90
00:05:30,080 --> 00:05:31,280
So that's step number two.

91
00:05:31,310 --> 00:05:35,780
Let's take one more pause right here and then go through steps number three and four in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,630 --> 00:00:03,750
All right, On to steps number three and four.

2
00:00:03,760 --> 00:00:06,970
So in step three, we need to create a Nest module and a controller.

3
00:00:06,970 --> 00:00:09,760
I want to very quickly tell you what a module and a controller are.

4
00:00:09,790 --> 00:00:11,860
So we're going to take a look at a couple of different diagrams.

5
00:00:12,280 --> 00:00:12,580
All right.

6
00:00:12,580 --> 00:00:16,300
So this diagram right here is probably going to look somewhat familiar if you've ever, ever put together

7
00:00:16,300 --> 00:00:17,380
any kind of server.

8
00:00:17,380 --> 00:00:22,630
So in just about every Http server you're ever going to create, you're going to have what is referred

9
00:00:22,630 --> 00:00:25,030
to as a request response cycle.

10
00:00:25,730 --> 00:00:28,760
Users are going to make requests to your server inside of your server.

11
00:00:28,760 --> 00:00:31,730
You're going to have some amount of code that's going to process that request.

12
00:00:31,760 --> 00:00:34,130
You might validate some data inside the request.

13
00:00:34,160 --> 00:00:38,120
You might handle the request differently depending upon what route it is addressed to.

14
00:00:38,120 --> 00:00:42,380
And then eventually you're going to formulate a response and send it back to whoever made the request.

15
00:00:43,040 --> 00:00:47,660
Now, just about every server you ever put together, this code right here that's going to process that

16
00:00:47,660 --> 00:00:51,250
request is almost always going to look rather similar in nature.

17
00:00:51,260 --> 00:00:56,990
In other words, this kind of request response cycle almost always has the same steps inside of it,

18
00:00:56,990 --> 00:01:02,510
regardless of the language framework library or whatever else that you use to build your server.

19
00:01:02,870 --> 00:01:07,670
So this code or that process almost always looks something like this.

20
00:01:07,970 --> 00:01:12,470
So just about every server that you're ever going to put together is going to receive a request.

21
00:01:12,500 --> 00:01:15,980
It then might do some validation on data contained inside that request.

22
00:01:15,980 --> 00:01:20,180
So for example, we might validate some body or some data in the body of the request.

23
00:01:20,480 --> 00:01:25,280
We then might make sure that the user is authenticated or authorized to send us a request.

24
00:01:25,990 --> 00:01:31,060
We're then going to route the request off to a particular function to handle the request that might

25
00:01:31,060 --> 00:01:36,130
result in us running some particular business logic and eventually maybe access or store some information

26
00:01:36,130 --> 00:01:37,540
inside of a database.

27
00:01:39,140 --> 00:01:43,550
Then as a result of this entire process, we're going to formulate a response and send the response

28
00:01:43,550 --> 00:01:45,560
back to whoever made us the request.

29
00:01:46,010 --> 00:01:51,110
So, again, doesn't really matter what language framework, anything that you're using, just about

30
00:01:51,110 --> 00:01:53,990
every server is going to have the same series of steps.

31
00:01:54,170 --> 00:01:58,310
In some cases we might not be doing authentication, so maybe some steps will fall out.

32
00:01:58,310 --> 00:02:01,190
But at the end of the day, this is kind of a very general case.

33
00:02:01,580 --> 00:02:07,940
So in Nestjs we get special tools to help us address each of these very particular steps.

34
00:02:07,940 --> 00:02:12,530
So in Nest we have tools to help us create something called, say, a pipe.

35
00:02:12,650 --> 00:02:16,460
Pipes and nest help us validate data on incoming request.

36
00:02:16,610 --> 00:02:20,270
We also have tools that allow us to create something called guards.

37
00:02:20,300 --> 00:02:25,370
Guards make sure that incoming requests are coming from users who are authenticated or authorized to

38
00:02:25,370 --> 00:02:26,480
use our application.

39
00:02:26,690 --> 00:02:29,510
We get also tools to create controllers.

40
00:02:29,540 --> 00:02:32,030
Controllers contain routing logic.

41
00:02:32,060 --> 00:02:34,240
We get tools to create something called services.

42
00:02:34,250 --> 00:02:36,350
Those contain business logic and so on.

43
00:02:36,650 --> 00:02:41,340
So throughout this course, as we work with Nest, we're going to learn about each of these different

44
00:02:41,340 --> 00:02:42,420
sets of tools.

45
00:02:42,420 --> 00:02:47,910
Each of these different sets of tools included in Nest are meant to help us kind of put together some

46
00:02:47,910 --> 00:02:53,190
piece of handling an incoming request besides just the five I'm showing right here, there's a couple

47
00:02:53,190 --> 00:02:54,910
of other tools we're going to look at.

48
00:02:54,930 --> 00:03:00,150
So these are all tools inside of Nest that help us make things that handle these incoming requests.

49
00:03:01,560 --> 00:03:04,750
So we still have controllers and services which we saw in the last diagram.

50
00:03:04,770 --> 00:03:06,810
We also have something called modules.

51
00:03:06,840 --> 00:03:10,830
We have pipes, filters, guards, interceptors and repositories.

52
00:03:11,130 --> 00:03:14,130
And again, we're going to learn about all these things throughout this course.

53
00:03:14,720 --> 00:03:20,400
Now, the absolute bare minimum that is required to put together the most simple application possible

54
00:03:20,430 --> 00:03:23,130
is a module and a controller.

55
00:03:23,340 --> 00:03:28,110
Every application you ever create is going to have at least one module and one controller inside of

56
00:03:28,110 --> 00:03:28,560
it.

57
00:03:28,800 --> 00:03:32,730
So in the diagram we were just looking at a moment ago, this one right here where we said we were going

58
00:03:32,730 --> 00:03:35,250
to create a Nest module and a controller.

59
00:03:35,280 --> 00:03:39,900
These are the two things that are required to make the most simple, basic app possible.

60
00:03:40,140 --> 00:03:44,670
So with that in mind, let's go back over to our editor and we're going to create a module and a controller.

61
00:03:46,130 --> 00:03:50,060
So back over here to get started, I'm going to create a new directory called SRC.

62
00:03:51,000 --> 00:03:54,330
Then inside there, I'm going to make a new file called Main.ts.

63
00:03:55,530 --> 00:03:59,520
Main.ts is going to be the first file that gets executed in any project.

64
00:03:59,520 --> 00:04:04,020
So we'll usually have some code inside of here to start up our application and start listening for traffic

65
00:04:04,020 --> 00:04:05,340
on a particular port.

66
00:04:06,620 --> 00:04:10,820
Usually we will create a module and a controller in their own separate files.

67
00:04:10,820 --> 00:04:15,510
But for right now we're going to create the module and the controller directly inside the main.ts file.

68
00:04:15,530 --> 00:04:17,510
So for right now, all logic inside this file.

69
00:04:17,510 --> 00:04:21,950
But we will very, very quickly extract the stuff into separate files.

70
00:04:23,210 --> 00:04:24,860
To create a module in a controller.

71
00:04:24,860 --> 00:04:28,970
We're going to add in an import statement at the very top, and I'm going to import something called

72
00:04:28,970 --> 00:04:34,490
controller and module from at Nestjs slash Common.

73
00:04:36,070 --> 00:04:41,320
So like I mentioned, these are tools that Nest provides to us to help us make a controller and make

74
00:04:41,320 --> 00:04:42,100
a module.

75
00:04:42,830 --> 00:04:44,690
To use them to create a controller.

76
00:04:44,690 --> 00:04:46,400
We're going to focus on the controller first.

77
00:04:46,430 --> 00:04:51,530
I'm going to create a class called App Controller.

78
00:04:52,380 --> 00:04:59,490
And then right above it, I'm going to add an at symbol controller and then call that like a function.

79
00:05:00,020 --> 00:05:05,630
So this is referred to as a decorator In this case, this decorator is telling Nest that we are trying

80
00:05:05,630 --> 00:05:09,420
to create a class that is going to serve as a controller inside of our application.

81
00:05:09,440 --> 00:05:12,800
It is a class that is going to handle and route incoming requests.

82
00:05:13,950 --> 00:05:16,230
Nest makes use of decorators quite heavily.

83
00:05:16,230 --> 00:05:20,520
So throughout this course, we're going to have a big focus on decorators, how they work and so on.

84
00:05:20,520 --> 00:05:23,940
But for right now, we're just going to add these decorators in and we'll discuss what they're really

85
00:05:23,940 --> 00:05:25,290
doing for us later on.

86
00:05:26,250 --> 00:05:29,820
Then inside of our class, we're going to add in a variety of different methods.

87
00:05:29,850 --> 00:05:33,960
Each method is designed to handle one kind of incoming request.

88
00:05:33,990 --> 00:05:38,880
Specifically, we want to add in an additional method anytime we want to have another route inside of

89
00:05:38,880 --> 00:05:39,750
our application.

90
00:05:40,020 --> 00:05:46,050
So, for example, if we want to handle a get request to the root route of our application, we might

91
00:05:46,050 --> 00:05:48,870
add in a new method here called something like.

92
00:05:49,940 --> 00:05:51,070
Get root.

93
00:05:51,080 --> 00:05:51,800
Root.

94
00:05:52,840 --> 00:05:58,330
Then whenever someone makes a request to our application, we want to route that request to this method

95
00:05:58,330 --> 00:05:59,080
right here.

96
00:05:59,230 --> 00:06:03,280
To do so, we're going to import another helper from the Common library.

97
00:06:03,550 --> 00:06:06,040
This helper is called the Jit decorator.

98
00:06:06,070 --> 00:06:11,650
This allows us to create route handlers that respond to incoming requests that have an Http method of

99
00:06:11,650 --> 00:06:12,250
Jit.

100
00:06:12,490 --> 00:06:17,290
So right above my method, I'm going to put in at Jit like so.

101
00:06:18,490 --> 00:06:23,710
Then inside my method, anytime someone makes a request to our root route, that's going to run this

102
00:06:23,710 --> 00:06:28,930
method right here, and then all we have to do to respond to that request is return a value from this

103
00:06:28,930 --> 00:06:29,570
method.

104
00:06:29,590 --> 00:06:32,830
So in this case, I'm going to return simply a string of Hi there.

105
00:06:34,510 --> 00:06:38,280
Now, in theory, if someone makes a request to our application, it will get routed to this method

106
00:06:38,280 --> 00:06:38,910
right here.

107
00:06:38,940 --> 00:06:43,080
We're going to return that string, and nest is going to automatically take that string and send it

108
00:06:43,080 --> 00:06:44,610
back to whoever made the request.

109
00:06:45,570 --> 00:06:45,900
All right.

110
00:06:45,900 --> 00:06:48,030
So a lot of talking here, a lot of stuff going on.

111
00:06:48,030 --> 00:06:49,830
Let's take a pause very, very quickly.

112
00:06:49,830 --> 00:06:52,500
Come back in just a moment and start to create our module.

113
00:06:52,690 --> 00:06:56,940
Last thing I want to mention is if these decorators and things are really confusing right now, don't

114
00:06:56,940 --> 00:06:57,360
sweat it.

115
00:06:57,360 --> 00:07:02,130
As I mentioned, we're going to be going over decorators a tremendous amount inside this course.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,590 --> 00:00:04,110
Let's continue on and create a module.

2
00:00:04,140 --> 00:00:06,670
A module is going to wrap up a controller.

3
00:00:06,690 --> 00:00:09,810
Every application we create must have one module inside of it.

4
00:00:09,840 --> 00:00:13,740
To create a module, we're going to create another class and apply a decorator to it.

5
00:00:13,740 --> 00:00:16,440
Very similar to what we did with the app controller right here.

6
00:00:16,530 --> 00:00:21,300
So right after that app controller thing, I'm going to add in at module.

7
00:00:22,260 --> 00:00:24,840
I'll then define a class called app module.

8
00:00:25,890 --> 00:00:26,670
Like so.

9
00:00:27,280 --> 00:00:32,530
When we make use of the module decorator, we are expected to pass a configuration option or object

10
00:00:32,530 --> 00:00:33,110
to it.

11
00:00:33,130 --> 00:00:37,600
So I'm going to pass in an object and we're going to give it right now just one single property.

12
00:00:38,430 --> 00:00:42,450
This is going to be the controllers property and it's going to list out all the different controllers

13
00:00:42,450 --> 00:00:44,280
that exist inside of our application.

14
00:00:44,460 --> 00:00:47,340
Right now, we only have one controller, the app controller.

15
00:00:47,340 --> 00:00:51,510
So I'm going to add in app controller right there.

16
00:00:53,340 --> 00:00:57,630
Now, whenever our application starts up, Nest is going to look at this app module.

17
00:00:57,630 --> 00:01:01,560
It's going to find all the controllers that are listed right here, and it's going to automatically

18
00:01:01,560 --> 00:01:05,190
create an instance of all of our different controller classes.

19
00:01:05,190 --> 00:01:09,180
So Nest will create an instance of this app controller for us.

20
00:01:09,180 --> 00:01:13,200
It's then going to take a look at all the different decorators we used inside there and set up route

21
00:01:13,200 --> 00:01:17,190
handlers for each of those different decorators that we used.

22
00:01:18,310 --> 00:01:22,570
So now the last thing we have to do is add in a function that is going to run anytime we start up our

23
00:01:22,570 --> 00:01:23,450
application.

24
00:01:23,470 --> 00:01:25,540
Let's add that at the very bottom of the file.

25
00:01:26,120 --> 00:01:33,330
So down here, I'm going to add in an async function and usually by default, this thing is called bootstrap.

26
00:01:33,350 --> 00:01:35,780
It is not required to call this function bootstrap at all.

27
00:01:35,780 --> 00:01:37,520
It's just a very common convention.

28
00:01:38,460 --> 00:01:44,340
Then inside of here, we're going to create a new nest application out of our app module.

29
00:01:44,550 --> 00:01:52,680
So I'm going to say const app is Await Nest Factory dot Create app module.

30
00:01:53,010 --> 00:01:56,190
Right away we will get an error from nest factory right there.

31
00:01:56,190 --> 00:01:59,190
We have to add in an import for nest factory at the very top of the file.

32
00:01:59,190 --> 00:02:00,630
Let's take care of that really quickly.

33
00:02:01,300 --> 00:02:02,770
So up here at the very top.

34
00:02:04,140 --> 00:02:05,640
I'm going to import.

35
00:02:07,480 --> 00:02:09,250
Nest factory.

36
00:02:09,950 --> 00:02:11,210
From Nest.

37
00:02:12,150 --> 00:02:14,010
JS score.

38
00:02:14,920 --> 00:02:16,870
And notice here something a little bit confusing.

39
00:02:16,870 --> 00:02:18,840
We now have two different import statements.

40
00:02:18,850 --> 00:02:24,550
One is importing something from Nestjs Common, the other is importing something from Nestjs core.

41
00:02:24,550 --> 00:02:29,830
In just about 95% of all import statements, you write where you need something from nest, you are

42
00:02:29,830 --> 00:02:32,770
going to import stuff from Nestjs Common.

43
00:02:33,580 --> 00:02:38,800
Your Main.ts file is one of the very few locations that you're ever going to import anything from Nestjs

44
00:02:39,100 --> 00:02:39,720
core.

45
00:02:39,730 --> 00:02:44,950
So if you ever get confused on whether you are importing stuff from common or core, remember it is

46
00:02:44,950 --> 00:02:46,900
just about always supposed to be common.

47
00:02:48,730 --> 00:02:49,030
All right.

48
00:02:49,030 --> 00:02:50,620
Down back down here at the very bottom.

49
00:02:51,840 --> 00:02:56,790
So as I mentioned, we are passing off the app module to this create function that's going to create

50
00:02:56,790 --> 00:02:59,130
an instance of our Nest application.

51
00:02:59,250 --> 00:03:03,900
Once we've created our app right there, we're going to tell this app to start to listen for incoming

52
00:03:03,930 --> 00:03:06,640
traffic on a particular port on our computer.

53
00:03:06,660 --> 00:03:13,350
So we're going to say Await app Dot, listen and we're going to listen on Port 3000.

54
00:03:14,940 --> 00:03:18,540
Finally, right after that, we're going to call the bootstrap function.

55
00:03:20,170 --> 00:03:20,500
All right.

56
00:03:20,500 --> 00:03:22,740
So let's go over this file in total very quickly.

57
00:03:22,750 --> 00:03:26,280
Whenever we run our application, we've got a couple imports up here.

58
00:03:26,290 --> 00:03:30,700
We're going to create one controller that has one route handler set up inside of it.

59
00:03:31,620 --> 00:03:37,200
We then create a module and we tell this app module about the app controller we had just created.

60
00:03:37,970 --> 00:03:41,880
We then define and immediately invoke the bootstrap function.

61
00:03:41,900 --> 00:03:46,040
The bootstrap function is going to create an instance of our application and then tell it to listen

62
00:03:46,040 --> 00:03:47,270
for incoming traffic.

63
00:03:48,410 --> 00:03:51,650
So right now we have exactly one route defined inside of our application.

64
00:03:51,650 --> 00:03:59,030
If we make a git request to localhost 3000 with no route on it, then that request will be routed to

65
00:03:59,030 --> 00:04:02,360
this route handler right here and we will return a string of.

66
00:04:02,360 --> 00:04:03,110
Hi there.

67
00:04:03,530 --> 00:04:05,300
So let's now go back over to our terminal.

68
00:04:05,300 --> 00:04:10,190
We're going to run our application and then we're going to try to test it out from our browser.

69
00:04:10,610 --> 00:04:15,230
So back over at my terminal, I'm going to make sure I'm inside of my project directory.

70
00:04:15,260 --> 00:04:23,120
I'm then going to run NP, TS, node dev and then SRC main dot ts.

71
00:04:23,390 --> 00:04:27,530
Now remember, right now we are running a project we have set up from scratch.

72
00:04:27,530 --> 00:04:31,850
That's why we are running this command in particular to start up our project very quickly.

73
00:04:31,850 --> 00:04:37,160
On the next application we work on, we're going to use the command line tool to generate an application

74
00:04:37,160 --> 00:04:37,870
for us.

75
00:04:37,880 --> 00:04:41,180
Once we do so, we'll have a much easier way of starting up our project.

76
00:04:41,180 --> 00:04:43,210
In other words, it will be a much simpler command.

77
00:04:43,220 --> 00:04:47,810
So we are only using this command for right now just because we have set up everything for this project

78
00:04:47,810 --> 00:04:48,840
from scratch.

79
00:04:49,500 --> 00:04:49,740
Okay.

80
00:04:49,740 --> 00:04:50,730
So I'm going to run that.

81
00:04:52,310 --> 00:04:54,410
Well, then very quickly, see some output right here.

82
00:04:54,410 --> 00:04:59,040
And then eventually we should see something that says nest application successfully started.

83
00:04:59,060 --> 00:05:03,590
If you do not see this message, if you instead see some kind of error, make sure that you don't already

84
00:05:03,590 --> 00:05:05,960
have another process on your computer.

85
00:05:05,960 --> 00:05:08,400
Listening to traffic on Port 3000.

86
00:05:08,420 --> 00:05:13,340
If you do, you can either stop that other process or change that 3000 port right there.

87
00:05:14,470 --> 00:05:14,710
All right.

88
00:05:14,710 --> 00:05:18,730
So now that our server is listening to traffic, let's open up our web browser and we're going to try

89
00:05:18,730 --> 00:05:20,910
to make a request to localhost 3000.

90
00:05:20,920 --> 00:05:22,810
And as I mentioned, we should see the text.

91
00:05:22,810 --> 00:05:23,260
Hi there.

92
00:05:23,260 --> 00:05:24,190
Appear on the screen.

93
00:05:25,370 --> 00:05:25,730
All right.

94
00:05:25,730 --> 00:05:30,890
So back over inside my browser, I'm going to go to localhost colon 3000.

95
00:05:31,070 --> 00:05:32,540
And sure enough, we see.

96
00:05:32,540 --> 00:05:33,410
Hi there.

97
00:05:33,530 --> 00:05:33,910
All right.

98
00:05:33,920 --> 00:05:34,850
Fantastic.

99
00:05:35,060 --> 00:05:39,620
Well, obviously, this is really simple and straightforward, and we definitely want to build out some

100
00:05:39,620 --> 00:05:41,600
more complicated applications.

101
00:05:41,600 --> 00:05:46,310
So let's take a pause right here and we're going to start to really enhance the kind of complexity of

102
00:05:46,310 --> 00:05:49,490
our application and go into greater detail in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:04,480
Well, our very basic application is working out just fine.

2
00:00:04,480 --> 00:00:08,280
But as I mentioned, we currently have all of our logic inside the Main.ts file.

3
00:00:08,290 --> 00:00:13,000
Usually we instead create just one single class per file inside of a Nest application.

4
00:00:13,000 --> 00:00:18,220
So in this video we're going to start to extract the app controller and the app module into their own

5
00:00:18,220 --> 00:00:19,200
separate files.

6
00:00:19,210 --> 00:00:23,650
Before we do so, I want to very quickly tell you about some file naming conventions in Nest.

7
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:28,430
So right now, as I mentioned, inside of Main.ts, we have all three of these different things.

8
00:00:28,450 --> 00:00:32,860
We're going to extract the app controller and the app module into their own separate files.

9
00:00:33,040 --> 00:00:36,480
These files are going to have very specific names applied to them.

10
00:00:36,490 --> 00:00:42,640
So the general template here that we're going to use is the name of the class without the type of the

11
00:00:42,670 --> 00:00:44,590
of the class it is.

12
00:00:45,200 --> 00:00:46,090
That's a little bit confusing.

13
00:00:46,090 --> 00:00:49,030
So these examples, I think, will kind of clear things up.

14
00:00:49,670 --> 00:00:53,330
In this case, we are creating a class called app Controller.

15
00:00:53,330 --> 00:00:56,090
So the kind of class we are making is controller.

16
00:00:56,090 --> 00:00:59,530
And the first part of the name of this class is simply app.

17
00:00:59,540 --> 00:01:04,850
So an appropriate name for this file would be app dot controller, dot ts.

18
00:01:05,240 --> 00:01:11,090
In the case of app module, the type of class we are creating is module, and the shorthand name for

19
00:01:11,090 --> 00:01:12,190
this thing is app.

20
00:01:12,200 --> 00:01:16,310
So an appropriate file name would be app.module.ts.

21
00:01:16,640 --> 00:01:20,660
We're going to repeat the same pattern for every class that we create inside of our application.

22
00:01:20,660 --> 00:01:22,850
They're all going to have the same format of name.

23
00:01:22,850 --> 00:01:29,480
So we should always be able to look at a file name or a class name and immediately understand the purpose

24
00:01:29,480 --> 00:01:30,200
of this thing.

25
00:01:30,200 --> 00:01:34,310
So in this case, we can look at the word controller right now right there and understand that this

26
00:01:34,310 --> 00:01:36,320
is a file that contains a controller.

27
00:01:36,500 --> 00:01:39,980
Likewise, if we look at the name of the class, it says controller in it.

28
00:01:39,980 --> 00:01:44,150
So we know immediately that this is a class that serves as a controller.

29
00:01:44,940 --> 00:01:46,560
So let's go and create two new files.

30
00:01:46,560 --> 00:01:51,270
One will be app controller, the other will be app module, and then we'll move some code around between

31
00:01:51,270 --> 00:01:51,690
them.

32
00:01:52,930 --> 00:01:53,260
All right.

33
00:01:53,260 --> 00:01:59,510
So back inside my editor, I'm going to create a new file called App dot controller, dot ts.

34
00:02:00,340 --> 00:02:02,260
And a second file called.

35
00:02:03,180 --> 00:02:05,520
App.module.ts.

36
00:02:07,150 --> 00:02:12,550
I'll then go back to our main file and let's take care of the app controller first.

37
00:02:12,730 --> 00:02:15,820
So I'm going to highlight all the code related to the app controller.

38
00:02:16,120 --> 00:02:17,290
I'm going to cut it.

39
00:02:17,920 --> 00:02:22,120
Then go over to our app controller file and paste all that stuff.

40
00:02:22,450 --> 00:02:24,310
Right away we get a couple of error messages.

41
00:02:24,310 --> 00:02:26,260
We just need to fix up some imports.

42
00:02:26,260 --> 00:02:34,240
So at the very top I will import controller and git from at nestjs slash common.

43
00:02:35,210 --> 00:02:38,030
I'm also going to make sure that I export this class as well.

44
00:02:38,060 --> 00:02:41,960
Don't forget that export, because we need to get access to this class from other files.

45
00:02:43,220 --> 00:02:44,590
Okay, so that's it for this file.

46
00:02:44,600 --> 00:02:46,100
So I'm going to close that file.

47
00:02:46,750 --> 00:02:49,030
Then back inside of Main.ts.

48
00:02:49,060 --> 00:02:52,420
I'm then going to cut the app module out.

49
00:02:54,200 --> 00:02:58,910
I'll go back over to the App.module.ts file, paste that thing in.

50
00:02:59,910 --> 00:03:02,340
We'll then do another couple imports at the very top.

51
00:03:02,340 --> 00:03:12,300
So we need to import module from Nestjs Common and we also need to import the app controller from same

52
00:03:12,300 --> 00:03:14,880
directory app dot module.

53
00:03:15,650 --> 00:03:15,690
Or.

54
00:03:15,710 --> 00:03:16,070
Excuse me.

55
00:03:16,400 --> 00:03:17,480
Controller My mistake.

56
00:03:20,460 --> 00:03:21,960
Okay, so that looks good as well.

57
00:03:22,230 --> 00:03:25,870
Finally, don't forget to export the app module class down here.

58
00:03:25,890 --> 00:03:27,870
So we'll add in the export keyword.

59
00:03:29,370 --> 00:03:30,420
Okay, so that looks good.

60
00:03:30,780 --> 00:03:33,450
So now finally back over to our Main.ts file.

61
00:03:33,480 --> 00:03:35,550
We have to clean up a couple of things inside of here.

62
00:03:35,580 --> 00:03:39,060
First, let's clean up the first import statement that we have.

63
00:03:39,090 --> 00:03:43,710
We do not need access to the controller module or git inside this file anymore.

64
00:03:43,950 --> 00:03:45,990
So I'm going to delete that entire first line.

65
00:03:47,760 --> 00:03:52,620
Next up, we are going to import the app module from the app dot module file.

66
00:03:52,830 --> 00:03:55,560
So import app module.

67
00:03:56,340 --> 00:03:59,100
From app dot module.

68
00:04:00,870 --> 00:04:01,290
All right.

69
00:04:01,290 --> 00:04:03,170
Well, that should be it for our refactor.

70
00:04:03,180 --> 00:04:08,820
So now if we save all these files and go back over to our terminal, our application should still be

71
00:04:08,820 --> 00:04:10,980
running and we should not have any error messages.

72
00:04:11,100 --> 00:04:16,440
In addition, we should be able to go back over to our browser refresh localhost 3000 and still see

73
00:04:16,470 --> 00:04:17,970
hi there, appear on the screen.

74
00:04:18,760 --> 00:04:19,360
All right, Well, let's see.

75
00:04:19,360 --> 00:04:20,350
This looks pretty good.

76
00:04:20,970 --> 00:04:26,400
So we've now got one class per file, which much better follows the conventions of Nestjs.

77
00:04:26,910 --> 00:04:31,110
Now, last thing I want to do inside this application is show you a little bit more around how these

78
00:04:31,110 --> 00:04:33,270
controller and git decorators work.

79
00:04:33,270 --> 00:04:35,400
So let's take a look at that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,610 --> 00:00:05,180
The last thing I want to show you inside this application is some stuff around the app controller,

2
00:00:05,180 --> 00:00:08,570
specifically the controller decorator and the Jit decorators.

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:12,920
Right now we have called the controller decorator and the Jit decorator and not passed any arguments

4
00:00:12,920 --> 00:00:13,530
to them.

5
00:00:13,610 --> 00:00:18,290
This causes nest to set up a route handler that is going to respond to our route route.

6
00:00:18,290 --> 00:00:19,910
And that's why we named this method right here.

7
00:00:19,940 --> 00:00:20,540
Get route.

8
00:00:20,540 --> 00:00:21,140
Route.

9
00:00:21,380 --> 00:00:26,140
We can also add in some routing rules through the controller decorator and the Jit decorator.

10
00:00:26,150 --> 00:00:28,400
So let me just give you a very quick example.

11
00:00:28,730 --> 00:00:31,880
I'm going to pass a string to this decorator of.

12
00:00:32,590 --> 00:00:34,810
Asdf Just keep it very simple.

13
00:00:35,080 --> 00:00:37,090
Notice the slash in there as well.

14
00:00:37,970 --> 00:00:40,790
If we then save this file and go back over to our browser.

15
00:00:41,860 --> 00:00:43,060
And refresh the page.

16
00:00:43,060 --> 00:00:45,960
Now we're going to get a 404 not found error.

17
00:00:45,970 --> 00:00:51,340
So by adding in slash Asdf right here, we have changed how this root handler works.

18
00:00:51,370 --> 00:00:57,250
Now this root handler is going to respond to get requests to a root of slash Asdf.

19
00:00:57,950 --> 00:01:03,080
So if I instead make a request to localhost 3000 slash Asdf.

20
00:01:03,470 --> 00:01:07,610
Now we are successfully making a request to that same route handler again.

21
00:01:09,120 --> 00:01:13,620
We can also use the same technique at the controller decorator up here as well.

22
00:01:13,740 --> 00:01:18,030
So I can put in something like slash app right there.

23
00:01:19,590 --> 00:01:24,270
Any route that we put inside the controller decorator is going to apply to all the route handlers that

24
00:01:24,270 --> 00:01:26,370
we define inside of this controller.

25
00:01:26,400 --> 00:01:32,310
So now to make a request to that route handler, we would have to make a request to slash app slash

26
00:01:32,340 --> 00:01:34,270
Asdf to test that out.

27
00:01:34,290 --> 00:01:35,760
We'll go back over to our browser.

28
00:01:36,030 --> 00:01:43,200
Now, if I refresh the page, we again get that 404, but if I change my request to slash app slash

29
00:01:43,230 --> 00:01:46,320
Asdf, once again, our application works.

30
00:01:47,500 --> 00:01:52,810
So we're going to use the controller decorator to control some high level routing rules that are going

31
00:01:52,810 --> 00:01:56,590
to apply to all of the different route handlers that we set up inside this controller.

32
00:01:56,590 --> 00:02:01,270
And then in theory, all the different methods we define inside this controller are going to have a

33
00:02:01,270 --> 00:02:02,380
different route.

34
00:02:02,740 --> 00:02:04,960
Let's try now creating a second route.

35
00:02:05,200 --> 00:02:10,720
So I'm going to define another method here called how about git by their.

36
00:02:11,640 --> 00:02:14,580
From it will return a string of by their.

37
00:02:15,270 --> 00:02:18,510
And then right above it, I'll add in another get decorator.

38
00:02:18,870 --> 00:02:27,090
So I'm going to say, if anyone makes a request to slash app slash by, we will respond with the text

39
00:02:27,120 --> 00:02:28,290
of by there.

40
00:02:28,650 --> 00:02:31,830
So again, I'll save this back over to the browser.

41
00:02:31,950 --> 00:02:36,630
So now I'll make a request to app slash by and I see by there.

42
00:02:36,660 --> 00:02:37,290
Up here.

43
00:02:38,640 --> 00:02:39,000
All right.

44
00:02:39,000 --> 00:02:42,310
So now we've got some basics around routing with controllers.

45
00:02:42,330 --> 00:02:46,470
Let's take one more pause right here and then move on to our next application in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


==================================================================================


# section 3: Generating Projects with the Nest CLI

1
00:00:01,300 --> 00:00:02,950
That is it for our first project.

2
00:00:02,950 --> 00:00:07,960
I know this was a very small project and so it might feel like we created a lot of different files and

3
00:00:07,960 --> 00:00:09,790
just kind of threw it all away very quickly.

4
00:00:09,790 --> 00:00:10,960
But there is a really good reason.

5
00:00:10,960 --> 00:00:12,490
We started off with this project.

6
00:00:12,490 --> 00:00:17,260
As soon as we make use of the CLI to generate a project for us, it is going to create many different

7
00:00:17,260 --> 00:00:21,070
files and folders and all these files and folders are going to be a little bit confusing.

8
00:00:21,070 --> 00:00:26,140
So I just wanted you to have one very simple project where you can see how little code it takes to get

9
00:00:26,140 --> 00:00:27,280
Nest up and running.

10
00:00:27,760 --> 00:00:31,600
Okay, so in this video we're going to generate a new project using the Nest CLI.

11
00:00:31,810 --> 00:00:34,900
We'll then discuss exactly what this project is going to do for us.

12
00:00:34,900 --> 00:00:39,910
So to get started, I'm going to close out this code editor, go back to my terminal and I'm going to

13
00:00:39,910 --> 00:00:41,290
stop that running server.

14
00:00:42,470 --> 00:00:47,570
I'll then change back to any workspace directory on my computer so this can be any folder on your machine

15
00:00:47,570 --> 00:00:49,280
where you want to place some amount of code.

16
00:00:49,610 --> 00:00:54,110
We're then going to run a command that is going to install the nest CLI globally.

17
00:00:54,200 --> 00:01:00,980
So I'm going to run NPM install g at nestjs slash cli.

18
00:01:01,490 --> 00:01:06,860
If you run this command and you get an error, i would recommend either prepending sudo.

19
00:01:07,710 --> 00:01:11,070
Onto the command, or alternatively you could try to run the command through NP.

20
00:01:13,270 --> 00:01:15,040
Okay, so I'm going to install that.

21
00:01:15,340 --> 00:01:20,650
And in my case, I sure enough do get an error here, So I'm just going to rerun it very quickly with

22
00:01:20,650 --> 00:01:22,180
sudo prepend it on.

23
00:01:25,550 --> 00:01:26,630
All right, Much better.

24
00:01:26,960 --> 00:01:30,920
So now that I have installed an I'm going to use it to generate a new project.

25
00:01:30,920 --> 00:01:36,590
To generate a new project, we will run, Nest, New, and then the name of our project.

26
00:01:36,620 --> 00:01:39,410
This first project is going to be all about storing messages.

27
00:01:39,410 --> 00:01:41,390
So it's going to be a pretty simple and straightforward app.

28
00:01:41,390 --> 00:01:44,300
So I'm going to call this application simply Messages.

29
00:01:45,450 --> 00:01:45,900
So I'm going to run.

30
00:01:45,900 --> 00:01:49,190
That command will then be prompted with a couple of different questions.

31
00:01:49,200 --> 00:01:53,310
If you have more than one package manager installed on your computer, you'll be prompted on which one

32
00:01:53,310 --> 00:01:54,300
you want to use.

33
00:01:54,330 --> 00:01:58,710
For the purposes of this course, I'm going to use NPM, which is likely what you're going to want to

34
00:01:58,710 --> 00:01:59,670
use as well.

35
00:02:00,440 --> 00:02:01,940
Well, then go ahead and hit enter.

36
00:02:01,940 --> 00:02:05,630
Now after that, it's going to take a couple of minutes to install a bunch of different dependencies.

37
00:02:05,630 --> 00:02:09,770
So while that's going on, let's very quickly talk about the application we are going to build.

38
00:02:10,280 --> 00:02:10,610
All right.

39
00:02:10,610 --> 00:02:12,200
So here's the goal of our application.

40
00:02:12,200 --> 00:02:15,230
As I mentioned, it's going to be pretty simple and straightforward.

41
00:02:15,260 --> 00:02:22,130
We are going to store and retrieve messages that are stored inside of a plain Json file inside of our

42
00:02:22,130 --> 00:02:23,270
project directory.

43
00:02:23,270 --> 00:02:26,330
Now, a message is really just a very simple string.

44
00:02:26,330 --> 00:02:32,120
So we are storing strings and retrieving strings out of a plain Json file and that's pretty much it.

45
00:02:32,780 --> 00:02:35,970
So in total, we're going to have three routes defined inside of our app.

46
00:02:35,990 --> 00:02:39,500
We're going to have one route to list all the messages that we have ever saved.

47
00:02:39,530 --> 00:02:46,720
We'll have one route to retrieve a message by its ID and one route to create a message as well for each

48
00:02:46,720 --> 00:02:47,630
of these different routes.

49
00:02:47,660 --> 00:02:52,340
We're going to go through a series of diagrams and we're going to try to think about what different

50
00:02:52,370 --> 00:02:56,810
types of classes we need to create to service each of these different routes.

51
00:02:57,770 --> 00:03:01,070
So let's go through one example of this, and I think you'll get the idea very quickly.

52
00:03:01,460 --> 00:03:07,850
So the first route we're going to consider is a request that is a git type request to a root of something

53
00:03:07,850 --> 00:03:11,060
like localhost, 3000 slash messages.

54
00:03:11,180 --> 00:03:16,400
And as you guess, if someone makes a request to that route, we probably want to get a list of messages

55
00:03:16,400 --> 00:03:18,800
and send them back to whoever made that request.

56
00:03:19,430 --> 00:03:24,470
So given a request like this, what are the different things that we are going to have to create?

57
00:03:25,100 --> 00:03:28,260
In this case, we don't really have any data that we need to validate.

58
00:03:28,280 --> 00:03:32,810
There's no data inside this request, so I don't think we need a pipe or anything like that.

59
00:03:33,550 --> 00:03:38,950
We've also not said anything about handling user authentication or authorization, so I don't think

60
00:03:38,950 --> 00:03:39,910
we need a guard.

61
00:03:40,940 --> 00:03:44,630
But we do want to have something to route that request to a particular function.

62
00:03:44,630 --> 00:03:46,970
So think we will want to have a controller.

63
00:03:47,240 --> 00:03:52,850
We probably also want to have some logic that will try to reach into the database or access repository

64
00:03:52,850 --> 00:03:54,410
and get a list of all these messages.

65
00:03:54,410 --> 00:03:56,120
So we probably do need a service.

66
00:03:56,120 --> 00:04:01,580
And then finally, although we don't have strictly a database per se, like a SQL type database or a

67
00:04:01,580 --> 00:04:06,110
Mongo database or something like that, we are going to treat that file where we store all these messages

68
00:04:06,140 --> 00:04:07,430
as our database.

69
00:04:07,430 --> 00:04:12,410
So think we need a repository to represent that file where we store all these messages.

70
00:04:12,560 --> 00:04:18,650
So in total for this first request right here, we definitely need at least one controller service and

71
00:04:18,650 --> 00:04:19,460
repository.

72
00:04:20,820 --> 00:04:24,030
Okay, let's repeat this process for our other two requests very quickly.

73
00:04:25,570 --> 00:04:27,640
The request to create a message.

74
00:04:27,790 --> 00:04:30,550
I think that a request to create a message will look like this.

75
00:04:30,580 --> 00:04:36,520
It will probably be a post request to slash messages and it will probably be a body on the request as

76
00:04:36,520 --> 00:04:40,300
well that has the content of the message that this user wants to add.

77
00:04:40,540 --> 00:04:43,540
So in this case, we do have some incoming data.

78
00:04:43,810 --> 00:04:48,040
It's the body of the request and we might want to validate this data right here and make sure that it

79
00:04:48,040 --> 00:04:50,420
has a content property that is a string.

80
00:04:50,440 --> 00:04:54,550
Maybe we also want to make sure that this string is not too long or anything like that.

81
00:04:54,640 --> 00:04:57,040
So in this case, I think we do need a pipe.

82
00:04:57,490 --> 00:05:02,530
However, we don't have any authentication still, so I think we do not need a guard.

83
00:05:03,580 --> 00:05:05,700
But we still need to have some routing logic.

84
00:05:05,710 --> 00:05:10,450
We still need a service to actually try to create the message and we still need a repository where we

85
00:05:10,450 --> 00:05:11,980
can store the message itself.

86
00:05:12,130 --> 00:05:16,660
So in total, we definitely need a pipe, a controller, a service and a repository.

87
00:05:16,720 --> 00:05:21,500
Now there's something I want to clarify right away and be really clear between these two diagrams.

88
00:05:21,520 --> 00:05:25,240
I've said that for this first request, we need a controller service and repository.

89
00:05:25,240 --> 00:05:28,570
And for the second one I said we need a pipe controller, service and repository.

90
00:05:28,600 --> 00:05:34,090
This doesn't mean that we're going to create two controllers and two services and two repositories.

91
00:05:34,120 --> 00:05:38,050
We're only going to make one controller, one service and repository to service all these different

92
00:05:38,050 --> 00:05:38,800
requests.

93
00:05:38,830 --> 00:05:43,930
My only goal right now is to say to you or convey the fact that we need to think about how we're going

94
00:05:43,930 --> 00:05:47,980
to handle each of these different requests and what different parts or what different things we need

95
00:05:47,980 --> 00:05:49,640
to create to handle them all.

96
00:05:49,660 --> 00:05:53,440
So in total, we're only going to make one controller service and repository.

97
00:05:55,020 --> 00:05:55,260
All right.

98
00:05:55,290 --> 00:05:56,400
On to the last one.

99
00:05:56,880 --> 00:06:01,350
So if a user wants to get a message with a particular ID, we'll probably have them make a request like

100
00:06:01,350 --> 00:06:01,850
this.

101
00:06:01,860 --> 00:06:06,810
Request to localhost 3000 slash messages, slash and then whatever the ID is.

102
00:06:06,990 --> 00:06:10,260
So now in this case, once again, I don't think we need a pipe.

103
00:06:10,290 --> 00:06:15,570
I don't think we need a guard, but I think we do need a controller as a service in a repository.

104
00:06:17,310 --> 00:06:17,640
All right.

105
00:06:17,640 --> 00:06:22,830
So in total, between these three different requests, we've established that we definitely do not need

106
00:06:22,830 --> 00:06:24,450
a guard in any case.

107
00:06:25,940 --> 00:06:27,170
But we do need a pipe.

108
00:06:27,200 --> 00:06:28,400
We need a controller.

109
00:06:28,400 --> 00:06:30,140
We need a service and a repository.

110
00:06:30,760 --> 00:06:35,050
We're going to name the controller, something like messages controller, because that's going to make

111
00:06:35,050 --> 00:06:40,300
it very clear that this is a controller related to handling messages or requests around messages.

112
00:06:40,480 --> 00:06:45,970
We'll name the service, something like messages service to make it very clear this is a service around

113
00:06:45,970 --> 00:06:47,130
handling messages.

114
00:06:47,140 --> 00:06:52,300
And finally, for repository, we'll probably end up with the name like messages repository or something

115
00:06:52,300 --> 00:06:53,410
very similar to it.

116
00:06:53,800 --> 00:06:59,110
Finally, one more thing that we haven't really discussed yet a module we make use of modules to wrap

117
00:06:59,110 --> 00:07:03,930
up sets of functionality or kind of groups of different classes inside of our application.

118
00:07:03,940 --> 00:07:08,950
So we're going to also create a module that's going to wrap up our controller service repository and

119
00:07:08,950 --> 00:07:10,930
any pipe related stuff as well.

120
00:07:11,110 --> 00:07:15,320
We'll call this either our app module or messages module.

121
00:07:15,700 --> 00:07:18,790
We'll figure out what an appropriate name is in just a moment.

122
00:07:19,570 --> 00:07:19,870
All right.

123
00:07:19,870 --> 00:07:20,620
So that's it.

124
00:07:20,620 --> 00:07:23,530
That's what we're going to create to build this application.

125
00:07:23,740 --> 00:07:25,050
I know this has been a long video.

126
00:07:25,060 --> 00:07:25,960
I apologize for that.

127
00:07:25,960 --> 00:07:29,350
But I just want to make sure it's super crystal clear what each of these different things are going

128
00:07:29,350 --> 00:07:30,640
to be doing for us.

129
00:07:31,940 --> 00:07:33,310
Well, back over at my terminal.

130
00:07:33,320 --> 00:07:37,190
It looks like the application has been set up correctly and successfully.

131
00:07:37,190 --> 00:07:41,570
So let's take a pause right here and then we'll start doing some development on our app in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,450 --> 00:00:07,000
My project has been generated and I started up my code editor inside of our new Messages project directory.

2
00:00:07,000 --> 00:00:12,340
So as had mentioned before, whenever you generate a project with an, you get many more files and folders

3
00:00:12,340 --> 00:00:13,810
created for you automatically.

4
00:00:13,810 --> 00:00:15,520
So a lot more stuff going on now.

5
00:00:16,100 --> 00:00:19,520
I want to show you a couple of things before we start writing any code on our project.

6
00:00:19,520 --> 00:00:22,940
So first off, is the package.json file inside of here.

7
00:00:22,970 --> 00:00:28,280
Just like any Package.json file, there is a script section and there are a variety of scripts that

8
00:00:28,280 --> 00:00:30,530
you can use to work around with your project.

9
00:00:30,740 --> 00:00:36,290
Right now, the most critical script for you to be aware of is the Start Colon Dev script.

10
00:00:36,320 --> 00:00:39,110
This will start up your project in a development mode.

11
00:00:39,170 --> 00:00:43,970
You will automatically get your server restarted any time you make a change to your project.

12
00:00:44,060 --> 00:00:48,290
Let's go over to our terminal right now and start up our project in this development mode.

13
00:00:48,590 --> 00:00:55,460
So over at my terminal, inside of the messages project directory, I'm going to execute NPM run start

14
00:00:55,460 --> 00:00:56,990
colon dev.

15
00:00:59,630 --> 00:01:00,470
And there we go.

16
00:01:00,470 --> 00:01:02,390
So the application is now up and running.

17
00:01:03,570 --> 00:01:06,420
All right, Back over inside my code editor.

18
00:01:06,450 --> 00:01:11,970
There's one other file I want you to be aware of, and that is the eslint rxjs file.

19
00:01:12,120 --> 00:01:15,540
So by default, nest makes use of eslint.

20
00:01:15,570 --> 00:01:21,000
This is a command line tool that will automatically take a look at your code and highlight possible

21
00:01:21,000 --> 00:01:26,130
errors or issues specifically around possibly formatting stuff like that that you have inside of your

22
00:01:26,130 --> 00:01:27,120
different files.

23
00:01:27,150 --> 00:01:30,560
Now, me personally, I cannot stand eslint.

24
00:01:30,570 --> 00:01:35,250
I do not care for it at all, especially when I'm making use of TypeScript because TypeScript already

25
00:01:35,250 --> 00:01:37,240
catches many different errors for me.

26
00:01:37,260 --> 00:01:40,590
So Vue eslint as a little bit of an annoyance.

27
00:01:40,740 --> 00:01:45,570
So for me personally, I'm going to disable Eslint to do so.

28
00:01:45,960 --> 00:01:53,370
I'm going to comment out the entire contents of the object inside of this Eslint Rxjs file.

29
00:01:54,660 --> 00:01:57,150
So there's the entire contents of the object.

30
00:01:57,570 --> 00:01:59,970
I'm going to comment all that stuff out and save the file.

31
00:02:00,000 --> 00:02:02,940
Now, you don't have to do this If you want to keep Eslint around.

32
00:02:02,970 --> 00:02:03,600
Totally fine.

33
00:02:03,600 --> 00:02:07,110
But me personally, I'm just going to disable eslint right away from the get go.

34
00:02:08,210 --> 00:02:08,510
All right.

35
00:02:08,510 --> 00:02:12,380
So now that we've done that little bit of setup, let's dive into that file.

36
00:02:13,160 --> 00:02:14,510
To me src folder.

37
00:02:15,830 --> 00:02:19,510
Now inside of here, you will notice a couple of files that already look a little bit familiar.

38
00:02:19,520 --> 00:02:23,750
We've got the Main.ts file, which is identical to the one you and I put together.

39
00:02:23,840 --> 00:02:29,990
We've got an appmodule file which looks very similar to the one we put together and an app controller,

40
00:02:29,990 --> 00:02:33,170
which also once again looks very similar to the one we put together.

41
00:02:33,920 --> 00:02:40,040
So on our project, remember we had said that we need to make a pipe, some controller, some service

42
00:02:40,040 --> 00:02:42,590
repository, and possibly a module as well.

43
00:02:43,250 --> 00:02:49,220
We could absolutely reuse the existing app module and the existing app controller and app service,

44
00:02:49,250 --> 00:02:51,530
all that stuff that was created for us automatically.

45
00:02:51,530 --> 00:02:54,850
But I want to show you how to wire up once again some of the stuff from scratch.

46
00:02:54,860 --> 00:02:59,480
So I do not want to use any of the existing app related stuff inside this project.

47
00:02:59,480 --> 00:03:04,310
So I'm going to find all four of these different app files.

48
00:03:04,310 --> 00:03:09,280
So app dot controller, dot spec app controller, app module and app dot service.

49
00:03:09,290 --> 00:03:11,090
I'm going to delete all four of them.

50
00:03:13,090 --> 00:03:17,860
And then finally inside of Maintz, we will immediately notice there's an import error.

51
00:03:17,860 --> 00:03:21,520
And you'll also probably notice back at your terminal that you're getting an error from over here as

52
00:03:21,520 --> 00:03:22,060
well.

53
00:03:22,150 --> 00:03:26,980
So we're going to create a new module and we're going to wire that module up to our main.ts file.

54
00:03:27,190 --> 00:03:30,550
Our module is going to be called the.

55
00:03:31,370 --> 00:03:37,460
Messages module because it's going to have a bunch of stuff inside of it related to the messages feature.

56
00:03:37,760 --> 00:03:43,070
To create this module, we can either create a new file manually, which we did a little bit ago, or

57
00:03:43,070 --> 00:03:45,500
alternatively we can make use of the nest cli.

58
00:03:45,860 --> 00:03:51,860
The nest cli can create different kinds of class files for us and put a little bit of starter code inside

59
00:03:51,860 --> 00:03:52,270
them.

60
00:03:52,280 --> 00:03:55,880
This is a great feature for putting together a project very quickly.

61
00:03:55,880 --> 00:03:57,620
So let's go over to our terminal.

62
00:03:57,620 --> 00:04:01,070
We're going to use the CLI to generate this module for us.

63
00:04:02,170 --> 00:04:02,500
All right.

64
00:04:02,500 --> 00:04:04,150
So back at my terminal.

65
00:04:06,260 --> 00:04:12,110
I'm going to open up a second terminal window still inside of my messages project directory, and then

66
00:04:12,110 --> 00:04:18,140
I'm going to run the command nest, generate module, and then the name of the module that I want to

67
00:04:18,140 --> 00:04:21,040
create without using the word module in it.

68
00:04:21,050 --> 00:04:25,250
So in this case, we want to make a module called the Messages module.

69
00:04:25,670 --> 00:04:30,020
So we're going to call it messages, but not put in that word module.

70
00:04:31,700 --> 00:04:35,090
So I'm going to run this generate module messages like so.

71
00:04:36,910 --> 00:04:40,930
And when I run that command, we'll very quickly see that a file has been created for us along with

72
00:04:40,930 --> 00:04:42,520
a new directory as well.

73
00:04:42,550 --> 00:04:48,130
So if we go back over to our editor, we'll see now the messages directory and inside there is the messages

74
00:04:48,130 --> 00:04:48,880
module.

75
00:04:48,970 --> 00:04:53,320
Notice how that generate command automatically put in the name module for us.

76
00:04:53,350 --> 00:04:59,080
So if you ran a command of something like Nest, generate module messages module.

77
00:04:59,080 --> 00:05:03,520
So if you put in that extra word in there, you would end up with a file and a class name.

78
00:05:03,520 --> 00:05:07,900
That was something like messages module module, which is probably not what you want.

79
00:05:09,720 --> 00:05:14,760
So even though creating a module or generating this file only saved us a couple of lines of code, as

80
00:05:14,760 --> 00:05:19,530
soon as we start creating controllers services and some other things with that generate command, we're

81
00:05:19,530 --> 00:05:22,380
going to get a lot of extra code added in for us automatically.

82
00:05:22,380 --> 00:05:25,800
So at the end, using that generate command does save us a lot of time.

83
00:05:26,440 --> 00:05:31,660
Now that we've got this new module, let's go over to the Main.ts file and hook everything up.

84
00:05:33,180 --> 00:05:39,210
So back inside of Main.ts, I'm going to remove that existing app module import statement and replace

85
00:05:39,210 --> 00:05:46,830
it with messages module from messages slash messages dot module.

86
00:05:48,780 --> 00:05:52,950
We'll then pass off messages module to the nest factory dot create function.

87
00:05:54,160 --> 00:05:55,540
Okay, so that should be it.

88
00:05:55,750 --> 00:05:58,360
We don't currently have any controllers set up inside of our application.

89
00:05:58,360 --> 00:06:01,960
So even though we should be able to run this app successfully, it's not really going to do a whole

90
00:06:01,960 --> 00:06:03,060
lot for us right now.

91
00:06:03,070 --> 00:06:04,620
So let's take a pause right here.

92
00:06:04,630 --> 00:06:08,890
We're going to come back in just a moment and start creating this messages, controller messages, service

93
00:06:08,890 --> 00:06:09,730
and so on.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:04,200
Let's continue on by creating our messages controller.

2
00:00:04,200 --> 00:00:08,700
Once we create this controller, we're then going to wire it up to the messages module, which we made

3
00:00:08,700 --> 00:00:09,660
just a moment ago.

4
00:00:09,660 --> 00:00:14,790
Remember that to create a Nest application and have it serve up some useful content, the absolute minimum

5
00:00:14,790 --> 00:00:17,160
is to have a controller and a module.

6
00:00:17,550 --> 00:00:22,050
Now to create this controller, we're going to once again use the nest to create a couple of different

7
00:00:22,050 --> 00:00:23,550
files for us automatically.

8
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:27,030
The command we're going to use this time around is going to be a little bit more complicated.

9
00:00:27,030 --> 00:00:32,150
So let's first run the command to generate these files and then discuss the exact command itself.

10
00:00:32,160 --> 00:00:36,180
So I'm going to execute once again inside my project directory nest.

11
00:00:36,180 --> 00:00:38,370
Generate controller.

12
00:00:39,620 --> 00:00:41,330
And then this part is gonna be a little bit strange.

13
00:00:41,330 --> 00:00:47,690
It's going to be messages slash messages and then dash dash flat.

14
00:00:48,600 --> 00:00:48,840
Okay.

15
00:00:48,840 --> 00:00:50,100
So let's go ahead and run that.

16
00:00:50,310 --> 00:00:50,850
We'll see.

17
00:00:50,850 --> 00:00:54,630
Once again, some files created and one file was updated as well.

18
00:00:54,810 --> 00:00:58,230
Let's go back over to our code editor and see exactly what happened.

19
00:00:59,990 --> 00:01:04,580
So now inside of our messages module directory, we also have a controller.

20
00:01:04,610 --> 00:01:08,690
This controller looks very similar to the one we put together on our last very small project.

21
00:01:08,720 --> 00:01:13,700
You will recall that whenever we put together a controller, we must add it to a module.

22
00:01:13,790 --> 00:01:19,190
If we go back over to our module file, you'll notice that this file was automatically updated for us.

23
00:01:19,900 --> 00:01:24,640
An import statement for the controller we just generated was added in automatically and the controller

24
00:01:24,640 --> 00:01:28,820
itself was added into the modules list of controllers as well.

25
00:01:28,840 --> 00:01:31,990
So this is where using that generate feature is really nice.

26
00:01:32,020 --> 00:01:36,460
It's going to not only create files for you, but it will also connect some things together for you

27
00:01:36,460 --> 00:01:38,470
as well, which is just fantastic.

28
00:01:39,370 --> 00:01:41,890
Now let's go over that command we ran just a moment ago.

29
00:01:42,250 --> 00:01:44,260
So a couple of different parts to it.

30
00:01:44,590 --> 00:01:49,600
So we executed Nest, generate controller messages, slash messages, Dash dash flat.

31
00:01:49,630 --> 00:01:51,220
The first part nest generate.

32
00:01:51,220 --> 00:01:52,360
Yeah, you can guess what that does.

33
00:01:52,360 --> 00:01:53,890
We're trying to generate something.

34
00:01:54,640 --> 00:01:59,440
We then put in the word controller, which tells Nest, as you guessed, that we want to create a class

35
00:01:59,440 --> 00:02:01,510
that is going to serve as a controller.

36
00:02:02,740 --> 00:02:05,820
Now, everything else inside of here is where things start to get a little bit tricky.

37
00:02:05,830 --> 00:02:09,100
So we put in messages, slash messages.

38
00:02:09,220 --> 00:02:16,870
This means that we want to create this new controller inside of the messages folder of our SRC directory.

39
00:02:17,050 --> 00:02:21,670
So that meant go into this messages folder and then create something inside there.

40
00:02:22,660 --> 00:02:26,000
We then put in a slash and then the word messages again.

41
00:02:26,020 --> 00:02:31,180
That means the class that we are creating or the controller we are creating, we want to call that the

42
00:02:31,180 --> 00:02:32,700
messages controller.

43
00:02:32,710 --> 00:02:35,050
So again, the first part there is the folder.

44
00:02:35,050 --> 00:02:36,610
We want to place this thing inside.

45
00:02:36,610 --> 00:02:39,490
The second one was the name of the class itself.

46
00:02:40,150 --> 00:02:43,530
Finally, we also added in the dash dash flat option.

47
00:02:43,540 --> 00:02:49,240
If you don't add in the dash dash flat, then the folder structure is going to be changed just a little

48
00:02:49,240 --> 00:02:49,720
bit.

49
00:02:49,750 --> 00:02:55,210
If you don't add in dash dash flat, then Nest will automatically create a new folder inside the messages

50
00:02:55,210 --> 00:03:01,660
directory for you called Controllers and it will add those newly generated files to that folder.

51
00:03:01,660 --> 00:03:03,430
So it would end up looking.

52
00:03:03,970 --> 00:03:05,020
I'm not going to do any changes here.

53
00:03:05,020 --> 00:03:05,380
There we go.

54
00:03:05,410 --> 00:03:10,150
It would end up looking exactly like this, so we'll automatically make that new folder for you and

55
00:03:10,150 --> 00:03:12,550
then place the newly generated files inside there.

56
00:03:12,790 --> 00:03:16,600
Whether or not you add in this extra directory is really totally up to you.

57
00:03:16,630 --> 00:03:19,630
In our case, we're not going to have many files inside the messages folder.

58
00:03:19,630 --> 00:03:24,130
So I think that further subdivision of the files inside of here is not super necessary.

59
00:03:24,220 --> 00:03:28,660
So for me personally in this case, I don't think we needed the extra controllers directory.

60
00:03:28,660 --> 00:03:33,550
But if you want to better organize your files, well naturally you can leave off that dash dash flat

61
00:03:33,580 --> 00:03:38,050
option and have the nest cli generate the controllers directory for you.

62
00:03:39,890 --> 00:03:43,610
Okay, so I'm going to undo that quick change I just made a moment ago.

63
00:03:44,890 --> 00:03:46,720
And hopefully everything is going to.

64
00:03:46,750 --> 00:03:48,250
Yeah, it's all working.

65
00:03:48,370 --> 00:03:50,350
I just need to do a quick reload here.

66
00:03:51,190 --> 00:03:51,820
There we go.

67
00:03:53,510 --> 00:03:58,230
Okay, so we've now got our controller and we've got the module that is wired up to as well.

68
00:03:58,250 --> 00:04:02,750
So now in theory, if we start adding in some route handlers to our controller, we should be able to

69
00:04:02,750 --> 00:04:05,460
access these different routes from our browser.

70
00:04:05,480 --> 00:04:09,650
So let's take one more pause right here and then start adding in some different routes to the messages

71
00:04:09,650 --> 00:04:10,400
controller.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,190 --> 00:00:04,540
Let's start setting up a couple of different routes inside of our messages controller.

2
00:00:04,550 --> 00:00:06,830
So remember to set up a route handler in Nest.

3
00:00:06,860 --> 00:00:11,690
We're going to define a method inside of a controller and then use a decorator on that method.

4
00:00:11,720 --> 00:00:16,070
When we start to set up these different methods that are going to serve as route handlers, we've got

5
00:00:16,070 --> 00:00:18,610
a couple of different options for setting up our routing.

6
00:00:18,620 --> 00:00:21,950
Let me just show you a diagram and help you understand what our options are here.

7
00:00:22,580 --> 00:00:22,880
Okay.

8
00:00:22,880 --> 00:00:25,850
So first, remember, there are three kinds of requests we want to handle.

9
00:00:25,850 --> 00:00:28,910
We want to handle a get request to slash messages.

10
00:00:28,940 --> 00:00:35,570
We want to handle a post to slash messages and a get request to messages slash and then a wild card.

11
00:00:35,570 --> 00:00:37,250
So in essentially.

12
00:00:38,320 --> 00:00:42,190
So we have two different options for setting up these three different routes inside of our controller.

13
00:00:42,430 --> 00:00:44,070
An option number one.

14
00:00:44,080 --> 00:00:45,790
Here's our controller right here.

15
00:00:45,910 --> 00:00:48,640
We can use the controller decorator right above it.

16
00:00:48,670 --> 00:00:52,520
Then inside of our controller, we will add in three different methods.

17
00:00:52,540 --> 00:00:55,450
Each one will make use of a route decorator.

18
00:00:55,540 --> 00:01:01,150
So the first one can use a route decorator of at Git, and then we can put in a route to that thing

19
00:01:01,180 --> 00:01:02,650
of slash messages.

20
00:01:02,980 --> 00:01:06,180
We'll tie that to maybe a method called list messages.

21
00:01:06,190 --> 00:01:11,860
So then anytime we make a get request to slash messages, that request will be automatically routed

22
00:01:11,860 --> 00:01:12,880
to that method.

23
00:01:14,020 --> 00:01:18,310
We can then use another decorator that we've not yet looked at, but behaves just about identically

24
00:01:18,310 --> 00:01:20,170
to the get decorator that we already used.

25
00:01:20,200 --> 00:01:21,800
It's called the post decorator.

26
00:01:21,820 --> 00:01:25,350
We can use the post decorator to set up a post request handler.

27
00:01:25,360 --> 00:01:31,150
