So if we use the post request decorator with a route of slash messages that will handle an incoming

28
00:01:31,150 --> 00:01:34,180
request of post slash messages.

29
00:01:34,330 --> 00:01:37,150
And then finally, very similar for the get decorator.

30
00:01:37,150 --> 00:01:40,150
We can do a get messages slash colon ID.

31
00:01:40,340 --> 00:01:42,430
Now we'll handle this last request down here.

32
00:01:44,260 --> 00:01:45,730
Now, the other option we have.

33
00:01:46,760 --> 00:01:52,370
You might notice that all three of these different requests start with slash messages so we can extract

34
00:01:52,370 --> 00:01:56,360
the slash messages part of the route to the controller decorator at the top.

35
00:01:56,390 --> 00:02:00,770
That will allow us to write out some much more simple routing decorators inside of our controller.

36
00:02:00,800 --> 00:02:05,420
Remember when deciding the different routes that exist inside of our application nest is going to take

37
00:02:05,420 --> 00:02:07,520
that controller decorator right there.

38
00:02:07,520 --> 00:02:11,510
It's going to take whatever string we pass to it, and it's going to join it together with whatever

39
00:02:11,510 --> 00:02:14,960
string we pass to each individual decorator inside the class.

40
00:02:15,260 --> 00:02:18,590
So this is completely equivalent to what we had in option number one.

41
00:02:18,590 --> 00:02:23,120
But now we do not have to write out slash messages for each of these individual decorators.

42
00:02:24,030 --> 00:02:30,420
So for the first request right here, a git to slash messages that would still be routed to list messages

43
00:02:30,420 --> 00:02:31,170
right there.

44
00:02:31,200 --> 00:02:36,870
The post would still be routed to that method and the git with an ID would still be routed to that method

45
00:02:36,870 --> 00:02:37,590
right there.

46
00:02:37,830 --> 00:02:42,510
We're going to go with option number two because again, it just saves us from having to write out slash

47
00:02:42,510 --> 00:02:45,270
messages all over the place inside of our controller.

48
00:02:45,480 --> 00:02:50,040
So let's go back over to our controller file, start to set up these different methods and use the get

49
00:02:50,040 --> 00:02:51,930
and post decorators on each of them.

50
00:02:53,170 --> 00:02:58,470
Okay, So back over here, I'm going to first begin by importing the git and the post decorators.

51
00:02:58,480 --> 00:03:00,820
Again, we've already made use of that git decorator.

52
00:03:00,820 --> 00:03:02,980
We are now making use of the post one as well.

53
00:03:02,980 --> 00:03:07,090
This behaves identically to the git one, but it allows us to set up a request handler that's going

54
00:03:07,090 --> 00:03:09,280
to handle a incoming post request.

55
00:03:10,160 --> 00:03:13,460
So then inside my controller, I'm going to add in three different methods.

56
00:03:13,490 --> 00:03:16,640
The first one will be called list messages.

57
00:03:17,890 --> 00:03:19,600
Then create message.

58
00:03:20,420 --> 00:03:22,820
And finally get message.

59
00:03:23,570 --> 00:03:25,910
I'll then add in my decorators to each one.

60
00:03:26,000 --> 00:03:32,240
So in the first method, I'll add on the get on the second one, we'll add in a post.

61
00:03:32,660 --> 00:03:38,120
And then finally on the last one, a get width slash colon ID.

62
00:03:39,320 --> 00:03:40,310
And that's it.

63
00:03:41,590 --> 00:03:46,420
You'll notice that when we generated this controller, it automatically got that message string added

64
00:03:46,420 --> 00:03:47,170
in up here.

65
00:03:47,170 --> 00:03:49,090
So we didn't have to add that in ourselves.

66
00:03:49,930 --> 00:03:50,230
All right.

67
00:03:50,230 --> 00:03:55,630
So now, in theory, if we run our application, we would have these three different requests handled

68
00:03:55,630 --> 00:03:58,600
by these three different methods that we added into our controller.

69
00:03:58,990 --> 00:04:02,950
So let's take a pause right here and then start to test these different route handlers out in just a

70
00:04:02,950 --> 00:04:03,610
moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,180 --> 00:00:05,140
We've now got a couple of route handlers put together and would really like to test these things out

2
00:00:05,140 --> 00:00:07,450
just to make sure that all of our routing is set up correctly.

3
00:00:07,480 --> 00:00:12,850
We can very easily test out the Git request handlers from our browser by just typing into the address

4
00:00:12,850 --> 00:00:19,210
bar something like localhost colon 3000 slash messages, but testing out a post request handler from

5
00:00:19,210 --> 00:00:21,010
our browser is a little bit more challenging.

6
00:00:21,010 --> 00:00:25,240
It require us to write out some actual JavaScript code inside of our browser console.

7
00:00:25,240 --> 00:00:27,670
I don't really want to do that throughout this entire course.

8
00:00:27,670 --> 00:00:33,490
So instead we are going to use an API client to test out all of our applications manually throughout

9
00:00:33,490 --> 00:00:34,300
this course.

10
00:00:34,820 --> 00:00:38,960
So I'm going to recommend one of two clients for you to use.

11
00:00:39,050 --> 00:00:42,830
If you already have an API client installed on your machine that you are familiar with.

12
00:00:42,860 --> 00:00:43,640
Fantastic.

13
00:00:43,640 --> 00:00:46,550
Pause this video right now and continue on to the next video.

14
00:00:46,580 --> 00:00:51,380
Otherwise, if you do not have an API client that you already use and are familiar with, I got to ask

15
00:00:51,380 --> 00:00:54,680
you to stick around and just go through a couple of installation steps with me.

16
00:00:54,980 --> 00:01:01,790
So I'm going to very quickly show you how to install and run Postman or the VS Code rest client extension.

17
00:01:01,820 --> 00:01:05,630
These are two different clients that you can use throughout this entire course.

18
00:01:05,840 --> 00:01:10,400
I'm going to tell you right away that I'm going to use vs code rest client throughout the entire course

19
00:01:10,400 --> 00:01:11,090
myself.

20
00:01:11,100 --> 00:01:16,070
I highly recommend you use this tool as well, but you can only use it if you are making use of VS code

21
00:01:16,070 --> 00:01:17,330
as your code editor.

22
00:01:17,330 --> 00:01:21,500
So if you're using any other code editor, you will have to use postman instead.

23
00:01:22,790 --> 00:01:27,260
I'm going to first walk you through the installation of Postman very quickly, the setup of it and how

24
00:01:27,260 --> 00:01:29,170
we actually use it to make requests to our server.

25
00:01:29,180 --> 00:01:34,610
After that, I'll very quickly show you how to set up the rest client inside the next video as well.

26
00:01:35,000 --> 00:01:38,960
Okay, so let's go through the Postman installation very quickly to get started.

27
00:01:38,960 --> 00:01:44,780
We're going to navigate inside of a new browser tab to Postman Comm, where you will find a page like

28
00:01:44,780 --> 00:01:45,470
this.

29
00:01:45,470 --> 00:01:50,660
Once you come to this page, you are unfortunately going to have to create a new account with Postman.

30
00:01:50,660 --> 00:01:54,170
So you'll want to find a sign up or sign in button somewhere around here.

31
00:01:54,380 --> 00:01:58,970
Once you are signed in, you will land on a dashboard that looks something like this.

32
00:01:58,970 --> 00:02:04,940
Now hidden on this page, hopefully down on the bottom left, but they might move this button around

33
00:02:04,940 --> 00:02:05,540
somewhere else.

34
00:02:05,540 --> 00:02:07,190
So you might have to do a little bit of digging.

35
00:02:07,190 --> 00:02:10,310
You want to find something called download Desktop app.

36
00:02:10,310 --> 00:02:15,350
So for me right now, as I'm recording this video, that link is at the very bottom left of the main

37
00:02:15,350 --> 00:02:16,280
dashboard page.

38
00:02:16,280 --> 00:02:20,960
Once you sign in, go ahead and download that desktop app and then run the installer.

39
00:02:21,530 --> 00:02:26,360
Once you've got postman installed on your local machine, launch it and you will come to a screen that

40
00:02:26,360 --> 00:02:27,710
looks somewhat like this.

41
00:02:27,740 --> 00:02:33,440
You might first be asked to log in to the desktop app, but if you are prompted to log in, there should

42
00:02:33,440 --> 00:02:34,370
be a little button.

43
00:02:34,370 --> 00:02:38,840
Once again on the bottom left hand side that says I will sign in later so you don't have to log in to

44
00:02:38,840 --> 00:02:42,590
the desktop app unless again, they change this at some future point in time.

45
00:02:43,260 --> 00:02:46,860
Once you've got this page open, I'm going to collapse the sidebar.

46
00:02:47,900 --> 00:02:52,640
I'm going to delete this welcome page right here and then click the little plus button on the top left

47
00:02:52,640 --> 00:02:53,420
hand side.

48
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:58,520
We can now use this interface to make requests to a running nestjs server.

49
00:02:58,530 --> 00:03:07,860
So I'm going to make a get request to http colon slash slash localhost 3000 slash messages.

50
00:03:08,640 --> 00:03:10,290
I'll then send off the request.

51
00:03:11,550 --> 00:03:13,500
I then will see the response at the very bottom.

52
00:03:13,520 --> 00:03:17,160
Now, in this case, we are not sending anything back from our request handler so you're not going to

53
00:03:17,160 --> 00:03:22,170
see any data coming back, but hopefully you will see on the right hand side something that says status

54
00:03:22,170 --> 00:03:22,800
200.

55
00:03:22,800 --> 00:03:23,490
Okay.

56
00:03:23,520 --> 00:03:27,240
That means that Nest did receive our request and it responded to it.

57
00:03:27,840 --> 00:03:28,560
So that's it.

58
00:03:28,560 --> 00:03:29,400
That's what we make use of.

59
00:03:29,400 --> 00:03:29,910
Postman.

60
00:03:29,910 --> 00:03:34,410
But before we move on, one other very quick thing I want to show you in just a little bit, we're going

61
00:03:34,410 --> 00:03:36,560
to start to send post requests to our server as well.

62
00:03:36,570 --> 00:03:40,590
So I just want to show you how to make a post request because it's a little bit tricky in this interface.

63
00:03:40,590 --> 00:03:47,250
To make a post request, you will change the get to post, you'll then go to body.

64
00:03:48,550 --> 00:03:49,810
On the body tab.

65
00:03:49,840 --> 00:03:52,600
Change it to raw.

66
00:03:53,700 --> 00:03:56,340
Once we change it to raw, we'll then see a little dropdown up here.

67
00:03:56,340 --> 00:04:02,760
Over here, you'll change that to Json and then you're going to write in your raw Json inside of this

68
00:04:02,760 --> 00:04:04,020
little code editor right here.

69
00:04:04,050 --> 00:04:06,900
Now keep in mind this is not JavaScript, this is Json.

70
00:04:06,900 --> 00:04:13,710
So if we want to send a post request that has the same format that we are expecting to send to our post

71
00:04:13,710 --> 00:04:18,390
request handler, where it's going to be some kind of object with a key of content and a value of Hi

72
00:04:18,420 --> 00:04:21,420
there, you would have to write out inside this little code editor.

73
00:04:21,450 --> 00:04:23,610
Double quotes, Content.

74
00:04:23,640 --> 00:04:24,750
Double quotes.

75
00:04:24,750 --> 00:04:25,370
Hi there.

76
00:04:25,380 --> 00:04:26,100
Like so.

77
00:04:26,940 --> 00:04:29,340
You then click send, and that's pretty much it.

78
00:04:30,560 --> 00:04:31,880
Okay, so that's postman.

79
00:04:32,540 --> 00:04:34,160
Let's take a quick pause right here.

80
00:04:34,160 --> 00:04:37,700
When we come back, the next video I'm going to show you the setup and installation of the rest client

81
00:04:37,700 --> 00:04:38,180
extension.

82
00:04:38,180 --> 00:04:42,410
And once again, that's the tool I recommend you use, but you can only use it if you're making use

83
00:04:42,410 --> 00:04:43,370
of VS code.

84
00:04:43,520 --> 00:04:46,970
So let's take a pause right now and go through that installation in just a moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,760 --> 00:00:05,030
We're still doing a little bit of client setup inside this video.

2
00:00:05,030 --> 00:00:09,230
So once again, if you've already gotten a client on your machine that you are happy with, pause this

3
00:00:09,230 --> 00:00:11,320
video right now and skip over to the next one.

4
00:00:11,330 --> 00:00:15,920
Otherwise, stick around and we're going to go through the VS code rest client extension setup.

5
00:00:15,920 --> 00:00:17,210
So let's get to it.

6
00:00:17,450 --> 00:00:19,370
I'm going to go back over to my code editor.

7
00:00:19,370 --> 00:00:24,230
I'm going to go up to the menu bar and find view and then extensions.

8
00:00:25,100 --> 00:00:26,790
Once here, I'm going to search for rest.

9
00:00:26,870 --> 00:00:27,530
Client.

10
00:00:27,560 --> 00:00:29,690
It should be the very first result.

11
00:00:29,810 --> 00:00:32,750
Once you find this extension, go ahead and install it.

12
00:00:33,500 --> 00:00:33,740
All right.

13
00:00:33,740 --> 00:00:37,220
So now that you've got the extension installed, let me show you very quickly how you can actually make

14
00:00:37,220 --> 00:00:39,050
use of it inside of your own projects.

15
00:00:39,380 --> 00:00:45,680
So I'm going to pull open my file explorer once again, and then inside of my root project directory,

16
00:00:45,680 --> 00:00:47,060
I'm going to make a new file.

17
00:00:47,760 --> 00:00:50,670
Called request Http.

18
00:00:51,770 --> 00:00:53,740
So this is how this extension works.

19
00:00:53,750 --> 00:00:58,310
We can create a new file and then inside that file we can write out some configuration for different

20
00:00:58,310 --> 00:01:00,290
requests that we want to make to our API.

21
00:01:00,320 --> 00:01:04,850
As soon as we write out these different requests, a couple of buttons will appear inside of this file

22
00:01:04,850 --> 00:01:08,090
that we can click on to send a request off to a running server.

23
00:01:08,090 --> 00:01:14,330
So I really like this extension because we can package or kind of enter into version control, a little

24
00:01:14,330 --> 00:01:17,870
file that describes all the different kinds of requests that we can make to our application.

25
00:01:17,870 --> 00:01:21,980
So it's kind of like some built in documentation that you can include with your source code.

26
00:01:21,980 --> 00:01:23,390
Absolutely fantastic.

27
00:01:23,390 --> 00:01:25,820
So again, let me just show you how this thing works.

28
00:01:26,000 --> 00:01:30,530
Remember that one of the requests that we should be able to send off to our running server is a get

29
00:01:30,530 --> 00:01:34,250
request to localhost 3000 slash messages.

30
00:01:34,520 --> 00:01:40,790
So back inside this file, I'm going to write out a little comment with pound, pound, pound that says

31
00:01:40,790 --> 00:01:42,980
list all messages.

32
00:01:43,220 --> 00:01:48,920
I'll then write out some configuration immediately after that comment to make a request to list all

33
00:01:48,920 --> 00:01:49,700
messages.

34
00:01:49,700 --> 00:01:58,950
So I'm going to write git with all capitals, http, colon slash, slash localhost, 3000 slash messages

35
00:01:58,950 --> 00:01:59,670
like so.

36
00:02:00,650 --> 00:02:04,580
As soon as I write out that line, a little button is going to appear right above it that I can click

37
00:02:04,580 --> 00:02:04,940
on.

38
00:02:04,970 --> 00:02:11,480
If you click on that button, then the rest client extension is going to make this request to that particular

39
00:02:11,480 --> 00:02:12,200
URL.

40
00:02:12,320 --> 00:02:16,670
So I'm going to click on that button right now and I see the response up here in a new window on the

41
00:02:16,670 --> 00:02:17,630
right hand side.

42
00:02:17,720 --> 00:02:20,750
So this is a response from our running web server.

43
00:02:21,390 --> 00:02:22,800
Again, totally fantastic.

44
00:02:22,800 --> 00:02:27,180
We've got this great documentation, very easy to make follow up requests and edit them over time as

45
00:02:27,180 --> 00:02:27,660
well.

46
00:02:29,510 --> 00:02:35,090
Now, I'm going to very quickly write out some additional requests to make a post request and a request

47
00:02:35,090 --> 00:02:36,880
for a very particular message as well.

48
00:02:36,890 --> 00:02:40,490
Remember, we still don't have any implementation around these handlers for right now.

49
00:02:40,490 --> 00:02:45,050
We're just adding in these requests so we can test out the handlers as soon as we finish them up.

50
00:02:46,200 --> 00:02:50,550
So after our existing request, I'm going to put in another couple lines and then a new comment and

51
00:02:50,550 --> 00:02:53,370
I'll say create a new message.

52
00:02:54,640 --> 00:03:00,600
So this one will be a post to http localhost 3000 messages.

53
00:03:00,610 --> 00:03:06,370
In this case, we do also have to add in a header because we're trying to send along some Json with

54
00:03:06,370 --> 00:03:07,270
this request.

55
00:03:07,270 --> 00:03:15,520
So right after on the line right below post, we're going to put in content type colon application slash

56
00:03:15,520 --> 00:03:16,270
Json.

57
00:03:16,970 --> 00:03:21,440
Please double check your spelling and capitalization in all formatting on this line.

58
00:03:21,440 --> 00:03:25,340
If you have anything different then the post request is not going to work as expected.

59
00:03:26,000 --> 00:03:30,510
Then to add on a body to the post request, I'm going to put in two new lines.

60
00:03:30,530 --> 00:03:36,320
So two new line characters, effectively one empty line, and then I'll put in the body of the request

61
00:03:36,320 --> 00:03:37,610
right underneath it.

62
00:03:37,790 --> 00:03:41,570
Once again, please make sure you've got an empty line right there.

63
00:03:42,950 --> 00:03:48,740
So then in our case, we're going to expect to send off a request that has a body with a content property

64
00:03:48,740 --> 00:03:50,360
and then some string value.

65
00:03:50,840 --> 00:03:55,670
So we will put in with double quotes because again, this is not JavaScript, this is Json we are writing

66
00:03:55,670 --> 00:03:56,390
right now.

67
00:03:56,570 --> 00:04:01,820
Content is hi there or some other message, whatever message you want to put inside there.

68
00:04:03,100 --> 00:04:04,840
So now we can test out this request.

69
00:04:05,320 --> 00:04:07,120
Once again, looks like we got a response.

70
00:04:07,120 --> 00:04:10,000
Didn't actually do anything, but we at least got a response.

71
00:04:11,060 --> 00:04:13,640
And then finally, one more request down here.

72
00:04:13,640 --> 00:04:15,440
So I'm going to put in another comment.

73
00:04:16,350 --> 00:04:19,530
Of get a particular message.

74
00:04:20,760 --> 00:04:27,930
This one will be a git to localhost 3000 messages and then some actual ID.

75
00:04:28,110 --> 00:04:33,090
Now we have not generated any messages yet, so we don't have any actual real IDs to put in here.

76
00:04:33,090 --> 00:04:37,800
So for right now, I'm going to just put in a fake ID and we'll come back later on and fix that thing

77
00:04:37,800 --> 00:04:41,490
up once we have some real IDs for real messages inside of our system.

78
00:04:42,690 --> 00:04:43,850
Ah, I'm going to send that one off.

79
00:04:43,850 --> 00:04:46,430
And once again, yep, that looks pretty good as well.

80
00:04:47,150 --> 00:04:48,200
Okay, so that's it.

81
00:04:48,200 --> 00:04:52,820
So now as we start to test out our application manually, we're going to just open up this request dot

82
00:04:52,830 --> 00:04:54,190
http file.

83
00:04:54,200 --> 00:04:58,940
We'll find each of these buttons, click each one, and it's just once again a really easy and straightforward

84
00:04:58,940 --> 00:05:02,570
way of testing out the different request handlers inside of our application.

85
00:05:03,810 --> 00:05:04,050
All right.

86
00:05:04,050 --> 00:05:05,280
So let's take a pause right here.

87
00:05:05,280 --> 00:05:10,620
And as you guess, start working on some implementation around our controller in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


==================================================================================


# section 4: Validating Request Data with Pipes

1
00:00:01,580 --> 00:00:03,170
In two of our different request handlers.

2
00:00:03,170 --> 00:00:07,410
We need to add in some code that will extract some information from the incoming request.

3
00:00:07,430 --> 00:00:12,170
So for the post request handler, we need to extract the body of the incoming request.

4
00:00:12,170 --> 00:00:18,620
And on the get one down here, we need to extract that little wild card part out of the root of the

5
00:00:18,620 --> 00:00:19,730
incoming request.

6
00:00:20,030 --> 00:00:25,220
So when we make use of Nest to extract information out of an incoming request, we're going to use a

7
00:00:25,220 --> 00:00:26,840
couple of different decorators.

8
00:00:26,870 --> 00:00:30,410
Let me show you a couple of diagrams and help you understand how these things work.

9
00:00:30,890 --> 00:00:31,190
All right.

10
00:00:31,190 --> 00:00:35,930
So first, a very fast reminder on how Http requests work in general.

11
00:00:36,050 --> 00:00:40,160
A request is really just a body of text, a couple of different lines of text.

12
00:00:41,090 --> 00:00:43,340
The first line is referred to as the start line.

13
00:00:43,340 --> 00:00:48,020
It has the method of the request and a full route, and it also has the protocol used.

14
00:00:48,230 --> 00:00:54,320
We then have a couple of lines for headers on the request and then if we have a bot or a post or a put

15
00:00:54,320 --> 00:00:58,790
or a patch or a couple of different other kinds of requests, the request might also have a body as

16
00:00:58,790 --> 00:00:59,420
well.

17
00:00:59,600 --> 00:01:03,320
So out of all this stuff, we might want to extract the body.

18
00:01:03,350 --> 00:01:05,690
We might want to extract specific headers.

19
00:01:05,720 --> 00:01:12,080
We might want to extract maybe part of a query string, or we might want to extract some URL parameter.

20
00:01:12,080 --> 00:01:17,930
So a wildcard, something like five right there for each of these different parts of information.

21
00:01:18,050 --> 00:01:21,300
Nest has a different decorator available for us to use.

22
00:01:21,320 --> 00:01:27,860
So if we ever want to extract a parameter or essentially a wildcard value out of the URL of the incoming

23
00:01:27,860 --> 00:01:30,740
request, we can use a decorator called Param.

24
00:01:31,680 --> 00:01:36,540
When we use the param decorator, we're going to pass in a string that describes which part of the or

25
00:01:36,540 --> 00:01:38,590
which wild card we want to access.

26
00:01:38,610 --> 00:01:45,120
So if we have a wild card part of the URL called colon ID, we would use the param decorator with a

27
00:01:45,120 --> 00:01:45,960
string of ID.

28
00:01:46,650 --> 00:01:50,400
If we want to extract part of the query string, we can use the query decorator.

29
00:01:50,490 --> 00:01:54,600
If we want to get access to the headers of the incoming request, we can use the headers decorator and

30
00:01:54,600 --> 00:01:55,980
finally the body decorator.

31
00:01:55,980 --> 00:01:58,620
If we want to get information out of the body of the request.

32
00:01:58,920 --> 00:02:05,610
So all these are different decorators that we can import from the common library up here at the top.

33
00:02:06,060 --> 00:02:12,150
Let's try to use the body decorator right away and the param decorator to extract the body of the request

34
00:02:12,150 --> 00:02:14,010
for the post request right here.

35
00:02:14,100 --> 00:02:18,180
And the ID wild card for the last route handler right there.

36
00:02:19,360 --> 00:02:24,620
So to get started, I'm going to first import the body and params decorators.

37
00:02:24,700 --> 00:02:29,350
Now how we use these decorators is a little bit different than how we've already made use of controller.

38
00:02:29,380 --> 00:02:30,730
The git and the post.

39
00:02:31,570 --> 00:02:36,310
This controller One up here is a class decorator because we are applying it to an entire class.

40
00:02:36,340 --> 00:02:41,260
The get and the post ones are referred to as method decorators because they are being applied to an

41
00:02:41,260 --> 00:02:42,250
entire method.

42
00:02:42,520 --> 00:02:46,970
But body and param are what we refer to as argument decorators.

43
00:02:46,990 --> 00:02:49,340
So we're going to use these in a slightly different fashion.

44
00:02:49,360 --> 00:02:52,030
We're going to put them in the argument list right here.

45
00:02:52,690 --> 00:02:57,490
So for the post request where we want to get the body of the incoming request, we're going to say at

46
00:02:57,490 --> 00:02:58,420
body.

47
00:03:00,040 --> 00:03:03,730
Then body and I'm going to give this thing for right now a type of any.

48
00:03:04,740 --> 00:03:08,640
So now whenever we get an incoming request that's going to go to this route handler.

49
00:03:09,520 --> 00:03:14,650
Nest is going to automatically extract the body of the incoming request and provide it as an argument

50
00:03:14,650 --> 00:03:16,900
to our route handler called body.

51
00:03:16,990 --> 00:03:20,740
So inside of here we could do a console dot log of body.

52
00:03:22,330 --> 00:03:26,740
We're going to use the decorator on the bottom route handler down here in a very similar way.

53
00:03:26,770 --> 00:03:29,680
So I'm going to say at parameter ID.

54
00:03:30,800 --> 00:03:33,230
We're going to receive an argument called ID.

55
00:03:33,800 --> 00:03:35,960
I'm going to annotate its type as string.

56
00:03:36,050 --> 00:03:39,380
And then once again, inside of here I'll do a console log of ID.

57
00:03:42,190 --> 00:03:47,380
Okay, So now I want to test out these two route handlers and I want to make sure that we can console

58
00:03:47,380 --> 00:03:51,970
log the ID of the incoming request in this case, console, log out the entire body.

59
00:03:52,060 --> 00:03:55,570
So let's open up our clients and test these two things out.

60
00:03:56,470 --> 00:03:57,130
All right.

61
00:03:57,160 --> 00:04:02,590
Now remember, I am using the VS code rest client extension.

62
00:04:02,590 --> 00:04:07,960
So whenever I say let's go over to our client, I'm going to be opening up a file like this and using

63
00:04:07,960 --> 00:04:09,540
this thing to send off requests.

64
00:04:09,550 --> 00:04:13,060
If you are using Postman, you're going to use Postman instead.

65
00:04:13,060 --> 00:04:17,140
If you are using some other client, you're going to use whatever other client that is.

66
00:04:17,140 --> 00:04:20,500
So again, when I open this file, you might not be doing the same thing.

67
00:04:20,500 --> 00:04:22,750
You might be opening up Postman or something else.

68
00:04:23,450 --> 00:04:24,980
Okay, so let's give this a shot.

69
00:04:25,070 --> 00:04:31,400
I'm going to first try to make a post request to localhost 3000 slash messages with a body of content.

70
00:04:31,430 --> 00:04:32,300
Hi there.

71
00:04:32,420 --> 00:04:36,590
So if I send off this request, I'm still going to get back an empty response.

72
00:04:36,590 --> 00:04:41,900
But if I go over to my terminal where my server is running, I should see a console log of the body

73
00:04:41,900 --> 00:04:42,800
of our request.

74
00:04:42,840 --> 00:04:44,060
And sure enough, I do right there.

75
00:04:44,090 --> 00:04:44,960
Perfect.

76
00:04:45,970 --> 00:04:52,720
Likewise, if I make a get request to messages slash anything, so anything right there and send that

77
00:04:52,720 --> 00:04:55,570
request off, it's still going to get an empty response.

78
00:04:55,570 --> 00:05:01,180
But if I go back over to my terminal, I can see the parameter or the parameter console logged out as

79
00:05:01,180 --> 00:05:01,720
well.

80
00:05:02,460 --> 00:05:02,910
All right.

81
00:05:02,910 --> 00:05:04,170
So that's pretty much it.

82
00:05:05,120 --> 00:05:05,900
So a nest.

83
00:05:05,930 --> 00:05:10,910
We make use of these different decorators to extract different parts of information out of the incoming

84
00:05:10,910 --> 00:05:11,630
request.

85
00:05:11,660 --> 00:05:16,230
We're going to use this body decorator and the param decorator quite a bit throughout this course.

86
00:05:16,250 --> 00:05:21,260
There are not many scenarios where we need to make use of headers just because the applications that

87
00:05:21,260 --> 00:05:24,090
we're going to build in this course don't really need to access any headers.

88
00:05:24,110 --> 00:05:25,700
Same thing for query as well.

89
00:05:25,730 --> 00:05:30,020
In general, we're not going to use the query decorator too often, but just, you know, it does exist.

90
00:05:30,020 --> 00:05:34,220
You can use the query decorator to get access to the query string out of the incoming request.

91
00:05:35,020 --> 00:05:37,840
Let's take a pause right here and continue on in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:05,080
Our controller is now looking pretty good inside of our post request handler.

2
00:00:05,080 --> 00:00:07,630
We are receiving the body of an incoming request.

3
00:00:07,660 --> 00:00:13,180
Remember that around this post request handler we had said that we wanted to do some validation of incoming

4
00:00:13,180 --> 00:00:18,610
data, so whenever we have an incoming post request where we should have some body that has a content

5
00:00:18,610 --> 00:00:23,650
property that is a string, we had said that we probably need a pipe here to validate that incoming

6
00:00:23,650 --> 00:00:29,110
body and if that data was somehow invalid, for example, if there was no content property or if content

7
00:00:29,110 --> 00:00:35,800
was instead maybe a number or something like that, then we should use a pipe to somehow reject this

8
00:00:35,800 --> 00:00:41,070
incoming request and send it back to whoever made the request before the request gets to our controller.

9
00:00:41,080 --> 00:00:43,570
So that's exactly what we're going to use a pipe for.

10
00:00:43,600 --> 00:00:48,370
We're going to validate this data that is coming into our application before it ever reaches our route

11
00:00:48,370 --> 00:00:49,060
handler.

12
00:00:49,240 --> 00:00:54,670
So that guarantees that by the time we run our create message method right here, we know that our body

13
00:00:54,670 --> 00:00:59,500
will definitely be an object that has a content property that is a string.

14
00:01:00,450 --> 00:01:05,580
Now we can definitely create pipes on our own manually, but usually instead we're going to rely upon

15
00:01:05,580 --> 00:01:09,060
a pipe that is provided for us by Nestjs itself.

16
00:01:09,150 --> 00:01:11,830
So this pipe is called the validation pipe.

17
00:01:11,850 --> 00:01:14,560
So again, this is a pipe that is built into nest.

18
00:01:14,580 --> 00:01:16,200
It handles validation for us.

19
00:01:16,230 --> 00:01:20,130
You're probably going to use this on just about every project you ever work on, and this is what we're

20
00:01:20,130 --> 00:01:24,690
going to use inside this course for doing the vast majority of our request validation.

21
00:01:24,990 --> 00:01:27,720
So for right now, we're just going to wire up this pipe.

22
00:01:27,720 --> 00:01:32,010
We're going to use it inside of our application and I'll show you how we actually make use of it in

23
00:01:32,010 --> 00:01:33,330
just a moment after that.

24
00:01:33,570 --> 00:01:39,450
So to initially set up this pipe, we're going to go back over to our editor and find the Main.ts file.

25
00:01:41,210 --> 00:01:50,990
Inside of here at the very top, we're going to add in another import for validation pipe from at Nestjs

26
00:01:50,990 --> 00:01:52,250
slash common.

27
00:01:54,370 --> 00:02:01,570
Then right after we create our application, we will add in app dot use global pipes.

28
00:02:03,040 --> 00:02:08,590
And to that function we are going to pass in new validation pipe like so.

29
00:02:09,490 --> 00:02:10,810
And that's pretty much it.

30
00:02:11,080 --> 00:02:14,890
So the function call right here of use global pipes is pretty descriptive.

31
00:02:14,920 --> 00:02:20,290
We can apply a pipe to just one single route handler if we want to, but in this case, we want to attempt

32
00:02:20,290 --> 00:02:22,300
to validate all incoming requests.

33
00:02:22,300 --> 00:02:24,700
So we're going to apply this pipe globally.

34
00:02:24,700 --> 00:02:30,370
So the validation pipe will attempt to validate every single incoming request that comes into our application.

35
00:02:30,580 --> 00:02:34,690
Now, this doesn't mean we need to add in validation rules to every single route handler.

36
00:02:34,960 --> 00:02:40,030
The validation pipe is very intelligent, and if we don't add in some validation rules to some particular

37
00:02:40,030 --> 00:02:42,790
handler, the validation pipe won't run on it.

38
00:02:42,910 --> 00:02:45,370
So in other words, this is kind of an opt in thing.

39
00:02:45,370 --> 00:02:49,450
We're going to run this code or use this line of code right here, but we're only going to validate

40
00:02:49,450 --> 00:02:51,550
very certain routes at the end of the day.

41
00:02:52,910 --> 00:02:54,410
Okay, so that's the initial wire up.

42
00:02:54,410 --> 00:02:59,630
So now let's take a pause and take a look at how this pipe actually works and how we apply some validation

43
00:02:59,630 --> 00:03:02,240
and set up validation rules in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,650 --> 00:00:06,660
We've now wired up the validation pipe and now we need to give the validation pipe a very special set

2
00:00:06,660 --> 00:00:10,040
of validation rules and make sure that it uses these validation rules.

3
00:00:10,050 --> 00:00:14,700
Any time a request comes into our create message route handler right here.

4
00:00:15,400 --> 00:00:18,880
So to do so, we're going to go through a little series of steps.

5
00:00:19,000 --> 00:00:24,400
So this is everything we have to do to set up automatic validation for a particular request handler.

6
00:00:24,430 --> 00:00:29,620
We already did step number one in the last video, we told Nest to use global validation by wiring up

7
00:00:29,620 --> 00:00:31,380
the validation pipe itself.

8
00:00:31,390 --> 00:00:34,960
So again, all we have to do, step number one, just one single time.

9
00:00:35,230 --> 00:00:40,120
We're then going to repeat steps two through four every single time that we want to use that validation

10
00:00:40,120 --> 00:00:42,010
pipe on a very particular route.

11
00:00:42,040 --> 00:00:47,320
Handler So you are going to be doing steps two, three and four quite a bit on your own whenever you

12
00:00:47,320 --> 00:00:50,860
start to do validation on the bodies of incoming requests.

13
00:00:51,620 --> 00:00:54,770
So for two, three and four, these steps are going to be just a little bit confusing.

14
00:00:54,770 --> 00:00:58,550
The actual code we have to write is going to be very small and very compact.

15
00:00:58,550 --> 00:01:02,300
But how this code is used by nest is a little bit of magic.

16
00:01:02,300 --> 00:01:05,420
So for right now, we're just going to write out all the code that is required.

17
00:01:05,420 --> 00:01:09,800
We'll then come back, take a look at a couple diagrams and understand what these steps are really doing

18
00:01:09,800 --> 00:01:10,850
behind the scenes.

19
00:01:11,030 --> 00:01:11,300
Okay.

20
00:01:11,300 --> 00:01:12,290
So let's get to it.

21
00:01:12,380 --> 00:01:17,390
Step number two, we're going to create a brand new file and create a new class inside there.

22
00:01:17,420 --> 00:01:22,430
This class is going to describe all the different properties that we expect this incoming request to

23
00:01:22,430 --> 00:01:23,060
have.

24
00:01:23,060 --> 00:01:26,480
And we're also going to apply some validation rules to that class as well.

25
00:01:27,780 --> 00:01:32,430
The class that we're going to create is referred to as a data transfer object.

26
00:01:32,460 --> 00:01:37,710
That's what we refer to this class as rather than saying data transfer object because it's a really

27
00:01:37,710 --> 00:01:42,250
long term, we very frequently abbreviate this simply as DTO.

28
00:01:42,270 --> 00:01:47,400
So whenever you see the letters DTO, that stands for Data Transfer Object.

29
00:01:47,430 --> 00:01:51,150
We will go into more detail on Dto's very shortly.

30
00:01:51,150 --> 00:01:55,050
But for right now, let's create this new class and add some validation rules to it.

31
00:01:55,970 --> 00:02:01,490
So back over inside my editor, inside my messages directory, I'm going to create a new folder called

32
00:02:01,490 --> 00:02:02,840
DTOs.

33
00:02:04,030 --> 00:02:12,640
Then inside there, I'm going to create a new file called Create dash message dot dto, dot ts.

34
00:02:14,740 --> 00:02:19,060
Then inside this file, we are going to create our brand new class that describes all the different

35
00:02:19,060 --> 00:02:22,990
properties that we expect our post request handler to receive.

36
00:02:23,570 --> 00:02:28,490
So I'm going to export a class called Create Message DTO.

37
00:02:29,390 --> 00:02:35,000
We expect the body of request coming into our request handler to have a content property that is going

38
00:02:35,000 --> 00:02:36,020
to be a string.

39
00:02:37,180 --> 00:02:39,040
So that's pretty much it for step two.

40
00:02:39,070 --> 00:02:43,210
We have created the class and we listed out all the different properties we expect to receive.

41
00:02:43,360 --> 00:02:50,020
So now step number three, we're going to use a library called Class Validator to add a couple of validation

42
00:02:50,020 --> 00:02:51,700
rules to the class itself.

43
00:02:51,850 --> 00:02:56,470
So for step number three, at the very top of this file, I'm going to import.

44
00:02:57,590 --> 00:02:59,270
Is string.

45
00:03:00,050 --> 00:03:03,680
From a library called Class Validator.

46
00:03:08,090 --> 00:03:10,550
We do get an error around class validator right away.

47
00:03:10,550 --> 00:03:15,320
So this is a additional library we have to install into our project to install it.

48
00:03:15,320 --> 00:03:16,940
I'm going to go back over to my terminal.

49
00:03:17,420 --> 00:03:19,280
I'm going to stop my running server.

50
00:03:20,490 --> 00:03:22,470
And do a quick NPM install.

51
00:03:22,710 --> 00:03:23,850
Class validator.

52
00:03:23,850 --> 00:03:29,580
And we're also going to get one other library very quickly called class transformer as well.

53
00:03:32,490 --> 00:03:37,710
Once the installation is complete, we'll then start up our server once again with another NPM run start

54
00:03:37,710 --> 00:03:38,880
colon dev.

55
00:03:42,630 --> 00:03:44,480
All right, then back over inside of our editor.

56
00:03:44,490 --> 00:03:45,660
This is string thing.

57
00:03:45,660 --> 00:03:48,360
Right here is another one of these decorators.

58
00:03:48,390 --> 00:03:54,210
We're going to apply this decorator to the string property inside of our Create message class.

59
00:03:54,300 --> 00:03:57,690
So right above I'm going to put in is string like so.

60
00:03:58,400 --> 00:04:00,110
So that is our validator.

61
00:04:00,140 --> 00:04:05,480
This is going to make sure that whenever we create an instance of create message DTO, we can make sure

62
00:04:05,480 --> 00:04:11,570
that the content property actually is a string as opposed to, say, a number undefined null or anything

63
00:04:11,570 --> 00:04:12,320
like that.

64
00:04:13,970 --> 00:04:15,450
Okay, So that's step number three.

65
00:04:15,470 --> 00:04:16,190
All done.

66
00:04:16,190 --> 00:04:17,910
I know that step is a little bit strange.

67
00:04:17,930 --> 00:04:18,260
Don't worry.

68
00:04:18,260 --> 00:04:21,290
We're going to come back to each of these steps in great detail in just a moment.

69
00:04:21,440 --> 00:04:22,760
So now the very last step.

70
00:04:22,760 --> 00:04:26,030
We're going to apply that class to our request handler.

71
00:04:26,030 --> 00:04:27,680
So just one more strange step.

72
00:04:28,780 --> 00:04:32,590
We're going to go back over to our controller at the very top of the file.

73
00:04:33,820 --> 00:04:36,460
I'm going to import the DTO that we just created.

74
00:04:36,460 --> 00:04:44,890
So create message DTO from DTOs create dash message DTO.

75
00:04:49,120 --> 00:04:53,080
So now that we've got that class, we're going to go down to our post request handler where we want

76
00:04:53,080 --> 00:04:54,420
to do some validation.

77
00:04:54,430 --> 00:04:55,960
And here's the crazy part.

78
00:04:55,960 --> 00:05:00,340
If you've got some strong knowledge of TypeScript, this is going to seem very strange and it's going

79
00:05:00,340 --> 00:05:03,520
to seem like it should not work as well as it's going to work.

80
00:05:03,520 --> 00:05:06,880
But once again, don't worry, we're going to go over all this stuff in great detail.

81
00:05:07,330 --> 00:05:11,980
I'm going to find the type of any that we've applied to body right there, and I'm going to replace

82
00:05:11,980 --> 00:05:16,320
that type with create message DTO.

83
00:05:17,960 --> 00:05:19,040
Okay, that's it.

84
00:05:19,070 --> 00:05:21,260
We have now set up validation.

85
00:05:21,560 --> 00:05:24,410
So we went through steps two, three and four.

86
00:05:24,440 --> 00:05:26,780
Let's now go back over to our client.

87
00:05:26,810 --> 00:05:32,000
We're going to try to make a post request once again with a couple of different varieties of data for

88
00:05:32,000 --> 00:05:33,200
the content property.

89
00:05:33,230 --> 00:05:34,880
We're going to make a request.

90
00:05:35,710 --> 00:05:40,120
Where the content property is a string, when it's not a string, when it's not present and so on.

91
00:05:40,120 --> 00:05:42,820
And we're going to see what happens inside of our application now.

92
00:05:43,400 --> 00:05:45,950
So again, I'm at my client.

93
00:05:45,950 --> 00:05:50,540
Remember, if you're not using the rest client inside of VS code, you should be using postman or something

94
00:05:50,540 --> 00:05:51,380
equivalent.

95
00:05:51,680 --> 00:05:56,270
We're going to make a post request this time with a content property that is a string.

96
00:05:56,270 --> 00:05:59,000
So this is in theory a valid property right here.

97
00:05:59,000 --> 00:06:03,830
We should be able to make this request and not get back some kind of error or anything like that.

98
00:06:03,950 --> 00:06:09,470
So if I send the request, sure enough, I do appear to get back some kind of successful status code.

99
00:06:10,260 --> 00:06:12,520
So now let's try to change the content property.

100
00:06:12,540 --> 00:06:15,510
I'm going to change that string to a number instead.

101
00:06:15,750 --> 00:06:19,650
Now, if I send off the request, I very quickly get back a big error.

102
00:06:20,610 --> 00:06:24,150
In this case, the air says that content must be a string.

103
00:06:24,270 --> 00:06:27,880
So it's very clear now that validation is taking place inside of our application.

104
00:06:27,900 --> 00:06:28,650
That's awesome.

105
00:06:28,650 --> 00:06:33,690
We've got some automatic validation and all we had to do was create that class and use a decorator on

106
00:06:33,690 --> 00:06:34,110
it.

107
00:06:34,730 --> 00:06:39,830
If we try to provide a content of say, null, we're going to get the same error.

108
00:06:40,560 --> 00:06:44,610
If we don't provide content at all, we're still going to get an error.

109
00:06:44,630 --> 00:06:46,570
If we misspell content.

110
00:06:46,590 --> 00:06:47,880
We're still going to get an error.

111
00:06:47,910 --> 00:06:54,840
It's only when we provide a content property that is definitely a string that the request works as expected.

112
00:06:56,040 --> 00:06:56,340
All right.

113
00:06:56,340 --> 00:06:59,850
So without a doubt, validation is now working correctly inside of our application.

114
00:06:59,850 --> 00:07:02,860
But as I mentioned, these steps are a little bit strange.

115
00:07:02,880 --> 00:07:05,610
What is this thing all about?

116
00:07:05,640 --> 00:07:09,630
What are those decorators coming from that class validation package all about?

117
00:07:09,630 --> 00:07:15,930
And when we apply that class to the request handler, we wired it up only as a type right there.

118
00:07:15,960 --> 00:07:17,430
That's pretty strange as well.

119
00:07:17,430 --> 00:07:22,590
Once again, if you're very familiar with TypeScript, you might kind of think that's a little bit strange

120
00:07:22,590 --> 00:07:27,360
because we've always been told, right, that when we make use of TypeScript, no type information is

121
00:07:27,360 --> 00:07:29,970
actually preserved in our JavaScript code.

122
00:07:30,300 --> 00:07:34,740
So let's take a pause right here and start to do a deeper dive on this entire validation process and

123
00:07:34,740 --> 00:07:36,360
understand what is going on.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,670 --> 00:00:05,180
Laten we een paar diagrammen bekijken en beginnen te begrijpen wat er achter de schermen gebeurt

2
00:00:05,180 --> 00:00:06,710
met de stappen twee, drie en vier.

3
00:00:06,950 --> 00:00:12,650
Dus allereerst, data transfer objects of dittos dittos zijn niet exclusief voor NASA.

4
00:00:13,310 --> 00:00:18,350
Er zijn dus veel andere talen en frameworks die veel gebruik maken van dittos of data transfer

5
00:00:18,350 --> 00:00:19,370
objects.

6
00:00:19,830 --> 00:00:26,990
Het doel van een DTA is informatie of gegevens te vervoeren tussen twee plaatsen, vaak in de vorm van netwerkverzoeken.

7
00:00:27,650 --> 00:00:31,460
Aan gegevensoverdrachtsobjecten is gewoonlijk geen enkele functionaliteit verbonden.

8
00:00:31,700 --> 00:00:32,870
Het zijn eenvoudige klassen.

9
00:00:32,900 --> 00:00:34,610
Ze noemen een paar verschillende eigenschappen.

10
00:00:35,090 --> 00:00:40,820
U kunt een gegevensoverdrachtsobject dus beschouwen als een zeer duidelijke beschrijving van hoe een bepaalde vorm

11
00:00:40,820 --> 00:00:44,330
van gegevens eruit ziet terwijl ze in een verzoek worden meegezonden.

12
00:00:45,990 --> 00:00:46,560
De volgende.

13
00:00:47,950 --> 00:00:48,990
Drie en vier.

14
00:00:49,240 --> 00:00:55,150
Dus ten eerste, drie, toen we begonnen met het toevoegen van enkele validatieregels aan onze DTO, dus dit is dring ding

15
00:00:55,150 --> 00:01:00,320
hier, hadden we heel snel het klasse validator pakket geïnstalleerd en een ander pakket genaamd een klasse

16
00:01:00,340 --> 00:01:01,420
transformator.

17
00:01:01,810 --> 00:01:05,160
Ik zal u snel wat informatie over deze twee pakketten laten zien.

18
00:01:05,980 --> 00:01:09,760
Eerst gaan we wat documentatie bekijken rond het klasse transformator pakket.

19
00:01:10,240 --> 00:01:14,110
Je vindt daar een link naar de GitHub-pagina voor een class transformer.

20
00:01:14,470 --> 00:01:16,510
Ik ga dat snel in mijn browser ophalen.

21
00:01:19,540 --> 00:01:25,420
Een laatste transformator is een eenvoudig pakket dat een gewoon object neemt en het omzet in een instantie van een

22
00:01:25,420 --> 00:01:25,970
klasse.

23
00:01:26,530 --> 00:01:30,400
Laten we eens kijken naar wat code in de leesmij om te begrijpen wat er gebeurt.

24
00:01:31,120 --> 00:01:35,320
Nu, als u de video hier wilt pauzeren en naar het eerste deel wilt kijken, de lees me dat zegt,

25
00:01:35,320 --> 00:01:36,970
wat is klasse transformator?

26
00:01:37,120 --> 00:01:38,350
Dat zou zeer nuttig zijn.

27
00:01:38,380 --> 00:01:39,610
Ik raad je aan dat te doen.

28
00:01:40,060 --> 00:01:41,860
Anders gaan we een codefragment bekijken.

29
00:01:42,460 --> 00:01:44,650
Dus in dit stukje code hier, kun je het zien.

30
00:01:44,650 --> 00:01:46,680
Ze hebben wat gewone JSON gegevens.

31
00:01:47,080 --> 00:01:49,450
Dit zijn gewone objecten in een array.

32
00:01:49,690 --> 00:01:52,650
Er zijn geen methoden, geen functionaliteit gebonden aan deze objecten.

33
00:01:53,140 --> 00:01:59,590
Het doel van dit klasse transformator pakket is om die objecten te nemen en ze te veranderen in instanties van

34
00:01:59,590 --> 00:02:01,180
een klasse of voorbeeld.

35
00:02:01,180 --> 00:02:05,230
Elk van deze objecten kan worden omgezet in een instantie van de klasse gebruiker.

36
00:02:05,830 --> 00:02:11,170
En als we eenmaal instanties van de klasse gebruiker hebben, dan hebben we ook enkele methoden die bij elk van die

37
00:02:11,170 --> 00:02:11,980
dingen horen.

38
00:02:12,870 --> 00:02:18,390
Class Transformer gaat dus over het automatiseren van het proces om deze gewone JSON-objecten of gewone

39
00:02:18,390 --> 00:02:23,460
JavaScript-objecten om te zetten in een veel vollediger instantie van een klasse.

40
00:02:24,660 --> 00:02:29,370
Ten tweede, we hebben klasse validator, dus nogmaals, ik ga de documentatie daarvoor hier

41
00:02:29,550 --> 00:02:30,090
ophalen.

42
00:02:31,810 --> 00:02:38,860
Klasse validator is een pakket van dezelfde mensen, hetzelfde project dat validatie van het einde van eigenschappen op een klasse afhandelt

43
00:02:38,860 --> 00:02:40,540
door decoratoren te gebruiken.

44
00:02:41,460 --> 00:02:46,500
Op deze, lees mij, gaan we een beetje naar beneden scrollen naar de inhoudsopgave en dan naar beneden

45
00:02:46,500 --> 00:02:48,990
naar de onderkant van de lijst met al deze links.

46
00:02:49,140 --> 00:02:53,010
En we willen hier een link vinden genaamd Validation Decorator's.

47
00:02:54,220 --> 00:02:58,960
Dit zijn dus verschillende decoratoren die we kunnen gebruiken om bepaalde eigenschappen

48
00:02:58,960 --> 00:03:03,880
in een klasse te valideren, dus we hebben er een gebruikt die string heet en hier staat hij.

49
00:03:04,030 --> 00:03:08,710
Maar er zijn andere decoratoren waarmee we ervoor kunnen zorgen dat een eigenschap een datum is, een

50
00:03:09,100 --> 00:03:10,780
boolean is, een integer array is.

51
00:03:11,290 --> 00:03:14,020
We kunnen ervoor zorgen dat een getal positief, negatief is.

52
00:03:14,500 --> 00:03:17,860
We kunnen ervoor zorgen dat een datum een minimale datum of een maximale datum heeft.

53
00:03:18,250 --> 00:03:22,630
U ziet dat er een groot aantal verschillende validatiemogelijkheden in dit pakket zitten.

54
00:03:23,230 --> 00:03:29,350
Dus, nogmaals, deze bibliotheek gaat over het valideren van eigenschappen op een instantie van een klasse door het gebruik

55
00:03:29,350 --> 00:03:30,610
van decoratoren.

56
00:03:31,880 --> 00:03:37,250
Goed, dus nu we deze twee bibliotheken in gedachten hebben, laten we nu eens kijken naar de volledige stroom,

57
00:03:37,250 --> 00:03:39,650
of tenminste het grootste deel ervan voor nu.

58
00:03:40,500 --> 00:03:43,320
Dus hier is hoe alles werkt, samen met de validatie pijp.

59
00:03:44,650 --> 00:03:50,950
Dus onze server ontvangt een verzoek, dat verzoek gaat nu automatisch naar de validatiepijp, de validatiepijp

60
00:03:50,950 --> 00:03:54,480
doorloopt drie verschillende stappen.

61
00:03:55,120 --> 00:04:00,460
Eerst gaat de validatiepijp automatisch dat class transformer pakket gebruiken om van

62
00:04:00,460 --> 00:04:04,700
de body van dat inkomende verzoek een instantie van de class te maken.

63
00:04:05,320 --> 00:04:12,610
Dus dit lichaam hier is veranderd in een instantie van create message DTO en dat heeft een heleboel validatie informatie eraan gekoppeld

64
00:04:12,940 --> 00:04:15,670
door het gebruik van deze decoratoren.

65
00:04:17,600 --> 00:04:22,760
Nu we een instantie van de klasse hebben, gaat de validatiepijp gebruik maken van de klasse validator

66
00:04:22,760 --> 00:04:25,120
om die instantie te valideren.

67
00:04:25,670 --> 00:04:30,010
Dus die stap is waar de validatiepijp naar deze decoratoren gaat kijken.

68
00:04:30,020 --> 00:04:34,340
Het gaat deze decoratoren gebruiken om de verschillende eigenschappen te valideren die we hebben op de instantie

69
00:04:34,340 --> 00:04:35,570
van ons maak bericht, DTL.

70
00:04:38,080 --> 00:04:43,210
Zodra die validatie is uitgevoerd, gaat de validatiepijp kijken of er validatiefouten zijn opgetreden, als er validatiefouten

71
00:04:43,660 --> 00:04:45,260
zijn opgetreden.

72
00:04:45,280 --> 00:04:48,160
Het stuurt onmiddellijk een antwoord terug naar degene die dit verzoek deed.

73
00:04:48,520 --> 00:04:52,780
Anders wordt het verzoek doorgegeven aan onze request handler om het te vinden in onze controller.

74
00:04:53,760 --> 00:04:57,600
Dus dat is wat er achter de schermen gebeurt met de validatiepijp, intern maakt de validatiepijp

75
00:04:57,600 --> 00:05:02,370
gebruik van class transformer en class validator om al deze validatiedingen automatisch voor je te doen.

76
00:05:02,880 --> 00:05:07,320
Class Transformer is op zijn beurt verantwoordelijk voor het nemen van dit vliegtuig.

77
00:05:07,320 --> 00:05:10,210
Jason, lichaam van ons verzoek.

78
00:05:10,680 --> 00:05:17,440
Dus dit vliegtuig, JSON object hier en het omzetten in een instantie van ons maak bericht, DTL, dat al die

79
00:05:17,460 --> 00:05:19,610
validatie informatie bevat.

80
00:05:20,160 --> 00:05:25,020
En nogmaals, Class Validator is verantwoordelijk voor het nemen van die instantie, het valideren ervan, het gebruik

81
00:05:25,020 --> 00:05:29,650
van informatie van die decoratoren en het teruggeven van het validatieresultaat aan de validatiepijp.

82
00:05:31,390 --> 00:05:35,560
Een heleboel dingen hoef je niet echt te weten, de reden dat ik je alles vertel in

83
00:05:35,560 --> 00:05:39,850
deze cursus is dat je een aantal van de dingen die achter de schermen van Nest gebeuren,

84
00:05:39,850 --> 00:05:41,420
kunt gaan begrijpen.

85
00:05:41,710 --> 00:05:46,960
Er zit veel functionaliteit in Nest die echt heel anders is dan veel andere typescript of JavaScript

86
00:05:46,960 --> 00:05:47,920
frameworks.

87
00:05:48,240 --> 00:05:50,110
Veel dingen zijn echt magisch.

88
00:05:50,470 --> 00:05:56,590
Er is geen duidelijk verband tussen wat in dit bericht staat en het eigenlijke

89
00:05:56,590 --> 00:05:58,750
validatieproces.

90
00:05:59,620 --> 00:06:01,890
Oké, er is nog één ding dat ik wil bespreken.

91
00:06:01,930 --> 00:06:03,580
We gaan even pauzeren en dan bespreken we het.

92
00:06:04,090 --> 00:06:07,150
Maar één ding dat we niet echt besproken hebben is hoe.

93
00:06:08,360 --> 00:06:14,360
We hebben het type bericht hier toegevoegd en hoe de validatiepijp het op dat moment begreep, want vergeet

94
00:06:14,360 --> 00:06:19,880
niet dat tijdens het validatieproces de klasse-transformator de body verandert in een instantie

95
00:06:19,880 --> 00:06:22,490
van specifiek de DTO-klasse.

96
00:06:22,880 --> 00:06:26,120
Dus in dit geval, een instantie van create message DTO.

97
00:06:26,690 --> 00:06:32,330
En de vraag is, hoe wist de validatiepijp het lichaam te veranderen in een instantie van grote boodschap?

98
00:06:33,290 --> 00:06:37,010
Omdat we hier alleen maar een grote boodschap hebben.

99
00:06:37,010 --> 00:06:39,680
Dit is de enige plaats waar we gebruik hebben gemaakt van die klasse.

100
00:06:40,100 --> 00:06:42,610
We hebben het toegepast als een type-annotatie op een functie.

101
00:06:42,830 --> 00:06:48,470
En denk eraan, wanneer we TypeScript schrijven, wordt onze toepassing eerst omgezet in JavaScript en vervolgens

102
00:06:48,470 --> 00:06:49,310
uitgevoerd.

103
00:06:50,160 --> 00:06:55,110
En wanneer we dat proces van conversie van typescript naar JavaScript uitvoeren, wordt ons in theorie

104
00:06:55,110 --> 00:06:58,800
altijd verteld dat alle type-informatie op dat moment verloren gaat.

105
00:06:59,960 --> 00:07:06,780
Dus in theorie bestaat dit kleine stukje informatie hier niet echt als onze applicatie wordt uitgevoerd.

106
00:07:06,980 --> 00:07:13,730
Dus, nogmaals, hoe weet de validatiepijp dat hij het inkomende lichaam moet veranderen in een specifieke

107
00:07:13,730 --> 00:07:15,520
berichtinstantie?

108
00:07:15,890 --> 00:07:17,060
Dat is de grote vraag.

109
00:07:17,760 --> 00:07:20,870
Laten we hier nog een pauze inlassen en dat dadelijk onderzoeken.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,510 --> 00:00:02,980
This is an optional video.

2
00:00:02,980 --> 00:00:07,570
In this video, we're going to discuss how the validation pipe knows to validate the incoming request

3
00:00:07,570 --> 00:00:13,540
and make sure that specifically for requests coming to this request handler, the body satisfies all

4
00:00:13,540 --> 00:00:16,780
the validation rules we set up inside that create message DTO.

5
00:00:16,780 --> 00:00:20,380
So if you are not interested in this, then no problem whatsoever.

6
00:00:20,380 --> 00:00:22,630
Pause the video right now and continue on to the next one.

7
00:00:22,630 --> 00:00:23,800
Otherwise stick around.

8
00:00:24,010 --> 00:00:28,450
Now, kind of told you at the end of the last video why this thing is so interesting.

9
00:00:28,450 --> 00:00:32,890
So let me just review that very quickly, help you understand why what we are seeing right now inside

10
00:00:32,890 --> 00:00:37,240
of our application is kind of extraordinary as far as TypeScript and JavaScript goes.

11
00:00:37,720 --> 00:00:41,140
Now, remember, we are currently writing TypeScript inside of our code editor.

12
00:00:41,170 --> 00:00:46,300
In the TypeScript world, we write out decorators, we write out things like type annotations and so

13
00:00:46,300 --> 00:00:46,810
on.

14
00:00:46,810 --> 00:00:51,040
But remember, very importantly, we never directly execute TypeScript.

15
00:00:51,040 --> 00:00:54,820
There is no engine out there that runs TypeScript code directly.

16
00:00:54,820 --> 00:01:00,940
Instead, whenever we run our application, all of our TypeScript is first converted into plain JavaScript

17
00:01:00,940 --> 00:01:02,680
and then it is executed.

18
00:01:03,650 --> 00:01:09,950
So when our application is running, all these decorators, all these type annotations, they get removed

19
00:01:09,950 --> 00:01:15,140
and they don't exist in the JavaScript world, at least in quite exactly the way that we see them right

20
00:01:15,140 --> 00:01:15,800
here.

21
00:01:16,190 --> 00:01:22,400
So how does the validation pipe know that it needs to somehow validate that incoming body against a

22
00:01:22,400 --> 00:01:23,660
type annotation?

23
00:01:23,660 --> 00:01:25,880
That type annotation we are applying right there?

24
00:01:26,090 --> 00:01:28,200
Well, let me show you how this works.

25
00:01:28,220 --> 00:01:33,770
It all goes back to something that we very briefly discussed on the very first application we worked

26
00:01:33,770 --> 00:01:34,220
on.

27
00:01:34,250 --> 00:01:40,100
You might recall that on that first app, we had created a tsconfig.json file manually.

28
00:01:40,100 --> 00:01:42,710
So here's my tsconfig.json file right here.

29
00:01:43,010 --> 00:01:49,810
And inside there we had put in two configuration options emit decorator metadata and experimental decorators.

30
00:01:49,820 --> 00:01:54,710
And I told you at the time that these two settings were extremely important for nest to work correctly.

31
00:01:54,980 --> 00:02:00,620
So in this video we're going to understand why that is specifically what the Emit decorator metadata

32
00:02:00,620 --> 00:02:02,480
setting is really doing for us.

33
00:02:03,720 --> 00:02:10,080
This line of code right here, or this configuration option allows a very small amount of type information

34
00:02:10,080 --> 00:02:14,200
to make it from the TypeScript world over to the JavaScript world.

35
00:02:14,220 --> 00:02:19,410
So even though when you are first learning TypeScript, you are told all the time that all type information

36
00:02:19,410 --> 00:02:23,820
is stripped out of your code when you convert to JavaScript, that is not totally true.

37
00:02:23,850 --> 00:02:27,060
If you have emit decorator metadata set to true.

38
00:02:27,270 --> 00:02:32,820
So if you have that option set to true, a very small amount of type annotations and information will

39
00:02:32,820 --> 00:02:34,170
make it over to JavaScript.

40
00:02:34,380 --> 00:02:40,020
Let's take a look at our converted JavaScript code for our project, and see where this little bit of

41
00:02:40,020 --> 00:02:42,480
type information actually surfaces.

42
00:02:43,020 --> 00:02:44,760
So back inside my editor.

43
00:02:45,460 --> 00:02:49,000
You'll notice that we have a dist directory inside of our project.

44
00:02:49,030 --> 00:02:52,810
This holds all of our converted TypeScript as JavaScript code.

45
00:02:53,020 --> 00:02:55,450
Inside of here I'm going to find the messages directory.

46
00:02:56,810 --> 00:03:00,470
And then find the messages controller js file.

47
00:03:01,310 --> 00:03:04,190
Now inside of here we are looking at converted TypeScript code.

48
00:03:04,190 --> 00:03:05,510
So this is JavaScript.

49
00:03:05,510 --> 00:03:09,140
And you're going to notice there is a ton of extra code inserted inside of here.

50
00:03:09,560 --> 00:03:14,180
Out of everything that's going on, we really just care for right now about 1 or 2 lines of code.

51
00:03:14,210 --> 00:03:22,460
Towards the very bottom of the file, specifically down here you'll find underscore, underscore, decorate

52
00:03:22,460 --> 00:03:24,620
and then common post right there.

53
00:03:24,620 --> 00:03:29,090
So this section right here is what we really really care about right now I'm going to delete everything

54
00:03:29,090 --> 00:03:30,320
else inside this file.

55
00:03:30,590 --> 00:03:33,110
Now if you do the same make sure you don't save this file.

56
00:03:33,140 --> 00:03:35,840
Because this is actually what is being used to run our server right now.

57
00:03:35,840 --> 00:03:37,640
So don't save any changes you make.

58
00:03:38,980 --> 00:03:43,480
So this is how decorators are actually applied once we convert TypeScript into JavaScript.

59
00:03:43,510 --> 00:03:46,000
Decorators don't actually exist in JavaScript itself.

60
00:03:46,000 --> 00:03:50,800
So this is kind of like a workaround to make the functionality of the decorator work in the JavaScript

61
00:03:50,800 --> 00:03:51,400
world.

62
00:03:51,550 --> 00:03:58,060
In this case, we are applying the post decorator to the create message method, and we are applying

63
00:03:58,060 --> 00:04:03,880
the body decorator to the first argument of the create message method.

64
00:04:04,120 --> 00:04:09,460
So we are really looking at some code that makes that decorator and that decorator right there work.

65
00:04:12,350 --> 00:04:17,810
Now as a part of the the body decorator that we're using, which is a parameter decorator, is being

66
00:04:17,810 --> 00:04:19,610
applied to the argument of our method.

67
00:04:19,940 --> 00:04:24,320
A little bit of type information, as I mentioned, is leaked out into the JavaScript world.

68
00:04:24,350 --> 00:04:27,350
That's what these metadata function calls are right here.

69
00:04:27,380 --> 00:04:33,440
Metadata refers to some type information or some kind of higher level information that is conveyed about

70
00:04:33,440 --> 00:04:37,730
our code and is persisted or kind of maintained over in the JavaScript world as well.

71
00:04:38,060 --> 00:04:42,500
One of these pieces of data is design colon param types.

72
00:04:42,620 --> 00:04:49,160
This little piece of information is going to tell us in the JavaScript world what the type annotation

73
00:04:49,160 --> 00:04:50,930
was for this argument.

74
00:04:52,360 --> 00:04:57,130
So if we take a look at that, it says design parameter types is going to be assigned a value of our

75
00:04:57,130 --> 00:04:59,590
create message DTO class.

76
00:05:00,200 --> 00:05:04,010
So this little bit of code right here is the absolute key.

77
00:05:04,040 --> 00:05:08,930
This is what allows a very small amount of type information to persist over to JavaScript.

78
00:05:09,170 --> 00:05:14,630
So when the validation pipe runs on an incoming request, it's going to look up that little property

79
00:05:14,630 --> 00:05:17,490
right there on the create message function.

80
00:05:17,510 --> 00:05:24,740
It's going to see that we were expecting to receive a body of type create message DTO.

81
00:05:24,890 --> 00:05:29,210
The validation pipe is then going to go and find the class definition for that thing.

82
00:05:29,210 --> 00:05:33,620
So that actual class is going to find all the validation rules on it as well.

83
00:05:33,890 --> 00:05:35,660
So that's what's going on behind the scenes.

84
00:05:35,660 --> 00:05:37,160
That's how all this stuff works.

85
00:05:37,160 --> 00:05:41,330
And that's how why we are applying a class right here as a type annotation.

86
00:05:41,330 --> 00:05:44,570
That's how some information about that type annotation is made.

87
00:05:44,570 --> 00:05:46,220
Use of in the JavaScript world.

88
00:05:47,160 --> 00:05:47,460
Okay.

89
00:05:47,460 --> 00:05:48,230
So that's it.

90
00:05:48,240 --> 00:05:51,390
So I'm going to close that file now I'm not going to save any changes.

91
00:05:52,090 --> 00:05:55,780
Now that we've got a better idea of what's going on behind the scenes, you're going to see the same

92
00:05:55,780 --> 00:05:57,430
pattern start to arise.

93
00:05:57,430 --> 00:06:00,100
And a lot of other uses in nest as well.

94
00:06:00,100 --> 00:06:04,900
So there are many other scenarios where a very small amount of type information is persisted over to

95
00:06:04,900 --> 00:06:07,180
JavaScript and made use of in some way.

96
00:06:07,790 --> 00:06:12,350
So now that we've got this extra little bit of info, let's take a pause right here and continue on

97
00:06:12,350 --> 00:06:14,060
with our controller in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# section 5:  Nest Architecture Services and Repositories


1
00:00:01,890 --> 00:00:05,490
Our controller is looking pretty good, but at this point in time, I don't think there's anything else

2
00:00:05,490 --> 00:00:06,910
we can really do inside of here.

3
00:00:06,930 --> 00:00:11,820
What we really need to do right now is have the ability to store and retrieve messages inside of our

4
00:00:11,820 --> 00:00:12,670
application.

5
00:00:12,730 --> 00:00:18,270
And remember, we had said that we were going to have a messages service and a messages repository to

6
00:00:18,270 --> 00:00:19,630
implement these two things.

7
00:00:19,650 --> 00:00:23,560
So in this video, we're going to start to focus on services and repositories.

8
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:27,420
First, a couple of notes on exactly what these things are and how they work together.

9
00:00:28,020 --> 00:00:32,130
Now, one of the biggest challenges I can just about guarantee that you're going to run into in the

10
00:00:32,130 --> 00:00:38,430
world of Nest is understanding why services exist and how they are different from a repository.

11
00:00:38,460 --> 00:00:43,170
So we're going to spend a good amount of time to clarify exactly what these things are and why we might

12
00:00:43,170 --> 00:00:44,580
use one over the other.

13
00:00:45,660 --> 00:00:50,280
So the first thing I want you to understand is that both services and repositories are always going

14
00:00:50,280 --> 00:00:53,340
to be classes very similar to everything else we create in nest.

15
00:00:53,370 --> 00:00:54,870
We're always creating classes here.

16
00:00:55,650 --> 00:01:00,030
We're always going to create a service anytime we want to write out some kind of business logic.

17
00:01:00,030 --> 00:01:03,300
So something to run, some kind of calculation or stuff like that.

18
00:01:03,330 --> 00:01:08,220
We also make use of services anytime that we want to fetch data from a repository.

19
00:01:08,860 --> 00:01:13,950
Repositories themselves are where we are going to write out any kind of storage related logic.

20
00:01:13,960 --> 00:01:19,210
So if we need to directly interact with the database, if we need to write information into a file,

21
00:01:19,210 --> 00:01:22,360
anything like that, we're going to place it into a repository.

22
00:01:23,190 --> 00:01:28,140
In our case, remember our messages repository that you and I are going to create.

23
00:01:28,170 --> 00:01:33,720
We are going to eventually have a plain text file inside of our project that stores all of our different

24
00:01:33,720 --> 00:01:34,510
messages.

25
00:01:34,530 --> 00:01:39,080
So in our case, our messages repository is going to manage that file.

26
00:01:39,090 --> 00:01:42,720
It's going to read data out of the file and write data into the file.

27
00:01:42,780 --> 00:01:48,930
The goal of the messages service is to make use of the repository and get access to messages through

28
00:01:48,930 --> 00:01:50,010
that repository.

29
00:01:52,930 --> 00:01:57,990
A service is going to frequently use one or more repositories to find and store data.

30
00:01:58,000 --> 00:02:00,820
So for right now, we only have one service and one repository.

31
00:02:00,820 --> 00:02:05,560
But in the future we might have a single service that makes use of multiple different repositories,

32
00:02:05,590 --> 00:02:09,940
gets many different kinds of data and combines it together in a very interesting way.

33
00:02:10,690 --> 00:02:15,640
Last thing I want to mention is that repositories usually end up being kind of like a wrapper around

34
00:02:15,640 --> 00:02:17,700
some other storage library.

35
00:02:17,710 --> 00:02:23,120
So it's very common instead of having a standalone repository to instead have something called a type

36
00:02:23,560 --> 00:02:24,190
entity.

37
00:02:24,250 --> 00:02:27,010
If you don't know what that is totally fine or a mongoose schema.

38
00:02:27,040 --> 00:02:30,360
Again, if you don't know what that is totally fine or something very similar.

39
00:02:30,400 --> 00:02:35,260
So even though in this application, you and I are going to build a repository from scratch, in many

40
00:02:35,260 --> 00:02:40,660
cases we are going to be using libraries that give us repositories that are already set up and already

41
00:02:40,660 --> 00:02:42,020
configured for us.

42
00:02:42,100 --> 00:02:46,060
So a lot of times we don't really have to worry about assembling that thing from scratch.

43
00:02:47,180 --> 00:02:51,800
Now, the very last thing I want to say for right now around the differences between these two things

44
00:02:51,830 --> 00:02:57,710
is that it is extremely common, as we're going to see, to have very similar methods between services

45
00:02:57,710 --> 00:02:59,690
and the repositories that they use.

46
00:02:59,720 --> 00:03:01,700
Let me give you an example of that right away.

47
00:03:02,470 --> 00:03:02,980
Okay.

48
00:03:03,620 --> 00:03:07,070
So I've got our messages, service and our messages repository.

49
00:03:07,100 --> 00:03:11,990
Remember, the goal of this repository over here is to store all of our different messages inside of

50
00:03:11,990 --> 00:03:15,950
a plain text file and eventually fetch messages out of there as well.

51
00:03:16,160 --> 00:03:20,900
The goal of the messages service is to retrieve information from the repository.

52
00:03:21,140 --> 00:03:25,850
So in the context of the service and the repository, let's try to think of some different method names

53
00:03:25,850 --> 00:03:28,400
or essentially what these two things are going to need to do.

54
00:03:29,030 --> 00:03:34,160
While the messages repository is definitely going to need methods where we can maybe open up a file

55
00:03:34,160 --> 00:03:36,890
and find a particular message based upon some ID.

56
00:03:37,250 --> 00:03:40,820
So maybe having a method name like find one makes a lot of sense.

57
00:03:41,120 --> 00:03:45,680
We probably are also going to need to open up that file and find all the different messages we have

58
00:03:45,680 --> 00:03:46,690
stored inside of it.

59
00:03:46,700 --> 00:03:49,760
So having a method name like Find all also makes sense.

60
00:03:50,030 --> 00:03:53,510
Finally, we definitely need to open up that file and add a new message in.

61
00:03:53,510 --> 00:03:58,070
So again, having a method named something like Create absolutely makes sense as well.

62
00:03:59,320 --> 00:04:01,090
So here's where things start to get interesting.

63
00:04:01,090 --> 00:04:05,920
Remember, we're not going to directly make use of repositories inside of a controller.

64
00:04:05,920 --> 00:04:11,440
Instead, it is up to the service to interact with the repository and our controller is going to work

65
00:04:11,440 --> 00:04:12,640
with the service.

66
00:04:12,820 --> 00:04:20,050
So our service needs to have a lot of functionality around finding, storing and retrieving different

67
00:04:20,050 --> 00:04:21,310
messages that we store.

68
00:04:22,340 --> 00:04:27,020
So let's think about some different methods that our messages service is going to need.

69
00:04:27,050 --> 00:04:30,530
Well, I think that the service is probably going to need to have the ability to find one individual

70
00:04:30,530 --> 00:04:32,120
message through the repository.

71
00:04:32,120 --> 00:04:35,000
So we'd probably give it a method name of something like find one.

72
00:04:35,720 --> 00:04:38,840
The messages Service also probably needs to find all the different messages.

73
00:04:38,840 --> 00:04:43,310
So we probably need some method name like find all and same thing for create.

74
00:04:43,400 --> 00:04:47,720
So at the end of the day, and this is why I said just a moment ago, it starts to get really confusing

75
00:04:47,720 --> 00:04:51,010
why we create a service and how it's different from a repository.

76
00:04:51,020 --> 00:04:57,410
And that's because we very frequently end up with methods on the service that look absolutely identical

77
00:04:57,440 --> 00:04:59,360
to the underlying repository.

78
00:04:59,360 --> 00:05:04,140
And you are going to end up in a scenario where you say, Why are we creating this service at all?

79
00:05:04,160 --> 00:05:05,620
Why does this thing exist?

80
00:05:05,630 --> 00:05:11,390
Because many in many cases, all it's going to do is turn around and call identical methods on some

81
00:05:11,390 --> 00:05:12,530
other class.

82
00:05:12,650 --> 00:05:15,110
This is something, like I said, 2 or 3 times now.

83
00:05:15,110 --> 00:05:16,820
It's going to be a little bit strange.

84
00:05:16,820 --> 00:05:19,700
You're going to think that the service is just not needed.

85
00:05:19,700 --> 00:05:24,200
But I'm here telling you right away, yes, we still want to make these services.

86
00:05:24,230 --> 00:05:29,600
There are very good reasons for setting up these services and having them behave as sort of like a proxy

87
00:05:29,600 --> 00:05:31,760
that sits in front of our repositories.

88
00:05:31,790 --> 00:05:37,560
And it all comes down to as soon as we want to access multiple different kinds of data, anytime we

89
00:05:37,560 --> 00:05:42,690
want to combine together multiple different repositories, anytime we want to start doing testing at

90
00:05:42,690 --> 00:05:44,010
the end of the day, just take my word for it.

91
00:05:44,010 --> 00:05:49,110
Right now there are still very good reasons to create a service and have it sit inside or in front of

92
00:05:49,110 --> 00:05:54,030
the repository, even though it seems like it's just duplicated or unnecessary code.

93
00:05:54,610 --> 00:05:58,600
So at the very end, what I really mean to say here is that if we have identical methods between the

94
00:05:58,600 --> 00:06:00,830
service and the repository, totally common.

95
00:06:00,850 --> 00:06:01,240
Totally.

96
00:06:01,240 --> 00:06:01,690
Okay.

97
00:06:01,720 --> 00:06:03,040
There is a reason for it.

98
00:06:03,870 --> 00:06:04,170
All right.

99
00:06:04,170 --> 00:06:08,430
So now that we got all this preamble out of the way, let's take a pause right here and then start to

100
00:06:08,430 --> 00:06:11,250
create our service and repository in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,760 --> 00:00:04,710
Let's get started creating that repository and that service.

2
00:00:04,730 --> 00:00:09,950
The nest does have commands we can use to automatically generate a service, and we can also use another

3
00:00:09,950 --> 00:00:13,820
very similar command to kind of somewhat generate a repository as well.

4
00:00:13,850 --> 00:00:18,290
But on this first go around, we're going to create all the necessary files from scratch on our own.

5
00:00:18,290 --> 00:00:19,520
So let's get to it.

6
00:00:19,670 --> 00:00:20,870
Back inside my editor.

7
00:00:20,870 --> 00:00:24,000
I'm going to find the messages folder inside our SRC directory.

8
00:00:24,020 --> 00:00:29,480
I'm going to create a new file called Messages dot Service dot ts.

9
00:00:30,060 --> 00:00:35,690
And then another file called Messages Dot repository dot ts.

10
00:00:38,280 --> 00:00:42,690
Now, just about everything inside of our service file is going to end up depending upon code that exists

11
00:00:42,690 --> 00:00:44,290
inside of our repository.

12
00:00:44,310 --> 00:00:49,200
So I think we should put together the repository first and then we'll put together the service.

13
00:00:49,380 --> 00:00:54,870
So I'm going to open up my messages repository file, and inside of here we're going to create a class

14
00:00:54,870 --> 00:00:59,610
because remember, they are always classes and we're going to start to add in these three different

15
00:00:59,610 --> 00:01:00,570
methods to it.

16
00:01:00,600 --> 00:01:04,590
Each of these methods are going to work on some file that is stored on our hard drive.

17
00:01:05,980 --> 00:01:11,350
So inside of here, I'm going to export a class called Messages Repository.

18
00:01:11,380 --> 00:01:13,780
Double check your spelling on repository, please.

19
00:01:14,650 --> 00:01:20,110
I'm then going to add in three different methods called Find one, find all and create.

20
00:01:20,810 --> 00:01:22,190
So find one.

21
00:01:22,390 --> 00:01:26,540
This thing is going to take in an argument of an ID that is a string.

22
00:01:26,900 --> 00:01:30,980
We're definitely going to have some asynchronous code inside of here because we need to read a file

23
00:01:31,010 --> 00:01:31,950
off the hard drive.

24
00:01:31,970 --> 00:01:34,250
So I'm also going to mark that method as async.

25
00:01:35,420 --> 00:01:38,270
Next up I'll do find all.

26
00:01:39,960 --> 00:01:41,580
And again, I'm going to mark that as a.

27
