00:01:42,420 --> 00:01:48,420
And then finally create or do we actually call it add on this diagram now called it create good enough?

28
00:01:49,290 --> 00:01:54,930
And we're going to receive an argument of some message that will be a string that we want to store inside

29
00:01:54,930 --> 00:01:55,680
that file.

30
00:01:56,620 --> 00:02:02,170
Next up, let's import some helper functions from the Node.js standard library.

31
00:02:02,200 --> 00:02:05,810
We're going to use these functions to read and write contents to a file.

32
00:02:05,830 --> 00:02:16,270
So at the very top I'm going to import, read, file and write file from FS slash promises.

33
00:02:18,110 --> 00:02:20,590
So again, files built into the Node standard library.

34
00:02:20,600 --> 00:02:24,650
We're going to use these to read and write contents to the file that's going to store all of our different

35
00:02:24,650 --> 00:02:25,460
messages.

36
00:02:25,760 --> 00:02:29,900
And right away, we probably need to think about what format we're going to store these messages in.

37
00:02:30,050 --> 00:02:35,150
So inside of my root project directory, I'm going to create a new file right now that is going to serve

38
00:02:35,150 --> 00:02:37,910
as our little storage of all of our different messages.

39
00:02:38,240 --> 00:02:41,780
I'm going to call this file messages dot Json.

40
00:02:43,140 --> 00:02:47,910
Then inside this file, I'm going to write out a quick example to show you how I would like to format

41
00:02:47,910 --> 00:02:50,150
all the different messages that get stored inside of here.

42
00:02:50,160 --> 00:02:56,070
So I would like to store all these messages inside of an object where the keys are going to be the ID

43
00:02:56,070 --> 00:02:57,410
of a message.

44
00:02:57,420 --> 00:03:02,370
So maybe if we store a message with ID 12, we'll have a key of 12.

45
00:03:04,440 --> 00:03:07,260
And then the value will be a message object.

46
00:03:07,350 --> 00:03:12,600
A message object is going to have a content property that is a string.

47
00:03:12,600 --> 00:03:14,760
So maybe something like Hi there.

48
00:03:14,970 --> 00:03:18,120
And it's also going to have the ID as well.

49
00:03:18,970 --> 00:03:21,350
So the ID is being somewhat duplicated here.

50
00:03:21,370 --> 00:03:22,150
That's totally fine.

51
00:03:22,150 --> 00:03:23,230
We can live with it.

52
00:03:23,560 --> 00:03:25,780
So this is the general format of this file.

53
00:03:25,780 --> 00:03:30,640
We've got one big overall object, and then we're going to have a variety of different keys inside of

54
00:03:30,640 --> 00:03:31,800
here that are IDs.

55
00:03:31,810 --> 00:03:35,290
And then for each key, we'll have a value that is a message object.

56
00:03:35,410 --> 00:03:40,390
So over time, as we start to add in additional messages, I would expect this file to eventually look

57
00:03:40,390 --> 00:03:41,280
something like this.

58
00:03:41,290 --> 00:03:44,530
And of course each ID will probably be slightly different.

59
00:03:46,120 --> 00:03:48,800
So this is what I want this file to eventually look like.

60
00:03:48,820 --> 00:03:54,940
So we need to make sure that we write out all of our storage code to store and retrieve data in this

61
00:03:54,940 --> 00:03:55,690
format.

62
00:03:55,780 --> 00:04:00,640
So just keep that in mind as we start to write this storage and retrieval code, I'm going to kind of

63
00:04:00,670 --> 00:04:01,960
go over it pretty quickly.

64
00:04:01,960 --> 00:04:05,410
We're not going to spend a lot of time on it because a lot of this reading to a file or writing to a

65
00:04:05,410 --> 00:04:08,080
file stuff is not super specific to Nestjs.

66
00:04:08,110 --> 00:04:11,070
This is just kind of some general JavaScript or TypeScript stuff.

67
00:04:11,080 --> 00:04:14,620
So I'm going to assume that you can probably figure out what's going on here on your own.

68
00:04:15,320 --> 00:04:19,730
Okay, so now the last thing we're going to do, we are not going to automatically generate this file.

69
00:04:19,730 --> 00:04:23,360
So we're not going to generate messages dot Json automatically inside of our code.

70
00:04:23,360 --> 00:04:27,110
Instead, we're going to assume that this file always exists.

71
00:04:27,230 --> 00:04:31,430
So I'm going to delete everything inside the file, but I'm not going to delete the file.

72
00:04:31,520 --> 00:04:32,810
If the file doesn't exist.

73
00:04:32,810 --> 00:04:37,460
When our code starts up, then all of our reading and writing logic is going to throw an error because

74
00:04:37,460 --> 00:04:40,970
it's going to say that the messages dot Json file doesn't exist at all.

75
00:04:41,680 --> 00:04:43,150
And we definitely would not want that.

76
00:04:44,080 --> 00:04:44,410
Okay.

77
00:04:44,410 --> 00:04:49,120
So now that we've got some reasonable idea of what's going to go inside that file, let's start to put

78
00:04:49,120 --> 00:04:52,060
together some implementation for each of these different methods.

79
00:04:52,330 --> 00:04:56,140
The implementation for each of them is going to end up being pretty similar in just about every single

80
00:04:56,140 --> 00:04:56,500
case.

81
00:04:56,500 --> 00:04:58,720
We're going to read the contents of that file.

82
00:04:58,720 --> 00:05:01,360
It's going to be given to us as a plain string.

83
00:05:01,360 --> 00:05:06,280
We're going have to take all that Json, parse it into an object, and then work with that object in

84
00:05:06,280 --> 00:05:06,970
some way.

85
00:05:07,210 --> 00:05:12,080
So let's first begin with the find one implementation inside of here.

86
00:05:12,100 --> 00:05:15,280
The first thing we need to do is read the contents of that file.

87
00:05:15,370 --> 00:05:18,280
So I'll say contents is.

88
00:05:19,270 --> 00:05:21,190
Await read file.

89
00:05:21,940 --> 00:05:23,890
Messages dot Json.

90
00:05:23,920 --> 00:05:27,610
And then as a second argument, I'm going to put in a string of UTF eight.

91
00:05:27,880 --> 00:05:33,190
UTF eight tells readfile exactly what format or what encoding this file uses.

92
00:05:33,190 --> 00:05:35,560
So we're going to get contents as a string.

93
00:05:35,560 --> 00:05:38,410
That is just the plain contents of that whole file.

94
00:05:38,440 --> 00:05:39,940
Remember that is Json.

95
00:05:39,940 --> 00:05:41,170
It is a plain string.

96
00:05:41,170 --> 00:05:44,770
So we then need to parse that string into an object.

97
00:05:44,980 --> 00:05:46,300
So to parse it.

98
00:05:47,040 --> 00:05:53,250
I'm going to say messages is Json dot parse contents like so.

99
00:05:55,130 --> 00:05:57,950
So now messages as a very quick example, don't write out this code.

100
00:05:57,950 --> 00:05:59,260
This is just an example.

101
00:05:59,270 --> 00:06:05,390
I would expect messages at this point in time to be an object with some keys that are IDs and then the

102
00:06:05,390 --> 00:06:10,850
values are going to be a message object that has an ID and a content property.

103
00:06:10,850 --> 00:06:13,370
So something like that, That's my expectation.

104
00:06:15,430 --> 00:06:19,660
So now that we've got that big object of all of our different messages, we're going to look up the

105
00:06:19,660 --> 00:06:25,030
very particular ID that the user was looking for inside that messages object and return whatever object

106
00:06:25,030 --> 00:06:25,490
is there.

107
00:06:25,510 --> 00:06:28,780
So we will return messages at ID.

108
00:06:30,350 --> 00:06:30,620
All right.

109
00:06:30,620 --> 00:06:33,500
So that's pretty much it for the find one implementation.

110
00:06:33,710 --> 00:06:38,300
Let's take a pause right here and start to work on find all and create in just a moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,230 --> 00:00:04,670
Next up, let's take care of find all.

2
00:00:04,690 --> 00:00:09,180
So, as you guessed, the goal of this function is to open up that messages Json file.

3
00:00:09,190 --> 00:00:13,270
We're going to pull out the big object of all of our different messages, and then we're going to just

4
00:00:13,270 --> 00:00:14,530
return the entire object.

5
00:00:14,530 --> 00:00:15,520
That's pretty much it.

6
00:00:15,520 --> 00:00:17,310
So it should be pretty straightforward.

7
00:00:17,350 --> 00:00:22,780
So to get started inside of find all, I'm going to first basically do exactly what we did just a moment

8
00:00:22,780 --> 00:00:23,050
ago.

9
00:00:23,080 --> 00:00:26,710
We're going to open up the entire contents of that file and read everything inside of it.

10
00:00:26,740 --> 00:00:30,940
So we'll say contents is await read file.

11
00:00:31,650 --> 00:00:34,170
Messages dot Json and UTF eight.

12
00:00:35,970 --> 00:00:38,340
We'll then say const messages.

13
00:00:38,340 --> 00:00:40,290
So this is our big object with messages.

14
00:00:40,290 --> 00:00:44,400
Inside of it is Json.parse contents.

15
00:00:45,450 --> 00:00:48,240
And then finally, we will return messages.

16
00:00:48,480 --> 00:00:49,440
And that's all.

17
00:00:51,570 --> 00:00:53,850
So then finally on to create.

18
00:00:53,880 --> 00:00:55,940
This one's going to be a little bit more intense.

19
00:00:55,950 --> 00:01:00,600
So we're going to have to once again open up the file, read the contents inside there.

20
00:01:00,630 --> 00:01:05,910
We're then going to randomly generate an ID, and for us, a randomly generated ID is just going to

21
00:01:05,910 --> 00:01:07,590
be a plain integer number.

22
00:01:08,510 --> 00:01:14,780
We're then going to add in this new message into our list of messages or the entire big object of messages.

23
00:01:14,780 --> 00:01:16,880
So let's just put together the implementation.

24
00:01:17,960 --> 00:01:18,170
Okay.

25
00:01:18,200 --> 00:01:20,960
Once again, we're going to first read the entire contents.

26
00:01:23,160 --> 00:01:24,960
Of the messages json file.

27
00:01:27,040 --> 00:01:29,440
I'll get my big object full of messages.

28
00:01:33,510 --> 00:01:35,700
We're then going to randomly generate a brand new ID.

29
00:01:36,000 --> 00:01:43,500
So we'll say const ID is going to be math.random times 999.

30
00:01:44,150 --> 00:01:48,020
We're then going to round that down because that will give us a decimal number.

31
00:01:48,020 --> 00:01:51,280
We don't want a decimal, we want an integer, really a whole number here.

32
00:01:51,290 --> 00:01:56,210
So I'm going to wrap all that in a set of parentheses and then do a math dot floor.

33
00:01:56,810 --> 00:02:00,050
Now technically we might get an ID of zero out of this.

34
00:02:00,080 --> 00:02:02,450
Not super conventional, but it's totally fine.

35
00:02:04,090 --> 00:02:10,540
So now that we've got that random ID, we're going to take that ID and add in a new object into our

36
00:02:10,540 --> 00:02:12,750
list of messages or that big messages object.

37
00:02:12,760 --> 00:02:16,360
So to just make sure it's super clear what operation we currently need to do.

38
00:02:16,390 --> 00:02:18,840
I'm going to first give you a very quick example right here.

39
00:02:18,850 --> 00:02:21,580
So I'm going to write out what the messages object looks like.

40
00:02:21,580 --> 00:02:27,940
Remember, it looks something like a big object with some keys, and the values are objects that have

41
00:02:27,940 --> 00:02:31,240
an ID and a content property like so.

42
00:02:31,570 --> 00:02:37,940
So our goal is to take this ID and add it in as a new property inside this object.

43
00:02:37,960 --> 00:02:41,740
So let's imagine for a second that we randomly generate an ID of 50.

44
00:02:41,740 --> 00:02:48,970
I want to add in a new key of 50, and I want to assign to that an object with an ID of 50 and a content

45
00:02:49,000 --> 00:02:52,770
of whatever message was just passed in to this function.

46
00:02:52,780 --> 00:02:55,390
So a content of message.

47
00:02:55,420 --> 00:02:58,600
That's the goal we want to add in that record right there.

48
00:02:59,270 --> 00:03:00,470
So to do so.

49
00:03:01,690 --> 00:03:06,280
We will say messages at ID equals.

50
00:03:07,800 --> 00:03:12,660
ID, comma, and then content of message.

51
00:03:13,530 --> 00:03:18,180
You know, now that I am reading this an argument name of message is a little bit misleading because

52
00:03:18,180 --> 00:03:20,040
it's really the content of a message.

53
00:03:20,040 --> 00:03:24,600
I think it might be a little bit more clear if we change that argument name to just be content instead.

54
00:03:25,390 --> 00:03:29,680
Because then we can also use the shortened syntax on this object right here and end up with just ID

55
00:03:29,680 --> 00:03:30,570
and content.

56
00:03:30,590 --> 00:03:33,700
So I think that's probably a little bit more clear as to what's going on here.

57
00:03:35,660 --> 00:03:38,320
So now our entire messages object should be updated.

58
00:03:38,330 --> 00:03:43,490
So we are now going to turn this entire thing back into a string and write it back into our messages.

59
00:03:43,490 --> 00:03:44,690
Dot Json file.

60
00:03:45,050 --> 00:03:49,250
So to do so, we will call await write file.

61
00:03:50,150 --> 00:03:51,270
Messages.

62
00:03:51,580 --> 00:03:54,420
Jason We want to write into that file.

63
00:03:54,440 --> 00:03:58,070
Json.stringify Messages.

64
00:03:59,490 --> 00:04:01,500
And once again, that should be it.

65
00:04:02,720 --> 00:04:05,790
Okay, so we've now got these three different methods put together.

66
00:04:05,810 --> 00:04:07,100
In theory, they all work.

67
00:04:07,100 --> 00:04:10,970
We haven't really tested them yet, but hopefully they're going to work as expected.

68
00:04:11,000 --> 00:04:15,590
Just to be clear, everything we've done inside of here so far is not really nest related code.

69
00:04:15,590 --> 00:04:17,870
This is just some general JavaScript or TypeScript.

70
00:04:17,870 --> 00:04:21,320
So if you are confused about what's going on inside of here, totally fine.

71
00:04:21,320 --> 00:04:24,110
Not really related to what's going on inside this course.

72
00:04:24,440 --> 00:04:29,090
So now that we've got this repository put together, there's just 1 or 2 other things I want to mention

73
00:04:29,090 --> 00:04:34,080
about the repository, and then we're going to start to work on our service very quickly after that.

74
00:04:34,100 --> 00:04:38,570
So once again, let's take a pause right here and continue in just a moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,550 --> 00:00:03,980
Our repository is now just about all complete.

2
00:00:03,980 --> 00:00:06,680
There's still, as I mentioned, 1 or 2 things I want to mention around it.

3
00:00:06,680 --> 00:00:11,140
But for right now, I think it's good enough that we can start to work on our messages service.

4
00:00:11,150 --> 00:00:15,680
So remember, the entire goal of this service is to essentially sit in front of the repository.

5
00:00:15,710 --> 00:00:20,420
Our controller is going to use the service to access data instead of the repository.

6
00:00:20,420 --> 00:00:24,950
Once again, want to repeat that the service is going to look just about identical to the repository.

7
00:00:24,950 --> 00:00:28,910
It's going to seem like the service is pointless, but again, there is a point to it.

8
00:00:29,270 --> 00:00:35,030
So let's open up our messages service file and we're going to start to put together our service implementation.

9
00:00:35,030 --> 00:00:37,310
This will be pretty quick and pretty fast.

10
00:00:37,340 --> 00:00:43,880
So to get started, I'm going to first create and export a class called Messages Service.

11
00:00:45,260 --> 00:00:51,200
This class is definitely going to need a copy of the repository because we need a repository where we

12
00:00:51,200 --> 00:00:54,530
can actually store data and retrieve data from.

13
00:00:54,740 --> 00:00:56,810
So let's import.

14
00:00:58,070 --> 00:01:05,030
The messages repository at the very top from messages dot repository.

15
00:01:07,670 --> 00:01:13,910
And then inside of our class constructor, we're going to make sure that we create a new instance of

16
00:01:13,910 --> 00:01:17,310
messages repository and assign it to a property on this class.

17
00:01:17,330 --> 00:01:19,430
So we're going to say this dot repo.

18
00:01:19,460 --> 00:01:26,570
How about this dot Messages repo short for repository is going to be a new messages repository.

19
00:01:27,280 --> 00:01:29,230
As soon as I do that, we will get a little air here.

20
00:01:29,230 --> 00:01:34,040
That's just because we need to make sure we say that this class is going to have a messages repo property.

21
00:01:34,060 --> 00:01:41,080
So right above the constructor say messages repo will be of type messages repository.

22
00:01:41,210 --> 00:01:47,140
Now, I want to pause right here and I want to point out something extremely important to you in this

23
00:01:47,140 --> 00:01:47,890
service.

24
00:01:47,920 --> 00:01:55,540
We have written in some code where the service is creating its own dependencies.

25
00:01:55,990 --> 00:01:56,620
Messages.

26
00:01:56,620 --> 00:01:59,690
Repository is a dependency of the service.

27
00:01:59,710 --> 00:02:04,720
In other words, the service cannot work correctly unless it has a repository.

28
00:02:05,080 --> 00:02:08,170
So we have set up a dependency between these two classes.

29
00:02:08,840 --> 00:02:11,840
And the service is creating the dependency on its own.

30
00:02:11,900 --> 00:02:15,350
This is something that we do not do in nest.

31
00:02:15,380 --> 00:02:19,520
So I'm going to put a comment right here that says don't do this on real apps.

32
00:02:19,700 --> 00:02:22,220
We do not have any class.

33
00:02:22,220 --> 00:02:25,100
Create its own dependencies inside of a constructor.

34
00:02:25,100 --> 00:02:30,320
Instead, we're going to use a very special system in nest referred to as dependency injection to set

35
00:02:30,320 --> 00:02:32,450
up dependencies between different classes.

36
00:02:32,480 --> 00:02:37,280
The reason that we're not doing that just yet is I want to have a real deep dive on dependency injection

37
00:02:37,280 --> 00:02:39,720
and help you really understand what it's all about.

38
00:02:39,740 --> 00:02:45,080
So this right here is a very temporary line of code that we're going to remove in very short order.

39
00:02:45,080 --> 00:02:46,550
Like in the next couple of videos.

40
00:02:46,580 --> 00:02:52,070
We're going to remove that and rely upon the dependency injection system instead to set up all the different

41
00:02:52,070 --> 00:02:54,530
dependencies between our different classes.

42
00:02:54,770 --> 00:02:55,970
So just keep that in mind.

43
00:02:56,000 --> 00:02:57,620
I'm going to remove that in just a moment.

44
00:02:59,500 --> 00:03:03,730
Okay, so now inside of our service, we can start put together to put together all these different

45
00:03:03,730 --> 00:03:04,420
methods.

46
00:03:04,450 --> 00:03:07,030
They're all going to look very similar to the message repository.

47
00:03:07,030 --> 00:03:11,650
And inside of each one we're going to call the essentially equivalent method on the repository itself.

48
00:03:11,650 --> 00:03:13,750
So let's just take a look at what this looks like.

49
00:03:14,620 --> 00:03:19,960
So inside my service, I'm going to first make an async function called find one that takes an ID that's

50
00:03:19,960 --> 00:03:20,920
going to be a string.

51
00:03:21,100 --> 00:03:24,100
And then inside of here, this is going to be look like it's totally pointless.

52
00:03:24,130 --> 00:03:25,280
Look like it's totally boring.

53
00:03:25,300 --> 00:03:30,040
We're going to return this dot messages repo dot.

54
00:03:30,040 --> 00:03:31,690
Find one ID.

55
00:03:32,650 --> 00:03:34,990
And in this case, you know, put on the async right there.

56
00:03:34,990 --> 00:03:36,280
We actually do not need that at all.

57
00:03:36,280 --> 00:03:38,980
It can be just find one with a return.

58
00:03:40,030 --> 00:03:44,600
So this is what I was talking about when I said it's going to seem like services are pointless.

59
00:03:44,620 --> 00:03:48,850
All the service does is turn around and call the identical method on the repo.

60
00:03:49,060 --> 00:03:53,290
So this is where your start going to probably start to say, why are we making a service at all?

61
00:03:53,290 --> 00:03:54,460
What is the point of this thing?

62
00:03:54,490 --> 00:03:59,050
As I mentioned, 4 or 5 times now, trust me, it's all going to become clear on why these services

63
00:03:59,050 --> 00:04:00,280
exist over time.

64
00:04:00,790 --> 00:04:05,140
So let's now repeat that process or find all and create.

65
00:04:05,350 --> 00:04:08,110
So I'll do a find all inside of here.

66
00:04:08,110 --> 00:04:11,410
We're going to return this dot messages repo dot find all.

67
00:04:12,700 --> 00:04:12,840
Oop.

68
00:04:12,910 --> 00:04:14,110
No semicolon right there.

69
00:04:14,930 --> 00:04:16,130
And create.

70
00:04:16,340 --> 00:04:19,640
So create will take in some content that will be a string.

71
00:04:21,990 --> 00:04:28,890
And then we will return this dot Messages Repo dot Create content.

72
00:04:29,690 --> 00:04:30,920
And that's pretty much it.

73
00:04:30,920 --> 00:04:36,580
That is our service, with the one exception of this kind of little shortcut right here inside the constructor.

74
00:04:36,590 --> 00:04:39,530
This is what a service very commonly will look like.

75
00:04:41,070 --> 00:04:41,400
All right.

76
00:04:41,400 --> 00:04:45,450
So now that we've got our service put together, the last thing we have to do is connect the service

77
00:04:45,450 --> 00:04:46,500
to our controller.

78
00:04:46,500 --> 00:04:49,350
And inside the controller we'll then call some appropriate method.

79
00:04:49,350 --> 00:04:54,720
Whenever someone makes a request to find a very particular message or call them or add a new one in.

80
00:04:54,900 --> 00:04:56,880
Let's take care of that last step in the next video.

81
00:04:56,880 --> 00:05:00,450
And I think we'll be able to do a manual test of our entire application.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1
00:00:01,310 --> 00:00:04,370
We are all done with our messages, service and the repository.

2
00:00:04,370 --> 00:00:08,690
So now we need to create an instance of this service and give it to the controller.

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:14,090
The controller is then going to receive incoming request to our three different route handlers and depending

4
00:00:14,090 --> 00:00:19,430
upon which handler is called, the controller is going to answer the request by using some data that

5
00:00:19,430 --> 00:00:21,140
it gets from the service.

6
00:00:21,290 --> 00:00:22,820
So let's go back over to our editor.

7
00:00:22,820 --> 00:00:24,350
We're going to find the controller file.

8
00:00:24,350 --> 00:00:29,600
We're going to make sure that this thing gets an instance of the service and uses it to respond to requests.

9
00:00:30,560 --> 00:00:30,770
Okay.

10
00:00:30,770 --> 00:00:32,240
So back over inside my editor.

11
00:00:32,480 --> 00:00:35,450
I'm going to find my messages controller file.

12
00:00:35,480 --> 00:00:36,620
Here it is right here.

13
00:00:36,950 --> 00:00:39,530
I'm then going to import my service at the very top.

14
00:00:39,530 --> 00:00:41,870
So messages Service.

15
00:00:45,520 --> 00:00:50,800
Then inside the controller, we're going to create an instance of that service in the same way that

16
00:00:50,800 --> 00:00:53,990
we created an instance, the repository inside the service itself.

17
00:00:54,010 --> 00:00:59,190
In other words, we're going to give the controller a service using some not very good code.

18
00:00:59,200 --> 00:01:04,990
So we're going to once again have the controller create a copy of the service in a not good way.

19
00:01:05,020 --> 00:01:10,060
We are going to come back very shortly and refactor this to use the way that we're supposed to do things

20
00:01:10,060 --> 00:01:10,990
inside of Nest.

21
00:01:11,020 --> 00:01:13,780
That's by using the dependency injection system.

22
00:01:14,370 --> 00:01:20,520
So back inside my controller, I'm going to say that my controller is going to have a messages service

23
00:01:20,520 --> 00:01:24,810
property that will be an instance of messages service.

24
00:01:26,310 --> 00:01:27,690
I'll then define the constructor.

25
00:01:28,710 --> 00:01:35,700
I'll say that the messages service property is going to be an instance of messages, service messages

26
00:01:35,700 --> 00:01:36,470
with an S.

27
00:01:37,090 --> 00:01:43,150
And once again, I'm going to put a comment right here that says don't do this on real app, use dependency

28
00:01:43,150 --> 00:01:43,960
injection.

29
00:01:43,960 --> 00:01:46,570
And again, we're going to come back and fix this in two seconds.

30
00:01:47,130 --> 00:01:51,100
So now inside of all three of our different route handlers, we can put in some code that's going to

31
00:01:51,100 --> 00:01:55,750
use that service to either list all messages, create one or get one.

32
00:01:56,640 --> 00:02:01,230
So these methods are going to end up looking very simple and straightforward on list.

33
00:02:01,260 --> 00:02:08,190
We will return this dot messages service dot find all and that's pretty much it.

34
00:02:08,340 --> 00:02:11,070
Don't forget that the key here is that return statement.

35
00:02:11,100 --> 00:02:15,810
If we don't return something from our method handler or our route handler, then Nest is not going to

36
00:02:15,810 --> 00:02:18,890
return or send anything back to whoever made the request.

37
00:02:18,900 --> 00:02:23,520
So if we just call, find all like that, we're not going to send anything back to whoever made the

38
00:02:23,520 --> 00:02:24,500
request to us.

39
00:02:24,510 --> 00:02:28,710
When we call, find all we need to make sure we return it so it actually gets sent back.

40
00:02:30,360 --> 00:02:34,140
We can then do something very similar inside of our other two route handlers as well.

41
00:02:34,230 --> 00:02:37,770
So inside of create message, I'm going to return.

42
00:02:38,910 --> 00:02:43,800
This dot messages service dot create and remember we set up our.

43
00:02:44,500 --> 00:02:49,420
Create method to take in a single string, and that's supposed to be the content that we want to add

44
00:02:49,420 --> 00:02:50,290
to the message.

45
00:02:51,130 --> 00:02:56,230
The input to this route handler itself is the create message DTO and if you do a command, click on

46
00:02:56,260 --> 00:02:56,380
that.

47
00:02:56,380 --> 00:03:01,990
DTO remember that we said that the incoming body object should have a content property.

48
00:03:02,610 --> 00:03:07,650
So when we call the create method right here, we want to pass in body dot content.

49
00:03:07,860 --> 00:03:13,380
So take the content property of the incoming request body, pass it off to create and that will be used

50
00:03:13,380 --> 00:03:15,180
as the actual message.

51
00:03:16,270 --> 00:03:18,220
And then finally get message down here.

52
00:03:18,220 --> 00:03:19,510
So we will return.

53
00:03:21,140 --> 00:03:22,350
Messages service.

54
00:03:22,370 --> 00:03:23,660
Find one.

55
00:03:23,840 --> 00:03:27,890
And once again, remember, we set up this method to assume that we're going to pass in an ID.

56
00:03:28,860 --> 00:03:33,060
So I will pass in the ID that comes in on the incoming request.

57
00:03:34,240 --> 00:03:34,540
Okay.

58
00:03:34,540 --> 00:03:35,800
So that should be it.

59
00:03:36,400 --> 00:03:40,390
So we should now be able to open up our client and test out our application.

60
00:03:40,420 --> 00:03:45,440
Before we do so, however, I'm going to open up my file explorer on the left hand side over here.

61
00:03:45,460 --> 00:03:50,320
I would like you to double check, make sure that you've got a messages dot Json file inside of here.

62
00:03:50,480 --> 00:03:53,560
So remember, this is the file where we're going to write all of our data.

63
00:03:55,230 --> 00:04:00,210
Once you make sure that you've got this file, open it up and would like you to add in just a plain

64
00:04:00,210 --> 00:04:01,830
empty object like so.

65
00:04:02,070 --> 00:04:06,870
If you don't put in an empty object, some of the Json parsing stuff is not going to work as expected.

66
00:04:06,900 --> 00:04:11,730
I know this is kind of a silly limitation, but again, it's just a simple demonstration application.

67
00:04:12,120 --> 00:04:14,400
So make sure you get an empty object inside there.

68
00:04:14,430 --> 00:04:16,980
Save the file and then go ahead and close it.

69
00:04:18,090 --> 00:04:21,540
Okay, So now, really, this time we're ready to test out our application.

70
00:04:21,690 --> 00:04:26,100
Make sure your server is up and running and that you don't have any errors at your terminal.

71
00:04:26,460 --> 00:04:28,800
Then go ahead and open up your client.

72
00:04:28,800 --> 00:04:31,200
So postman or whatever else you want to use.

73
00:04:31,710 --> 00:04:36,000
I'm then going to first begin by trying to list all my different messages, so I'm going to send that

74
00:04:36,000 --> 00:04:36,840
request off.

75
00:04:37,630 --> 00:04:40,660
And get back as a response, an empty object.

76
00:04:40,690 --> 00:04:43,900
That means that we currently have no messages whatsoever.

77
00:04:43,930 --> 00:04:48,100
And before I say anything else, if you see some kind of error over here, if you see something that

78
00:04:48,100 --> 00:04:51,340
says 500 as the status code and internal server error.

79
00:04:51,370 --> 00:04:55,510
I would encourage you to go back over to your terminal and see what the error message says.

80
00:04:55,510 --> 00:04:59,980
You will see an error over here that has some more descriptive stuff about what just happened.

81
00:05:01,640 --> 00:05:05,390
Assuming that you did get back some good outputs like that empty object.

82
00:05:05,480 --> 00:05:08,960
Let's now try to create a message and then try to fetch it.

83
00:05:09,140 --> 00:05:14,270
So I'm going to send a post request and my content from my message will be Asdf.

84
00:05:14,300 --> 00:05:15,530
I'm going to send that off.

85
00:05:15,970 --> 00:05:17,410
It looks like it was created.

86
00:05:17,420 --> 00:05:20,450
So now if I once again get all messages.

87
00:05:21,260 --> 00:05:23,450
I'll now see that object right there.

88
00:05:23,660 --> 00:05:24,740
So there's my message.

89
00:05:24,740 --> 00:05:29,660
It was given a random ID of 885 and its content is Asdf.

90
00:05:30,560 --> 00:05:31,250
Awesome.

91
00:05:32,550 --> 00:05:36,030
So now we should also be able to fetch a very particular message.

92
00:05:36,150 --> 00:05:42,420
So I'm going to go to my request handler or my request down here where I'm going to make a get request

93
00:05:42,420 --> 00:05:46,920
to messages slash and then the ID of the message that I want to retrieve.

94
00:05:47,630 --> 00:05:52,550
Now, at this point in time, my message, the one I just created, has an ID of eight eight, five.

95
00:05:52,580 --> 00:05:55,370
So I'm going to make a request to slash messages.

96
00:05:55,370 --> 00:05:56,960
Slash eight, eight, five.

97
00:05:57,080 --> 00:06:01,970
You will need to update that ID right there to reflect whatever ID was randomly assigned to you because

98
00:06:01,970 --> 00:06:06,320
you're definitely going to have or it's very likely, I should say, that you're going to have an ID

99
00:06:06,320 --> 00:06:07,250
different than mine.

100
00:06:07,250 --> 00:06:10,160
So make sure you put whatever ID was generated for you right there.

101
00:06:10,160 --> 00:06:13,220
Instead, I'm then going to send off that request.

102
00:06:14,430 --> 00:06:17,250
And looks like I was able to fetch that one as well.

103
00:06:17,910 --> 00:06:19,890
Okay, well, this is looking pretty good.

104
00:06:19,890 --> 00:06:22,530
So we are now able to create a message.

105
00:06:22,530 --> 00:06:23,970
I'm going to create another one right now.

106
00:06:26,080 --> 00:06:32,020
We are able to list all messages and we can get a very particular message.

107
00:06:33,870 --> 00:06:36,630
So I'd say that this is a pretty good success so far.

108
00:06:36,810 --> 00:06:40,250
But as I mentioned, still a couple of shortcomings.

109
00:06:40,260 --> 00:06:42,990
First, we need to do that refactor that I promised.

110
00:06:43,380 --> 00:06:48,750
The other thing I want to point out here is that if we try to fetch a particular message using an ID

111
00:06:48,750 --> 00:06:55,860
that definitely doesn't exist, we get back a status code of 200 whenever we try to find something on

112
00:06:55,860 --> 00:07:01,350
a remote API and we are not able to find it, I would really expect to get back a 404 or something like

113
00:07:01,350 --> 00:07:01,880
that.

114
00:07:01,890 --> 00:07:06,570
So I think it's pretty clear that although it looks like everything is working, maybe we still need

115
00:07:06,570 --> 00:07:08,850
to handle some edge cases a little bit better.

116
00:07:08,970 --> 00:07:12,540
So let's take care of these last little steps in the next couple of videos.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:04,320
We still have a couple of things to fix up, so let's get to it.

2
00:00:04,350 --> 00:00:08,940
The first thing I'd like to address is the fact that if we ever try to make request to fetch a message

3
00:00:08,940 --> 00:00:14,730
with an ID that does not exist, we get back a request or a response with a status code of 200.

4
00:00:14,760 --> 00:00:19,800
We should be sending back a status code of 404 and say sorry, but we were not able to find a message

5
00:00:19,800 --> 00:00:21,120
with that particular ID.

6
00:00:21,630 --> 00:00:25,110
So to do so, we're going to go back to our controller file.

7
00:00:26,270 --> 00:00:27,590
So here's my messages.

8
00:00:27,590 --> 00:00:29,120
Controllers file.

9
00:00:29,940 --> 00:00:34,860
Then at the very top, I'm going to find our import statement from Nestjs Common.

10
00:00:35,190 --> 00:00:40,530
And in addition to all these other things we are importing, we're going to import one last thing called

11
00:00:40,530 --> 00:00:42,750
NotFound exception.

12
00:00:43,950 --> 00:00:46,080
And we'll discuss what this thing is in just a second.

13
00:00:46,110 --> 00:00:51,420
First, let's go back to down to the very bottom of this file and find our route handler that deals

14
00:00:51,420 --> 00:00:54,960
with a git request that's looking for a very particular message.

15
00:00:55,140 --> 00:01:00,300
So inside this route handler, instead of always returning the result of calling, find one immediately.

16
00:01:00,330 --> 00:01:04,350
We're going to instead assign the result to a new variable called message.

17
00:01:04,740 --> 00:01:10,140
As soon as we do so, remember that find one is returning a promise to us because it takes some amount

18
00:01:10,140 --> 00:01:14,070
of time to open up that messages dot Json file and read its contents.

19
00:01:14,250 --> 00:01:17,580
So we need to make sure that we await find one.

20
00:01:18,810 --> 00:01:24,750
As soon as we put in that await, we need to also make sure we add in async to the method itself.

21
00:01:26,230 --> 00:01:31,600
So now message is going to be either a message that we found inside of our file or it's going to be

22
00:01:31,600 --> 00:01:32,260
undefined.

23
00:01:32,260 --> 00:01:34,960
If the user asked for a message that does not exist.

24
00:01:35,050 --> 00:01:38,810
So we're going to add in a check to see if we actually found a message.

25
00:01:38,830 --> 00:01:45,190
We're going to say if there is not a message or if that value is falsy, then we're going to immediately

26
00:01:45,190 --> 00:01:49,720
throw a new not found exception.

27
00:01:51,220 --> 00:01:55,190
And I'm going to give this thing a string describing exactly what went wrong.

28
00:01:55,210 --> 00:01:58,090
I'm going to say message not found.

29
00:01:59,700 --> 00:02:00,930
Then after that check.

30
00:02:01,740 --> 00:02:02,340
We will.

31
00:02:02,340 --> 00:02:06,390
Assuming that we did find a message, we will return the message that we found.

32
00:02:07,600 --> 00:02:09,340
Okay, so let's save this file now.

33
00:02:09,370 --> 00:02:10,480
Go test this out.

34
00:02:10,510 --> 00:02:15,790
See what happens when we try to request a message that does not exist and see what is going to happen.

35
00:02:16,060 --> 00:02:18,160
So I'll go back over to my client.

36
00:02:20,010 --> 00:02:23,250
I'm going to try to make a request for a message that definitely doesn't exist.

37
00:02:23,430 --> 00:02:29,850
And now when we do that, I get back much more appropriately, a status code of 404, along with a really

38
00:02:29,850 --> 00:02:32,940
nice response that describes exactly what occurred.

39
00:02:32,970 --> 00:02:38,730
So we've got a distinct error message of not found and a message that says Sorry, but we were not able

40
00:02:38,730 --> 00:02:40,290
to find that particular thing.

41
00:02:40,470 --> 00:02:45,600
So this is a much better response to send back if we are not able to find a particular message.

42
00:02:46,660 --> 00:02:48,710
So it looks like everything is working much better.

43
00:02:48,730 --> 00:02:51,670
So now let's figure out what this NotFound exception thing is.

44
00:02:51,790 --> 00:02:55,720
This is a kind of error that is defined inside of Nest itself.

45
00:02:55,750 --> 00:03:00,970
There are a couple of errors that nest defines, and if you ever throw them during a request cycle,

46
00:03:00,970 --> 00:03:06,370
then Nest is going to automatically capture that error and turn it into a very nice looking response

47
00:03:06,370 --> 00:03:07,810
to send back to the user.

48
00:03:08,440 --> 00:03:13,330
So Nest captured the area that we just threw right here, captures that error and it's going to take

49
00:03:13,330 --> 00:03:14,810
some information out of the error.

50
00:03:14,830 --> 00:03:17,890
Specifically, the status code, which ends up being 404.

51
00:03:17,920 --> 00:03:21,160
It's also going to get the message that we put in as a string right there.

52
00:03:22,500 --> 00:03:27,330
In addition to not found exception, there are a couple of other exceptions that are defined inside

53
00:03:27,330 --> 00:03:28,590
the common library.

54
00:03:28,830 --> 00:03:33,870
You can either take a look at the documentation to get a full list or a much better trick for this.

55
00:03:33,900 --> 00:03:38,490
If you go back up to the very top and find our import statement, do a command or control.

56
00:03:38,490 --> 00:03:44,310
If you're on Windows, click on NotFound exception that will take you to the definition of this class.

57
00:03:45,110 --> 00:03:52,220
If you then right click on that file or that tab right there and go to reveal in sidebar, it will take

58
00:03:52,220 --> 00:03:56,150
you to a folder where all these different kinds of exceptions are defined.

59
00:03:56,270 --> 00:04:00,620
So these are all the different kinds of exceptions that Nest defines for us.

60
00:04:00,650 --> 00:04:05,960
You can throw any of these exceptions and Nest will automatically convert them into a appropriate status

61
00:04:05,960 --> 00:04:09,080
code, an appropriate error message to send back to the user.

62
00:04:10,440 --> 00:04:16,140
Each of these different exceptions are kind of following the pattern set up by the Http standard.

63
00:04:16,140 --> 00:04:20,790
So all the different common exceptions or all the different kind of errors that we get around Http requests

64
00:04:20,790 --> 00:04:30,780
such as 404 not found 400 bad request, is it 401 or 403 forbidden 500 internal error 503 timeout.

65
00:04:30,780 --> 00:04:31,680
I think it is.

66
00:04:31,710 --> 00:04:37,470
All these very common Http status codes are wrapped up inside these different exceptions.

67
00:04:37,500 --> 00:04:40,770
The ones we're probably gonna use the most are not found exception.

68
00:04:40,800 --> 00:04:43,470
The bad request exception.

69
00:04:44,280 --> 00:04:47,460
A gateway timeout in some rare cases.

70
00:04:47,460 --> 00:04:51,510
And then also I frequently end up using what is it?

71
00:04:53,980 --> 00:04:54,790
Unauthorized.

72
00:04:54,790 --> 00:04:55,270
There we go.

73
00:04:55,510 --> 00:04:57,990
Unauthorized and unprocessable entity.

74
00:04:58,000 --> 00:05:02,590
So these are all exceptions we're going to use rather frequently when we're trying to return some kind

75
00:05:02,590 --> 00:05:03,700
of error to the user.

76
00:05:04,780 --> 00:05:05,140
All right.

77
00:05:05,140 --> 00:05:06,280
So this looks pretty good.

78
00:05:06,280 --> 00:05:10,720
We're now handling the case in which the user asks for a message that does not exist in a slightly better

79
00:05:10,720 --> 00:05:11,410
fashion.

80
00:05:11,770 --> 00:05:13,180
So let's pause right here.

81
00:05:13,180 --> 00:05:17,920
And in the next video, we're going to start to get into the topic of dependency injection and solve

82
00:05:17,920 --> 00:05:19,210
all this stuff inside the constructor.

83
00:05:19,210 --> 00:05:21,970
That said specifically, don't do so.

84
00:05:21,970 --> 00:05:23,230
This will be a lot of fun.

85
00:05:23,230 --> 00:05:26,320
Kind of let's give it a shot in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,840 --> 00:00:04,260
The last thing that we have to take care of inside of our application is fix up some of these comments

2
00:00:04,260 --> 00:00:06,180
around our service and our controller.

3
00:00:06,180 --> 00:00:11,040
So left in these comments and I said, don't write code like this on real application, use the dependency

4
00:00:11,040 --> 00:00:12,320
injection system instead.

5
00:00:12,330 --> 00:00:16,140
So in this video, in the next couple, we're going to start to focus on dependency injection.

6
00:00:16,290 --> 00:00:20,670
Everything in Nest revolves around this system, everything.

7
00:00:20,670 --> 00:00:23,730
It's extremely important for you to understand dependency injection.

8
00:00:23,730 --> 00:00:25,080
But here's the downside.

9
00:00:25,110 --> 00:00:27,810
Dependency injection is a little bit complicated.

10
00:00:27,810 --> 00:00:34,520
And what's more, beyond just being complicated, it can be very challenging to understand why it exists.

11
00:00:34,560 --> 00:00:39,310
So rather than just showing you dependency injection, above all, what I really want you to understand

12
00:00:39,310 --> 00:00:42,000
in this course is why this system exists.

13
00:00:42,000 --> 00:00:43,200
I can show you without a doubt.

14
00:00:43,200 --> 00:00:47,370
And you can read tons of tutorials that say, Hey, here's how you do dependency injection.

15
00:00:47,370 --> 00:00:48,270
That's not important.

16
00:00:48,270 --> 00:00:50,880
Understanding how to do it, not important.

17
00:00:50,880 --> 00:00:53,820
What is important is understanding why it exists at all.

18
00:00:54,030 --> 00:00:55,230
So that's what we're going to focus on.

19
00:00:55,230 --> 00:00:58,740
First, we're going to start to talk about why dependency injection exists.

20
00:00:58,920 --> 00:01:03,040
To do so, we're going to take a look at a couple of different diagrams over the next couple of videos.

21
00:01:03,040 --> 00:01:04,210
So let's get to it.

22
00:01:04,420 --> 00:01:08,200
The first thing I want to do is give you a very quick reminder inside of our application right now,

23
00:01:08,200 --> 00:01:10,600
we've got our controller service and repository.

24
00:01:10,630 --> 00:01:15,220
There is a very clear dependency or hierarchy between these different classes.

25
00:01:15,370 --> 00:01:19,180
So the service depends upon the repository to work correctly.

26
00:01:19,180 --> 00:01:23,140
If that repository did not exist, our service would not work correctly.

27
00:01:24,300 --> 00:01:27,300
Likewise, the controller depends upon the service.

28
00:01:27,330 --> 00:01:30,940
If the service did not exist, the controller would not work correctly.

29
00:01:30,960 --> 00:01:35,370
So again, between these three different classes there are very clear dependencies.

30
00:01:35,520 --> 00:01:42,870
What's more is that right now our controller and our service are creating these dependencies on their

31
00:01:42,870 --> 00:01:43,310
own.

32
00:01:43,320 --> 00:01:50,100
So whenever we create an instance of our messages service, that class is automatically on its own,

33
00:01:50,100 --> 00:01:52,080
creating its own dependency.

34
00:01:52,170 --> 00:01:53,490
Same thing for the controller.

35
00:01:53,490 --> 00:01:57,390
Whenever we create an instance of the controller, it is creating its own dependency.

36
00:01:58,760 --> 00:02:00,590
Okay, so now we got that review out of the way.

37
00:02:00,830 --> 00:02:05,120
Let's start to take a look at some of the reasons that dependency injection exists.

38
00:02:05,300 --> 00:02:09,510
So the first thing we're going to discuss is the inversion of control principle.

39
00:02:09,530 --> 00:02:14,870
This is an idea in software engineering that says that if you follow this principle, it might be a

40
00:02:14,870 --> 00:02:18,680
little bit easier to build reusable code or write reusable code.

41
00:02:18,770 --> 00:02:23,600
I'm going to give you some more reasons on why this in principle is really important in just a minute.

42
00:02:23,600 --> 00:02:26,120
But for right now, let's just focus on the principle itself.

43
00:02:26,210 --> 00:02:32,180
So this principle says that if you want to have reusable code, you should write classes that do not

44
00:02:32,180 --> 00:02:35,900
create instances of their own dependencies on their own.

45
00:02:36,140 --> 00:02:42,290
In other words, the inversion of control principle says that the code that we have written right here

46
00:02:42,290 --> 00:02:45,230
is bad and that we should not do stuff like this.

47
00:02:45,950 --> 00:02:50,960
So we should not try to create our dependencies on our own inside of a class.

48
00:02:51,260 --> 00:02:54,710
So immediately you might start to say, well, how else would we do that?

49
00:02:54,740 --> 00:02:55,760
Good question.

50
00:02:55,790 --> 00:03:00,080
Let's take a look at a couple of different versions of our code and try to figure out a couple of different

51
00:03:00,080 --> 00:03:04,010
ways to rewrite it to better conform to the inversion of control principle.

52
00:03:04,910 --> 00:03:05,090
Okay.

53
00:03:05,090 --> 00:03:07,050
So we're going to take a look at three different copies.

54
00:03:07,070 --> 00:03:11,450
This is the code that we currently have inside of our service, and I've labeled it as bad because we

55
00:03:11,450 --> 00:03:14,780
are not following the inversion of control principle right now.

56
00:03:14,810 --> 00:03:21,140
Our messages service, whenever we create a copy of it, is creating its own copy of the messages repository

57
00:03:21,140 --> 00:03:21,800
as well.

58
00:03:21,800 --> 00:03:23,870
So not following inversion of control.

59
00:03:23,870 --> 00:03:25,070
So I'm calling it bad.

60
00:03:26,830 --> 00:03:32,020
If we wanted to improve this code a little bit and make it slightly better, then rather than having

61
00:03:32,020 --> 00:03:38,380
the messages service create its own dependency, we could set it up so that it receives its dependency

62
00:03:38,380 --> 00:03:40,080
as an argument to the constructor.

63
00:03:40,090 --> 00:03:44,050
So take a careful look at this code right here and compare it against what we currently have inside

64
00:03:44,050 --> 00:03:45,360
of our existing service.

65
00:03:45,370 --> 00:03:49,030
I would even encourage you to pause the video and study this code very closely.

66
00:03:49,210 --> 00:03:54,850
So in this snippet, which is arguably better than what we had before, we would say that whenever we

67
00:03:54,880 --> 00:04:01,900
create an instance of messages service, we would pass in a copy an already existing copy of the messages

68
00:04:01,900 --> 00:04:02,830
repository.

69
00:04:04,520 --> 00:04:06,860
So we create this repository ahead of time.

70
00:04:06,860 --> 00:04:10,130
And then whenever we want a service, we're going to pass it in to the service.

71
00:04:10,710 --> 00:04:12,180
Why is this better?

72
00:04:12,450 --> 00:04:14,170
I'll tell you why in just a moment.

73
00:04:14,190 --> 00:04:16,380
First, last diagram over here on the right.

74
00:04:16,590 --> 00:04:18,390
So we've now seen the bad.

75
00:04:18,420 --> 00:04:24,150
We've seen slightly better, but we can even make this snippet better than it currently is right now.

76
00:04:24,150 --> 00:04:30,780
The one downside to Messages service is that it always relies upon specifically a copy of messages repository

77
00:04:30,780 --> 00:04:32,520
being passed into the constructor.

78
00:04:32,520 --> 00:04:36,540
So we always have to create specifically messages repository.

79
00:04:36,870 --> 00:04:39,630
So let's take a look at the best solution over here.

80
00:04:41,150 --> 00:04:41,420
Okay.

81
00:04:41,420 --> 00:04:43,130
So this one is definitely the most complicated.

82
00:04:43,130 --> 00:04:48,290
So again, I would encourage you to probably pause this video and take a look at this code snippet and

83
00:04:48,290 --> 00:04:50,120
really think about what it does for us.

84
00:04:52,330 --> 00:04:52,630
All right.

85
00:04:52,630 --> 00:04:58,960
So in this snippet, we've got our messages service still and we still are expecting to be given our

86
00:04:58,960 --> 00:04:59,770
dependency.

87
00:04:59,770 --> 00:05:06,400
But this time around, rather than specifically wanting to get a copy of messages repository, we have

88
00:05:06,400 --> 00:05:10,330
instead defined an interface called simply repository.

89
00:05:10,860 --> 00:05:16,500
And we said that whenever you create a copy or an instance of messages service, you must provide an

90
00:05:16,500 --> 00:05:19,890
object that satisfies the repository interface.

91
00:05:20,510 --> 00:05:27,920
So now the reason this is so good, the reason this is best is that we do not rely upon getting exactly

92
00:05:27,920 --> 00:05:29,340
the messages repository.

93
00:05:29,360 --> 00:05:32,990
Instead, our code can work perfectly fine as long as you pass it.

94
00:05:32,990 --> 00:05:35,820
Any object that satisfies this interface.

95
00:05:35,840 --> 00:05:37,760
It could be the messages repository.

96
00:05:37,790 --> 00:05:41,360
It could be a totally different kind of repository with a totally different implementation.

97
00:05:42,090 --> 00:05:47,310
Now, let me pause right there, because your first question might be, why would that ever be useful?

98
00:05:47,340 --> 00:05:52,500
Why would we ever want to give the messaging service something besides the repository it was designed

99
00:05:52,500 --> 00:05:53,160
to use?

100
00:05:53,190 --> 00:05:54,820
Very good question.

101
00:05:54,840 --> 00:05:56,310
I'm going to assume you actually asked that.

102
00:05:58,370 --> 00:05:58,640
Okay.

103
00:05:58,640 --> 00:06:01,580
So this is why the good case is so good.

104
00:06:02,300 --> 00:06:07,730
When we have our code set up with an interface like this, as opposed to a very direct reference to

105
00:06:07,730 --> 00:06:11,620
some other class, we can start to do really interesting things.

106
00:06:11,630 --> 00:06:13,070
This is just one example.

107
00:06:13,070 --> 00:06:18,020
There are many other scenarios where having our dependencies come in or be defined as an interface is

108
00:06:18,020 --> 00:06:18,440
really good.

109
00:06:18,440 --> 00:06:20,450
This is just one particular case.

110
00:06:20,750 --> 00:06:26,270
So let's imagine that we are doing active development or maybe even deploying our application.

111
00:06:27,250 --> 00:06:29,470
And we are following the good case.

112
00:06:29,470 --> 00:06:33,670
So we're kind of imagining that we are writing good code right now or following the inversion of control

113
00:06:33,670 --> 00:06:35,080
principle very closely.

114
00:06:35,260 --> 00:06:41,710
So if we were doing so and we had a class of messages service that was expecting or defined its constructor

115
00:06:41,710 --> 00:06:46,360
using that interface for the argument, then that class would effectively be saying to the rest of the

116
00:06:46,360 --> 00:06:52,450
application, Hey, rest of the application, I need something that has a defined find one, find all

117
00:06:52,450 --> 00:06:53,260
and create method.

118
00:06:53,260 --> 00:06:58,240
If you give me something, if you give me an object that has those methods, I will work somewhat correctly.

119
00:06:59,730 --> 00:07:05,220
So then in production, we would want to have or give this thing a repository that wrote to a file on

120
00:07:05,220 --> 00:07:09,270
the hard drive because of course in production we actually want to store users messages and so on.

121
00:07:09,480 --> 00:07:16,140
So in the production environment, we could give this service the actual real true messages repository.

122
00:07:16,170 --> 00:07:19,290
This thing has find one, find all and the create method.

123
00:07:19,290 --> 00:07:22,110
It takes all these messages and saves them to a file on the hard drive.

124
00:07:22,110 --> 00:07:24,450
It has the perfect correct implementation.

125
00:07:24,600 --> 00:07:30,720
The one downside to messages repository, however, just is a very small side note is that all the code

126
00:07:30,720 --> 00:07:33,350
inside their writes to a file on the hard disk.

127
00:07:33,360 --> 00:07:39,330
So to store a message to save one to fetch one, it takes just a little bit of time to store and fetch

128
00:07:39,330 --> 00:07:41,820
messages when we use this copy of the repository.

129
00:07:42,000 --> 00:07:43,770
So again, that's in production.

130
00:07:43,770 --> 00:07:46,560
That's when we want to really, truly run our code.

131
00:07:47,610 --> 00:07:50,430
Now let's think about what happens in a testing environment.

132
00:07:50,460 --> 00:07:53,370
So let's imagine that we write out some automated tests.

133
00:07:53,370 --> 00:07:54,990
That's what I mean by while testing.

134
00:07:54,990 --> 00:08:01,440
I'm going to update that and say while automated, whenever we write out automated testing, we want

135
00:08:01,440 --> 00:08:03,870
our code to run as fast as possible.

136
00:08:03,870 --> 00:08:06,380
We want the test to execute very, very quickly.

137
00:08:06,390 --> 00:08:11,910
Whenever you are doing automated testing, it is generally bad practice to have any tests that actually

138
00:08:11,910 --> 00:08:15,480
try to write files or retrieve resources from the hard disk.

139
00:08:15,690 --> 00:08:22,260
So if we were trying to write a test around messages service, ideally we would not use the real messages

140
00:08:22,260 --> 00:08:24,870
repository because that thing tries to write to the hard disk.

141
00:08:25,760 --> 00:08:29,810
Instead what we could do in this automated testing environment.

142
00:08:29,810 --> 00:08:36,440
If we were using this good version of our code, we could very quickly create a fake repository.

143
00:08:36,440 --> 00:08:40,580
So some extra class that only exists in the automated testing world.

144
00:08:40,700 --> 00:08:45,860
This class could have all the required methods like find one, find all and create.

145
00:08:45,950 --> 00:08:50,600
But rather than actually writing a file to the hard drive and reading from a file on the hard drive,

146
00:08:50,630 --> 00:08:55,260
maybe the fake repository could just store a list of messages in memory.

147
00:08:55,280 --> 00:08:59,000
That means that the fake repository would work very, very quickly.

148
00:08:59,540 --> 00:09:03,980
So in a testing environment, we could use this very fast version of a repository.

149
00:09:03,980 --> 00:09:10,130
And in all other environments we could use the real, although a little bit slower version of a repository.

150
00:09:11,250 --> 00:09:14,720
This would be a huge win because now we've got messages service.

151
00:09:14,730 --> 00:09:17,910
It's going to run very quickly in production and run correctly.

152
00:09:17,920 --> 00:09:23,370
But while we are doing testing, we can test the messages service and it's going to run extremely fast,

153
00:09:23,400 --> 00:09:25,560
which again is very much ideal.

154
00:09:25,950 --> 00:09:29,280
This entire situation right here is only going to happen.

155
00:09:29,280 --> 00:09:36,120
It's only allowable if we follow this good pattern of code, if we follow this best pattern, because

156
00:09:36,120 --> 00:09:41,760
it's only through the use of this interface right here that we can pass in any kind of object to be

157
00:09:41,760 --> 00:09:43,230
used as a repository.

158
00:09:43,500 --> 00:09:48,660
We cannot use that pattern if we follow the better one, because in this case we have to specifically

159
00:09:48,660 --> 00:09:52,680
pass in messages repository and we can't really pass in effectively anything else.

160
00:09:53,370 --> 00:09:58,470
And we definitely can't do anything like this if we use the bad case because messages service is always

161
00:09:58,470 --> 00:10:03,150
going to specifically create an instance of messages repository and that thing always wants to write

162
00:10:03,150 --> 00:10:05,040
to an actual file on the hard drive.

163
00:10:06,190 --> 00:10:06,790
Okay.

164
00:10:07,510 --> 00:10:12,610
So at this point in time, we've now discussed inversion of control, and it says that we don't want

165
00:10:12,610 --> 00:10:13,450
to write code like this.

166
00:10:13,480 --> 00:10:16,540
We don't want to allow a class to create its own dependencies.

167
00:10:16,690 --> 00:10:21,940
We then saw one possible way of writing out this code a little bit better, and then we saw another

168
00:10:21,940 --> 00:10:25,120
possible way of making it even better than that.

169
00:10:26,200 --> 00:10:31,810
The best case is the best case because whenever we create a copy of messages service, we can pass in

170
00:10:31,810 --> 00:10:37,600
any implementation of a repository, so long as it has these methods that work in this particular way.

171
00:10:38,220 --> 00:10:44,160
And the reason that is so useful is that at some points in time we can swap out the dependency for messages

172
00:10:44,160 --> 00:10:48,790
service if perhaps it's more advantageous to give it a different repository.

173
00:10:48,810 --> 00:10:54,870
So without a doubt, this makes messages service easier to test and possibly more usable as well.

174
00:10:56,810 --> 00:11:00,890
So it seems like this is all the best thing around and we should write all of our code following this

175
00:11:00,890 --> 00:11:02,140
kind of good path.

176
00:11:02,150 --> 00:11:07,610
But it turns out that this entire idea of inversion of control is sometimes a little bit more trouble

177
00:11:07,610 --> 00:11:08,580
than it's worth.

178
00:11:08,600 --> 00:11:09,820
So let's take a pause right here.

179
00:11:09,830 --> 00:11:13,820
We're going to take a look at some issues around inversion of control and then take a look at how the

180
00:11:13,820 --> 00:11:16,070
dependency injection system fixes them.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,220 --> 00:00:05,480
I've now made it seem like inversion of control is a fantastic technique and we should use it all the

2
00:00:05,480 --> 00:00:06,540
time, everywhere.

3
00:00:06,560 --> 00:00:10,690
But it turns out that inversion of control does have some really big downsides.

4
00:00:10,700 --> 00:00:14,870
So I want to first begin this video by writing out a very quick code snippet for you.

5
00:00:14,900 --> 00:00:16,880
You do not have to write out this code snippet.

6
00:00:16,880 --> 00:00:18,580
I'm going to delete it very quickly.

7
00:00:18,590 --> 00:00:20,300
It's just a very quick demonstration.

8
00:00:20,510 --> 00:00:26,960
So let's imagine that we refactor our controller, our service and our repository to make use of inversion

9
00:00:26,990 --> 00:00:28,040
of control.

10
00:00:28,190 --> 00:00:32,330
Let's then think about what we would have to do to create a controller.

11
00:00:32,330 --> 00:00:35,210
So to make a controller, we'd have to first create a service.

12
00:00:35,240 --> 00:00:38,120
And to make a service we first have to create a repository.

13
00:00:38,270 --> 00:00:40,970
So we would have to write out some code that looks like this.

14
00:00:41,000 --> 00:00:46,460
We'd have to say repo is going to be a new messages repository.

15
00:00:47,670 --> 00:00:49,350
I then have to create my service.

16
00:00:51,520 --> 00:00:53,320
And pass in the repository.

17
00:00:53,470 --> 00:00:56,110
And then finally, I can make my controller.

18
00:00:58,460 --> 00:00:59,870
And pass in the service.

19
00:01:00,110 --> 00:01:04,730
So that is three lines of code to create one instance of a class that we actually need.

20
00:01:05,090 --> 00:01:11,080
This is three times as many lines of code as what we would have to write at present to make a controller

21
00:01:11,090 --> 00:01:16,130
right now inside of our current code base where we are not currently making use of inversion of control,

22
00:01:16,130 --> 00:01:21,170
we can create a brand new controller with all the appropriate dependencies by just writing out that

23
00:01:21,170 --> 00:01:22,210
snippet right there.

24
00:01:22,220 --> 00:01:27,530
So again, we have to write out about three times as much code to make a controller once we start making

25
00:01:27,530 --> 00:01:28,880
use of inversion of control.

26
00:01:28,880 --> 00:01:34,160
And this is for one of the most simple basic dependency systems or hierarchies we can come up with in

27
00:01:34,160 --> 00:01:35,120
an application.

28
00:01:35,510 --> 00:01:40,430
It's entirely possible that we might eventually make a service that requires maybe 2 or 3 different

29
00:01:40,430 --> 00:01:46,160
repositories, and each of those repositories might have their own dependencies and a controller might

30
00:01:46,160 --> 00:01:49,280
have more than one service that it needs to work correctly.

31
00:01:49,370 --> 00:01:55,040
So if we start to make a rather complex application to make one single controller, we might have to

32
00:01:55,040 --> 00:02:02,220
write out some amount of code that looks like this just to get the required number of repositories and

33
00:02:02,220 --> 00:02:07,830
services and whatnot created to satisfy all the different dependencies between these different classes.

34
00:02:08,100 --> 00:02:12,840
So this is a tremendous amount of code to write just to make use of inversion of control.

35
00:02:13,050 --> 00:02:18,210
So it's pretty clear that as our application starts to grow in complexity, strictly following inversion

36
00:02:18,210 --> 00:02:22,620
of control as it stands right now, as we've understood it in the last video, is probably not going

37
00:02:22,620 --> 00:02:25,800
to work at all because we just have to write too much code.

38
00:02:26,530 --> 00:02:32,380
So to solve this problem and still be able to use inversion of control, we're going to introduce a

39
00:02:32,380 --> 00:02:35,170
technique referred to as dependency injection.

40
00:02:35,200 --> 00:02:40,540
Dependency injection is all about making use of inversion of control, but not having to create a ton

41
00:02:40,570 --> 00:02:44,080
of different classes or a ton of different instances every single time.

42
00:02:44,080 --> 00:02:45,400
We want a controller.

43
00:02:46,080 --> 00:02:48,840
So let's take a look at how inversion of control works.

44
00:02:50,810 --> 00:02:51,170
All right.

45
00:02:51,170 --> 00:02:56,180
So the total linchpin of how dependency injection works is something called a container.

46
00:02:56,210 --> 00:03:00,650
We'll frequently call this a container being short for dependency injection.

47
00:03:00,680 --> 00:03:05,030
You'll also sometimes see this referred to as an injector as well.

48
00:03:06,860 --> 00:03:08,840
So either container or injector.

49
00:03:09,140 --> 00:03:13,160
This container or injector thing, whatever you want to call it, I'm going to call it container is

50
00:03:13,160 --> 00:03:16,040
an object that has a couple of different properties in it.

51
00:03:16,070 --> 00:03:20,720
For the sake of discussion, we're going to simplify these properties and say that it stores two sets

52
00:03:20,750 --> 00:03:21,740
of information.

53
00:03:22,010 --> 00:03:26,120
First, it's going to store a list of all the different classes that we've created inside of our app

54
00:03:26,120 --> 00:03:27,590
and their dependencies.

55
00:03:27,830 --> 00:03:32,390
And then second, it's going to store a list of all the different instances that this container has

56
00:03:32,390 --> 00:03:33,110
created.

57
00:03:33,200 --> 00:03:35,420
Don't worry about this second piece of data just yet.

58
00:03:35,450 --> 00:03:37,640
Let's first focus on just this top one.

59
00:03:38,710 --> 00:03:39,040
All right.

60
00:03:39,040 --> 00:03:44,650
So let's walk through a situation where we messed up or when we create our Nest application, when we

61
00:03:44,650 --> 00:03:48,340
first boot up our app and how this container is actually going to be used.

62
00:03:48,890 --> 00:03:50,870
So here's what's going to go on behind the scenes.

63
00:03:51,020 --> 00:03:55,370
Whenever we create a new nest application, a container is created for us.

64
00:03:55,460 --> 00:03:59,900
When we start up our application, we are going to take a look at all the different classes that we

65
00:03:59,900 --> 00:04:05,630
have created inside of our application except for our controllers, and we're going to attempt to register

66
00:04:05,630 --> 00:04:08,660
each of these different classes with our container.

67
00:04:09,280 --> 00:04:14,200
So we're going to take, for example, our messages service and feed it into the container.

68
00:04:14,470 --> 00:04:17,690
The container is then going to analyze our class right here.

69
00:04:17,710 --> 00:04:20,860
It's going to take a look at all the different dependencies that are required.

70
00:04:20,860 --> 00:04:24,790
Specifically, it's going to take a look at all the different constructor arguments that we're seeing

71
00:04:24,790 --> 00:04:26,920
that messages service needs to work correctly.

72
00:04:27,680 --> 00:04:33,020
The container is then going to set up some internal records that say, okay, if we ever want to create

73
00:04:33,020 --> 00:04:35,030
a copy of Messages Service.

74
00:04:36,920 --> 00:04:42,950
Then we have to make sure that we first create a copy of messages repository because that is a dependency

75
00:04:42,950 --> 00:04:44,180
of our service.

76
00:04:47,070 --> 00:04:50,140
We'll then continue registering all of our other classes as well.

77
00:04:50,160 --> 00:04:54,150
So the next one we might register will be our messages repository.

78
00:04:54,300 --> 00:04:57,350
The messages repository does not have any dependencies.

79
00:04:57,360 --> 00:05:02,040
So inside of our container we would add in another class that exists inside of our application and we

80
00:05:02,040 --> 00:05:04,890
would say that that thing does not have any dependencies at all.

81
00:05:05,750 --> 00:05:10,000
So that then at some point in time we're going to want to eventually create a controller.

82
00:05:10,010 --> 00:05:14,360
We want to create an instance of our messages controller and use it to handle incoming requests.

83
00:05:15,550 --> 00:05:20,380
So we are going to take our controller, we're going to feed it into the container and we're going to

84
00:05:20,380 --> 00:05:24,790
say, Hey, container, we want to create an instance of this controller.

85
00:05:24,820 --> 00:05:29,620
Please create the instance for us and create all of its required dependencies as well.

86
00:05:30,210 --> 00:05:35,070
So internally, when we feed in our controller right here, the container is going to take a look at

87
00:05:35,070 --> 00:05:38,250
that controller and take a look at the constructor arguments.

88
00:05:38,610 --> 00:05:42,630
And it's going to take a look at all the different dependencies that our controller requires.

89
00:05:42,900 --> 00:05:48,390
So in our case, the container is going to notice that it needs to create a copy of messages service.

90
00:05:49,180 --> 00:05:52,900
So the containers then going to take a look at messages service and it's going to say, oh, well,

91
00:05:52,930 --> 00:05:57,220
before I can create a service, I first have to create an instance of the repo.

92
00:05:57,490 --> 00:06:02,610
It's then going to take a look at the messages repo and say, okay, this thing doesn't have any dependencies,

93
00:06:02,620 --> 00:06:08,170
so we can directly create the repo, then use that to create the service, then use that to create the

94
00:06:08,170 --> 00:06:08,920
controller.

95
00:06:09,780 --> 00:06:10,770
So internally.

96
00:06:11,770 --> 00:06:17,500
The container is going to automatically create an instance of the messages repository and it's going

97
00:06:17,500 --> 00:06:22,300
to add it into an internal list of all the different instances that this thing has created over time.

98
00:06:22,940 --> 00:06:26,510
So it's now got internally stored one copy of the messages repo.

99
00:06:26,930 --> 00:06:32,060
It'll then use that instance to create an instance of the messages service.

100
00:06:34,560 --> 00:06:40,290
The container will then use the service to create an instance of our controller and return it.

101
00:06:41,060 --> 00:06:43,220
So we get back out of our container.

102
00:06:43,250 --> 00:06:45,200
A fully initialized copy.

103
00:06:46,500 --> 00:06:54,210
Of the messages controller with all the appropriate dependencies wired up to it and added in or assigned

104
00:06:54,210 --> 00:06:55,830
to that instance of the controller.

105
00:06:57,140 --> 00:07:01,670
So the entire goal of this container, we're going to first step one, register all of our different

106
00:07:01,670 --> 00:07:02,200
classes.

107
00:07:02,210 --> 00:07:05,450
The container is going to record all these different classes, dependencies.

108
00:07:05,480 --> 00:07:09,110
We'll then use the container to create a new instance of a controller.

109
00:07:09,110 --> 00:07:14,390
And when we do so, the container is going to take a look at the controller's dependencies, create

110
00:07:14,390 --> 00:07:19,550
all the appropriate instances for us, and then feed those dependencies into the controller and then

111
00:07:19,550 --> 00:07:21,830
eventually give us a controller instance back.

112
00:07:23,150 --> 00:07:27,670
So the big upside to this approach is that we can continue to use this idea of inversion of control,

113
00:07:27,680 --> 00:07:30,800
but we don't have to worry about creating all these dependencies ourselves.

114
00:07:30,830 --> 00:07:33,500
The container is going to take care of that for us.

115
00:07:34,810 --> 00:07:35,110
All right.

116
00:07:35,110 --> 00:07:39,460
So last thing, I want to go over this flow just one more time and spell it out in one single diagram

117
00:07:39,460 --> 00:07:40,330
crystal clear.

118
00:07:40,330 --> 00:07:42,760
So we can easily refer back to this diagram in the future.

119
00:07:43,660 --> 00:07:44,710
So here's the flow.

120
00:07:45,430 --> 00:07:46,090
At startup.

121
00:07:46,090 --> 00:07:49,510
We're going to register all of our different classes with a container.

122
00:07:50,360 --> 00:07:54,080
The container is going to take a look at all these different classes and figure out which dependencies

123
00:07:54,080 --> 00:07:55,190
each one has.

124
00:07:55,370 --> 00:08:00,710
Then at some future point in time, after our application kind of initially does some registering of

125
00:08:00,710 --> 00:08:05,330
these different classes, this initial start up, we're then going to ask the container to create an

126
00:08:05,330 --> 00:08:06,770
instance of a class for us.

127
00:08:06,800 --> 00:08:09,200
And this is almost always going to be a controller.

128
00:08:10,870 --> 00:08:16,180
The container is then going to find all the different dependencies that the controller needs.

129
00:08:16,210 --> 00:08:18,910
It's going to create an instance of those dependencies.

130
00:08:19,000 --> 00:08:23,080
It's then going to use those to create the controller and then give us back the controller.

131
00:08:23,900 --> 00:08:27,560
The last thing I want to mention here is the container also holds on to the created dependencies.

132
00:08:27,560 --> 00:08:31,580
So the instances we had created and put into this little list right here.

133
00:08:32,470 --> 00:08:36,820
And if we ever need to create another class in the future that has a similar dependency.

134
00:08:36,820 --> 00:08:41,830
So if we try to create some other controller, maybe that also needs the message service rather than

135
00:08:41,830 --> 00:08:46,630
creating an additional instance of the messages service, it's going to reuse the instance it already

136
00:08:46,630 --> 00:08:47,290
created.

137
00:08:48,850 --> 00:08:52,810
Now that last part where it's going to reuse instances that were already created, we're going to see

138
00:08:52,810 --> 00:08:53,830
that in greater detail.

139
00:08:53,860 --> 00:08:55,270
Don't worry about that too much just yet.

140
00:08:55,270 --> 00:08:58,270
I just mention it to be a little bit complete in this entire description.

141
00:08:59,640 --> 00:09:00,030
All right.

142
00:09:00,030 --> 00:09:04,530
So now that we've got some kind of background on dependency injection here, I do know this stuff is

143
00:09:04,530 --> 00:09:05,520
awfully complicated.

144
00:09:05,520 --> 00:09:09,990
And at the end of the day, the only way to really understand it is to go and write some code out ourselves

145
00:09:09,990 --> 00:09:12,030
and see all this stuff happen in action.

146
00:09:12,030 --> 00:09:13,380
So let's take a pause right here.

147
00:09:13,380 --> 00:09:18,510
In the next video, we're going to start to refactor our code to make use of an inversion of control.

148
00:09:19,360 --> 00:09:24,550
We're then going to make use of the system to automatically create all of our dependencies for us.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:07,170
Alustame selle refaktoriga, et kasutada värvainete konteinerit meie rakenduse sees, et alustada.

2
00:00:07,200 --> 00:00:11,550
Me läheme tagasi meie teenuse ja meie kontrolleri juurde ja me muudame neid nii, et nad võtaksid

3
00:00:11,550 --> 00:00:14,270
oma sõltuvusi konstruktori argumentidena vastu.

4
00:00:14,970 --> 00:00:20,790
Tuletame meelde, et meil oli vahet parem ja parim lähenemine varem ütles, et ideaalis oleks meil meie

5
00:00:20,790 --> 00:00:24,090
konstruktori argumendid, B tüüpi, mis on liidesed.

6
00:00:24,390 --> 00:00:25,740
Me ei kavatse selle pärast veel muretseda.

7
00:00:26,570 --> 00:00:31,500
Alustame kõigepealt, alustame meie sõnumite teenuse refaktoorimist, et seda asja refaktoorida.

8
00:00:31,720 --> 00:00:33,120
Ma leian konstruktori.

9
00:00:33,390 --> 00:00:34,560
Ma eemaldan selle kommentaari.

10
00:00:35,590 --> 00:00:42,070
saab siis argumendina juba initsialiseeritud koopia sõnumite hoidlast ja see

11
00:00:42,070 --> 00:00:44,290
on tüüpi sõnumite hoidla.

12
00:00:46,360 --> 00:00:49,450
Me siis märk, et tänaseni, et.

13
00:00:50,510 --> 00:00:52,070
Sõnumite repo niimoodi.

14
00:00:54,200 --> 00:00:58,640
Nüüd, kui soovite, võiksime kasutada natuke tüüpscript süntaktilist suhkrut, et tihendada

15
00:00:58,640 --> 00:01:03,260
kõiki neid koodiridu siin, see, mida te praegu ekraanil näete, on 100 protsenti samaväärne

16
00:01:03,260 --> 00:01:12,080
selle asemel, et kirjutada public messages, repo ja seejärel kustutada see omistus konstruktori sees ja kustutada ka omaduse initsialiseerimine.

17
00:01:13,740 --> 00:01:18,000
Nii et me kasutame seda mustrit üsna palju kogu kursuse jooksul, kus me saame konstruktorisse argumendid,

18
00:01:18,000 --> 00:01:22,180
me märgime need avalikuks ja see tähendab, et need argumendid saavad automaatselt, automaatselt

19
00:01:22,380 --> 00:01:24,570
omistatakse klassile omadustena.

20
00:01:25,720 --> 00:01:27,980
OK, nüüd me lõpetasime selle esimese refaktooringu.

21
00:01:28,030 --> 00:01:30,810
Lähme meie kontrolleri juurde ja muudame ka selle ümber.

22
00:01:32,150 --> 00:01:38,540
Nii et siin on kontroller meie konstruktori argumentide loendi sees, lisame sisse public

23
00:01:38,540 --> 00:01:45,260
messages, teenus on tüüpi messages service, ja nüüd veel kord, saame eemaldada kõik konstruktori

24
00:01:45,260 --> 00:01:48,200
sees ja ka omaduse määratluse.

25
00:01:50,920 --> 00:01:55,660
OK, seega on tegur, mida me ei pea muutma meie repositooriumis, sest sellel asjal ei olnud

26
00:01:55,660 --> 00:01:58,110
oma konstruktori sees mingeid sõltuvusi.

27
00:01:58,900 --> 00:02:01,660
Nii et me järgime nüüd põhimõttelist kontrolli ümberpööramist.

28
00:02:02,230 --> 00:02:06,700
Nüüd peame veenduma, et me ühendame kõik need erinevad klassid meie värvainete konteineriga.

29
00:02:07,270 --> 00:02:07,900
Selleks.

30
00:02:08,350 --> 00:02:10,390
Meil on kaks eraldi etappi, mille peame läbima.

31
00:02:10,930 --> 00:02:15,190
Me impordime teise dekoraatori igasse meie erinevatesse sõltuvusfailidesse.

32
00:02:15,820 --> 00:02:18,070
Seejärel rakendame seda dekoraatorit klassile.

33
00:02:18,100 --> 00:02:23,430
Ja siis lõpuks lisame need klassid meie moodulisse, need sammud siin.

34
00:02:23,470 --> 00:02:25,390
Nende sõnad kõlavad kuidagi segadusttekitavalt.

35
00:02:25,600 --> 00:02:28,560
Tegelikult on see, mida me peame siin tegema, väga lihtne ja lihtne.

36
00:02:29,080 --> 00:02:31,810
Nii et lubage mul näidata teile meie teenuse sisemust.

37
00:02:32,020 --> 00:02:35,040
Me tahame lisada selle klassi meie DI konteinerisse.

38
00:02:35,530 --> 00:02:37,960
Nii et kõige tipus hakkame me importima.

39
00:02:38,910 --> 00:02:43,080
SJS Common'i süstitav.

40
00:02:44,950 --> 00:02:48,820
Siis otse klassi kohal, ma kavatsen lisada sisse süstitava.

41
00:02:50,000 --> 00:02:55,640
Nii et see tähistab seda klassi registreerimiseks värvainete konteineri sees, see registreerimisprotsess toimub

42
00:02:55,640 --> 00:02:58,790
automaatselt nüüd, kui me oleme rakendanud dekoraatorit.

43
00:03:00,520 --> 00:03:02,830
Järgmisena läheme tagasi hoidlasse.

44
00:03:04,110 --> 00:03:10,110
Me tahame lisada selle klassi ka meie konteinerisse, seega impordime ka injectable'i.

45
00:03:11,220 --> 00:03:12,590
Ka siia.

46
00:03:15,290 --> 00:03:17,780
Ja seejärel rakendage süstitavat kaunistajat.

47
00:03:20,130 --> 00:03:25,620
OK, seega oleme nüüd registreerinud need kaks erinevat klassi meie värvainete konteineriga, me ei

48
00:03:25,620 --> 00:03:31,200
