pea registreerima kontrollerit otse meie konteineriga, sest kontroller tarbib ainult klassid.

49
00:03:31,450 --> 00:03:35,910
Pidage meeles, et me üritame lõpuks luua instantsi või tõesti NEST on automaatselt meie jaoks.

50
00:03:36,150 --> 00:03:41,550
Nest püüab luua meie kontrolleri instantsi meie eest, nii et me ei pea registreerima kontrollereid meie värvainete

51
00:03:41,550 --> 00:03:42,780
konteinerites.

52
00:03:43,230 --> 00:03:48,360
Me peame registreerima ainult peaaegu iga teise klassi üldiselt teenused ja hoidlad, mida

53
00:03:48,360 --> 00:03:48,990
me loome.

54
00:03:51,100 --> 00:03:55,780
Nüüd, kui me oleme rakendanud selle dekoraatori, on viimane asi, mida me peame tegema, on lisada

55
00:03:55,780 --> 00:03:59,470
need kaks klassi, teenus ja hoidla meie moodulite pakkujate nimekirja.

56
00:04:00,070 --> 00:04:04,000
Selleks avame oma sõnumite mooduli.

57
00:04:05,180 --> 00:04:05,900
See on siinsamas.

58
00:04:07,800 --> 00:04:09,780
Kõige tipuks hakkame importima.

59
00:04:11,490 --> 00:04:17,130
Need kaks klassi, mida me just märgistasime injectable'iks, nii et see on sõnumite repositoorium teenuses, nii

60
00:04:17,130 --> 00:04:19,050
et me impordime sõnumite teenust.

61
00:04:22,470 --> 00:04:24,090
Ja sõnumeid.

62
00:04:26,480 --> 00:04:27,230
Hoiukoht.

63
00:04:30,580 --> 00:04:35,560
Seejärel lisame uue omaduse teenusepakkujate mudeli dekoraatorisse.

64
00:04:36,450 --> 00:04:45,630
Termin providers siin, võib-olla parem termin selle jaoks oleks asjad, mida saab kasutada sõltuvustena või teiste klassidena,

65
00:04:46,080 --> 00:04:51,840
ilmselt ei ole ideaalne omadusnimi, kuid see on parem viis sellest mõelda kui termin

66
00:04:52,020 --> 00:04:53,520
providers.

67
00:04:53,550 --> 00:04:58,710
Kui te näete provider või providers, tähendab see tegelikult asju, mida saab kasutada teiste klasside

68
00:04:58,710 --> 00:04:59,610
sõltuvustena.

69
00:05:00,420 --> 00:05:03,060
Kuid USGSi maailmas nimetame seda teenusepakkujateks.

70
00:05:04,590 --> 00:05:09,240
Ja me lisame sellele sõnumeid, teenust ja sõnumeid.

71
00:05:10,240 --> 00:05:10,960
Hoiukoht.

72
00:05:11,920 --> 00:05:12,670
Ja see ongi kõik.

73
00:05:14,350 --> 00:05:19,200
Nii, nüüd oleme refaktooringu lõpetanud ja te märkate, et selle refaktooringu käigus ei pidanud me mingil

74
00:05:19,200 --> 00:05:24,070
hetkel puudutama midagi, mida nimetatakse konteineriks või injektoriks või midagi sellist.

75
00:05:24,520 --> 00:05:29,950
Mitte mingil hetkel ei pidanud me muretsema mingi selgesõnalise registreerimise pärast, peale selle

76
00:05:29,950 --> 00:05:34,030
dekoraatori lisamise ja iga klassi lisamise teenusepakkujate nimekirja.

77
00:05:34,580 --> 00:05:39,400
Seega, kuigi konteiner on meie rakenduse sees olemas, suhtleme sellega harva

78
00:05:39,400 --> 00:05:40,270
otseselt.

79
00:05:40,540 --> 00:05:42,760
Selle asemel kasutame lihtsalt mõningaid dekoraatoreid.

80
00:05:42,910 --> 00:05:47,950
Me lisame moodulisse mõned asjad ja pesa hoolitseb kõige muu eest automaatselt ise.

81
00:05:48,990 --> 00:05:52,860
Nüüd, viimane asi, mida me peame tegema, on tegelikult testida meie rakendust ja veenduda, et

82
00:05:52,860 --> 00:05:57,540
kõik töötab ikka veel ootuspäraselt, esimene asi, mida ma teen, on minna tagasi oma terminali ja veenduda, et minu

83
00:05:57,540 --> 00:05:59,850
server töötab ikka veel ilma igasuguste vigadeta.

84
00:06:00,270 --> 00:06:01,200
Näeb siin hea välja.

85
00:06:02,220 --> 00:06:04,740
Nii et siis ma avan taas oma API-kliendi.

86
00:06:07,560 --> 00:06:12,840
Ma proovin saada nimekirja kõigist sõnumitest, OK, tundub, et see töötab,

87
00:06:12,840 --> 00:06:18,420
ma proovin luua sõnumi, seega ütlen pärast DI lisamist sõnumi.

88
00:06:19,520 --> 00:06:21,080
Saada see ära, näeb hea välja.

89
00:06:22,150 --> 00:06:26,710
Ja siis lõpuks, ma püüan saada väga konkreetset sõnumit, tundub mulle kehtiv ID nagu

90
00:06:26,710 --> 00:06:30,820
kolmekümne ühele, nii et ma teen taotluse sõnumeid kolmekümne ühele.

91
00:06:31,820 --> 00:06:34,070
Ja ma saan ka väga konkreetse sõnumi.

92
00:06:35,010 --> 00:06:41,010
OK, see ongi kõik, me oleme nüüd DI-süsteemi rakenduses sisse seadistanud ja kõik, mida me pidime tegema,

93
00:06:41,010 --> 00:06:43,140
oli see lihtne voog siinsamas.

94
00:06:43,950 --> 00:06:47,520
Nii et üldiselt iga klassi puhul, mille me loome, see ei ole kontroller.

95
00:06:47,550 --> 00:06:53,370
Nii et üldiselt teenused ja repositooriumid, me lisame selle injectable decorator ja seejärel lisame

96
00:06:53,370 --> 00:06:56,130
klassi meie moodulite pakkujate loendisse.

97
00:06:56,850 --> 00:06:59,640
Siis toimub kõik muu automaatselt.

98
00:06:59,910 --> 00:07:02,130
Järgmisena luuakse konteiner automaatselt meie jaoks.

99
00:07:02,160 --> 00:07:08,210
See loob meile automaatselt ka kontrollerid ja kontrollerid saavad kõik vajalikud sõltuvused.

100
00:07:09,030 --> 00:07:12,870
Kuigi tundub, et me ei pidanud palju tegema, et see asi toimiks, on ikkagi uskumatult

101
00:07:12,870 --> 00:07:17,790
palju väikeseid näpunäiteid ja nippe ning lihtsalt pisiasju selle DII-süsteemi ümber, millest te peate teadlik

102
00:07:17,790 --> 00:07:18,420
olema.

103
00:07:18,720 --> 00:07:24,570
Lisaks, kuigi me kasutame nüüd sõltuvust, tundub süstimine nagu, noh, mida me saavutasime?

104
00:07:25,140 --> 00:07:27,540
Tundub, et me ei saanud sel hetkel eriti palju juurde.

105
00:07:28,110 --> 00:07:32,160
Nii et on veel paar väga väikest lõpumärkust, mida ma tahan Diefi ümber praegu teha.

106
00:07:32,160 --> 00:07:35,190
Nii et veel üks video süsteemi kohta ja siis läheme sealt edasi.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,140 --> 00:00:05,340
Є кілька останніх моментів, які я хочу згадати про систему, тож перше,

2
00:00:05,340 --> 00:00:10,860
що ви можете помітити, це те, що ми використовували барвник за кращим принципом.

3
00:00:10,950 --> 00:00:13,980
Пам'ятаєте, на цій схемі я показав, що ми робили спочатку.

4
00:00:13,980 --> 00:00:14,910
Я сказав, що це погано.

5
00:00:15,160 --> 00:00:18,090
Тоді я сказав, що є кращий підхід, а є ще кращий підхід.

6
00:00:18,450 --> 00:00:23,220
А в найкращому випадку, ми б визначили всі наші залежності як такі, що мають типи, визначені

7
00:00:23,220 --> 00:00:24,120
інтерфейсами.

8
00:00:24,480 --> 00:00:27,440
І це дозволило б нам дуже легко замінювати різні реалізації.

9
00:00:27,960 --> 00:00:30,100
Проте ми використовуємо кращі варіанти.

10
00:00:30,480 --> 00:00:31,560
Так чому ж так?

11
00:00:32,070 --> 00:00:36,240
Виявляється, що використання такої найкращої системи, де всі наші різні інтерфейси або

12
00:00:36,240 --> 00:00:40,710
всі наші різні залежності визначаються інтерфейсами, насправді є дещо складним завданням

13
00:00:40,710 --> 00:00:42,630
через деякі обмеження у типізації.

14
00:00:43,260 --> 00:00:47,400
Ми все ще можемо обійти це, використовуючи кілька різних трюків, і я покажу

15
00:00:47,400 --> 00:00:49,200
вам ці трюки пізніше в курсі.

16
00:00:49,650 --> 00:00:54,900
Але зараз зрозумійте, що майже все в Nest передбачає, що ви дотримуєтесь цього

17
00:00:54,900 --> 00:00:56,220
шаблону саме тут.

18
00:00:56,610 --> 00:01:01,320
Передбачається, що всі ваші різні сервіси, репозиторії, контролери і т.д. матимуть

19
00:01:01,320 --> 00:01:07,680
типи залежностей, які є прямими посиланнями на інші класи, що існують всередині вашого проекту, на відміну від

20
00:01:07,950 --> 00:01:11,010
того, що було б ідеальним варіантом - інтерфейсу.

21
00:01:11,760 --> 00:01:14,520
Тож, знову ж таки, в ідеалі, ми б хотіли використовувати найкращу систему.

22
00:01:14,850 --> 00:01:18,720
Але через деякі обмеження та типографіку ми змушені використовувати кращі варіанти.

23
00:01:18,870 --> 00:01:21,660
І нарешті, я покажу вам кілька обхідних шляхів.

24
00:01:23,330 --> 00:01:28,070
Далі, ще одне дуже маленьке пояснення, яке я хочу зробити, трохи раніше я показував вам цю діаграму

25
00:01:28,310 --> 00:01:33,380
і говорив, що кожного разу, коли настає час створювати контролер, Nest бере наш клас контролера, а потім

26
00:01:33,800 --> 00:01:38,420
просить контейнер з барвником автоматично створити для нас екземпляр цього контролера.

27
00:01:39,020 --> 00:01:43,640
Потім контейнер подивиться на ці речі, залежності і створить новий екземпляр з усіх відповідних

28
00:01:43,640 --> 00:01:46,040
залежностей і підключить все це.

29
00:01:46,670 --> 00:01:51,650
І як частина цього процесу, я вже згадував, що контейнер буде зберігати

30
00:01:51,650 --> 00:01:54,680
список усіх створених ним екземплярів.

31
00:01:55,340 --> 00:01:59,660
Тож це дуже важлива річ для розуміння світу ін'єкцій залежності.

32
00:02:00,200 --> 00:02:04,700
Щоразу, коли ваш контейнер створює для вас залежність, коли він створює екземпляр

33
00:02:04,700 --> 00:02:10,190
повідомлень, сервісу, сховища або чогось іншого, він зберігатиме цей екземпляр у себе і з часом використовуватиме

34
00:02:10,190 --> 00:02:15,300
його повторно, якщо ви знову звернетесь до нього за тією ж залежністю.

35
00:02:15,740 --> 00:02:20,510
Отже, якщо у вашому додатку є два різні класи, які обидва звертаються до служби повідомлень,

36
00:02:20,840 --> 00:02:26,900
буде створено лише один екземпляр служби повідомлень, і цей екземпляр буде спільно використовуватися цими двома

37
00:02:26,900 --> 00:02:28,100
класами.

38
00:02:28,730 --> 00:02:30,640
Я можу показати вам це дуже, дуже легко.

39
00:02:31,250 --> 00:02:35,210
Отже, повернувшись всередину нашого контролера, я знайду наш конструктор ось тут.

40
00:02:36,380 --> 00:02:42,950
І на додаток до того, що я прошу лише одну залежність служби повідомлень, я також попрошу

41
00:02:42,950 --> 00:02:44,270
дві інші копії.

42
00:02:45,660 --> 00:02:49,800
Інші копії я збираюся назвати дещо по-іншому, тобто повідомлення, служба

43
00:02:50,490 --> 00:02:54,030
два і три, тому зараз я прошу три копії служби повідомлень.

44
00:02:54,510 --> 00:02:58,950
Але система ін'єкції залежностей створить лише один екземпляр цієї залежності

45
00:02:58,950 --> 00:03:02,850
і надасть той самий екземпляр для всіх трьох різних аргументів.

46
00:03:03,400 --> 00:03:08,640
Я можу дуже легко перевірити це, зробивши консольний лог всередині конструктора і порівнявши повідомлення.

47
00:03:08,640 --> 00:03:12,960
Сервіс зі знаком дорівнює прирівнюється до повідомлень.

48
00:03:12,960 --> 00:03:13,680
Обслуговування.

49
00:03:14,900 --> 00:03:20,690
І я зроблю те ж саме для повідомлень, послуга для вас, повідомлення, послуга три, так що якщо я побачу це і повернуся

50
00:03:21,260 --> 00:03:22,490
до свого терміналу.

51
00:03:23,810 --> 00:03:27,530
Я прокручу трохи вгору і подивлюся, чи є в журналах правдиві записи.

52
00:03:28,980 --> 00:03:35,640
Отже, навіть якщо ми попросили три копії служби повідомлень, контейнер створив лише

53
00:03:35,940 --> 00:03:40,200
одну копію і передав її скрізь, куди б ми не звернулися.

54
00:03:40,770 --> 00:03:44,080
Це матиме досить великий вплив на те, як ви розробляєте деякі з ваших послуг.

55
00:03:44,400 --> 00:03:49,980
Ми завжди розробляємо наші сервіси, припускаючи, що один і той самий екземпляр сервісу буде використовуватися в різних місцях

56
00:03:49,980 --> 00:03:51,480
всередині нашого проекту.

57
00:03:52,170 --> 00:03:54,150
Є кілька способів обійти це обмеження.

58
00:03:54,420 --> 00:03:59,490
Тож якщо у вас є послуга, для якої вам завжди потрібно створювати нову копію, не хвилюйтеся, ми можемо

59
00:03:59,490 --> 00:04:01,290
це зробити, якщо ви цього хочете.

60
00:04:01,530 --> 00:04:04,350
І це ще одна тема, до якої ми повернемося у свій час.

61
00:04:04,430 --> 00:04:08,730
Зараз я просто хочу, щоб ви знали про цей цікавий маленький вид сайдкіків.

62
00:04:09,940 --> 00:04:10,870
Я збираюся почистити цей код.

63
00:04:12,430 --> 00:04:12,930
Поїхали.

64
00:04:14,200 --> 00:04:18,040
Гаразд, і останнє, про що я хочу сказати, це те, що коли ви почнете вивчати цей код,

65
00:04:18,040 --> 00:04:20,710
ви, можливо, почнете думати: "А в чому ж вигода?".

66
00:04:20,830 --> 00:04:22,240
Що ми отримали насправді?

67
00:04:22,480 --> 00:04:25,460
Яка користь від усього, що ми зробили?

68
00:04:25,510 --> 00:04:27,190
Хто піклується про використання ін'єкцій від залежності?

69
00:04:27,730 --> 00:04:31,780
Чесно кажучи, іноді я навіть погоджуюся з тим, що ти не збираєшся брехати.

70
00:04:32,110 --> 00:04:36,580
Іноді, коли ми інвестуємо, використовуючи ін'єкції залежності, здається, що ми просто перестрибуємо через

71
00:04:36,580 --> 00:04:39,100
додаткові обручі, не отримуючи при цьому багато чого.

72
00:04:39,520 --> 00:04:41,440
Тож якщо ви так відчуваєте, це цілком нормально.

73
00:04:41,800 --> 00:04:46,300
Але я можу сказати вам без сумніву, що тестування вашого додатку, коли ви використовуєте ін'єкцію

74
00:04:46,300 --> 00:04:52,830
залежностей і всю цю техніку інверсії контролю, тестування вашого додатку буде набагато простішим, набагато простішим.

75
00:04:53,470 --> 00:04:57,820
Отже, з часом, коли ми почнемо писати тести для нашого додатку, ми побачимо, що тестування окремих

76
00:04:57,820 --> 00:05:02,770
класів всередині нашого додатку буде дуже простим і зрозумілим, порівняно з тим, якби ми не використовували

77
00:05:02,770 --> 00:05:05,770
інверсію управління та ін'єкцію залежностей.

78
00:05:06,260 --> 00:05:08,290
Тому просто майте на увазі, що це і є виплата.

79
00:05:08,710 --> 00:05:12,340
Все це окупається, коли ми починаємо писати тести.

80
00:05:13,900 --> 00:05:19,450
Звідси випливає, що ви зацікавлені в тестуванні і хочете займатися тестуванням.

81
00:05:19,630 --> 00:05:25,060
Якщо ви з тих, хто каже, що ніколи не тестує свій код, Лінді може бути не дуже корисною для

82
00:05:25,060 --> 00:05:25,510
вас.

83
00:05:26,260 --> 00:05:31,660
Отже, якщо ви не дуже любите робити "зроби сам" і не хочете займатися тестуванням, можливо, Nest не найкращий фреймворк для вас,

84
00:05:31,660 --> 00:05:35,860
тому що в іншому випадку вам доведеться працювати в обхід цієї системи "зроби сам", не отримуючи від

85
00:05:35,860 --> 00:05:37,110
неї реальної користі.

86
00:05:38,450 --> 00:05:42,860
Гаразд, це мої додаткові кілька зауважень, тож досить лекцій, ми вже прочитали тонну лекцій в останніх

87
00:05:42,860 --> 00:05:44,150
двох відеороликах.

88
00:05:44,300 --> 00:05:47,500
Тож давайте зупинимось тут і почнемо писати більше коду за мить.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================




# section 6: Nest Architecture Organizing Code with Modules


1
00:00:00,950 --> 00:00:02,900
We finished up our messages application.

2
00:00:02,900 --> 00:00:05,410
So now we're going to move on and start to work on our next app.

3
00:00:05,420 --> 00:00:09,710
This next app is not going to be a true full application that really does anything.

4
00:00:09,710 --> 00:00:14,990
Instead, we're going to do a very quick little side project, and the entire goal of this side project

5
00:00:14,990 --> 00:00:19,910
is to get a really good understanding of how modules and dependency injection work together.

6
00:00:20,030 --> 00:00:23,360
So the app that we're going to build again, not really a lot of point to it.

7
00:00:23,360 --> 00:00:25,580
It's just a simple demonstration.

8
00:00:25,580 --> 00:00:28,100
So let me help you understand what we're going to build.

9
00:00:28,340 --> 00:00:32,420
We're going to make a very small project that is going to kind of imitate a computer.

10
00:00:32,510 --> 00:00:36,620
So every computer that you've ever used has a couple of different components inside of it.

11
00:00:36,650 --> 00:00:42,980
A typical computer will have a CPU for processing data and a hard disk of sorts for storing data.

12
00:00:43,190 --> 00:00:46,100
Both these devices are powered by a power supply.

13
00:00:46,130 --> 00:00:48,650
So a power supply is what supplies electricity.

14
00:00:48,830 --> 00:00:54,440
A typical computer will also have some other very common components inside of it, such as networking

15
00:00:54,440 --> 00:00:56,210
capabilities and Ram.

16
00:00:56,210 --> 00:00:58,070
So a form of memory and so on.

17
00:00:58,070 --> 00:00:59,450
But we're not going to worry about those.

18
00:00:59,480 --> 00:01:05,130
We're just going to model a very simple computer that is going to have a CPU, a disk and a power supply.

19
00:01:05,250 --> 00:01:08,970
So let me show you how we are going to model this thing and how dependency injection is going to come

20
00:01:08,970 --> 00:01:09,750
into play.

21
00:01:10,890 --> 00:01:12,510
Okay, so here's the general goal.

22
00:01:12,540 --> 00:01:17,800
We're going to make a separate module that serves or kind of models each different part of a computer.

23
00:01:17,820 --> 00:01:21,730
So at the lowest level down here, we are going to create something called the power module.

24
00:01:21,750 --> 00:01:25,710
Inside the power module is going to be a service called the Power Service.

25
00:01:25,920 --> 00:01:29,310
The power service will have a single method called supply power.

26
00:01:29,760 --> 00:01:35,400
We're then going to create a module and a disk module which will both have a service of their own.

27
00:01:36,190 --> 00:01:39,370
A CPU and a disk can only work if they have power.

28
00:01:39,370 --> 00:01:44,770
So we need to make sure that both the CPU module and the disk module have access to some code that we

29
00:01:44,770 --> 00:01:46,750
put together inside the power module.

30
00:01:47,560 --> 00:01:52,510
Once we've got the CPU and the disk stuff put together, we're then going to make use of both those

31
00:01:52,510 --> 00:01:56,980
modules inside of our topmost module which we are going to call the computer module.

32
00:01:57,630 --> 00:02:00,690
So we have a very strict hierarchy here of modules.

33
00:02:00,720 --> 00:02:07,980
The computer module depends upon CPU and disk and CPU and disk in turn depend upon the power module.

34
00:02:08,490 --> 00:02:13,170
And in each of these different modules, there's exactly one service or controller for that matter,

35
00:02:13,170 --> 00:02:15,990
that's going to rely upon some other service.

36
00:02:16,260 --> 00:02:17,820
So that's the general goal.

37
00:02:17,880 --> 00:02:22,320
Along the way, as I mentioned, we're going to get a better idea of what modules are, why we use them,

38
00:02:22,320 --> 00:02:25,800
and a really good idea of some rules around dependency injection.

39
00:02:26,370 --> 00:02:30,570
So let's get started on this project right away by going back over to our terminal and we're going to

40
00:02:30,570 --> 00:02:33,030
generate a new project using the CLI.

41
00:02:35,170 --> 00:02:37,000
So I've stopped my running application.

42
00:02:37,000 --> 00:02:39,450
I'm back inside of a workspace directory of sorts.

43
00:02:39,460 --> 00:02:45,610
I'm going to generate this new project with Nest New, and then I'm going to call my project D short

44
00:02:45,610 --> 00:02:48,760
for dependency injection because that's what we're really learning about here.

45
00:02:49,510 --> 00:02:50,490
So I'll run that command.

46
00:02:50,500 --> 00:02:52,970
I'll be prompted to select a package manager.

47
00:02:52,990 --> 00:02:54,180
I'm going to go with NPM.

48
00:02:54,800 --> 00:02:57,860
And the installation is going to kick off as usual.

49
00:02:57,860 --> 00:02:59,060
That's going to take a moment or two.

50
00:02:59,090 --> 00:03:00,260
So I'm going to take a pause right here.

51
00:03:00,260 --> 00:03:04,190
We'll come back the next video, and we're going to start to generate a couple of different modules

52
00:03:04,190 --> 00:03:05,540
and services.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,980 --> 00:00:06,290
I finished generating my project, I've changed into the new project directory and I've opened up my

2
00:00:06,290 --> 00:00:08,530
code editor inside that directory as well.

3
00:00:08,540 --> 00:00:13,310
So at this point I would encourage you to open up your code editor and open your project directory as

4
00:00:13,310 --> 00:00:13,820
well.

5
00:00:14,520 --> 00:00:19,260
So the first thing I want to do is open up our SRC directory and I want to delete just about all the

6
00:00:19,260 --> 00:00:21,420
different files that were generated for us.

7
00:00:21,930 --> 00:00:25,540
I'm going to delete everything inside of here except for the main.ts file.

8
00:00:25,560 --> 00:00:29,460
I'm going to keep that one around just to save us from having to type out some really basic code.

9
00:00:29,670 --> 00:00:31,800
So I'm going to delete all the other files.

10
00:00:33,340 --> 00:00:34,210
Looks good.

11
00:00:34,850 --> 00:00:36,350
Now let's go back over to our terminal.

12
00:00:36,380 --> 00:00:41,810
We're going to generate all four of these different modules and the services for each one well, service

13
00:00:41,810 --> 00:00:46,370
or controller because remember, our computer module in particular is going to have a controller.

14
00:00:47,400 --> 00:00:47,790
All right.

15
00:00:47,790 --> 00:00:52,110
So back over at my terminal, we're going to run a couple of different commands to generate all these

16
00:00:52,110 --> 00:00:53,010
different things.

17
00:00:53,020 --> 00:00:59,280
So I'm going to first generate the computer module with Nest G module computer.

18
00:01:02,270 --> 00:01:06,470
And then go on to repeat the same command and make our CPU module.

19
00:01:08,580 --> 00:01:10,320
The disk module.

20
00:01:11,970 --> 00:01:13,830
And finally, the power module.

21
00:01:15,210 --> 00:01:17,880
Okay, so there's all four of our different modules.

22
00:01:18,090 --> 00:01:24,180
So now we're going to generate a service disk, service, power service, and finally the computer controller.

23
00:01:25,180 --> 00:01:29,500
So to generate these different services, we'll do a G service.

24
00:01:30,040 --> 00:01:34,000
We're going to start How about with the CPU service?

25
00:01:36,540 --> 00:01:37,560
So I'm going to run that.

26
00:01:39,910 --> 00:01:42,130
I'll then generate the power service.

27
00:01:43,460 --> 00:01:45,830
And then finally, the disk service.

28
00:01:47,870 --> 00:01:48,190
Okay.

29
00:01:48,200 --> 00:01:48,920
Looks good.

30
00:01:49,150 --> 00:01:51,290
So now the last thing we need is our controller.

31
00:01:52,320 --> 00:01:54,780
So nest G controller.

32
00:01:54,990 --> 00:01:57,630
And we're going to call this the computer controller.

33
00:02:00,030 --> 00:02:00,480
Okay.

34
00:02:00,510 --> 00:02:02,010
File generation complete.

35
00:02:02,040 --> 00:02:04,250
Let's now go back over to our code editor.

36
00:02:04,260 --> 00:02:05,520
Back inside of our editor.

37
00:02:05,520 --> 00:02:07,800
I'm going to find the Main.ts file.

38
00:02:07,980 --> 00:02:12,550
We left that main.ts file around from when we initially generated our project.

39
00:02:12,570 --> 00:02:16,370
So the default main.ts file tries to import the app module.

40
00:02:16,380 --> 00:02:17,580
We deleted that module.

41
00:02:17,580 --> 00:02:22,330
So we need to fix up this import to get our main module, which is now the computer module.

42
00:02:22,350 --> 00:02:27,270
We're then going to make sure that we pass off the computer module to the nest factory dot create function.

43
00:02:27,480 --> 00:02:35,610
So at the top I'm going to delete the app module import and replace it with computer module from computer.

44
00:02:35,970 --> 00:02:38,010
Computer dot module.

45
00:02:39,720 --> 00:02:43,350
And then going to pass off that module to nest factory dot create.

46
00:02:44,430 --> 00:02:44,790
All right.

47
00:02:44,790 --> 00:02:46,980
So that should be it for initial setup.

48
00:02:47,040 --> 00:02:50,090
So now we've got our computer module set up as the main module.

49
00:02:50,100 --> 00:02:54,540
We have all these three other modules, but they're not connected together in any way.

50
00:02:54,660 --> 00:02:59,500
So we're going to start implementing each of these different modules using a bottom up approach.

51
00:02:59,520 --> 00:03:04,560
So we're going to go to the one module that does not depend upon anything else inside of our project.

52
00:03:04,560 --> 00:03:06,090
That is the power module.

53
00:03:06,360 --> 00:03:11,040
We're going to open up our power service and we're going to define a method on there called the supply

54
00:03:11,070 --> 00:03:12,090
power method.

55
00:03:12,120 --> 00:03:16,260
This method is going to eventually be called by some other modules inside of our project.

56
00:03:18,040 --> 00:03:21,070
So I'm going to find the power directory inside there.

57
00:03:21,070 --> 00:03:22,990
I will find the power service.

58
00:03:24,000 --> 00:03:28,830
And then on that class, I'm going to define supply power.

59
00:03:29,160 --> 00:03:32,010
Now, remember, our project doesn't really do anything per se.

60
00:03:32,040 --> 00:03:35,130
We're not really going to be supplying power or anything like that.

61
00:03:35,130 --> 00:03:39,630
But just for the sake of having at least some functionality here, I'm going to say that the supply

62
00:03:39,660 --> 00:03:40,710
power function.

63
00:03:41,560 --> 00:03:43,630
Is going to receive some argument.

64
00:03:44,700 --> 00:03:45,960
That I'm going to call what's.

65
00:03:45,960 --> 00:03:51,630
So this is going to be the amount of power that maybe some other service is requesting to be supplied.

66
00:03:51,750 --> 00:03:57,180
Then inside the power service, I'm just going to do a console log and say supplying.

67
00:03:58,360 --> 00:04:00,580
Watts worth of power.

68
00:04:01,360 --> 00:04:02,320
And that's it.

69
00:04:04,030 --> 00:04:07,990
So now we've got essentially a dummy method, doesn't really do anything, but if we ever call it,

70
00:04:07,990 --> 00:04:12,430
the expectation is that we should supply the number of watts that some other service is going to need

71
00:04:12,430 --> 00:04:13,420
to work correctly.

72
00:04:14,770 --> 00:04:17,140
Well, that's pretty much it for our power service.

73
00:04:17,140 --> 00:04:18,400
That's all we need to put together.

74
00:04:18,400 --> 00:04:24,610
So now we need to somehow take this service and get access to it inside of both our CPU module and the

75
00:04:24,610 --> 00:04:25,390
disk module.

76
00:04:25,420 --> 00:04:30,310
Because remember, in order for the CPU service and the disk service to work correctly, they need some

77
00:04:30,310 --> 00:04:31,300
amount of power.

78
00:04:31,300 --> 00:04:35,790
So this is where the actual interesting nest stuff starts to kick in.

79
00:04:35,800 --> 00:04:41,410
So let's take a pause right here and understand how we start to share services between different modules.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,490 --> 00:00:05,360
Nyní jsme vygenerovali všechny tyto různé soubory a začneme tyto různé moduly

2
00:00:05,360 --> 00:00:10,250
propojovat, takže se nejprve zaměříme na připojení napájecího modulu a v něm obsažené napájecí

3
00:00:10,250 --> 00:00:12,800
služby k modulu CPU a modulu disku.

4
00:00:13,130 --> 00:00:15,880
Nezapomeňte, že si představujeme, že modelujeme počítač.

5
00:00:16,070 --> 00:00:21,380
Naše služba CPU tedy pravděpodobně potřebuje ke správné funkci instanci služby napájení.

6
00:00:22,070 --> 00:00:28,210
Nyní se budeme zabývat pouze vztahem mezi napájecím modulem a modulem CPU.

7
00:00:28,220 --> 00:00:29,540
Takže teď jen tuto část.

8
00:00:29,660 --> 00:00:34,820
A poté velmi rychle rozšíříme náš příklad a přidáme také diskové a počítačové moduly.

9
00:00:35,600 --> 00:00:40,160
Dobře, takže celkem máme dva moduly, v každém z nich je jedna služba.

10
00:00:40,580 --> 00:00:45,410
A víme, že potřebujeme dostat instanci služby napájení do služby CPU.

11
00:00:45,860 --> 00:00:49,040
To znamená, že budeme sdílet kód mezi různými moduly.

12
00:00:49,730 --> 00:00:53,930
Sdílení služeb mezi různými moduly bude fungovat trochu jinak než sdílení kódu

13
00:00:53,930 --> 00:00:55,040
v rámci modulu.

14
00:00:55,490 --> 00:00:59,420
Dovolte mi tedy, abych vám v krátkosti připomněl, co jsme doposud probírali v souvislosti s injekcí závislostí.

15
00:01:00,720 --> 00:01:06,810
V tomto okamžiku jsme v aplikaci zpráv využili systém vstřikování závislostí v rámci jednoho

16
00:01:06,810 --> 00:01:10,740
modulu, takže si jen zopakujme, jak to funguje.

17
00:01:11,250 --> 00:01:15,960
Představme si na chvíli, že bychom uvnitř našeho modulu napájení měli dvě různé služby,

18
00:01:16,230 --> 00:01:19,050
třeba službu napájení a službu regulátoru.

19
00:01:19,380 --> 00:01:24,480
A z nějakého důvodu možná tato služba regulátoru potřebuje ke správné funkci instanci napájení.

20
00:01:25,990 --> 00:01:31,240
Pokud bychom tedy chtěli sdílet službu napájení s regulátorem pomocí systému vstřikování závislostí,

21
00:01:31,240 --> 00:01:33,220
prošli bychom těmito třemi kroky.

22
00:01:33,560 --> 00:01:36,340
Jedná se o stejné tři kroky, o kterých jsme se učili před chvílí.

23
00:01:36,880 --> 00:01:39,730
Přidáme tedy dekorátor injectable do služby power.

24
00:01:40,330 --> 00:01:44,320
Do seznamu poskytovatelů energetických modulů bychom přidali službu napájení.

25
00:01:45,270 --> 00:01:50,100
A pak bychom vrežimu regulátoru definovali metodu konstruktoru av seznamu argumentů

26
00:01:50,100 --> 00:01:51,890
bychom uvedli službu napájení.

27
00:01:52,680 --> 00:01:55,470
Opět se jedná o sdílení kódu uvnitř modulu.

28
00:01:56,450 --> 00:01:58,370
Nyní se tedy nacházíme v poněkud odlišném scénáři.

29
00:01:58,580 --> 00:02:04,040
Nyní se snažíme sdílet kód mezi různými moduly, takže stále budeme mít zhruba tři různé

30
00:02:04,040 --> 00:02:07,290
kroky, ale celý proces bude jen trochu jiný.

31
00:02:08,240 --> 00:02:12,890
V našem případě tedy chceme přenést instanci služby napájení do služby CPU.

32
00:02:13,460 --> 00:02:15,860
Zde jsou tři různé kroky, kterými budeme procházet.

33
00:02:16,700 --> 00:02:18,410
Nejprve otevřeme náš napájecí modul.

34
00:02:18,830 --> 00:02:22,510
Do dekorátoru výkonových modulů přidáme novou vlastnost.

35
00:02:22,850 --> 00:02:25,370
Pojďme hned teď najít dekorátor výkonových modulů.

36
00:02:26,780 --> 00:02:29,630
Vrátím se tedy do editoru a najdu modul napájení.

37
00:02:30,560 --> 00:02:36,200
Zde je dekorátor modulu napájení, když jsme vygenerovali všechny tyto různé soubory, služba napájení byla automaticky

38
00:02:36,200 --> 00:02:38,540
přidána do seznamu poskytovatelů.

39
00:02:39,840 --> 00:02:43,170
Nebudeme tedy přidávat novou vlastnost tohoto dekorátoru.

40
00:02:43,620 --> 00:02:49,440
Systému pro vstřikování závislostí explicitně sdělíme, že chceme exportovat službu napájení.

41
00:02:49,770 --> 00:02:55,770
Chceme, aby kopie služby napájení byla ve výchozím nastavení k dispozici pro import do jiných modulů.

42
00:02:55,770 --> 00:03:00,990
Všechny různé služby, které připojíme k modulu a uvedeme v poli tohoto zprostředkovatele, jsou soukromé.

43
00:03:02,070 --> 00:03:06,920
Jinými slovy, ve výchozím nastavení nemá ke službě napájení přístup žádný jiný modul.

44
00:03:07,410 --> 00:03:13,350
Abychom toto chování změnili, přidáme do dekorátoru modulu novou vlastnost s názvem exports.

45
00:03:14,860 --> 00:03:20,680
A pak do ní vložíme službu NRA a seznam napájení, což znamená, že nyní chceme explicitně zpřístupnit

46
00:03:20,680 --> 00:03:25,120
tuto třídu ostatním modulům v rámci našeho projektu.

47
00:03:26,150 --> 00:03:26,990
To je první krok.

48
00:03:29,500 --> 00:03:33,640
Nebudeme přecházet na druhý krok, takže ve druhém kroku otevřeme

49
00:03:34,030 --> 00:03:40,900
náš modul napájení, najdeme jeho dekorátor modules a celý modul napájení importujeme do modulu CPU.

50
00:03:41,820 --> 00:03:43,010
Použijme tedy krok číslo dvě.

51
00:03:43,760 --> 00:03:46,520
Najdu modul procesoru.

52
00:03:47,900 --> 00:03:49,580
Nahoře budu importovat.

53
00:03:52,450 --> 00:03:53,710
Napájecí modul.

54
00:03:59,970 --> 00:04:06,630
A pak do dekorátoru modelu přidám import, pole, a vypíšu modul napájení

55
00:04:06,630 --> 00:04:07,710
takto.

56
00:04:08,880 --> 00:04:14,400
Takže to je krok číslo dvě, nyní jsme propojili modul napájení a modul CPU, když přidáme

57
00:04:14,400 --> 00:04:19,590
příkaz import, modul CPU a všechny různé služby v něm nyní mají přístup ke všem

58
00:04:19,590 --> 00:04:23,670
našim exportovaným službám z modulu napájení.

59
00:04:25,070 --> 00:04:30,080
Nyní můžeme konečně využít službu napájení uvnitř služby procesoru.

60
00:04:30,500 --> 00:04:35,720
Uděláme tak krok číslo tři, definujeme metodu konstruktoru služby CPU a do jejího

61
00:04:35,720 --> 00:04:39,050
seznamu argumentů přidáme službu napájení.

62
00:04:39,970 --> 00:04:40,840
Takže se chystám najít.

63
00:04:41,990 --> 00:04:42,850
Modul CPU.

64
00:04:44,230 --> 00:04:48,130
Je tam složka, ve které najdu službu, jste úplně nahoře.

65
00:04:48,400 --> 00:04:52,150
Chystám se importovat službu napájení.

66
00:04:58,960 --> 00:05:01,600
Při službě CPU v metodě konstruktoru.

67
00:05:03,370 --> 00:05:06,430
A stejně jako předtím, i nyní vložím do soukromí.

68
00:05:07,440 --> 00:05:11,790
Power service a anotace typu pak budou power service.

69
00:05:13,430 --> 00:05:18,410
Kdykoli tedy nyní Nasta vytvoří instanci služby CPU, v zákulisí se ujistí,

70
00:05:18,410 --> 00:05:23,930
že nejprve vytvoří instanci služby napájení a poskytne ji konstruktoru CPU.

71
00:05:24,920 --> 00:05:29,540
Nyní tedy můžeme uvnitř naší služby CPU definovat několik různých metod, které nějakým způsobem využívají

72
00:05:29,540 --> 00:05:30,890
službu napájení.

73
00:05:32,560 --> 00:05:36,160
Dobře, tak to je vše, takto jsme nastavili vstřikování závislostí mezi různými moduly.

74
00:05:36,250 --> 00:05:38,970
Tímto způsobem sdílíme služby mezi různými moduly.

75
00:05:39,790 --> 00:05:45,550
Série kroků je zde tedy poněkud podobná tomu, co děláme, když se snažíme sdílet kód uvnitř

76
00:05:45,550 --> 00:05:46,090
modulu.

77
00:05:47,140 --> 00:05:52,060
Jediný rozdíl je v tom, že nyní se trochu více zabýváme seznamem exportů a také

78
00:05:52,060 --> 00:05:54,010
seznamem importů modulu.

79
00:05:55,020 --> 00:05:57,180
Udělejme si zde pauzu a za chvíli pokračujme.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:04,440
We've now connected the power service to our service.

2
00:00:04,440 --> 00:00:09,270
So now just to kind of finish up this example for at least the CPU part, I want to define a little

3
00:00:09,270 --> 00:00:13,860
compute method on the CPU service that will make use of power in some way.

4
00:00:14,280 --> 00:00:18,870
So inside of my CPU service, I'm going to define a method called compute.

5
00:00:19,580 --> 00:00:21,390
And the compute method is going to be very simple.

6
00:00:21,410 --> 00:00:24,680
We're going to imagine that we're going to just take in two numbers.

7
00:00:26,290 --> 00:00:29,950
We're going to add them together and return them, and that's pretty much it.

8
00:00:30,310 --> 00:00:37,480
Also inside of here, I'm going to put together a console log and I'm going to say drawing ten watts

9
00:00:37,480 --> 00:00:40,510
of power from power service.

10
00:00:41,200 --> 00:00:49,420
I'm then going to call the power services supply power function, and I'm going to pass in the number

11
00:00:49,420 --> 00:00:49,990
ten.

12
00:00:50,290 --> 00:00:52,780
So this again, doesn't really do anything right here.

13
00:00:52,780 --> 00:00:56,530
It's just an example to say, hey, we're actually making use of that service in some way.

14
00:00:57,690 --> 00:01:00,240
Okay, so that is it for the service.

15
00:01:00,540 --> 00:01:03,900
So now we're going to backtrack a little bit.

16
00:01:04,980 --> 00:01:07,110
We're going to go over to our disk module.

17
00:01:07,740 --> 00:01:13,370
The disk service inside there also needs to get access to power service so we have another dependency.

18
00:01:13,380 --> 00:01:19,140
So we're going to go through this exact same series of steps to import the power module into the disk

19
00:01:19,140 --> 00:01:24,180
module so that we can get access to the power service inside of the disk service.

20
00:01:24,540 --> 00:01:26,370
So same series of steps.

21
00:01:26,760 --> 00:01:30,270
We are trying to share some code between two different modules.

22
00:01:30,270 --> 00:01:35,520
So step number one, we're going to open up our power module and add the power service to the power

23
00:01:35,520 --> 00:01:36,990
modules list of exports.

24
00:01:36,990 --> 00:01:39,980
Well, we did that just a moment ago so we don't have to do it again.

25
00:01:39,990 --> 00:01:42,480
Here's the power module right here.

26
00:01:42,510 --> 00:01:45,780
We already added in power service to the list of exports.

27
00:01:45,810 --> 00:01:50,760
Remember, by default, anything we list inside the array of providers right here is going to be accessible

28
00:01:50,760 --> 00:01:54,660
only inside the module unless we add it to the list of exports.

29
00:01:55,980 --> 00:01:57,730
So now we can move on to step number two.

30
00:01:57,750 --> 00:02:02,850
We're going to import the power module into this case now, the disk module.

31
00:02:04,630 --> 00:02:06,310
So I'll find my disk module.

32
00:02:07,170 --> 00:02:08,970
At the top I'm going to import.

33
00:02:09,910 --> 00:02:10,840
Our module.

34
00:02:16,400 --> 00:02:20,660
I'll then add in a list of imports and put power module.

35
00:02:21,310 --> 00:02:21,970
In their.

36
00:02:23,230 --> 00:02:29,890
So now anything inside of our disk module can import anything that gets exported from power module.

37
00:02:31,910 --> 00:02:34,130
So now let's open up our disk service.

38
00:02:36,130 --> 00:02:36,610
At the top.

39
00:02:36,610 --> 00:02:39,850
We are going to import power service.

40
00:02:46,060 --> 00:02:48,010
Well, then define our constructor method.

41
00:02:49,770 --> 00:02:52,140
And add the power service into the list of arguments.

42
00:02:57,210 --> 00:02:57,720
There we go.

43
00:02:57,720 --> 00:03:00,360
So we've now connected these two different services together.

44
00:03:00,360 --> 00:03:05,040
So same exact series of steps anytime we want to share code between different modules.

45
00:03:06,170 --> 00:03:10,280
Now on the disk module itself, let's define just some kind of imaginary function.

46
00:03:10,280 --> 00:03:11,900
Again, not really going to do anything.

47
00:03:11,900 --> 00:03:13,340
We'll just call it Getdata.

48
00:03:14,190 --> 00:03:16,770
So on the disc surface, I'll define Git data.

49
00:03:17,370 --> 00:03:23,550
I'll do another console log here and I'll say drawing 20W of power from power service.

50
00:03:24,890 --> 00:03:26,450
I'll call power service.

51
00:03:28,000 --> 00:03:30,040
Supply power.

52
00:03:31,240 --> 00:03:32,440
With 20.

53
00:03:34,610 --> 00:03:35,210
There we go.

54
00:03:36,370 --> 00:03:38,230
And then for Getdata, I'm just going to return.

55
00:03:38,230 --> 00:03:40,660
How about a string that says data?

56
00:03:40,690 --> 00:03:42,010
That's the data that we're fetching.

57
00:03:44,660 --> 00:03:49,520
Okay, so now that we've connected these two modules together, we've got one more step.

58
00:03:49,730 --> 00:03:54,860
We need to now connect the CPU module and the disk module to the computer module.

59
00:03:55,480 --> 00:03:59,920
So the computer controller right here needs a copy of the service.

60
00:04:01,480 --> 00:04:03,400
And the disc service.

61
00:04:04,510 --> 00:04:08,670
So we're going to use the exact same steps that we just went through twice over.

62
00:04:08,680 --> 00:04:10,990
So these same steps right here.

63
00:04:13,070 --> 00:04:17,360
When we use these two steps or when we go through these three different steps and we connect the CPU

64
00:04:17,360 --> 00:04:23,060
module and disk module up to computer, we do not have to also import the power module so we don't have

65
00:04:23,060 --> 00:04:29,180
to import all this power stuff up to the computer module because we have already imported the dependencies

66
00:04:29,180 --> 00:04:30,920
into CPU and disk.

67
00:04:31,010 --> 00:04:35,810
So we only have to connect the CPU module and the disk module to computer and that's it.

68
00:04:36,140 --> 00:04:38,540
So let's take care of this last step in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,130 --> 00:00:03,050
Let's form out this last connection.

2
00:00:03,050 --> 00:00:09,260
In order for our computer controller to work correctly, we need an instance of CPU and disk.

3
00:00:09,380 --> 00:00:15,890
So we're going to go through these same three steps once again to connect the CPU module and the disk

4
00:00:15,890 --> 00:00:17,720
module to the computer module.

5
00:00:18,560 --> 00:00:18,830
Okay.

6
00:00:18,830 --> 00:00:19,790
So let's get to it.

7
00:00:19,820 --> 00:00:25,460
Step number one, we're going to go into our CPU module and the disk module, and we're going to make

8
00:00:25,460 --> 00:00:30,590
sure that we export the services that we want to make available to other modules inside of our project.

9
00:00:30,710 --> 00:00:33,080
So let's first begin with CPU module.

10
00:00:33,440 --> 00:00:38,930
The CPU module defines the CPU service and we want to use this service somewhere else inside of our

11
00:00:38,930 --> 00:00:39,680
project.

12
00:00:39,710 --> 00:00:44,360
So I'm going to add in exports and put in CPU service.

13
00:00:44,600 --> 00:00:46,130
So on repeat for a third time.

14
00:00:46,130 --> 00:00:50,450
Now whenever you define a service inside of a module, that service is private.

15
00:00:50,450 --> 00:00:55,100
It does not automatically get shared anywhere else inside of your project unless you add it into the

16
00:00:55,100 --> 00:00:56,240
exports array.

17
00:00:56,920 --> 00:01:00,250
We'll then repeat the same step inside of our disk module.

18
00:01:01,100 --> 00:01:08,060
So on the disk module, I'm going to add in exports and I will export the disk service.

19
00:01:10,020 --> 00:01:11,250
So there's step one.

20
00:01:13,340 --> 00:01:17,630
Next up, step number two, we're going to go into our target module, the thing where we want to get

21
00:01:17,630 --> 00:01:23,390
access to some services and we're going to add in some other modules on the imports list.

22
00:01:23,690 --> 00:01:26,930
So in this case, we're going to open up our computer module file.

23
00:01:28,800 --> 00:01:32,150
At the top, we will import the two modules that we need inside of here.

24
00:01:32,160 --> 00:01:34,530
So that's going to be the CPU module.

25
00:01:38,730 --> 00:01:40,320
And the disk module.

26
00:01:44,400 --> 00:01:47,820
We'll then add in these two modules into a list of imports.

27
00:01:55,340 --> 00:01:57,020
And then finally, step number three.

28
00:01:57,050 --> 00:02:02,300
So we're going to go to the controller or the service or whatever that needs to get access to some other

29
00:02:02,300 --> 00:02:05,160
service, and we're going to define the constructor method.

30
00:02:05,180 --> 00:02:10,870
So in our case, we want to make sure that the computer controller gets access to CPU and disk.

31
00:02:10,880 --> 00:02:16,610
So we'll define the constructor on computer controller and add in the CPU and disk services to the argument

32
00:02:16,610 --> 00:02:17,150
list.

33
00:02:18,390 --> 00:02:18,600
Okay.

34
00:02:18,600 --> 00:02:20,640
So inside of computer controller.

35
00:02:23,200 --> 00:02:23,860
At the very top.

36
00:02:23,860 --> 00:02:27,220
I'm going to import our two services.

37
00:02:38,930 --> 00:02:40,430
I'll then define my constructor.

38
00:02:44,770 --> 00:02:55,900
And add in private service will be of type service and private disk service will be of type disk service.

39
00:02:56,480 --> 00:02:58,010
And that should be it.

40
00:02:58,890 --> 00:03:00,900
So now to actually test this thing out.

41
00:03:00,930 --> 00:03:04,200
Let's very quickly define a route handler on the controller.

42
00:03:04,820 --> 00:03:08,450
We're going to define a route handler called something like Run, and we're going to imagine that we

43
00:03:08,450 --> 00:03:10,550
are running or starting up our computer.

44
00:03:10,670 --> 00:03:18,320
The run method is then going to call compute on the CPU service and call get data on the disk service.

45
00:03:18,350 --> 00:03:23,390
We'll then take whatever gets returned from those two function calls and return it from our route handler.

46
00:03:23,510 --> 00:03:25,190
So let's give this a shot.

47
00:03:25,610 --> 00:03:30,530
Back inside the controller, I'm going to find the common import up here and I'm going to get the Jit

48
00:03:30,530 --> 00:03:31,400
decorator.

49
00:03:32,390 --> 00:03:34,910
I'll then define a route handler inside of here.

50
00:03:36,650 --> 00:03:37,160
That I'll call.

51
00:03:37,160 --> 00:03:38,150
Simply run.

52
00:03:38,940 --> 00:03:41,940
Then inside of run, I'm going to return an array.

53
00:03:42,150 --> 00:03:46,260
The first element of the array will be the result of making use of the service.

54
00:03:46,260 --> 00:03:49,550
And the second element will be making use of the disk service.

55
00:03:49,560 --> 00:03:54,450
So I'm going to return an array with this dot service dot compute.

56
00:03:54,450 --> 00:03:58,400
And remember, compute is supposed to receive two numbers that we're going to just add together.

57
00:03:58,410 --> 00:03:59,970
So I'll pass in one and two.

58
00:04:01,160 --> 00:04:03,260
I'll then call disk service.

59
00:04:04,080 --> 00:04:05,250
Get data.

60
00:04:06,970 --> 00:04:08,260
And that should be it.

61
00:04:08,980 --> 00:04:11,310
So let's now save all these different changes.

62
00:04:11,320 --> 00:04:14,230
We're going to go back over to our terminal and start our project up.

63
00:04:14,410 --> 00:04:19,780
So back over at my terminal, I'll do an NPM run start colon dev.

64
00:04:20,520 --> 00:04:22,620
And should not see any errors.

65
00:04:22,650 --> 00:04:27,360
If I see any errors right now, it means that I probably missed some import statement or some export

66
00:04:27,360 --> 00:04:29,550
statement inside of one of my different modules.

67
00:04:29,640 --> 00:04:33,960
So if you get any errors at this point, make sure you double check all the different import statements,

68
00:04:33,960 --> 00:04:35,880
all the different export statements and so on.

69
00:04:36,830 --> 00:04:43,340
Once I've got this all put together, I'm then going to make a request to localhost 3000 slash computer.

70
00:04:43,790 --> 00:04:45,740
I don't have to use an API client for this.

71
00:04:45,740 --> 00:04:50,360
Since we define this thing as a git handler, we can make requests directly from our browser.

72
00:04:51,540 --> 00:04:53,130
So I'll start up my browser.

73
00:04:57,830 --> 00:04:59,300
I'm making use of chromium.

74
00:04:59,630 --> 00:05:00,950
Hopefully it's actually going to start up here.

75
00:05:00,950 --> 00:05:02,370
Sometimes it lags a little bit.

76
00:05:02,390 --> 00:05:04,880
I'll tell you what, I'm not sure chromium is going to start up.

77
00:05:04,910 --> 00:05:06,170
Oh, there it is.

78
00:05:06,210 --> 00:05:07,220
I actually do have it.

79
00:05:07,230 --> 00:05:07,580
Okay.

80
00:05:07,580 --> 00:05:11,570
So localhost 3000 slash computer.

81
00:05:13,020 --> 00:05:14,050
And there we go.

82
00:05:14,070 --> 00:05:18,480
So see the result of our service and the result of our data service.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,640 --> 00:00:06,770
Let's wrap up this example by taking a look at a diagram or two and just imagine how all this module

2
00:00:06,770 --> 00:00:09,560
stuff relates to the dependency injection system.

3
00:00:10,130 --> 00:00:15,140
Okay, so we're going to focus on the power module and the CPU module and how they interact.

4
00:00:15,290 --> 00:00:17,210
So we are creating the power module.

5
00:00:17,210 --> 00:00:20,750
We list power service in the array of providers.

6
00:00:20,930 --> 00:00:22,700
So that's going to create our container.

7
00:00:22,730 --> 00:00:26,600
We've got our listing of all the different classes in this container and their dependencies.

8
00:00:26,630 --> 00:00:29,270
The power service has no dependencies, of course.

9
00:00:30,140 --> 00:00:34,190
So the big addition we've now added in is the idea of exports.

10
00:00:34,190 --> 00:00:39,830
So with this exports field, we can kind of imagine that the container now has an additional listing

11
00:00:39,830 --> 00:00:40,490
inside of it.

12
00:00:40,520 --> 00:00:45,320
It says, Here's a list of all the different classes that can be used in other containers or in other

13
00:00:45,320 --> 00:00:46,160
modules.

14
00:00:46,980 --> 00:00:53,220
So now whenever we decide to import the power module into some other module, such as the CPU, we can

15
00:00:53,220 --> 00:00:56,460
imagine that this is what is going on behind the scenes.

16
00:00:56,490 --> 00:01:00,420
So we've still got all the same stuff up here, but now we've got our CPU module.

17
00:01:01,060 --> 00:01:07,840
The module lists out the CPU service as a provider, and it's also got a listing of imports as well.

18
00:01:08,170 --> 00:01:15,970
So this import step we can kind of imagine means go into the power module and get that entire listing

19
00:01:15,970 --> 00:01:22,000
of everything that was exported from it and make it available to all our different services inside this

20
00:01:22,000 --> 00:01:22,720
container.

21
00:01:22,820 --> 00:01:26,890
So now the CPU service can correctly look up the power service.

22
00:01:28,070 --> 00:01:32,060
Now, just so you know, there's not actually multiple different containers behind the scenes.

23
00:01:32,060 --> 00:01:37,850
Instead, we have one container, but everything inside of it is scoped to limit what different classes

24
00:01:37,850 --> 00:01:38,360
can see.

25
00:01:38,390 --> 00:01:39,470
Other classes.

26
00:01:39,890 --> 00:01:44,570
Nonetheless, from the perspective of a diagram, it's kind of helpful to just imagine that we have

27
00:01:44,570 --> 00:01:46,310
multiple different containers.

28
00:01:47,150 --> 00:01:47,420
Okay.

29
00:01:47,420 --> 00:01:49,250
So that's pretty much it for modules for right now.

30
00:01:49,250 --> 00:01:53,420
So let's take a pause right here and start working on our next application in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


==================================================================================


# section 7: Big Project Time!

1
00:00:00,800 --> 00:00:04,130
I think we're at a really good stopping point on this messages application.

2
00:00:04,130 --> 00:00:07,340
So in this video we're going to start to discuss the next app we're going to put together.

3
00:00:07,370 --> 00:00:10,130
The next app is going to be kind of similar in nature to this messages.

4
00:00:10,130 --> 00:00:15,710
One where we are going to put together a relatively small API and continue to explore some of the features

5
00:00:15,710 --> 00:00:16,580
around Nest.

6
00:00:16,580 --> 00:00:20,420
So let's begin by discussing what this next application is going to be all about.

7
00:00:20,750 --> 00:00:24,360
Okay, so we're going to make a used car pricing API.

8
00:00:24,710 --> 00:00:29,360
Don't know if you've ever tried to soul sell a car before, but if you've ever tried to sell a car,

9
00:00:29,360 --> 00:00:31,760
it's kind of hard to figure out what you should sell your car for.

10
00:00:31,760 --> 00:00:33,470
In other words, how much money.

11
00:00:33,500 --> 00:00:38,150
So we're going to make an API that will allow a user to get an estimate on what they should probably

12
00:00:38,150 --> 00:00:39,530
sell their car for.

13
00:00:39,530 --> 00:00:44,370
Inside of our application, a user is going to first sign up with email and password.

14
00:00:44,390 --> 00:00:49,250
Once the user is logged in to our application, they can then use our app to get an estimate on how

15
00:00:49,250 --> 00:00:54,530
much their car is probably worth, and we're going to create that estimate based upon the make the model

16
00:00:54,560 --> 00:00:56,690
the year and the mileage of their vehicle.

17
00:00:57,680 --> 00:01:01,180
After user gets an estimate, hopefully they will go off and sell their car.

18
00:01:01,190 --> 00:01:05,690
And once they have sold their car, we're going to allow users to come back to our application and enter

19
00:01:05,690 --> 00:01:09,170
in what they sold their car for the actual dollar amount.

20
00:01:09,260 --> 00:01:15,170
We're then going to use this to further enhance our ability to estimate what cars are worth in the future.

21
00:01:16,110 --> 00:01:17,670
Finally to prevent abuse.

22
00:01:17,700 --> 00:01:20,940
We're also going to have administrator users inside of our application.

23
00:01:20,970 --> 00:01:24,750
These administrators are going to review all the different reported sales.

24
00:01:24,750 --> 00:01:29,820
So essentially all these reports from this step right here before actually adding them into our data

25
00:01:29,820 --> 00:01:30,390
set.

26
00:01:30,420 --> 00:01:35,970
This is to make sure that users don't add in some car sale amount where they say that they sold a car

27
00:01:35,970 --> 00:01:40,050
for like $5 million or something like that or $0 for that matter.

28
00:01:40,200 --> 00:01:46,260
So whenever user reports a vehicle being sold, we're going to require that an administrator reviews

29
00:01:46,260 --> 00:01:50,190
that report and approves it before we add that data into our data set.

30
00:01:51,380 --> 00:01:57,080
Okay, So that's pretty much it feature wise or I should say kind of like the flow of this entire application

31
00:01:57,080 --> 00:02:01,070
is going to be relatively small, but you're going to very quickly start to see that there are a lot

32
00:02:01,070 --> 00:02:04,840
of kind of side features we have to add in to put together an app like this.

33
00:02:04,850 --> 00:02:08,780
So even though it might seem like it's small, there still is a pretty good amount of code we have to

34
00:02:08,780 --> 00:02:09,470
write out.

35
00:02:10,010 --> 00:02:13,820
Well, now that we've got a rough idea on what we're building, let's take a pause right here and then

36
00:02:13,820 --> 00:02:17,870
start to investigate some kind of high level architecture for our app in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,260 --> 00:00:04,920
Now that we've got an idea of what our application is going to do, let's go back over to our terminal

2
00:00:04,920 --> 00:00:07,170
and generate a new nest project.

3
00:00:07,200 --> 00:00:10,620
So back over at my terminal, I'm going to stop my running server.

4
00:00:11,720 --> 00:00:13,850
I'm going to go back to a workspace directory.

5
00:00:13,850 --> 00:00:18,890
So essentially anywhere on your computer and we're going to generate another project with Nest New.

6
00:00:18,890 --> 00:00:21,670
And then remember, it's the name of our project.

7
00:00:21,680 --> 00:00:26,990
I'm going to call my project, my CV, which is short for my car value.

8
00:00:27,020 --> 00:00:30,350
If you want to name your project something else, totally fine, go for it.

9
00:00:31,190 --> 00:00:32,660
All right, so I'm going to start the installation.

10
00:00:32,660 --> 00:00:35,630
We will be prompted on what package manager we want to use.

11
00:00:35,630 --> 00:00:40,190
If you have both NPM and yarn installed, I'm going to use NPM once again.

12
00:00:41,130 --> 00:00:41,400
All right.

13
00:00:41,430 --> 00:00:46,200
Now that the installation is going, let's go back over to our sort of design document over here.

14
00:00:46,230 --> 00:00:49,980
I first want to begin with the design of our application by thinking about some of the different routes

15
00:00:49,980 --> 00:00:51,570
that we probably need to implement.

16
00:00:51,690 --> 00:00:56,400
So I went through each of these different line items and thought about some of the different routes

17
00:00:56,400 --> 00:00:58,380
that you and I probably have to put together.

18
00:00:58,470 --> 00:01:00,300
And here's what I came up with.

19
00:01:01,710 --> 00:01:06,330
So I think in total we need at least five routes or so to implement this application.

20
00:01:06,480 --> 00:01:09,330
In this diagram, I've got the method and route.

21
00:01:09,950 --> 00:01:14,660
Some information that might be contained inside the body or query string of the request, and then a

22
00:01:14,810 --> 00:01:17,240
description of the purpose of this route.

23
00:01:17,450 --> 00:01:21,610
So the first two are all related to signup of users and sign in.

24
00:01:21,620 --> 00:01:27,380
So we're going to allow a user to sign up or sign in using a post request that includes the email and

25
00:01:27,380 --> 00:01:28,790
password of the user.

26
00:01:28,880 --> 00:01:34,100
Well then of course either create a new user and sign them in or we will sign in the user as an existing

27
00:01:34,100 --> 00:01:34,700
user.

28
00:01:35,240 --> 00:01:40,370
Once a user is authenticated, we're then going to allow them to do two very specific things.

29
00:01:40,520 --> 00:01:45,740
They can either get a list of reports or essentially try to get an estimate for the value of their car.

30
00:01:45,770 --> 00:01:51,380
If a user makes a get request to slash reports, we're going to require them to also add in some information

31
00:01:51,380 --> 00:01:56,990
in the query string of that request, and that can specify the make and the model the year, the mileage

32
00:01:56,990 --> 00:02:00,770
and longitude and latitude for an estimate of a vehicle.

33
00:02:01,010 --> 00:02:03,230
So we're going to use all that information.

34
00:02:03,230 --> 00:02:08,390
So the make model year mileage and the physical location in the world to generate an estimate for the

35
00:02:08,390 --> 00:02:10,280
user's value of their car.

36
00:02:11,420 --> 00:02:13,670
We'll then send that back as a very simple response.

37
00:02:13,670 --> 00:02:17,990
So pretty much just a simple number that says your car is worth X dollars.

38
00:02:18,970 --> 00:02:22,020
We will also allow a user to submit a report.

39
00:02:22,030 --> 00:02:27,370
So remember, a submitted report is essentially a user saying, Hey, I sold my vehicle for this amount

40
00:02:27,370 --> 00:02:29,840
of money and here's some information about the vehicle.

41
00:02:29,860 --> 00:02:33,010
So a user can make a post request to slash reports.

42
00:02:33,660 --> 00:02:39,480
Including the make mileage model, year, longitude, latitude and the price of the vehicle that they

43
00:02:39,480 --> 00:02:40,090
just sold.

44
00:02:40,110 --> 00:02:43,970
And as I mentioned in the last video, this is going to add in to our data set.

45
00:02:43,980 --> 00:02:48,060
It's going to give us some more information about what the current state of the market looks like.

46
00:02:49,050 --> 00:02:53,580
And then finally, remember that whenever a user submits a report, we're not just going to immediately

47
00:02:53,580 --> 00:02:57,740
add that into our data set because a user might give us some fake or phony data.

48
00:02:57,750 --> 00:03:02,280
So we're going to make sure that before we ever add in a report to our data set immediately, we're

49
00:03:02,280 --> 00:03:07,760
going to first require an administrator user to make a request, which is going to be a patch to slash

50
00:03:07,770 --> 00:03:08,580
reports.

51
00:03:08,580 --> 00:03:13,770
And we also want to know the ID of the report that they're going to approve and then the user is going

52
00:03:13,770 --> 00:03:18,200
to or the admin is going to include whether or not that report is approved.

53
00:03:18,210 --> 00:03:21,360
So approved of true means, yes, this looks like a valid report.

54
00:03:21,360 --> 00:03:26,580
Let's allow it into our data set and approved a false means no, this is probably an invalid or phony

55
00:03:26,580 --> 00:03:27,150
report.

56
00:03:27,150 --> 00:03:29,550
Let's just go ahead and delete it or something like that.

57
00:03:30,440 --> 00:03:31,340
All right, so that's it.

58
00:03:31,340 --> 00:03:32,450
Five different routes.

59
00:03:32,480 --> 00:03:37,070
Now, along the way, I want you to know right away here, as we were building this application, we're

60
00:03:37,070 --> 00:03:40,910
going to realize that we probably need to tweak some of these different routes, and we're probably

61
00:03:40,910 --> 00:03:43,770
going to need to add some different routes as well.

62
00:03:43,790 --> 00:03:47,990
But let's start to kind of figure out what those different adds or tweaks or deletions are going to

63
00:03:47,990 --> 00:03:50,270
be once we start putting our app together.

64
00:03:51,090 --> 00:03:55,680
Well, now that we've got a better idea of what we need to do for all of our different routes, let's

65
00:03:55,680 --> 00:04:00,900
start to think about what different controllers, models, entities, stuff like that, services as

66
00:04:00,900 --> 00:04:01,350
well.

67
00:04:01,350 --> 00:04:03,530
We need to build inside our application as well.

68
00:04:03,540 --> 00:04:07,560
So let's take care of the kind of innards of our server in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,990 --> 00:00:04,620
Let's do just one last video on some high level design of our application.

2
00:00:04,650 --> 00:00:06,150
So now we've seen all these different routes.

3
00:00:06,150 --> 00:00:10,470
Let's think about the different controllers, services and repositories we're probably going to have

4
00:00:10,470 --> 00:00:15,030
to create, as I read through all these different routes right here and their purposes, it looks like

5
00:00:15,030 --> 00:00:18,270
we've got two kind of general goals inside of our app right now.

6
00:00:18,300 --> 00:00:24,300
We've got some routes related to handling users and authentication and we have some routes related to

7
00:00:24,300 --> 00:00:26,160
handling and creating reports.

8
00:00:26,340 --> 00:00:32,160
So I think that a good place to get started would be to assume that we have one controller related to

9
00:00:32,160 --> 00:00:33,720
handling users.

10
00:00:34,170 --> 00:00:36,510
Maybe we need a single service.

11
00:00:38,400 --> 00:00:43,800
To handle data access and business logic around those users, we should probably have a repository.

12
00:00:46,220 --> 00:00:49,490
That will handle the storage of these users in some kind of database.

13
00:00:49,490 --> 00:00:53,980
And then we're going to have a very similar structure for handling all of our different reports as well.

14
00:00:53,990 --> 00:01:03,170
So we probably want to have a reports controller, a reports service and a reports repository whenever

15
00:01:03,170 --> 00:01:07,100
you are starting off your own Nest application, this is a pretty safe way to get started.

16
00:01:07,100 --> 00:01:10,520
Identify the different kinds of resources that you have inside of your application.

17
00:01:10,520 --> 00:01:15,910
So in our case, we definitely have a resource like users and a resource of reports.

18
00:01:15,920 --> 00:01:20,660
Once you've identified those different resources, we can generally assume that we probably want to

19
00:01:20,660 --> 00:01:24,410
have a controller, a service and a repository for each of them.

20
00:01:25,120 --> 00:01:29,740
Once we've identified these different controllers, services and repositories, we're then going to

21
00:01:29,740 --> 00:01:34,720
very frequently group these different things together into their own separate modules.

22
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:37,810
So we are going to have a users module.

23
00:01:38,680 --> 00:01:40,180
And a reports.

24
00:01:41,180 --> 00:01:42,590
Module as well.

25
00:01:43,070 --> 00:01:45,880
We haven't really gone too much into modules just yet.

26
00:01:45,890 --> 00:01:46,940
At this point in the course.

27
00:01:46,940 --> 00:01:50,600
All I've said around them is that we use them to kind of wrap up different things we create inside of

28
00:01:50,600 --> 00:01:51,470
our application.

29
00:01:51,470 --> 00:01:56,270
So one of the big focuses on this application is going to be why we create modules, how we share code

30
00:01:56,270 --> 00:01:57,970
between them and stuff like that.

31
00:01:57,980 --> 00:02:02,390
So when put on these modules on here, if you're saying why do these modules exist, don't sweat it.

32
00:02:02,390 --> 00:02:04,550
We're going to go into great detail around that.

33
00:02:04,940 --> 00:02:07,580
So once again, this is a great place to get started.

34
00:02:07,580 --> 00:02:12,260
When you are beginning your own application, just assume that you're going to create a module for each

35
00:02:12,260 --> 00:02:17,930
separate resource, and each module can have a controller, a service and a repository to manage that

36
00:02:17,930 --> 00:02:20,330
kind of data or that resource that you're dealing with.

37
00:02:20,360 --> 00:02:25,460
Now, as I mentioned, as we start building this application, we are going to realize that there are

38
00:02:25,460 --> 00:02:29,540
some different routes that we need to create and there are some other kinds of data that we need to

39
00:02:29,540 --> 00:02:31,100
add into our application as well.

40
00:02:31,190 --> 00:02:34,400
So we are going to have to come back and tweak this setup a little bit.

41
00:02:34,400 --> 00:02:37,220
But once again, it's a good place to get started.

42
00:02:37,760 --> 00:02:38,090
All right.

43
00:02:38,090 --> 00:02:42,600
So now that we've got a little bit of a place to get started here, at least some idea, let's take

44
00:02:42,720 --> 00:02:43,620
one last pause.

45
00:02:43,650 --> 00:02:47,250
We're going to come back the next video and we're going to start generating some code to implement these

46
00:02:47,250 --> 00:02:48,180
different pieces.

47
00:02:48,210 --> 00:02:52,470
We're also going to start to take a look at how we're going to implement our different repositories,

48
00:02:52,470 --> 00:02:55,830
because this time around, we're not going to store data inside of a plain file.

49
00:02:55,830 --> 00:02:58,320
We're going to instead use a very real database.

50
00:02:58,320 --> 00:02:59,640
So a lot of stuff to come.

51
00:02:59,640 --> 00:03:01,980
Let's start writing some code in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,520 --> 00:00:06,170
Let's go back over to our terminal and we're going to use the nest to generate our controllers, our

2
00:00:06,170 --> 00:00:07,550
services and our module.

3
00:00:07,580 --> 00:00:11,540
Remember, we do not use the nest to generate repositories for us.

4
00:00:11,570 --> 00:00:16,550
The reason for this is that these repositories are almost always going to be very, very different depending

5
00:00:16,550 --> 00:00:18,250
upon how we are storing our data.

6
00:00:18,260 --> 00:00:22,660
So whether we are storing our data inside of a plain file or making use of a database.

7
00:00:22,670 --> 00:00:26,840
So we are going to eventually instead create these repository things on our own manually.

8
00:00:26,840 --> 00:00:31,880
So for right now, we're going to create six different things two controllers, two services and two

9
00:00:31,880 --> 00:00:32,600
modules.

10
00:00:32,600 --> 00:00:33,920
So let's get to it.

11
00:00:34,250 --> 00:00:42,890
Back over at my terminal, I will change into the my project directory and then do a Nest module users.

12
00:00:45,630 --> 00:00:46,860
Okay, so there's our module.

13
00:00:47,040 --> 00:00:52,050
Next up, we'll do a Nest module reports.

14
00:00:54,640 --> 00:00:55,960
Okay, there's the other module.

15
00:00:55,960 --> 00:01:00,880
And now we're going to repeat the process two more times to create two services and two controllers.

16
00:01:01,090 --> 00:01:05,170
So I'll first do nest G controller users.

17
00:01:06,980 --> 00:01:10,100
Nest G controller reports.

18
00:01:12,620 --> 00:01:17,270
And then finally, nest g service users.

19
00:01:19,040 --> 00:01:22,250
And nest G service reports.

20
00:01:24,710 --> 00:01:25,100
All right.

21
00:01:25,100 --> 00:01:26,090
So a lot of files.

22
00:01:26,120 --> 00:01:30,680
Let's open up our code editor inside that project directory and just inspect what we've got now.

23
00:01:31,130 --> 00:01:32,060
So here's my.

24
00:01:32,090 --> 00:01:36,860
My project directory already opened up my code editor between the last video and this one.

25
00:01:37,250 --> 00:01:42,020
If I go into the SRC directory now, I should see the reports folder inside there.

26
00:01:42,020 --> 00:01:45,140
I've got a module, the service and the controller.

27
00:01:45,790 --> 00:01:48,850
I should then see the exact same thing inside the users folder as well.

28
00:01:48,850 --> 00:01:51,820
So I should have a module, a controller and a service.

29
00:01:52,470 --> 00:01:59,550
And then finally, if I open up my app module, I should see that the users and reports modules have

30
00:01:59,550 --> 00:02:04,740
automatically been imported and I should see those two modules listed on this import slide right here

31
00:02:04,740 --> 00:02:05,370
as well.

32
00:02:05,520 --> 00:02:10,050
Remember, we haven't really gone into modules that deep just yet, so if you're not really sure what

33
00:02:10,050 --> 00:02:11,440
this imports thing right here does.

34
00:02:11,460 --> 00:02:12,030
No problem.

35
00:02:12,030 --> 00:02:14,880
We're going to go over that in great detail inside this application.

36
00:02:15,780 --> 00:02:19,680
All right, so there's our first start, a couple of different files and folders created for us.

37
00:02:19,680 --> 00:02:22,440
But as I mentioned, we still have to create these repositories.

38
00:02:22,440 --> 00:02:26,310
So let's take a pause right here and start moving forward on this application.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# section 8: Persisting Data with TypeORM

1
00:00:01,980 --> 00:00:04,830
Next up, let's take care of these two repositories.

2
00:00:04,860 --> 00:00:08,020
Now for this application, we're not going to store data inside of a plain file.

3
00:00:08,040 --> 00:00:10,980
Instead, we're going to use a very real database solution.

4
00:00:11,280 --> 00:00:16,040
When we start making use of databases with Nest, we've got two general options.

5
00:00:16,050 --> 00:00:21,330
So just to kind of set some high level disclaimer here, you can use just about any any database you

6
00:00:21,330 --> 00:00:26,130
want to with Nest, and it's going to work out just fine so you don't have to worry about using some

7
00:00:26,130 --> 00:00:26,910
particular database.

8
00:00:26,910 --> 00:00:29,190
You can use just about anything you want to use.

9
00:00:29,190 --> 00:00:34,860
However, there are two solutions that work very particularly well with Nest just right out of the box.

10
00:00:35,070 --> 00:00:37,770
The first is a library called Type ORM.

11
00:00:37,800 --> 00:00:44,340
Type is a library that can interface very nicely with a variety of different databases, not only traditional

12
00:00:44,580 --> 00:00:48,660
SQL type databases, but some NoSQL types as well, such as MongoDB.

13
00:00:49,290 --> 00:00:54,570
The other option that works very well right out of the box is Mongoose, which works only with MongoDB.

14
00:00:55,110 --> 00:00:57,300
In this course, we're going to focus on the use of type.

15
00:00:57,330 --> 00:01:02,230
ORM type and Nest together are really just a match made in heaven.

16
00:01:02,260 --> 00:01:06,350
They cooperate very fantastically and kind of support each other in a couple of ways.

17
00:01:06,370 --> 00:01:11,260
For example, type in a couple of areas is a little bit deficient and sometimes hard to use.

18
00:01:11,260 --> 00:01:17,080
But there are some tools inside of Nest that make type just a total pleasure and joy to make use of.

19
00:01:17,230 --> 00:01:21,340
So with that in mind, here's what we're going to do for our application for right now.

20
00:01:21,370 --> 00:01:26,650
We're going to be making use of Type ORM and as our actual database that we're going to interface with,

21
00:01:26,680 --> 00:01:29,410
we're going to be making use of a copy of SQLite.

22
00:01:29,530 --> 00:01:34,240
We're only doing this right now just because SQLite is very easy to use and very easy to get started

23
00:01:34,240 --> 00:01:34,780
with.

24
00:01:35,170 --> 00:01:40,210
Later on, as we start to finish up our application, however, we're going to swap out SQLite for a

25
00:01:40,210 --> 00:01:41,950
much more robust solution.

26
00:01:41,950 --> 00:01:43,920
We're going to use Postgres instead.

27
00:01:43,930 --> 00:01:48,070
So eventually on this application we're going to be making use of type and Postgres.

28
00:01:48,730 --> 00:01:53,560
Right now let's just install type at our terminal along with a couple of other libraries that we're

29
00:01:53,560 --> 00:01:56,770
going to use to get type and nest to work together nicely.

30
00:01:57,920 --> 00:01:59,360
So back over at my terminal.

31
00:02:00,130 --> 00:02:02,620
Still inside the project directory.

32
00:02:02,650 --> 00:02:07,110
We will do an NPM install at nestjs slash type.

33
00:02:07,130 --> 00:02:13,240
ORM type ORM and finally SQL lite three.

34
00:02:13,300 --> 00:02:15,250
So these are three separate libraries.

35
00:02:15,250 --> 00:02:18,820
Let me kind of get a couple of new lines in there.

36
00:02:18,850 --> 00:02:19,390
Come on.

37
00:02:19,390 --> 00:02:20,090
There we go.

38
00:02:20,110 --> 00:02:23,230
So we are installing the first library nestjs slash type.

39
00:02:23,560 --> 00:02:27,560
That is the library that makes type and nest work together very nicely.

40
00:02:27,580 --> 00:02:29,940
Then is the type library itself.

41
00:02:29,950 --> 00:02:35,170
And then finally is the very particular database implementation we are using in this case SQLite three.

42
00:02:35,320 --> 00:02:38,810
Eventually we are going to come back and install the Postgres client as well.

43
00:02:38,830 --> 00:02:40,300
We'll take care of that later on.

44
00:02:41,090 --> 00:02:42,230
Okay, so let's run that.

45
00:02:42,230 --> 00:02:47,240
And while that installs, we'll take another quick pause and then start to create these different repository

46
00:02:47,270 --> 00:02:50,300
files or something like them in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,010 --> 00:00:06,830
Just a moment ago, I said that getting type and nest to work together is really simple and straightforward.

2
00:00:06,830 --> 00:00:11,810
But even then, it turns out that we have to write out a pretty good amount of code to get type and

3
00:00:11,810 --> 00:00:13,130
nest to cooperate.

4
00:00:13,160 --> 00:00:16,100
It's not really the issue being that we have to write a lot of code.

5
00:00:16,100 --> 00:00:20,960
It's that we have to place a lot of code in some different locations and it can be kind of confusing

6
00:00:20,960 --> 00:00:24,710
about what all this different these different pieces of code are really doing for us.

7
00:00:24,740 --> 00:00:30,140
So before we start to integrate, type into our project at all, I want to show you one very important

8
00:00:30,140 --> 00:00:30,860
diagram.

9
00:00:31,010 --> 00:00:35,540
So this is what's going to be happening behind the scenes inside of our application right now.

10
00:00:35,540 --> 00:00:41,220
As we generated our app, we automatically got one module created for us that was called the App Module.

11
00:00:41,240 --> 00:00:45,200
We then generated two other modules called Users and Reports.

12
00:00:45,410 --> 00:00:50,780
So as we start to integrate type, we're going to do a couple of different things between these three

13
00:00:50,780 --> 00:00:51,950
different modules.

14
00:00:51,980 --> 00:00:57,730
First, inside of our app module, you and I are going to create a connection to a SQLite database.

15
00:00:57,740 --> 00:01:01,440
As a quick note, if you are not familiar with SQLite at all, totally fine.

16
00:01:01,470 --> 00:01:02,400
Don't sweat it.

17
00:01:02,970 --> 00:01:06,580
We're going to create this connection to this database inside of the app module.

18
00:01:06,600 --> 00:01:12,180
When we do so, that connection is going to be automatically shared down into the users module and the

19
00:01:12,180 --> 00:01:13,320
reports module.

20
00:01:13,500 --> 00:01:18,420
So when we start up our application, this single connection to our database is going to be automatically

21
00:01:18,420 --> 00:01:22,350
shared and spread throughout all the different modules of our project.

22
00:01:24,000 --> 00:01:29,400
Then inside both of our users module and our reports module, you and I are going to create two very

23
00:01:29,400 --> 00:01:30,480
special files.

24
00:01:30,510 --> 00:01:33,150
We refer to these as entity files.

25
00:01:33,180 --> 00:01:38,430
An entity is very similar to a model in just about any other framework or language with a couple of

26
00:01:38,430 --> 00:01:39,930
very small exceptions.

27
00:01:39,960 --> 00:01:46,620
An entity files defines a single kind of resource or a single kind of thing that we want to store inside

28
00:01:46,620 --> 00:01:47,520
of our application.

29
00:01:47,520 --> 00:01:53,340
So these entity files are going to say that there is a user entity, there is a user resource, there's

30
00:01:53,340 --> 00:01:58,020
a user kind of thing we want to store inside of a database, and these entity files are going to list

31
00:01:58,020 --> 00:02:02,760
out all the different properties that a user or a report is supposed to have.

32
00:02:02,760 --> 00:02:08,700
So, for example, a user probably should have say, an email and a password or something like that.

33
00:02:08,700 --> 00:02:13,710
So inside the entity file, we're going to say that a user should have a email that is going to be a

34
00:02:13,710 --> 00:02:16,890
string and a password that is probably going to be a string as well.

35
00:02:17,220 --> 00:02:20,700
We're going to do the same thing inside of our report entity file as well.

36
00:02:21,750 --> 00:02:27,330
Once we create these two entity files, we're going to feed them into nest and behind the scenes.

37
00:02:27,360 --> 00:02:34,950
Nest and type are going to work together to create a repository or a users repository for us and a reports

38
00:02:34,950 --> 00:02:36,030
repository.

39
00:02:36,180 --> 00:02:39,750
So this is very important when we are using type ORM.

40
00:02:39,780 --> 00:02:45,660
You and I do not have to create repository files manually as we did on our last application.

41
00:02:45,690 --> 00:02:50,910
Instead, these repositories are going to be created for us automatically behind the scenes.

42
00:02:50,940 --> 00:02:54,990
We don't even end up seeing a generated file or anything like that.

43
