00:02:55,020 --> 00:02:57,900
These are two classes that will be created for us.

44
00:02:58,830 --> 00:02:59,250
Okay.

45
00:02:59,250 --> 00:03:04,080
So at the end of the day, what we really have to do here is do a little bit of connection setup inside

46
00:03:04,080 --> 00:03:05,030
the app module.

47
00:03:05,040 --> 00:03:10,560
We then have to create a user entity inside the users module and a report entity inside the reports

48
00:03:10,560 --> 00:03:12,090
module, and that's it.

49
00:03:12,360 --> 00:03:16,590
So let's start right away by creating this connection inside the app module.

50
00:03:18,580 --> 00:03:19,720
Back inside my editor.

51
00:03:19,720 --> 00:03:24,010
I'll find the SRC directory and go to the app.module.ts file.

52
00:03:24,280 --> 00:03:31,810
Then inside of here at the very top I'm going to import type ORM module from.

53
00:03:32,400 --> 00:03:35,250
Nestjs type ORM.

54
00:03:37,150 --> 00:03:42,130
Notice that right here is lowercase, so make sure you do not have capital M.

55
00:03:44,110 --> 00:03:48,460
We're then going to add in type to the list of imports on this module.

56
00:03:48,820 --> 00:03:51,490
So type ORM module.

57
00:03:52,130 --> 00:03:57,620
We're then going to call the four route method on it and into four route, we're going to pass a configuration

58
00:03:57,620 --> 00:03:58,400
object.

59
00:03:58,730 --> 00:04:02,300
So what we are doing right now is this step right here.

60
00:04:02,300 --> 00:04:07,490
We are setting up that connection to the SQLite database and as you might be able to kind of guess by

61
00:04:07,490 --> 00:04:12,590
that four route method name, that connection is going to be automatically shared down into the all

62
00:04:12,590 --> 00:04:14,600
the other modules inside of our project.

63
00:04:16,500 --> 00:04:17,820
Inside this configuration object.

64
00:04:17,820 --> 00:04:19,920
We're going to add in a couple of different properties.

65
00:04:19,950 --> 00:04:23,490
The first one is going to be Type SQLite.

66
00:04:24,090 --> 00:04:27,870
So type, as I mentioned, can work with many different kinds of databases.

67
00:04:27,960 --> 00:04:33,270
With this type property, we are saying that we want type to create a SQLite database for us.

68
00:04:33,540 --> 00:04:36,030
We're then going to provide a name for the database.

69
00:04:37,210 --> 00:04:41,710
So I'm going to put in database and put in DB dot SQLite.

70
00:04:43,650 --> 00:04:46,950
We'll then add in an entities array that is going to start off as empty.

71
00:04:46,980 --> 00:04:52,440
For right now, this entities array is going to eventually list out all the different entities or things

72
00:04:52,440 --> 00:04:54,120
we want to store inside of our application.

73
00:04:54,120 --> 00:04:58,710
So we are going to eventually put in the user entity and the report entity.

74
00:04:59,800 --> 00:05:01,360
And then finally, we'll add on.

75
00:05:01,360 --> 00:05:02,440
Synchronize.

76
00:05:03,650 --> 00:05:09,500
Of true like, so we're going to go into a lot more detail on what synchronized means in just a little

77
00:05:09,500 --> 00:05:10,040
bit.

78
00:05:10,750 --> 00:05:11,170
Okay.

79
00:05:11,170 --> 00:05:12,340
And that's it for right now.

80
00:05:12,340 --> 00:05:13,630
So I'm going to save this file.

81
00:05:13,810 --> 00:05:17,680
Now, as I was typing all that stuff out, you'll notice I was getting a lot of errors popping up on

82
00:05:17,680 --> 00:05:18,280
the screen.

83
00:05:18,280 --> 00:05:20,320
Once again, that's all coming from Eslint.

84
00:05:20,320 --> 00:05:25,030
And as I mentioned earlier on, inside the course, I personally really don't care for Eslint, So it's

85
00:05:25,030 --> 00:05:26,110
a very quick aside.

86
00:05:26,110 --> 00:05:31,690
I'm going to go into my Eslint Rxjs file right here and I'm going to comment everything out inside that

87
00:05:31,690 --> 00:05:37,390
object because I really don't need all those extra errors coming up when I know that I've got some invalid

88
00:05:37,390 --> 00:05:38,860
syntax or something like that.

89
00:05:40,810 --> 00:05:41,080
All right.

90
00:05:41,080 --> 00:05:44,620
So now the last thing we're going to do is start up our application from the terminal.

91
00:05:44,620 --> 00:05:46,180
So back over at my terminal.

92
00:05:47,280 --> 00:05:51,060
I'm going to do a NPM run, start colon watch.

93
00:05:53,800 --> 00:05:56,310
And it looks like I might have missed a npm install.

94
00:05:56,320 --> 00:05:58,480
O start dash dev.

95
00:05:58,510 --> 00:05:59,410
My mistake.

96
00:06:00,210 --> 00:06:02,040
Start Colon Dev, not dash.

97
00:06:02,070 --> 00:06:03,000
That's better.

98
00:06:06,310 --> 00:06:06,610
All right.

99
00:06:06,610 --> 00:06:08,140
So it looks like everything is starting up.

100
00:06:08,940 --> 00:06:12,420
And now, very importantly, once the application finally starts up, there's just one last thing I

101
00:06:12,420 --> 00:06:13,350
want to show you.

102
00:06:14,040 --> 00:06:15,540
So eventually it will start up.

103
00:06:15,540 --> 00:06:16,560
I promise.

104
00:06:16,620 --> 00:06:17,350
Come on.

105
00:06:17,370 --> 00:06:17,790
There we go.

106
00:06:17,790 --> 00:06:18,480
Finally.

107
00:06:18,990 --> 00:06:20,910
Now, back over at my editor.

108
00:06:21,000 --> 00:06:25,200
You might notice that inside of your root project directory, you now have a new file listed inside

109
00:06:25,200 --> 00:06:28,170
of here, and it's called the DB dot SQLite file.

110
00:06:28,290 --> 00:06:31,290
So SQLite is a file based database.

111
00:06:31,290 --> 00:06:35,590
That means it's going to store all the information related to your database inside of one single file.

112
00:06:35,610 --> 00:06:42,090
So by putting in this database property right here of DB dot SQLite, that's going to cause type through

113
00:06:42,090 --> 00:06:47,070
SQLite to automatically create a new database file inside the root directory of your project.

114
00:06:47,100 --> 00:06:51,780
The really nice thing about this is that now you can open up this file and eventually we're going to

115
00:06:51,780 --> 00:06:54,330
see the raw data of our database inside of here.

116
00:06:54,510 --> 00:06:59,580
Obviously looking at raw data which is going to be encoded and just about unreadable to you and I,

117
00:06:59,790 --> 00:07:04,950
we can at least get a variety of different tools that will allow us to very easily inspect the data

118
00:07:04,950 --> 00:07:05,860
inside this file.

119
00:07:05,880 --> 00:07:09,780
So as we are doing active development inside of our application, we can take a look at the contents

120
00:07:09,780 --> 00:07:14,410
of that file with a variety of different tools and see all the data for our application up here inside

121
00:07:14,410 --> 00:07:14,960
there.

122
00:07:14,980 --> 00:07:20,380
So again, making use of SQLite in a little kind of development environment for right now is going to

123
00:07:20,380 --> 00:07:22,690
end up being really nice and really useful.

124
00:07:23,200 --> 00:07:24,940
Okay, So a lot of stuff in this video.

125
00:07:24,940 --> 00:07:28,630
So let's take a pause right here and move on with type in just a moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,420 --> 00:00:03,970
We've set up our connection inside the app module.

2
00:00:03,970 --> 00:00:07,360
So now we're going to go into our users module and the reports module.

3
00:00:07,360 --> 00:00:10,000
Inside both of those, we're going to create an entity file.

4
00:00:10,000 --> 00:00:13,750
When we create the entity file, we're then going to do some kind of wire up with it.

5
00:00:13,750 --> 00:00:19,090
And behind the scenes, as I mentioned, Type and Nest are going to automatically create repositories

6
00:00:19,090 --> 00:00:20,380
for both these entities.

7
00:00:20,410 --> 00:00:23,830
Remember, all these steps are around making use of some database.

8
00:00:23,830 --> 00:00:28,660
So by going through all these hoops, Tapeworm is going to do a lot of setup for us and allow us to

9
00:00:28,660 --> 00:00:31,720
write and persist data into some database of our choosing.

10
00:00:31,720 --> 00:00:33,730
In this case, we're making use of SQLite.

11
00:00:34,120 --> 00:00:38,710
So this entire process of creating an entity, unfortunately is just a little bit confusing because

12
00:00:38,710 --> 00:00:40,600
there are a couple of different steps to it.

13
00:00:40,690 --> 00:00:46,030
So to create one entity which is going to model some kind of resource inside of our application, we

14
00:00:46,030 --> 00:00:47,740
got three different steps.

15
00:00:48,810 --> 00:00:52,110
First we're going to create an entity file and create a class inside of it.

16
00:00:52,140 --> 00:00:57,180
This class is going to list out all the different properties that we expect our entity to have.

17
00:00:57,210 --> 00:01:02,970
So, for example, a user is probably going to have an ID, an email and a password, and a report will

18
00:01:02,970 --> 00:01:07,290
probably have something like an ID and maybe the price at which a car was sold at.

19
00:01:07,470 --> 00:01:11,070
So let's get started with step number one for our user entity.

20
00:01:11,100 --> 00:01:15,390
We're going to create an entity file, create a class in there, and list out a couple of different

21
00:01:15,390 --> 00:01:16,680
properties inside of it.

22
00:01:17,390 --> 00:01:20,900
So back inside my editor, I'm going to find the users directory.

23
00:01:21,380 --> 00:01:25,090
I'll then create a new file inside there manually called User Entity.

24
00:01:25,270 --> 00:01:26,180
It's.

25
00:01:27,410 --> 00:01:34,190
Then inside this file, I'm going to add in an import statement at the very top for entity column and

26
00:01:34,190 --> 00:01:36,620
primary generated column.

27
00:01:38,500 --> 00:01:43,240
I'm going to import all three of these from the type package.

28
00:01:46,400 --> 00:01:50,750
And I might have oh primary generated Mr. D there at the very end.

29
00:01:50,780 --> 00:01:51,590
That's better.

30
00:01:53,410 --> 00:01:55,300
All three of these things are decorators.

31
00:01:55,330 --> 00:01:59,680
These decorators are going to help type understand some of the different properties that we are going

32
00:01:59,680 --> 00:02:00,880
to add to our entity.

33
00:02:00,910 --> 00:02:05,350
We're going to go into great detail on exactly what these decorators do, how we can configure them

34
00:02:05,350 --> 00:02:06,760
and so on over time.

35
00:02:07,340 --> 00:02:10,760
Next up inside this file, we're going to create and export a class.

36
00:02:11,930 --> 00:02:13,650
Called simply user.

37
00:02:13,670 --> 00:02:18,350
Now, you might recall earlier on inside the course, I made a really big deal around naming conventions

38
00:02:18,350 --> 00:02:23,690
and said that for every every class that we create inside of Nest, we're always going to label the

39
00:02:23,690 --> 00:02:28,940
class with a name of the thing that this is tied to and then the type of class that it is.

40
00:02:28,970 --> 00:02:34,280
So if we were following that naming convention, we would call this something like user entity.

41
00:02:34,370 --> 00:02:39,500
However, by community convention, this is the one time that we're going to leave off the type of class

42
00:02:39,500 --> 00:02:40,450
from the name.

43
00:02:40,460 --> 00:02:44,210
So we're not going to call it user entity, we're going to call it simply user.

44
00:02:44,940 --> 00:02:46,860
And again, this is by convention.

45
00:02:46,860 --> 00:02:48,480
So it's not a strict requirement.

46
00:02:48,480 --> 00:02:50,790
You can call this thing user entity if you want to.

47
00:02:50,790 --> 00:02:52,170
It is totally up to you.

48
00:02:53,670 --> 00:02:57,840
Next up inside this class, we're going to list out all the different properties that we expect a user

49
00:02:57,840 --> 00:03:01,770
to have and all the different properties around a user that we want to store inside of our database

50
00:03:01,770 --> 00:03:02,550
eventually.

51
00:03:02,640 --> 00:03:07,950
So as I mentioned just a moment ago, all of our different users are probably going to have an ID that

52
00:03:07,950 --> 00:03:08,850
is a number.

53
00:03:09,270 --> 00:03:15,240
They're probably going to have an email that is a string and a password that is a string as well.

54
00:03:15,540 --> 00:03:18,300
Now, to be clear, I'm just kind of making these properties up.

55
00:03:18,300 --> 00:03:24,540
So based on my experience, I know based on applications I've built in the past that a user, if we're

56
00:03:24,540 --> 00:03:28,590
storing one inside of our application, should probably have these three properties tied to them.

57
00:03:28,740 --> 00:03:32,570
So it's not just a given that every user is always going to have an ID, email and password.

58
00:03:32,580 --> 00:03:34,920
It really comes down to the kind of application you are building.

59
00:03:34,920 --> 00:03:39,210
But in this case, I think these properties are very reasonable to store inside of our database.

60
00:03:41,230 --> 00:03:44,800
Next up, we're going to apply these three different decorators to the class.

61
00:03:44,890 --> 00:03:49,030
So I'm going to mark the overall class as an entity.

62
00:03:49,820 --> 00:03:54,230
I'm going to mark ID as primary generated column.

63
00:03:55,610 --> 00:04:01,580
And then I will mark email as a column and password with column.

64
00:04:04,020 --> 00:04:08,430
Now, as I mentioned just a moment ago, we will come back and go into great detail on what these decorators

65
00:04:08,430 --> 00:04:09,660
are doing for us.

66
00:04:09,690 --> 00:04:12,270
Right now, let's continue with the setup of our entity.

67
00:04:12,630 --> 00:04:14,580
So we are now done with step number one.

68
00:04:14,580 --> 00:04:15,990
We've got that entity file.

69
00:04:15,990 --> 00:04:17,820
We created the class inside of it.

70
00:04:17,850 --> 00:04:19,410
So now on to step number two.

71
00:04:19,440 --> 00:04:22,620
We need to connect this entity to the parent module.

72
00:04:23,240 --> 00:04:25,610
So to connect the entity to the parent module.

73
00:04:26,540 --> 00:04:28,690
I'm going to find our users module.

74
00:04:28,700 --> 00:04:30,110
So here's users module.

75
00:04:31,830 --> 00:04:32,850
Inside of here.

76
00:04:33,030 --> 00:04:43,530
We're going to add in an import at the very top for type ORM module from at Nestjs slash type ORM.

77
00:04:44,580 --> 00:04:50,850
We're also going to import the user entity that we just created inside of the user entity file.

78
00:04:53,220 --> 00:04:57,810
This is the same module we used back inside of our app module, so we're going to use that same module

79
00:04:57,810 --> 00:05:00,120
again, but we're going to call a different method on it.

80
00:05:00,120 --> 00:05:05,550
This time inside the module decorator, we're going to add in a new property here of imports.

81
00:05:05,910 --> 00:05:14,160
I'm going to put in an array, and inside that array we are going to call type ORM module dot for feature.

82
00:05:15,020 --> 00:05:18,080
We're going to call that put in an array.

83
00:05:18,080 --> 00:05:21,680
And inside that array we're going to list the user entity.

84
00:05:21,980 --> 00:05:25,730
So this step right here is what creates that repository for us.

85
00:05:29,220 --> 00:05:31,050
Then finally on to step number three.

86
00:05:31,080 --> 00:05:32,370
So we're all done with number two.

87
00:05:32,400 --> 00:05:36,420
Step number three, we're going to connect the entity to the root connection.

88
00:05:36,630 --> 00:05:39,180
So that's going to occur back inside of our app module.

89
00:05:40,100 --> 00:05:44,060
So back inside of our app module, I know these are a lot of files going on here.

90
00:05:44,060 --> 00:05:46,700
We're almost done back inside of our app module.

91
00:05:47,820 --> 00:05:52,250
At the very top, we are going to import the user entity that we just created.

92
00:05:52,260 --> 00:05:56,100
So I will import user from.

93
00:05:56,910 --> 00:06:00,540
Users slash user identity.

94
00:06:03,920 --> 00:06:08,930
And then in our array of entities right here, which is currently an empty array, we're going to add

95
00:06:08,930 --> 00:06:11,300
in the user entity that we just created.

96
00:06:12,170 --> 00:06:13,610
And that should be it.

97
00:06:13,730 --> 00:06:20,360
So now if we save all this stuff, we should be able to go back over to our terminal and we should not

98
00:06:20,360 --> 00:06:21,870
see any errors over here.

99
00:06:21,890 --> 00:06:26,060
If you see any errors at your terminal right now, then you likely made a mistake as we went through

100
00:06:26,060 --> 00:06:26,630
this process.

101
00:06:26,630 --> 00:06:28,280
Maybe a very small typo somewhere.

102
00:06:28,280 --> 00:06:30,350
So try to hunt down that very small typo.

103
00:06:31,740 --> 00:06:32,100
Okay.

104
00:06:32,100 --> 00:06:33,210
So now in theory.

105
00:06:34,300 --> 00:06:39,720
Type and nest working together should have created this users repository for us.

106
00:06:39,730 --> 00:06:44,380
So this is a class that we can use in the same way that we use the messages repository inside of our

107
00:06:44,380 --> 00:06:45,340
last application.

108
00:06:45,340 --> 00:06:51,130
We should be able to use this class to store data inside of our database, retrieve data, update,

109
00:06:51,220 --> 00:06:55,600
change, query, whatever else we want to do inside of our database.

110
00:06:55,600 --> 00:06:58,570
All that stuff is going to occur through this repository.

111
00:06:58,780 --> 00:07:04,210
Now, before we take a look at that repository directly, let's repeat this entire process very quickly

112
00:07:04,210 --> 00:07:07,800
in the next video and set up our report entity as well.

113
00:07:07,810 --> 00:07:08,590
So quick pause.

114
00:07:08,590 --> 00:07:12,490
We'll go through the same thing once again for the report and we'll move on from there.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,660 --> 00:00:02,560
All right, my friends.

2
00:00:02,560 --> 00:00:04,900
Who does not love practice, right?

3
00:00:04,900 --> 00:00:06,670
Practice is like the best thing in the world.

4
00:00:06,670 --> 00:00:10,480
So we're going to get some practice right now in creating an entity because we're going to do everything

5
00:00:10,480 --> 00:00:14,340
we just did in the last video once again for our report entity.

6
00:00:14,350 --> 00:00:17,180
So we have to go through the same three steps once again.

7
00:00:17,200 --> 00:00:18,280
So let's get to it.

8
00:00:18,280 --> 00:00:21,360
We're going to go through all these things much more quickly, of course, this time around.

9
00:00:21,370 --> 00:00:24,880
So to get started, we will create our entity file that's going to be a class.

10
00:00:24,880 --> 00:00:29,200
We're going to list out some properties in it that we would expect a report inside of our database to

11
00:00:29,200 --> 00:00:29,590
have.

12
00:00:29,620 --> 00:00:36,180
And remember, a port or a report inside of our application is meant to represent a user selling a car.

13
00:00:36,190 --> 00:00:40,690
So it's going to have some facts around that sale, such as what car was sold, the mileage where it

14
00:00:40,690 --> 00:00:42,550
was sold, the price and so on.

15
00:00:43,180 --> 00:00:44,680
So to get started.

16
00:00:45,930 --> 00:00:46,950
Back inside of our editor.

17
00:00:46,950 --> 00:00:49,020
I'm going to find the reports directory.

18
00:00:49,020 --> 00:00:53,250
I will create a new file inside there called report dot Entity dot ts.

19
00:00:53,910 --> 00:00:57,180
At the very top of this file I will import.

20
00:00:57,850 --> 00:01:01,030
Entity, column and primary.

21
00:01:02,130 --> 00:01:03,870
Generated column.

22
00:01:04,650 --> 00:01:07,800
And I'm going to import all that from Type ORM.

23
00:01:10,150 --> 00:01:15,010
Next up, we will create an export our class called report.

24
00:01:15,400 --> 00:01:19,330
I'm going to just right away apply the entity decorator because we know we need it.

25
00:01:19,900 --> 00:01:21,130
I'm going to add in the ID.

26
00:01:22,580 --> 00:01:24,590
And I'm going to mark that as a primary.

27
00:01:25,760 --> 00:01:27,380
Generated column.

28
00:01:30,340 --> 00:01:35,320
And then for right now, the only property that I definitely know that we want a report to have is the

29
00:01:35,320 --> 00:01:37,160
price at which a car was sold.

30
00:01:37,180 --> 00:01:41,560
As I mentioned, we're probably also going to have stuff around the location of the sale and what car

31
00:01:41,560 --> 00:01:42,820
was sold and stuff like that.

32
00:01:42,850 --> 00:01:44,620
We'll figure all those properties out later.

33
00:01:44,620 --> 00:01:49,300
For right now, again, the only property that I know for 100% is going to exist around report is the

34
00:01:49,330 --> 00:01:50,310
sale price.

35
00:01:50,320 --> 00:01:53,710
So I'm going to say that every report is going to have a price.

36
00:01:53,710 --> 00:01:54,850
That is a number.

37
00:01:55,580 --> 00:01:58,910
And I'm going to mark this with that same column decorator.

38
00:02:00,910 --> 00:02:02,860
Okay, so that looks good for our entity.

39
00:02:02,890 --> 00:02:06,100
So now we need to connect that entity to the parent module.

40
00:02:07,250 --> 00:02:10,940
So for that we will find our reports module.

41
00:02:11,330 --> 00:02:12,290
Here it is right here.

42
00:02:13,350 --> 00:02:15,510
Inside the reports module file.

43
00:02:15,510 --> 00:02:22,980
At the very top, we will first import type ORM module from Nestjs.

44
00:02:22,980 --> 00:02:25,140
Slash type ORM.

45
00:02:27,310 --> 00:02:30,130
We will import our report entity.

46
00:02:33,040 --> 00:02:36,010
We will then add in our list of imports on the module.

47
00:02:38,190 --> 00:02:43,560
Inside that array type or module or feature.

48
00:02:44,160 --> 00:02:49,650
Put in an array and to the array we will add in our actual entity of report.

49
00:02:53,950 --> 00:02:54,040
Okay.

50
00:02:54,100 --> 00:02:55,300
That is step number two.

51
00:02:55,330 --> 00:02:57,320
So finally on to step number three.

52
00:02:57,340 --> 00:03:00,010
We need to connect the entity to the root connection.

53
00:03:00,100 --> 00:03:02,470
So that's going to occur inside of our app module.

54
00:03:02,590 --> 00:03:06,250
So back inside of our App.module.ts file.

55
00:03:08,010 --> 00:03:08,730
At the very top.

56
00:03:08,730 --> 00:03:15,870
We will import the report from reports report entity.

57
00:03:18,370 --> 00:03:23,530
And then finally add that entity right there to our array of entities inside of our root connection.

58
00:03:24,430 --> 00:03:29,440
So we will put in report right next to user and that should be it.

59
00:03:29,650 --> 00:03:31,120
So let's save this file.

60
00:03:31,120 --> 00:03:35,440
And now if we save everything that we just changed, we should once again be able to go back over to

61
00:03:35,440 --> 00:03:38,170
our terminal and not see any errors over here.

62
00:03:38,170 --> 00:03:40,060
So for me, definitely looks good.

63
00:03:40,830 --> 00:03:41,070
It's not.

64
00:03:41,070 --> 00:03:46,860
The last thing I want to do for right now is go back into my file explorer on the left hand side here.

65
00:03:46,860 --> 00:03:50,190
And remember, I'd mentioned that when we set up that root connection.

66
00:03:50,990 --> 00:03:52,310
Inside the app module.

67
00:03:53,030 --> 00:03:59,480
C, type and SQLite are going to work together to create a database file for us because SQLite stores

68
00:03:59,480 --> 00:04:01,910
information inside of a very plain file.

69
00:04:02,030 --> 00:04:06,760
And it was this database property right here that dictated the name of that database.

70
00:04:06,770 --> 00:04:11,480
So inside of our root project directory, a file called DB Dot SQLite was created.

71
00:04:11,570 --> 00:04:16,550
If I now try to open that file, I now get an error around not being able to display the contents of

72
00:04:16,580 --> 00:04:17,310
that file.

73
00:04:17,330 --> 00:04:22,810
So just a little bit ago when we first created that file, it opened up as just a plain empty file.

74
00:04:22,820 --> 00:04:25,750
So now it looks like there's probably some data inside there.

75
00:04:25,760 --> 00:04:28,760
So I would like to take a look at what is going on inside this file.

76
00:04:28,760 --> 00:04:33,620
I want to do a little bit of debugging and understand what data is contained inside this database.

77
00:04:33,830 --> 00:04:40,070
To do so, we can either install some kind of extension or some external program to take a look at that

78
00:04:40,070 --> 00:04:41,180
SQLite extension.

79
00:04:41,180 --> 00:04:46,520
Or alternatively, we can install a extension directly through VS code that will take us allow us to

80
00:04:46,520 --> 00:04:48,380
take a look at the contents of this file.

81
00:04:48,380 --> 00:04:49,610
So let's try that out.

82
00:04:49,610 --> 00:04:54,150
I'm going to make use of an extension inside of VS code to look at the database inside of here.

83
00:04:55,590 --> 00:05:00,060
So to install this extension, I'm going to go up to the top bar and find view.

84
00:05:00,360 --> 00:05:02,190
I'll then go to extensions.

85
00:05:02,980 --> 00:05:05,110
We will search for SQLite.

86
00:05:05,790 --> 00:05:07,680
Should be the first result right here.

87
00:05:08,160 --> 00:05:10,650
And then go ahead and just click on install.

88
00:05:11,780 --> 00:05:17,450
Once this is installed back at the main screen, I'm going to go to View.

89
00:05:18,910 --> 00:05:20,530
And then command palette.

90
00:05:20,800 --> 00:05:27,700
The hotkey for this if you're on Mac is command shift P So either way, open up that command palette.

91
00:05:27,880 --> 00:05:33,400
We will then search for SQLite and we want to find an option that says open database.

92
00:05:33,790 --> 00:05:35,650
So if you run that option.

93
00:05:36,240 --> 00:05:41,010
You should then be given an option to open up the SQLite database that is stored inside of our project

94
00:05:41,010 --> 00:05:41,720
directory.

95
00:05:41,730 --> 00:05:43,230
So I'm going to click on that.

96
00:05:43,230 --> 00:05:48,210
And now on the very bottom left hand side of your editor, you should see a new little expandable bar

97
00:05:48,210 --> 00:05:50,880
down here called SQLite Explorer.

98
00:05:51,150 --> 00:05:58,440
If I expand that, I'll now see the DB dot SQLite file and if I expand that, I should now see our two

99
00:05:58,440 --> 00:05:59,630
collections of data.

100
00:05:59,640 --> 00:06:01,650
So here is our list of reports.

101
00:06:01,650 --> 00:06:04,470
And every report has an ID and a price.

102
00:06:04,470 --> 00:06:07,830
And I should also see my user collection as well.

103
00:06:07,830 --> 00:06:11,130
And every user should have an ID, an email and a password.

104
00:06:11,400 --> 00:06:12,930
So I would say this looks pretty good.

105
00:06:12,930 --> 00:06:15,440
It looks like we have now correctly set up a database.

106
00:06:15,450 --> 00:06:16,590
We've connected to it.

107
00:06:16,620 --> 00:06:21,390
We've created a couple of collections of data inside of it and each of these collections have the appropriate

108
00:06:21,390 --> 00:06:22,290
properties.

109
00:06:22,530 --> 00:06:27,720
So without a doubt, Type and Nest have collaborated to do a lot of heavy lifting and a lot of work

110
00:06:27,720 --> 00:06:28,440
for us.

111
00:06:28,440 --> 00:06:30,540
So that's definitely fantastic.

112
00:06:31,890 --> 00:06:36,150
Now that we've done some initial setup, it's time for us to actually understand how to work with type

113
00:06:36,690 --> 00:06:39,600
and how we're going to use it to store and fetch data over time.

114
00:06:39,660 --> 00:06:41,070
So we're going to take a pause right here.

115
00:06:41,070 --> 00:06:44,630
And in the coming videos, we're going to learn a lot more about Type ORM.

116
00:06:44,730 --> 00:06:49,620
We're going to learn more about these entities, the repositories that get created out of them and all

117
00:06:49,620 --> 00:06:51,170
that kind of associated stuff.

118
00:06:51,180 --> 00:06:52,300
So a lot of content.

119
00:06:52,320 --> 00:06:54,150
Let's get to it in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,790 --> 00:00:03,580
We've got a ton of type code set up inside of our project.

2
00:00:03,580 --> 00:00:07,540
So now we're going to go back over everything we just did and take a look at some of this type related

3
00:00:07,540 --> 00:00:10,120
code and understand what it is really doing for us.

4
00:00:10,150 --> 00:00:14,660
The first thing I want to focus on is some code that we wrote inside of our app module file.

5
00:00:14,680 --> 00:00:16,900
So here's the App.module.ts file.

6
00:00:16,900 --> 00:00:21,910
And inside of here, recall we had set up this type module, so we spoke about some of these different

7
00:00:21,910 --> 00:00:22,480
settings.

8
00:00:22,480 --> 00:00:27,010
We said that we can make use of type ORM with many kinds of different databases.

9
00:00:27,010 --> 00:00:31,660
So in this case we said specifically we want to use SQLite for right now as our database.

10
00:00:31,750 --> 00:00:36,700
We then specified the file that we want to save all of our data into, and then we loaded up our list

11
00:00:36,700 --> 00:00:37,720
of entities.

12
00:00:38,020 --> 00:00:42,700
And then finally the first option right here that I really want to focus on and help you understand

13
00:00:42,700 --> 00:00:45,340
what a very significant setting this is right here.

14
00:00:45,340 --> 00:00:46,570
We said synchronize.

15
00:00:46,570 --> 00:00:47,320
True.

16
00:00:47,590 --> 00:00:52,900
So let me give you a little bit of backstory on SQL type databases and help you understand why that

17
00:00:52,900 --> 00:00:54,640
is a very important setting.

18
00:00:55,820 --> 00:00:56,540
All right.

19
00:00:56,540 --> 00:01:01,030
So inside this diagram, I've got a database example up here at the very top.

20
00:01:01,040 --> 00:01:05,600
So we're going to imagine that maybe inside of our database we have a table of users.

21
00:01:05,600 --> 00:01:09,200
So that is a listing or a collection of different user records.

22
00:01:09,350 --> 00:01:11,720
A table has many different columns inside of it.

23
00:01:11,720 --> 00:01:15,170
So in this case, I've got a ID column, email and password.

24
00:01:15,410 --> 00:01:20,120
Whenever we create a SQL database, we always enforce a very rigid structure.

25
00:01:20,120 --> 00:01:25,790
So a table is always going to have the exact same number of columns with the same exact names, with

26
00:01:25,790 --> 00:01:27,980
the same types of data inside them.

27
00:01:28,130 --> 00:01:32,990
Anytime that we want to change this structure of our database in any way, we run something called a

28
00:01:32,990 --> 00:01:33,980
migration.

29
00:01:34,250 --> 00:01:40,010
A migration is a very carefully designed little piece of code that somehow changes the structure of

30
00:01:40,010 --> 00:01:40,910
our database.

31
00:01:40,910 --> 00:01:45,230
So this might rename a column, it might change the type of data in a column.

32
00:01:45,230 --> 00:01:47,120
It might add or remove a column.

33
00:01:47,120 --> 00:01:50,180
It might even add or remove a brand new table.

34
00:01:50,710 --> 00:01:56,170
So, for example, if we wanted to go to our list of users and add in a new piece of data around them,

35
00:01:56,170 --> 00:02:02,440
maybe if we wanted to record the username of every user, normally in just about any typical application

36
00:02:02,440 --> 00:02:07,330
out there that uses a SQL database, we would do this by writing a migration.

37
00:02:07,330 --> 00:02:12,450
And again, this migration would be a little snippet of configuration or really kind of SQL code.

38
00:02:12,460 --> 00:02:18,850
And this migration would tell our database to look at the table of users and automatically add in this

39
00:02:18,850 --> 00:02:20,140
new column right here.

40
00:02:21,240 --> 00:02:25,350
So you might notice that inside of our project right now, we have not really done anything like that.

41
00:02:25,380 --> 00:02:27,450
We have not written out any migration.

42
00:02:27,450 --> 00:02:29,700
We have not manually created our database.

43
00:02:29,700 --> 00:02:32,910
We have not really set up any rigid structure around our data.

44
00:02:33,030 --> 00:02:39,630
That is because Type ORM has a very special feature in it referred to as the synchronization feature.

45
00:02:39,870 --> 00:02:45,000
This Synchronize true feature right here is only for use in the development environment.

46
00:02:45,090 --> 00:02:50,460
This option, when set to true, is going to cause type to take a look at the structure of all your

47
00:02:50,460 --> 00:02:55,650
different entities and then automatically update the structure of your database.

48
00:02:55,770 --> 00:02:57,780
It's going to create and remove tables.

49
00:02:57,780 --> 00:03:03,450
It will add and remove columns and it will change the type of data stored in these columns as well.

50
00:03:03,480 --> 00:03:08,730
And this is all done automatically through type ORM with the use of those entities and all those different

51
00:03:08,730 --> 00:03:09,630
decorators.

52
00:03:09,900 --> 00:03:13,980
So as an example of this, let's take a look at our user entity file.

53
00:03:15,360 --> 00:03:20,760
Let's take a look at some of the different decorators we used in here and understand how to use those

54
00:03:20,760 --> 00:03:25,860
decorators to build a new table definition for us inside of our SQLite database.

55
00:03:27,130 --> 00:03:29,830
All right, so here's our class right here.

56
00:03:30,810 --> 00:03:36,840
So the first thing we did was we marked the entire class with the entity decorator that tells type that

57
00:03:36,840 --> 00:03:42,720
it needs to take a look at this class and it needs to create a new table to model this class or really

58
00:03:42,720 --> 00:03:45,380
a collection of instances of users.

59
00:03:45,390 --> 00:03:51,210
So by just putting on that entity decorator right there that tells type to reach out to our database

60
00:03:51,210 --> 00:03:55,080
at start up and make sure that there is a table inside there.

61
00:03:55,970 --> 00:03:57,410
Called users.

62
00:03:58,120 --> 00:04:02,320
So that's why we saw inside of our SQLite viewer just a moment ago that there was a table of users in

63
00:04:02,350 --> 00:04:02,800
there.

64
00:04:03,890 --> 00:04:10,250
Next up, the primary generated column decorator is going to be read in by type and it's going to cause

65
00:04:10,280 --> 00:04:15,890
type to take a look at the table of users and add a new column into it called ID.

66
00:04:18,120 --> 00:04:23,510
That column is going to serve as the ID of every user that we add into this table.

67
00:04:23,520 --> 00:04:27,960
And as I mentioned, this ID is going to be automatically generated for us.

68
00:04:29,040 --> 00:04:31,260
So it's going to be marked as a number.

69
00:04:31,290 --> 00:04:35,760
We never have to put in an ID if we're trying to create a new record, one will be assigned to a user

70
00:04:35,760 --> 00:04:37,020
for us automatically.

71
00:04:37,830 --> 00:04:38,090
Type.

72
00:04:38,100 --> 00:04:40,740
Aurum is then going to take a look at our two column decorators.

73
00:04:40,740 --> 00:04:44,070
And as you guess, it's going to create two columns inside this table.

74
00:04:44,100 --> 00:04:47,890
One will be named email and it's going to hold strings.

75
00:04:47,910 --> 00:04:50,960
The other is going to be named Password and it's going to hold strings as well.

76
00:04:50,970 --> 00:04:55,020
So we would get an email column that holds strings.

77
00:04:55,050 --> 00:05:00,460
Technically, inside of SQLite, it's called a var char varchar.

78
00:05:00,480 --> 00:05:02,130
You can really think of just meaning string.

79
00:05:02,130 --> 00:05:03,960
So I'm just going to label it string on this diagram.

80
00:05:04,980 --> 00:05:08,580
And then we get the password column that's going to have a string inside of it as well.

81
00:05:08,910 --> 00:05:14,820
So all of this updating of the structure of our SQLite database is done for us automatically by type

82
00:05:15,240 --> 00:05:16,170
at startup.

83
00:05:16,170 --> 00:05:20,510
And it's all thanks to this synchronize true flag right here.

84
00:05:20,520 --> 00:05:24,660
So that means that if we go back over to our entity and we start to add in some additional properties

85
00:05:24,660 --> 00:05:29,370
and mark them with that column decorator, or if we start to even change the type of these different

86
00:05:29,370 --> 00:05:34,740
columns, maybe if we change email to number or something like that when our application next starts

87
00:05:34,740 --> 00:05:39,270
up or when it next runs at our terminal type is going to see that update, it's going to reach out to

88
00:05:39,270 --> 00:05:44,880
the users table and update that email column or password or whatever else in some particular way.

89
00:05:45,280 --> 00:05:48,690
I want to highlight just one more time that this is very atypical.

90
00:05:48,720 --> 00:05:53,810
Most SQL Orms, which is what we're using right now, don't really behave in this fashion.

91
00:05:53,820 --> 00:05:58,620
Normally you and I have to write out these migration files that are going to change the structure of

92
00:05:58,620 --> 00:05:59,490
our database.

93
00:06:00,280 --> 00:06:04,460
The last thing I want to mention is that we only are going to make use of the synchronize feature in

94
00:06:04,460 --> 00:06:05,990
a development environment.

95
00:06:06,020 --> 00:06:12,020
It is extremely important that we never run that migrate or that synchronize feature of true in a production

96
00:06:12,020 --> 00:06:18,650
environment because it's very easy for that Synchronize flag to accidentally say delete a column so

97
00:06:18,650 --> 00:06:23,000
we could accidentally delete all the user emails in our production database, which would obviously

98
00:06:23,000 --> 00:06:24,080
be very bad.

99
00:06:25,010 --> 00:06:29,330
So when we start to move our application into a production environment, we're not going to rely upon

100
00:06:29,330 --> 00:06:30,830
that synchronized flag anymore.

101
00:06:30,860 --> 00:06:34,170
Instead, you and I are going to write out these migration files instead.

102
00:06:34,190 --> 00:06:38,360
So eventually, yes, you and I are going to understand more about these migrations, how we create

103
00:06:38,360 --> 00:06:40,970
them with type ORM and how we run them as well.

104
00:06:41,030 --> 00:06:45,140
But when we are strictly doing development of our application and we're not really sure about the structure

105
00:06:45,140 --> 00:06:46,370
of our database just yet.

106
00:06:46,400 --> 00:06:51,560
Well, making use of that synchronized feature is really, really nice because it saves us that entire

107
00:06:51,560 --> 00:06:56,210
task of having to write out those migrations, which can sometimes be just a little bit tedious.

108
00:06:56,930 --> 00:06:57,230
Okay.

109
00:06:57,230 --> 00:07:01,880
So now we understand the purpose of these decorators a little bit more and we have a better idea of

110
00:07:01,880 --> 00:07:03,620
what type is doing for us.

111
00:07:03,650 --> 00:07:07,750
Let's kind of start to focus on the repositories that were created for us.

112
00:07:07,760 --> 00:07:12,620
As we discussed in great detail over the last couple of videos and understand how to use these repositories

113
00:07:12,620 --> 00:07:13,730
in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,610 --> 00:00:05,650
When we wired everything up with type just a little bit ago, remember that we now have access to something

2
00:00:05,650 --> 00:00:08,850
called the users repository and the reports repository.

3
00:00:08,860 --> 00:00:12,790
So these are classes that are created for us automatically by Type ORM.

4
00:00:12,790 --> 00:00:17,380
We can now make use of dependency injection to get access to these things inside of things like, say,

5
00:00:17,380 --> 00:00:18,460
our services.

6
00:00:19,060 --> 00:00:23,200
We're going to make use of these repositories to find, update, delete and so on.

7
00:00:23,230 --> 00:00:26,930
The entity that this repository is responsible for dealing with.

8
00:00:26,950 --> 00:00:32,230
So in other words, if we ever want to persist or update or delete a user, we're going to always make

9
00:00:32,230 --> 00:00:33,970
use of the users repository.

10
00:00:34,000 --> 00:00:36,070
Same thing for the reports repository.

11
00:00:36,160 --> 00:00:40,270
Now we're going to understand and learn about how these repositories work in great detail in just a

12
00:00:40,270 --> 00:00:40,660
moment.

13
00:00:40,660 --> 00:00:43,690
But for right now, I just want to give you a very high level overview.

14
00:00:43,720 --> 00:00:47,950
My fear right now is that it might get a little bit confusing what the difference is between an entity

15
00:00:47,950 --> 00:00:48,860
and a repository.

16
00:00:48,880 --> 00:00:52,930
That's why I just want to be really crystal clear right now and help you understand what we use the

17
00:00:52,930 --> 00:00:54,070
repository for.

18
00:00:54,610 --> 00:00:59,500
So these repositories have a set of methods attached to them that we're going to use to work with data

19
00:00:59,500 --> 00:01:00,760
inside of our database.

20
00:01:00,790 --> 00:01:05,740
You can take a look at the full API or all the different methods we have access to at that link to the

21
00:01:05,740 --> 00:01:07,210
documentation right there.

22
00:01:07,480 --> 00:01:11,980
So we have access to methods like create, save, find, find one and remove.

23
00:01:12,010 --> 00:01:15,160
You don't have to memorize the definition I've put on here for each method.

24
00:01:15,160 --> 00:01:18,400
Just kind of a high level overview of what each method is about.

25
00:01:18,400 --> 00:01:21,570
And in some cases some of these methods might be a little bit confusing.

26
00:01:21,580 --> 00:01:24,640
For example, what's the difference between create and save?

27
00:01:24,670 --> 00:01:27,040
Don't worry, we're going to go over that in great detail.

28
00:01:27,490 --> 00:01:31,540
The other thing I want to really highlight right away, especially if you start to go and look at that

29
00:01:31,540 --> 00:01:37,810
official documentation, is that you might very quickly start to realize that inside the API documentation

30
00:01:37,810 --> 00:01:43,000
for these repositories, it seems like there are some methods that look very, very similar in nature.

31
00:01:43,090 --> 00:01:47,980
So for example, if you go to that documentation page and look up the save method, it says that you

32
00:01:47,980 --> 00:01:52,660
can use the save method to add data to your database or to update data.

33
00:01:52,780 --> 00:01:58,310
And it seems like what the Save method does is very similar to what two other methods do that are attached

34
00:01:58,310 --> 00:02:01,280
to the repository called insert and update.

35
00:02:01,580 --> 00:02:06,360
You might also notice that there is a remove method, but there's also a delete method as well.

36
00:02:06,380 --> 00:02:11,030
So what is the difference between these different sets of methods that appear to be just about identical

37
00:02:11,030 --> 00:02:11,780
in nature?

38
00:02:12,020 --> 00:02:16,730
Well, we're not going to go into super big detail just yet on what the exact differences are between

39
00:02:16,730 --> 00:02:17,470
these things.

40
00:02:17,480 --> 00:02:22,910
All we want to do is really highlight to you right away as you start to use type ORM, that inside this

41
00:02:22,910 --> 00:02:28,460
library there are always more than one way to do something in just about every scenario.

42
00:02:28,490 --> 00:02:34,250
There's even probably three different ways of creating a repository and getting access to it, not just

43
00:02:34,250 --> 00:02:35,300
including Nest.

44
00:02:35,300 --> 00:02:38,420
And this all this dependency injection stuff that we just set up.

45
00:02:38,540 --> 00:02:44,600
If you're just using type ORM by itself, there are seriously three different ways of creating a repository.

46
00:02:45,090 --> 00:02:49,770
So the one thing I really want you to understand is that as you read the documentation, they are always

47
00:02:49,770 --> 00:02:54,300
going to give you multiple different ways to achieve just about anything you want to do.

48
00:02:54,360 --> 00:02:59,950
And the difference between these different approaches really comes down to some very fine grained details.

49
00:02:59,970 --> 00:03:02,760
So unfortunately, this is one of the downsides to the library.

50
00:03:02,790 --> 00:03:07,440
You kind of have to understand some of the behind the scenes details of SQL and how databases work to

51
00:03:07,440 --> 00:03:13,860
understand whether you want to call, save or insert, or whether you want to call remove versus delete.

52
00:03:13,950 --> 00:03:19,110
So I'm going to do the best I can over time to help you understand why you might go with one route of

53
00:03:19,110 --> 00:03:20,970
implementation versus another with type.

54
00:03:21,000 --> 00:03:26,790
Or am I just again want you to understand that as you look at this documentation, sometimes things

55
00:03:26,790 --> 00:03:28,740
will be just a little bit unclear.

56
00:03:29,190 --> 00:03:29,610
All right.

57
00:03:29,610 --> 00:03:31,740
So enough of the scary stories.

58
00:03:31,740 --> 00:03:33,240
I think we've had enough of those.

59
00:03:33,240 --> 00:03:35,000
So let's take one last pause.

60
00:03:35,010 --> 00:03:37,530
And in just a moment, here we go.

61
00:03:38,160 --> 00:03:39,630
We're going to come back the next video.

62
00:03:39,630 --> 00:03:41,910
We're going to start working on our users service.

63
00:03:41,910 --> 00:03:48,640
We're going to use this service to make use of our users repository and create users, sign up users,

64
00:03:48,640 --> 00:03:49,860
all that kind of good stuff.

65
00:03:49,870 --> 00:03:52,570
So work on our user service in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,430 --> 00:00:05,060
All right, let's get back to writing some code and actually putting together our application.

2
00:00:05,060 --> 00:00:07,670
So in theory, at this point in time, we've got our repository set up.

3
00:00:07,670 --> 00:00:11,870
We haven't really seen how to use them just yet, but in theory they exist and in theory they are working.

4
00:00:11,900 --> 00:00:16,700
So we're going to start to put together our controllers and our services and these services are going

5
00:00:16,700 --> 00:00:20,150
to make use of the repositories that were created for us automatically.

6
00:00:20,540 --> 00:00:24,500
Now, remember, for our users in particular, we had said that we were going to have two different

7
00:00:24,500 --> 00:00:25,130
routes.

8
00:00:25,160 --> 00:00:28,250
We were going to have one route to sign up and one to sign in.

9
00:00:28,550 --> 00:00:31,940
However, we're going to do a very quick little diversion here.

10
00:00:31,940 --> 00:00:36,830
Rather than doing a sign up and sign in route, we're going to instead put together a slightly different

11
00:00:36,830 --> 00:00:39,380
set of routes around our user resource.

12
00:00:39,380 --> 00:00:41,060
So here's what we're going to do in total.

13
00:00:41,810 --> 00:00:47,360
So just as a very temporary thing for this application to help you better understand type and how it

14
00:00:47,360 --> 00:00:51,880
works, we're going to put together a couple of other routes around dealing with users.

15
00:00:51,890 --> 00:00:56,960
So we're going to have a route dedicated to finding a user with a particular ID, We're going to put

16
00:00:56,960 --> 00:01:02,060
together another for finding all the different users with a given email address for updating user and

17
00:01:02,060 --> 00:01:03,320
deleting a user.

18
00:01:03,320 --> 00:01:07,730
So these are routes that are not really required for the final version of our application.

19
00:01:07,730 --> 00:01:11,730
Again, we're just going to put them together to help you better understand type ORM.

20
00:01:12,350 --> 00:01:18,230
So for each of these different routes, we're going to put together a method on our controller that's

21
00:01:18,230 --> 00:01:20,510
going to serve as a route handler.

22
00:01:20,540 --> 00:01:25,550
So, for example, for this user creation route handler, we're going to have a method called create

23
00:01:25,550 --> 00:01:26,150
user.

24
00:01:26,860 --> 00:01:28,510
For finding a very particular user.

25
00:01:28,510 --> 00:01:30,910
We're going to have a method called find user and so on.

26
00:01:32,830 --> 00:01:37,420
Then to service or help implement each of these different methods On the controller, we're going to

27
00:01:37,420 --> 00:01:39,820
have some matching methods on our service.

28
00:01:39,970 --> 00:01:46,840
So whenever a request comes in to post auth slash signup, the goal of that request is to create a new

29
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:47,320
user.

30
00:01:47,320 --> 00:01:50,170
So that's going to call a method on our controller called create user.

31
00:01:50,170 --> 00:01:55,720
And the controller in turn is going to turn around to the service and call create and the service in

32
00:01:55,720 --> 00:02:00,880
turn is going to turn around to the user's repository and use the repository to actually make a new

33
00:02:00,880 --> 00:02:01,540
user.

34
00:02:01,870 --> 00:02:06,730
And once again, I just want to repeat, yes, it probably seems like we've got multiple layers here

35
00:02:06,730 --> 00:02:08,200
of kind of duplicated code.

36
00:02:08,200 --> 00:02:09,430
I totally understand that.

37
00:02:09,430 --> 00:02:14,020
But this is the application where you're going to see how nice it is to kind of spread out all this

38
00:02:14,020 --> 00:02:16,390
functionality over a couple of different classes.

39
00:02:16,390 --> 00:02:21,400
This really is where it's going to start to become more clear why this service exists at all.

40
00:02:22,210 --> 00:02:23,140
Okay, so that's the idea.

41
00:02:23,140 --> 00:02:27,930
We're going to put together these couple of extra route handlers just to better understand type ORM,

42
00:02:27,940 --> 00:02:32,230
and then we're going to eventually either comment these things out or delete them or maybe just leave

43
00:02:32,230 --> 00:02:33,190
them in place.

44
00:02:33,280 --> 00:02:35,110
The final version of our application.

45
00:02:35,230 --> 00:02:39,270
Last thing I want to mention is that we are going to be storing passwords here.

46
00:02:39,280 --> 00:02:43,330
We are going to eventually encrypt these passwords when we put them into the database, but we're not

47
00:02:43,330 --> 00:02:47,410
going to worry about that just yet as we're putting together these kind of initial route handlers.

48
00:02:47,410 --> 00:02:50,800
So we will add in some really good security, but we won't worry about that.

49
00:02:50,800 --> 00:02:52,630
More complicated stuff just right now.

50
00:02:53,290 --> 00:02:53,650
Okay.

51
00:02:53,650 --> 00:02:55,600
So enough of all this setup.

52
00:02:55,600 --> 00:02:58,030
I know in the last video I said we were just about to write some code.

53
00:02:58,030 --> 00:03:00,040
I think we've had enough lecturing for right now.

54
00:03:00,040 --> 00:03:04,600
So let's start to work on some of these different controller methods and server methods.

55
00:03:04,630 --> 00:03:07,750
Tongue twister service methods in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,310 --> 00:00:02,060
Enough talking.

2
00:00:02,060 --> 00:00:03,620
Let's get back to writing some code.

3
00:00:03,650 --> 00:00:07,870
So in this video, we're going to start to focus on the entirety of this first route right here.

4
00:00:07,880 --> 00:00:11,540
So we are going to add in a new method to our controller called Create User.

5
00:00:11,570 --> 00:00:16,640
We're going to make sure that it handles a post request for auth slash signup that has a body with an

6
00:00:16,640 --> 00:00:17,750
email and password.

7
00:00:17,780 --> 00:00:22,700
We'll then start to work on our service which will take in that email and password, handle it from

8
00:00:22,700 --> 00:00:27,860
the controller and then use the repository to create and save a new user inside of our database.

9
00:00:27,860 --> 00:00:30,680
So we're going to do the entire flow of this first route.

10
00:00:30,830 --> 00:00:32,390
This will take 1 or 2 videos.

11
00:00:32,390 --> 00:00:34,790
So without further ado, let's get to it.

12
00:00:34,970 --> 00:00:37,280
First off, back inside my editor.

13
00:00:37,430 --> 00:00:39,860
I'm going to find my user's controller file.

14
00:00:40,100 --> 00:00:41,390
Inside this controller.

15
00:00:41,390 --> 00:00:45,590
You'll notice that when we first generated this thing, we automatically got that controller decorator

16
00:00:45,590 --> 00:00:48,050
added in, and it has a string of users.

17
00:00:48,050 --> 00:00:49,760
Remember what that string does for us?

18
00:00:49,790 --> 00:00:54,560
It's used as a prefix for all the different route handlers we define inside this class.

19
00:00:54,560 --> 00:01:00,380
So if we define any routes inside of here, the first part of the route will be slash users and then

20
00:01:00,380 --> 00:01:02,610
whatever else for the rest of that particular route.

21
00:01:02,640 --> 00:01:07,470
Handler And we said back inside of our routing diagram over here that all of our different routes would

22
00:01:07,470 --> 00:01:09,630
be prefixed with slash off.

23
00:01:09,630 --> 00:01:13,350
So let's update that string right there to say off instead.

24
00:01:13,710 --> 00:01:18,690
So now all of our different route handlers inside of here will have a prefix of auth instead.

25
00:01:19,470 --> 00:01:20,100
Much better.

26
00:01:20,100 --> 00:01:21,480
That's exactly what we want.

27
00:01:22,840 --> 00:01:27,340
Next up, we're going to import from the common package right here.

28
00:01:27,370 --> 00:01:29,320
The post request decorator.

29
00:01:29,740 --> 00:01:34,330
So remember, we use this decorator to create a handler that will deal with incoming post requests.

30
00:01:35,250 --> 00:01:38,730
I'll then define a new method inside the controller with a post decorator.

31
00:01:39,120 --> 00:01:46,620
So we're going to say anytime someone makes a request to auth slash signup, we want to call the method

32
00:01:46,770 --> 00:01:48,240
create user.

33
00:01:51,040 --> 00:01:55,840
Whenever we call create user and we have this incoming post request, we definitely want to receive

34
00:01:55,840 --> 00:01:58,510
and validate the incoming body of the request.

35
00:01:58,540 --> 00:02:04,120
Remember that to set up validation inside of a Nest application, we're going to create a DTO and to

36
00:02:04,120 --> 00:02:06,880
create a DTO, we're going to use that class validator package.

37
00:02:06,880 --> 00:02:11,980
We also have to do some high level setup inside of our app, specifically inside of our Main.ts file

38
00:02:11,980 --> 00:02:16,570
and tell Nest that we want it to do some validation on all incoming requests.

39
00:02:16,870 --> 00:02:18,190
So a couple of different tasks here.

40
00:02:18,190 --> 00:02:21,880
We have to create the DTO, we have to set up some validation in the DTO.

41
00:02:21,910 --> 00:02:26,400
We have to add in that DTO to this post request handler right here.

42
00:02:26,410 --> 00:02:31,840
And then we also have to update our Main.ts file and tell Nest we want to use that global validation

43
00:02:31,840 --> 00:02:32,320
pipe.

44
00:02:32,590 --> 00:02:35,050
Let's first start with the highest level task.

45
00:02:35,050 --> 00:02:37,750
Let's do the configuration around nest itself.

46
00:02:38,560 --> 00:02:41,890
So inside of our main.ts file.

47
00:02:43,550 --> 00:02:45,560
At the very top we will import.

48
00:02:46,510 --> 00:02:47,920
Validation pipe.

49
00:02:48,700 --> 00:02:49,360
From.

50
00:02:50,180 --> 00:02:52,550
Nestjs common.

51
00:02:54,550 --> 00:03:02,020
Then right after we create our app, we'll do an app dot, use global pipes and provide new validation

52
00:03:02,020 --> 00:03:02,650
pipe.

53
00:03:03,460 --> 00:03:07,990
We're going to once again put in a configuration object, just like we did last time with a white list

54
00:03:07,990 --> 00:03:08,800
of true.

55
00:03:09,130 --> 00:03:13,660
And I recall, I think I said that I was going to explain what that property was all about, but I think

56
00:03:13,660 --> 00:03:14,230
I didn't.

57
00:03:14,230 --> 00:03:17,530
So I'll make sure that we clarify what this thing is for in just a little bit.

58
00:03:18,920 --> 00:03:20,040
Okay, that looks good.

59
00:03:20,060 --> 00:03:23,480
So now, next up, we can create a new file.

60
00:03:23,480 --> 00:03:29,510
And remember, DTOs are used to validate incoming request bodies inside my users directory.

61
00:03:29,510 --> 00:03:32,150
I'll create a new folder called DTOs.

62
00:03:32,450 --> 00:03:38,980
And then inside that directory we'll make a new file called Create User DTO dot ts.

63
00:03:40,810 --> 00:03:45,400
So inside this file, we're going to write out a very simple class that lists all the different properties

64
00:03:45,400 --> 00:03:48,220
that an incoming request to create a user should have.

65
00:03:48,250 --> 00:03:50,920
We're also going to define some validation rules around it.

66
00:03:50,950 --> 00:03:52,640
Let's define the class first.

67
00:03:52,660 --> 00:03:56,560
So we'll say export class, create user.

68
00:03:57,590 --> 00:03:58,280
DTO.

69
00:03:59,360 --> 00:04:04,400
And we expect an incoming request that is intended to create a user to have an email that is probably

70
00:04:04,400 --> 00:04:09,050
supposed to be an email or a string and a password that should probably be a string as well.

71
00:04:09,810 --> 00:04:15,780
So we'll say that the create user will have an email that is a string and a password that is a string.

72
00:04:16,600 --> 00:04:20,320
Recall that just adding these properties in doesn't really do a whole lot for us.

73
00:04:20,350 --> 00:04:25,120
We also have to make use of that class validator package and define some validation rules around these

74
00:04:25,120 --> 00:04:26,200
different properties.

75
00:04:26,560 --> 00:04:31,360
Let's first install that class validator package into our project and then we can use those different

76
00:04:31,360 --> 00:04:34,300
validators to validate email and password.

77
00:04:35,370 --> 00:04:38,580
So back at our terminal, I'm going to stop my running server.

78
00:04:39,680 --> 00:04:47,270
And then do an NPM install for class validator and the companion package of class transformer.

79
00:04:50,490 --> 00:04:54,990
We'll then do another NPM run, start colon dev to start our server back up.

80
00:04:57,820 --> 00:04:58,090
Okay.

81
00:04:59,050 --> 00:05:06,760
Now back over at our editor at the very top we will import from class validator and we probably want

82
00:05:06,790 --> 00:05:10,750
one validator that is going to make sure that our email actually looks like an email and we probably

83
00:05:10,750 --> 00:05:15,130
want another that makes sure that our password actually looks like a password or is actually a string

84
00:05:15,130 --> 00:05:16,300
or something like that.

85
00:05:16,660 --> 00:05:21,790
So we're going to get the is email decorator and is string.

86
00:05:22,060 --> 00:05:25,390
Remember that we looked at some documentation around class validator.

87
00:05:25,390 --> 00:05:28,740
So these are two decorators that are implemented by that package.

88
00:05:28,750 --> 00:05:31,450
We just apply them to the properties that we want to validate.

89
00:05:32,290 --> 00:05:34,630
So we'll then apply the first decorator.

90
00:05:36,250 --> 00:05:37,330
And the second one.

91
00:05:40,340 --> 00:05:45,620
Okay, so we got our DTO, we have set up our pipe back inside the Main.ts file.

92
00:05:45,650 --> 00:05:48,490
So now the last thing we have to do is go back over to our controller.

93
00:05:48,500 --> 00:05:53,630
We're going to import the create user DTO, we're going to tell Nest that we expect this route handler

94
00:05:53,630 --> 00:05:58,670
to receive a body and we want it to be validated against the create user DTO.

95
00:05:59,540 --> 00:06:02,930
So at the very top of this file, we will first import.

96
00:06:04,400 --> 00:06:06,260
The create user.

97
00:06:07,960 --> 00:06:08,340
All right.

98
00:06:08,350 --> 00:06:09,160
There we go.

99
00:06:10,000 --> 00:06:14,590
From DTOs create user DTO.

100
00:06:15,420 --> 00:06:20,010
And then to specifically tell Nest that we want it to extract the body of the incoming request.

101
00:06:20,040 --> 00:06:24,840
We will also import the body decorator from Nestjs Common.

102
00:06:24,930 --> 00:06:26,670
Remember, this is a very common theme.

103
00:06:26,670 --> 00:06:32,070
We make use of decorators to tell Nest that we want to extract information from the incoming request.

104
00:06:32,940 --> 00:06:35,790
So then we'll make use of the body decorator.

105
00:06:37,540 --> 00:06:43,120
We're going to call this argument simply body and we're going to say that it should be of type create

106
00:06:43,150 --> 00:06:44,620
user DTO.

107
00:06:46,310 --> 00:06:50,540
So now, in theory, whenever we make this post request, Nest is going to validate the body of the

108
00:06:50,540 --> 00:06:54,650
incoming request against that DTO and make sure that we have an email that looks like an email and a

109
00:06:54,650 --> 00:06:56,090
password that is a string.

110
00:06:56,750 --> 00:07:00,770
Finally, let's just do for right now a simple console log of body.

111
00:07:02,310 --> 00:07:04,140
Okay, so this all looks pretty good.

112
00:07:04,170 --> 00:07:07,470
Now, this is a very long video, so let's take a quick pause right here.

113
00:07:07,470 --> 00:07:11,940
We'll come back in just a moment and we'll make use of our client to test out this route handler and

114
00:07:11,940 --> 00:07:15,180
make sure that the validation and everything is working as expected.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,250 --> 00:00:05,990
Let's open up our clients and use them to test out this new route handler we just put together.

2
00:00:06,020 --> 00:00:10,880
Now, remember, you can use Postman if you want or any other client, but I'm going to use the VS code

3
00:00:10,880 --> 00:00:12,730
extension rest client.

4
00:00:12,740 --> 00:00:17,150
So to use that extension, I would create a file somewhere inside my project.

5
00:00:17,150 --> 00:00:22,220
Back on the messages application, we created an Http file inside of our root project directory.

6
00:00:22,220 --> 00:00:25,010
But on this application we're going to have several different controllers.

7
00:00:25,010 --> 00:00:30,740
So I'm going to instead create an Http file inside my users directory and this will have a couple of

8
00:00:30,740 --> 00:00:34,880
sample requests that I'm going to issue against, specifically the user's controller.

9
00:00:35,090 --> 00:00:41,660
So I will call this file request dot http and I created it once again inside my users directory.

10
00:00:42,400 --> 00:00:49,270
So then inside of here we want to make a post request to localhost 3000 slash auth slash sign up.

11
00:00:50,290 --> 00:00:51,760
So I'm going to say post.

12
00:00:53,360 --> 00:00:54,260
Localhost.

13
00:00:54,260 --> 00:00:55,280
3000.

14
00:00:56,880 --> 00:00:58,230
Off, sign up.

15
00:00:58,680 --> 00:01:00,600
I'm going to put a comment on this thing as well.

16
00:01:00,600 --> 00:01:03,000
Just remember exactly what this thing is meant to do.

17
00:01:03,000 --> 00:01:06,090
I'm going to say create a new user.

18
00:01:07,050 --> 00:01:08,460
Because it is a post request.

19
00:01:08,460 --> 00:01:12,420
I have to put in a content type of application Json.

20
00:01:13,200 --> 00:01:17,460
Well, then put in one empty line right there and I'll put in the body of the request.

21
00:01:17,490 --> 00:01:19,830
So my first request is going to be 100% valid.

22
00:01:19,830 --> 00:01:23,580
So I want to put in an email that looks like an email.

23
00:01:26,050 --> 00:01:27,340
And a password.

24
00:01:28,940 --> 00:01:29,990
That is a string.

25
00:01:32,640 --> 00:01:34,440
And then going to send the request off.

26
00:01:35,010 --> 00:01:37,380
It looks like I got back a successful status code.

27
00:01:37,380 --> 00:01:38,220
That's very good.

28
00:01:38,220 --> 00:01:42,780
And if I go back over to my terminal, I should see the body of the request console logged right there.

29
00:01:42,810 --> 00:01:43,590
Perfect.

30
00:01:43,800 --> 00:01:47,190
So now if we ever provide an email that is invalid.

31
00:01:47,220 --> 00:01:51,870
So let's say we put in just a plain string like so and send the request off.

32
00:01:51,900 --> 00:01:56,430
We should now get back a status code of 400 and have something that says email must be an email.

33
00:01:57,160 --> 00:01:57,460
All right.

34
00:01:57,480 --> 00:01:58,840
Let's say this looks pretty good.

35
00:01:59,170 --> 00:02:03,160
If we also remove a property and send the request off.

36
00:02:03,280 --> 00:02:03,730
Yep.

37
00:02:03,730 --> 00:02:05,080
Just like we saw previously.

38
00:02:05,110 --> 00:02:08,320
Definitely captures the fact that we did not provide a valid password.

39
00:02:09,130 --> 00:02:13,150
Okay, So let's say that step one here of setting up the controller and getting the body of the request

40
00:02:13,150 --> 00:02:15,220
is working just as expected.

41
00:02:16,880 --> 00:02:19,520
Before we move on, there's one other quick thing I want to mention.

42
00:02:19,520 --> 00:02:24,740
Remember, back inside of our Main.ts file, we had added in that white list of true property, and

43
00:02:24,740 --> 00:02:28,220
I told you that was going to explain on this application what that was all about.

44
00:02:28,250 --> 00:02:33,080
The purpose of this property is to make sure that incoming requests don't have extraneous properties

45
00:02:33,080 --> 00:02:35,090
in the body that we are not expecting.

46
00:02:35,090 --> 00:02:41,270
So for example, inside of our create user, DTO, we had said that only the email and password properties

47
00:02:41,270 --> 00:02:44,420
should exist by adding in white list of true right there.

48
00:02:44,420 --> 00:02:48,410
That's going to make sure that any additional properties that we send along with the request will be

49
00:02:48,410 --> 00:02:50,110
stripped out for us automatically.

50
00:02:50,120 --> 00:02:53,600
So if I put in an extra property right there and send the request off.

51
00:02:54,160 --> 00:02:57,970
Well, let me make sure I've actually got a valid email really quickly.

52
00:02:58,000 --> 00:02:58,450
There we go.

53
00:02:58,450 --> 00:02:59,210
That's better.

54
00:02:59,230 --> 00:03:02,110
So now if I send the request off, I see a success.

55
00:03:02,110 --> 00:03:08,290
But if we go back over to our terminal and look at the body, you will notice that we do not have that

56
00:03:08,290 --> 00:03:10,720
extra property inside the body of the request.

57
00:03:10,990 --> 00:03:16,510
This is done primarily as a security concern because we don't want to allow users to add in additional

58
00:03:16,510 --> 00:03:22,570
properties to incoming requests such as, say, maybe admin true and try to create a new user that is

59
00:03:22,570 --> 00:03:24,580
marked as an admin or something like that.

60
00:03:24,610 --> 00:03:27,550
Now, our code doesn't actually make use of this flag at all.

61
00:03:27,550 --> 00:03:28,900
This is just an example.

62
00:03:28,990 --> 00:03:34,600
Rarely do we ever want users to be able to add in additional properties to request unless we are explicitly

63
00:03:34,600 --> 00:03:36,200
expecting those properties.

64
00:03:36,220 --> 00:03:40,420
So again, adding in whitelist of true, just a very small security thing.

65
00:03:41,570 --> 00:03:41,930
Okay.

66
00:03:41,930 --> 00:03:45,870
So I'd say that our first test of our API is looking pretty good.

67
00:03:45,890 --> 00:03:51,170
So let's take one more pause and then continue on to our users service and create a brand new user in

68
00:03:51,170 --> 00:03:52,040
just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


==================================================================================



# section 9: Creating and Saving User Data

1
00:00:01,540 --> 00:00:03,190
We put together our users controller.

2
00:00:03,190 --> 00:00:08,020
So let's now go over to our service and we're going to create a create method over there inside of our

3
00:00:08,020 --> 00:00:08,570
service.

4
00:00:08,590 --> 00:00:12,640
The Create method is going to make use of the users repository and that means we're going to have to

5
00:00:12,640 --> 00:00:14,470
hook up the repository to the service.

6
00:00:14,500 --> 00:00:18,700
We're going to do that hook up using dependency injection and that hook up is going to be just a little

7
00:00:18,700 --> 00:00:22,300
bit confusing, a little bit complicated, but hey, we'll get through it.

8
00:00:22,910 --> 00:00:27,620
To get started, let's go back over to our editor, find the users directory and the users service file

9
00:00:27,620 --> 00:00:28,400
inside there.

10
00:00:28,640 --> 00:00:30,440
So here's my users service.

11
00:00:30,530 --> 00:00:34,320
Now, at the very top, I'm going to first begin with a couple of different imports.

12
00:00:34,340 --> 00:00:36,860
We are going to get repository.

13
00:00:39,240 --> 00:00:40,620
From type ORM.

14
00:00:41,800 --> 00:00:45,250
We will get inject repository.

15
00:00:46,400 --> 00:00:48,020
From Nestjs.

16
00:00:48,380 --> 00:00:49,700
Type ORM.

17
00:00:50,570 --> 00:00:55,100
And we'll get our user entity from user.

18
00:00:55,760 --> 00:00:56,600
Entity.

19
00:00:58,450 --> 00:01:02,890
I really want you to think back to our messages service and the messages repository.

20
00:01:02,920 --> 00:01:08,080
Remember, we had a dependency between those two classes, so we need to make sure that our messages

21
00:01:08,080 --> 00:01:12,600
service had a copy of the messages repository and we did that hook up using dependency injection.

22
00:01:12,610 --> 00:01:14,270
Same thing is going to occur here.

23
00:01:14,290 --> 00:01:18,940
The only difference is that this time around the syntax is going to be just a slight bit more complicated.

24
00:01:19,030 --> 00:01:20,540
So here's what we're going to do.

25
00:01:20,560 --> 00:01:22,690
I'm going to define a constructor method.

26
00:01:24,820 --> 00:01:28,810
And then inside the constructor argument list, we're going to define all of our different dependencies

27
00:01:28,810 --> 00:01:30,410
for our user service.

28
00:01:30,430 --> 00:01:36,200
So without a doubt, our service needs a copy or it needs to get access to the user's repository.

29
00:01:36,220 --> 00:01:40,560
So we're going to list inside of here repo that's we're going to call our repository.

30
00:01:40,570 --> 00:01:46,930
We're going to abbreviate it simply as repo, it's going to be of type repository, and then we're going

31
00:01:46,930 --> 00:01:49,180
to apply a generic type of user.

32
00:01:50,710 --> 00:01:56,020
Then inside the constructor, we're going to take that repo and assign it to a repo property.

33
00:01:58,030 --> 00:02:02,590
We definitely have to set up a property inside the class and say that this class is going to have a

34
00:02:02,590 --> 00:02:03,770
property of repo.

35
00:02:03,790 --> 00:02:06,940
So I'm going to say repo is of type.

36
00:02:07,680 --> 00:02:09,270
Repository user.

37
00:02:09,930 --> 00:02:14,040
Now, at this point, this looks just about identical to what we had back on our messages application.

38
00:02:14,070 --> 00:02:20,310
Let's now do one very quick refactor so we don't have to put in this property definition and that assignment

39
00:02:20,310 --> 00:02:21,380
inside the constructor.

40
00:02:21,390 --> 00:02:29,640
So I'm going to refactor this to say private repo and now we can remove that assignment and remove the

41
00:02:29,640 --> 00:02:30,930
property definition.

42
00:02:32,780 --> 00:02:33,100
All right.

43
00:02:33,110 --> 00:02:34,100
One last step.

44
00:02:34,340 --> 00:02:37,220
The very last step right in front of this argument.

45
00:02:37,250 --> 00:02:39,980
We're going to use this inject repository thing.

46
00:02:40,010 --> 00:02:42,130
This is yet another one of those decorators.

47
00:02:42,140 --> 00:02:47,570
So we are going to add in at inject repository.

48
00:02:48,590 --> 00:02:50,540
And call that with user.

49
00:02:51,580 --> 00:02:52,540
And that's it.

50
00:02:53,050 --> 00:02:53,860
Now I say that's it.

51
00:02:53,860 --> 00:02:55,750
But that's a lot of code there.

52
00:02:55,780 --> 00:02:57,250
That's a lot of stuff going on.

53
00:02:57,250 --> 00:02:59,940
So let's kind of break this apart and understand what is going on.

54
00:02:59,950 --> 00:03:01,830
So first, our argument name is Repo.

55
00:03:01,840 --> 00:03:02,740
Simple enough.

56
00:03:02,770 --> 00:03:07,480
We have private inside there just so we can abbreviate that property definition and assignment, which

57
00:03:07,480 --> 00:03:08,860
we saw just a moment ago.

58
00:03:09,690 --> 00:03:14,420
The type annotation on this is repository and we applied a generic type to it of user.

59
00:03:14,430 --> 00:03:21,330
So this right here means that repo is going to be an instance of a type repository that deals with instances

60
00:03:21,330 --> 00:03:22,100
of users.

61
00:03:22,110 --> 00:03:25,590
That's what user right there is meant to say this repository is going to handle users.

62
00:03:25,980 --> 00:03:31,740
So then the last part of this that's definitely the weirdest part is inject repository with user.

63
00:03:31,830 --> 00:03:36,060
So this is a little bit of an aid to the dependency injection system.

64
00:03:36,060 --> 00:03:40,860
This is what is going to tell the dependency injection system that we need the user repository.

65
00:03:40,980 --> 00:03:45,690
You might recall that when we spoke about dependency injection a little bit ago, we said that the dependency

66
00:03:45,690 --> 00:03:50,670
injection system uses this type annotation right here to figure out what instance it needs to inject

67
00:03:50,670 --> 00:03:52,290
into this class at runtime.

68
00:03:52,620 --> 00:03:57,300
Unfortunately, the dependency injection system does not play nicely with generics, which is what this

69
00:03:57,300 --> 00:03:58,110
is right here.

70
00:03:58,230 --> 00:04:03,570
So this decorator is required simply because we have to use a generic type right here.

71
00:04:05,050 --> 00:04:08,290
At the end of the day, if this line is confusing, don't sweat it.

72
00:04:08,290 --> 00:04:13,300
It's pretty much just memorize the syntax, repeat it anytime you need a typo repository.

73
00:04:14,270 --> 00:04:19,190
Okay, so now that we've got our repository, we're going to define a method inside this class called

74
00:04:19,190 --> 00:04:19,970
Create.

75
00:04:22,630 --> 00:04:28,360
And we'll have this create method for right now, we will manually pass through an email that is a string

76
00:04:28,450 --> 00:04:30,360
and a password that is a string.

77
00:04:30,370 --> 00:04:34,120
We are going to refactor that argument list in just a moment and make it something a little bit easier

78
00:04:34,120 --> 00:04:34,780
to use.

79
00:04:34,780 --> 00:04:36,670
But this is good enough for right now.

80
00:04:37,320 --> 00:04:43,230
So then inside of here, we need to take this email and password and use them along with our repository

81
00:04:43,260 --> 00:04:46,260
to create and save a new user into our database.

82
00:04:46,590 --> 00:04:50,570
Now, recall back on this diagram right here, our Repository API.

83
00:04:50,910 --> 00:04:56,670
I told you that we've got two kind of similar looking methods that we use to create a new user and save

84
00:04:56,670 --> 00:04:57,780
it into the database.

85
00:04:57,960 --> 00:05:04,650
So on a repository to create a new instance of a user inside of our application code, we use the Create

86
00:05:04,650 --> 00:05:05,280
method.

87
00:05:05,780 --> 00:05:08,900
We then get back an instance of our user entity.

88
00:05:08,930 --> 00:05:11,600
We're then going to pass off that entity to the save method.

89
00:05:11,600 --> 00:05:15,230
And the save method is what is going to actually save our user to the database.

90
00:05:15,620 --> 00:05:17,570
Now, yet another confusing thing right here.

91
00:05:17,570 --> 00:05:19,760
I know what is the difference between create and save?

92
00:05:19,790 --> 00:05:24,620
Well, once again, as usual, let's just write out the code for this and then we'll figure out exactly

93
00:05:24,620 --> 00:05:27,110
what the difference is between these two things in just a moment.

94
00:05:28,450 --> 00:05:35,470
Okay, so inside of Create, we're going to first create an instance of our user with this repo create,

95
00:05:35,590 --> 00:05:40,960
we're going to put in an object with the email and password that was supplied to this method.

96
00:05:42,420 --> 00:05:48,840
We're then going to call and return the result of this dot repo dot save user like so.

97
00:05:50,000 --> 00:05:51,650
And that's it, believe it or not.

98
00:05:53,520 --> 00:05:58,920
Now, before we discuss exactly what's going on with Create and Save, let's go hook up this service

99
00:05:58,920 --> 00:06:00,240
back to our controller.

100
00:06:00,270 --> 00:06:03,720
We're going to use the service to create a new user.

101
00:06:03,750 --> 00:06:08,580
We'll then test this all out from our client and we'll come back to this method and do a deep discussion

102
00:06:08,580 --> 00:06:09,790
on what's going on with Create.

103
00:06:09,810 --> 00:06:11,850
What's going on with Save and all that kind of stuff.

104
00:06:12,590 --> 00:06:17,900
So to hook up our service to our controller, once again, we're going to use dependency injection.

105
00:06:19,430 --> 00:06:25,880
To use dependency injection at the very top of our controller, we will import our users service.

106
00:06:26,700 --> 00:06:28,860
From users service.

107
00:06:30,910 --> 00:06:33,130
Well, then define our constructor method.

108
00:06:35,620 --> 00:06:41,080
Say private users service will be an instance of user service.

109
