00:06:41,820 --> 00:06:43,440
And now inside of our body.

110
00:06:44,330 --> 00:06:46,160
I'm going to the body of this method.

111
00:06:46,160 --> 00:06:50,990
I'm going to replace that console log with this Dot users service.

112
00:06:51,940 --> 00:06:57,850
We'll call create and we're going to pass in the email and password from the incoming body of the request.

113
00:06:57,850 --> 00:07:01,390
So body email and body dot password.

114
00:07:03,110 --> 00:07:04,370
And that should be it.

115
00:07:05,230 --> 00:07:09,160
So now if we save both these files, we can open up our client again.

116
00:07:10,390 --> 00:07:11,770
Here's my client.

117
00:07:11,920 --> 00:07:15,790
I'm going to send off this post request with a valid email and a password.

118
00:07:17,020 --> 00:07:19,630
Once again, we get back some supposed success right here.

119
00:07:19,630 --> 00:07:22,090
But how do we actually know if our data was saved?

120
00:07:22,120 --> 00:07:27,580
Well, if you installed that SQLite viewer tool that I showed you just a little bit ago, we can use

121
00:07:27,580 --> 00:07:31,600
that SQLite explorer to take a look at all the different users we have inside of our database.

122
00:07:31,630 --> 00:07:36,760
If you are not using VS code right now and you did not install that SQLite explorer extension with me,

123
00:07:36,790 --> 00:07:37,650
totally fine.

124
00:07:37,660 --> 00:07:42,160
We're going to eventually fetch a list of all of our different users that we saved inside of one of

125
00:07:42,160 --> 00:07:46,570
these other request handlers we are going to put together so specifically that one right there.

126
00:07:46,750 --> 00:07:51,850
So if you don't have this extension installed as I installed it a couple of videos ago, totally fine,

127
00:07:51,850 --> 00:07:54,820
you will be able to see all of your different users in just a little bit.

128
00:07:55,830 --> 00:08:01,020
If you close this extension, remember, you can open it back up by opening your command palette inside

129
00:08:01,020 --> 00:08:06,210
of VS code, which you can do with a command shift P or control shift P on Windows.

130
00:08:06,360 --> 00:08:09,960
You'll then search for SQLite and find open database.

131
00:08:09,960 --> 00:08:15,030
And remember, you'll only see this option right here if you installed the SQLite extension.

132
00:08:16,490 --> 00:08:18,200
So then we'll get this little panel.

133
00:08:19,830 --> 00:08:22,920
I'm going to take a look at users by clicking that play button.

134
00:08:22,920 --> 00:08:24,450
And now I should see.

135
00:08:24,600 --> 00:08:25,050
Yep.

136
00:08:25,080 --> 00:08:28,020
Sure enough, I've got a user in here with an ID of one.

137
00:08:28,050 --> 00:08:29,820
The email and the password.

138
00:08:31,180 --> 00:08:31,870
All right.

139
00:08:31,870 --> 00:08:32,740
So that's it.

140
00:08:32,770 --> 00:08:37,000
We've actually saved some data into a real database, or at least a somewhat real database.

141
00:08:37,000 --> 00:08:41,050
SQLite is not super appropriate for a web application, but remember, we're going to swap it out with

142
00:08:41,050 --> 00:08:42,310
Postgres at some point.

143
00:08:42,580 --> 00:08:44,910
So once again, we've written a lot of code inside of here.

144
00:08:44,920 --> 00:08:47,020
We've also written a lot of kind of mysterious code.

145
00:08:47,020 --> 00:08:52,330
So let's do a quick recap on everything we had to do to get to this point between our controller, the

146
00:08:52,330 --> 00:08:53,740
service and the repository.

147
00:08:53,770 --> 00:08:57,880
We're going to learn more about the repository itself, Take care of all the stuff in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1
00:00:00,860 --> 00:00:04,610
I don't know about you, but it sure seems like we had to write out a tremendous amount of code inside

2
00:00:04,610 --> 00:00:09,440
of many different files just to put together some very basic functionality in nest where we make a post

3
00:00:09,440 --> 00:00:12,100
request and then save some data into a database.

4
00:00:12,110 --> 00:00:16,490
So in this video I want to very quickly get our bearings and just review all the different parts of

5
00:00:16,490 --> 00:00:17,360
our application.

6
00:00:17,360 --> 00:00:22,010
We're then going to focus on our users service and really understand what we did inside that create

7
00:00:22,010 --> 00:00:22,610
method.

8
00:00:23,350 --> 00:00:26,350
Okay, so here's what's going on inside of our app.

9
00:00:26,350 --> 00:00:28,780
We've already gone over this flow more than once.

10
00:00:28,780 --> 00:00:31,240
Specifically, we saw it back on our messages application.

11
00:00:31,240 --> 00:00:35,470
But just one more time, I want to go over all these different parts because there's a lot of different

12
00:00:35,470 --> 00:00:39,850
things going on here and it might be really confusing what is service is and how it's different from

13
00:00:39,850 --> 00:00:41,920
the entity or repository and so on.

14
00:00:41,920 --> 00:00:45,370
So let's just trace out this entire flow one more time very quickly.

15
00:00:45,700 --> 00:00:51,370
So everything begins with the incoming request that has a body with hopefully an email and a password.

16
00:00:51,400 --> 00:00:53,080
We want to validate that data.

17
00:00:53,080 --> 00:00:56,590
So we set up a validation pipe inside the Main.ts file.

18
00:00:57,960 --> 00:01:03,510
That validation pipe is going to validate the body of the incoming request using validating information

19
00:01:03,510 --> 00:01:10,440
inside of our Create user DTO So the pipe is going to make sure that the body conforms with the validation

20
00:01:10,440 --> 00:01:11,970
we set up inside the DTO.

21
00:01:12,660 --> 00:01:18,120
If the body looks valid, then the body and the entire request will be sent on to our users controller.

22
00:01:18,360 --> 00:01:24,090
As a reminder, controllers are all about defining routes and picking interesting pieces of information

23
00:01:24,090 --> 00:01:25,560
from incoming requests.

24
00:01:25,590 --> 00:01:29,020
The controller itself doesn't implement any kind of business logic.

25
00:01:29,040 --> 00:01:33,390
Instead, it's just going to take this interesting information and pass it along to a method that we

26
00:01:33,420 --> 00:01:35,580
define in some kind of service.

27
00:01:35,910 --> 00:01:40,770
Services are where we are going to do all the real business logic around our application.

28
00:01:41,400 --> 00:01:47,400
Once we get some information about the email and password inside of our service, we then take the email

29
00:01:47,400 --> 00:01:53,280
and password, turn it into an instance of a user entity, and then make use of the user's repository

30
00:01:53,280 --> 00:01:56,100
to actually save this new user into our database.

31
00:01:56,310 --> 00:02:01,440
Remember that the repository is created for us automatically by type ORM, and we can really view this

32
00:02:01,440 --> 00:02:05,130
repository as being our interface to the SQLite database.

33
00:02:06,890 --> 00:02:08,500
Okay, so now we've got that quick review.

34
00:02:08,510 --> 00:02:14,180
I now want to focus on our users service and understand what we did inside the Create method.

35
00:02:14,600 --> 00:02:20,210
In particular, I want to really focus on why we are calling first, create and then save.

36
00:02:20,240 --> 00:02:24,320
So these are two different methods on the repository and they have very similar names.

37
00:02:24,350 --> 00:02:26,480
Why are we using one and then the other?

38
00:02:26,510 --> 00:02:28,820
Well, once again, let's take a look at a diagram.

39
00:02:30,210 --> 00:02:30,780
Okay.

40
00:02:32,520 --> 00:02:34,890
So here's our application on the left hand side.

41
00:02:35,130 --> 00:02:37,800
And then on the right hand side is the SQLite database.

42
00:02:38,010 --> 00:02:43,410
So inside of our app, this diagram is really focused on what is going on inside of our service right

43
00:02:43,410 --> 00:02:44,010
here.

44
00:02:44,100 --> 00:02:48,270
So we are receiving as arguments an email and a password.

45
00:02:48,300 --> 00:02:53,070
We then pass those arguments into the create function that is tied to the repository.

46
00:02:53,100 --> 00:02:58,650
The create function does not persist or save any information inside of your database.

47
00:02:58,680 --> 00:03:05,400
Instead, it takes in some information, creates a new instance of a user entity, and then assigns

48
00:03:05,400 --> 00:03:09,690
that data, assigns the email and password you supplied to create to that entity.

49
00:03:09,720 --> 00:03:15,480
So if we feed in an email of abc.com and a password of A to the create function, we're going to get

50
00:03:15,480 --> 00:03:19,560
back a user entity instance with the assigned email and password.

51
00:03:21,140 --> 00:03:24,350
We then pass the entity off to the save method.

52
00:03:24,380 --> 00:03:29,470
The save method is what actually takes a entity and saves it into our database.

53
00:03:29,480 --> 00:03:32,240
So that's the big difference between create and save.

54
00:03:32,270 --> 00:03:35,360
Create is used to create an instance of an entity.

55
00:03:35,390 --> 00:03:37,880
Save is used for actual persistence.

56
00:03:39,180 --> 00:03:45,000
Now, one interesting little bit of trivia here, the save method, we can call it, with any kind of

57
00:03:45,000 --> 00:03:48,000
object that has the properties that a user should have.

58
00:03:48,030 --> 00:03:54,240
In other words, rather than creating an instance of the user entity first, we could just as well have

59
00:03:54,240 --> 00:03:56,930
passed through email and password like so.

60
00:03:56,940 --> 00:03:59,340
So we could have just taken that email right there.

61
00:03:59,340 --> 00:04:04,140
The password right there created an object with email and password properties and hand it off to save

62
00:04:04,140 --> 00:04:06,300
and everything would have worked as expected.

63
00:04:06,420 --> 00:04:12,180
So the question becomes, why do we bother first creating an instance of the user entity?

64
00:04:12,270 --> 00:04:16,230
The answer is very simple inside of a user entity file.

65
00:04:16,500 --> 00:04:21,660
As I as we've discussed around entities in the past, the entity file is going to define all the different

66
00:04:21,660 --> 00:04:25,890
properties that a user entity has, and we're usually not going to put in a lot of business logic inside

67
00:04:25,890 --> 00:04:27,450
of here, if any at all.

68
00:04:27,540 --> 00:04:32,370
However, there are some scenarios in which we might want to put in some validation logic inside of

69
00:04:32,370 --> 00:04:32,840
here.

70
00:04:32,850 --> 00:04:39,120
So for example, we might add in an import for that class validator package and then put a validator

71
00:04:39,220 --> 00:04:43,120
on the email property and say, Hey, let's make sure this thing is an email.

72
00:04:43,360 --> 00:04:49,000
So we might tie our validation logic directly to an entity as opposed to the incoming DTO.

73
00:04:49,960 --> 00:04:55,150
If we did that, then we would want to make sure that before we saved our user off into our database.

74
00:04:55,150 --> 00:05:00,100
So before we called Save, we would make sure we would want to make sure that we actually ran that validation.

75
00:05:00,100 --> 00:05:05,110
So if we wanted to run that validation, we would first have to create an instance of our user.

76
00:05:05,110 --> 00:05:08,380
And then right here we could do our actual validating step.

77
00:05:08,860 --> 00:05:13,540
Now you might be kind of curious why would we do validation that is tied to the entity as opposed to

78
00:05:13,540 --> 00:05:14,710
inside of our DTO?

79
00:05:14,740 --> 00:05:18,610
Well, there are a couple of scenarios where we would want to do that and we're going to take a look

80
00:05:18,610 --> 00:05:20,950
at some of them later on inside this application.

81
00:05:21,070 --> 00:05:26,890
Right now, just understand that in general, we do want to first create an instance of our entity and

82
00:05:26,890 --> 00:05:32,530
then save it off, as opposed to just passing off our data to be saved directly to the save method.

83
00:05:33,600 --> 00:05:34,170
Okay.

84
00:05:35,570 --> 00:05:36,950
Let me undo that change.

85
00:05:36,950 --> 00:05:37,610
There we go.

86
00:05:38,390 --> 00:05:39,950
So now we've kind of got our bearings here.

87
00:05:39,950 --> 00:05:44,240
And now that we've got a better idea of what's going on inside of our create method, let's take a quick

88
00:05:44,240 --> 00:05:44,780
pause.

89
00:05:44,810 --> 00:05:49,040
We're going to come back in just a moment and start to implement some of these other methods.

90
00:05:49,040 --> 00:05:53,150
And trust me, they're going to come together a lot more easily now that we've done the first one.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,120 --> 00:00:06,220
At the end of the last video, I said that inside of our user service we could technically call save

2
00:00:06,220 --> 00:00:09,370
directly with an object that looks like a user.

3
00:00:09,370 --> 00:00:12,670
So we could pass in directly an object with an email and a password.

4
00:00:12,670 --> 00:00:16,930
And I'd said, Hey, this code would work fine, but didn't really go much beyond that.

5
00:00:16,930 --> 00:00:21,760
So in this video, I want to give you at least one reason that we decide to first create an instance

6
00:00:21,760 --> 00:00:27,430
of an entity and then call save on that as opposed to just directly passing in an email and a password.

7
00:00:27,430 --> 00:00:29,740
So let's take a look at a very quick example.

8
00:00:29,890 --> 00:00:32,880
I'm going to first begin by opening up my user entity file.

9
00:00:32,890 --> 00:00:34,330
So here's my entity.

10
00:00:34,630 --> 00:00:38,440
Now inside of here, I want to define a little bit of extra logic.

11
00:00:38,440 --> 00:00:44,140
I want to add in a function that's going to log out every operation that we do on a user.

12
00:00:44,140 --> 00:00:48,070
So in other words, if we try to create a new user and save it to the database.

13
00:00:48,100 --> 00:00:54,160
If we try to update a user or if we try to remove an existing user, I want to do a console log that

14
00:00:54,160 --> 00:00:57,160
says we are updating, saving, whatever.

15
00:00:57,730 --> 00:01:03,640
One way we can very easily implement this is by using a feature inside of type called hooks.

16
00:01:03,790 --> 00:01:09,010
Hooks allow us to define functions on an entity that will be called automatically at certain points

17
00:01:09,010 --> 00:01:09,650
in time.

18
00:01:09,670 --> 00:01:13,460
So let me show you a very simple example at the very top of this file.

19
00:01:13,480 --> 00:01:15,010
I'm going to import.

20
00:01:16,110 --> 00:01:17,880
After insert.

21
00:01:18,270 --> 00:01:22,650
This is a decorator that we can apply to a method defined inside of our entity.

22
00:01:23,010 --> 00:01:28,020
I'm going to define a method inside of here called log insert.

23
00:01:28,660 --> 00:01:32,290
And then will mark it with that after insert decorator.

24
00:01:32,650 --> 00:01:38,560
So now whenever we insert a new user into our database, this function right here should be executed.

25
00:01:38,590 --> 00:01:45,520
So to just log the fact that we have inserted a new record, I'm going to do a console log and say inserted.

26
00:01:46,240 --> 00:01:51,080
User with ID, and then I'm going to print out the user's ID that was just inserted.

27
00:01:51,100 --> 00:01:53,680
We can do so by this.id.

28
00:01:54,010 --> 00:01:57,820
So this is a reference to the entity that we just inserted.

29
00:01:58,920 --> 00:02:04,110
I'm then going to repeat the same process and take care of whenever we update a user or whenever we

30
00:02:04,110 --> 00:02:05,670
remove one as well.

31
00:02:05,880 --> 00:02:09,300
To handle those cases, we're going to import two other decorators.

32
00:02:09,300 --> 00:02:11,700
So these are also hook decorators.

33
00:02:11,790 --> 00:02:15,750
So we're going to add in an import for after remove.

34
00:02:16,880 --> 00:02:18,620
And after update.

35
00:02:21,090 --> 00:02:23,020
I'll then define two more methods inside of here.

36
00:02:23,040 --> 00:02:27,030
So after update and we'll say log update.

37
00:02:27,150 --> 00:02:33,630
I'll do a console log of updated user with ID and then print out the ID.

38
00:02:35,780 --> 00:02:38,390
And then finally after remove.

39
00:02:40,080 --> 00:02:45,810
And we'll log remove and do a console log of removed user with ID.

40
00:02:48,830 --> 00:02:52,340
Okay, so we've now got some great logging logic inside of our application.

41
00:02:52,340 --> 00:02:56,480
We're going to get a ton of console logs, of course, if we have a very busy app, but this is good

42
00:02:56,480 --> 00:02:57,740
for a demonstration.

43
00:02:57,830 --> 00:03:00,950
So now that we've defined these hooks, let's very quickly just test one.

44
00:03:00,950 --> 00:03:04,940
I'm going to try to create a new user and I'm going to make sure that I see some console log that says

45
00:03:04,940 --> 00:03:07,370
we just inserted a user with some given ID.

46
00:03:07,940 --> 00:03:12,740
So to test this out, I'm going to go back over to my client.

47
00:03:14,080 --> 00:03:15,700
I'm going to send off this request.

48
00:03:16,630 --> 00:03:18,220
Looks like it was successful.

49
00:03:18,220 --> 00:03:20,320
And if I go back over to my terminal.

50
00:03:21,490 --> 00:03:25,090
I should now see a console log right here that says inserted user with ID two.

51
00:03:25,360 --> 00:03:27,130
Okay, well, that looks fantastic.

52
00:03:27,280 --> 00:03:29,740
So let's get back on topic here.

53
00:03:29,770 --> 00:03:31,100
We've made use of hooks.

54
00:03:31,120 --> 00:03:33,580
Looks like they are pretty useful to some degree.

55
00:03:34,340 --> 00:03:38,690
But how does that relate to this entire discussion of create versus save?

56
00:03:38,720 --> 00:03:40,460
Well, here's the gotcha.

57
00:03:41,420 --> 00:03:46,250
This is where type starts to get a little bit tricky because there's a lot of just tiny little gotchas,

58
00:03:46,250 --> 00:03:48,590
tiny little details like this that you need to be aware of.

59
00:03:48,800 --> 00:03:51,800
So we're going to call save at certain points in time.

60
00:03:51,800 --> 00:03:57,080
And as I mentioned previously, at the end of the last video, we can call save either with a user entity

61
00:03:57,080 --> 00:03:59,560
instance or with just a plain object.

62
00:03:59,570 --> 00:04:01,760
And either way, our data will be saved.

63
00:04:01,790 --> 00:04:08,360
The difference, however, is that if we save an entity instance, all the hooks tied to that instance

64
00:04:08,360 --> 00:04:09,670
will be executed.

65
00:04:09,680 --> 00:04:12,350
But if we pass in an object and try to save it.

66
00:04:12,350 --> 00:04:17,180
So if we just pass in a very plain object, no hooks get executed whatsoever.

67
00:04:17,330 --> 00:04:19,700
So let me give you an example of this.

68
00:04:20,790 --> 00:04:22,400
Back over inside of our service.

69
00:04:22,410 --> 00:04:28,260
I'm going to call, save and pass in directly the email and the object inside of an object.

70
00:04:28,880 --> 00:04:30,950
Let's try testing out this now.

71
00:04:30,950 --> 00:04:32,570
So I'm going to send off the request.

72
00:04:35,150 --> 00:04:37,250
And if I now go back over to my terminal.

73
00:04:38,470 --> 00:04:40,360
I get no console log whatsoever.

74
00:04:40,570 --> 00:04:44,380
And if I open up my SQLite database explorer very quickly.

75
00:04:44,920 --> 00:04:46,770
So I'm going to take a look at my users table.

76
00:04:46,780 --> 00:04:50,830
It looks like that user was created but did not get any console logs.

77
00:04:50,950 --> 00:04:55,330
So again, if we call save with a plain object, hooks are not executed.

78
00:04:55,360 --> 00:05:03,040
Now this can result in some incredibly hard to detect bugs if we start to mix saving a plain object

79
00:05:03,040 --> 00:05:08,440
versus an entity because we might define some hooks inside of our entity, assuming that every single

80
00:05:08,440 --> 00:05:12,750
time that we insert, update or remove a user, those hooks will be executed.

81
00:05:12,760 --> 00:05:18,520
But if there's some other engineer on our team who is trying to save objects directly, like a plain

82
00:05:18,520 --> 00:05:21,040
object, those hooks are not going to run.

83
00:05:21,040 --> 00:05:23,800
And so in some cases hooks are running, other cases they are not.

84
00:05:23,800 --> 00:05:29,110
And that would be a really tough bug to try to nail down and figure out why sometimes hooks are running

85
00:05:29,110 --> 00:05:30,580
and why sometimes they aren't.

86
00:05:30,760 --> 00:05:35,590
So as a general rule of thumb, we really need to decide up front if we want to use any hooks inside

87
00:05:35,590 --> 00:05:36,460
of our project.

88
00:05:36,490 --> 00:05:42,830
Chances are you do, and if you do, then whenever you are trying to insert a new record, update a

89
00:05:42,830 --> 00:05:49,490
record or remove a record, you need to first get your entity instance and then call save or update

90
00:05:49,490 --> 00:05:53,990
or remove with it, as opposed to just trying to save a plain object.

91
00:05:54,290 --> 00:05:58,010
This is also one of the differences between some of these different methods we discussed a little bit

92
00:05:58,010 --> 00:05:58,400
ago.

93
00:05:58,400 --> 00:06:04,160
So remember I told you that on a repo there's a save method and that looks kind of similar to two other

94
00:06:04,160 --> 00:06:07,740
methods on a repo instance which are insert and update.

95
00:06:07,760 --> 00:06:10,280
There's also a remove and there's also a delete.

96
00:06:10,280 --> 00:06:12,890
So all these different methods look very similar.

97
00:06:14,230 --> 00:06:19,810
Save and remove are expected to be called with entity instances, and if you call them with an entity,

98
00:06:19,840 --> 00:06:21,450
hooks will be executed.

99
00:06:21,460 --> 00:06:28,210
But if you make use of insert, update or delete to directly insert a record, directly update or directly

100
00:06:28,210 --> 00:06:32,950
delete a record, then your hooks will not be executed, which is sometimes not going to be what you

101
00:06:32,950 --> 00:06:33,700
expect.

102
00:06:34,090 --> 00:06:40,930
So again, in all scenarios up front, we are going to say that anytime we want to insert a record update

103
00:06:40,930 --> 00:06:46,300
or delete, we're going to first get an entity instance and then work with that as opposed to trying

104
00:06:46,300 --> 00:06:52,090
to save a plain object or try to call these other related methods that do not execute hooks, because

105
00:06:52,090 --> 00:06:56,740
otherwise we're just going to end up in these weird scenarios where our hooks are not running in quite

106
00:06:56,770 --> 00:06:58,120
the way we expect them to.

107
00:06:58,890 --> 00:06:59,220
All right.

108
00:06:59,220 --> 00:07:06,030
So hopefully now at long last, we've got some more information or understanding why we've got the difference

109
00:07:06,030 --> 00:07:11,430
between create and save and why we choose to create an instance of a user entity with Create before

110
00:07:11,430 --> 00:07:14,880
we try to save it, as opposed to just directly passing in an object.

111
00:07:15,120 --> 00:07:19,470
So last step here, I'm going to put in user like I had before and that looks good.

112
00:07:19,800 --> 00:07:22,330
I'm also going to leave the log statements inside of here.

113
00:07:22,330 --> 00:07:26,220
I'm just going to leave them because these are actually kind of nice to some degree, kind of nice to

114
00:07:26,220 --> 00:07:30,930
see console logs and understand when data is being inserted or updated or removed.

115
00:07:31,620 --> 00:07:31,980
All right.

116
00:07:31,980 --> 00:07:33,080
So good progress.

117
00:07:33,090 --> 00:07:35,850
Let's take a pause right here and continue on in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,780 --> 00:00:03,790
We've got our first route handler put together.

2
00:00:03,790 --> 00:00:05,740
So now we're going to start to work on these other ones.

3
00:00:05,770 --> 00:00:10,570
Now, I'll be honest with you, the different route handlers we need to put together are all very similar

4
00:00:10,570 --> 00:00:14,230
to some route handlers we already assembled back on our messages application.

5
00:00:14,230 --> 00:00:17,740
So to keep things interesting here, I've got a little bit of an idea.

6
00:00:17,770 --> 00:00:22,180
Let's first focus on just putting together all the different methods of our service, because then we

7
00:00:22,180 --> 00:00:27,840
can focus on just understanding how type works and how we can interact with that users repository in

8
00:00:27,840 --> 00:00:29,170
an efficient way.

9
00:00:29,200 --> 00:00:33,940
Once we get all the service methods put together, we'll then very quickly go back and put together

10
00:00:33,940 --> 00:00:38,110
all of our different route handlers inside of our controller, which is something that we kind of already

11
00:00:38,110 --> 00:00:39,070
know how to do.

12
00:00:39,310 --> 00:00:41,890
So we can go over that entire process really quickly.

13
00:00:41,920 --> 00:00:46,120
This will in general be a lot faster than trying to go through each route handler one by one, which

14
00:00:46,120 --> 00:00:47,410
would be a lot slower.

15
00:00:47,500 --> 00:00:53,020
So with that in mind, we're going to start to focus on putting together find one, find update and

16
00:00:53,020 --> 00:00:55,000
remove inside of our service.

17
00:00:55,180 --> 00:00:59,650
We're going to assume some of these different methods are called with different arguments, and then

18
00:00:59,650 --> 00:01:01,490
we eventually try to call them from our controller.

19
00:01:01,490 --> 00:01:05,120
We're going to make sure that we provide the correct arguments to each of these different methods.

20
00:01:05,330 --> 00:01:07,370
So without further ado, let's get to it.

21
00:01:07,370 --> 00:01:10,970
Let's go into our service and define these four different methods.

22
00:01:12,870 --> 00:01:14,780
Okay, so here's my user service.

23
00:01:14,790 --> 00:01:19,650
I'm going to first begin inside of here by just writing out a quick scaffolding for those four different

24
00:01:19,650 --> 00:01:20,330
methods.

25
00:01:20,340 --> 00:01:22,380
So I will add in find one.

26
00:01:23,630 --> 00:01:27,770
Find, update and remove.

27
00:01:28,280 --> 00:01:32,420
Now, once again, in just about all four of these different methods, we're pretty much just going

28
00:01:32,420 --> 00:01:38,540
to take in some argument and then turn right around to our repository and call some very identical looking

29
00:01:38,540 --> 00:01:40,790
function on the repository itself.

30
00:01:41,490 --> 00:01:44,370
That's a very common thing that we're going to see with more basic services.

31
00:01:44,370 --> 00:01:48,360
And again, it's totally fine to have methods between your service and your repository that look pretty

32
00:01:48,360 --> 00:01:49,410
similar in nature.

33
00:01:50,280 --> 00:01:50,550
All right.

34
00:01:50,550 --> 00:01:54,600
So now for each of these different methods, let's take a guess at what arguments they should receive

35
00:01:54,600 --> 00:01:59,520
and then use those arguments to access a repository and retrieve data, find data update or whatever

36
00:01:59,520 --> 00:02:00,080
else.

37
00:02:00,090 --> 00:02:02,640
So first, find one with find one.

38
00:02:02,640 --> 00:02:08,190
We probably want to find one particular user inside of a repository given their ID, So I'm going to

39
00:02:08,190 --> 00:02:12,840
assume that we're going to receive an argument of ID that will be some kind of number.

40
00:02:13,170 --> 00:02:19,260
So then we need to take that ID and run a query on our repository, looking back to our list of methods

41
00:02:19,260 --> 00:02:20,370
on a repository.

42
00:02:20,700 --> 00:02:24,780
So remember this diagram, We've got a couple of different methods that are defined on the repository

43
00:02:24,780 --> 00:02:25,680
automatically.

44
00:02:25,710 --> 00:02:32,250
One method we have access to is find one we can use, find one to find a particular record given either

45
00:02:32,250 --> 00:02:35,010
some search criteria or just a plain ID.

46
00:02:35,580 --> 00:02:38,100
So in this case, we're going to return.

47
00:02:39,440 --> 00:02:40,640
This dot repo dot.

48
00:02:40,640 --> 00:02:43,970
Find one and put in the ID of the user we are looking for.

49
00:02:44,300 --> 00:02:49,090
Other ways we can make use of find one is to put in an object with some search criteria.

50
00:02:49,100 --> 00:02:54,890
So for example, if we wanted to find all the users or really just one user with a very particular email

51
00:02:54,890 --> 00:03:02,480
address, we can put in an object right here and say, find the first user with an email of asdf@asdf.com.

52
00:03:03,200 --> 00:03:04,760
So find one is pretty flexible.

53
00:03:04,760 --> 00:03:09,680
We can either look up one particular record with a given ID or use some search criteria.

54
00:03:10,790 --> 00:03:12,740
But in this case, we of course, want just the ID.

55
00:03:15,120 --> 00:03:16,680
Okay, let's move on to find.

56
00:03:16,950 --> 00:03:20,450
So with find, we're really just going to take in an email.

57
00:03:20,460 --> 00:03:22,470
So some email that we're looking for.

58
00:03:22,500 --> 00:03:27,390
We're then going to use the find method built into the repository API.

59
00:03:28,890 --> 00:03:32,160
So in this case, we will receive an email that's going to be a string.

60
00:03:32,840 --> 00:03:40,400
And then return this dot repo dot find and want to find all the different users with an email of the

61
00:03:40,400 --> 00:03:42,470
email that was passed in to this function.

62
00:03:43,150 --> 00:03:45,700
We can collapse that down to be just email like so.

63
00:03:46,640 --> 00:03:51,770
So this kind of implies that different users inside of our application can have an identical email address.

64
00:03:51,800 --> 00:03:56,390
We're going to eventually enforce some uniqueness around email addresses, but for right now, users

65
00:03:56,390 --> 00:03:57,650
can have identical emails.

66
00:03:57,650 --> 00:03:59,000
We'll say that's totally okay.

67
00:03:59,540 --> 00:04:04,880
Now, just to be really clear, the difference between find one and Find is that find is always going

68
00:04:04,880 --> 00:04:09,830
to return one record or null if no user is found with that given criteria.

69
00:04:11,270 --> 00:04:16,250
Find, on the other hand, is going to return an array of all the different records that match that

70
00:04:16,250 --> 00:04:17,300
search criteria.

71
00:04:17,600 --> 00:04:20,360
If no records are found with that given criteria.

72
00:04:20,360 --> 00:04:25,860
So let's say we ask for all the users with an email of some really uncommon email address.

73
00:04:25,880 --> 00:04:28,790
If we find no results, we'll get back an empty array.

74
00:04:32,330 --> 00:04:32,410
Yeah.

75
00:04:32,510 --> 00:04:33,380
So that looks good.

76
00:04:34,560 --> 00:04:37,680
Now update and remove are going to be just a little bit more complicated.

77
00:04:37,680 --> 00:04:41,370
So let's take a quick pause right here and then take care of those two in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1
00:00:01,750 --> 00:00:04,130
Let's start to work on our update and remove methods.

2
00:00:04,150 --> 00:00:08,200
So first off, update, we need to first think about the different arguments that this function will

3
00:00:08,200 --> 00:00:08,770
take.

4
00:00:08,800 --> 00:00:14,140
I think that a very reasonable first argument will probably be the ID of the user that we want to update.

5
00:00:14,320 --> 00:00:17,500
So then what other arguments should we provide here?

6
00:00:17,530 --> 00:00:22,120
One approach would be to say that we should maybe pass in the email address that we want to update the

7
00:00:22,120 --> 00:00:24,460
user with or their password or something like that.

8
00:00:24,490 --> 00:00:29,020
So one possible approach that I do not recommend is to do something like this.

9
00:00:29,050 --> 00:00:34,750
We could say that we're going to pass in some new email address that we want to assign to the user and

10
00:00:34,750 --> 00:00:38,110
maybe a new password to assign to the user as well.

11
00:00:38,440 --> 00:00:40,510
But this is not a really good design.

12
00:00:40,540 --> 00:00:46,330
The reason is that if we ever want to update only the password for this user, what would we provide

13
00:00:46,330 --> 00:00:48,010
for the new email argument?

14
00:00:48,040 --> 00:00:49,520
Would we give it an empty string?

15
00:00:49,540 --> 00:00:52,120
Would we give it undefined or null or what?

16
00:00:52,330 --> 00:00:58,030
In addition, this argument list would start to get really confusing if we add a lot of other properties

17
00:00:58,030 --> 00:01:00,230
associated with the user that we might want to update.

18
00:01:00,250 --> 00:01:04,820
So, for example, if we also have a user with a maybe an age.

19
00:01:06,280 --> 00:01:08,110
And also a name.

20
00:01:09,230 --> 00:01:10,190
And so on.

21
00:01:10,220 --> 00:01:14,190
This argument list would start to get very, very long and really hard to read.

22
00:01:14,210 --> 00:01:19,250
So without a doubt, passing in each property update one by one is probably not a good idea.

23
00:01:19,250 --> 00:01:22,370
So we need to think of a different way of calling update.

24
00:01:23,190 --> 00:01:27,240
The best way to think about how to implement this update method is to think about how you would want

25
00:01:27,240 --> 00:01:28,020
to call it.

26
00:01:28,050 --> 00:01:31,320
So let me show you a little trick little example.

27
00:01:31,500 --> 00:01:36,210
I'm going to, as a very quick example, create an instance of this user service at the bottom of the

28
00:01:36,210 --> 00:01:40,860
file, and I'm going to think about how I would want to call the update function so you don't have to

29
00:01:40,860 --> 00:01:42,030
write this code at the very bottom.

30
00:01:42,030 --> 00:01:44,940
This is just a quick example and I'm going to delete it very quickly.

31
00:01:45,180 --> 00:01:47,670
So down here I'm going to try to create an instance.

32
00:01:49,230 --> 00:01:50,460
Of the user service.

33
00:01:52,580 --> 00:01:57,110
For the argument to the constructor we are supposed to pass in a repository.

34
00:01:57,470 --> 00:02:00,440
I'm going to just trick TypeScript because we're not going to actually run this code.

35
00:02:00,440 --> 00:02:01,840
This is just a quick example.

36
00:02:01,850 --> 00:02:07,580
I'm going to put in an empty object and say object as any, and that's just going to trick TypeScript

37
00:02:07,580 --> 00:02:09,890
into thinking that we just gave it a valid repository.

38
00:02:10,620 --> 00:02:15,030
I'll then try to call users service and call the update method on it.

39
00:02:15,580 --> 00:02:18,980
So again, let's think about how we would want to call the update function.

40
00:02:19,000 --> 00:02:23,500
Let's imagine that we want to call, update and update the user with an ID of one.

41
00:02:24,210 --> 00:02:27,030
And then give them maybe just a new email address.

42
00:02:27,030 --> 00:02:31,980
So ideally, I want to give them maybe some kind of object that says I want to update only the email

43
00:02:31,980 --> 00:02:35,790
property and update that email address to something like this.

44
00:02:36,820 --> 00:02:39,550
As soon as I put that in, we're going to get a nasty error here.

45
00:02:39,550 --> 00:02:42,400
It's because we need to update our type annotation on the update function.

46
00:02:42,400 --> 00:02:44,650
We need to receive that additional argument.

47
00:02:44,680 --> 00:02:46,470
But here's the challenge.

48
00:02:46,480 --> 00:02:52,840
We need to put in a type annotation that's going to be very flexible and allow us to put in any property

49
00:02:52,840 --> 00:02:54,300
that is tied to a user.

50
00:02:54,310 --> 00:03:00,970
So I should be able to provide an object with an email or an object with an email or an object with

51
00:03:00,970 --> 00:03:06,880
a password, or I should be able to update both the email and the password at the same time by doing

52
00:03:06,880 --> 00:03:07,930
something like that.

53
00:03:08,080 --> 00:03:13,480
So we need a type definition applied right there that's going to be very flexible and allow us to update

54
00:03:13,480 --> 00:03:16,330
either the email or the password or both.

55
00:03:17,540 --> 00:03:20,090
So to do so, here is a little trick we're going to use.

56
00:03:20,420 --> 00:03:25,160
I'm going to put in an argument right here called Adders, short for attributes.

57
00:03:25,490 --> 00:03:29,750
I'm then going to annotate it as partial user.

58
00:03:30,810 --> 00:03:32,280
Okay, so what's going on here?

59
00:03:32,500 --> 00:03:36,390
Or user first off, remember is a reference to our user entity.

60
00:03:36,420 --> 00:03:41,130
So if we go back over to our user entity file, we've got a class right here that says a user is going

61
00:03:41,130 --> 00:03:43,830
to have an ID, an email and a password.

62
00:03:44,940 --> 00:03:46,770
We then made use of this partial.

63
00:03:46,800 --> 00:03:49,730
This is a type helper defined in TypeScript itself.

64
00:03:49,740 --> 00:03:53,010
It is not something we have to import into this file or anything like that.

65
00:03:53,100 --> 00:04:00,390
This partial type helper tells us that Attrs can be any object that has at least or none some of the

66
00:04:00,390 --> 00:04:02,830
properties of the user class.

67
00:04:02,850 --> 00:04:09,450
So if we provide any object that has no properties, has at least one property or has all the properties

68
00:04:09,450 --> 00:04:12,540
of a user, that will be considered to be a valid argument.

69
00:04:12,660 --> 00:04:17,880
So now we can put in an object that has an email and a password that's going to work just fine.

70
00:04:17,910 --> 00:04:21,660
We can put in an object that has just a password.

71
00:04:21,900 --> 00:04:27,510
We can put in an object that has just an email, or we can put in an object that has nothing at all.

72
00:04:27,810 --> 00:04:33,420
If we try to put in an object that has properties that don't belong to a user, so let's say color,

73
00:04:33,450 --> 00:04:37,230
we will get an error because color is not a property of user.

74
00:04:38,120 --> 00:04:41,870
So this is a pretty good way of putting together our update function.

75
00:04:41,870 --> 00:04:44,120
We've now got some type checking with TypeScript.

76
00:04:44,120 --> 00:04:48,290
It's going to make sure that we only try to update properties that actually belong to a user.

77
00:04:50,390 --> 00:04:50,690
All right.

78
00:04:50,690 --> 00:04:54,570
So now that we've got our arguments put together, I'm going to clean up all this extra code down here.

79
00:04:54,710 --> 00:04:57,830
Now we're going to figure out how we're going to actually apply this update.

80
00:04:57,860 --> 00:05:02,660
How are we going to find a user with that given ID and update some attributes tied to them?

81
00:05:02,810 --> 00:05:07,070
So to understand this, unfortunately, we're going to have to write some kind of involved code inside

82
00:05:07,070 --> 00:05:07,270
of here.

83
00:05:07,280 --> 00:05:10,220
So let me show you a diagram or two and help you understand what we're going to do.

84
00:05:10,400 --> 00:05:14,220
The first thing I want you to understand is something we just spoke about two seconds ago.

85
00:05:14,240 --> 00:05:20,330
Remember, any time that we try to save a user entity instance hooks that are associated with that user

86
00:05:20,330 --> 00:05:22,100
entity will be executed.

87
00:05:22,100 --> 00:05:25,450
And that's just about always what we want to have happen inside of our app.

88
00:05:25,460 --> 00:05:28,280
If we ever tried to save a plane object.

89
00:05:28,310 --> 00:05:33,560
Hooks will not be executed, which is generally probably not good because it will lead to some tough

90
00:05:33,560 --> 00:05:34,910
to debug errors.

91
00:05:35,090 --> 00:05:36,260
So with that in mind.

92
00:05:37,050 --> 00:05:38,910
I now want to give you another reminder.

93
00:05:38,910 --> 00:05:43,830
So a little bit ago when we first discussed a repository, I told you that a repository has some methods

94
00:05:43,830 --> 00:05:46,080
that really look kind of identical in nature.

95
00:05:46,080 --> 00:05:51,750
So, for example, on a repository we have a save method and this will either insert or update data

96
00:05:51,750 --> 00:05:53,070
inside of a database.

97
00:05:53,070 --> 00:05:57,060
And at the same time we have two other methods called insert and update.

98
00:05:57,090 --> 00:05:59,460
Same thing for remove and and delete.

99
00:05:59,580 --> 00:06:02,190
All these different methods really look identical in nature.

100
00:06:02,190 --> 00:06:03,960
So what is the difference between them?

101
00:06:04,920 --> 00:06:09,930
Well, this kind of goes back to the same thing we just discussed a little bit ago around the Save method

102
00:06:09,930 --> 00:06:12,690
and how we can optionally call it with a plain object.

103
00:06:12,990 --> 00:06:18,890
So insert and update are made to be used with plain objects.

104
00:06:18,900 --> 00:06:23,400
If we make use of insert and update, then these plain objects, we're going to update them, we're

105
00:06:23,400 --> 00:06:29,520
going to insert or update data inside the database, but no hooks associated with them will be executed

106
00:06:29,520 --> 00:06:32,730
because we are not working with a entity instance.

107
00:06:32,940 --> 00:06:39,090
So if we want to make use of hooks and again, we usually do, we're going to rely upon save and remove

108
00:06:39,120 --> 00:06:42,990
because save and remove are designed to work with entity instances.

109
00:06:42,990 --> 00:06:46,290
So in general, we want to go save and remove wherever possible.

110
00:06:46,470 --> 00:06:51,930
But there's kind of a downside to this approach in order to call, save and apply an update to some

111
00:06:51,930 --> 00:06:58,320
record that implies that we first have to fetch out of our database the user that we are trying to update.

112
00:06:58,350 --> 00:07:00,300
Let me show you a diagram just to explain.

113
00:07:01,920 --> 00:07:05,100
Okay, so here's how we are going to apply an update inside of our application.

114
00:07:05,100 --> 00:07:07,440
And again, it's a little bit inefficient.

115
00:07:07,710 --> 00:07:12,280
So in order to apply an update, we first need a user entity instance.

116
00:07:12,300 --> 00:07:17,610
We need to understand what we're trying to update and we need to have an entity instance so we can run

117
00:07:17,610 --> 00:07:19,690
all the associated hooks and whatnot.

118
00:07:19,710 --> 00:07:26,190
So to get this user entity instance, we first have to go into our database and run a query to find

119
00:07:26,190 --> 00:07:28,620
a user with the ID we are looking for.

120
00:07:28,770 --> 00:07:32,610
Once we get that user entity instance, we'll then apply the update.

121
00:07:32,610 --> 00:07:36,210
So we'll update the user's email or the password or whatever else.

122
00:07:36,480 --> 00:07:40,920
And then once we've applied that update, we'll then call save with that instance.

123
00:07:41,460 --> 00:07:46,080
The save method is used not only for inserting new records as we've seen already, it's also used for

124
00:07:46,080 --> 00:07:47,690
updating records as well.

125
00:07:47,700 --> 00:07:52,770
So type is going to look at the changes we just made to that user entity instance and then save all

126
00:07:52,770 --> 00:07:55,180
those updates back into our list of users.

127
00:07:55,200 --> 00:08:00,270
So in this case, we would go and find the user with ID one and it looks like we changed their email

128
00:08:00,300 --> 00:08:01,930
to be babcom.

129
00:08:01,930 --> 00:08:04,210
So we would expect to see the user with ID one.

130
00:08:04,970 --> 00:08:07,070
With an email of Babcom.

131
00:08:08,710 --> 00:08:13,060
The reason this is inefficient is that it requires two trips to our database.

132
00:08:13,090 --> 00:08:18,400
One trip just to find the user in the first place and then a second trip to save the changes we just

133
00:08:18,400 --> 00:08:19,000
made.

134
00:08:19,030 --> 00:08:24,610
If we were making use of the update method directly, that would require just one single trip to our

135
00:08:24,610 --> 00:08:25,240
database.

136
00:08:25,270 --> 00:08:27,460
We can apply just one update.

137
00:08:27,460 --> 00:08:28,450
Update a single row.

138
00:08:28,480 --> 00:08:28,930
That's it.

139
00:08:28,960 --> 00:08:29,500
We're done.

140
00:08:29,500 --> 00:08:31,330
No additional round trips required.

141
00:08:31,420 --> 00:08:37,060
So again, we're kind of paying in performance here for having some fancy functionality that we probably

142
00:08:37,060 --> 00:08:37,360
want.

143
00:08:37,390 --> 00:08:40,300
Again, you probably want hooks not required.

144
00:08:40,300 --> 00:08:42,250
You can definitely build applications without them.

145
00:08:42,250 --> 00:08:45,220
But I just want to show you the kind of full featured approach here.

146
00:08:45,850 --> 00:08:46,240
Okay.

147
00:08:46,240 --> 00:08:50,200
So all this in mind, let's finish up update.

148
00:08:50,230 --> 00:08:52,270
So here's what we need to do inside of here.

149
00:08:52,300 --> 00:08:55,450
We're going to first attempt to find a user with a given ID.

150
00:08:55,780 --> 00:09:00,010
We've already got a function that's going to do that right here, so I'm going to call.

151
00:09:01,570 --> 00:09:03,610
Await this dot.

152
00:09:03,610 --> 00:09:04,490
Find one.

153
00:09:04,510 --> 00:09:07,780
We're going to make use of the function that we already defined inside of here, and we're going to

154
00:09:07,780 --> 00:09:11,560
try to find a user with the ID that was just passed into this function.

155
00:09:13,520 --> 00:09:16,280
Finding a user out of the database is an asynchronous operation.

156
00:09:16,280 --> 00:09:18,480
So that's why we're using the await keyword right there.

157
00:09:18,500 --> 00:09:21,230
Let's make sure that we mark the enclosing function as async.

158
00:09:22,370 --> 00:09:25,940
Whenever we try to find a user, it is entirely possible that we're going to try to find a user that

159
00:09:25,940 --> 00:09:26,870
does not exist.

160
00:09:26,870 --> 00:09:30,230
So maybe we call update with an ID that is just not valid.

161
00:09:30,230 --> 00:09:32,690
So we need to take a look at what comes back right here.

162
00:09:32,690 --> 00:09:36,640
And if a user was not found, we should probably throw an error or something like that.

163
00:09:36,650 --> 00:09:44,330
So I'll say if we did not find a user, let's just throw a plain error for right now and say user not

164
00:09:44,330 --> 00:09:45,050
found.

165
00:09:47,870 --> 00:09:52,640
If we did find a user, then let's go ahead and make an update to them to do the update.

166
00:09:52,670 --> 00:09:56,600
We will use the built in function object.assign.

167
00:09:58,370 --> 00:10:03,260
We're going to put in the user we just found and then an object describing all the updates we want to

168
00:10:03,260 --> 00:10:04,690
copy over to that object.

169
00:10:04,700 --> 00:10:06,400
So that's going to be adders.

170
00:10:06,410 --> 00:10:08,750
So I'll put in adders as a second argument.

171
00:10:08,990 --> 00:10:13,520
If you're not familiar with Object.assign, we're going to take all the properties and values off of

172
00:10:13,520 --> 00:10:17,900
Adders and just copy them directly over to user overriding any properties that are already there.

173
00:10:19,170 --> 00:10:27,270
So then finally, to actually save our update, we will return this repo save and we're going to save

174
00:10:27,270 --> 00:10:29,130
the updated user entity.

175
00:10:29,430 --> 00:10:30,930
And that should be it.

176
00:10:32,160 --> 00:10:32,790
Okay.

177
00:10:33,590 --> 00:10:37,040
There's a lot of stuff going on here around update, a lot of stuff to understand.

178
00:10:37,040 --> 00:10:42,740
But hopefully once we put together this method right here and well, for that matter, even kind of

179
00:10:42,740 --> 00:10:49,280
the create one up here, you might start to understand why creating these services are really is really

180
00:10:49,280 --> 00:10:53,270
useful because we've now got a fair amount of code right here that I would not really want to tuck away

181
00:10:53,270 --> 00:10:54,310
inside of a controller.

182
00:10:54,320 --> 00:10:58,700
This is kind of involved stuff and we might want to make use of this code again at some point in time

183
00:10:58,700 --> 00:10:59,590
in the future.

184
00:10:59,600 --> 00:11:04,970
So a little blocks of code like this, this is exactly why we create services.

185
00:11:04,970 --> 00:11:09,230
So we have a location to put together some more complicated logic just like this.

186
00:11:09,680 --> 00:11:10,100
All right.

187
00:11:10,100 --> 00:11:14,540
I apologize, as usual, for the very long video, but I really just wanted one single video where we

188
00:11:14,540 --> 00:11:16,550
discussed everything related to updates.

189
00:11:16,550 --> 00:11:18,350
So let's take one more quick pause.

190
00:11:18,350 --> 00:11:22,510
We'll deal with remove, and then we'll be able to start testing these things by implementing some route

191
00:11:22,520 --> 00:11:24,140
handlers inside of our controller.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,550 --> 00:00:03,110
All right, On to our last function.

2
00:00:03,110 --> 00:00:03,680
Thank goodness.

3
00:00:03,710 --> 00:00:04,430
Remove.

4
00:00:04,460 --> 00:00:08,450
So this function is going to be similar in nature to update as an argument, we're going to take in

5
00:00:08,450 --> 00:00:11,390
the ID of the user that we want to delete out of our database.

6
00:00:11,390 --> 00:00:16,040
But then inside of here, we're going to once again run into an identical issue to what we just discussed

7
00:00:16,040 --> 00:00:17,570
a moment ago in the last video.

8
00:00:18,130 --> 00:00:21,890
So on our repository we have two very similar methods.

9
00:00:21,910 --> 00:00:22,780
One is delete.

10
00:00:22,810 --> 00:00:23,890
One is remove.

11
00:00:23,920 --> 00:00:26,740
Remove is designed to work with an entity.

12
00:00:26,770 --> 00:00:31,730
Delete is designed to work with just a plain ID or some kind of search criteria.

13
00:00:31,750 --> 00:00:36,140
So for example, we could say delete all the users with an email of blah.

14
00:00:37,170 --> 00:00:39,000
If we make use of delete.

15
00:00:39,870 --> 00:00:42,240
And we just pass in an ID directly.

16
00:00:42,270 --> 00:00:44,910
This will take just one trip to our database.

17
00:00:44,910 --> 00:00:49,440
So one query that we send off that says find all the users with this ID and just delete them.

18
00:00:49,440 --> 00:00:50,670
So it's very quick.

19
00:00:50,700 --> 00:00:56,040
The downside is that if we make use of delete any hooks that we have associated with, our entity will

20
00:00:56,040 --> 00:00:57,270
not be executed.

21
00:00:57,270 --> 00:01:02,060
And remember, in our case, we do have a hook associated with deletion.

22
00:01:02,070 --> 00:01:07,680
So back over inside of our user entity file at the very bottom we put together after remove right here

23
00:01:07,680 --> 00:01:10,080
or we made use of the after remove decorator.

24
00:01:10,260 --> 00:01:16,080
So if we call delete to delete a user, this hook right here will not be called.

25
00:01:16,110 --> 00:01:19,680
If we want this thing to be executed, then we have to make use.

26
00:01:21,410 --> 00:01:23,390
Of the remove method instead.

27
00:01:23,390 --> 00:01:26,000
And that means we need to first go into our database.

28
00:01:26,030 --> 00:01:29,270
We need to fetch that entity and then call remove on it.

29
00:01:29,270 --> 00:01:33,620
So that means two round trips to the database, one to get the user and then one to actually delete

30
00:01:33,620 --> 00:01:34,110
it.

31
00:01:34,160 --> 00:01:40,490
So twice the work just to get these hooks to run again, if you want to use hooks and you probably do.

32
00:01:40,520 --> 00:01:42,110
This is what you have to do.

33
00:01:42,870 --> 00:01:43,110
Okay.

34
00:01:43,110 --> 00:01:45,630
So with that in mind, let's give this a shot.

35
00:01:45,810 --> 00:01:49,680
Back inside of my user service, we're going to have some code that looks just about identical to what

36
00:01:49,680 --> 00:01:51,350
we have up here inside of update.

37
00:01:51,360 --> 00:01:53,430
So I'm going to say find the user.

38
00:01:55,350 --> 00:01:56,010
By their ID.

39
00:01:57,600 --> 00:02:00,840
We're making use of the await keyword, so I'm going to mark that thing with async.

40
00:02:02,520 --> 00:02:03,420
Then inside of here.

41
00:02:03,420 --> 00:02:06,270
Interesting question for you to decide on your own.

42
00:02:06,300 --> 00:02:09,750
Do you want to throw an error if a user was not found?

43
00:02:09,900 --> 00:02:14,580
If we didn't find a user with a given ID, well, that implies that they've already been removed.

44
00:02:14,580 --> 00:02:16,970
So we're kind of fulfilling the expectation here.

45
00:02:16,980 --> 00:02:20,400
It's up to you if you want to throw an error if the user was not found.

46
00:02:20,670 --> 00:02:25,020
I personally probably think that we probably should throw an error because if we try to remove a user

47
00:02:25,020 --> 00:02:30,660
with a given ID, I kind of want to be told if a user with that ID was not found because that indicates

48
00:02:30,660 --> 00:02:33,250
that I thought a user existed when they really didn't.

49
00:02:33,270 --> 00:02:34,770
But again, it's totally up to you in my case.

50
00:02:34,770 --> 00:02:36,150
I'm just going to say, you know what?

51
00:02:36,150 --> 00:02:39,030
If we don't find a user, let's throw an error.

52
00:02:42,180 --> 00:02:48,900
Then after that, we're going to return this repo, remove the user entity and again, that is a user

53
00:02:48,900 --> 00:02:55,080
entity instance, which means all of our hooks associated with that entity will be called they'll be

54
00:02:55,080 --> 00:02:55,770
executed.

55
00:02:57,150 --> 00:02:57,480
All right.

56
00:02:57,480 --> 00:02:58,710
So that is it.

57
00:02:58,740 --> 00:03:00,480
We've got our service put together.

58
00:03:00,570 --> 00:03:02,370
So now I just want to repeat one more time.

59
00:03:02,370 --> 00:03:07,320
The service actually turned out to be kind of beefy, with the exception of Find one and Find.

60
00:03:07,350 --> 00:03:10,740
We've got some decent amount of logic associated with each of these different methods.

61
00:03:10,740 --> 00:03:15,270
So hopefully now you're starting to see why services, although they kind of start to look identical

62
00:03:15,270 --> 00:03:19,650
to repositories in the beginning, eventually start to have a fair amount of logic inside them.

63
00:03:19,650 --> 00:03:25,170
Even if you've got the most basic of operations, such as just updating and removing without any additional

64
00:03:25,170 --> 00:03:26,790
logic or checks around them.

65
00:03:27,540 --> 00:03:30,050
Okay, so our service is in a good spot.

66
00:03:30,060 --> 00:03:30,870
I like it.

67
00:03:30,900 --> 00:03:33,540
Now all we have to do is go back over to our controller.

68
00:03:33,570 --> 00:03:38,880
We have to define a couple of route handlers inside of here and then call the associated methods we

69
00:03:38,880 --> 00:03:40,590
just defined inside of our service.

70
00:03:40,620 --> 00:03:44,430
We'll then do some manual testing and just make sure everything works as expected.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,110 --> 00:00:05,140
Let's add in all these different route handlers to our controller.

2
00:00:05,140 --> 00:00:08,170
So inside of our controller, we're going to define all these different methods.

3
00:00:08,200 --> 00:00:13,570
We're going to pull off some information from the incoming request and then pass that information along

4
00:00:13,600 --> 00:00:16,760
to the relevant method that we just defined inside of our service.

5
00:00:16,780 --> 00:00:19,140
Some of these are going to be really easy and straightforward.

6
00:00:19,150 --> 00:00:22,300
Others are going to be just a little bit more complicated in nature.

7
00:00:22,300 --> 00:00:23,650
So let's get to it.

8
00:00:25,060 --> 00:00:28,060
Back inside of my users controller at the very top.

9
00:00:28,060 --> 00:00:31,660
I'm going to first import a bunch of different things from the common package.

10
00:00:32,140 --> 00:00:37,780
I'm going to import git patch, ram and query.

11
00:00:37,990 --> 00:00:42,260
These are all different decorators that we're going to use to implement these remaining route handlers.

12
00:00:42,280 --> 00:00:47,110
So the first handler we will put together is going to be the one to fetch a user with a very particular

13
00:00:47,110 --> 00:00:47,410
ID.

14
00:00:49,020 --> 00:00:53,500
So for that inside my controller, I'll add in the Jit decorator.

15
00:00:53,520 --> 00:00:58,260
I'm going to add in a wild card of ID, I'm going to call this thing find user.

16
00:00:59,120 --> 00:01:04,490
Then inside of our argument list, we're going to make use of the param decorator that we just imported.

17
00:01:04,520 --> 00:01:08,900
Remember, this param decorator can be used to extract some information out of the incoming request

18
00:01:08,900 --> 00:01:09,590
route.

19
00:01:09,860 --> 00:01:14,270
So we'll add in at param and want to get the ID wild card.

20
00:01:14,720 --> 00:01:19,310
I'm going to call this thing ID and then give it a type of string.

21
00:01:20,810 --> 00:01:21,710
Now very important here.

22
00:01:21,710 --> 00:01:25,840
Remember, inside of our application, we've said that IDs are going to be numbers.

23
00:01:25,850 --> 00:01:27,800
That is how they are stored inside of our database.

24
00:01:27,800 --> 00:01:29,330
They are numbers.

25
00:01:29,330 --> 00:01:35,840
But whenever we receive a request, every single part of the URL is a string, even if we think that

26
00:01:35,840 --> 00:01:37,060
it looks like a number.

27
00:01:37,070 --> 00:01:42,440
So whenever it parses our incoming request, whenever it looks at that route, even if we have a number

28
00:01:42,440 --> 00:01:43,400
on the incoming request.

29
00:01:43,400 --> 00:01:44,480
So something like.

30
00:01:45,180 --> 00:01:47,210
Off some number.

31
00:01:47,220 --> 00:01:50,460
Nest is not going to automatically parse that thing into a number for you.

32
00:01:50,490 --> 00:01:52,670
It is given to you as a string.

33
00:01:52,680 --> 00:01:54,630
So we are given ID is a string.

34
00:01:54,630 --> 00:01:59,850
We need to take that string and parse it into a number and then we can pass it off to our user service,

35
00:01:59,880 --> 00:02:03,630
which you will recall is expecting an ID That is a number.

36
00:02:05,550 --> 00:02:06,540
So in total.

37
00:02:07,460 --> 00:02:08,930
We are going to return.

38
00:02:10,200 --> 00:02:11,880
This dot users.

39
00:02:12,440 --> 00:02:13,460
Service.

40
00:02:13,760 --> 00:02:20,570
We're going to find one and we're going to pass in the result of parse int with id like so.

41
00:02:21,510 --> 00:02:22,800
And that should be it.

42
00:02:23,860 --> 00:02:24,910
So let's save this.

43
00:02:25,980 --> 00:02:29,670
Well, then go back over to our client and try to test out that route handler.

44
00:02:30,470 --> 00:02:32,450
Okay, here's my client.

45
00:02:32,810 --> 00:02:35,120
I'm going to add in a new comment inside of here.

46
00:02:35,210 --> 00:02:38,330
I'll say find a particular user with a given ID.

47
00:02:39,470 --> 00:02:40,850
I'll make a get request.

48
00:02:43,450 --> 00:02:45,430
Two off one.

49
00:02:45,640 --> 00:02:50,920
I know that I have a user with an ID of one by default numbering of IDs inside of SQLite starts at one,

50
00:02:50,920 --> 00:02:53,110
so chances are you have an ID of one as well.

51
00:02:53,470 --> 00:02:54,940
I'm going to send that request off.

52
00:02:55,300 --> 00:02:56,680
And hey, there we go.

53
00:02:56,680 --> 00:02:57,710
We've got our user.

54
00:02:57,730 --> 00:02:58,660
Fantastic.

55
00:02:58,780 --> 00:03:01,720
So it looks like we get back the ID, email and password.

56
00:03:01,750 --> 00:03:06,100
Naturally, we probably do not want to send back the password of a user.

57
00:03:06,130 --> 00:03:06,840
Don't sweat it.

58
00:03:06,850 --> 00:03:09,490
We're going to make sure we filter that field out eventually.

59
00:03:10,570 --> 00:03:10,900
Okay.

60
00:03:10,900 --> 00:03:12,490
So this is a good start.

61
00:03:12,520 --> 00:03:15,730
Let's just continue on and take care of the next one.

62
00:03:15,730 --> 00:03:17,470
Find all users right away.

63
00:03:17,650 --> 00:03:21,790
So in this case, we're going to have a get request to slash auth and then it's going to have a query

64
00:03:21,790 --> 00:03:25,130
string with the email that we want to find.

65
00:03:25,150 --> 00:03:29,920
So we need to look into the query string, find that email property and then call on our service the

66
00:03:29,920 --> 00:03:31,210
find method.

67
00:03:33,470 --> 00:03:36,290
So I'll define our next route handler of Git.

68
00:03:37,470 --> 00:03:41,070
We'll call it find all users.

69
00:03:43,430 --> 00:03:46,040
Because we want to pull some information out of the query string.

70
00:03:46,040 --> 00:03:49,400
We're going to use the query decorator that we imported at the top.

71
00:03:50,460 --> 00:03:56,100
So we want to look into the query string and pull off the email out of that query string.

72
00:03:58,070 --> 00:04:00,140
We'll then receive the email as a string.

73
00:04:01,820 --> 00:04:03,590
Then inside of here, we're going to return.

74
00:04:04,850 --> 00:04:06,410
Users service.

75
00:04:07,190 --> 00:04:08,760
Find and remember.

76
00:04:08,780 --> 00:04:13,460
Find our expectations that we're going to call this thing with just an email, just whatever string

77
00:04:13,460 --> 00:04:14,360
we're looking for.

78
00:04:14,660 --> 00:04:16,280
So we'll pass in the email like so.

79
00:04:16,280 --> 00:04:17,390
And that should be it.

80
00:04:18,320 --> 00:04:19,790
So once again, we'll save this file.

81
00:04:19,790 --> 00:04:21,560
Open up our client.

82
00:04:23,910 --> 00:04:25,950
I'm going to add in a new request inside of here.

83
00:04:27,100 --> 00:04:30,790
Find all users with a given email.

84
00:04:31,240 --> 00:04:33,790
I'll do a git request to localhost.

85
00:04:34,680 --> 00:04:37,320
3000 off and I'll put a query string in.

86
00:04:37,320 --> 00:04:41,780
So question mark, email equals and then whatever email we're looking for.

87
00:04:41,790 --> 00:04:48,330
In my case, I already created a user with an email of asdf@asdf.com, so I'm going to try to find all

88
00:04:48,330 --> 00:04:52,260
the users with an email of asdf@asdf.com.

89
00:04:53,140 --> 00:04:54,070
We'll send this off.

90
00:04:54,310 --> 00:04:55,360
And there we go.

91
00:04:56,080 --> 00:04:58,030
Now get back three different users.

92
00:04:58,030 --> 00:05:03,040
And that is because I have recorded these videos a couple of times and I've created a few different

93
00:05:03,040 --> 00:05:03,640
users.

94
00:05:03,670 --> 00:05:05,950
All of them have that same email address.

95
00:05:05,950 --> 00:05:08,920
So if you get back just one user right now, totally fine.

96
00:05:08,920 --> 00:05:10,740
That's probably what we expect.

97
00:05:10,750 --> 00:05:15,280
If you have created any additional users, I would expect to see all the different users you have created

98
00:05:15,280 --> 00:05:18,310
with that given email address listed inside this array.

99
00:05:20,090 --> 00:05:24,980
Naturally, if we try to find all the different users with an email address, that definitely does not

100
00:05:24,980 --> 00:05:25,610
exist.

101
00:05:25,610 --> 00:05:29,030
So no users for me right now have an email like that.

102
00:05:29,150 --> 00:05:32,330
If I send that request off, I'll get back an empty array instead.

103
00:05:33,610 --> 00:05:35,860
All right, So let's say this looks pretty good.

104
00:05:36,130 --> 00:05:40,210
I'm going to revert that email right there to be just asdf@asdf.com.

105
00:05:42,060 --> 00:05:43,560
Well, let's take a pause right here.

106
00:05:43,560 --> 00:05:48,180
Rather than ending up with another very long video and we're going to start to take care of updating

107
00:05:48,180 --> 00:05:49,770
and deleting in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1
00:00:01,500 --> 00:00:02,850
Just two more requests.

2
00:00:02,850 --> 00:00:06,570
So in this video, we're going to skip over Patch very quickly because that one's going to be just a

3
00:00:06,570 --> 00:00:07,730
little bit more involved.

4
00:00:07,740 --> 00:00:09,060
It won't take care of delete.

5
00:00:09,180 --> 00:00:16,020
So with delete, we need to set up a delete route handler to auth slash and then some ID we're going

6
00:00:16,020 --> 00:00:18,760
to define that using a controller method of remove user.

7
00:00:18,760 --> 00:00:22,380
And I'm just going to turn right around and call the remove method on our service.

8
00:00:22,380 --> 00:00:23,610
So let's get to it.

9
00:00:24,940 --> 00:00:25,240
All right.

10
00:00:25,240 --> 00:00:29,980
So back inside my editor, I'm going to make use of the delete decorator, which I think we imported.

11
00:00:29,980 --> 00:00:30,880
No, we did not.

12
00:00:30,970 --> 00:00:32,770
Got to import that decorator at the very top.

13
00:00:32,770 --> 00:00:33,880
I'll do that right away.

14
00:00:38,130 --> 00:00:42,030
Well, then say that we want to handle incoming delete requests to slash colon ID.

15
00:00:43,290 --> 00:00:45,810
So we're going to call this method remove user.

16
00:00:47,220 --> 00:00:52,320
We're going to make use of the param decorator once again to receive the ID wild card.

17
00:00:52,530 --> 00:00:55,890
We're going to call that ID, And once again, it's going to start off as a string.

18
00:00:55,890 --> 00:00:57,870
We're going to have to convert that into a number.

19
00:00:58,760 --> 00:01:04,280
Then we will just turn right around and return this Dot users service.

20
00:01:04,990 --> 00:01:06,220
We'll remove.

21
00:01:07,270 --> 00:01:11,050
Pass int id and that should be it.

22
00:01:12,380 --> 00:01:13,230
One thing I would mention.

23
00:01:13,230 --> 00:01:15,930
Remember that back inside of our users service.

24
00:01:15,960 --> 00:01:17,010
Here it is right here.

25
00:01:17,630 --> 00:01:22,400
We define the remove method and it is possible that remove is going to throw an error.

26
00:01:22,430 --> 00:01:24,480
We're not going to worry about handling these errors just yet.

27
00:01:24,500 --> 00:01:28,790
We're going to finish off this delete handler and the update one and we'll come back and have a deeper

28
00:01:28,790 --> 00:01:30,530
discussion around error handling.

29
00:01:30,710 --> 00:01:32,180
So let's now go and test this.

30
00:01:32,210 --> 00:01:36,770
When you test it, if you get an error saying error with the server or something like that, don't sweat

31
00:01:36,770 --> 00:01:36,890
it.

32
00:01:36,890 --> 00:01:42,080
You probably try to delete a user that did not actually exist and the error is being caused by this

33
00:01:42,080 --> 00:01:42,650
line right here.

34
00:01:42,650 --> 00:01:44,330
So again, we're going to come back and fix that up.

35
00:01:46,020 --> 00:01:49,530
So to test out that route handler, I'll again pull up my client.

36
00:01:49,590 --> 00:01:54,360
I'll put in another comment and say delete a user with a given ID.

37
00:01:55,320 --> 00:01:57,420
I'll then make a delete request.

38
00:02:00,980 --> 00:02:03,560
And try to delete a user with an ID of one.

39
00:02:04,450 --> 00:02:07,180
I'll send it off and looks good.

40
00:02:08,300 --> 00:02:14,090
So we deleted the appropriate user, hopefully to make sure that deletion actually took place.

41
00:02:14,120 --> 00:02:18,650
Let's now make a follow up request and find all the users with that email address, the one that we

42
00:02:18,650 --> 00:02:19,460
just deleted.

43
00:02:19,490 --> 00:02:20,750
So I'm going to send that off.

44
00:02:20,750 --> 00:02:23,890
And now I no longer see a user with an ID of one.

45
00:02:23,900 --> 00:02:25,820
I only see two and three in my case here.

46
00:02:25,820 --> 00:02:27,980
And again, you might not have two and three.

47
00:02:28,010 --> 00:02:31,190
You might have an empty array if you have not created any additional users.

48
00:02:31,460 --> 00:02:33,620
So let's say that this looks pretty good for right now.

49
00:02:33,620 --> 00:02:38,870
But again, if we try to call delete a second time here, you might end up with an error like this.

50
00:02:38,870 --> 00:02:41,600
So we still have a little bit of error handling to take care of.

51
00:02:42,610 --> 00:02:43,820
All right, So this looks good.

52
00:02:43,840 --> 00:02:45,250
Just one last handler.

53
00:02:45,250 --> 00:02:49,360
So we're going to deal with this patch or the update one, and we'll come back and have a discussion

54
00:02:49,360 --> 00:02:51,010
around that error handling stuff.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,470 --> 00:00:02,610
Onto our update route.

2
00:00:02,640 --> 00:00:05,590
Handler So this will be a patch request coming in.

3
00:00:05,610 --> 00:00:09,610
It's going to have a body with the email and password that we want to update a user with.

4
00:00:09,630 --> 00:00:14,220
As I mentioned, this handlers can be just a little bit more complicated than the others for one simple

5
00:00:14,220 --> 00:00:16,020
reason It's going to have a body.

6
00:00:16,210 --> 00:00:20,730
So remember the other requests we have that has a body inside of it are post request to create a user.

7
00:00:20,730 --> 00:00:25,160
We had created a create user DTO to validate that incoming body.

8
00:00:25,170 --> 00:00:30,330
So we probably need a very similar step for the patch handler or the update handler as well.

9
00:00:30,600 --> 00:00:32,910
There's just one little difficulty around it.

10
00:00:33,480 --> 00:00:35,700
Okay, so here is our request.

11
00:00:36,600 --> 00:00:37,890
For creating a user.

12
00:00:37,890 --> 00:00:40,170
We've got an incoming request with an email and a password.

13
00:00:40,170 --> 00:00:44,910
We're going to validate it against the create user DTO, and it goes into the create user handler for

14
00:00:44,910 --> 00:00:45,720
update.

15
00:00:45,840 --> 00:00:48,990
The body of these requests is going to be very different in nature.

16
00:00:48,990 --> 00:00:55,590
In some cases, a user might be trying to update just their email or just their password or both the

17
00:00:55,590 --> 00:00:57,200
email and the password.

18
00:00:57,210 --> 00:01:02,100
So you and I are going to have to create a brand new DTO, and this is going to have some more complicated

19
00:01:02,100 --> 00:01:08,640
validation logic inside of it that is going to allow an email or a password or both an email and a password.

20
00:01:08,850 --> 00:01:13,080
So we're going to have to create this new update user DTO.

21
00:01:14,240 --> 00:01:16,790
And then wired up to our update user handler.

22
00:01:16,790 --> 00:01:18,080
So let's get to it.

23
00:01:19,070 --> 00:01:23,180
All right, back over inside my editor, inside my DTOs directory.

24
00:01:23,180 --> 00:01:28,160
I'm going to create a new file called update User DTO dot ts.

25
00:01:28,970 --> 00:01:30,380
I'm then going to import.

26
00:01:31,440 --> 00:01:33,960
Is email is string.

27
00:01:33,960 --> 00:01:38,910
And then one other and this is the key decorator we really need in this case is optional.

28
00:01:40,600 --> 00:01:43,390
All that's gonna come from class validator.

29
00:01:47,510 --> 00:01:48,710
The decorator is optional.

30
00:01:48,710 --> 00:01:50,510
Does kind of what you would expect.

31
00:01:50,540 --> 00:01:52,700
We can apply it to any property inside of a class.

32
00:01:52,700 --> 00:01:58,160
And if it has any other decorators such as is email or is string, then that property will be marked

33
00:01:58,160 --> 00:01:58,790
as optional.

34
00:01:58,790 --> 00:02:01,160
It does not have to be provided.

35
00:02:01,740 --> 00:02:06,770
So inside of here, we'll then define and export our class of update user DTO.

36
00:02:07,790 --> 00:02:11,900
We're going to say that if you want to update a user, you have to provide or can provide.

37
00:02:11,900 --> 00:02:12,500
That's the key.

38
00:02:12,530 --> 00:02:13,550
It's going to be optional.

39
00:02:13,550 --> 00:02:15,980
An email that's a string and a password.

40
00:02:15,980 --> 00:02:16,850
That's a string.

41
00:02:18,400 --> 00:02:21,400
The email will have a is email decorator.

42
00:02:21,400 --> 00:02:25,390
And then once again, the key here is is optional.

43
00:02:25,390 --> 00:02:30,970
So if you want to provide an email property that's great it will be validated with the is email decorator.

44
00:02:31,000 --> 00:02:35,440
Otherwise, if you do not provide an email, no problem, you're still going to pass validation.

45
00:02:35,560 --> 00:02:39,550
So we can do the same thing with password as well is string.

46
00:02:41,240 --> 00:02:43,160
And is optional.

47
00:02:45,140 --> 00:02:46,400
And that should be it.

48
00:02:47,300 --> 00:02:50,060
So now we'll go back over to our users controller.

49
00:02:51,020 --> 00:02:53,180
Here's the controller at the very top.

50
00:02:53,180 --> 00:02:58,070
I'm going to import our other DTO, So update user DTO.

51
00:03:03,920 --> 00:03:08,120
I'm then going to use our patch decorator to define a patch request handler.

52
00:03:08,580 --> 00:03:11,930
So down at the very bottom of the controller, we'll define patch.

53
00:03:13,570 --> 00:03:17,800
We'll have a wild card telling us what the user wants to update.

54
00:03:17,830 --> 00:03:19,990
We'll call this method Update User.

55
00:03:20,500 --> 00:03:21,220
Update User.

56
00:03:21,220 --> 00:03:21,700
Not Update.

57
00:03:21,700 --> 00:03:22,390
Update.

58
00:03:23,820 --> 00:03:27,330
We're then going to get two pieces of information from the incoming request.

59
00:03:27,330 --> 00:03:31,190
So the first will be the ID parameter and the second will be the body of the request.

60
00:03:31,200 --> 00:03:38,550
So we'll do an ID or ID and that will be a string, and then we'll get the entire body, we'll call

61
00:03:38,550 --> 00:03:43,110
that body and that will be validated against that.

62
00:03:43,110 --> 00:03:44,610
Update user DTO.

63
00:03:49,400 --> 00:03:49,910
All right.

64
00:03:49,910 --> 00:03:56,540
So then inside this method, we will return the result of this Dot users service update.

65
00:03:56,570 --> 00:04:01,370
We want to update the user with an ID of ID, and once again, we have to parse that into a number.

66
00:04:04,360 --> 00:04:07,030
And then as a second argument, we'll provide the body.

67
00:04:09,390 --> 00:04:09,930
All right.

68
00:04:09,930 --> 00:04:10,530
I think that's it.

69
00:04:10,560 --> 00:04:12,210
I think we're ready for a test.

70
00:04:12,450 --> 00:04:16,740
So once again, let's open up our request client right here.

71
00:04:16,740 --> 00:04:18,000
Here's the client.

72
00:04:18,790 --> 00:04:21,370
And I'm going to update a user.

73
00:04:21,970 --> 00:04:24,190
I'm going to send off a patch request.

74
00:04:25,330 --> 00:04:25,900
The localhost.

75
00:04:25,900 --> 00:04:26,830
3000.

76
00:04:28,070 --> 00:04:33,350
I'm going to try to update a user with ID because remember, I deleted the one with an ID of one and

77
00:04:33,350 --> 00:04:35,840
I'm going to try to change their email address.

78
00:04:36,870 --> 00:04:37,160
Two.

79
00:04:37,170 --> 00:04:40,800
How about a@a.com.

80
00:04:43,130 --> 00:04:43,740
Little mistake here.

81
00:04:43,800 --> 00:04:46,920
Need to also apply that header of content.

82
00:04:47,900 --> 00:04:50,600
Type application Json.

83
00:04:51,920 --> 00:04:54,110
Okay, let's send this off and see how we're doing.

84
00:04:54,530 --> 00:04:55,400
So we'll send it off.

85
00:04:55,400 --> 00:04:57,470
And sure enough, I get back the updated user.

86
00:04:57,500 --> 00:04:59,090
They've got that updated email.

87
00:04:59,090 --> 00:05:01,640
And of course, if I want to, I can do a further update.

88
00:05:01,640 --> 00:05:04,690
So I'll try changing the email address to be.

89
00:05:06,430 --> 00:05:07,630
And if I send that off?

90
00:05:07,660 --> 00:05:07,960
Yep.

91
00:05:07,960 --> 00:05:09,100
Looks like that works.

92
00:05:09,400 --> 00:05:14,150
And I should also be able to do an independent update of just the password by itself as well.

93
00:05:14,170 --> 00:05:18,220
So if I try to update just the password to a.

94
00:05:19,310 --> 00:05:19,700
Yep.

95
00:05:19,700 --> 00:05:20,990
There's my updated password.

96
00:05:21,850 --> 00:05:24,130
Okay, so that is our update handler.

97
00:05:24,130 --> 00:05:27,340
And as a matter of fact, that's the last handler we have to put together.

98
00:05:27,340 --> 00:05:32,650
So we've now got all of our request handlers, not only for creating a user, but also finding, deleting,

99
00:05:32,650 --> 00:05:34,000
updating and so on.

100
00:05:34,000 --> 00:05:39,640
So this is now a pretty good example of a typical request or a typical controller in a typical service

101
00:05:39,670 --> 00:05:41,500
inside of a Nest application.

102
00:05:41,680 --> 00:05:43,180
So this all looks pretty good.

103
00:05:43,360 --> 00:05:46,180
Now moving forward from here, we've got two options.

104
00:05:46,180 --> 00:05:51,040
We can either start to work on actually handling authentication inside of our app because remember,

105
00:05:51,040 --> 00:05:53,860
that was the entire original idea behind this user's controller.

106
00:05:53,860 --> 00:05:56,770
We wanted to handle user signup and sign in.

107
00:05:56,770 --> 00:06:00,190
We can also go and take a look at the other feature we're gonna have inside of our application, which

108
00:06:00,190 --> 00:06:01,570
is the report stuff.

109
00:06:01,570 --> 00:06:05,680
