So let's take a pause right here and then decide what direction we want to go in in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,950 --> 00:00:04,040
At the end of the last video, I said we were going to move on to our next feature.

2
00:00:04,070 --> 00:00:09,190
There actually is one last very quick thing I want to mention around our controller and our service.

3
00:00:09,200 --> 00:00:14,930
So specifically, some of the errors that we are throwing inside of our user service, down inside of

4
00:00:14,930 --> 00:00:19,340
our update method and remove, we are currently throwing some plain errors.

5
00:00:19,490 --> 00:00:24,020
Now ideally we would not throw a plain error object because Nest doesn't really know how to extract

6
00:00:24,020 --> 00:00:25,290
any information from that.

7
00:00:25,310 --> 00:00:30,560
Instead, we should be throwing exceptions that are implemented or created by nest, such as the not

8
00:00:30,560 --> 00:00:32,750
found exception, the bad request exception.

9
00:00:32,750 --> 00:00:36,860
All those other ones we looked at previously when we were working on our messages application.

10
00:00:37,460 --> 00:00:40,400
So we should probably update these objects we are throwing.

11
00:00:40,580 --> 00:00:44,660
But before we do, there's just one very small consideration I want to mention.

12
00:00:44,930 --> 00:00:51,070
You might recall that back on our messages application, we were throwing errors directly from the controller,

13
00:00:51,080 --> 00:00:53,780
so the controller was in charge of throwing errors.

14
00:00:53,810 --> 00:00:56,810
We threw exceptions like NotFound exception and so on.

15
00:00:56,960 --> 00:01:02,780
But in this case, we have our user service that is currently throwing some errors around, not being

16
00:01:02,780 --> 00:01:05,810
able to find a user or update and remove.

17
00:01:05,840 --> 00:01:09,080
So in this case, errors are coming from our service instead.

18
00:01:09,650 --> 00:01:15,440
So a very obvious solution here or something we should probably do would be to update these plain air

19
00:01:15,440 --> 00:01:17,510
objects to not found exceptions.

20
00:01:17,510 --> 00:01:22,490
Instead, if we throw a not found exception, then our controller or nest itself is really going to

21
00:01:22,490 --> 00:01:27,860
capture that error, extract some information from the air and send back a 404 response to whoever made

22
00:01:27,860 --> 00:01:29,000
the request to us.

23
00:01:29,360 --> 00:01:30,810
But that's kind of the issue here.

24
00:01:30,830 --> 00:01:35,030
The issue is that we are throwing errors from the user's service now.

25
00:01:35,120 --> 00:01:37,490
So here's why this is such a problem.

26
00:01:39,120 --> 00:01:40,870
Okay, so here's our user service.

27
00:01:40,890 --> 00:01:44,280
We're saying that we probably want it to throw a NotFound exception.

28
00:01:44,430 --> 00:01:48,570
If we throw an exception like that, it's going to flow back up right now to our users controller,

29
00:01:48,570 --> 00:01:50,370
which communicates over Http.

30
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:56,490
But remember, Nest itself is designed assuming that you might eventually handle communication protocols

31
00:01:56,490 --> 00:01:59,500
besides besides Http requests.

32
00:01:59,520 --> 00:02:04,200
So for example, we might eventually have another kind of controller inside of our application designed

33
00:02:04,200 --> 00:02:06,000
to handle websocket traffic.

34
00:02:06,030 --> 00:02:10,230
We also might have a totally different controller designed to handle gRPC requests.

35
00:02:10,890 --> 00:02:15,430
Not found exceptions are not compatible with any other kind of communication protocol.

36
00:02:15,450 --> 00:02:20,910
So if we throw an error like not found exception from the user service and that user service is being

37
00:02:20,910 --> 00:02:25,890
used by these other kinds of controllers, these controllers are not going to properly capture that

38
00:02:25,890 --> 00:02:30,600
error, extract information from it, and send a response back to whoever made the request to us.

39
00:02:30,810 --> 00:02:36,840
So all I want you to understand here is that if we start throwing Http specific errors from our user

40
00:02:36,840 --> 00:02:42,570
service, we start to have a kind of tough time reusing this service on future controllers that make

41
00:02:42,570 --> 00:02:44,700
use of different communication protocols.

42
00:02:45,150 --> 00:02:46,710
This is a very small thing.

43
00:02:46,710 --> 00:02:51,180
I just want you to understand it up front, because if you start to introduce additional communication

44
00:02:51,180 --> 00:02:55,410
protocols into your application, well, you just got to keep this little limitation in mind.

45
00:02:55,710 --> 00:03:00,120
A very easy thing to do here would be to implement your own exception filter.

46
00:03:00,150 --> 00:03:05,070
That's not something we're going to do inside this course Exception Filters for our purposes are very

47
00:03:05,070 --> 00:03:11,500
well implemented by nest itself, but you can implement your own custom exception filter and add it

48
00:03:11,500 --> 00:03:17,380
into these other kinds of controllers to capture exceptions like this one, like a not found exception.

49
00:03:17,380 --> 00:03:21,250
So again, just a very small side topic, just something I want you to be aware of.

50
00:03:21,490 --> 00:03:26,470
So we're not going to worry about this problem too much because we're not going to really try to reuse

51
00:03:26,470 --> 00:03:29,080
our user service with these other communication protocols.

52
00:03:29,080 --> 00:03:33,040
So for you and I just throwing a NotFound exception is going to work out okay.

53
00:03:33,580 --> 00:03:33,850
All right.

54
00:03:33,850 --> 00:03:34,750
So with that in mind.

55
00:03:35,410 --> 00:03:37,210
Let's go back over to our service.

56
00:03:38,540 --> 00:03:39,290
At the very top.

57
00:03:39,290 --> 00:03:43,010
I'm going to find our nestjs common import.

58
00:03:43,700 --> 00:03:46,700
I'm going to get the NotFound exception.

59
00:03:47,710 --> 00:03:50,890
We'll then go down to our update method right here.

60
00:03:50,890 --> 00:03:55,720
And rather than throwing a new error, we'll throw a not found exception.

61
00:03:57,370 --> 00:03:59,950
I'm also going to do the same thing for remove as well.

62
00:04:02,700 --> 00:04:07,650
Now, the last place where we might want to throw an error is around our find one method.

63
00:04:08,190 --> 00:04:10,530
Now, usually whenever you call, find one.

64
00:04:10,530 --> 00:04:14,100
It's totally fine to just return null if you're not able to find a particular record.

65
00:04:14,100 --> 00:04:16,080
So we kind of have to make a decision here.

66
00:04:16,080 --> 00:04:21,030
If we call, find one and we're not able to find a particular user, should we throw an error or just

67
00:04:21,030 --> 00:04:22,170
return null?

68
00:04:22,350 --> 00:04:25,760
It's kind of a question that I leave up to you which way you want to take it.

69
00:04:25,770 --> 00:04:31,920
Personally, I like being able to call, find one and get back a just plain null value as opposed to

70
00:04:31,920 --> 00:04:32,670
throwing an error.

71
00:04:32,700 --> 00:04:34,430
That's my particular preference.

72
00:04:34,440 --> 00:04:39,360
So rather than throwing an error directly from this method, I'm going to go back over to our controller.

73
00:04:39,950 --> 00:04:44,750
And inside of our controller, I'm going to find our find user route handler right there.

74
00:04:45,050 --> 00:04:47,150
So this is where we call find one.

75
00:04:47,270 --> 00:04:50,000
So if I call, find one, I want to get back null.

76
00:04:50,030 --> 00:04:55,970
But in this very particular case, if I get back null, then I want to throw a not found exception to

77
00:04:55,970 --> 00:04:59,060
make sure that I return a 404 response back to the user.

78
00:04:59,480 --> 00:05:02,530
This is once again just kind of very specific semantics.

79
00:05:02,540 --> 00:05:07,580
You can very easily decide to throw a not found exception back inside the service if you want to, but

80
00:05:07,580 --> 00:05:10,340
I choose to implement this inside my controller instead.

81
00:05:10,580 --> 00:05:14,780
The only reason I'm not doing this for the update and delete route handlers is that it's kind of more

82
00:05:14,780 --> 00:05:19,340
challenging for us to kind of get a little hook in there and say, Oh, well, capture an error or return

83
00:05:19,340 --> 00:05:22,280
null or something like that if no update or removal was applied.

84
00:05:23,370 --> 00:05:23,670
All right.

85
00:05:23,670 --> 00:05:24,720
So inside the controller.

86
00:05:25,820 --> 00:05:26,420
At the very top.

87
00:05:26,420 --> 00:05:31,610
I'm going to find the common import and will import not found exception.

88
00:05:32,610 --> 00:05:38,370
Then down inside of our git handler rather than immediately returning the result of that, I'm going

89
00:05:38,370 --> 00:05:41,820
to assign the result to user find.

90
00:05:41,820 --> 00:05:45,390
One itself is an asynchronous call, so we need to await that.

91
00:05:45,960 --> 00:05:48,600
That means I also have to mark this function as async.

92
00:05:49,140 --> 00:05:51,240
I'll then check to see if we were able to find a user.

93
00:05:51,240 --> 00:06:00,000
So I'll say if there is not a user, then let's throw a new not found exception and I'll put in a message

94
00:06:00,000 --> 00:06:01,920
of user not found.

95
00:06:03,130 --> 00:06:07,270
And then if we were able to find a user, let's just go ahead and return it.

96
00:06:09,170 --> 00:06:10,850
Okay, so one last test here.

97
00:06:11,270 --> 00:06:13,220
Let's open up our client.

98
00:06:13,760 --> 00:06:19,280
I'm going to try to find a user that definitely does not exist, so I'll make a request to auth slash

99
00:06:19,280 --> 00:06:20,000
some ID.

100
00:06:20,630 --> 00:06:23,180
If I send that request off, I should get back a 404.

101
00:06:23,210 --> 00:06:24,080
Very good.

102
00:06:24,590 --> 00:06:30,680
And then if I try to delete or update a user that definitely does not exist, I should get a 404.

103
00:06:31,680 --> 00:06:32,910
And for the patch.

104
00:06:33,930 --> 00:06:34,790
Send that off.

105
00:06:34,800 --> 00:06:35,910
Get a 404.

106
00:06:36,390 --> 00:06:36,720
Okay.

107
00:06:36,720 --> 00:06:38,100
So that looks pretty good.

108
00:06:38,940 --> 00:06:39,330
All right.

109
00:06:39,330 --> 00:06:43,710
So now we're all done finally with this user service and users controller.

110
00:06:43,710 --> 00:06:48,030
So now we can start to move on to some other features around our project in just a moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# section 10: Custom Data Serialization


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,090 --> 00:00:01,960
I'm so sorry.

2
00:00:01,960 --> 00:00:02,920
I am such a liar.

3
00:00:02,920 --> 00:00:06,850
I've said twice now that we're going to move on to our next feature, but there is still a little bit

4
00:00:06,850 --> 00:00:09,790
more I want to go over around all this users related stuff.

5
00:00:09,790 --> 00:00:13,810
These are some topics I was going to cover later on inside the course, but realized that it just made

6
00:00:13,810 --> 00:00:17,770
a lot more sense to cover them right now because these are some concepts and ideas that we're going

7
00:00:17,770 --> 00:00:21,610
to use for all the different services controllers and so on that we put together.

8
00:00:21,700 --> 00:00:24,700
So let me show you what we're going to fix up in this video.

9
00:00:24,940 --> 00:00:26,710
I've got my client open.

10
00:00:26,710 --> 00:00:31,330
I'm going to make a Git request to fetch a very particular user, so I'll send off the request.

11
00:00:31,330 --> 00:00:35,290
And our response right here includes the password of our user.

12
00:00:35,320 --> 00:00:38,530
Definitely not a property that we want to include inside the response.

13
00:00:38,560 --> 00:00:42,840
Remember, we are going to eventually encrypt our passwords before we store them inside the database.

14
00:00:42,850 --> 00:00:44,440
We just haven't done that yet.

15
00:00:44,470 --> 00:00:49,000
Nonetheless, even after encrypting these passwords, we still don't want to send them back inside a

16
00:00:49,000 --> 00:00:49,810
response.

17
00:00:49,810 --> 00:00:55,090
So we need to make sure that whenever we send a user back, we edit out that password property.

18
00:00:55,120 --> 00:00:56,770
We just want to totally remove it.

19
00:00:58,020 --> 00:01:02,340
So to do so, we are going to follow first.

20
00:01:03,240 --> 00:01:08,190
A recommendation on how to do this based on some recommendations from the documentation.

21
00:01:08,190 --> 00:01:13,920
So we're going to essentially do exactly what the documentation excuse me, documentation says you should

22
00:01:13,920 --> 00:01:14,490
do.

23
00:01:14,520 --> 00:01:18,560
We're then going to take a look at some downsides with this recommended approach.

24
00:01:18,570 --> 00:01:23,460
And we'll take a look at a slightly more customized, slightly more complicated solution.

25
00:01:23,460 --> 00:01:27,240
That's, I think, definitely a lot better and much, much more flexible.

26
00:01:27,240 --> 00:01:31,830
And I think you'll eventually want to use this custom solution on your own projects as well.

27
00:01:32,130 --> 00:01:37,260
So first we're going to focus on what Nest says you should do to remove that password property.

28
00:01:37,980 --> 00:01:38,280
Okay.

29
00:01:38,280 --> 00:01:43,050
So first, kind of a quick review on what's going on behind the scenes inside of our request handlers

30
00:01:43,050 --> 00:01:43,710
right now.

31
00:01:44,890 --> 00:01:50,890
So I just sent in a request that was a git to auth slash to that's going to flow into our users controller.

32
00:01:50,920 --> 00:01:53,440
User's controller will turn around to the service.

33
00:01:53,470 --> 00:01:58,600
Service will make use of the repository and eventually get and return a user entity instance.

34
00:01:58,810 --> 00:02:04,630
We then return that back to the controller and that instance gets returned from the controller itself.

35
00:02:04,630 --> 00:02:09,070
And then Nest takes that entity instance and turns it into Json for us.

36
00:02:09,070 --> 00:02:16,030
When it turns that entity instance into Json, it will include the ID property, the password and the

37
00:02:16,030 --> 00:02:17,050
email as well.

38
00:02:17,690 --> 00:02:19,160
So that's what's going on right now.

39
00:02:19,250 --> 00:02:23,990
So to fix everything up using the nest recommended solution, here's what we're going to do.

40
00:02:24,630 --> 00:02:29,820
First inside of our user entity instance, we're going to make use of a library that's going to attach

41
00:02:29,820 --> 00:02:36,540
a very small set of rules on how to take an instance of a user entity and turn it into a plain object

42
00:02:36,540 --> 00:02:38,130
and then into Json.

43
00:02:39,270 --> 00:02:42,450
So inside the entity instance, just a very small set of rules.

44
00:02:43,100 --> 00:02:47,030
Then add our controller, specifically the find user route handler.

45
00:02:47,060 --> 00:02:52,010
We're going to set up a decorator that is called a class serializer Interceptor.

46
00:02:52,040 --> 00:02:58,400
An interceptor interceptor is a tool inside of Nest that allows us to intercept incoming requests or

47
00:02:58,400 --> 00:03:01,670
outgoing responses and mess around with them in some way.

48
00:03:01,700 --> 00:03:05,600
So in this case, we are going to intercept the outgoing response.

49
00:03:05,630 --> 00:03:11,690
We're going to take the user entity inside there and we're going to turn that user entity into a plain

50
00:03:11,720 --> 00:03:16,370
object based upon the rules that we set up inside the entity itself.

51
00:03:17,160 --> 00:03:18,170
So that's it.

52
00:03:18,180 --> 00:03:21,960
Now, if this seems complicated, at the end of the day, the amount of code we have to write for this

53
00:03:21,990 --> 00:03:23,670
is actually very, very small.

54
00:03:23,670 --> 00:03:27,550
So let's just go ahead and update our project and implement all this stuff right away.

55
00:03:27,570 --> 00:03:32,430
So step number one, we're going to set up some rules inside of our user entity instance.

56
00:03:33,330 --> 00:03:36,600
Back over inside my editor, I'm going to find my user entity file.

57
00:03:38,120 --> 00:03:44,480
Then at the very top we are going to import exclude from class transformer.

58
00:03:45,510 --> 00:03:47,910
Remember that we installed this package a little bit ago.

59
00:03:47,910 --> 00:03:53,010
It is the companion package to the class validator, which we've been making use of quite a bit.

60
00:03:53,280 --> 00:03:58,590
So exclude is a decorator and this is a decorator that's going to create this little, little set of

61
00:03:58,590 --> 00:04:04,020
rules that describe how to take an instance of a user and turn it into a plain object.

62
00:04:04,730 --> 00:04:08,010
We're going to apply that exclude decorator to the password property.

63
00:04:08,030 --> 00:04:12,260
So, as you guessed, that means whenever you take an instance of a user and turn them into a plain

64
00:04:12,260 --> 00:04:15,460
object and then into Json, just exclude the password.

65
00:04:15,470 --> 00:04:16,580
That's pretty much it.

66
00:04:18,100 --> 00:04:18,370
Okay.

67
00:04:18,370 --> 00:04:19,510
So I'm going to save this.

68
00:04:19,660 --> 00:04:21,980
We're then going to go back over to our controller.

69
00:04:22,000 --> 00:04:26,560
We're going to import a couple of things inside there, and then we're going to wire up an interceptor

70
00:04:26,560 --> 00:04:30,190
to our users controller, find user route handler.

71
00:04:32,080 --> 00:04:37,240
So back inside the controller at the very top, we're going to find our import from Nestjs Common.

72
00:04:37,270 --> 00:04:41,570
We've already imported a lot of stuff from it, but we still have a couple more things to import.

73
00:04:41,590 --> 00:04:46,750
So in this case, we want to get a decorator called Use Interceptors.

74
00:04:47,620 --> 00:04:52,720
And then something else called a class serializer interceptor.

75
00:04:53,020 --> 00:04:58,330
So these are two tools that we're going to use to intercept the outgoing response and mess around with

76
00:04:58,330 --> 00:04:59,380
it in some way.

77
00:05:01,550 --> 00:05:07,460
I'll then go on down to our find user route handler and then right above it.

78
00:05:08,370 --> 00:05:12,420
We're going to wire up use interceptors.

79
00:05:13,680 --> 00:05:19,020
And then inside their class serializer interceptor like so.

80
00:05:20,240 --> 00:05:21,500
And that should be it.

81
00:05:22,240 --> 00:05:23,350
So let's now save this.

82
00:05:23,350 --> 00:05:24,810
We're going to save both files.

83
00:05:24,820 --> 00:05:27,190
I'm going to go back over to my client.

84
00:05:27,370 --> 00:05:29,080
I'm going to make that same request.

85
00:05:29,080 --> 00:05:31,900
And now our password is no longer included.

86
00:05:32,820 --> 00:05:33,030
All right.

87
00:05:33,030 --> 00:05:34,050
So that definitely looks good.

88
00:05:34,050 --> 00:05:35,880
Definitely solved the issue.

89
00:05:36,060 --> 00:05:41,220
But as we're going to very quickly realize, even though it solved the issue, there are some well,

90
00:05:41,220 --> 00:05:43,310
kind of small downsides to this approach.

91
00:05:43,320 --> 00:05:48,420
So quick pause right here and we'll take a look at why this recommended solution is not really the best

92
00:05:48,420 --> 00:05:49,560
thing in the world.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:06,060
Now that we have implemented the recommended solution on how to hide that password property, we're

2
00:00:06,060 --> 00:00:10,380
going to take a look at some downsides with this approach and then start to implement a little bit better

3
00:00:10,380 --> 00:00:11,640
way of dealing with this.

4
00:00:11,790 --> 00:00:12,090
Okay.

5
00:00:12,090 --> 00:00:14,160
So first, here's the big issue.

6
00:00:14,460 --> 00:00:18,960
I want you to imagine that at some point in time we decide to start storing more information around

7
00:00:18,960 --> 00:00:22,380
our users, maybe their age, their name and so on.

8
00:00:22,530 --> 00:00:27,390
And then at some further point in time, maybe we decide to put together some admin functionality inside

9
00:00:27,390 --> 00:00:27,990
of our app.

10
00:00:27,990 --> 00:00:33,360
So maybe there are administrator users and an administrator should be able to look up a user and see

11
00:00:33,360 --> 00:00:39,480
a lot of detailed information around them to service that we might decide to add in a new route handler

12
00:00:39,480 --> 00:00:40,530
to our application.

13
00:00:40,530 --> 00:00:46,260
So something like admin auth to if a user makes a get request to that, we're going to make sure that

14
00:00:46,260 --> 00:00:48,870
the request is coming from a valid administrator.

15
00:00:50,030 --> 00:00:53,330
We'll then set up a custom route handler just for that route.

16
00:00:53,540 --> 00:00:58,520
That thing will reach out to our user service, find a particular user, and then send back a ton of

17
00:00:58,520 --> 00:01:00,380
information related to that person.

18
00:01:00,380 --> 00:01:03,740
So their ID, their email, their age, their name and so on.

19
00:01:04,070 --> 00:01:06,530
But that's just the admin route handler.

20
00:01:06,530 --> 00:01:11,660
We still might want to have routes inside of our application that allows some random person to look

21
00:01:11,660 --> 00:01:14,780
up another user but not see all that info.

22
00:01:14,780 --> 00:01:21,200
So we still probably want a public route to look up a very particular user, but only get back just

23
00:01:21,200 --> 00:01:24,680
the ID and the email and not the age and the name.

24
00:01:26,200 --> 00:01:30,730
So this route handler still called maybe something like find user would reach out to the exact same

25
00:01:30,730 --> 00:01:33,610
user's service and try to find one particular user.

26
00:01:33,880 --> 00:01:38,950
So now the whole problem here using the nest base solution is that we're saying that we want to take

27
00:01:38,950 --> 00:01:40,720
the same kind of user entity.

28
00:01:40,750 --> 00:01:44,740
In both cases, we want to send back that same user entity.

29
00:01:44,740 --> 00:01:49,780
But between these two different route handlers, we want to send back very different sets of information

30
00:01:49,780 --> 00:01:50,790
around that user.

31
00:01:50,800 --> 00:01:53,470
In one case, we want to include a lot of different properties.

32
00:01:53,470 --> 00:01:56,170
In the other case, we want to include a very few.

33
00:01:56,380 --> 00:02:02,410
So we really need to customize that exclude directive and how it gets applied on the user entity instance

34
00:02:02,410 --> 00:02:05,310
based upon which route handler is being accessed.

35
00:02:05,320 --> 00:02:10,240
And that is really not possible by using the approach recommended by Nest.

36
00:02:10,570 --> 00:02:14,380
So we're going to take a look, as I mentioned, at a slightly different approach that's going to address

37
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:16,440
this whole issue very, very easily.

38
00:02:16,450 --> 00:02:19,180
So let me show you a diagram of what you and I are going to do.

39
00:02:20,330 --> 00:02:21,080
All right.

40
00:02:22,420 --> 00:02:28,000
So we are no longer going to tie any formatting information or anything around serialization directly

41
00:02:28,000 --> 00:02:33,850
to our user entity instance because it's pretty clear that we cannot attack or attach any view related

42
00:02:33,850 --> 00:02:38,500
logic to that entity because it's really not going to scale and serve multiple different route handlers

43
00:02:38,500 --> 00:02:39,490
appropriately.

44
00:02:39,640 --> 00:02:43,110
Instead, you and I are going to create a custom interceptor.

45
00:02:43,120 --> 00:02:48,460
And remember, an interceptor is a class that's going to mess around in some way with the response before

46
00:02:48,460 --> 00:02:53,300
it gets sent back to someone making the request inside of this custom interceptor.

47
00:02:53,320 --> 00:03:00,020
We are going to serialize or turn our user entity instance into a plain object and then eventually into

48
00:03:00,020 --> 00:03:05,920
a plain Json by using some serialization rules set up inside of a DTO.

49
00:03:06,130 --> 00:03:11,950
Now, at this point in time, remember we've only seen DTOs around handling incoming requests, but

50
00:03:11,950 --> 00:03:15,400
the term DTO stands for data transfer object.

51
00:03:15,430 --> 00:03:21,280
DTOs in general, in other words, in other languages and other frameworks are not only used for handling

52
00:03:21,280 --> 00:03:27,020
incoming data, DTOs are also used in other languages, other frameworks for handling the formatting

53
00:03:27,020 --> 00:03:29,030
of outgoing data as well.

54
00:03:29,240 --> 00:03:36,050
So we are going to create a user DTO that describes exactly how we want to format a user entity.

55
00:03:36,080 --> 00:03:39,770
In other words, what properties we want to include for a very particular route.

56
00:03:39,800 --> 00:03:43,430
Handler So maybe just one user DTO.

57
00:03:45,270 --> 00:03:50,700
For the admin controller, so we might have one for this one right here that says let's pull off the

58
00:03:50,700 --> 00:03:56,520
ID email, age and name for every user, and then we might create a second DTO with a different set

59
00:03:56,520 --> 00:03:59,010
of of serialization rules on it.

60
00:03:59,100 --> 00:04:04,950
The second DTO might say, well, if we're trying to serialize a user for this public route, we're

61
00:04:04,950 --> 00:04:07,380
going to only pull off the ID and the email.

62
00:04:08,350 --> 00:04:12,040
We're going to apply this custom interceptor to each of our different route handlers.

63
00:04:12,040 --> 00:04:17,860
So on a route handler basis, we'll be able to describe exactly how we want to format our outgoing Json.

64
00:04:17,860 --> 00:04:21,760
And we can very easily say that we want to include this property, but not that one, and so on.

65
00:04:22,060 --> 00:04:23,410
So that's the idea.

66
00:04:23,440 --> 00:04:24,580
Custom interceptor.

67
00:04:24,580 --> 00:04:30,550
We're going to wire up our own custom DTOs to describe how to serialize these different user entities.

68
00:04:30,880 --> 00:04:34,780
So now that we understand what we need to do, let's take a pause right here and start to work on this

69
00:04:34,780 --> 00:04:37,030
custom interceptor in just a moment.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,010 --> 00:00:04,860
Let's start to put together our own custom interceptor.

2
00:00:04,890 --> 00:00:10,050
Now, in this video, we're not going to worry about any stuff around serialization or DTOs or anything

3
00:00:10,050 --> 00:00:10,760
like that.

4
00:00:10,770 --> 00:00:15,870
I want to first begin by just understanding how to create an interceptor and how we actually use them

5
00:00:15,870 --> 00:00:17,090
inside of our project.

6
00:00:17,100 --> 00:00:21,690
So just interceptors for right now, then we'll come back to this idea of serializing users and all

7
00:00:21,690 --> 00:00:22,740
that kind of good stuff.

8
00:00:23,640 --> 00:00:23,940
All right.

9
00:00:23,940 --> 00:00:25,130
So a couple of diagrams.

10
00:00:25,140 --> 00:00:31,440
So the first thing to understand is that interceptors can be used to intercept outgoing responses and

11
00:00:31,440 --> 00:00:33,690
or incoming requests.

12
00:00:33,840 --> 00:00:38,910
So we can really think of interceptors as being very similar to Middlewares in many other frameworks

13
00:00:38,910 --> 00:00:39,690
or languages.

14
00:00:39,690 --> 00:00:45,180
If you're at all familiar with Middlewares, we can have as many interceptors intercept incoming requests

15
00:00:45,180 --> 00:00:47,520
or outgoing responses as we want.

16
00:00:48,620 --> 00:00:54,110
Whenever we create an interceptor, we can apply that interceptor either to individual root handlers.

17
00:00:54,950 --> 00:00:56,150
So like so.

18
00:00:56,360 --> 00:01:00,170
Or we can apply the interceptor to the entire controller.

19
00:01:00,440 --> 00:01:05,000
So if we apply it to the controller, that will make sure that we run this interceptor on every handler

20
00:01:05,000 --> 00:01:06,090
inside the controller.

21
00:01:06,110 --> 00:01:09,280
Or alternatively, we can apply an interceptor globally.

22
00:01:09,290 --> 00:01:14,840
So in other words, for every request or response that comes into or goes out of our project, we can

23
00:01:14,840 --> 00:01:16,280
run our interceptor.

24
00:01:16,430 --> 00:01:20,720
In our case, for the one you and I are putting together, we probably are going to want to wire up

25
00:01:20,720 --> 00:01:25,160
this interceptor, either at the controller level or on the root handler level.

26
00:01:26,640 --> 00:01:30,900
Now for the interceptor itself to create one, we are going to create a class.

27
00:01:30,930 --> 00:01:35,340
We can call it anything we want, but we're going to follow our general naming conventions, which is

28
00:01:35,340 --> 00:01:40,050
going to be pretty much some name and then the word interceptor, just to indicate that this is a class

29
00:01:40,050 --> 00:01:42,000
that serves as an interceptor.

30
00:01:42,300 --> 00:01:48,330
Then inside of our class, the only requirement is that you define a method called specifically intercept.

31
00:01:48,420 --> 00:01:53,130
This method is going to be called automatically anytime our interceptor needs to run.

32
00:01:53,130 --> 00:01:56,400
So handle some incoming request or outgoing response.

33
00:01:57,030 --> 00:02:01,770
The two arguments to this method are going to be something that we refer to as context and something

34
00:02:01,770 --> 00:02:02,850
called next.

35
00:02:03,150 --> 00:02:08,130
Context is essentially a wrapper around some information on the incoming request.

36
00:02:08,310 --> 00:02:15,060
And then next is kind of and I use the term kind of here very lightly because it's a pretty fancy object,

37
00:02:15,060 --> 00:02:20,490
but you can really think of this next thing as being somewhat like our actual root handler inside of

38
00:02:20,490 --> 00:02:21,310
our controller.

39
00:02:21,330 --> 00:02:23,480
It is not exactly the root handler.

40
00:02:23,490 --> 00:02:27,780
It's actually a rxjs observable if you're familiar with rxjs at all.

41
00:02:27,780 --> 00:02:32,790
But for the purposes of discussion, you can really kind of imagine this next argument is kind of like

42
00:02:32,790 --> 00:02:34,320
our root handler itself.

43
00:02:35,420 --> 00:02:35,660
All right.

44
00:02:35,660 --> 00:02:38,800
So now that we got this high level overview, let's go back over to our editor.

45
00:02:38,810 --> 00:02:41,210
We're going to first do a little bit of code cleanup.

46
00:02:41,210 --> 00:02:47,630
In particular, we're going to remove that includes decorator or excludes decorator from our entity.

47
00:02:47,660 --> 00:02:50,390
We'll then start putting together our custom interceptor.

48
00:02:51,660 --> 00:02:55,650
So back inside my editor, I'm going to first find my user entity.

49
00:02:56,540 --> 00:02:59,770
Remember inside of here we had imported that exclude decorator.

50
00:02:59,870 --> 00:03:01,000
Don't really want this anymore.

51
00:03:01,010 --> 00:03:06,500
So I'm going to take that out and I'll remove the use of the exclude decorator on the password property.

52
00:03:07,300 --> 00:03:10,630
So that is not strictly required as a part of the interceptor creation process.

53
00:03:10,630 --> 00:03:13,060
I just want to make sure that we cleaned up that little bit of code.

54
00:03:13,920 --> 00:03:20,280
Next up inside of my SRC directory, I'm going to create a new folder called interceptors.

55
00:03:20,520 --> 00:03:25,380
I'm creating this folder inside the SRC directory because I want to eventually use this interceptor

56
00:03:25,380 --> 00:03:30,180
that you and I are going to create across all of our different modules, not just the user module.

57
00:03:30,930 --> 00:03:34,410
Then inside that folder, I'll create a new file called Serialize.

58
00:03:35,010 --> 00:03:37,190
Interceptor BTS.

59
00:03:37,200 --> 00:03:39,540
So that's going to be the name of our custom interceptor.

60
00:03:39,540 --> 00:03:45,210
We're going to call it the serialize interceptor because it's going to take a object and then serialize

61
00:03:45,210 --> 00:03:46,980
it eventually into Json.

62
00:03:48,700 --> 00:03:52,900
Then inside of here at the very top, we're going to add in immediately a whole bunch of imports.

63
00:03:52,900 --> 00:03:54,280
So a little bit of typing.

64
00:03:54,700 --> 00:03:58,240
We're going to first get use interceptors.

65
00:03:58,510 --> 00:04:00,250
We will get Nest.

66
00:04:01,020 --> 00:04:02,220
Interceptor.

67
00:04:02,980 --> 00:04:10,270
Execution, context, call handler and all those are going to come from Nest JS.

68
00:04:10,300 --> 00:04:11,080
Common.

69
00:04:12,090 --> 00:04:16,590
After that, we're going to get observable from rxjs.

70
00:04:18,170 --> 00:04:20,990
Something called map from Rxjs.

71
00:04:21,020 --> 00:04:22,100
Operators.

72
00:04:22,850 --> 00:04:31,310
And then finally, we're going to get plane to class from class transformer like so.

73
00:04:35,520 --> 00:04:37,830
Then after that, we'll go ahead and define our interceptor.

74
00:04:37,830 --> 00:04:43,710
So I'm going to create a class and let's export it right away called serialize Interceptor.

75
00:04:44,710 --> 00:04:47,350
Now the next step, the next thing we're going to do is optional.

76
00:04:47,350 --> 00:04:52,060
This is a little feature of TypeScript we're going to use just to make putting our interceptor together

77
00:04:52,090 --> 00:04:53,190
a little bit easier.

78
00:04:53,200 --> 00:04:56,710
So after our class name, we're going to add in implements.

79
00:04:57,460 --> 00:04:59,440
Nest interceptor.

80
00:04:59,620 --> 00:05:02,770
So a quick reminder on what the implements keyword does for us.

81
00:05:02,830 --> 00:05:05,710
Implements is not the same as extends.

82
00:05:05,740 --> 00:05:09,470
We make use of extends whenever we are subclassing an existing class.

83
00:05:09,490 --> 00:05:16,060
We make use of implements anytime that we want to create a new class that satisfies all the requirements

84
00:05:16,060 --> 00:05:20,390
of either an abstract class or an interface, in this case, an interface.

85
00:05:20,410 --> 00:05:25,960
So by adding on implements nest interceptor, TypeScript is going to check all the methods that exist

86
00:05:25,960 --> 00:05:27,070
in this interface.

87
00:05:27,100 --> 00:05:31,450
It's then going to make sure that our class properly implements all those different methods.

88
00:05:31,690 --> 00:05:36,760
So in this case, if we hover over our class name right now, we are told that in order to create an

89
00:05:36,760 --> 00:05:40,090
interceptor, we must define a method called intercept.

90
00:05:40,120 --> 00:05:41,830
So let's do that right away.

91
00:05:42,100 --> 00:05:44,350
So I'll define a method called intercept.

92
00:05:46,490 --> 00:05:48,590
And as soon as we do that, we'll then get another error.

93
00:05:48,590 --> 00:05:53,180
And we're going to be told that if we want to define this method, then it must receive two different

94
00:05:53,180 --> 00:05:53,960
arguments.

95
00:05:53,990 --> 00:05:58,430
We must receive that context thing and that next thing.

96
00:05:58,640 --> 00:06:01,250
So let's make sure we receive those two arguments.

97
00:06:01,490 --> 00:06:03,680
I'm going to put in context.

98
00:06:04,750 --> 00:06:06,910
Of execution context.

99
00:06:08,050 --> 00:06:12,730
And then handler of type call handler.

100
00:06:13,650 --> 00:06:18,690
Even then, we're still getting an error here because in order to properly implement this intercept

101
00:06:18,690 --> 00:06:24,780
method, we must also make sure that we return something of type, either observable or a promise.

102
00:06:25,050 --> 00:06:28,260
The last thing we're going to do here just to help guide us along.

103
00:06:29,160 --> 00:06:33,630
Right at the very end of the argument list, I'm going to put in a return type annotation.

104
00:06:33,990 --> 00:06:39,030
So I'm going to put in a colon observable any like so.

105
00:06:39,900 --> 00:06:45,000
So remember, by putting in a type annotation that's not strictly required, it just helps TypeScript

106
00:06:45,000 --> 00:06:47,960
understand what we are trying to really do inside of this method.

107
00:06:47,970 --> 00:06:52,220
And TypeScript is going to make sure that we eventually return something of type observable.

108
00:06:52,230 --> 00:06:56,880
So again, just a little bit of help coming from TypeScript here to make sure we put together everything

109
00:06:56,880 --> 00:06:57,900
as expected.

110
00:06:59,200 --> 00:06:59,620
Okay.

111
00:06:59,620 --> 00:07:02,680
So that's a lot of kind of crazy code, a lot of stuff going on.

112
00:07:02,680 --> 00:07:06,850
And then inside this intercept method, it's going to get a little bit crazier.

113
00:07:06,850 --> 00:07:09,010
So I want to kind of cut to the chase here.

114
00:07:09,010 --> 00:07:12,520
I want to tell you exactly what you need to know about this intercept method.

115
00:07:12,520 --> 00:07:15,280
So here's what you really, truly need to know.

116
00:07:16,000 --> 00:07:21,790
If you ever want to run some code before a request goes into a request handler, you're going to place

117
00:07:21,790 --> 00:07:23,320
that code right here.

118
00:07:23,350 --> 00:07:29,590
So I'm going to put in a comment that says run something before a request is handled.

119
00:07:31,000 --> 00:07:32,920
By the request handler.

120
00:07:34,580 --> 00:07:38,020
Right now, I'll do just a simple console log of how about that context thing?

121
00:07:39,470 --> 00:07:44,210
And just to be really clear on that console log, let's also put in a string right before it that says

122
00:07:44,210 --> 00:07:47,090
I'm running before the handler.

123
00:07:49,400 --> 00:07:54,470
Then if we ever want to run some code after we have ran our request handler.

124
00:07:54,470 --> 00:07:58,640
So if we want to somehow mess around with the outgoing response, that's going to involve a little bit

125
00:07:58,640 --> 00:07:59,390
more code down here.

126
00:07:59,400 --> 00:08:01,810
So we're going to put in a rather long statement.

127
00:08:01,820 --> 00:08:06,770
I'm going to say return next dot handle dot pipe.

128
00:08:08,860 --> 00:08:11,230
I'm going to open up the parentheses for pipe right there.

129
00:08:14,210 --> 00:08:16,010
And we're going to add in map.

130
00:08:16,160 --> 00:08:18,110
We're going to call it with a function.

131
00:08:18,910 --> 00:08:24,190
We're going to receive an argument that will refer to as simply data with a type of any.

132
00:08:25,690 --> 00:08:30,790
Then inside of here, I'm going to put in another comment that says run something.

133
00:08:31,520 --> 00:08:32,840
Before the.

134
00:08:33,580 --> 00:08:35,290
Response is sent.

135
00:08:36,080 --> 00:08:36,560
Out.

136
00:08:37,550 --> 00:08:42,130
This data argument right here is the data that we're going to send back in the outgoing response.

137
00:08:42,140 --> 00:08:49,700
So I'm going to do a console log and say I'm running before response is sent out and I'll console log,

138
00:08:49,700 --> 00:08:51,500
whatever that data argument is.

139
00:08:52,120 --> 00:08:53,950
I've also got a very small typo right here.

140
00:08:53,950 --> 00:08:55,030
I put in next right there.

141
00:08:55,030 --> 00:08:57,100
That's supposed to be a reference to our handler.

142
00:08:57,100 --> 00:08:58,450
So total typo on my side.

143
00:08:58,450 --> 00:09:02,200
I think I was saying next at the time I typed that that should be handler like so.

144
00:09:04,080 --> 00:09:04,320
Okay.

145
00:09:04,320 --> 00:09:05,640
So that's kind of the cut to the chase.

146
00:09:05,640 --> 00:09:06,990
That's what you really need to know.

147
00:09:06,990 --> 00:09:11,070
Everything around this and inside this file is kind of just extra fluff.

148
00:09:11,070 --> 00:09:14,250
At the end of the day, what's really important for you to understand is that if you want to do something

149
00:09:14,250 --> 00:09:16,650
before a request is handled, put that code right here.

150
00:09:16,650 --> 00:09:21,270
If you want to do something before the response is sent out with the outgoing data, put your code right

151
00:09:21,270 --> 00:09:21,840
there.

152
00:09:21,840 --> 00:09:23,250
And that's pretty much it.

153
00:09:23,800 --> 00:09:27,720
So now let's save this file and we're going to go back over to our controller.

154
00:09:27,720 --> 00:09:29,580
We're going to make use of our interceptor.

155
00:09:29,580 --> 00:09:32,970
We're going to test it out very quickly and just make sure we get these console logs when we expect

156
00:09:32,970 --> 00:09:37,710
to see them, and then we'll start to worry about doing all that serialization logic and whatnot.

157
00:09:38,210 --> 00:09:40,190
So back inside of our controller.

158
00:09:41,090 --> 00:09:43,070
I'm going to find my users controller.

159
00:09:43,790 --> 00:09:44,990
Inside of that controller.

160
00:09:44,990 --> 00:09:49,760
At the very top, I will import the serialize interceptor.

161
00:09:50,850 --> 00:09:55,830
From up one directory interceptors serialize interceptor.

162
00:09:58,510 --> 00:10:03,070
Then to make use of that interceptor, we're going to go down to where we were making use of the use

163
00:10:03,070 --> 00:10:05,110
interceptors decorator just a moment ago.

164
00:10:05,200 --> 00:10:10,420
I'm going to take out the existing class serializer interceptor and I'm going to replace it with the

165
00:10:10,420 --> 00:10:11,830
one that we just imported.

166
00:10:11,860 --> 00:10:15,310
So I'll say serialize interceptor.

167
00:10:16,170 --> 00:10:17,190
And that's it.

168
00:10:17,340 --> 00:10:18,900
So let's now save this.

169
00:10:18,930 --> 00:10:23,580
We're then going to make a request to our Git request handler right here, and we're going to just make

170
00:10:23,580 --> 00:10:29,550
sure that we see those two console logs also to make sure that we can actually ensure that we are getting

171
00:10:29,550 --> 00:10:33,450
the first console log before the handler and the second one after the handler.

172
00:10:33,630 --> 00:10:37,200
I'm going to also add in a very quick console log into the handler itself.

173
00:10:37,500 --> 00:10:41,160
So I'll put in a console log of handler is running.

174
00:10:41,280 --> 00:10:46,410
So now we should see three console logs come out and they should be in the order of number one.

175
00:10:47,140 --> 00:10:48,370
Number two.

176
00:10:49,050 --> 00:10:50,340
And number three.

177
00:10:50,910 --> 00:10:52,620
Okay, so now time to test this.

178
00:10:52,860 --> 00:10:54,690
I'm going to open up my client.

179
00:10:56,360 --> 00:11:00,080
I'll make a request to our git request handler because that's where we just wired up the interceptor

180
00:11:00,080 --> 00:11:00,770
to.

181
00:11:00,920 --> 00:11:02,150
I'm going to send that off.

182
00:11:02,180 --> 00:11:03,920
Looks like something good happened.

183
00:11:03,950 --> 00:11:08,390
We didn't actually get a response, so maybe stuff is not working quite as expected, but it looks like

184
00:11:08,390 --> 00:11:09,560
our code generally worked.

185
00:11:09,560 --> 00:11:13,670
So I'm now going to go back over to my terminal and just make sure I see those three console logs.

186
00:11:13,880 --> 00:11:20,750
And sure enough, back over at my terminal I see one console log up here with I'm running before the

187
00:11:20,780 --> 00:11:25,820
handler and we've got this execution context thing that has a lot of information about the incoming

188
00:11:25,820 --> 00:11:26,510
request.

189
00:11:28,560 --> 00:11:34,110
Then down here I see that the handler is running and then I see I'm running before the response is sent

190
00:11:34,110 --> 00:11:34,650
out.

191
00:11:34,650 --> 00:11:38,490
And there is the response that we're trying to send back to whoever made the request to us.

192
00:11:39,360 --> 00:11:40,320
Okay, so that's it.

193
00:11:40,320 --> 00:11:42,530
That's the basics of an interceptor.

194
00:11:42,540 --> 00:11:47,370
So now if we ever want to do once again just about anything with the incoming request or the outgoing

195
00:11:47,400 --> 00:11:49,650
response, that's where we would place the code.

196
00:11:49,890 --> 00:11:54,150
So now that we've got the basics of interceptors put together, let's take a quick pause right here.

197
00:11:54,150 --> 00:12:00,000
We're going to come back in the next video and start to actually do all this fancy serialization logic

198
00:12:00,000 --> 00:12:01,140
with a DTO.

199
00:12:01,170 --> 00:12:05,040
All that stuff we spoke about in that diagram before tore it apart.

200
00:12:05,980 --> 00:12:06,370
Yeah.

201
00:12:06,370 --> 00:12:06,970
There we go.

202
00:12:06,970 --> 00:12:07,810
That's what we want.

203
00:12:07,840 --> 00:12:09,370
All this stuff.

204
00:12:09,370 --> 00:12:12,550
Make sure we actually handle serialization of the user entity and so on.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,160 --> 00:00:03,290
We've got the basics of interceptors down.

2
00:00:03,290 --> 00:00:07,160
So now we're going to go back and start to add in some actual serialization logic.

3
00:00:07,280 --> 00:00:11,450
Now, I've been saying that inside of our interceptor, we're going to take the response that's coming

4
00:00:11,450 --> 00:00:12,660
out of our request handler.

5
00:00:12,680 --> 00:00:17,990
We're then going to take that plane object, turn it into our or something like that, turn that into

6
00:00:17,990 --> 00:00:20,000
a plane object, and that goes into Json.

7
00:00:20,030 --> 00:00:22,040
Let's not 100% accurate.

8
00:00:22,040 --> 00:00:25,710
So let me help you understand what's really going to go on inside of our interceptor.

9
00:00:25,730 --> 00:00:30,680
If you go back over to your terminal and take a look at the console log that is coming from that map

10
00:00:30,680 --> 00:00:31,760
statement at the bottom.

11
00:00:32,470 --> 00:00:33,640
So here's mine right here.

12
00:00:33,640 --> 00:00:36,040
It said, I'm running before a response is sent out.

13
00:00:36,040 --> 00:00:37,960
And we see the word user right there.

14
00:00:37,990 --> 00:00:43,150
So that means that we are still working with an instance of the user entity inside of our interceptor.

15
00:00:43,330 --> 00:00:47,620
So putting that in diagram format, here's our interceptor coming into it.

16
00:00:47,650 --> 00:00:50,260
We are getting an instance of the user entity.

17
00:00:50,350 --> 00:00:55,720
Now normally whenever we finish all of our request handlers, whenever we finish all these interceptors,

18
00:00:55,750 --> 00:01:00,520
nest is going to take whatever comes out of all this stuff and turn it into Json for us.

19
00:01:00,730 --> 00:01:06,550
So usually this user entity instance will be turned into Json, but we're going to kind of hijack that

20
00:01:06,550 --> 00:01:06,910
process.

21
00:01:06,910 --> 00:01:09,340
We're going to put in an extra step inside there.

22
00:01:09,490 --> 00:01:12,430
You and I are going to take that user entity instance.

23
00:01:12,460 --> 00:01:14,740
We're going to convert it into a user.

24
00:01:14,740 --> 00:01:21,220
DTO instance, this user DTO instance thing is going to have all of our different serialization rules.

25
00:01:21,250 --> 00:01:26,560
It's going to have the rules that say, okay, I want to show the ID and the email, but not the password

26
00:01:26,560 --> 00:01:27,340
and so on.

27
00:01:28,430 --> 00:01:31,310
We are then going to directly return that instance.

28
00:01:31,640 --> 00:01:33,860
Then Nest is going to take that instance.

29
00:01:33,890 --> 00:01:37,910
It's going to turn it into Json automatically and send that back in the response.

30
00:01:39,120 --> 00:01:41,040
All right, so now we've got the general idea.

31
00:01:41,400 --> 00:01:47,460
Let's go and create a user DTO so this will be very similar to all the DTOs we have already put together.

32
00:01:47,490 --> 00:01:51,210
The only difference is that this time around we're going to list out properties inside of it that we

33
00:01:51,210 --> 00:01:53,370
want to include in outgoing responses.

34
00:01:53,400 --> 00:01:59,640
We are also not going to add in any validation to the DTO because we don't really need to validate outgoing

35
00:01:59,670 --> 00:02:00,240
data.

36
00:02:01,280 --> 00:02:02,450
So to get started.

37
00:02:04,140 --> 00:02:05,460
Back inside my editor.

38
00:02:06,790 --> 00:02:09,670
I'm going to find the Dto's directory inside there.

39
00:02:09,670 --> 00:02:13,990
I will make a new file called simply user dto dot ts.

40
00:02:14,260 --> 00:02:21,010
So you'll notice that I've chosen a very simple name of user dot dto I'm not using something like show

41
00:02:21,040 --> 00:02:23,290
user or view user or anything like that.

42
00:02:23,290 --> 00:02:25,930
You can give a more descriptive name to this if you want.

43
00:02:25,960 --> 00:02:30,430
The reason I'm using a very simple name is that I want this to be understood to all the other developers

44
00:02:30,430 --> 00:02:31,300
inside my app.

45
00:02:31,420 --> 00:02:36,460
This is kind of the default way of displaying or showing a user to the outside world.

46
00:02:37,240 --> 00:02:44,770
So then inside of here we are going to export a class of user DTO and inside of here I'm going to list

47
00:02:44,770 --> 00:02:48,290
out all the different properties that I want to share with the outside world.

48
00:02:48,310 --> 00:02:51,850
So I'm going to put in an ID that's a number.

49
00:02:53,060 --> 00:02:54,380
And an email.

50
00:02:55,190 --> 00:02:56,090
That's a string.

51
00:02:57,550 --> 00:03:01,230
I'm then going to annotate these two properties with some decorators.

52
00:03:01,240 --> 00:03:05,500
We're going to eventually use the information from these decorators to say, Hey, these properties

53
00:03:05,500 --> 00:03:07,150
should go to the outside world.

54
00:03:07,300 --> 00:03:09,580
So at the very top, I'm going to import.

55
00:03:12,130 --> 00:03:13,270
Expose.

56
00:03:14,480 --> 00:03:16,790
From class transformer.

57
00:03:16,910 --> 00:03:22,010
You might recall that back inside of our entity a little bit ago, we used a slightly different directive

58
00:03:22,040 --> 00:03:24,230
called what was it exclude?

59
00:03:24,250 --> 00:03:25,740
I think it was exclude.

60
00:03:25,760 --> 00:03:30,050
So the exclude directive means do not try to share this property.

61
00:03:30,500 --> 00:03:33,620
Expose means specifically do share this property.

62
00:03:33,710 --> 00:03:39,200
So inside of our DTOs, we're going to explicitly say, here are the properties, I want to be sent

63
00:03:39,200 --> 00:03:40,700
out inside of responses.

64
00:03:40,880 --> 00:03:42,920
So I'm going to use that expose decorator.

65
00:03:43,980 --> 00:03:44,730
Right there.

66
00:03:45,490 --> 00:03:46,330
And right there.

67
00:03:47,800 --> 00:03:49,480
Okay, so there's our DTO.

68
00:03:49,720 --> 00:03:53,380
So now all we have to do is back inside of our interceptor.

69
00:03:53,710 --> 00:03:59,530
We're going to import this class definition, and whenever we get in a user entity instance, we're

70
00:03:59,530 --> 00:04:00,850
going to do this conversion process.

71
00:04:00,850 --> 00:04:05,140
We're going to take that user entity, turn it into an instance of this DTO and then return it.

72
00:04:05,170 --> 00:04:08,930
When Nest tries to serialize this object or turn it into Json.

73
00:04:08,950 --> 00:04:14,590
All these decorator rules will be applied and we're going to only expose the ID and the email.

74
00:04:16,020 --> 00:04:16,320
Okay.

75
00:04:16,320 --> 00:04:18,300
So now back inside our interceptor.

76
00:04:19,370 --> 00:04:20,270
At the very top.

77
00:04:20,270 --> 00:04:22,580
So here's the interceptor at the very top.

78
00:04:22,580 --> 00:04:25,580
I'm going to import the DTO we just put together.

79
00:04:26,630 --> 00:04:28,400
So I'm going to import user.

80
00:04:29,510 --> 00:04:30,200
DTO.

81
00:04:31,710 --> 00:04:32,550
From users.

82
00:04:32,550 --> 00:04:34,770
DTO users DTO.

83
00:04:35,700 --> 00:04:37,980
And I think I've probably got a typo in that path right there.

84
00:04:38,010 --> 00:04:39,360
Oh, up one directory.

85
00:04:39,360 --> 00:04:40,170
That's better.

86
00:04:40,470 --> 00:04:41,460
Or it should be better.

87
00:04:41,790 --> 00:04:42,870
Oh, DTOs.

88
00:04:42,900 --> 00:04:43,950
All right, now I've got it.

89
00:04:45,500 --> 00:04:48,350
By wiring up the user dto directly to our interceptor.

90
00:04:48,350 --> 00:04:52,040
That means our interceptor is not going to be able to serve up any other kind of data.

91
00:04:52,040 --> 00:04:54,350
So we won't be able to use this interceptor anywhere else.

92
00:04:54,380 --> 00:04:55,070
Don't sweat it.

93
00:04:55,100 --> 00:04:59,690
We are going to make a quick refactor to the interceptor in just a little bit so that the data that

94
00:04:59,690 --> 00:05:02,330
we use is not hard coded to the interceptor.

95
00:05:03,370 --> 00:05:09,610
So now inside of our intercept method, I no longer need to do anything before we run a request handler.

96
00:05:09,610 --> 00:05:12,840
I only put that console.log in there just to help you understand what was going on.

97
00:05:12,850 --> 00:05:16,810
So I'm going to remove that entire block of code just to keep things a little bit more concise.

98
00:05:18,640 --> 00:05:22,720
Then inside this map statement, I don't really need the comment or console.log in there anymore, so

99
00:05:22,720 --> 00:05:23,950
I'm going to delete that stuff.

100
00:05:24,830 --> 00:05:30,950
And then finally inside this map statement, this is where we are going to take the incoming user entity.

101
00:05:30,980 --> 00:05:33,290
So that's what this data argument right here is.

102
00:05:33,320 --> 00:05:36,860
We're then going to turn it into an instance of the user DTO.

103
00:05:37,100 --> 00:05:40,340
And to do so, we're going to use this method class to plane.

104
00:05:40,340 --> 00:05:41,660
And you know what I just realized?

105
00:05:41,660 --> 00:05:42,260
I mixed that up.

106
00:05:42,260 --> 00:05:43,640
We don't want class to plane.

107
00:05:43,640 --> 00:05:45,830
We actually want plane to class.

108
00:05:45,830 --> 00:05:46,640
That's better.

109
00:05:47,840 --> 00:05:48,830
So plane to class.

110
00:05:48,830 --> 00:05:51,020
My my mistake reversed the two words.

111
00:05:51,740 --> 00:05:56,020
And finally, inside the map statement is where we are going to do this conversion process.

112
00:05:56,030 --> 00:05:57,920
So we are going to return.

113
00:05:58,860 --> 00:06:00,780
Plane to class.

114
00:06:01,830 --> 00:06:06,880
We want to turn data into an instance of user DTO.

115
00:06:06,900 --> 00:06:10,020
So we're going to provide user DTO.

116
00:06:11,080 --> 00:06:12,250
As the first argument.

117
00:06:13,440 --> 00:06:16,620
And then our user entity as the second argument.

118
00:06:17,220 --> 00:06:21,780
We are also going to put in a little options object inside this options object.

119
00:06:21,780 --> 00:06:26,040
I'm going to say exclude extraneous values of true.

120
00:06:26,940 --> 00:06:32,100
This setting right here is the absolute key to making sure everything works as expected by adding this

121
00:06:32,100 --> 00:06:32,740
setting.

122
00:06:32,760 --> 00:06:39,120
It ensures that whenever we have an instance of user DTO and try to turn it into plain Json, it is

123
00:06:39,120 --> 00:06:44,730
only going to share or expose the different properties that are specifically marked with that expose

124
00:06:44,730 --> 00:06:45,570
directive.

125
00:06:46,740 --> 00:06:51,090
So if there are any other properties that are going to be absolutely removed immediately and it's all

126
00:06:51,090 --> 00:06:56,490
thanks to exclude extraneous values, if we do not have that property on there, then any other properties

127
00:06:56,490 --> 00:06:59,850
inside of our user instance would would be shared.

128
00:06:59,880 --> 00:07:01,950
They would be sent inside the outgoing response.

129
00:07:03,310 --> 00:07:04,270
Okay, so that's it.

130
00:07:04,300 --> 00:07:05,960
We've now got everything put together.

131
00:07:05,980 --> 00:07:08,500
So now let's test this out.

132
00:07:08,530 --> 00:07:11,590
I'm going to go back over to my client.

133
00:07:13,760 --> 00:07:15,860
I'm going to send off a git request.

134
00:07:16,730 --> 00:07:20,750
And sure enough, I still do not see the password, which is perfect.

135
00:07:20,750 --> 00:07:22,110
That's exactly what we want.

136
00:07:22,130 --> 00:07:24,810
Just make sure that this is working as expected.

137
00:07:24,830 --> 00:07:30,470
I'm going to try going back into my user DTO and I'm going to delete that entire or how about just comment

138
00:07:30,470 --> 00:07:32,150
out, save ourselves some trouble here.

139
00:07:32,180 --> 00:07:36,560
The entire email property and now if I save this and make a request again.

140
00:07:37,880 --> 00:07:40,790
Now we only get back the perfect.

141
00:07:41,650 --> 00:07:43,870
Okay, So this is definitely a proof of concept.

142
00:07:43,870 --> 00:07:48,970
It shows that we've got something that can kind of do the same serialization logic as the built in nest

143
00:07:49,000 --> 00:07:49,930
solution.

144
00:07:50,140 --> 00:07:52,960
So now we need to make a couple of different improvements.

145
00:07:52,990 --> 00:07:55,510
We need to make sure that our interceptor.

146
00:07:56,220 --> 00:08:02,310
Is not hardcoded to always use user DTO because we might want to use this interceptor in many different

147
00:08:02,310 --> 00:08:07,230
locations inside of our app and we might want to try to format outgoing responses that return not only

148
00:08:07,230 --> 00:08:13,020
users but maybe also photos or messages or blog posts or whatever else our application does.

149
00:08:13,230 --> 00:08:17,610
So one more quick pause and we're going to do a little refactor to our class in just a moment to make

150
00:08:17,610 --> 00:08:19,590
this thing a little bit more reusable.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,130 --> 00:00:04,010
There's one last thing we have to do inside of our interceptor right now.

2
00:00:04,010 --> 00:00:08,930
This thing is hardcoded to always serialize outgoing information using the rules we had set up inside

3
00:00:08,930 --> 00:00:09,950
the user DTO.

4
00:00:09,980 --> 00:00:14,720
This is not very appropriate because if we ever are returning other kinds of resources inside of our

5
00:00:14,720 --> 00:00:20,720
application from a request handler such as, say, photos or messages or blog posts or comments, we

6
00:00:20,720 --> 00:00:25,320
would want to serialize those other resources using a different DTO.

7
00:00:25,340 --> 00:00:29,780
So in other words, we need to be able to customize that argument right there.

8
00:00:30,080 --> 00:00:32,130
So let me show you how we would do this.

9
00:00:32,150 --> 00:00:36,120
First, I want to show you ideally what we would actually do or how we would customize it.

10
00:00:36,140 --> 00:00:42,620
So ideally, inside of a controller such as our users controller, we would be able to import.

11
00:00:43,520 --> 00:00:47,810
A DTO that we want to use for formatting the outgoing response.

12
00:00:47,810 --> 00:00:53,720
So I'm going to import the user DTO inside of here from Dto's user DTO.

13
00:00:54,720 --> 00:01:01,050
And then on a per request handler basis, I would be able to tell this copy of Serialize interceptor

14
00:01:01,050 --> 00:01:05,600
that I wanted to use specifically this DTO for serialization.

15
00:01:05,610 --> 00:01:11,910
So in other words, I would ideally be able to do something like New Serialize interceptor, and I want

16
00:01:11,910 --> 00:01:16,980
this copy of the interceptor to use the user DTO So that's ideal.

17
00:01:16,980 --> 00:01:18,570
That's what I want to be able to do.

18
00:01:18,840 --> 00:01:23,910
So to make this reality, all we have to do is define a constructor on our interceptor.

19
00:01:23,940 --> 00:01:29,070
We're going to receive that DTO, we're going to save it as a property to our class, and then we'll

20
00:01:29,070 --> 00:01:33,090
use that DTO anytime that we need to run our serialization logic.

21
00:01:33,360 --> 00:01:35,400
So let's go back over to our interceptor.

22
00:01:37,000 --> 00:01:37,600
Inside of here.

23
00:01:37,600 --> 00:01:40,330
I'm going to define a constructor.

24
00:01:41,810 --> 00:01:43,130
I'm going to receive.

25
00:01:44,090 --> 00:01:46,250
An argument that I'm going to mark as private.

26
00:01:47,040 --> 00:01:49,920
We'll call it DTO, and right now it's type will be any.

27
00:01:52,320 --> 00:01:58,500
Then rather than using the hard coded user right here, I'm going to call plain to class with the that

28
00:01:58,500 --> 00:02:01,590
was passed in when we created an instance of the interceptor.

29
00:02:01,800 --> 00:02:03,570
So now it's fully customized.

30
00:02:03,780 --> 00:02:07,200
We should no longer have to import user at the top.

31
00:02:08,120 --> 00:02:09,500
And that's pretty much it.

32
00:02:10,070 --> 00:02:11,870
It's not going to save this file.

33
00:02:11,900 --> 00:02:13,400
I'm going to save the controller.

34
00:02:13,400 --> 00:02:15,110
And let's go and do a quick test.

35
00:02:15,530 --> 00:02:20,120
Back inside of my client, I'm going to again try to make a request for a user with a given ID.

36
00:02:20,540 --> 00:02:21,320
And, yep.

37
00:02:21,320 --> 00:02:23,360
Looks like everything works as expected.

38
00:02:23,570 --> 00:02:24,260
Very good.

39
00:02:26,480 --> 00:02:26,810
Okay.

40
00:02:26,810 --> 00:02:29,780
So that is pretty much it for the interceptor itself.

41
00:02:29,780 --> 00:02:36,190
But now we got just one other problem inside of our controller to set up all this interceptor stuff.

42
00:02:36,200 --> 00:02:38,510
It requires a really long line of code.

43
00:02:38,510 --> 00:02:40,550
Like that's a lot of stuff to have to worry about.

44
00:02:40,550 --> 00:02:44,180
In addition, we also have to add in three imports to the top of this file.

45
00:02:44,180 --> 00:02:46,400
We have to import the use interceptors thing.

46
00:02:46,400 --> 00:02:50,030
We have to import our interceptor and the DTO itself.

47
00:02:50,030 --> 00:02:55,700
So the last thing I want to do is try to refactor or create something new to allow us to set up this

48
00:02:55,700 --> 00:02:58,940
interceptor without such a ridiculously long line of code.

49
00:02:58,940 --> 00:03:01,970
So let's take care of that last step in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,160 --> 00:00:04,230
As I mentioned, very last thing we're going to do here.

2
00:00:04,230 --> 00:00:07,350
We're going to try to refactor or create something new.

3
00:00:07,380 --> 00:00:10,800
Who knows what so we don't have to write out such a long line of code.

4
00:00:10,920 --> 00:00:16,320
So to refactor this, we're going to create our own very, very simple custom decorator.

5
00:00:16,350 --> 00:00:20,210
This custom decorator is going to essentially run exactly that.

6
00:00:20,220 --> 00:00:21,960
But we're just going to kind of condense everything down.

7
00:00:21,960 --> 00:00:25,490
So it's going to end up as a very, very small and very convenient call.

8
00:00:25,560 --> 00:00:27,350
So let's go back over to our interceptor.

9
00:00:27,360 --> 00:00:28,830
We're going to create the decorator in there.

10
00:00:28,830 --> 00:00:31,560
And again, this decorator is going to be extremely simple.

11
00:00:32,600 --> 00:00:32,870
Okay.

12
00:00:32,870 --> 00:00:38,570
So back inside the interceptor file at the very top, I'm going to export a new decorator.

13
00:00:38,780 --> 00:00:41,480
Behind the scenes decorators are plain functions.

14
00:00:41,480 --> 00:00:44,900
So what we are really going to export here is, well, just a plain function.

15
00:00:45,050 --> 00:00:47,900
We're going to export a function called serialize.

16
00:00:48,860 --> 00:00:54,260
To this function we're going to pass in the data that we want to use for serialization.

17
00:00:54,260 --> 00:00:59,120
So I'm going to accept an argument called DTO, and again, I'm going to mark its type as any.

18
00:00:59,150 --> 00:01:01,880
We're going to fix up these any types in just a moment, by the way.

19
00:01:02,690 --> 00:01:08,060
So then inside of here, we're going to return exactly that right there.

20
00:01:08,060 --> 00:01:08,600
More or less.

21
00:01:08,600 --> 00:01:10,790
Exactly that, except for user on the end.

22
00:01:10,880 --> 00:01:13,250
So inside of serialize, I'm going to return.

23
00:01:14,340 --> 00:01:16,770
Use interceptors.

24
00:01:17,710 --> 00:01:21,400
We're going to put in a new instance of serialize interceptor.

25
00:01:21,820 --> 00:01:26,680
And as the DTO to be used, we'll pass in the DTO that was passed to the serialize function.

26
00:01:27,730 --> 00:01:28,480
Like so.

27
00:01:29,470 --> 00:01:30,730
And that's our decorator.

28
00:01:31,650 --> 00:01:37,380
Now, if I lost you during any step of this process of all this interceptor stuff, I would expect might

29
00:01:37,380 --> 00:01:38,890
have just lost you right there.

30
00:01:38,910 --> 00:01:42,550
So if you're not super familiar with decorators, don't sweat it that much.

31
00:01:42,570 --> 00:01:47,670
All we're doing here is kind of wrapping up this really long decorator inside of much more simple and

32
00:01:47,670 --> 00:01:48,660
straightforward one.

33
00:01:48,940 --> 00:01:54,000
So now the last thing we need to do is make use of our new custom decorator that is very small and very

34
00:01:54,000 --> 00:01:54,620
convenient.

35
00:01:54,630 --> 00:01:56,520
So let me show you how we would use this.

36
00:01:57,510 --> 00:01:59,940
Back inside our controller at the very top.

37
00:02:01,410 --> 00:02:04,320
I'm no longer going to import the entire serialize interceptor.

38
00:02:04,320 --> 00:02:07,440
We don't need that anymore because we created this new custom decorator.

39
00:02:07,740 --> 00:02:10,320
I'm going to instead import just the decorator.

40
00:02:13,460 --> 00:02:15,320
Then back where we set up our interceptor.

41
00:02:15,320 --> 00:02:22,400
Rather than having this very long line of code, we're going to instead put in just at serialize user

42
00:02:22,430 --> 00:02:24,650
DTO and that's it.

43
00:02:25,140 --> 00:02:30,260
I think you'd agree that this is far more compact and easier to read than the line above it.

44
00:02:31,070 --> 00:02:32,480
It's now one last test.

45
00:02:32,600 --> 00:02:33,800
I'm going to save the file.

46
00:02:33,830 --> 00:02:37,220
Go back over to my client, send the request off.

47
00:02:37,250 --> 00:02:37,660
Yep.

48
00:02:37,670 --> 00:02:38,570
Still works.

49
00:02:40,060 --> 00:02:43,940
Okay, so this looks good, but 1 or 2 outstanding issues.

50
00:02:43,960 --> 00:02:47,770
First off, back inside of our interceptor, we have the type of any all over the place.

51
00:02:47,770 --> 00:02:49,810
I'd like to fix that up to some degree.

52
00:02:49,840 --> 00:02:55,180
I'd also like to make sure that we apply this serialized decorator inside of our controller, not only

53
00:02:55,180 --> 00:02:59,860
to this one single request handler right here, but to probably all of our different request handlers

54
00:02:59,860 --> 00:03:03,630
inside of here, because they're all returning users in one way or another.

55
00:03:03,640 --> 00:03:06,940
So just two last steps here in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,090 --> 00:00:02,450
All right.

2
00:00:02,450 --> 00:00:03,290
Last two steps.

3
00:00:03,290 --> 00:00:07,490
So first thing we'll take care of is making sure that we apply this decorator not just to this one request

4
00:00:07,490 --> 00:00:10,340
handler, but to all the handlers inside this controller.

5
00:00:10,340 --> 00:00:12,410
So there are two ways we can do this.

6
00:00:12,440 --> 00:00:17,270
We can either take serialize, user DTO and just copy paste it around to all these different request

7
00:00:17,270 --> 00:00:18,050
handlers.

8
00:00:18,090 --> 00:00:22,850
Alternatively, we can apply that decorator to the entire controller.

9
00:00:22,850 --> 00:00:27,830
If we apply this decorator to the whole controller, then it's going to intercept all outgoing responses

10
00:00:27,830 --> 00:00:32,210
from all the request handlers inside of here, and it's going to format all the users that get returned

11
00:00:32,210 --> 00:00:33,230
from each of them.

12
00:00:33,230 --> 00:00:35,960
So I think that would be for right now a little bit more convenient.

13
00:00:35,990 --> 00:00:41,840
So I'm going to cut serialize user DTO right there and I'm going to paste it right above the controller.

14
00:00:44,560 --> 00:00:49,360
So if I save this and go back over to my client now, we can make use of just about any of our different

15
00:00:49,360 --> 00:00:50,440
request handlers.

16
00:00:51,580 --> 00:00:55,030
And it should have no password in the response for any of them.

17
00:00:55,150 --> 00:00:56,370
So no password.

18
00:00:56,380 --> 00:00:59,050
I'm going to try to find all users with a given email.

19
00:00:59,380 --> 00:01:00,250
No password.

20
00:01:00,250 --> 00:01:01,090
Very good.

21
00:01:01,720 --> 00:01:03,490
And if I try to update a user.

22
00:01:03,520 --> 00:01:04,270
No password.

23
00:01:04,270 --> 00:01:05,050
Very good.

24
00:01:06,340 --> 00:01:06,630
All right.

25
00:01:06,640 --> 00:01:09,730
Well, I'd say that definitely works now, although it works in this case.

26
00:01:09,730 --> 00:01:14,380
The one thing I do want to mention, remember we originally put together this serialize or the entire

27
00:01:14,380 --> 00:01:19,300
interceptor, for that matter, so that we can have different format responses going out from different

28
00:01:19,300 --> 00:01:20,350
request handlers.

29
00:01:20,350 --> 00:01:24,880
So even though this is convenient in this use case, remember, if you have two different request handlers

30
00:01:24,880 --> 00:01:28,180
and you want to customize the response from each of them, no problem.

31
00:01:28,180 --> 00:01:36,160
You would just move that decorator to say some particular request handler and you can put in 1 or 1

32
00:01:36,160 --> 00:01:38,350
set of serialization rules right there.

33
00:01:38,350 --> 00:01:43,590
And then on your other handler put in the same serialized decorator and then put in a different DTO

34
00:01:43,630 --> 00:01:44,410
right there.

35
00:01:44,410 --> 00:01:48,850
And the other DTO should have a different set of serialization rules inside of it.

36
00:01:50,070 --> 00:01:54,740
Now for right now, because all of our request handlers are all returning users.

37
00:01:54,750 --> 00:01:57,630
I am going to apply the decorator to the entire controller.

38
00:01:57,630 --> 00:02:03,600
But again, if you wanted to have per handler responses or serialization, no problem, just use that

39
00:02:03,600 --> 00:02:05,670
on the individual request handlers.

40
00:02:06,950 --> 00:02:08,240
Okay, so this looks good.

41
00:02:09,450 --> 00:02:10,610
Last little bit of clean up here.

42
00:02:10,610 --> 00:02:12,560
I'm going to delete that big comment right there.

43
00:02:12,560 --> 00:02:13,880
We don't need that anymore.

44
00:02:14,030 --> 00:02:19,250
I'm also going to delete the console log inside of find user and I think that's pretty much it.

45
00:02:19,340 --> 00:02:23,810
The very last thing I would like to take care of besides, oh, maybe cleaning up a couple imports in

46
00:02:23,810 --> 00:02:24,950
here that we're not using.

47
00:02:25,640 --> 00:02:29,990
Is the fact that back inside our interceptor we've got a type annotation of any.

48
00:02:30,020 --> 00:02:32,050
Just all over the place inside of here.

49
00:02:32,060 --> 00:02:37,430
So ideally we do not make use of a type annotation of any as much as possible.

50
00:02:37,430 --> 00:02:42,350
So I would like to at least put in a little bit of effort and see if we can't get a better type description

51
00:02:42,350 --> 00:02:44,450
inside of all these different locations.

52
00:02:44,450 --> 00:02:45,260
So quick pause.

53
00:02:45,260 --> 00:02:48,170
That's going to be the last thing around all this interceptor stuff.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,090 --> 00:00:05,810
As I mentioned, the last thing we're going to take care of is try to get rid of some of these different

2
00:00:05,810 --> 00:00:07,430
any type annotations.

3
00:00:07,670 --> 00:00:11,120
I've got to be honest with you, this is really challenging and really hard.

4
00:00:11,150 --> 00:00:16,850
The primary reason is that applying any kind of type safety around decorators and TypeScript is really

5
00:00:16,850 --> 00:00:17,870
challenging right now.

6
00:00:17,870 --> 00:00:19,290
At the time I'm making this video.

7
00:00:19,310 --> 00:00:23,720
TypeScript doesn't really have great support for making use of types around decorators in general.

8
00:00:24,050 --> 00:00:28,040
In addition, inside of our Intercept method, we've got that map call right there.

9
00:00:28,040 --> 00:00:33,040
And remember the argument called data, That is the outgoing data that is inside of our response.

10
00:00:33,050 --> 00:00:40,040
So it's kind of hard for us to get information from where we apply our decorator right here and somehow

11
00:00:40,040 --> 00:00:45,140
relate that decorator to what is being returned from the method that it is attached to.

12
00:00:45,170 --> 00:00:46,760
That's pretty darn challenging.

13
00:00:46,970 --> 00:00:52,790
So rather than trying to remove every instance of any inside of here, let me set a little bit smaller

14
00:00:52,790 --> 00:00:53,570
target.

15
00:00:53,660 --> 00:00:58,610
Remember, one of the primary goals of types and TypeScript in general is to just capture errors or

16
00:00:58,610 --> 00:01:01,800
help us catch errors before we actually run our code.

17
00:01:01,830 --> 00:01:05,310
So let me show you one very basic error that we can make right now.

18
00:01:05,310 --> 00:01:06,300
Without a doubt.

19
00:01:06,330 --> 00:01:10,610
Back inside my users controller, I'm going to find where we made use of that serialized decorator.

20
00:01:10,650 --> 00:01:14,400
So right now I have my user DTO inside of here.

21
00:01:14,430 --> 00:01:20,570
But if I put in any kind of value inside of here, like anything whatsoever, I don't get any error.

22
00:01:20,580 --> 00:01:25,170
But naturally, if we try to run this, we're going to very quickly get an error when our code actually

23
00:01:25,170 --> 00:01:26,170
gets executed.

24
00:01:26,190 --> 00:01:31,650
So for right now, at least as just a little bit of a fix up, at least some improvement, let's at

25
00:01:31,650 --> 00:01:36,480
least try to make sure that whenever we make use of the serialized decorator, whatever we pass to it

26
00:01:36,480 --> 00:01:38,240
must be a class.

27
00:01:38,250 --> 00:01:40,260
That's the only requirement we're going to put in here.

28
00:01:40,260 --> 00:01:41,940
We're just going to say whatever you put in.

29
00:01:41,970 --> 00:01:43,320
It's got to be a class.

30
00:01:44,830 --> 00:01:51,520
So to enforce that restriction back inside of our interceptor file, right above our decorator of serialize,

31
00:01:51,730 --> 00:01:54,280
I'm going to define a new interface.

32
00:01:54,960 --> 00:01:57,210
Called class constructor.

33
00:01:58,390 --> 00:02:05,140
And then inside there I'm going to put in new dot, dot dot args colon any as an array.

34
00:02:06,400 --> 00:02:08,770
And then put in just a plain object like so.

35
00:02:09,940 --> 00:02:14,200
So this is an interface that means any class, basically any class whatsoever.

36
00:02:14,200 --> 00:02:17,280
As long as you give me a class, I'm going to be happy.

37
00:02:17,290 --> 00:02:21,260
So we can now make use of this interface as the argument for right here.

38
00:02:21,280 --> 00:02:24,220
So I'm going to put in class constructor.

39
00:02:25,350 --> 00:02:28,530
So now if I save this and go back over to my users controller.

40
00:02:28,770 --> 00:02:34,470
Now, if I try to call serialize with, say, a string or a number, I will in fact get an error.

41
00:02:34,470 --> 00:02:40,080
So at least a reasonable, if very small improvement, but at least we're able to capture some very

42
00:02:40,080 --> 00:02:41,010
basic errors.

43
00:02:41,900 --> 00:02:42,200
Okay.

44
00:02:42,200 --> 00:02:46,220
So again, ideally we would be able to put in some more type safety than this, but because we making

45
00:02:46,220 --> 00:02:51,590
use of decorators and TypeScript unfortunately very, very challenging to do as it stands today.

46
00:02:52,290 --> 00:02:57,570
All right, my friends, I think that's pretty much it for all this stuff around interceptors and DTOs

47
00:02:57,570 --> 00:02:59,700
on outgoing responses and whatnot.

48
00:02:59,880 --> 00:03:04,290
So now here at the conclusion of at least this stage, because trust me, we still have a ton to do

49
00:03:04,290 --> 00:03:10,590
inside this application, we have a decorator we can put either on an entire controller or on an individual

50
00:03:10,890 --> 00:03:16,800
route handler, and that decorator will take the outgoing response and serialize it based upon some

51
00:03:16,800 --> 00:03:17,110
rules.

52
00:03:17,110 --> 00:03:22,530
So we put inside of that DTO So I do expect that you will use this a decent amount anytime you want

53
00:03:22,530 --> 00:03:24,600
to format outgoing responses.

54
00:03:25,820 --> 00:03:27,080
Okay, so now we've got this done.

55
00:03:27,080 --> 00:03:28,190
Quick pause right here.

56
00:03:28,190 --> 00:03:30,470
Move on to our next feature in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# section 11: Authentication From Scratch


1
00:00:00,960 --> 00:00:05,740
