At long last, we are all done with the initial take on this users related stuff.

2
00:00:05,760 --> 00:00:10,980
So remember the entire goal of all this stuff around the controller, the service and so on was to just

3
00:00:10,980 --> 00:00:17,040
give you a very solid and straightforward example using type for some very basic operations, like getting

4
00:00:17,040 --> 00:00:20,010
records, updating records, deleting records and so on.

5
00:00:20,280 --> 00:00:24,960
So now that we've got these rather basic examples put together, we're going to get back on track.

6
00:00:24,990 --> 00:00:30,570
Remember, we had originally said that all this user related stuff was going to be tied to a user authentication

7
00:00:30,570 --> 00:00:35,220
system, and we had said that we were going to put together these two root handlers to handle user sign

8
00:00:35,220 --> 00:00:36,540
up and sign in.

9
00:00:36,810 --> 00:00:42,000
Only after we started those root handlers do we start that diversion into doing all these other operations,

10
00:00:42,000 --> 00:00:45,900
like finding a user, updating a user, deleting users and so on.

11
00:00:45,900 --> 00:00:51,000
So all those extra roots, they really were just extra roots that are not necessarily required to get

12
00:00:51,000 --> 00:00:52,950
our application working as we expect.

13
00:00:53,430 --> 00:00:57,960
Now that we've got all that extra stuff complete, we're going to get back on track and start to focus

14
00:00:57,960 --> 00:01:00,000
on authentication in this video.

15
00:01:01,270 --> 00:01:05,650
Okay, So for authentication, let's first begin by taking a look at a couple of different diagrams

16
00:01:05,650 --> 00:01:10,000
and understand the general flow of how we're going to deal with auth inside of our application.

17
00:01:11,560 --> 00:01:11,860
All right.

18
00:01:11,860 --> 00:01:13,300
So here's the overall flow.

19
00:01:13,330 --> 00:01:18,640
I first want you to understand how things are going to kind of ping pong back and forth between a client

20
00:01:18,640 --> 00:01:19,810
and our server.

21
00:01:19,810 --> 00:01:23,980
And we'll start to focus on where we are going to implement some logic around authentication inside

22
00:01:23,980 --> 00:01:24,640
of our server.

23
00:01:24,640 --> 00:01:30,040
So in other words, are we going to play stuff inside of a controller or a service or where, Okay,

24
00:01:30,040 --> 00:01:35,140
so here's the overall flow, not worrying about services or controllers or anything like that just yet.

25
00:01:35,680 --> 00:01:42,460
So at some point in time, some client, be it an client, a mobile device, a web browser or whatever

26
00:01:42,460 --> 00:01:46,770
else is going to make a request to our application, an intent to sign up to our app.

27
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:51,940
So that'll be a post request to auth slash sign up and we'll have an email and password inside there.

28
00:01:52,700 --> 00:01:57,260
Then inside of our server, we're going to take a look at the email that the user is trying to sign

29
00:01:57,260 --> 00:01:58,670
up to our application with.

30
00:01:58,730 --> 00:02:01,890
We're going to make sure that the email is not already in use.

31
00:02:01,910 --> 00:02:07,670
So right now we are allowing users to have the same exact email, but we're going to update that in

32
00:02:07,670 --> 00:02:08,510
just a moment.

33
00:02:08,540 --> 00:02:12,590
We're going to make sure that every single user has a completely unique email address.

34
00:02:13,570 --> 00:02:17,860
If the user is trying to sign up with an email that's already in use, then we're going to immediately

35
00:02:17,860 --> 00:02:18,760
return an error.

36
00:02:18,790 --> 00:02:23,470
Otherwise, we'll go on to our next step, which will be to encrypt the user's password.

37
00:02:23,530 --> 00:02:27,790
So as I've mentioned many times, we are going to store all of our passwords securely inside of our

38
00:02:27,790 --> 00:02:28,540
database.

39
00:02:29,240 --> 00:02:34,010
Once we've encrypted the user's password, we're then going to create a new record inside of our database

40
00:02:34,010 --> 00:02:38,120
saying, here's the user, here's their email, and here's their encrypted password.

41
00:02:39,330 --> 00:02:43,380
Then we're going to send back a response to whoever made that signup request.

42
00:02:43,410 --> 00:02:50,280
We're going to send back a cookie that contains specifically the ID tied to this brand new user record.

43
00:02:50,460 --> 00:02:53,720
If you are not super familiar with cookies and how they work, don't sweat it.

44
00:02:53,730 --> 00:02:58,110
We're going to have a lot of discussion around cookies in just a little bit as soon as we start to take

45
00:02:58,110 --> 00:02:59,010
care of that step.

46
00:02:59,280 --> 00:03:03,720
The only thing you need to know right now around cookies is that a cookie stores a very small amount

47
00:03:03,720 --> 00:03:04,810
of information.

48
00:03:04,830 --> 00:03:10,410
Your browser or mobile device or whatever else is going to automatically manage this cookie for you.

49
00:03:11,460 --> 00:03:16,290
Anytime you make a follow up request from your browser or mobile device or whatever else, you're going

50
00:03:16,290 --> 00:03:20,220
to automatically have that data inside the cookie attached to the request.

51
00:03:20,780 --> 00:03:26,120
So some time later, if we try to make a request back to our application again, maybe a post request

52
00:03:26,120 --> 00:03:30,290
to create a report, the information inside that cookie that we had originally sent back.

53
00:03:30,290 --> 00:03:34,670
So a user with an ID of 50 is going to be attached to that request.

54
00:03:35,060 --> 00:03:37,370
So we're going to receive that request.

55
00:03:37,520 --> 00:03:41,720
And the first thing we're going to do on our server is take a look at the information stored inside

56
00:03:41,720 --> 00:03:42,440
that cookie.

57
00:03:42,470 --> 00:03:46,790
We're going to make sure that the user did not tamper with the information inside there or anything

58
00:03:46,790 --> 00:03:47,630
like that.

59
00:03:48,860 --> 00:03:53,480
Once we've verified that the user did not mess around with the cookie data, we're going to look at

60
00:03:53,480 --> 00:03:58,640
the user ID inside the cookie and we're going to fetch that user out of our database.

61
00:03:58,640 --> 00:04:05,630
So the user with ID 50 and we should be guaranteed that whoever is making the request to us is the user

62
00:04:05,630 --> 00:04:08,960
who originally signed up and was assigned an ID of 50.

63
00:04:09,760 --> 00:04:10,690
So that's pretty much it.

64
00:04:10,690 --> 00:04:12,460
That is our authentication flow.

65
00:04:12,580 --> 00:04:15,820
Now this is an extremely standard authentication flow.

66
00:04:15,850 --> 00:04:21,980
This is how authentication works in a wide, wide majority of applications out there, not just nestjs.

67
00:04:22,000 --> 00:04:26,740
So all the techniques we're going to learn here are directly applicable to other frameworks, languages

68
00:04:26,740 --> 00:04:27,550
and so on.

69
00:04:28,590 --> 00:04:32,130
So now that we understand the flow, want to very quickly discuss where are we going to write all the

70
00:04:32,130 --> 00:04:33,390
code for this?

71
00:04:33,720 --> 00:04:38,670
Well, without a doubt, there are a couple of different steps right around here on our server that's

72
00:04:38,670 --> 00:04:41,940
going to involve working with the user stored inside of our database.

73
00:04:41,970 --> 00:04:47,790
We've already got a lot of infrastructure inside of our project right now around querying, updating,

74
00:04:47,790 --> 00:04:49,110
creating and so on.

75
00:04:49,140 --> 00:04:51,020
Users inside of our database.

76
00:04:51,090 --> 00:04:56,280
So the first thing we should probably decide is whether or not we want to add a whole bunch of authentication

77
00:04:56,280 --> 00:04:59,850
related logic into our existing users service.

78
00:05:00,180 --> 00:05:01,790
So that's option number one.

79
00:05:01,800 --> 00:05:03,930
So let's take a look at a diagram for option number one.

80
00:05:03,930 --> 00:05:08,840
This is what it would be, option number one for implementing a lot of this authentication related stuff.

81
00:05:08,850 --> 00:05:12,150
We can add in some additional methods to our user service.

82
00:05:12,150 --> 00:05:16,170
So we might have methods inside there called something like sign up or sign in.

83
00:05:17,540 --> 00:05:22,850
So presumably sign up would take in an email and a password and it would go through these steps right

84
00:05:22,850 --> 00:05:23,340
here.

85
00:05:23,360 --> 00:05:27,980
Check to see if the email is in use, encrypt the password store record and then send back probably

86
00:05:27,980 --> 00:05:28,970
just the user ID.

87
00:05:30,420 --> 00:05:32,910
When I say send back, I mean send it back to the controller.

88
00:05:32,910 --> 00:05:36,210
And the controller would be in charge of actually setting the cookie and whatnot.

89
00:05:36,960 --> 00:05:38,210
That's option number one.

90
00:05:38,330 --> 00:05:44,060
Now, option number two is a little bit more involved, a little bit more complex in option number two.

91
00:05:44,090 --> 00:05:48,740
We could instead create a brand new service called the authentication service.

92
00:05:48,830 --> 00:05:53,900
And this service would be in charge of implementing a signup method and a sign in method.

93
00:05:54,650 --> 00:05:59,690
These two methods definitely need to eventually reach into the database and find users, create users

94
00:05:59,690 --> 00:06:00,650
and whatnot.

95
00:06:00,680 --> 00:06:06,200
We could have the auth service, make use of our users repository itself, but instead, well, we've

96
00:06:06,200 --> 00:06:12,400
already got a lot of that data fetching or database access logic implemented inside our user service.

97
00:06:12,410 --> 00:06:17,210
So rather than re-implementing all that stuff inside the auth service, we can have the auth service

98
00:06:17,210 --> 00:06:20,120
just rely upon the users service.

99
00:06:20,690 --> 00:06:22,020
So that's option number two.

100
00:06:22,700 --> 00:06:27,130
So now, obviously, the question is, should we go with option number one or option number two?

101
00:06:27,460 --> 00:06:31,500
Well, for a very small application, I would say that option number one makes a lot of sense.

102
00:06:31,510 --> 00:06:35,800
We've got one single location tied to everything related to users.

103
00:06:35,890 --> 00:06:40,450
But as soon as your application starts growing in size, I really recommend going with option number

104
00:06:40,450 --> 00:06:42,400
two, and here's why.

105
00:06:42,430 --> 00:06:47,610
Let's imagine that at some point in time we want to add in some more features tied to users.

106
00:06:47,620 --> 00:06:53,230
So maybe you want to have users have a list of preferences, maybe we want to implement, say, password

107
00:06:53,230 --> 00:06:58,030
reset, maybe we want to add in who knows what other feature tied directly to users.

108
00:06:58,150 --> 00:07:04,780
If we right now add in more functionality around our users directly to the users service, that kind

109
00:07:04,780 --> 00:07:09,820
of implies that all these additional features we might add in in the future will just be additional

110
00:07:09,820 --> 00:07:11,830
methods inside of our user service.

111
00:07:11,830 --> 00:07:17,920
So we might just add in more stuff tied to preferences, more stuff tied to password reset and eventually

112
00:07:17,920 --> 00:07:23,270
more methods tied to whatever other features we have that are directly related to users.

113
00:07:23,480 --> 00:07:29,630
So this is definitely a bad pattern to get into because our user service is just going to grow in size

114
00:07:29,660 --> 00:07:30,590
nonstop.

115
00:07:30,590 --> 00:07:33,200
And it would be kind of hard to eventually say, Hey, you know what?

116
00:07:33,200 --> 00:07:36,650
We should probably split these things out into their own separate services.

117
00:07:37,040 --> 00:07:39,080
So again, for a small application.

118
00:07:39,110 --> 00:07:41,330
Option number one, definitely fine.

119
00:07:41,660 --> 00:07:46,550
But for anything larger or anything that might be larger, I think option number two is a little bit

120
00:07:46,550 --> 00:07:47,150
better.

121
00:07:48,110 --> 00:07:52,580
So with that in mind in this course, because I really want you to or I want to show you how to build

122
00:07:52,580 --> 00:07:55,550
larger applications, we're going to take option number two.

123
00:07:55,580 --> 00:07:59,730
So we are going to create a brand new service called the authentication Service.

124
00:07:59,750 --> 00:08:04,250
It's going to be responsible for everything related to signing up and signing in.

125
00:08:04,520 --> 00:08:09,590
And it's going to rely upon some functionality that we already built into the users service.

126
00:08:10,860 --> 00:08:11,250
Okay.

127
00:08:11,250 --> 00:08:13,260
So I think we've got our path set.

128
00:08:13,290 --> 00:08:14,580
We know what we're doing here.

129
00:08:14,580 --> 00:08:16,220
So let's take a pause right here.

130
00:08:16,230 --> 00:08:20,460
We're going to come back in the next video and get started on the authentication service.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,120 --> 00:00:04,250
Let's get started creating this brand new authentication service.

2
00:00:04,270 --> 00:00:08,980
First thing I want to do is get a quick reminder on what's going on inside of our users module and just

3
00:00:08,980 --> 00:00:13,270
think about the dependence hierarchy between all of our different classes inside there.

4
00:00:13,300 --> 00:00:18,070
We are going to add the authentication service inside this existing module because it is very closely

5
00:00:18,070 --> 00:00:20,890
related to the idea of exactly what a user is.

6
00:00:21,160 --> 00:00:24,320
So inside of here, we're now going to have four different classes.

7
00:00:24,340 --> 00:00:28,030
I just want to think about the hierarchy once again between these different things.

8
00:00:28,510 --> 00:00:34,630
Our controller is still going to need to get access to our users service so that the controller can

9
00:00:34,630 --> 00:00:40,660
attempt to remove users, fetch a user, delete users, update users, all that kind of good stuff.

10
00:00:41,430 --> 00:00:45,960
Our controller is also going to need to make use of this new authentication service for signing up and

11
00:00:45,960 --> 00:00:47,250
signing in users.

12
00:00:47,880 --> 00:00:53,340
The authentication service is going to eventually need to create and query users so that thing is going

13
00:00:53,340 --> 00:00:55,080
to need the user service.

14
00:00:55,740 --> 00:00:59,940
And then the authentication service is not going to reach out to the user's repository directly.

15
00:00:59,940 --> 00:01:04,410
Instead, it's going to make use of functionality we exposed inside of the service.

16
00:01:04,410 --> 00:01:09,270
So the user's repository is still only going to be used directly by that service.

17
00:01:10,970 --> 00:01:11,660
So that's it.

18
00:01:11,660 --> 00:01:12,860
That's our hierarchy.

19
00:01:12,890 --> 00:01:17,780
So this means that when we start to create our authentication service, we need to wire it up to the

20
00:01:17,780 --> 00:01:23,300
dependency injection system in nest, specifically the system inside of our users module.

21
00:01:23,420 --> 00:01:28,130
Remember, that's the only way that we connect different classes together inside of a single module.

22
00:01:28,430 --> 00:01:31,520
So quick reminder on how we make use of dependency injection.

23
00:01:32,590 --> 00:01:35,890
The first thing we have to do during the container start up flow.

24
00:01:35,920 --> 00:01:38,900
We went over this diagram previously when we were talking about dependency injection.

25
00:01:38,920 --> 00:01:40,570
We are going to create our class.

26
00:01:40,600 --> 00:01:47,320
We're going to mark it as injectable and we're also going to add that class to our modules list of providers.

27
00:01:47,350 --> 00:01:53,230
These are steps we already did previously when we originally put together our or our users service.

28
00:01:53,230 --> 00:01:57,820
So let's go back over to our code editor and just get a quick reminder on how we set everything up with

29
00:01:57,820 --> 00:02:01,420
the dependency injection system, because it has been a while since we discussed that.

30
00:02:01,990 --> 00:02:02,260
All right.

31
00:02:02,260 --> 00:02:03,790
So back over inside my editor.

32
00:02:03,790 --> 00:02:05,980
I'm going to find the users service file.

33
00:02:06,190 --> 00:02:09,940
So first, remember, we're going to mark our classes as injectable.

34
00:02:10,090 --> 00:02:14,170
And then if we ever need to get access to some other dependency, we're going to add it into our list

35
00:02:14,170 --> 00:02:15,580
of constructor arguments.

36
00:02:16,160 --> 00:02:20,640
Then finally, we're going to take our service and inside of our module.

37
00:02:20,660 --> 00:02:25,520
So users module right here, we will add that service to our list of providers.

38
00:02:27,350 --> 00:02:29,210
Okay, so now we've got all these reminders.

39
00:02:29,210 --> 00:02:34,050
Let's actually create our authentication service and add it into that list of providers.

40
00:02:34,070 --> 00:02:41,540
So inside of my users directory, I'm going to make another new file called off dot service dot ts.

41
00:02:42,790 --> 00:02:46,450
Then inside of here at the very top, I will immediately import injectable.

42
00:02:47,320 --> 00:02:50,980
From Nestjs Common.

43
00:02:52,170 --> 00:02:55,170
I'll use the injectable decorator and export.

44
00:02:56,080 --> 00:02:59,260
A class called auth service.

45
00:03:00,520 --> 00:03:01,900
Well, then save this file.

46
00:03:01,930 --> 00:03:04,630
Go back over to our users module.

47
00:03:05,450 --> 00:03:07,850
We're going to import that new service at the top.

48
00:03:14,630 --> 00:03:16,760
And add it into our list of providers.

49
00:03:21,820 --> 00:03:22,900
Yeah, that looks good.

50
00:03:24,050 --> 00:03:30,260
So then finally inside of our auth service file, let's immediately wire up the dependency of our authentication

51
00:03:30,260 --> 00:03:32,090
service for this thing to work correctly.

52
00:03:32,090 --> 00:03:34,760
It needs a copy of the user's service.

53
00:03:34,760 --> 00:03:37,160
So we are going to import into this file.

54
00:03:38,910 --> 00:03:41,010
The users service.

55
00:03:41,850 --> 00:03:43,710
From users service.

56
00:03:45,350 --> 00:03:46,760
We'll define our constructor.

57
00:03:47,980 --> 00:03:51,010
But in private users service.

58
00:03:51,010 --> 00:03:55,960
And that's going to be of type users service and that should be it.

59
00:03:57,110 --> 00:04:01,100
And we are going to, of course, have to eventually import this thing and make use of it inside of

60
00:04:01,100 --> 00:04:01,640
our controller.

61
00:04:01,640 --> 00:04:03,200
But we're not going to worry about that just yet.

62
00:04:03,230 --> 00:04:07,790
Let's first put together some basic functionality together around the authentication service and then

63
00:04:07,790 --> 00:04:10,280
we'll test that stuff out inside the controller eventually.

64
00:04:10,400 --> 00:04:15,020
So a quick pause right here and we'll get started on some of our authentication logic in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,940 --> 00:00:03,100
Let's start working on the authentication service.

2
00:00:03,100 --> 00:00:06,320
So inside of here, we're going to eventually have two different methods.

3
00:00:06,340 --> 00:00:10,150
We're going to call one sign up and the other sign in.

4
00:00:10,180 --> 00:00:15,160
I do want to say right away that I'm not super happy with these method names because the method name

5
00:00:15,160 --> 00:00:20,770
of sign in kind of implies that once we call this thing, a user will kind of intrinsically be signed

6
00:00:20,770 --> 00:00:23,140
into our system and that's not actually the case.

7
00:00:23,170 --> 00:00:26,950
Sign in is going to make sure that a user provided the correct email and password.

8
00:00:26,950 --> 00:00:31,280
So it's more like an authentication step as opposed to actually signing the user in.

9
00:00:31,300 --> 00:00:34,420
So if you're not happy with these method names, I kind of agree with you.

10
00:00:34,420 --> 00:00:37,930
I'm not a really big fan of them either, but I think they're good enough for right now.

11
00:00:38,730 --> 00:00:41,250
So we're going to first focus on signup.

12
00:00:41,250 --> 00:00:47,790
So our arguments to sign up are going to be some email supplied by the user and some password also supplied

13
00:00:47,790 --> 00:00:48,630
by the user.

14
00:00:48,840 --> 00:00:53,310
Then inside of here, we're going to go through all the steps we had mentioned right here.

15
00:00:53,460 --> 00:00:58,680
So first thing we have to do is take a look at the email that the user supplied that they're trying

16
00:00:58,680 --> 00:00:59,550
to sign up with.

17
00:00:59,580 --> 00:01:04,710
We're going to use our users service and see if there's already a user inside of our application who

18
00:01:04,710 --> 00:01:05,720
has the same email.

19
00:01:05,730 --> 00:01:07,470
And if they do, we'll throw an error.

20
00:01:07,560 --> 00:01:11,310
We'll then do the encryption store, the record, all that kind of stuff.

21
00:01:12,190 --> 00:01:16,850
So inside of signup, let's first just put in a couple of different comments just to guide ourselves.

22
00:01:16,870 --> 00:01:20,380
So first, see if email is in use.

23
00:01:20,410 --> 00:01:22,180
Second, we're going to.

24
00:01:23,320 --> 00:01:25,510
Hash the user's password.

25
00:01:26,650 --> 00:01:30,160
Will then create a new user and save it.

26
00:01:30,160 --> 00:01:32,530
And then finally, we are going to return.

27
00:01:32,680 --> 00:01:34,030
The user.

28
00:01:34,580 --> 00:01:38,180
As a reminder, this signup method is going to be called from our controller.

29
00:01:38,180 --> 00:01:41,300
So once the controller calls sign up, we're going to send them back.

30
00:01:41,330 --> 00:01:45,380
Either an error if something went wrong or the newly signed up user.

31
00:01:45,410 --> 00:01:50,930
It will then be up to our controller to take that newly signed up user and set up all this cookie stuff.

32
00:01:51,350 --> 00:01:56,000
So inside of the method we are working on, we are really just concerned with these three steps right

33
00:01:56,000 --> 00:01:56,510
here.

34
00:01:57,870 --> 00:01:58,170
Okay.

35
00:01:58,170 --> 00:01:59,640
So first see if the email is in use.

36
00:01:59,640 --> 00:02:00,690
Let's take care of that.

37
00:02:00,840 --> 00:02:05,790
So for this you might recall that inside of our users service, which we've already wired up to this

38
00:02:05,790 --> 00:02:08,400
thing, we had put together a find method.

39
00:02:08,610 --> 00:02:11,310
Let's go take a look at that service very quickly as a reminder.

40
00:02:12,030 --> 00:02:13,740
So here's the user service.

41
00:02:14,620 --> 00:02:15,250
Inside of here.

42
00:02:15,250 --> 00:02:20,260
We've got the find method and that will give us a list of all the different users with a given email.

43
00:02:20,260 --> 00:02:22,640
So we can really just call this find method.

44
00:02:22,660 --> 00:02:28,030
We'll get back an array and if the array has any length to it, that means that someone is already using

45
00:02:28,030 --> 00:02:32,770
this email address and we should probably just immediately throw an error and say sorry, but this email

46
00:02:32,770 --> 00:02:33,550
is taken.

47
00:02:34,960 --> 00:02:36,940
So inside of authentication service.

48
00:02:37,660 --> 00:02:38,890
We're going to use.

49
00:02:40,340 --> 00:02:42,680
Our users service.

50
00:02:42,710 --> 00:02:45,890
We're going to call, find and pass in the email.

51
00:02:46,720 --> 00:02:49,000
When we call find, we're going to get back a promise.

52
00:02:49,000 --> 00:02:50,770
So we need to await that.

53
00:02:52,120 --> 00:02:55,120
And we'll need to mark the enclosing function as async as well.

54
00:02:56,270 --> 00:02:59,480
So then we can check and see if there are any users in this array.

55
00:02:59,480 --> 00:03:03,980
So we'll say if users dot length, did we find any users?

56
00:03:04,010 --> 00:03:06,340
If so, then we'll throw an error right away.

57
00:03:06,350 --> 00:03:12,530
In this case, we will throw a new bad request exception.

58
00:03:13,710 --> 00:03:16,890
And we'll give it a message of email in use.

59
00:03:19,200 --> 00:03:20,400
I'm then going to go up to the top.

60
00:03:20,400 --> 00:03:22,170
We'll make sure we import that exception.

61
00:03:27,520 --> 00:03:28,180
Very good.

62
00:03:29,600 --> 00:03:30,770
Okay, so that's step number one.

63
00:03:30,770 --> 00:03:33,110
So now on to hashing the user's password.

64
00:03:33,140 --> 00:03:37,760
Now, this entire hashing process is going to require just a little bit of backstory and explanation

65
00:03:37,760 --> 00:03:38,990
of what we have to do here.

66
00:03:38,990 --> 00:03:43,400
So let's discuss hashing or encrypting the user's password in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,910 --> 00:00:03,640
Let's get started working on hashing the user's password.

2
00:00:03,670 --> 00:00:06,350
Now, in this video, we're not going to write out any code just yet.

3
00:00:06,370 --> 00:00:10,660
Instead, I'm going to tell you what it means to hash a user's password and how this is going to give

4
00:00:10,660 --> 00:00:13,220
us any level of security inside of our application.

5
00:00:13,240 --> 00:00:17,650
If you are already familiar with password hashing, I would encourage you to skip over this video and

6
00:00:17,650 --> 00:00:18,710
just go to the next one.

7
00:00:18,730 --> 00:00:20,320
This will be a long video.

8
00:00:20,320 --> 00:00:21,080
Very long.

9
00:00:21,100 --> 00:00:21,970
As a matter of fact.

10
00:00:21,970 --> 00:00:25,540
And we're going to be 100% focused just on the password hashing process.

11
00:00:25,540 --> 00:00:26,830
So let's get to it.

12
00:00:27,880 --> 00:00:30,780
Okay, So here's what we're currently doing inside of our database.

13
00:00:30,790 --> 00:00:32,230
We've got our table of users.

14
00:00:32,260 --> 00:00:35,230
We have an ID, email and password for each of them.

15
00:00:35,230 --> 00:00:38,920
And right now, of course, we are storing our passwords as plain text.

16
00:00:38,950 --> 00:00:41,260
This is very, very bad practice.

17
00:00:41,290 --> 00:00:44,500
It's something we never want to do inside of any real application.

18
00:00:44,500 --> 00:00:50,620
If any malicious person ever got a copy of our database, they would have the email and password of

19
00:00:50,620 --> 00:00:52,000
all of our different users.

20
00:00:52,030 --> 00:00:57,670
The reason this is a big deal is not only because some malicious person could sign into our app as another

21
00:00:57,670 --> 00:00:58,270
person.

22
00:00:58,270 --> 00:01:04,270
It's also a very big deal because our users might use the same email password combination as on our

23
00:01:04,270 --> 00:01:10,030
application, as they do on other applications, such as maybe their banking service or their private

24
00:01:10,030 --> 00:01:11,590
email address and so on.

25
00:01:11,710 --> 00:01:17,320
So by anyone getting access to our user's email and password, it is a threat not just to our application

26
00:01:17,320 --> 00:01:21,010
but to our users entire online presence in general.

27
00:01:21,280 --> 00:01:24,850
So we need to make sure that we do not store passwords in plain text.

28
00:01:24,940 --> 00:01:30,440
So the rest of this video is going to be all about how we avoid this very bad practice right here.

29
00:01:31,410 --> 00:01:31,680
Okay.

30
00:01:31,680 --> 00:01:37,230
So to understand how we are going to encrypt our users passwords, the first thing we need to understand

31
00:01:37,260 --> 00:01:42,090
is something that might seem completely unrelated called a hashing function.

32
00:01:42,120 --> 00:01:47,940
This hashing function is going to be the absolute key to everything we do around handling a user's password.

33
00:01:48,360 --> 00:01:53,880
Hashing functions are not only used for password storage, they are used for a lot of different security

34
00:01:53,880 --> 00:01:54,960
applications.

35
00:01:55,080 --> 00:01:58,080
You and I are not going to write out our own hashing function.

36
00:01:58,080 --> 00:02:03,180
Instead, there are a variety of different hashing functions that are already implemented in the Node

37
00:02:03,180 --> 00:02:04,250
standard library.

38
00:02:04,260 --> 00:02:07,230
So we're going to use one of these already built hashing functions.

39
00:02:07,650 --> 00:02:11,670
The goal of a hashing function is to take in some kind of input.

40
00:02:11,700 --> 00:02:13,230
It can be just about any format.

41
00:02:13,230 --> 00:02:15,020
In our case, it's going to be a plain string.

42
00:02:15,030 --> 00:02:22,270
It's then going to calculate a hash based on that input and then spit out a sort of digital fingerprint.

43
00:02:22,290 --> 00:02:25,670
So we're going to get back a string of numbers and letters.

44
00:02:25,680 --> 00:02:30,220
This string of numbers and letters has two very important characteristics around it.

45
00:02:30,340 --> 00:02:37,030
So the first characteristic is that if we ever change the input, we're going to get back a completely

46
00:02:37,030 --> 00:02:38,110
different output.

47
00:02:38,110 --> 00:02:42,700
So, for example, if I have my password, I put it into the hashing function.

48
00:02:42,700 --> 00:02:46,660
I will get back a string of 47720, blah, blah, blah.

49
00:02:46,990 --> 00:02:52,000
If I put in this same string to the hashing function again, I'll get back the exact same output.

50
00:02:52,000 --> 00:02:55,690
So the same string always results in the exact same output.

51
00:02:55,690 --> 00:03:01,120
But if we ever make even the smallest change to that string, if we change one letter, if we capitalize

52
00:03:01,120 --> 00:03:06,460
the letter, if we remove a letter, any change whatsoever, we're going to get a dramatically different

53
00:03:06,490 --> 00:03:07,270
output.

54
00:03:07,450 --> 00:03:10,390
So we're not talking about changing one number or letter in the output.

55
00:03:10,420 --> 00:03:12,790
We're talking about a completely different string.

56
00:03:12,820 --> 00:03:17,650
These are two very real hashes right here for a string of my password and my password.

57
00:03:17,650 --> 00:03:22,570
One, You'll notice that even though we only added on one number between these two strings, the output

58
00:03:22,600 --> 00:03:25,330
hash is dramatically, dramatically different.

59
00:03:26,690 --> 00:03:27,020
Okay.

60
00:03:27,020 --> 00:03:32,480
So now that we kind of understand the basics of a hashing function, one other thing that's a very important

61
00:03:32,480 --> 00:03:33,500
property of them.

62
00:03:34,440 --> 00:03:41,250
Is that once we apply a hashing function to a string and we get some output, we cannot undo that process.

63
00:03:41,340 --> 00:03:48,300
So for example, if I hash the string my password and I get back a string of 47720 something, something,

64
00:03:48,300 --> 00:03:49,000
something.

65
00:03:49,020 --> 00:03:54,750
I cannot take this string, put it back into the hashing function as an input and figure out what the

66
00:03:54,750 --> 00:03:56,400
original input was.

67
00:03:56,430 --> 00:04:01,740
If I tried to hash that value right there, I'll just get back another totally different hash.

68
00:04:01,950 --> 00:04:07,800
So once we have hashed an output or hashed an input and we've got just the output by itself, if we

69
00:04:07,800 --> 00:04:14,760
have only that string, we have no way whatsoever of figuring out what the original input was.

70
00:04:14,940 --> 00:04:17,820
So if I were to zoom on this right here.

71
00:04:19,560 --> 00:04:24,390
If I showed you just this string and asked you what the original input to this was, there would be

72
00:04:24,390 --> 00:04:29,670
no way you could ever answer my question without, of course, already seeing my password over here.

73
00:04:30,340 --> 00:04:34,840
So by just having this string alone, we can't figure out what the original input was.

74
00:04:35,640 --> 00:04:35,910
All right.

75
00:04:35,910 --> 00:04:40,110
So that's the two very important qualities of hashing functions first.

76
00:04:41,400 --> 00:04:45,150
Any very small change to the input will result in a very different completely.

77
00:04:45,490 --> 00:04:47,120
Excuse me, a very different hash.

78
00:04:47,130 --> 00:04:48,300
And second.

79
00:04:48,880 --> 00:04:51,670
Once we have hashed an input, we can't go backwards.

80
00:04:51,670 --> 00:04:54,370
We can't figure out what the original input was.

81
00:04:55,430 --> 00:05:00,260
Now that we've got these basics down, let's take a look at how we're going to handle our password encryption

82
00:05:00,260 --> 00:05:02,120
or hashing process.

83
00:05:03,560 --> 00:05:03,890
All right.

84
00:05:03,890 --> 00:05:09,030
So our entire sign up and sign in flow is going to revolve around these hashing functions.

85
00:05:09,050 --> 00:05:11,240
So here's what we're going to do during sign up.

86
00:05:11,240 --> 00:05:14,810
We'll then take a look at how we're going to handle sign in after that point.

87
00:05:15,080 --> 00:05:18,920
So during our sign up process, a user is going to give us a password.

88
00:05:18,920 --> 00:05:23,350
Right now we would just take that plain password and stick it directly into our database.

89
00:05:23,360 --> 00:05:25,080
So we're not going to do that anymore.

90
00:05:25,100 --> 00:05:30,100
Instead, we're going to first take that password right there and run it through our hashing function.

91
00:05:30,110 --> 00:05:33,380
That's going to give us back a series of numbers and letters.

92
00:05:33,500 --> 00:05:39,890
We're then going to store this inside of our database as the password field instead of the original

93
00:05:39,890 --> 00:05:40,590
password.

94
00:05:40,610 --> 00:05:44,660
So at no point in time are we going to directly store this original password.

95
00:05:44,810 --> 00:05:51,920
So if a user signed up with my password and an email of something like a@a.com, I would expect that

96
00:05:51,920 --> 00:05:57,560
inside of our database we would have a record of some ID with that given email.

97
00:05:58,640 --> 00:06:03,710
And we would see the hash of the user's password as opposed to their real password.

98
00:06:03,890 --> 00:06:08,900
So we are going to store hashed passwords as opposed to the original plain text ones.

99
00:06:10,020 --> 00:06:12,960
Now, this might seem like it doesn't really do a whole lot for us.

100
00:06:12,990 --> 00:06:16,440
The key is understanding the sign in process.

101
00:06:16,440 --> 00:06:18,290
So this is just what occurs during sign up.

102
00:06:18,300 --> 00:06:23,130
Let's take a look at what happens when a user comes back to our application and tries to sign back in

103
00:06:23,130 --> 00:06:24,570
at some point in the future.

104
00:06:25,460 --> 00:06:28,630
So at some point in the future, a user is going to come back to our application.

105
00:06:28,640 --> 00:06:34,850
They're going to fill out our sign in form, and that's going to give our server an email and a password.

106
00:06:35,570 --> 00:06:41,360
As a part of this sign in process, you and I are going to immediately go into our database and find

107
00:06:41,360 --> 00:06:43,610
a user with that given email address.

108
00:06:43,610 --> 00:06:45,410
So AOL.com.

109
00:06:45,710 --> 00:06:50,810
So we'd find this record right here and we would see that hashed password.

110
00:06:51,860 --> 00:06:53,240
So then here's the key.

111
00:06:53,390 --> 00:06:56,510
We're going to take the user's supplied password right here.

112
00:06:56,540 --> 00:06:59,570
We're going to hash it with the same hashing function.

113
00:06:59,570 --> 00:07:04,550
And remember, if we ever run the same hashing function two times or five times or a million times with

114
00:07:04,550 --> 00:07:08,090
the same input, we will always get the exact same output.

115
00:07:08,270 --> 00:07:13,730
So we're going to again hash this supplied password right here and get the output.

116
00:07:15,650 --> 00:07:21,920
Once we've got that output, we'll then compare this hashed output against what was stored inside the

117
00:07:21,920 --> 00:07:22,730
database.

118
00:07:22,970 --> 00:07:28,820
If these two values are identical, that means that the user during the sign in process must have just

119
00:07:28,820 --> 00:07:33,440
given us the same password that they gave us during the original sign up flow.

120
00:07:34,120 --> 00:07:40,220
So the original or the supplied password right here must be identical to the original password supplied.

121
00:07:40,240 --> 00:07:44,650
That means that the user has given us some valid credentials and we would now consider them to be signed

122
00:07:44,650 --> 00:07:45,160
in.

123
00:07:45,560 --> 00:07:48,370
So now what would happen if a user gave us an invalid password?

124
00:07:48,400 --> 00:07:52,240
Well, if a user gave us a totally different password right here, like maybe my.

125
00:07:52,270 --> 00:07:53,680
Or how about Apple?

126
00:07:53,710 --> 00:07:54,900
Maybe that's my password.

127
00:07:54,910 --> 00:07:58,760
When I run that through the hashing algorithm, I'm going to get a totally different hash.

128
00:07:58,780 --> 00:08:00,100
So something like that.

129
00:08:00,400 --> 00:08:04,020
Once again, we will compare that value against the stored hash.

130
00:08:04,030 --> 00:08:05,890
In this case, they are not the same.

131
00:08:05,890 --> 00:08:11,440
So that means that the password supplied right here must not be the same as the original.

132
00:08:11,440 --> 00:08:14,920
And we have just been given an invalid email and password.

133
00:08:16,030 --> 00:08:17,020
Okay, so that's it.

134
00:08:17,020 --> 00:08:20,110
That is the entire sign up and sign in process.

135
00:08:20,140 --> 00:08:26,350
This allows us to store hashed passwords inside of our database as opposed to passwords in plain text.

136
00:08:26,620 --> 00:08:28,750
Now, I wish I could say that was the entire flow.

137
00:08:28,780 --> 00:08:30,910
I wish I could say that's all we have to write.

138
00:08:30,910 --> 00:08:35,230
But unfortunately, there is a little weakness to this entire technique.

139
00:08:36,470 --> 00:08:42,050
Remember, turns out that people across the Internet, we all tend to use the exact same passwords everywhere.

140
00:08:42,050 --> 00:08:48,170
So chances are someone, maybe you or someone you know has used the password password at some point

141
00:08:48,170 --> 00:08:53,090
in their life or something like I think a very popular password across the world is something like,

142
00:08:53,090 --> 00:08:53,830
I love you.

143
00:08:53,840 --> 00:08:56,870
There are a couple passwords out there that are just extremely common.

144
00:08:57,020 --> 00:09:04,190
So malicious people can use that fact to still kind of attack our database and figure out what a person's

145
00:09:04,190 --> 00:09:07,810
password is, even though we are storing it as a hashed password.

146
00:09:07,820 --> 00:09:10,280
This is referred to as a rainbow attack.

147
00:09:10,280 --> 00:09:14,300
So let's figure out what a rainbow attack is, because this is something that we absolutely have to

148
00:09:14,300 --> 00:09:16,100
address inside of our application as well.

149
00:09:16,970 --> 00:09:22,940
The idea behind a rainbow table attack is that some malicious person out there is going to get a list

150
00:09:22,940 --> 00:09:26,530
of all the different most popular passwords across the world.

151
00:09:26,540 --> 00:09:28,070
So they've got some list.

152
00:09:28,100 --> 00:09:33,680
They're then going to run through this list of maybe hundreds or thousands or even millions of different

153
00:09:33,680 --> 00:09:35,030
very common passwords.

154
00:09:35,620 --> 00:09:41,560
And they're going to calculate the hash of every one of these very common passwords ahead of time so

155
00:09:41,560 --> 00:09:42,970
they can do this any day of the week.

156
00:09:42,970 --> 00:09:43,990
They can do it right now.

157
00:09:43,990 --> 00:09:48,910
And once they've done this calculation and they've got these password and hash pairs, they can just

158
00:09:48,910 --> 00:09:49,990
store this table.

159
00:09:50,020 --> 00:09:52,180
They never have to run this calculation again.

160
00:09:53,510 --> 00:09:59,540
So then if this malicious user ever got access to our database in some way, they could take a look

161
00:09:59,540 --> 00:10:00,890
at our hashed password.

162
00:10:00,920 --> 00:10:07,130
They could then compare our stored hashed password right here against all these pre-calculated hashes

163
00:10:07,130 --> 00:10:08,850
that they ran ahead of time.

164
00:10:08,870 --> 00:10:14,780
So they would look at this user's password, see it's 0089, and they would compare it against the list

165
00:10:14,780 --> 00:10:17,630
of all hashes they computed ahead of time.

166
00:10:17,630 --> 00:10:19,620
So they'd say, Are these two identical?

167
00:10:19,640 --> 00:10:20,240
Nope.

168
00:10:20,330 --> 00:10:21,230
How about those two?

169
00:10:21,260 --> 00:10:21,860
Nope.

170
00:10:21,890 --> 00:10:23,120
How about these two?

171
00:10:23,300 --> 00:10:24,650
Oh, hey, it's a match.

172
00:10:24,680 --> 00:10:31,970
Well, because these hashes are identical, that means that this person's password must be my password.

173
00:10:32,330 --> 00:10:37,940
So this malicious person, by making use of this rainbow table attack, would know that our user has

174
00:10:37,940 --> 00:10:40,100
an email of a a.com.

175
00:10:40,100 --> 00:10:43,130
And their original password was my password.

176
00:10:43,370 --> 00:10:49,370
So this malicious person could then take this email and that password and try to go to log in to a variety

177
00:10:49,370 --> 00:10:53,610
of maybe different bank websites or different personal email addresses.

178
00:10:53,610 --> 00:10:59,280
And basically we're back to the original scenario where this malicious person now has our user's email

179
00:10:59,280 --> 00:11:01,410
and plain text password.

180
00:11:01,920 --> 00:11:05,400
So we have to figure out a way to deal with these rainbow table attacks.

181
00:11:05,460 --> 00:11:08,150
Fortunately, it's pretty easy and straightforward to deal.

182
00:11:08,160 --> 00:11:09,240
Well, don't want to say straightforward.

183
00:11:09,240 --> 00:11:12,330
It's definitely not straightforward, but it is easy to deal with.

184
00:11:12,360 --> 00:11:15,790
We don't have to write a lot of code to prevent this kind of attack.

185
00:11:15,810 --> 00:11:17,310
So here's what we're going to do.

186
00:11:18,760 --> 00:11:24,020
We're going to make our signup process and our sign in process just a little bit more complicated.

187
00:11:24,040 --> 00:11:29,950
So we're going to say that during the sign up process, a user is going to once again give us a password

188
00:11:29,950 --> 00:11:31,090
that they want to use.

189
00:11:31,880 --> 00:11:38,060
You and I simultaneously inside of all of our sign up related code for this particular person who is

190
00:11:38,060 --> 00:11:42,890
signing up to our application, we are going to generate a random series of numbers and letters, and

191
00:11:42,890 --> 00:11:45,100
we're going to refer to this as a salt.

192
00:11:45,110 --> 00:11:49,730
So maybe our salt would be something like A1D01.

193
00:11:49,850 --> 00:11:56,210
So again, randomly generated the length of the salt can be as long or technically as short as we want.

194
00:11:56,240 --> 00:11:57,980
Ideally it would be longer.

195
00:11:59,430 --> 00:12:05,310
We're then going to take the user's password and the salt and join them together into one single string.

196
00:12:05,550 --> 00:12:08,700
So we're going to join that, join that into one string.

197
00:12:09,060 --> 00:12:13,140
So we'd end up with something like my password A101.

198
00:12:14,120 --> 00:12:14,570
Oops.

199
00:12:14,720 --> 00:12:15,410
There we go.

200
00:12:15,950 --> 00:12:20,900
We're then going to run that join string through our hashing function and we're going to get some output

201
00:12:20,900 --> 00:12:21,580
down here.

202
00:12:21,590 --> 00:12:26,570
Maybe something like, I don't know, 010066D.

203
00:12:28,360 --> 00:12:32,950
So that is the hashed output of joining together the password and the salt.

204
00:12:33,220 --> 00:12:34,330
Now, we're not done yet.

205
00:12:34,420 --> 00:12:39,700
We're now going to take that output and join it together with the original salt.

206
00:12:41,210 --> 00:12:47,030
We would usually put a period or some kind of separating character between that output right there and

207
00:12:47,030 --> 00:12:50,960
the salt so we can tell which string is which or where one begins and the other begins.

208
00:12:51,110 --> 00:12:59,210
So we might end up with something like 010066D and then maybe a period or slash or two slashes, any

209
00:12:59,210 --> 00:13:01,340
kind of separating character we want to put in here.

210
00:13:01,340 --> 00:13:03,230
And then we would put in the salt.

211
00:13:04,220 --> 00:13:08,750
We would refer to this as the hashed and salted password.

212
00:13:09,450 --> 00:13:12,970
And this value is what we are going to store inside of our database.

213
00:13:12,990 --> 00:13:15,690
So we take that value and put it right there.

214
00:13:17,720 --> 00:13:19,550
So again, that's the signup flow.

215
00:13:19,850 --> 00:13:21,780
So how does the salt really help us?

216
00:13:21,800 --> 00:13:24,210
Well, let's go take a look at the sign in flow.

217
00:13:24,230 --> 00:13:28,640
Understand how we make use of the salt and then eventually we'll see how this solves problems around

218
00:13:28,640 --> 00:13:30,200
that rainbow table attack.

219
00:13:30,290 --> 00:13:32,420
So now the sign in flow.

220
00:13:33,410 --> 00:13:33,590
Okay.

221
00:13:33,590 --> 00:13:40,130
So later on during sign in, a user is going to give us some email and a password as a first step during

222
00:13:40,130 --> 00:13:41,330
the sign in process.

223
00:13:41,330 --> 00:13:43,430
We're not going to generate a salt randomly.

224
00:13:43,430 --> 00:13:47,270
Instead, we're going to take a look at the email address that the user just supplied to us.

225
00:13:47,300 --> 00:13:52,430
We're going to go into our database and find a user inside the database with that email.

226
00:13:52,430 --> 00:13:54,290
So we would find this right here.

227
00:13:54,530 --> 00:14:00,200
Remember, we had stored the hash and then a period and then the salt right after it.

228
00:14:00,380 --> 00:14:05,600
So we're going to find this user, we're going to pull out that string right there and separate it into

229
00:14:05,600 --> 00:14:06,860
its component parts.

230
00:14:06,890 --> 00:14:09,950
We're going to separate it into the hash.

231
00:14:13,410 --> 00:14:14,880
And the salt.

232
00:14:15,360 --> 00:14:17,370
I'll fix up that formatting here.

233
00:14:19,810 --> 00:14:20,530
A little bit better.

234
00:14:20,530 --> 00:14:20,910
Come on.

235
00:14:20,920 --> 00:14:21,670
There we go.

236
00:14:22,150 --> 00:14:26,170
So remember, we stored those two things together in one string with a separator character inside them

237
00:14:26,170 --> 00:14:27,280
or separating the two.

238
00:14:27,310 --> 00:14:29,870
So we know which one is the salt and we know which one is the hash.

239
00:14:29,890 --> 00:14:31,650
So we're going to separate them into the two.

240
00:14:31,660 --> 00:14:35,320
And now we know that our original salt was that string right there.

241
00:14:35,500 --> 00:14:40,630
So we're going to take that salt, put it right here, and then we're going to repeat the exact same

242
00:14:40,630 --> 00:14:42,340
process we had done previously.

243
00:14:42,370 --> 00:14:47,890
So we're going to take the user's password and the stored salt that we had inside of our database.

244
00:14:47,920 --> 00:14:49,960
We're going to join the two together.

245
00:14:52,670 --> 00:14:54,980
And we would end up with my password.

246
00:14:55,100 --> 00:14:57,140
Ab2 K334.

247
00:14:57,380 --> 00:15:06,260
We would then run that through our hashing function and we would get our output of maybe A1D01066 or

248
00:15:06,260 --> 00:15:07,070
whatever else.

249
00:15:08,240 --> 00:15:10,490
So that is our hash right there.

250
00:15:10,490 --> 00:15:13,940
So the A1D01066.

251
00:15:14,150 --> 00:15:16,910
So now we're going to go back to the same user right here.

252
00:15:16,910 --> 00:15:21,530
And remember, we have the original hash right there and we're going to compare it, the hash we just

253
00:15:21,530 --> 00:15:24,470
calculated with the hash we have stored inside of our database.

254
00:15:24,470 --> 00:15:29,000
And once again, if that hash right there and the one we just calculated are identical, that means

255
00:15:29,000 --> 00:15:34,070
the user must have supplied the correct password if they gave us a different password.

256
00:15:34,070 --> 00:15:40,640
So if they gave us something like Asdf, then our input to the hashing function would have been Asdf

257
00:15:40,640 --> 00:15:41,810
and then the salt.

258
00:15:41,840 --> 00:15:46,250
We would have gotten a very different output from the hashing function, would have had a very different

259
00:15:46,280 --> 00:15:47,000
hash.

260
00:15:47,000 --> 00:15:49,130
And so these two would not be the same.

261
00:15:49,130 --> 00:15:52,550
And that means that the user must have given us the incorrect password.

262
00:15:52,910 --> 00:15:56,480
So at the end of the day, adding in the salt, just about totally identical.

263
00:15:56,510 --> 00:16:02,510
The only difference is that during the sign up process we are generating that salt and storing it inside

264
00:16:02,510 --> 00:16:03,530
of our database.

265
00:16:03,920 --> 00:16:09,690
And then during the sign in process we're going to go into the database, find that salt and use it

266
00:16:09,690 --> 00:16:13,680
inside of our hash, hash hash, the password hashing process.

267
00:16:14,630 --> 00:16:15,890
Now, I know that's confusing.

268
00:16:16,820 --> 00:16:20,970
Last thing I want to say here is why this solves the rainbow table attack.

269
00:16:21,010 --> 00:16:27,090
Remember now the output of our hashing function right here is not just the user's plain password.

270
00:16:27,120 --> 00:16:31,770
Now it is the user's password, plus a random series of numbers and letters.

271
00:16:31,950 --> 00:16:37,770
So that means that enable or in order to do a rainbow table attack, which all revolves around this

272
00:16:37,770 --> 00:16:43,890
idea of kind of pre calculating these different hashes ahead of time someone to put together this rainbow

273
00:16:43,890 --> 00:16:49,500
table attack would now have to do a series of different entries where they take a password and then

274
00:16:49,500 --> 00:16:50,730
they guess a salt.

275
00:16:50,730 --> 00:16:56,370
So they would say if I have a password of this and a salt of this, I would end up with a hash that

276
00:16:56,370 --> 00:16:57,300
looks like this.

277
00:16:57,920 --> 00:17:02,870
This malicious user could then compare that hash against the one we have stored inside of our database.

278
00:17:02,870 --> 00:17:08,060
But the key thing here is that the user or this malicious person would have to regenerate this entire

279
00:17:08,060 --> 00:17:13,430
rainbow table with a different salt for every different possible salt there is in the world out there.

280
00:17:13,430 --> 00:17:17,450
And there are millions or billions or trillions of different combinations of numbers and letters we

281
00:17:17,450 --> 00:17:18,500
can use as our salt.

282
00:17:18,530 --> 00:17:23,420
They would have to generate a totally different rainbow table so they'd have one rainbow table with

283
00:17:23,420 --> 00:17:24,200
this salt.

284
00:17:24,230 --> 00:17:26,960
They would then have to generate another one.

285
00:17:28,220 --> 00:17:32,360
So here's the second one they generate with a slightly different salt.

286
00:17:32,360 --> 00:17:37,640
So maybe this time they use all these numbers and letters with a one at the end and that would give

287
00:17:37,730 --> 00:17:40,520
them a totally different set of hashes over here.

288
00:17:40,730 --> 00:17:46,730
So by adding on the salt, it turns this rainbow table attack into just way too much work because there's

289
00:17:46,730 --> 00:17:52,400
no way that any malicious user would ever want to make a number of different rainbow tables that have

290
00:17:52,400 --> 00:17:56,870
every possible different password out there with every different possible salt combination.

291
00:17:56,870 --> 00:17:58,940
It is just plain not feasible.

292
00:17:59,560 --> 00:18:02,680
So that's how we address issues with rainbow table attacks.

293
00:18:03,470 --> 00:18:04,010
All right.

294
00:18:04,010 --> 00:18:04,820
So that's it.

295
00:18:04,820 --> 00:18:05,730
That's what's going on here.

296
00:18:05,750 --> 00:18:10,940
Now, if in this video you got lost at any point in time, don't sweat it, because I guarantee you,

297
00:18:10,940 --> 00:18:15,530
as soon as we start writing the code out for this, everything is going to become crystal clear because

298
00:18:15,530 --> 00:18:20,240
it turns out the code ends up being pretty darn straightforward, especially some of the stuff around

299
00:18:20,240 --> 00:18:23,450
comparing the salt and generating the salt and storing that stuff.

300
00:18:23,450 --> 00:18:26,960
All that stuff is going to be pretty straightforward as soon as we start writing the code.

301
00:18:27,200 --> 00:18:29,540
So at this point in time, let's take a pause right here.

302
00:18:29,540 --> 00:18:30,980
We're going to come back in the next video.

303
00:18:30,980 --> 00:18:36,560
We're going to implement all this stuff manually ourselves with a one exception of that hashing function.

304
00:18:36,560 --> 00:18:40,880
Again, remember, in general, we make use of hashing functions that are already built for us.

305
00:18:40,880 --> 00:18:44,720
And the Node Standard library has a collection of different hashing functions we can use.

306
00:18:45,440 --> 00:18:48,290
Okay, So let's start our implementation in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,970 --> 00:00:06,010
We just had a very long video discussing how to hash and salt passwords and then store them inside of

2
00:00:06,010 --> 00:00:06,840
our database.

3
00:00:06,850 --> 00:00:11,710
So now back inside of our auth service, we're going to start to put together this hash, the user password

4
00:00:11,710 --> 00:00:13,180
step to get started.

5
00:00:13,180 --> 00:00:16,870
I'm going to put in three separate comments just to describe exactly what we're going to do inside of

6
00:00:16,870 --> 00:00:17,350
here.

7
00:00:17,590 --> 00:00:22,360
So very quick reminder, by the way, if you watch the last video, remember there is a very big difference

8
00:00:22,360 --> 00:00:25,630
between the signup process and the sign in process.

9
00:00:25,630 --> 00:00:28,870
Right now, we are just focused on the sign up process.

10
00:00:28,870 --> 00:00:34,600
So we need to generate a salt hash, the salt along with the password, and then store both the hashed

11
00:00:34,600 --> 00:00:36,880
password and the salt inside of our database.

12
00:00:36,880 --> 00:00:38,830
So that's what we're going to do right here.

13
00:00:38,830 --> 00:00:40,900
So a couple comments just to guide us.

14
00:00:40,900 --> 00:00:43,960
First, we're going to generate a salt.

15
00:00:45,360 --> 00:00:50,040
We'll then hash the salt and the password together.

16
00:00:52,290 --> 00:00:58,290
And then finally, we're going to join the hashed result and the salt together.

17
00:00:59,520 --> 00:01:03,870
And then the result of that right there, that is what we're going to actually store inside of our database.

18
00:01:05,670 --> 00:01:09,750
Now out of these three steps, we're going to implement these using some different functions out of

19
00:01:09,750 --> 00:01:11,190
the Node standard library.

20
00:01:11,190 --> 00:01:12,840
So let's go up to the very top of this file.

21
00:01:12,840 --> 00:01:16,350
We're going to import these helper functions and then we'll make use of them.

22
00:01:16,470 --> 00:01:22,080
So up here at the very top, I'm going to add in an import statement for random bytes.

23
00:01:23,260 --> 00:01:26,920
And script from the crypto package.

24
00:01:27,250 --> 00:01:29,510
So crypto is a part of the node standard library.

25
00:01:29,530 --> 00:01:31,930
Random bytes we're going to use to generate our salt.

26
00:01:31,960 --> 00:01:35,470
It's going to give us just a random string of numbers and letters.

27
00:01:36,140 --> 00:01:39,020
And then script is our actual hashing function.

28
00:01:39,020 --> 00:01:43,730
So that's the thing that's going to do the heavy lifting, the actual hashing of the input password.

29
00:01:45,150 --> 00:01:46,350
Before we make use of script.

30
00:01:46,350 --> 00:01:48,630
However, one little gotcha here.

31
00:01:48,660 --> 00:01:54,930
This script function is asynchronous in nature, but rather than giving us back a promise to deal with

32
00:01:54,930 --> 00:02:00,330
that asynchronicity, we instead have to make use of callbacks with this plain version of script.

33
00:02:00,750 --> 00:02:05,280
Remember that in general you and I as modern JavaScript and TypeScript developers, we don't really

34
00:02:05,280 --> 00:02:06,990
like callbacks terribly much.

35
00:02:06,990 --> 00:02:11,610
So rather than using this plain version of script, we're going to wrap it up a little bit.

36
00:02:11,640 --> 00:02:16,680
We're going to make sure that script instead gives us back a password rather or not a password, but

37
00:02:16,680 --> 00:02:19,920
a promise rather than making us use a callback.

38
00:02:20,010 --> 00:02:21,900
So one extra little step here.

39
00:02:22,020 --> 00:02:28,800
Right after that import, we're going to add in an import for promisify from util.

40
00:02:29,770 --> 00:02:35,380
This is another package built into the node Standard library from SFI is a function that will take in

41
00:02:35,380 --> 00:02:40,420
a function that makes use of callbacks, and it's going to give us back a version of that exact same

42
00:02:40,420 --> 00:02:43,110
function that instead makes use of promises.

43
00:02:43,120 --> 00:02:48,910
So we're just using Promisify to make a script a little bit palatable to our very sensitive taste here

44
00:02:48,910 --> 00:02:51,880
and making sure that we can use a promise rather than a callback.

45
00:02:53,660 --> 00:02:55,280
Okay, So now one other step.

46
00:02:56,240 --> 00:03:01,550
I'm going to rename the script import to underscore script.

47
00:03:01,550 --> 00:03:04,280
So I'm going to say script as underscore script.

48
00:03:04,370 --> 00:03:07,040
The only reason I'm doing this is for the next line of code we're going to write.

49
00:03:07,040 --> 00:03:13,310
So right underneath those imports, we're going to say script is promisify underscore script.

50
00:03:13,580 --> 00:03:19,970
So the only goal that rename right there is so that inside this file we're going to reference our very

51
00:03:19,970 --> 00:03:23,960
good promise based version of script as a variable called script.

52
00:03:24,050 --> 00:03:28,250
Because otherwise if we just left this thing named as script, we'd have to pick out a different name

53
00:03:28,250 --> 00:03:29,510
for this variable right here.

54
00:03:29,660 --> 00:03:30,560
I don't really want to do that.

55
00:03:30,560 --> 00:03:33,200
I want to just call it function script and have it be done with.

56
00:03:34,650 --> 00:03:36,870
Okay, so now one more thing around this step.

57
00:03:36,900 --> 00:03:42,030
Unfortunately, we are making use of TypeScript here in TypeScript sometimes gets a little bit confused

58
00:03:42,030 --> 00:03:43,920
whenever we make use of promisify.

59
00:03:43,950 --> 00:03:47,730
Usually it's very good about understanding what's going on, but in this case, TypeScript is just going

60
00:03:47,730 --> 00:03:50,730
to have no idea what this line of code really does.

61
00:03:50,760 --> 00:03:54,790
It's not really going have any idea what is going to be returned from calling script.

62
00:03:54,810 --> 00:03:59,550
So very shortly we're going to have to give TypeScript a little bit of help and understand what calling

63
00:03:59,550 --> 00:04:03,780
this thing is going to give us, give us back and return, what kind of value it returns to us.

64
00:04:05,500 --> 00:04:05,830
Okay.

65
00:04:05,830 --> 00:04:11,140
So now down where we just put those comments in, let's put together these three different steps.

66
00:04:11,170 --> 00:04:20,500
So first off, we're going to generate our salt with salt is random bytes eight and then to string hex

67
00:04:20,500 --> 00:04:21,310
like so.

68
00:04:22,890 --> 00:04:23,700
The random bites.

69
00:04:23,700 --> 00:04:25,440
We're putting in a number right here.

70
00:04:25,740 --> 00:04:30,000
This function is going to return back to us, something called a buffer, which is very similar to an

71
00:04:30,000 --> 00:04:30,570
array.

72
00:04:30,600 --> 00:04:34,080
The difference is that a buffer holds some raw data inside of it.

73
00:04:34,080 --> 00:04:38,950
So raw binary like ones and zeros, we don't really want to work with ones and zeros.

74
00:04:38,970 --> 00:04:42,380
Instead, we want to work with a string of random numbers and letters.

75
00:04:42,390 --> 00:04:47,940
So we're going to take all those random ones and zeros and turn it into a hexadecimal string, which

76
00:04:47,940 --> 00:04:51,030
basically just means a bunch of random numbers and letters.

77
00:04:52,640 --> 00:04:57,110
The eight right here means that our buffer is going to have eight bytes worth of data inside of it.

78
00:04:57,140 --> 00:05:02,840
Every one byte of data turns into two characters when we convert it to Hex.

79
00:05:02,840 --> 00:05:06,590
So our salt is going to be a 16 character long string.

80
00:05:06,590 --> 00:05:11,300
So in other words, this string right here, whatever, we randomly generate 16 characters inside of

81
00:05:11,300 --> 00:05:13,880
it or numbers, characters or numbers.

82
00:05:15,950 --> 00:05:16,250
All right.

83
00:05:16,280 --> 00:05:18,950
Next up, we're going to hash the salt and the password together.

84
00:05:18,950 --> 00:05:21,320
So this is where we're going to use that script function.

85
00:05:21,980 --> 00:05:27,020
So we'll say hash is a wait script.

86
00:05:27,140 --> 00:05:29,690
We're going to put in our password as the first argument.

87
00:05:29,690 --> 00:05:33,560
And remember, this password is an argument to our function.

88
00:05:35,910 --> 00:05:38,130
The second argument will be our salt.

89
00:05:38,550 --> 00:05:42,030
And then finally, 32 as the third argument.

90
00:05:43,080 --> 00:05:46,830
So when we call script, we're going to get back some kind of hash result.

91
00:05:46,860 --> 00:05:51,760
We can customize how many characters are, how long that hash is with this third argument.

92
00:05:51,780 --> 00:05:56,370
So 32 means just give us back 32 characters or 32 bytes worth.

93
00:05:56,370 --> 00:05:59,880
I forget exactly which one it is as the output of script.

94
00:06:00,270 --> 00:06:01,770
32 is totally fine.

95
00:06:01,770 --> 00:06:05,310
You can go longer, you can go a little bit shorter, but no one's going to question you if you have

96
00:06:05,310 --> 00:06:06,840
32 characters for this.

97
00:06:07,860 --> 00:06:12,270
So now here's the issue around TypeScript and that Promisify function that I mentioned just a moment

98
00:06:12,270 --> 00:06:12,760
ago.

99
00:06:12,780 --> 00:06:18,030
If we mouse over hash right here, TypeScript is going to say that it thinks its type is unknown.

100
00:06:18,030 --> 00:06:23,200
In other words, TypeScript just has no idea what gets returned from script whatsoever.

101
00:06:23,220 --> 00:06:29,640
So just to help TypeScript out a little bit, we're going to wrap this up with a set of parentheses,

102
00:06:29,640 --> 00:06:33,480
and then at the very end we'll say as buffer like so.

103
00:06:35,910 --> 00:06:39,150
So a buffer is what is actually returned from calling script.

104
00:06:39,150 --> 00:06:41,550
So again, just giving TypeScript a little bit of help.

105
00:06:43,320 --> 00:06:49,020
So then finally right here, we're going to join together our salt and the hashed result.

106
00:06:49,020 --> 00:06:52,140
And we're going to put a separator character between the two.

107
00:06:53,070 --> 00:06:54,150
So we'll say.

108
00:06:55,230 --> 00:06:59,220
Result is salt plus a dot.

109
00:06:59,220 --> 00:07:01,290
So that is going to be my separator character.

110
00:07:01,380 --> 00:07:06,480
So when we eventually store all this stuff inside of our database, we're going to have the salt, then

111
00:07:06,480 --> 00:07:08,610
a period and then the hash password.

112
00:07:08,640 --> 00:07:10,860
This is what is going to allow us to in the future.

113
00:07:10,860 --> 00:07:15,330
Take a look at this user, figure out what the salt was for them and what the hashed password was for

114
00:07:15,330 --> 00:07:16,080
them as well.

115
00:07:17,760 --> 00:07:19,860
And then I'll join on their hash.

116
00:07:19,860 --> 00:07:21,870
Two string hex.

117
00:07:22,730 --> 00:07:27,200
So the two string right here with a hex very similar to what we had to do back up here with the salt

118
00:07:27,230 --> 00:07:27,950
out of script.

119
00:07:27,980 --> 00:07:29,220
We get a buffer.

120
00:07:29,240 --> 00:07:32,600
We can't very easily just take a buffer and join it together with a string.

121
00:07:32,600 --> 00:07:35,110
You technically can, but we probably don't want to do that.

122
00:07:35,120 --> 00:07:40,730
So instead we're going to first turn that series of random bytes into hexadecimal.

123
00:07:43,040 --> 00:07:44,240
And that should be it.

124
00:07:45,400 --> 00:07:50,690
So now the last thing we have to do is take that result and we're going to create a new user with it.

125
00:07:50,930 --> 00:07:55,520
This result right here is going to effectively be the user's password as it is stored inside of our

126
00:07:55,520 --> 00:07:56,270
database.

127
00:07:56,600 --> 00:07:58,340
So let's take a pause right here.

128
00:07:58,370 --> 00:08:02,840
We'll then create that new user, save it, and then test all this stuff out in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,950 --> 00:00:03,840
All right, let's use that result.

2
00:00:03,840 --> 00:00:09,210
And the email that was fed into this function to create a new user and save it into our database.

3
00:00:09,240 --> 00:00:15,210
We're going to do this save operation using the users service, which we added into the auth service

4
00:00:15,210 --> 00:00:16,890
by using dependency injection.

5
00:00:18,700 --> 00:00:24,850
Okay, So we'll say that our user is going to be a wait user service.

6
00:00:25,150 --> 00:00:30,250
We're going to call the create function on our user service and we'll pass in as the first argument

7
00:00:30,250 --> 00:00:35,200
that email and then the second argument will be that result because that is effectively our password

8
00:00:35,200 --> 00:00:36,100
for the user.

9
00:00:39,110 --> 00:00:41,270
And then finally, we'll just return the user.

10
00:00:42,440 --> 00:00:44,000
And that should be it.

11
00:00:44,210 --> 00:00:46,250
So now it's time to actually test this thing out.

12
00:00:46,280 --> 00:00:49,830
To test this out, let's go back over to our users controller.

13
00:00:49,850 --> 00:00:52,230
We're going to update our signup method inside there.

14
00:00:52,250 --> 00:00:56,240
We're going to make sure that rather than directly reaching out to the user service, we're going to

15
00:00:56,240 --> 00:01:00,500
have our controller call that method right there to create a new user.

16
00:01:01,830 --> 00:01:03,450
So back inside our controller.

17
00:01:05,620 --> 00:01:08,860
We're going to first import our authentication service at the top.

18
00:01:16,580 --> 00:01:19,820
Well, then wire up that service to the controller using dependency injection.

19
00:01:19,820 --> 00:01:22,160
So inside of my constructor argument list.

20
00:01:22,990 --> 00:01:27,100
I'll add in private auth service.

21
00:01:30,320 --> 00:01:36,080
And then finally inside of our post request sign up handler rather than making use of the user service

22
00:01:36,080 --> 00:01:41,930
to create a user because that's going to store a plain password, I'm going to delete that and instead

23
00:01:41,930 --> 00:01:45,680
return this dot auth service dot signup.

24
00:01:46,040 --> 00:01:49,610
And then from that body object we're going to feed in the email.

25
00:01:50,720 --> 00:01:51,800
And the password.

26
00:01:54,290 --> 00:01:55,640
And that should be it.

27
00:01:56,000 --> 00:02:00,200
So let's save this and then open up our client and test this thing out.

28
00:02:00,260 --> 00:02:04,760
So our goal right now is to just create a user and then look into our database and make sure that we

29
00:02:04,760 --> 00:02:07,340
are storing a hashed and salted password.

30
00:02:07,940 --> 00:02:13,430
So inside my client, I'm going to put in a completely unique email address because remember, right

31
00:02:13,430 --> 00:02:17,360
now we are now checking for uniqueness inside of our database.

32
00:02:17,510 --> 00:02:20,510
So I'll put in a unique password which mean a unique email.

33
00:02:20,540 --> 00:02:22,040
I'm going to send that request off.

34
00:02:22,340 --> 00:02:23,960
It looks like it was successful.

35
00:02:24,050 --> 00:02:30,110
So now if I open up my SQLite database Explorer and remember that was an optional step earlier on inside

36
00:02:30,110 --> 00:02:30,790
the course.

37
00:02:30,800 --> 00:02:37,550
So if you installed that extension into VS code, you can now take a look at your user table and inside

38
00:02:37,550 --> 00:02:45,380
there you should have whatever user you just created along with the salt, then a period and then the

39
00:02:45,410 --> 00:02:47,930
salted and hashed password right there.

40
00:02:48,170 --> 00:02:49,430
And there it is.

41
00:02:51,580 --> 00:02:53,410
Okay, so this looks very good.

42
00:02:53,650 --> 00:02:58,570
So now that we've got the signup process inside of here, we're going to essentially do kind of the

43
00:02:58,570 --> 00:03:03,940
same thing more or less back inside of our auth service to implement the sign in method.

44
00:03:04,060 --> 00:03:09,970
So inside of sign in, given an email and a password, we're going to find that user inside the database.

45
00:03:10,000 --> 00:03:13,600
We're going to take a look at the stored password for them, split off the salt.

46
00:03:13,630 --> 00:03:15,360
We're going to go through all those steps.

47
00:03:15,370 --> 00:03:18,490
I'm not going to list them all out right now because we're going to put them together in two seconds.

48
00:03:18,490 --> 00:03:21,520
So quick pause right here and we'll deal with this in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:04,840
Let's take care of the sign in method inside our authentication service next.

2
00:00:04,860 --> 00:00:09,690
So remember, sign in is going to be just a little bit more complex than sign up, but ultimately it

3
00:00:09,690 --> 00:00:11,500
is going to be very similar in nature.

4
00:00:11,580 --> 00:00:16,410
So for sign in, a user is going to give us an email and a password.

5
00:00:16,440 --> 00:00:20,880
We first have to go into our database and try to find a user with that email.

6
00:00:20,970 --> 00:00:26,370
Assuming we actually find one, we're going to take their stored password, which contains a salt and

7
00:00:26,370 --> 00:00:27,220
a hash.

8
00:00:27,240 --> 00:00:29,010
We're going to split the two apart.

9
00:00:29,040 --> 00:00:34,590
We're then going to take the salt and join it together with the user's password or the one that they

10
00:00:34,590 --> 00:00:36,690
supplied, the one they're trying to sign in with.

11
00:00:36,780 --> 00:00:38,730
We'll then hash the result.

12
00:00:38,760 --> 00:00:44,580
Take the output from that and compare that hash against the stored hash that we have inside of our database.

13
00:00:44,820 --> 00:00:49,170
So again, a little bit complex logic, but as soon as we write out the code for it, I think you'll

14
00:00:49,170 --> 00:00:51,210
have a reasonable idea of what's going on here.

15
00:00:52,100 --> 00:00:56,120
So back inside of our sign in method, we're going to first add in some arguments.

16
00:00:56,120 --> 00:01:00,680
So I'm going to assume that this function will be called with an email that the user is trying to sign

17
00:01:00,680 --> 00:01:03,280
in with and the password they are trying to sign with.

18
00:01:03,290 --> 00:01:04,610
Sign in with as well.

19
00:01:06,460 --> 00:01:11,380
So then the first thing is to look through our database and find a user with that particular email.

20
00:01:11,940 --> 00:01:16,230
We can write out a query for that by using our users service.

21
00:01:16,500 --> 00:01:17,700
One little gotcha there.

22
00:01:17,700 --> 00:01:19,290
One very small issue.

23
00:01:19,440 --> 00:01:23,010
Remember that inside of our user service, if we pull it up very quickly.

24
00:01:23,840 --> 00:01:28,790
We do have a find one method but that attempts to find a user based on their ID.

25
00:01:29,270 --> 00:01:34,430
The only way we can find a user based on their email is the find method, but unfortunately that's going

26
00:01:34,430 --> 00:01:38,900
to give us back a list of different emails or a list of different users.

27
00:01:38,900 --> 00:01:41,390
So essentially an array of users.

28
00:01:41,390 --> 00:01:44,180
In this case we only want to find one single user.

29
00:01:44,180 --> 00:01:48,170
So give us a user or don't, but we don't really want a whole big array.

30
00:01:48,170 --> 00:01:53,390
So just one little thing we have to work around to deal with that back inside of sign in, we're going

31
00:01:53,390 --> 00:01:57,500
to add in const and then square brackets user.

32
00:01:58,770 --> 00:02:05,100
And then await this Dot users service find and pass in the email.

33
00:02:05,460 --> 00:02:07,770
Also make sure you mark this function as async.

34
00:02:07,830 --> 00:02:10,170
Think I might have done that before this video by mistake.

35
00:02:11,010 --> 00:02:13,100
So we're using a little bit of destructuring here.

36
00:02:13,110 --> 00:02:17,150
We're saying, let's take a look at all the different users with this given email.

37
00:02:17,160 --> 00:02:20,010
We're going to assume that we get back one user right here.

38
00:02:20,190 --> 00:02:23,680
So we need to check and make sure that a user actually was found.

39
00:02:23,700 --> 00:02:27,780
If we didn't find one, that means that the user gave us or the person who's trying to sign in, I should

40
00:02:27,780 --> 00:02:29,730
say, gave us an invalid email.

41
00:02:29,940 --> 00:02:33,270
So we'll say if there is no user.

42
00:02:34,940 --> 00:02:39,560
Then let's throw a new NotFound exception.

43
00:02:40,300 --> 00:02:43,450
And we'll say user not found.

44
00:02:44,850 --> 00:02:47,670
Let's also import that exception at the very top of this file.

45
00:02:47,670 --> 00:02:50,790
So at the very top from Nestjs Common.

46
00:02:51,830 --> 00:02:53,360
I'll get NotFound exception.

47
00:02:56,890 --> 00:03:01,240
Okay, so we've got a user and now it's time for us to do all this password comparison stuff.

48
00:03:01,420 --> 00:03:04,870
So the first thing we need to do is take a look at their stored password.

49
00:03:04,900 --> 00:03:11,410
Remember, inside of our database, we're going to have the salt and then a period and then the hash.

50
00:03:11,530 --> 00:03:16,360
So we need to take a look at that password property, split it based on that period and get back the

51
00:03:16,360 --> 00:03:17,680
salt and the hash.

52
00:03:19,060 --> 00:03:20,530
So for that we'll say.

53
00:03:21,620 --> 00:03:22,340
Square brackets.

54
00:03:22,340 --> 00:03:24,530
Once again, we'll do some array destructuring here.

55
00:03:24,920 --> 00:03:28,370
Salt and hash is going to be.

56
00:03:29,880 --> 00:03:33,660
Userpassword split on period.

57
00:03:37,620 --> 00:03:38,220
Okay.

58
00:03:39,570 --> 00:03:44,610
Next up, we're going to do the same hashing process we did back up here with script.

59
00:03:44,910 --> 00:03:50,730
So we're going to rehash this thing once again using the supplied password and our stored salt.

60
00:03:50,880 --> 00:03:55,140
We're then going to take the output from that and make sure the output is identical to the hash we had

61
00:03:55,140 --> 00:03:55,610
stored.

62
00:03:55,620 --> 00:03:59,400
And if it is identical, that means that the user did just sign in successfully.

63
00:04:00,820 --> 00:04:02,710
So I'm going to first.

64
00:04:04,590 --> 00:04:07,280
Say we have a variable called hash.

65
00:04:07,290 --> 00:04:09,300
How about new hat?

66
00:04:09,330 --> 00:04:10,890
No, I don't want to call it new hash.

67
00:04:10,920 --> 00:04:12,450
Let's call this hash right here.

68
00:04:12,450 --> 00:04:13,500
Let's call that stored hash.

69
00:04:13,500 --> 00:04:14,870
That makes a lot more sense.

70
00:04:14,880 --> 00:04:17,670
So then the new hash we'll just call simply hash.

71
00:04:17,850 --> 00:04:21,000
This will be a weight script.

72
00:04:21,300 --> 00:04:23,910
We'll pass in the supplied password.

73
00:04:26,650 --> 00:04:30,700
And the salt and it will get once again a length of 32.

74
00:04:31,320 --> 00:04:32,460
Just like before.

75
00:04:32,490 --> 00:04:36,210
We're going to wrap that with a set of parentheses and then say as buffer.

76
00:04:38,970 --> 00:04:40,680
And now we can finally do our comparison.

77
00:04:40,680 --> 00:04:46,290
So we're going to say if this hash right here, after turning it into a hexadecimal string is identical

78
00:04:46,290 --> 00:04:49,740
to the stored hash, that means the user gave us a valid password.

79
00:04:50,460 --> 00:04:59,460
