So we'll say if stored hash is equal to hash dot two string hex, that means we have a good password.

80
00:04:59,460 --> 00:05:01,940
The user is now considered to be logged in.

81
00:05:01,950 --> 00:05:05,640
So in this case, let's just return the user that they signed in as.

82
00:05:05,670 --> 00:05:08,040
So I'm going to return the user entity.

83
00:05:10,620 --> 00:05:11,700
And we'll say else.

84
00:05:11,700 --> 00:05:17,210
So if they gave us a bad password, let's just throw an error and say forbidden or something like that.

85
00:05:17,250 --> 00:05:18,730
Or how about just bad request?

86
00:05:18,750 --> 00:05:25,530
We've already imported that exception at the top, so I will do a throw new bad request exception.

87
00:05:25,800 --> 00:05:27,690
We'll say bad password.

88
00:05:30,470 --> 00:05:34,370
Listing off a function with an if else statement is something I personally just don't really care for.

89
00:05:34,400 --> 00:05:36,770
So we're going to invert the logic here just a little bit.

90
00:05:36,800 --> 00:05:42,830
We're going to say if the stored or the stored hash is not equal to hash, dot to string.

91
00:05:42,830 --> 00:05:44,750
So if those things are not the same.

92
00:05:45,500 --> 00:05:47,660
Then let's throw an exception.

93
00:05:47,660 --> 00:05:52,700
And thanks to that, now we can remove our else case and then just put return user at the bottom of

94
00:05:52,700 --> 00:05:53,360
the function.

95
00:05:53,360 --> 00:05:56,690
So just a little bit easier to read and understand what's going on inside of here.

96
00:05:58,790 --> 00:05:59,150
Okay.

97
00:05:59,150 --> 00:06:00,860
So once again, that should be it.

98
00:06:00,860 --> 00:06:04,820
So I'm going to save this file and then we'll go back over to our controller very quickly.

99
00:06:04,820 --> 00:06:08,720
We're going to add in a brand new route handler that's going to call sign in.

100
00:06:11,100 --> 00:06:12,690
So back inside my controller.

101
00:06:14,790 --> 00:06:18,090
Underneath our existing post request to sign up right there.

102
00:06:18,480 --> 00:06:22,110
I'm going to add in another post request handler to sign in.

103
00:06:23,660 --> 00:06:24,380
I'll call this method.

104
00:06:24,380 --> 00:06:25,460
Simply sign in.

105
00:06:27,030 --> 00:06:30,060
We're going to have a body here.

106
00:06:31,800 --> 00:06:37,200
We're going to reuse the create user DTO not super appropriate just because the name we're not really

107
00:06:37,200 --> 00:06:41,850
trying to create a user here or anything like that, but the create user DTO really just says that our

108
00:06:41,850 --> 00:06:43,980
body is going to have an email and a password.

109
00:06:43,980 --> 00:06:48,660
So it actually is kind of appropriate for sign in, even though it doesn't have the best name we can

110
00:06:48,660 --> 00:06:49,440
use it for right now.

111
00:06:49,470 --> 00:06:50,310
Totally fine.

112
00:06:52,390 --> 00:06:56,400
So then inside of sign in, we will return this auth service.

113
00:06:56,410 --> 00:06:59,230
Sign in with email.

114
00:07:00,090 --> 00:07:01,080
And password.

115
00:07:03,570 --> 00:07:04,080
Okay.

116
00:07:04,110 --> 00:07:05,010
Looks good.

117
00:07:05,280 --> 00:07:06,830
Let's go and do a test.

118
00:07:06,840 --> 00:07:09,120
So back inside of our client.

119
00:07:11,380 --> 00:07:18,670
I'm going to make a post request this time to auth slash sign in and I'm going to make sure that I've

120
00:07:18,670 --> 00:07:21,550
got a valid email and password combination.

121
00:07:21,790 --> 00:07:23,650
So I'm going to send this request off.

122
00:07:24,130 --> 00:07:25,420
And it looks good to me.

123
00:07:26,780 --> 00:07:27,350
I like it.

124
00:07:27,350 --> 00:07:32,960
So we've got an email and the ID, which means we did just sign in successfully, or at least we provided

125
00:07:32,960 --> 00:07:34,760
a valid email and password.

126
00:07:35,150 --> 00:07:40,520
Now if we change the password to something invalid, so if I put in a totally different string right

127
00:07:40,520 --> 00:07:41,030
here.

128
00:07:42,630 --> 00:07:43,850
And send the request.

129
00:07:43,860 --> 00:07:45,330
I'll get back a 400.

130
00:07:45,840 --> 00:07:49,470
And if I try to put in an email address that does not exist.

131
00:07:49,500 --> 00:07:51,570
I should get back a 404.

132
00:07:51,600 --> 00:07:52,920
It says User not found.

133
00:07:53,690 --> 00:07:54,590
Perfect.

134
00:07:54,800 --> 00:07:56,980
Well, I'd say that this looks pretty good.

135
00:07:56,990 --> 00:08:01,370
I think that we've got a reasonable solution that handles signing up and signing in.

136
00:08:01,580 --> 00:08:03,380
So now the very last thing we have to do.

137
00:08:04,440 --> 00:08:07,830
You might recall a couple diagrams ago, here we are right here.

138
00:08:07,830 --> 00:08:10,170
So we've handled the sign up process.

139
00:08:10,220 --> 00:08:14,040
Last thing we really have to do here is all that logic around this cookie.

140
00:08:14,040 --> 00:08:18,600
And then we need to make sure that whenever users make follow up requests, we retrieve information

141
00:08:18,600 --> 00:08:21,630
out of that cookie and do some validation around it as well.

142
00:08:21,840 --> 00:08:24,870
So we are almost all set with our authentication logic.

143
00:08:24,870 --> 00:08:27,780
Let's start to wrap this stuff up in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,150 --> 00:00:01,690
In this video.

2
00:00:01,690 --> 00:00:04,600
We're going to start working on the second half of this entire flow.

3
00:00:04,600 --> 00:00:10,330
So we have the ability to sign up a user, which creates a new user record and sign in a user which

4
00:00:10,330 --> 00:00:12,700
verifies a user supplied email and password.

5
00:00:12,700 --> 00:00:16,720
But we still don't have the ability to say, Hey, this person is signed in and this person is not,

6
00:00:16,720 --> 00:00:17,470
and so on.

7
00:00:17,470 --> 00:00:20,230
So that's what all this cookie stuff is all about.

8
00:00:20,230 --> 00:00:24,370
And to understand how this cookie stuff is going to work, I first want to help you understand a little

9
00:00:24,370 --> 00:00:28,600
package we're going to use to manage our cookies called cookie session.

10
00:00:28,600 --> 00:00:29,890
So quick diagram.

11
00:00:31,040 --> 00:00:33,690
Okay, so this is an imaginary request handler.

12
00:00:33,710 --> 00:00:35,390
This does not exist inside of our application.

13
00:00:35,390 --> 00:00:39,050
We're just going to imagine what cookie session does behind the scenes for us.

14
00:00:39,200 --> 00:00:43,580
So we're going to imagine that we make a get request to Asdf or something like that.

15
00:00:43,580 --> 00:00:48,080
And inside this request we are going to have a very special header called Cookie.

16
00:00:48,200 --> 00:00:53,030
Cookie is going to be a string that has what looks like a random series of numbers and letters.

17
00:00:53,720 --> 00:00:58,760
When this request comes into our server, the cookie session library is going to receive this request

18
00:00:58,760 --> 00:01:01,790
and take a look at all the different headers included inside of it.

19
00:01:01,820 --> 00:01:05,150
If it finds a header called Cookie, it's going to jump into action.

20
00:01:05,270 --> 00:01:09,200
First cookie session is going to take that encrypted value right there.

21
00:01:09,230 --> 00:01:14,150
It looks like a random series of numbers and letters, but it's actually an encrypted string that stores

22
00:01:14,150 --> 00:01:16,490
some plain JavaScript object inside of it.

23
00:01:18,100 --> 00:01:22,750
Cookie session is going to decode that string, and that's going to result in an object which referred

24
00:01:22,750 --> 00:01:24,610
to as our session object.

25
00:01:24,730 --> 00:01:28,050
That object can have any information stored inside of it that we want.

26
00:01:28,060 --> 00:01:32,080
So maybe in this case we have a color property that is a value of red.

27
00:01:33,280 --> 00:01:40,060
Then inside of our route handler, we're going to make use of a decorator to receive this session object.

28
00:01:40,060 --> 00:01:45,370
So on any of our given route handlers, we'll make use of yet another decorator to extract this session

29
00:01:45,400 --> 00:01:47,530
object from the incoming request.

30
00:01:48,210 --> 00:01:53,370
Then inside of our route handler, we can read information off that session object or we can add remove

31
00:01:53,370 --> 00:01:55,230
change properties on it as well.

32
00:01:55,260 --> 00:02:00,660
So maybe in this case we decide to update the session object and change the color property to blue instead.

33
00:02:01,400 --> 00:02:05,630
As soon as we make that change, the cookie session library is going to see that we have made a change

34
00:02:05,630 --> 00:02:10,460
to that session object and it's going to realize, Oh, I need to take this updated object, I need

35
00:02:10,460 --> 00:02:15,740
to take all the data inside of it and turn it back into an encrypted string and then send that string

36
00:02:15,740 --> 00:02:20,480
back in the outgoing response as a header called Set dash Cookie.

37
00:02:21,650 --> 00:02:26,120
Then right there, we'll have some kind of encrypted string that includes this new piece of data that

38
00:02:26,120 --> 00:02:29,780
says color is now blue as opposed to red, which it was before.

39
00:02:30,200 --> 00:02:35,480
Inside the outgoing cookie of set cookie right here, there's going to be a couple of other pieces of

40
00:02:35,480 --> 00:02:36,770
information tied to it.

41
00:02:36,770 --> 00:02:39,020
So some other stuff beyond just that encrypted string.

42
00:02:39,020 --> 00:02:41,540
But for right now, that's the only value that we really care about.

43
00:02:42,900 --> 00:02:46,710
So that's what's going to be going on behind the scenes when we make use of the cookie session library.

44
00:02:46,740 --> 00:02:48,960
Now we're going to use this cookie, this little.

45
00:02:48,990 --> 00:02:53,040
We're going to take advantage of the fact that we can tie some information to this incoming request

46
00:02:53,040 --> 00:02:58,380
and outgoing response to store the ID of the user that this person just signed up to our application

47
00:02:58,380 --> 00:03:00,660
as or signed into our application as.

48
00:03:01,700 --> 00:03:05,930
Step number one, however, is to just understand cookies session by itself and understand how the session

49
00:03:05,930 --> 00:03:06,890
object works.

50
00:03:06,890 --> 00:03:09,740
So let's not even think about authentication for right now.

51
00:03:09,740 --> 00:03:13,820
Instead, let's just get a better idea of how cookie session by itself works.

52
00:03:14,030 --> 00:03:18,680
Step number one, we have to install this library into our project, so let's take care of that right

53
00:03:18,680 --> 00:03:19,280
away.

54
00:03:19,790 --> 00:03:21,380
Back over at my terminal.

55
00:03:23,110 --> 00:03:24,820
I'm going to start my running web server.

56
00:03:25,780 --> 00:03:31,030
And run npm install cookie session and at types.

57
00:03:31,830 --> 00:03:34,320
Cookie session as well.

58
00:03:35,940 --> 00:03:39,720
Once I've got that installed, I'll then start my server back up with NPM run.

59
00:03:39,720 --> 00:03:40,860
Start colon dev.

60
00:03:43,250 --> 00:03:44,000
Very good.

61
00:03:44,360 --> 00:03:48,230
Then back over inside of our editor, we're going to find our Main.ts file.

62
00:03:49,300 --> 00:03:53,800
So we're going to wire up the cookie session middleware inside of here at the very top.

63
00:03:53,800 --> 00:03:55,480
We'll first import cookie session.

64
00:03:55,480 --> 00:04:01,900
So of course, normally we would write something like, say, cookie session from cookie session.

65
00:04:01,990 --> 00:04:03,910
That would be what we normally do.

66
00:04:03,910 --> 00:04:05,800
Unfortunately, cookie session.

67
00:04:05,800 --> 00:04:11,380
This library has a couple of settings or a couple of things inside of it that don't work nicely with

68
00:04:11,380 --> 00:04:14,170
our current Tsconfig setup.

69
00:04:14,170 --> 00:04:20,080
So in other words, thanks to some settings that we have inside of our config file that are required

70
00:04:20,080 --> 00:04:24,760
by Nest, we cannot make use of a normal import statement to make use of cookie session.

71
00:04:24,760 --> 00:04:27,190
So it's just a very small compatibility thing.

72
00:04:27,280 --> 00:04:31,510
So to get around this very small problem, we cannot make use of a normal import statement.

73
00:04:31,510 --> 00:04:35,980
Instead, we're going to kind of regress, go back to the old way of doing things, which is to use

74
00:04:35,980 --> 00:04:37,840
a simple require statement.

75
00:04:38,050 --> 00:04:42,220
So we're going to add in a cookie session.

76
00:04:43,110 --> 00:04:45,810
And that's going to be require cookie session.

77
00:04:47,780 --> 00:04:52,910
So again, only doing this thanks to a little bit of configuration mismatch between our Nest project

78
00:04:52,910 --> 00:04:54,680
and cookie session itself.

79
00:04:55,950 --> 00:05:00,060
Then inside of our bootstrap function, we're going to find app right here.

80
00:05:00,060 --> 00:05:01,830
We're going to call app dot use.

81
00:05:03,230 --> 00:05:05,120
We're going to pass in cookie session.

82
00:05:05,330 --> 00:05:10,340
We're going to call cookie session and to the cookie session function call, we'll put in a configuration

83
00:05:10,340 --> 00:05:11,120
object.

84
00:05:11,450 --> 00:05:16,250
The only option we're going to pass in inside this object for right now is a keys property.

85
00:05:16,280 --> 00:05:19,190
That's going to be an array with a string inside of it.

86
00:05:19,190 --> 00:05:20,990
And that string can be anything you want.

87
00:05:21,020 --> 00:05:23,420
Just a series of random numbers and letters.

88
00:05:23,750 --> 00:05:28,250
This string is going to be used to encrypt the information that is stored inside the cookie.

89
00:05:28,430 --> 00:05:33,470
So again, this right here, the info inside the cookie is encrypted and it cannot be easily read or

90
00:05:33,470 --> 00:05:35,040
changed by a user.

91
00:05:35,060 --> 00:05:39,800
So the keys array that we just put in is being used for this encryption step.

92
00:05:40,340 --> 00:05:43,820
We're going to eventually come back and do something a little bit better than just putting in a plain

93
00:05:43,820 --> 00:05:44,660
string right here.

94
00:05:44,660 --> 00:05:47,660
But for right now in the development environment, this is totally fine.

95
00:05:49,100 --> 00:05:50,210
Okay, so that's it.

96
00:05:50,240 --> 00:05:52,150
We've now got cookie session all set up.

97
00:05:52,160 --> 00:05:53,470
So let's take a pause right here.

98
00:05:53,480 --> 00:05:54,680
We're going to come back in the next video.

99
00:05:54,680 --> 00:05:59,720
And as I mentioned, we're going to put together 1 or 2 very small request handlers and just test out

100
00:05:59,720 --> 00:06:01,520
this entire session thing.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,240 --> 00:00:05,290
I'm back inside of our users controller So inside of here we're going to understand how to make use

2
00:00:05,290 --> 00:00:08,350
of cookies session and the session object that it creates.

3
00:00:08,680 --> 00:00:11,950
So let me tell you what we're going to do as a very quick exercise in this video.

4
00:00:11,980 --> 00:00:15,610
We're going to write out a little bit of code and then delete it by the very end of the video.

5
00:00:15,610 --> 00:00:20,710
So I want to create a new Git request handler inside of our users controller.

6
00:00:20,710 --> 00:00:26,710
It's going to have a route of something like slash auth colors and we'll put in a wildcard token right

7
00:00:26,710 --> 00:00:27,280
here.

8
00:00:27,280 --> 00:00:28,600
So colon color.

9
00:00:28,600 --> 00:00:33,880
So that might be something like red or blue or whatever else inside of our route handler We're going

10
00:00:33,880 --> 00:00:39,190
to read in that wild card and then apply it to the color property of the session object.

11
00:00:39,310 --> 00:00:44,380
So this right here should be the color param from the incoming request.

12
00:00:44,770 --> 00:00:49,930
We'll then add in a second request handler to take a look at the cookie and really just take a look

13
00:00:49,930 --> 00:00:52,210
at the or the color property on the session.

14
00:00:52,210 --> 00:00:54,790
We're going to print out whatever color is stored there.

15
00:00:54,790 --> 00:00:56,380
So let's give this a shot.

16
00:00:57,510 --> 00:01:02,970
Back inside of users Controller I'm going to go up to the very top and from Nestjs Common, we're going

17
00:01:02,970 --> 00:01:05,760
to import another decorator called Session.

18
00:01:06,060 --> 00:01:11,140
So very similar to how we have many other decorators for extracting information from incoming requests.

19
00:01:11,160 --> 00:01:12,790
Session is yet another.

20
00:01:12,810 --> 00:01:17,820
This is going to work with the cookies session library to get us access to this entire object inside

21
00:01:17,820 --> 00:01:19,590
of any of our different route handlers.

22
00:01:21,400 --> 00:01:22,720
Down inside of our controller.

23
00:01:23,970 --> 00:01:26,180
Right above our post sign up route.

24
00:01:26,190 --> 00:01:28,320
I'm going to add in two different route handlers.

25
00:01:28,320 --> 00:01:33,300
The first one will be a git to colors colon color.

26
00:01:34,670 --> 00:01:35,480
I'll call this one.

27
00:01:35,480 --> 00:01:36,350
Set color.

28
00:01:37,310 --> 00:01:38,630
Inside of the argument list.

29
00:01:38,630 --> 00:01:41,330
We're going to first extract that color parameter right there.

30
00:01:41,330 --> 00:01:43,890
So whatever wild card for color is provided.

31
00:01:43,910 --> 00:01:46,520
So let's say at parameter color.

32
00:01:47,700 --> 00:01:49,380
Color and that'll be a string.

33
00:01:49,680 --> 00:01:54,180
And then as a second argument, we're going to receive this entire session object.

34
00:01:54,540 --> 00:01:57,750
So we'll say at session.

35
00:02:00,230 --> 00:02:01,460
Too many S's on there.

36
00:02:01,490 --> 00:02:02,240
There we go.

37
00:02:03,180 --> 00:02:04,680
I'm going to call the argument session.

38
00:02:04,680 --> 00:02:07,170
And for right now, I'm going to give it a type of any.

39
00:02:09,720 --> 00:02:15,090
So then inside of set color, we're going to receive that color wild card and use it to update our session

40
00:02:15,090 --> 00:02:15,800
object.

41
00:02:15,810 --> 00:02:18,030
So we'll say session.

42
00:02:19,110 --> 00:02:22,710
Dot color is color, and that should be it.

43
00:02:23,600 --> 00:02:28,760
Then we'll set up another route handler where we're going to read or just print out or just return whatever

44
00:02:28,760 --> 00:02:31,100
color is stored on the session object.

45
00:02:31,370 --> 00:02:34,010
So I'll define a second route handler called get.

46
00:02:35,160 --> 00:02:36,090
Colors.

47
00:02:37,520 --> 00:02:38,660
I'll call the method itself.

48
00:02:38,660 --> 00:02:39,530
Git color.

49
00:02:40,540 --> 00:02:44,380
We're going to receive the session object as an argument.

50
00:02:45,260 --> 00:02:49,490
And then inside of here we will return session dot color.

51
00:02:50,880 --> 00:02:51,870
And that's it.

52
00:02:52,370 --> 00:02:57,950
So the only goal here is to communicate or show to you with a very simple example how we can store information

53
00:02:57,950 --> 00:03:01,940
on the session object and then retrieve it on follow up requests very easily.

54
00:03:02,150 --> 00:03:03,950
So let's now go over to our client.

55
00:03:03,950 --> 00:03:07,790
We're going to try to call both these route handlers and just make some observations about the entire

56
00:03:07,790 --> 00:03:08,600
process.

57
00:03:09,600 --> 00:03:11,370
So inside of my client.

58
00:03:12,580 --> 00:03:14,680
I'm going to go down to the very bottom of this file.

59
00:03:17,680 --> 00:03:20,830
Put in a new comment down here and say set a color.

60
00:03:20,830 --> 00:03:22,270
So this will be a git.

61
00:03:23,340 --> 00:03:24,990
To localhost 3000.

62
00:03:25,020 --> 00:03:25,690
Auth.

63
00:03:25,710 --> 00:03:26,610
Colors.

64
00:03:26,640 --> 00:03:28,800
And I'm going to first set my color to red.

65
00:03:29,860 --> 00:03:34,150
Then I'll make a follow up request down here to get the color.

66
00:03:35,120 --> 00:03:36,650
And this will be a gift to.

67
00:03:37,590 --> 00:03:39,150
Localhost 3000.

68
00:03:39,270 --> 00:03:40,890
Auth colors.

69
00:03:41,460 --> 00:03:42,840
Okay, so let's try this out.

70
00:03:43,020 --> 00:03:47,880
When I send off the first request, I'll see that inside my list of response headers.

71
00:03:47,880 --> 00:03:49,750
I've now got a set cookie header.

72
00:03:49,770 --> 00:03:55,770
So there's a very long string of random numbers and letters right here that is encrypting our object

73
00:03:55,770 --> 00:03:56,820
as one property.

74
00:03:56,820 --> 00:04:00,990
And inside of it it's color and it should have a value of red.

75
00:04:01,850 --> 00:04:06,980
So now when we make our follow up request, that little string right there is going to be attached automatically

76
00:04:06,980 --> 00:04:08,240
to the outgoing request.

77
00:04:08,660 --> 00:04:14,150
Cookie session is going to receive that encrypted string right there and decrypt it into our session

78
00:04:14,150 --> 00:04:14,930
object.

79
00:04:14,930 --> 00:04:19,040
And so now at this point in time, the color property on it should be red.

80
00:04:19,280 --> 00:04:23,390
So inside of our request handler, we're going to take off that red string and just return it.

81
00:04:23,390 --> 00:04:27,290
So if we send off the second request, we should get back simply Red.

82
00:04:27,710 --> 00:04:28,400
Very good.

83
00:04:29,330 --> 00:04:31,670
And of course we can test this out as much as we want.

84
00:04:31,670 --> 00:04:35,690
So instead of colors red, we can do blue or some really long string.

85
00:04:35,720 --> 00:04:36,770
Send that off.

86
00:04:37,450 --> 00:04:41,140
Then do the second request and get the string right there.

87
00:04:42,080 --> 00:04:43,290
Okay, so this looks great.

88
00:04:43,310 --> 00:04:48,590
This is proof that we can take some information stored inside the cookie and then retrieve that information

89
00:04:48,590 --> 00:04:50,360
on a later follow up request.

90
00:04:51,000 --> 00:04:55,740
So as the very last thing, I'm going to delete the two requests we have inside of my request file right

91
00:04:55,740 --> 00:04:56,250
here.

92
00:04:56,280 --> 00:04:58,710
Because again, that was just a very quick example.

93
00:04:58,980 --> 00:05:03,960
And then inside of my controller, once again, I'm going to remove the two route handlers we put together.

94
00:05:03,960 --> 00:05:06,000
If you want to, you can leave these things inside of here.

95
00:05:06,000 --> 00:05:07,830
They're not going to conflict with any other code.

96
00:05:07,830 --> 00:05:10,860
I just want to have a very concise example for you to walk away with.

97
00:05:10,860 --> 00:05:15,540
And we're not going to use these route handlers anymore, so me personally am going to delete them.

98
00:05:16,800 --> 00:05:20,910
Okay, So now that we've got a better idea of what all this session stuff is all about and how we can

99
00:05:20,910 --> 00:05:24,570
store information between different requests, let's go back in the next video.

100
00:05:24,570 --> 00:05:30,240
We're going to update our create user and sign in root handlers inside of both those root handlers.

101
00:05:30,240 --> 00:05:36,210
After user successfully signs up or signs in inside of our session object, we're going to store the

102
00:05:36,210 --> 00:05:37,140
user ID.

103
00:05:38,830 --> 00:05:43,060
Of whoever the user just signed up as or whoever they just signed in as.

104
00:05:43,390 --> 00:05:45,520
So let's take care of that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,360 --> 00:00:03,970
Inside of our sign up and sign in request handlers.

2
00:00:03,970 --> 00:00:09,460
We're now going to take the user that is returned from either the signup method right there or the sign

3
00:00:09,460 --> 00:00:10,710
in method right there.

4
00:00:10,720 --> 00:00:13,240
We get back a user entity from both those.

5
00:00:13,270 --> 00:00:17,710
We're going to take the ID of that user entity and then assign it to the session object.

6
00:00:17,710 --> 00:00:23,020
And that's going to be our tool for deciding whether or not a user is signed in on follow up requests.

7
00:00:23,020 --> 00:00:26,620
So let's update both these methods and put this into reality.

8
00:00:26,860 --> 00:00:31,990
First thing we're going to do on both of these methods, we're going to receive the session object by

9
00:00:31,990 --> 00:00:33,670
using the session decorator.

10
00:00:34,360 --> 00:00:37,370
Now I'm still going to annotate these as type N for right now.

11
00:00:42,980 --> 00:00:46,610
Then on both of them, I'm going to mark them with the async keyword.

12
00:00:49,180 --> 00:00:52,780
From sign up and sign in rather than immediately returning the value.

13
00:00:52,810 --> 00:00:56,580
We're going to receive the user entity that is created or returned from both of them.

14
00:00:56,650 --> 00:00:58,810
So I'm going to remove the return statements.

15
00:01:01,650 --> 00:01:06,480
And we'll assign the result of both of these to a variable that we'll call user.

16
00:01:06,780 --> 00:01:09,090
And don't forget the await keyword on there as well.

17
00:01:10,100 --> 00:01:17,150
So then after we either successfully sign up or sign in, we're going to take the ID of the user and

18
00:01:17,150 --> 00:01:20,630
assign it to the user ID property of our session object.

19
00:01:22,020 --> 00:01:26,610
So we'll say session dot user id is user.id.

20
00:01:27,690 --> 00:01:30,540
Same thing on the other handler as well.

21
00:01:31,860 --> 00:01:38,580
And then in both cases, we're going to return the user because we still want to send back the response,

22
00:01:38,580 --> 00:01:40,320
the user that was created.

23
00:01:41,310 --> 00:01:42,720
Okay, so that should be it.

24
00:01:42,750 --> 00:01:48,540
So now if we create a brand new user or really sign up or if we sign in our session object and therefore

25
00:01:48,570 --> 00:01:53,130
our cookie should be getting updated with the ID of the person that we just signed in as.

26
00:01:53,250 --> 00:01:57,960
So we don't really have a good way of testing this out, but let's at least make a request to sign up

27
00:01:57,960 --> 00:02:00,000
and sign in and just see what happens.

28
00:02:01,580 --> 00:02:03,170
Back inside my client.

29
00:02:03,710 --> 00:02:06,200
I'm going to find my first request right here.

30
00:02:07,160 --> 00:02:12,050
At some point in time, I have a comment on here called Create a User, but I change this root to sign

31
00:02:12,050 --> 00:02:12,440
in.

32
00:02:12,440 --> 00:02:14,900
So I'm going to change that back to sign up.

33
00:02:15,630 --> 00:02:17,520
I'll then duplicate that entire block.

34
00:02:18,210 --> 00:02:20,260
And paste it right underneath.

35
00:02:20,280 --> 00:02:21,360
So two copies of it.

36
00:02:21,360 --> 00:02:30,930
Now on the second copy, I'm going to change the comment to say sign in as an existing user and I'll

37
00:02:30,930 --> 00:02:33,450
change the route right there to sign in.

38
00:02:34,910 --> 00:02:40,560
Okay, so let's now sign up as a brand new user and then immediately attempt to sign in as them as well.

39
00:02:40,580 --> 00:02:43,910
And again, we don't really have anything to say whether or not this is going to be successful just

40
00:02:43,910 --> 00:02:44,330
yet.

41
00:02:44,330 --> 00:02:47,210
I just want to make sure we don't get any errors or anything like that.

42
00:02:47,860 --> 00:02:51,400
So to sign up, I'm going to change the email to something that I'll definitely remember.

43
00:02:51,400 --> 00:02:53,950
So for me, I'm going to do email at test.

44
00:02:54,760 --> 00:02:56,740
Dot com about test at test.com.

45
00:02:56,740 --> 00:02:57,480
Nice and simple.

46
00:02:57,490 --> 00:02:58,840
I can definitely remember that.

47
00:02:59,380 --> 00:03:01,990
I'm then going to set my password to 12345.

48
00:03:02,960 --> 00:03:04,430
So I'll send off that request.

49
00:03:04,430 --> 00:03:05,480
And then the response.

50
00:03:05,480 --> 00:03:07,460
I get a much longer cookie right here.

51
00:03:09,020 --> 00:03:13,430
So now we are taking the user's ID and setting it on that session object.

52
00:03:16,130 --> 00:03:18,050
So then on sign in.

53
00:03:18,700 --> 00:03:19,900
I'm going to try to.

54
00:03:20,590 --> 00:03:25,570
Sign in as test at test.com with the valid password.

55
00:03:25,570 --> 00:03:28,930
And now when we send this off, we're going to get kind of a surprising response.

56
00:03:28,930 --> 00:03:29,980
So I'm going to send that off.

57
00:03:29,980 --> 00:03:34,780
And in this case, I do not have a headers property or I don't have any headers whatsoever.

58
00:03:34,780 --> 00:03:35,740
I don't have anything.

59
00:03:35,740 --> 00:03:36,610
Well, I do have headers.

60
00:03:36,610 --> 00:03:40,570
I got content type and content length, but I don't have a set cookie header.

61
00:03:40,570 --> 00:03:42,460
So here's an interesting little thing.

62
00:03:42,460 --> 00:03:45,730
Whenever we try to make a change to that session object.

63
00:03:46,150 --> 00:03:49,780
Cookie session is going to take a look at whatever change we just made and it's going to say, well,

64
00:03:49,780 --> 00:03:54,550
if you didn't actually change any properties, if they're all identical, it's not going to send back

65
00:03:54,550 --> 00:03:56,710
any updated cookie in the response.

66
00:03:56,710 --> 00:04:01,870
So in our case, between sign up and sign in, in both cases, the session object is going to end up

67
00:04:01,870 --> 00:04:03,220
being completely identical.

68
00:04:03,220 --> 00:04:08,680
In both cases, we're gonna have a user ID property set to the user's ID So between these two different

69
00:04:08,680 --> 00:04:14,020
request handlers, because there have been no actual changes to the session object, whenever we make

70
00:04:14,050 --> 00:04:19,000
a request to the second one, we're not going to see any updated cookie come back because there is no

71
00:04:19,000 --> 00:04:19,600
updated cookie.

72
00:04:19,600 --> 00:04:26,330
We have not made any changes to the session object and that's why we saw on the sign up, we got back

73
00:04:26,330 --> 00:04:27,410
the set cookie header.

74
00:04:27,410 --> 00:04:30,590
But then on the second one we did not see anything of the sort.

75
00:04:31,510 --> 00:04:31,900
Okay.

76
00:04:31,900 --> 00:04:36,010
So like I said, it kind of looks like things are working, but we don't really have any good proof

77
00:04:36,010 --> 00:04:36,990
of that just yet.

78
00:04:37,000 --> 00:04:38,470
So let's take one more pause right here.

79
00:04:38,470 --> 00:04:39,790
We're going to come back in just a moment.

80
00:04:39,820 --> 00:04:44,260
We're going to make sure that we can put together a route handler that can only be accessed if a user

81
00:04:44,260 --> 00:04:46,870
is signed in or something like that.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,540 --> 00:00:05,260
In this video, we're going to put together another route handler just to make sure that all this session

2
00:00:05,260 --> 00:00:06,950
stuff is working as expected.

3
00:00:06,970 --> 00:00:11,830
The goal of this route handler is to tell the user who they are currently signed into our application

4
00:00:11,860 --> 00:00:12,490
as.

5
00:00:12,520 --> 00:00:16,270
Let's start putting this thing together and think you'll have a very good idea of what we're doing very

6
00:00:16,270 --> 00:00:16,930
quickly.

7
00:00:17,050 --> 00:00:22,390
So right above my create user method, I'm going to add in a new handler that will deal with get requests

8
00:00:22,390 --> 00:00:23,530
to slash.

9
00:00:23,530 --> 00:00:24,820
Who am I?

10
00:00:25,760 --> 00:00:29,240
I'm then going to tie this to a route handler called Who am I?

11
00:00:31,460 --> 00:00:32,450
Inside the argument list.

12
00:00:32,450 --> 00:00:34,760
We're going to receive our session object.

13
00:00:36,680 --> 00:00:44,360
And then inside the body, I'm going to return the result of this Dot users service bind one with session

14
00:00:44,390 --> 00:00:46,730
dot user ID and that's it.

15
00:00:47,440 --> 00:00:52,300
So if a user is signed into our application, that means they have a defined user ID property inside

16
00:00:52,300 --> 00:00:53,020
their cookie.

17
00:00:53,050 --> 00:00:57,430
So we're going to take that session object, look at that user ID property, we're going to look up

18
00:00:57,430 --> 00:01:01,480
a user inside of our database with that ID and then just return the result.

19
00:01:01,690 --> 00:01:08,020
If the user is not signed in, then session dot user ID will be something like undefined and hopefully

20
00:01:08,020 --> 00:01:12,820
we will not find a user who is who has an ID of undefined or anything like that.

21
00:01:13,700 --> 00:01:14,030
Okay.

22
00:01:14,030 --> 00:01:15,920
So to test this out, I'm going to save the file.

23
00:01:15,950 --> 00:01:21,440
I'll then go back over to my client and anywhere inside of here, I'm going to add in a new request.

24
00:01:22,240 --> 00:01:27,190
So I'll put in a comment that says get the currently signed in user.

25
00:01:27,520 --> 00:01:31,690
This will be a get request to localhost 3000.

26
00:01:33,560 --> 00:01:33,950
Off.

27
00:01:33,980 --> 00:01:34,850
Who am I?

28
00:01:35,210 --> 00:01:40,340
And now if I send off that request, I should get back whoever I am signed in as.

29
00:01:40,490 --> 00:01:41,300
Perfect.

30
00:01:41,600 --> 00:01:47,180
If I sign in as a different user and make the same request, I should then get back a completely different

31
00:01:47,180 --> 00:01:48,080
response.

32
00:01:48,990 --> 00:01:53,640
So I'd say this looks good and it is proof that our application is pretty much working as expected.

33
00:01:53,820 --> 00:01:58,020
So now one of the last big things we're going to take care of is making sure you can sign out of our

34
00:01:58,020 --> 00:01:58,910
application as well.

35
00:01:58,920 --> 00:02:01,020
So let's take care of that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,780 --> 00:00:03,900
We should probably allow users to sign out of our application.

2
00:00:03,900 --> 00:00:08,430
So let's add in another request handler that will deal with allowing a user to sign out.

3
00:00:08,730 --> 00:00:15,090
Right above my sign up handler, I'm going to add in a new post request handler to sign out.

4
00:00:16,330 --> 00:00:19,990
This will be not create user but sign out.

5
00:00:20,020 --> 00:00:21,580
Just keep the name very simple.

6
00:00:22,060 --> 00:00:24,490
I'll then receive that session object.

7
00:00:26,400 --> 00:00:27,780
And then to sign a user out.

8
00:00:27,780 --> 00:00:30,430
All we have to do is remove that user ID property.

9
00:00:30,450 --> 00:00:31,370
That's pretty much it.

10
00:00:31,380 --> 00:00:35,520
So I'm going to say session dot user ID equals null.

11
00:00:35,610 --> 00:00:40,980
So now whenever a user makes a follow up request to us, they will send a user ID of Null, which means

12
00:00:40,980 --> 00:00:42,360
they are not signed in.

13
00:00:42,390 --> 00:00:46,950
Now, unfortunately, it's going to be a little bit more challenging than that thanks to a little oddity

14
00:00:46,950 --> 00:00:48,330
inside of SQLite.

15
00:00:48,330 --> 00:00:50,250
But let's just try this out very quickly.

16
00:00:51,610 --> 00:00:54,280
So first I'm going to make sure that I am correctly signed in.

17
00:00:54,370 --> 00:00:56,530
So I'll sign in as a real user.

18
00:00:57,860 --> 00:01:00,080
And then going to get my current user.

19
00:01:00,230 --> 00:01:01,430
Okay, that looks good.

20
00:01:01,550 --> 00:01:03,950
Let's now add in another request helper.

21
00:01:04,400 --> 00:01:06,800
So I'll say sign out.

22
00:01:07,160 --> 00:01:11,960
That will be a get to localhost 3000 auth sign out.

23
00:01:12,720 --> 00:01:14,610
So I'm going to send off that request.

24
00:01:16,440 --> 00:01:17,880
Hope did I put in the wrong.

25
00:01:18,770 --> 00:01:19,100
They're not.

26
00:01:19,100 --> 00:01:20,090
Save this file.

27
00:01:20,180 --> 00:01:21,410
Sign out.

28
00:01:21,440 --> 00:01:22,160
Sign out.

29
00:01:22,160 --> 00:01:22,910
O post request.

30
00:01:22,910 --> 00:01:23,690
My mistake.

31
00:01:23,730 --> 00:01:24,800
Should be a post.

32
00:01:24,830 --> 00:01:25,220
There we go.

33
00:01:25,220 --> 00:01:25,970
That's better.

34
00:01:26,890 --> 00:01:27,820
You can send that off.

35
00:01:27,820 --> 00:01:31,870
So there's our updated cookie right there, which hopefully inside there should basically say, hey,

36
00:01:31,870 --> 00:01:33,670
this user is no longer signed in.

37
00:01:33,790 --> 00:01:38,380
And if I now make a request to get the currently signed in user, as I mentioned, we're going to see

38
00:01:38,380 --> 00:01:40,090
that this is a little bit more challenging.

39
00:01:40,270 --> 00:01:44,050
So I'm going to send that off and unfortunately, I still get back a response here.

40
00:01:44,050 --> 00:01:48,980
But you'll notice that the user that I fetched is not the same user who was signed in as.

41
00:01:49,000 --> 00:01:50,480
So what's happening?

42
00:01:50,500 --> 00:01:56,000
Well, we are updating the session object correctly, so our sign out method is working as expected.

43
00:01:56,020 --> 00:01:58,990
What is going wrong is the Who am I?

44
00:01:59,020 --> 00:01:59,710
Method.

45
00:01:59,830 --> 00:02:05,050
So if we call, find one with a ID of null right here.

46
00:02:05,080 --> 00:02:09,160
We're going to get back the very first user inside of our list of users.

47
00:02:09,160 --> 00:02:10,510
So that's what's going on right now.

48
00:02:10,540 --> 00:02:13,810
Our find one method itself is just not working as expected.

49
00:02:13,990 --> 00:02:19,090
So we should probably update the find one method to make sure that it handles being given a ID that

50
00:02:19,090 --> 00:02:19,900
is null.

51
00:02:19,990 --> 00:02:23,290
So let's go take care of that very quickly inside of our users service.

52
00:02:24,840 --> 00:02:26,710
I'm going to find my user service.

53
00:02:26,730 --> 00:02:29,220
I'm going to find the find one method right here.

54
00:02:29,220 --> 00:02:34,980
And I'm going to say, if there is no ID, so if ID is Falsy, if it's zero, if it's null, if it's

55
00:02:34,980 --> 00:02:40,380
undefined, whatever, I'm going to just return null right away and I'm not going to try to find any

56
00:02:40,380 --> 00:02:42,840
user with that undefined or null ID.

57
00:02:44,590 --> 00:02:49,150
Okay, so let's go and test out our sign out functionality again.

58
00:02:50,260 --> 00:02:51,940
So I'm going to sign out again.

59
00:02:52,360 --> 00:02:58,330
I'm going to try to get the currently signed in user and now I get back a response of null or essentially

60
00:02:58,330 --> 00:02:59,260
just empty.

61
00:02:59,500 --> 00:03:03,940
We might want to update the response code or the status code to 404 or something like that.

62
00:03:03,940 --> 00:03:07,360
I'll leave that as an exercise to you if you want to go through that process.

63
00:03:08,110 --> 00:03:11,320
Otherwise, to test this out one more time, I'm going to sign in again.

64
00:03:11,470 --> 00:03:11,770
Okay.

65
00:03:11,770 --> 00:03:12,580
There we are.

66
00:03:12,760 --> 00:03:15,100
I'm going to get my currently signed in user.

67
00:03:15,130 --> 00:03:16,030
Looks good.

68
00:03:16,210 --> 00:03:17,380
I'm going to sign out.

69
00:03:18,060 --> 00:03:21,750
I'm going to get my currently signed in user again and get empty response.

70
00:03:22,260 --> 00:03:23,010
Perfect.

71
00:03:23,730 --> 00:03:25,440
Okay, so this looks fantastic.

72
00:03:25,440 --> 00:03:28,650
And I think our users controller is just about complete.

73
00:03:28,680 --> 00:03:35,130
We've now got all the basic functionality around signing in, signing up, signing out and figuring

74
00:03:35,130 --> 00:03:37,740
out who the currently signed in user is as well.

75
00:03:38,040 --> 00:03:39,360
So let's take a pause right here.

76
00:03:39,360 --> 00:03:44,250
There are a little bit more or is a little bit more around authentication I want to discuss with you,

77
00:03:44,250 --> 00:03:46,140
but we'll take care of that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,430 --> 00:00:05,870
Our authentication logic is looking pretty good and we've got a lot of very common features that just

2
00:00:05,870 --> 00:00:07,750
about any authentication system needs.

3
00:00:07,760 --> 00:00:13,100
So we have sign up, sign in, sign out, and we can also return who the currently signed in user is.

4
00:00:13,100 --> 00:00:18,860
But there are two other things that our authentication system probably needs to work just perfectly.

5
00:00:19,280 --> 00:00:24,350
Unlike the other things we've worked on so far, which are all root handlers, these last two features

6
00:00:24,350 --> 00:00:28,820
are going to be little tools so we can use elsewhere inside of our project just to automate a couple

7
00:00:28,820 --> 00:00:30,920
of authentication related tasks.

8
00:00:31,280 --> 00:00:36,110
So we're going to create an interceptor or a decorator or something like that.

9
00:00:36,110 --> 00:00:37,310
We're going to create two of them.

10
00:00:37,310 --> 00:00:41,810
The first one is going to reject requests to certain root handlers.

11
00:00:41,810 --> 00:00:47,810
If the user is not signed in, then the second one is going to automatically tell a request handler

12
00:00:47,810 --> 00:00:52,640
who the currently signed in user is, or essentially who the person making the request is.

13
00:00:52,760 --> 00:00:58,940
So again, these are going to be functions or interceptors or decorators or something like that to just

14
00:00:58,940 --> 00:01:03,720
automate these tasks inside of other root handlers that we eventually put together.

15
00:01:04,800 --> 00:01:07,620
So the things we're actually going to create for each of these.

16
00:01:07,650 --> 00:01:10,230
The first one, we're going to make something called a guard.

17
00:01:11,320 --> 00:01:14,100
A guard does just about exactly what the name implies.

18
00:01:14,110 --> 00:01:18,730
It guards a route and it forbids access to that route if some condition is not met.

19
00:01:18,760 --> 00:01:23,260
So our guard is going to check to see if whoever is making a request to a particular route is signed

20
00:01:23,260 --> 00:01:23,630
in.

21
00:01:23,650 --> 00:01:26,080
If they are not signed in, we're going to reject the request.

22
00:01:27,050 --> 00:01:27,860
For the second one.

23
00:01:27,860 --> 00:01:31,250
Something to automatically tell a handler who the currently signed in user is.

24
00:01:31,280 --> 00:01:33,540
We're going to have to create two different things.

25
00:01:33,560 --> 00:01:35,120
One is going to be the interceptor.

26
00:01:35,150 --> 00:01:36,920
The other is going to be a decorator.

27
00:01:37,610 --> 00:01:39,050
Now do these two different features.

28
00:01:39,050 --> 00:01:41,620
We're going to first work on that one.

29
00:01:41,630 --> 00:01:45,710
This is definitely the more complicated of the two, which is why we're going to take care of it first.

30
00:01:46,610 --> 00:01:47,870
So let's take a pause right here.

31
00:01:47,870 --> 00:01:52,280
When we come back, the next video, we're going to start working on a custom interceptor and decorator

32
00:01:52,280 --> 00:01:56,180
to automatically, automatically tell us who the currently signed in user is.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,950 --> 00:00:06,420
In this video, we're going to focus on making an interceptor and a decorator that will tell a handler

2
00:00:06,450 --> 00:00:09,270
very easily who the currently signed in user is.

3
00:00:09,360 --> 00:00:13,560
So let me first begin by giving you an example of what we're going to try to achieve here.

4
00:00:13,560 --> 00:00:18,240
Here is an example of how I want to use this interceptor and decorator that we are going to create.

5
00:00:18,600 --> 00:00:22,890
I'm going to comment out who am I very quickly and show you this example.

6
00:00:23,040 --> 00:00:26,370
So eventually I want to be able to write out something like that.

7
00:00:26,370 --> 00:00:29,400
Same route with the same method name.

8
00:00:30,430 --> 00:00:35,650
But then inside the method, rather than getting access to the session and then making use of the user

9
00:00:35,650 --> 00:00:41,920
service, I instead want to have our own custom parameter decorator called current user.

10
00:00:42,710 --> 00:00:45,590
So we're going to make a custom decorator called current User.

11
00:00:45,590 --> 00:00:51,650
And if we ever make use of that inside the argument list to a method, it should give us the currently

12
00:00:51,650 --> 00:00:53,520
signed in user as an argument.

13
00:00:53,540 --> 00:00:58,640
So then inside of here I could return the user, I could console, log them, I could make a follow

14
00:00:58,640 --> 00:01:01,690
up query to the database with that user, anything I want to do.

15
00:01:01,700 --> 00:01:07,010
But the point is we're going to make a custom decorator, and making a custom decorator by itself is

16
00:01:07,010 --> 00:01:08,710
its own interesting task.

17
00:01:08,720 --> 00:01:13,670
So the first thing we're going to do is make a very simple, straightforward custom decorator.

18
00:01:13,760 --> 00:01:18,350
We're still going to call it current user, but for right now it's just going to return some plain static

19
00:01:18,350 --> 00:01:21,110
data like a number or a string or something like that.

20
00:01:21,230 --> 00:01:25,610
Well, then come back once we've got the very basics of a decorator put together and actually worry

21
00:01:25,610 --> 00:01:27,110
about finding a user.

22
00:01:27,910 --> 00:01:28,150
Okay.

23
00:01:28,150 --> 00:01:31,450
So I'm going to leave this code right here for just a little bit because we are eventually going to

24
00:01:31,450 --> 00:01:32,380
make use of that.

25
00:01:32,380 --> 00:01:33,610
But for right now.

26
00:01:34,700 --> 00:01:37,670
I'm going to go back to my users directory inside there.

27
00:01:37,670 --> 00:01:40,700
I'm going to make a new folder called Decorators.

28
00:01:41,290 --> 00:01:48,430
And then inside of that directory I'll make a new file called current user dot decorator dot ts.

29
00:01:50,320 --> 00:01:54,910
Inside this new file at the very top, we're going to add in one import statement.

30
00:01:54,910 --> 00:01:58,330
For right now we're going to get create params.

31
00:01:58,360 --> 00:01:59,320
Decorator.

32
00:02:00,670 --> 00:02:03,070
And execution context.

33
00:02:03,160 --> 00:02:06,610
And both these are going to come from the Nestjs Common Library.

34
00:02:08,630 --> 00:02:12,350
Then inside of here I'm going to export const.

35
00:02:13,190 --> 00:02:14,630
Current user.

36
00:02:15,790 --> 00:02:18,490
And the value of this is going to be create params.

37
00:02:18,520 --> 00:02:19,570
Decorator.

38
00:02:22,440 --> 00:02:24,660
We're going to pass in a function to this thing.

39
00:02:27,800 --> 00:02:30,830
The arguments to this function are going to be something called data.

40
00:02:30,950 --> 00:02:36,500
We're going to give that a type of any and actually we'll call it contacts.

41
00:02:36,500 --> 00:02:38,990
That's actually what we call it elsewhere inside of our project.

42
00:02:38,990 --> 00:02:42,620
And its type is going to be execution context.

43
00:02:44,080 --> 00:02:49,270
Okay, So inside this function we're going to write out some amount of logic to inspect the incoming

44
00:02:49,270 --> 00:02:50,050
request.

45
00:02:50,080 --> 00:02:52,330
That's what this context argument essentially is.

46
00:02:52,360 --> 00:02:55,090
It is kind of a wrapper around the incoming request.

47
00:02:55,120 --> 00:03:01,510
The reason it's referred to as an execution context as opposed to just simply request is that this thing,

48
00:03:01,510 --> 00:03:08,290
this object right here, can be used to kind of abstract a websocket incoming message, a gRPC request,

49
00:03:08,290 --> 00:03:09,770
an Http request.

50
00:03:09,790 --> 00:03:12,240
A lot of different incoming kinds of requests.

51
00:03:12,250 --> 00:03:16,750
So rather than specifically calling this thing request, which would kind of imply that we're making

52
00:03:16,750 --> 00:03:20,920
use of Http, it is instead called execution context.

53
00:03:20,920 --> 00:03:27,670
And that allows us to write some code that might work equally well with WebSockets, gRPC, Http, GraphQL,

54
00:03:27,670 --> 00:03:29,860
or any other kind of communication protocol.

55
00:03:30,940 --> 00:03:34,120
But effectively, at the end of the day, it is the incoming request.

56
00:03:34,790 --> 00:03:35,720
The data argument.

57
00:03:35,720 --> 00:03:38,060
We're not going to worry about just too much just yet.

58
00:03:38,800 --> 00:03:43,840
So then inside this function, we are going to do some work on the incoming request.

59
00:03:43,870 --> 00:03:47,970
We're going to produce some amount of data or some information, and we're going to return it.

60
00:03:47,980 --> 00:03:54,280
Whatever we return from this function right here is going to show up as our user argument wherever we

61
00:03:54,280 --> 00:03:55,450
use that decorator.

62
00:03:56,020 --> 00:03:59,650
So let's go through a very fast and very simple example for right now.

63
00:04:00,320 --> 00:04:01,310
Inside this function.

64
00:04:01,310 --> 00:04:03,080
I'm going to return simply.

65
00:04:03,290 --> 00:04:04,070
Hi there.

66
00:04:04,400 --> 00:04:05,480
And that's it.

67
00:04:07,260 --> 00:04:10,620
Let's go back over to our controller now and make use of that decorator.

68
00:04:11,370 --> 00:04:15,150
So inside the controller at the very top, I'm going to import that decorator.

69
00:04:20,150 --> 00:04:21,560
From decorators.

70
00:04:21,830 --> 00:04:24,620
Current user decorator.

71
00:04:26,750 --> 00:04:28,220
And then down at this temporary.

72
00:04:28,220 --> 00:04:28,850
Who am I?

73
00:04:28,880 --> 00:04:30,300
Route that I put together.

74
00:04:30,320 --> 00:04:35,600
We're not actually returning a user anymore, so I'm going to update the type of that thing to be string.

75
00:04:37,060 --> 00:04:38,760
And then we'll still just return user.

76
00:04:38,770 --> 00:04:40,960
Now remember, in our case, user is not actually user.

77
00:04:40,960 --> 00:04:42,070
It is going to be the string.

78
00:04:42,070 --> 00:04:42,880
Hi there.

79
00:04:44,430 --> 00:04:44,760
Right now.

80
00:04:44,760 --> 00:04:46,290
Let's just go ahead and save this.

81
00:04:46,620 --> 00:04:50,910
Go back over to our client and we'll make a request to the Who am I?

82
00:04:50,940 --> 00:04:51,720
Route.

83
00:04:52,290 --> 00:04:54,990
So I'll make my request to who am I?

84
00:04:55,410 --> 00:04:56,790
And sure enough, we get back.

85
00:04:56,790 --> 00:04:57,570
Hi there.

86
00:04:58,210 --> 00:04:59,140
So that's it.

87
00:04:59,140 --> 00:05:03,170
That's the basics of putting together our own custom decorator.

88
00:05:03,190 --> 00:05:05,300
We're going to call create decorator.

89
00:05:05,320 --> 00:05:09,160
We pass in a function, and then whatever we return from, that function is going to show up as the

90
00:05:09,160 --> 00:05:11,110
argument to our route handler.

91
00:05:11,870 --> 00:05:16,790
So now that we've seen the basics of a decorator, well, unfortunately, from here it's only going

92
00:05:16,790 --> 00:05:18,840
to get a little bit more confusing.

93
00:05:18,890 --> 00:05:23,840
The reason is that you might have noticed I said back on this diagram, we need both a decorator and

94
00:05:23,840 --> 00:05:24,910
an interceptor.

95
00:05:24,920 --> 00:05:29,800
So unfortunately, we cannot really put together this entire decorator with just the decorator by itself.

96
00:05:29,810 --> 00:05:32,170
We're going to have to create an interceptor as well.

97
00:05:32,180 --> 00:05:34,280
But I'll tell you why that is in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:05,070
In the last video, I told you that I was going to mention what the data argument right here is all

2
00:00:05,070 --> 00:00:05,580
about.

3
00:00:05,610 --> 00:00:10,980
The data argument is going to contain any data or any argument that we provide to our decorator when

4
00:00:10,980 --> 00:00:12,180
we actually make use of it.

5
00:00:12,210 --> 00:00:17,010
So back inside of our controller, where we are making use of current user, I can provide say a number

6
00:00:17,010 --> 00:00:21,930
right here or maybe a string or an object or whatever I want to provide.

7
00:00:21,930 --> 00:00:27,630
Whatever we provide to the decorator is going to show up inside the decorator function as this first

8
00:00:27,630 --> 00:00:28,410
argument.

9
00:00:28,500 --> 00:00:34,590
So in our case, if we provide a string of Asdf right here, then data would be that same string would

10
00:00:34,620 --> 00:00:36,240
be the string of Asdf.

11
00:00:36,360 --> 00:00:40,580
In our case, our decorator does not need any arguments provided to it.

12
00:00:40,590 --> 00:00:43,890
The current user decorator is going to be just the decorator by itself.

13
00:00:43,890 --> 00:00:45,670
No arguments, nothing like that.

14
00:00:45,690 --> 00:00:50,850
So in our case, a better type annotation for data than any might be.

15
00:00:50,880 --> 00:00:51,720
Never.

16
00:00:51,960 --> 00:00:56,790
The type annotation of never means this value is never going to be used accessed in any way, shape

17
00:00:56,790 --> 00:00:57,540
or form.

18
00:00:57,900 --> 00:01:03,310
As soon as we change that to never back inside of our users controller, I'm now getting an error around

19
00:01:03,310 --> 00:01:04,980
providing Asdf right here.

20
00:01:04,990 --> 00:01:10,060
So that type annotation of never is a good signal to everyone else making use of our decorator that

21
00:01:10,060 --> 00:01:13,060
hey, don't give our decorator any arguments because it doesn't need them.

22
00:01:15,090 --> 00:01:17,160
Okay, so now back inside of our decorator.

23
00:01:17,190 --> 00:01:21,050
Now that we've got a better idea of how this thing works, all we have to do is return a value.

24
00:01:21,060 --> 00:01:22,020
That's pretty much it.

25
00:01:22,050 --> 00:01:27,390
Let's actually use this thing to actually fetch the current user and then provide that user as an argument

26
00:01:27,390 --> 00:01:28,640
to our route handler.

27
00:01:28,650 --> 00:01:32,820
So inside of our decorator, here's what we need to put this thing together.

28
00:01:32,850 --> 00:01:39,420
We need first the session object because the session object tells us the user ID of the person making

29
00:01:39,420 --> 00:01:40,470
a request to us.

30
00:01:41,180 --> 00:01:46,220
We also need a copy of the user's service because that service is what is going to allow us to go into

31
00:01:46,220 --> 00:01:48,980
our database and find a user with the given ID.

32
00:01:50,020 --> 00:01:53,230
So let's first focus on just getting at that session object.

33
00:01:53,260 --> 00:01:58,630
I want to look up the session object and I want to try to find our user ID property inside there.

34
00:02:00,300 --> 00:02:03,570
So to get our session object, we're going to make use of that context argument.

35
00:02:03,600 --> 00:02:07,050
Remember, context is kind of like a wrapper around the incoming request.

36
00:02:08,040 --> 00:02:13,680
So right above our return, I'm going to add in request is ctcss.

37
00:02:14,010 --> 00:02:18,330
Switch to Http and sorry, putting ctcss right.

38
00:02:18,330 --> 00:02:19,290
There should be context.

39
00:02:19,290 --> 00:02:20,190
That's better.

40
00:02:20,430 --> 00:02:24,180
Switch to http get request.

41
00:02:25,250 --> 00:02:29,150
So that is going to give us the underlying request that is coming into our application.

42
00:02:30,870 --> 00:02:37,110
Then to access the session, we would reference request dot session and then we can look up any property

43
00:02:37,110 --> 00:02:38,400
we want on the session.

44
00:02:38,400 --> 00:02:40,050
So for example, user ID.

45
00:02:41,460 --> 00:02:42,060
Right now.

46
00:02:42,060 --> 00:02:44,160
Let's just do a console log of that.

47
00:02:45,930 --> 00:02:47,100
So I'm going to save this.

48
00:02:47,510 --> 00:02:50,190
I'm making use of the decorator on who am I?

49
00:02:50,430 --> 00:02:52,110
So let's open up our client.

50
00:02:52,110 --> 00:02:53,900
We're going to sign into our application.

51
00:02:53,910 --> 00:02:58,080
We're going to make a request to this handler, and we're going to expect to see a console log of our

52
00:02:58,080 --> 00:02:59,220
current user ID.

53
00:03:00,510 --> 00:03:00,990
Okay.

54
00:03:00,990 --> 00:03:02,940
So back inside my client.

55
00:03:04,000 --> 00:03:07,360
I'm going to first make sure that I am signed in as an existing user.

56
00:03:07,660 --> 00:03:08,190
Okay.

57
00:03:08,200 --> 00:03:10,930
I am signed in as user with ID 19.

58
00:03:11,800 --> 00:03:14,080
I'll then make a request to who am I?

59
00:03:15,310 --> 00:03:15,820
I get back.

60
00:03:15,820 --> 00:03:16,210
Hi there.

61
00:03:16,210 --> 00:03:16,990
Totally fine.

62
00:03:16,990 --> 00:03:20,470
But let's go back over to our terminal and see what we got for that console log.

63
00:03:21,560 --> 00:03:22,940
So at my terminal, I can see.

64
00:03:22,970 --> 00:03:23,510
Perfect.

65
00:03:23,540 --> 00:03:25,490
We've got rid of 19.

66
00:03:26,320 --> 00:03:28,930
Okay, so that looks good inside of our decorator.

67
00:03:28,930 --> 00:03:34,390
We absolutely can get access to the session object and we can read off the user ID from it.

68
00:03:34,480 --> 00:03:39,760
So now the only thing to do is make use of the user service to look up the user with that given ID,

69
00:03:40,180 --> 00:03:44,200
and unfortunately that's where things are going to start to get challenging.

70
00:03:44,530 --> 00:03:49,480
Remember, our user service is a part of the dependency injection system.

71
00:03:49,570 --> 00:03:53,670
We can't just import the user service and create a new instance of it ourselves.

72
00:03:53,680 --> 00:03:59,620
The service makes use of the user's repository and that user's repository is set up only through dependency

73
00:03:59,620 --> 00:04:00,400
injection.

74
00:04:01,210 --> 00:04:02,770
Now, that's probably a reminder.

75
00:04:02,770 --> 00:04:05,410
We know that user service is a part of dependency injection.

76
00:04:05,410 --> 00:04:06,670
It makes use of the repository.

77
00:04:06,700 --> 00:04:08,020
We've gone over that quite a bit.

78
00:04:08,020 --> 00:04:09,940
But why does that matter in this case?

79
00:04:10,120 --> 00:04:16,530
Well, unfortunately, we cannot make use of dependency injection with a parameter decorator.

80
00:04:16,540 --> 00:04:17,620
That's what you and I are creating.

81
00:04:17,620 --> 00:04:23,560
We're making a parameter decorator, and this decorator cannot reach into the system in any way and

82
00:04:23,560 --> 00:04:27,070
try to get access to some instance of anything inside there.

83
00:04:27,250 --> 00:04:32,680
So we currently have a decorator that just plain can't get access to our users service.

84
00:04:32,980 --> 00:04:38,350
So although we can get the session object, we can get the user ID, we can't get the real thing that

85
00:04:38,350 --> 00:04:41,350
we need to make the query to find that user.

86
00:04:42,710 --> 00:04:44,570
Now, of course, there is a way to work around this.

87
00:04:44,570 --> 00:04:46,580
And that's why I told you a moment ago.

88
00:04:47,520 --> 00:04:50,790
That we have to create both a decorator and an interceptor.

89
00:04:50,790 --> 00:04:53,080
So the interceptor is going to solve this problem.

90
00:04:53,100 --> 00:04:54,480
Here's what we're going to do.

91
00:04:56,210 --> 00:04:59,510
So we're going to create an interceptor called current user.

92
00:04:59,540 --> 00:05:03,550
All interceptors that we make can be a part of the dependency injection system.

93
00:05:03,560 --> 00:05:07,620
So this interceptor is going to read in the session object.

94
00:05:07,640 --> 00:05:12,890
It's also going to read in through dependency injection, our user service.

95
00:05:13,100 --> 00:05:18,320
And then inside the interceptor, that is where we are going to do our lookup for the current user.

96
00:05:18,500 --> 00:05:21,420
And then eventually we're going to expose that value that we find.

97
00:05:21,440 --> 00:05:24,740
We're going to expose the user through our decorator.

98
00:05:24,770 --> 00:05:29,540
Now, that last step where we I'm saying that we're going to expose that user through the decorator.

99
00:05:29,540 --> 00:05:33,350
If that sounds confusing, I'm kind of leaving it intentionally vague for right now.

100
00:05:33,990 --> 00:05:38,430
So all I really want you to understand is that at this point in time, our decorator can't get access

101
00:05:38,430 --> 00:05:39,540
to dependency injection.

102
00:05:39,540 --> 00:05:41,850
So instead, we have to create this interceptor.

103
00:05:41,880 --> 00:05:46,290
The interceptor is going to be what is responsible for actually fetching the user out of our database.

104
00:05:46,290 --> 00:05:50,640
And we're going to take that user that we find and somehow communicate it over to the decorator.

105
00:05:50,640 --> 00:05:55,530
And the decorator in turn is going to provide the value to our route handler.

106
00:05:56,800 --> 00:05:57,430
Okay.

107
00:05:57,610 --> 00:06:02,440
So again, I know this probably seems vague right now and I kind of want it to be that way because the

108
00:06:02,440 --> 00:06:05,620
whole system is just a little bit it's a little bit confusing.

109
00:06:05,620 --> 00:06:06,850
I totally understand that.

110
00:06:06,850 --> 00:06:07,930
So let's take a pause right here.

111
00:06:07,930 --> 00:06:11,380
We're going to come back in just a moment and start putting together this interceptor.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:05,250
All right, my friends, in this video, we're going to work on this current user interceptor.

2
00:00:05,250 --> 00:00:09,900
So, again, its entire goal is to just take in that session object, find the user ID, and then find

3
00:00:09,900 --> 00:00:12,840
a user out of our database using the user's service.

4
00:00:12,840 --> 00:00:18,120
And we've already put together an interceptor once before inside of our SRC directory, the interceptors

5
00:00:18,120 --> 00:00:20,880
folder we call this thing serialize interceptor.

6
00:00:20,940 --> 00:00:25,650
So the new interceptor we are going to put together is going to be very similar in nature to this one.

7
00:00:25,650 --> 00:00:30,120
But the code inside of it, specifically inside the intercept function, won't be quite as bad.

8
00:00:30,980 --> 00:00:35,870
The new interceptor we're going to put together is going to be created inside of our users folder rather

9
00:00:35,870 --> 00:00:37,960
than the parent interceptors directory.

10
00:00:37,970 --> 00:00:40,070
And I'll show you why in just a little bit.

11
00:00:40,100 --> 00:00:44,480
So inside of users, I'm going to create a new folder called interceptors.

12
00:00:46,070 --> 00:00:53,240
And then inside of there we'll make a new file called Current dash User dot Interceptor dot ts.

13
00:00:55,590 --> 00:00:59,220
Then inside this file at the very top, I'm going to import.

14
00:00:59,950 --> 00:01:01,810
Nest interceptor.

15
00:01:03,150 --> 00:01:05,220
Execution context.

16
00:01:07,540 --> 00:01:11,140
And call handler and think that's all we need for right now.

17
00:01:11,140 --> 00:01:12,070
Oh, one more.

18
00:01:12,100 --> 00:01:13,600
We also need injectable.

19
00:01:14,620 --> 00:01:17,830
And this is all going to come from Nestjs Common.

20
00:01:19,230 --> 00:01:22,950
Then in addition, we're also going to get our users service because remember, that's what we're going

21
00:01:22,950 --> 00:01:25,140
to use to actually find the user we care about.

22
00:01:25,410 --> 00:01:29,010
So we'll add in import users service.

23
00:01:31,590 --> 00:01:34,680
From up one directory users service.

24
00:01:35,970 --> 00:01:41,670
So then finally we will export a class called Current User Interceptor.

25
00:01:42,320 --> 00:01:44,690
Just as we did with our last interceptor.

26
00:01:44,690 --> 00:01:45,980
We're also going to add in.

27
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:47,950
Implements.

28
00:01:49,730 --> 00:01:50,750
Nest interceptor.

29
00:01:50,750 --> 00:01:56,120
And remember, the entire goal of that is to make sure that we've got that kind of guide that tells

30
00:01:56,120 --> 00:01:59,150
us through TypeScript exactly how to put an interceptor together.

31
00:01:59,150 --> 00:02:04,040
So if we now mouse over current user interceptor, we're told we have to add in an intercept method

32
00:02:04,040 --> 00:02:06,920
if we want to use this thing as an interceptor at some point in time.

33
00:02:07,590 --> 00:02:12,630
So then inside of here, we're going to first make use of dependency injection to get access to a copy

34
00:02:12,630 --> 00:02:14,240
of the user's service.

35
00:02:14,250 --> 00:02:16,140
So we'll add in constructor.

36
00:02:18,080 --> 00:02:18,920
Private.

37
00:02:20,040 --> 00:02:24,600
User service is going to be an instance of users service.

38
00:02:25,830 --> 00:02:27,870
To make use of dependency injection at all.

39
00:02:27,870 --> 00:02:30,370
We also have to mark the class as injectable.

40
00:02:30,390 --> 00:02:33,690
So right above we'll put in injectable.

41
00:02:35,810 --> 00:02:41,030
So then we can define our asynchronous intercept method.

42
00:02:42,760 --> 00:02:44,740
This will be called with context.

43
00:02:46,680 --> 00:02:48,240
And our handler.

44
00:02:50,560 --> 00:02:56,350
So quick reminder on interceptors context right here that's very similar to the decorator context.

45
00:02:56,350 --> 00:02:58,510
It's like a wrapper around the incoming request.

46
00:02:58,540 --> 00:03:03,370
We've also got Handler, and that's a reference to the actual route handler that is going to run at

47
00:03:03,370 --> 00:03:04,330
some point in time.

48
00:03:04,660 --> 00:03:10,660
So now inside of here, between context and user service, we're going to find our user.

49
00:03:10,690 --> 00:03:14,530
So we're going to write out some code that looks very similar to what we just did inside of our decorator.

50
00:03:14,630 --> 00:03:18,100
I'll say request is context.

51
00:03:18,490 --> 00:03:20,530
Switch to Http.

52
00:03:22,720 --> 00:03:23,980
Yet request.

53
00:03:25,260 --> 00:03:28,230
I'm then going to find the user ID from inside the session object.

54
00:03:28,230 --> 00:03:33,000
So we'll say user ID is request dot session.

55
00:03:37,320 --> 00:03:40,510
We're then going to check and make sure that the user ID is actually defined.

56
00:03:40,530 --> 00:03:45,960
So if the user ID is not defined, if it's false or null or undefined or something like that, we don't

57
00:03:45,960 --> 00:03:50,490
even need to make a query with the user service because the user is definitely not signed in.

58
00:03:50,790 --> 00:03:57,420
So we're going to say if user ID does exist, if it is defined, let's go ahead and make a query with

59
00:03:57,420 --> 00:03:58,470
the user service.

60
00:03:58,500 --> 00:04:00,600
Otherwise, just continue on.

61
00:04:01,020 --> 00:04:07,050
So in the continue on case down here, outside the if statement, we will return and alert handle.

62
00:04:07,050 --> 00:04:11,430
And remember, that essentially means just go ahead and run the actual route handler.

63
00:04:12,280 --> 00:04:13,900
So if we did have a user ID.

64
00:04:15,340 --> 00:04:17,350
Let's go ahead and try to find our user.

65
00:04:18,850 --> 00:04:20,620
With the user's service.

66
00:04:20,890 --> 00:04:24,400
So we'll find one and pass in the user ID.

67
00:04:25,610 --> 00:04:28,940
So now we've got the user, but what are we going to actually do with it?

68
00:04:28,970 --> 00:04:30,530
Well, here's the kind of trick.

69
00:04:30,530 --> 00:04:32,270
Here's where I just mentioned a moment ago.

70
00:04:32,270 --> 00:04:36,070
We need to somehow communicate the user we found down into the decorator.

71
00:04:36,080 --> 00:04:41,630
So the little trick we're going to use is to just take the user we found and assign it to the request

72
00:04:41,630 --> 00:04:47,270
object because the request object can be retrieved or we can get access to that object inside of our

73
00:04:47,270 --> 00:04:47,690
decorator.

74
00:04:47,690 --> 00:04:49,640
As a matter of fact, we already did right there.

75
00:04:49,880 --> 00:04:52,310
So we're going to take the user we just found.

76
00:04:53,030 --> 00:04:54,890
Inside of our interceptor.

77
00:04:55,720 --> 00:05:00,100
And we will assign it to request dot current user like so.

78
00:05:02,260 --> 00:05:05,980
So then later on down the line when our decorator actually runs.

79
00:05:06,100 --> 00:05:07,510
So here's the decorator.

80
00:05:08,470 --> 00:05:11,920
We're going to take that request object and we're going to return from it.

81
00:05:13,230 --> 00:05:15,570
Request dot current user.

82
00:05:16,950 --> 00:05:17,700
And that's the key.

83
00:05:17,700 --> 00:05:18,720
That's the secret.

84
00:05:21,030 --> 00:05:26,310
So inside the interceptor, do all the dependency injection stuff, pull out the session, get the user

85
00:05:26,310 --> 00:05:32,310
ID, find the user using the service, sign it to the request, and then inside the decorator just look

86
00:05:32,310 --> 00:05:34,080
up the request, find current user and return it.

87
00:05:34,080 --> 00:05:34,800
That's all.

88
00:05:35,040 --> 00:05:39,120
So now, at this point in time, you might be curious why are we making use of this interceptor?

89
00:05:39,120 --> 00:05:40,720
Why are we making use of the decorator?

90
00:05:40,740 --> 00:05:43,170
Couldn't we just use the interceptor by itself?

91
00:05:43,320 --> 00:05:45,210
Well, yes, we absolutely could.

92
00:05:45,240 --> 00:05:50,040
But remember, one of the requirements of our controller one one of the things that I said was that

93
00:05:50,040 --> 00:05:55,850
I want to specifically have a decorator called Current User, and that thing should give us our user.

94
00:05:55,860 --> 00:06:00,930
If we did not have the decorator, and if we only had the interceptor, then we would have to write

95
00:06:00,930 --> 00:06:01,890
out some code that looks like this.

96
00:06:01,920 --> 00:06:08,070
Instead, we'd have to say at request that would give us access to the incoming request object.

97
00:06:10,910 --> 00:06:14,160
And then inside of here we could say request dot current user.

98
00:06:14,180 --> 00:06:15,950
So it's just a little bit more tedious.

99
00:06:15,980 --> 00:06:18,350
We have to make use of this kind of mysterious decorator.

100
00:06:18,770 --> 00:06:21,160
We have to access current user on the request object.

101
00:06:21,170 --> 00:06:23,290
There's just a little bit more going on here.

102
00:06:23,300 --> 00:06:29,540
So personally, I think that making use of the extra decorator just makes it super crystal clear what

103
00:06:29,540 --> 00:06:30,380
this argument is.

104
00:06:30,380 --> 00:06:31,460
It is the current user.

105
00:06:31,460 --> 00:06:32,330
That's it.

106
00:06:33,860 --> 00:06:36,200
Okay, So this interceptor is now good.

107
00:06:36,200 --> 00:06:37,340
It is ready to go.

108
00:06:37,340 --> 00:06:40,940
But before we make use of it, we have to wire it up to our application.

109
00:06:40,970 --> 00:06:47,060
You might recall that we previously made use of our serialize interceptor back inside of our controller.

110
00:06:47,800 --> 00:06:52,720
By wiring it up at the very top through the use of a serialized decorator.

111
00:06:53,810 --> 00:06:57,170
In this case, we have an interceptor that is making use of dependency injection.

112
00:06:57,170 --> 00:07:01,400
So we're going to wire it up to our application in a slightly different way than we did the last time

113
00:07:01,400 --> 00:07:02,060
around.

114
00:07:02,150 --> 00:07:03,550
So let's take a pause right here.

115
00:07:03,560 --> 00:07:06,770
We're going to wire up this interceptor to the rest of our app in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,480 --> 00:00:04,510
Let's take a look at how we're going to use our interceptor and decorator together.

2
00:00:04,540 --> 00:00:08,890
Now, the key thing to keep in mind here is that whenever we want to use that decorator, we first have

3
00:00:08,890 --> 00:00:10,470
to run the interceptor.

4
00:00:10,480 --> 00:00:15,640
So the interceptor has to run first to make sure that we assign the current user to the request object.

5
00:00:15,670 --> 00:00:21,550
After the interceptor runs, then our decorator can run and return request dot current user.

6
00:00:22,970 --> 00:00:23,330
Okay.

7
00:00:23,330 --> 00:00:26,660
So to make use of this thing, I'm going to show you two different methodologies.

8
00:00:26,690 --> 00:00:29,930
The first way is going to be a little bit tedious to reuse throughout our project.

9
00:00:29,930 --> 00:00:34,490
The second way is going to be a lot easier to use, but a little bit more confusing on what's going

10
00:00:34,490 --> 00:00:35,600
on behind the scenes.

11
00:00:35,660 --> 00:00:41,450
So to make use of our interceptor, I'm going to first go back over to our users module.

12
00:00:41,750 --> 00:00:46,730
Inside the users module, we need to wire up that interceptor to our list of providers.

13
00:00:46,730 --> 00:00:51,950
Remember, this is an integral step using dependency injection to use dependency injection, we first

14
00:00:51,950 --> 00:00:57,200
mark a class as injectable with that decorator and then add the class to the list of providers inside

15
00:00:57,200 --> 00:00:58,040
of a module.

16
00:00:58,460 --> 00:01:03,950
So inside this file at the top, I'm going to import current user interceptor.

17
00:01:05,710 --> 00:01:07,330
From interceptors.

18
00:01:07,630 --> 00:01:10,750
Current Dash User Interceptor.

19
00:01:12,880 --> 00:01:15,610
I'll then add in that class to the list of providers.

20
00:01:16,030 --> 00:01:16,720
Here we go.

21
00:01:18,860 --> 00:01:19,160
Okay.

22
00:01:19,160 --> 00:01:24,260
So now to make use of that interceptor, we're going to go into our users controller.

23
00:01:25,750 --> 00:01:26,770
At the very top.

24
00:01:27,070 --> 00:01:33,340
I'm going to find our import from Nestjs common and add in use interceptors.

25
00:01:33,550 --> 00:01:38,140
Remember we made use of this decorator a little bit ago when we were first putting together all that

26
00:01:38,140 --> 00:01:43,840
session serialized stuff so we can apply this decorator to an entire controller and that makes sure

27
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:48,670
that we're going to run a given interceptor before any handler inside of that controller.

28
00:01:49,750 --> 00:01:54,970
Besides just that decorator, we're also going to imply or import our interceptor.

29
00:01:55,120 --> 00:01:58,450
So current user interceptor.

30
00:02:00,450 --> 00:02:01,920
From interceptors.

31
00:02:03,250 --> 00:02:04,150
Current user.

32
00:02:05,370 --> 00:02:06,300
Interceptor.

33
00:02:10,410 --> 00:02:19,830
Then right above our controller, we will add in use interceptors and then current user interceptor.

34
00:02:20,400 --> 00:02:21,480
And that's it.

35
00:02:22,920 --> 00:02:27,000
So now whenever a request comes in to anything inside of our users controller, we're going to run that

36
00:02:27,000 --> 00:02:32,070
interceptor, pull out the user ID from the session object, find the user from our database and assign

37
00:02:32,070 --> 00:02:33,330
it to the request object.

38
00:02:33,970 --> 00:02:38,440
The request is then going to go on to one of our request handlers inside the controller.

39
00:02:38,770 --> 00:02:43,090
And if we are making use of the current user decorator, that decorator should pull off the current

40
00:02:43,090 --> 00:02:47,110
user and give us give it to us as an argument called in this case user.

41
00:02:47,590 --> 00:02:52,460
I'm going to update the annotation of user right here because well, it really is a user entity.

42
00:02:52,480 --> 00:02:53,800
It's definitely not a string.

43
00:02:53,890 --> 00:02:57,130
So let's mark this as being of type user.

44
00:02:58,270 --> 00:03:02,530
So that's supposed to be user entity to get a reference or to make use of that type, we do have to

45
00:03:02,530 --> 00:03:05,490
import the user entity class into this file as well.

46
00:03:05,500 --> 00:03:06,970
So let's take care of that very quickly.

47
00:03:06,970 --> 00:03:09,040
At the top I will import.

48
00:03:09,850 --> 00:03:10,660
User.

49
00:03:11,870 --> 00:03:14,930
From entities or do we create an entities directory?

50
00:03:14,960 --> 00:03:15,930
No, we did not.

51
00:03:15,950 --> 00:03:18,680
So it's just user dot entity.

52
00:03:18,710 --> 00:03:19,400
There we go.

53
00:03:21,970 --> 00:03:22,450
Okay.

54
00:03:22,450 --> 00:03:24,580
So that should be it.

55
00:03:24,640 --> 00:03:27,130
Think we're now ready for a test at long last.

56
00:03:27,370 --> 00:03:30,520
As usual, I'm going to open up my client.

57
00:03:31,290 --> 00:03:33,480
I'm going to make sure that I am signed in.

58
00:03:33,780 --> 00:03:34,040
Okay.

59
00:03:34,050 --> 00:03:35,160
Definitely signed in.

60
00:03:35,830 --> 00:03:39,100
I'm then going to attempt to get the currently signed in user.

61
00:03:39,250 --> 00:03:40,690
So I'll send the request.

62
00:03:41,400 --> 00:03:45,270
And sure enough I get my currently signed in user that looks pretty good.

63
00:03:45,480 --> 00:03:50,970
If I now sign out and then try to get the currently signed in user once again, I get back an empty

64
00:03:50,970 --> 00:03:51,780
response.

65
00:03:52,520 --> 00:03:54,120
Okay, Let's say this looks pretty good.

66
00:03:54,140 --> 00:03:55,760
Definitely works the way we expect.

67
00:03:56,350 --> 00:03:58,990
