However, there is a downside to it.

68
00:03:58,990 --> 00:04:03,790
And that downside is that now anytime we want to use that decorator, we have to import the current

69
00:04:03,790 --> 00:04:04,840
user interceptor.

70
00:04:04,870 --> 00:04:10,060
We have to import use interceptors and we have to apply this entire thing onto our controller.

71
00:04:11,010 --> 00:04:16,230
Now, if we eventually have, say, 10 or 15 controllers inside of our application, that's a lot of

72
00:04:16,230 --> 00:04:21,000
duplicated code all over the place because we're going to have to add in the import.

73
00:04:21,000 --> 00:04:24,990
And that call right there in every one of our different controllers, wherever we want to figure out

74
00:04:24,990 --> 00:04:26,490
who the current user is.

75
00:04:26,520 --> 00:04:28,220
Definitely not ideal.

76
00:04:28,230 --> 00:04:29,760
So let's take a pause right here.

77
00:04:29,760 --> 00:04:33,840
When we come back in next video, I'm going to show you the second way of wiring up this interceptor

78
00:04:33,840 --> 00:04:34,680
that I mentioned.

79
00:04:34,710 --> 00:04:38,370
So it's going to make this entire process just a little bit more straightforward.

80
00:04:38,670 --> 00:04:43,080
So let's pause right here and take a look at a better way of using an interceptor in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,270 --> 00:00:04,270
Right now, our interceptor is being applied to one single controller.

2
00:00:04,270 --> 00:00:09,730
So if we start to add in some additional controllers, we'd have to make use of that same use interceptors

3
00:00:09,730 --> 00:00:14,860
line inside of every single one of these different controllers, because the interceptor is only being

4
00:00:14,860 --> 00:00:19,140
applied to one controller at a time, we'd refer to it as being controller scoped.

5
00:00:19,150 --> 00:00:22,900
So this instance of the interceptor is being applied to that controller.

6
00:00:22,930 --> 00:00:25,120
This one is applied to that one and so on.

7
00:00:25,450 --> 00:00:30,820
This is very tedious because we just have to apply the interceptor all over the place over our entire

8
00:00:30,820 --> 00:00:31,660
application.

9
00:00:31,660 --> 00:00:37,600
So let's take a look at a better way of setting this up rather than having a controller scoped interceptor,

10
00:00:37,600 --> 00:00:41,530
we're going to make our interceptor globally scoped instead.

11
00:00:41,530 --> 00:00:47,380
So the one copy, one single instance of our interceptor is going to be applied to every request that

12
00:00:47,380 --> 00:00:51,580
comes into our application, regardless of what controller that request is going to.

13
00:00:52,390 --> 00:00:57,100
That's going to significantly, significantly simplify the amount of code or the amount of times we

14
00:00:57,100 --> 00:01:00,310
have to set up the interceptor, which is definitely going to be great.

15
00:01:01,210 --> 00:01:01,570
Okay.

16
00:01:01,570 --> 00:01:05,160
So to make this thing work, we're going to go back over to our controller.

17
00:01:05,170 --> 00:01:09,250
We're going to find that use interceptors line at the top and just delete it.

18
00:01:10,710 --> 00:01:14,490
I'll then also delete the import for current user interceptor.

19
00:01:15,130 --> 00:01:18,760
And will also delete the import for use interceptors.

20
00:01:19,470 --> 00:01:24,240
So now if we make a request to our application, our decorator is not going to work as expected because

21
00:01:24,240 --> 00:01:27,720
we are currently not taking a look at the session and finding the relevant user.

22
00:01:28,990 --> 00:01:31,390
To reapply our interceptor on a global scale.

23
00:01:31,390 --> 00:01:33,760
We're going to go back over to our users module.

24
00:01:34,680 --> 00:01:35,790
Inside of here.

25
00:01:35,790 --> 00:01:43,440
I'm going to go up to the top and add in a new import statement for a very special thing called app

26
00:01:43,620 --> 00:01:47,160
interceptor, all capitals with a lowercase in between.

27
00:01:48,250 --> 00:01:52,240
And we're going to import this from Nestjs core.

28
00:01:54,180 --> 00:01:56,640
I'm then going to find my providers array right here.

29
00:01:57,970 --> 00:02:00,940
I'm going to find where we added in current user interceptor.

30
00:02:02,070 --> 00:02:04,530
I'm going to wrap that thing with an object.

31
00:02:05,440 --> 00:02:10,300
And assign the interceptor to a property called use class.

32
00:02:11,550 --> 00:02:15,300
And then I'm also right above that, going to put in another property called provide.

33
00:02:16,070 --> 00:02:16,520
App.

34
00:02:17,390 --> 00:02:18,680
Interceptor.

35
00:02:19,580 --> 00:02:20,660
Inter.

36
00:02:21,330 --> 00:02:21,860
Scepter.

37
00:02:21,960 --> 00:02:22,650
There we go.

38
00:02:23,300 --> 00:02:26,720
So that is how we set up a globally scoped interceptor.

39
00:02:26,750 --> 00:02:31,910
We apply an object or we add an object to the providers array that has a provide property and a use

40
00:02:31,910 --> 00:02:33,110
class property.

41
00:02:33,140 --> 00:02:38,450
So now any request that comes in anywhere to our application, not just controllers that are defined

42
00:02:38,450 --> 00:02:43,370
inside this module, but anywhere inside of our application will have this interceptor applied to it.

43
00:02:44,090 --> 00:02:47,520
So let's save these different files and go try to make another request.

44
00:02:47,540 --> 00:02:48,080
Now.

45
00:02:49,410 --> 00:02:53,310
So if I try to get my currently signed in user, I'm currently not signed in, so that doesn't work.

46
00:02:53,310 --> 00:02:54,600
Let me first sign in.

47
00:02:54,630 --> 00:02:55,380
There we go.

48
00:02:55,380 --> 00:02:57,780
And now if I get my currently signed in user.

49
00:02:57,810 --> 00:02:59,280
Yep, we're all set.

50
00:03:00,340 --> 00:03:01,720
I can sign out.

51
00:03:02,670 --> 00:03:04,170
If I make that request again.

52
00:03:04,650 --> 00:03:05,820
Empty response.

53
00:03:05,850 --> 00:03:06,690
Perfect.

54
00:03:07,600 --> 00:03:07,930
Okay.

55
00:03:07,930 --> 00:03:10,280
So that is a globally scoped interceptor.

56
00:03:10,300 --> 00:03:14,380
Now, the one downside to this approach is that there might be some controllers out there that don't

57
00:03:14,380 --> 00:03:16,330
care about who the current user is.

58
00:03:16,630 --> 00:03:21,370
In those cases, we are going to make a request to our database to find the current user, even though

59
00:03:21,370 --> 00:03:25,900
the given controller might not care about it, or even individual handlers might not care about the

60
00:03:25,900 --> 00:03:26,720
current user.

61
00:03:26,740 --> 00:03:29,570
So there are definitely upsides and downsides to this approach.

62
00:03:29,590 --> 00:03:34,450
We are kind of over fetching data in some cases, but it definitely makes our app easier to develop

63
00:03:34,450 --> 00:03:35,080
for.

64
00:03:36,280 --> 00:03:38,750
Okay, So let's say this is looking pretty good right now.

65
00:03:38,770 --> 00:03:42,910
So now the very last piece of our authentication system we have to add in.

66
00:03:42,940 --> 00:03:47,930
We need to make sure that we can reject requests to certain handlers if the user is not signed in.

67
00:03:47,950 --> 00:03:51,790
So let's take care of that very last step around authentication in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,930 --> 00:00:03,160
Let's get started on creating a guard.

2
00:00:03,180 --> 00:00:07,530
So remember, this is going to be something that allows us to prevent access to certain controllers

3
00:00:07,530 --> 00:00:11,160
or route handlers based upon whether or not the user is signed in.

4
00:00:11,580 --> 00:00:15,030
A guard in general is implemented by creating a class.

5
00:00:15,060 --> 00:00:18,000
This class must have a method called can activate.

6
00:00:18,030 --> 00:00:21,600
This method is going to be called automatically anytime there's an incoming request.

7
00:00:21,600 --> 00:00:27,090
And our goal is to look at that request and either return a truthy value or a falsy value.

8
00:00:27,120 --> 00:00:30,900
If we return a value that is truthy in nature, then the request can go through.

9
00:00:30,930 --> 00:00:36,810
Otherwise, if we return something that is falsy such as undefined null zero empty string, false,

10
00:00:36,810 --> 00:00:39,720
and so on, then the request will be automatically rejected.

11
00:00:40,370 --> 00:00:42,800
Once we create a guard, there are several different locations.

12
00:00:42,800 --> 00:00:43,830
We can apply it.

13
00:00:43,850 --> 00:00:49,760
So we can make use of a guard to prevent any traffic or kind of guard traffic from coming into our entire

14
00:00:49,760 --> 00:00:50,660
application.

15
00:00:50,750 --> 00:00:55,850
We can apply a guard to an individual controller, which would mean that it would limit access to all

16
00:00:55,850 --> 00:00:57,360
the different handlers inside there.

17
00:00:57,380 --> 00:01:01,370
And finally, we can also apply guards to individual handlers themselves.

18
00:01:01,610 --> 00:01:04,430
So very similar to interceptors, three different locations.

19
00:01:04,430 --> 00:01:05,660
We can apply these things.

20
00:01:06,790 --> 00:01:11,170
Well, without further ado, let's go back over to our editor and get started on this authentication

21
00:01:11,170 --> 00:01:11,860
guard.

22
00:01:13,480 --> 00:01:19,570
Cibulkova inside my editor inside of my SRC directory, I'm going to make a new folder called Guards.

23
00:01:19,720 --> 00:01:24,180
And then inside there I'll make a new file called Off Guards.

24
00:01:24,920 --> 00:01:25,640
At the very top.

25
00:01:25,640 --> 00:01:31,610
We will immediately add in some imports from Nestjs common.

26
00:01:31,910 --> 00:01:38,300
In this case we're going to get can activate and execution context.

27
00:01:39,870 --> 00:01:44,520
We will then export a new class called Auth Guard.

28
00:01:45,450 --> 00:01:48,150
Very similar to our interceptor, which we defined a little bit ago.

29
00:01:48,180 --> 00:01:50,550
We're also going to add on the implements keyword on here.

30
00:01:50,550 --> 00:01:53,760
And in this case, we're going to try to implement can activate.

31
00:01:55,250 --> 00:01:59,980
Can activate is an interface once again, very similar to interface to interceptors.

32
00:01:59,990 --> 00:02:04,310
This is just going to make sure that we define all the appropriate functions on our class so that it

33
00:02:04,310 --> 00:02:06,740
actually will behave as a guard correctly.

34
00:02:06,740 --> 00:02:11,720
In this case, if we mouse over the error, it now tells us that we have to define a can activate function.

35
00:02:12,050 --> 00:02:14,210
So I'm going to define that function right away.

36
00:02:16,090 --> 00:02:21,370
This function is going to be called with something we call context, and it will be of type execution

37
00:02:21,370 --> 00:02:22,240
context.

38
00:02:22,420 --> 00:02:25,060
This is the third time we've seen this argument type.

39
00:02:25,060 --> 00:02:31,310
So remember, this is very similar to kind of like a request coming into an Http based application.

40
00:02:31,330 --> 00:02:36,700
The reason we've got this execution context as opposed to a plain request is because this auth guard

41
00:02:36,730 --> 00:02:40,570
in theory could be used with different communication protocols, but we're not going to worry about

42
00:02:40,570 --> 00:02:41,920
that too much for right now.

43
00:02:42,850 --> 00:02:47,980
So now inside of here, we need to take a look at that incoming request and then we can decide whether

44
00:02:47,980 --> 00:02:49,690
or not to allow the request through.

45
00:02:49,750 --> 00:02:54,010
So in our case, we want to reject the request if a user is not signed in.

46
00:02:54,130 --> 00:03:00,040
So we're going to use the presence of a user ID property on the user's session to decide whether or

47
00:03:00,040 --> 00:03:01,150
not they are signed in.

48
00:03:02,160 --> 00:03:06,750
So inside of here, the first thing we're going to do is see if the user or excuse me, we're going

49
00:03:06,750 --> 00:03:08,450
to get that session property.

50
00:03:08,460 --> 00:03:11,160
So we will say request.

51
00:03:12,500 --> 00:03:13,580
Is context.

52
00:03:14,060 --> 00:03:18,170
Switch to http dot request.

53
00:03:18,800 --> 00:03:19,160
Excuse me.

54
00:03:19,160 --> 00:03:21,180
I think that's git request.

55
00:03:21,200 --> 00:03:22,010
That's better.

56
00:03:24,520 --> 00:03:28,780
We will then return request dot session user id.

57
00:03:29,750 --> 00:03:31,760
So if the ID exists, great.

58
00:03:31,760 --> 00:03:33,050
That is a truthy value.

59
00:03:33,080 --> 00:03:34,670
We're going to return that from this function.

60
00:03:34,670 --> 00:03:38,300
And that means the user can access whatever route we've applied the guard to.

61
00:03:38,480 --> 00:03:44,450
Otherwise, if user ID is false, null undefined or anything like that, then that will be a falsy value.

62
00:03:44,450 --> 00:03:47,870
So that will prevent access to a given handler or controller.

63
00:03:48,640 --> 00:03:50,300
And that's pretty much it for our guard.

64
00:03:50,320 --> 00:03:51,580
That's all we have to add in.

65
00:03:51,910 --> 00:03:57,220
So now we need to go to some controller or some route handler or remember even the entire application

66
00:03:57,220 --> 00:03:58,870
and actually apply this guard.

67
00:03:59,110 --> 00:04:05,140
So let's go into our our user's controller and we're going to try to apply this guard to one single

68
00:04:05,140 --> 00:04:06,730
handler just to test it out.

69
00:04:08,420 --> 00:04:11,420
Back inside of my users controller.

70
00:04:12,530 --> 00:04:15,440
At the very top I'm going to import.

71
00:04:16,670 --> 00:04:17,810
Off guard.

72
00:04:19,050 --> 00:04:20,220
From up one directory.

73
00:04:20,250 --> 00:04:21,210
Guards.

74
00:04:21,420 --> 00:04:22,890
Off guard.

75
00:04:23,520 --> 00:04:28,480
And then we also have to add in an import statement from Nestjs Common as well.

76
00:04:28,500 --> 00:04:33,360
So in this case, very similar to what we did back on interceptors, we're going to import something

77
00:04:33,360 --> 00:04:34,620
called use guards.

78
00:04:34,650 --> 00:04:39,990
This is a decorator that allows us to say run some guard before sending a request off either to the

79
00:04:39,990 --> 00:04:42,510
entire controller or to a particular handler.

80
00:04:43,750 --> 00:04:48,250
Now, just to test out our auth guard, let's try restricting access to who am I?

81
00:04:48,280 --> 00:04:53,170
So we're going to say that you can only access this route handler if you are signed in to our application.

82
00:04:53,850 --> 00:05:01,260
So I'm going to apply use guards and I'm going to pass to it our off guard.

83
00:05:01,910 --> 00:05:03,260
And that should be it.

84
00:05:03,350 --> 00:05:08,000
So now if a user is not signed in, they won't be able to access this route handler and we're going

85
00:05:08,000 --> 00:05:09,440
to send them back automatically.

86
00:05:09,470 --> 00:05:14,240
A response with a status of 403, which means, sorry, but you can't access this route.

87
00:05:14,390 --> 00:05:18,710
Otherwise, if they are signed in, then we're going to allow them access to the route and we're going

88
00:05:18,710 --> 00:05:21,500
to send back the user that they are currently signed in as.

89
00:05:22,540 --> 00:05:25,480
Well, let's open up our client and do a quick test.

90
00:05:27,660 --> 00:05:29,880
So I'm going to first sign into my application.

91
00:05:30,060 --> 00:05:30,320
Okay.

92
00:05:30,330 --> 00:05:31,500
Definitely signed in.

93
00:05:31,800 --> 00:05:35,790
I'm then going to try to do the Who am I Root.

94
00:05:36,390 --> 00:05:38,140
So it looks like that worked just fine.

95
00:05:38,160 --> 00:05:39,330
Just to show you for sure.

96
00:05:39,330 --> 00:05:39,660
Yep.

97
00:05:39,660 --> 00:05:41,370
Definitely making that request just fine.

98
00:05:41,670 --> 00:05:44,280
But if I then sign out of my application.

99
00:05:45,390 --> 00:05:51,720
Okay, I'm now signed out and if I try to go to who am I again, I'm going to get a 403.

100
00:05:52,050 --> 00:05:52,650
Perfect.

101
00:05:52,650 --> 00:05:54,270
That is exactly what we want.

102
00:05:55,290 --> 00:06:00,870
Well, believe it or not, at long last, after a lot of work inside this section, we've now got our

103
00:06:00,870 --> 00:06:03,030
full authentication service put together.

104
00:06:03,420 --> 00:06:06,480
So we've got the ability to handle.

105
00:06:07,600 --> 00:06:10,150
Let's go back to this diagram right here.

106
00:06:10,180 --> 00:06:11,290
Nope, not that one.

107
00:06:11,650 --> 00:06:12,430
Where'd it go?

108
00:06:12,460 --> 00:06:13,750
Okay, that one will work just fine.

109
00:06:13,750 --> 00:06:19,030
So we've now got the ability to sign up, sign in, sign out, get whoever is currently signed in.

110
00:06:19,030 --> 00:06:22,840
And we've also got some tools to be used inside of other controllers as well.

111
00:06:22,840 --> 00:06:26,620
So we can reject incoming request if a user is not signed in.

112
00:06:26,620 --> 00:06:32,050
And we've also got a very handy little decorator to figure out exactly who is signed in very easily

113
00:06:32,050 --> 00:06:33,460
inside of a handler.

114
00:06:33,700 --> 00:06:38,200
So that is pretty much it for our authentication system in terms of new features.

115
00:06:38,940 --> 00:06:42,920
I do want to take a look very quickly at adding in some tests, some automated tests around our auth

116
00:06:42,930 --> 00:06:43,470
system.

117
00:06:43,470 --> 00:06:45,720
So let's take a stab at that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
==================================================================================


# section 12: Getting Started with Unit Testing

1
00:00:00,770 --> 00:00:05,270
We've now written out a tremendous amount of code inside of our project and we've been doing only manual

2
00:00:05,270 --> 00:00:06,520
testing along the way.

3
00:00:06,530 --> 00:00:08,480
So we're going to start to change that In this video.

4
00:00:08,480 --> 00:00:11,510
We're going to start to take a look at automated testing inside of Nest.

5
00:00:11,550 --> 00:00:15,530
I first want to begin by describing the different kinds of testing that we usually do inside of a Nest

6
00:00:15,530 --> 00:00:16,310
project.

7
00:00:16,310 --> 00:00:18,320
So first we have unit testing.

8
00:00:18,320 --> 00:00:23,300
This is where we take a look at just one class at a time and make sure that every method of that class

9
00:00:23,300 --> 00:00:24,680
works as expected.

10
00:00:24,980 --> 00:00:28,720
We also do integration or end to end testing as well.

11
00:00:28,730 --> 00:00:33,140
Integration or end to end testing is where we launch a full copy of our application.

12
00:00:33,140 --> 00:00:36,980
We make requests to it and we ensure that we get the appropriate response back.

13
00:00:37,280 --> 00:00:42,500
We're going to first focus on unit testing because you're going to understand a lot more around dependency

14
00:00:42,500 --> 00:00:46,780
injection and why it exists in general once you see unit testing in action.

15
00:00:46,790 --> 00:00:49,250
So with that in mind, let's go back over to our code editor.

16
00:00:49,250 --> 00:00:50,570
A couple of things I want to show you.

17
00:00:51,930 --> 00:00:53,940
The first inside of our root project directory.

18
00:00:53,940 --> 00:00:55,650
There is a test directory.

19
00:00:55,710 --> 00:00:58,520
Inside of this folder there are two different files.

20
00:00:58,530 --> 00:00:59,970
Let's open up that app.

21
00:00:59,970 --> 00:01:02,370
Dot E2E spec file.

22
00:01:02,490 --> 00:01:06,450
This is an example of an integration or end to end test.

23
00:01:06,450 --> 00:01:10,050
So in this file there are some code to start up a copy of our application.

24
00:01:10,050 --> 00:01:15,210
So an actual web server and then make requests to it and make sure we get the appropriate response back.

25
00:01:15,780 --> 00:01:18,230
So we're not worried about end to end tests just yet.

26
00:01:18,300 --> 00:01:23,090
Instead, we are going to be writing out unit tests which are placed inside of files inside of our SRC

27
00:01:23,130 --> 00:01:23,940
directory.

28
00:01:24,060 --> 00:01:29,340
A couple of unit test files have already been generated for us when we generated a couple of different

29
00:01:29,340 --> 00:01:31,710
services and whatnot using the nest cli.

30
00:01:32,190 --> 00:01:39,480
So for example, inside of the SRC users folder, we've got the user's controller spec file and inside

31
00:01:39,480 --> 00:01:43,350
of here is a test to make sure that the controller is working as expected.

32
00:01:44,190 --> 00:01:48,600
So we're going to write out a test very similar to the one that you see inside of here.

33
00:01:48,720 --> 00:01:52,200
We're going to first be testing our authentication service.

34
00:01:52,200 --> 00:01:54,720
So the auth service file right here.

35
00:01:56,680 --> 00:02:01,000
Now to test the auth service, we're going to run into a little bit of a challenge here as soon as we

36
00:02:01,000 --> 00:02:03,370
start putting together some code and whatnot.

37
00:02:03,700 --> 00:02:07,120
Obviously, if we're going to test this thing at some point in time, we need to create an instance

38
00:02:07,120 --> 00:02:09,860
of this class so we can call the different methods inside of it.

39
00:02:09,880 --> 00:02:15,190
So, for example, we need to be able to call, sign up and sign in and then make sure that they return

40
00:02:15,190 --> 00:02:16,870
some appropriate output.

41
00:02:17,050 --> 00:02:23,500
The problem with testing this, however, is that we need to create a copy of the user service in order

42
00:02:23,500 --> 00:02:28,800
to create a copy of the authentication service and in turn to make a copy of the user service.

43
00:02:28,810 --> 00:02:35,440
That class right there depends upon our users repo and the users repo in turn depends upon SQLite.

44
00:02:35,680 --> 00:02:40,960
So to just test our authentication service, we start to get into this kind of dependency nightmare

45
00:02:40,960 --> 00:02:42,190
once again.

46
00:02:42,370 --> 00:02:45,100
So all we care about are these two methods right here.

47
00:02:45,100 --> 00:02:50,560
But to just test those methods, presumably we have to create a copy of the user service and the users

48
00:02:50,560 --> 00:02:50,920
repo.

49
00:02:50,950 --> 00:02:52,990
We have to worry about SQLite configuration.

50
00:02:52,990 --> 00:02:54,640
A lot of stuff going on there.

51
00:02:54,850 --> 00:02:59,730
So in order to make testing a lot more straightforward, we're going to use a little trick.

52
00:02:59,750 --> 00:03:03,880
So this is a primary feature, a primary goal of dependency injection.

53
00:03:03,890 --> 00:03:08,910
We're going to use the dependency injection system to avoid having to create all these different repositories,

54
00:03:09,080 --> 00:03:10,490
these different dependencies.

55
00:03:10,790 --> 00:03:16,070
So the trick is we are going to make a fake copy of the user's service.

56
00:03:16,070 --> 00:03:21,410
So this will be a temporary class that we define inside of our test file that has only the methods we

57
00:03:21,440 --> 00:03:23,090
care about defined on it.

58
00:03:23,690 --> 00:03:28,670
Once we create that class, we're then going to create an instance of our authentication service using

59
00:03:28,670 --> 00:03:30,080
that fake user service.

60
00:03:30,080 --> 00:03:31,230
Or did I say user service?

61
00:03:31,260 --> 00:03:34,490
I'm an auth service right there using the fake user service.

62
00:03:35,430 --> 00:03:37,440
Now, what does that actually look like in practice?

63
00:03:37,470 --> 00:03:41,250
Well, remember what goes on behind the scenes when our application runs normally.

64
00:03:41,280 --> 00:03:46,320
When our application normally runs, we put a bunch of classes in all their dependencies inside of our

65
00:03:46,590 --> 00:03:47,370
container.

66
00:03:48,290 --> 00:03:53,840
So, for example, we've got our service and some listing says the service depends upon user service.

67
00:03:53,840 --> 00:03:56,930
And the user service in turn depends upon the user's repo.

68
00:03:57,140 --> 00:04:03,410
We are going to create a small kind of testing container where we kind of short circuit this entire

69
00:04:03,410 --> 00:04:04,580
dependency list.

70
00:04:04,610 --> 00:04:10,790
We are going to create a testing container and inside there we're going to put in our authentication

71
00:04:10,790 --> 00:04:16,760
service and we're going to trick the container into thinking that the auth service depends upon a class

72
00:04:16,760 --> 00:04:18,530
that you and I are going to put together.

73
00:04:18,530 --> 00:04:23,360
And it's going to be a very simple, very basic implementation of the user's service.

74
00:04:24,350 --> 00:04:24,710
Okay.

75
00:04:24,710 --> 00:04:29,270
So as I start to discuss all this stuff once again, Nest is a little bit complicated.

76
00:04:29,270 --> 00:04:30,080
Not gonna lie.

77
00:04:30,110 --> 00:04:32,660
So a lot of stuff going on here with this testing setup.

78
00:04:32,660 --> 00:04:36,020
And the best way to really approach this is really to just write some code.

79
00:04:36,050 --> 00:04:41,450
So let's take a pause right here and start a test file for our auth service in just a moment.

And then finally, users service as well.

Then inside of here, we're going to first write out the absolute minimum amount of code required to

So in other words, our first test is not going to look like some of the tests that you might see inside

15

00:01:06,160 --> 00:01:10,120
So inside of here, first thing we're going to do is write out a test block.

So we're going to say it can create an instance of auth service.

19
00:01:16,050 --> 00:01:21,120
So the goal of this test is to just say, hey, you know what, we can create a copy of the auth service.

We can get one up and running.

21
00:01:23,720 --> 00:01:28,160

22

it.

as well.

We're going to put in a configuration object and, you know, put testing right there.

That should be just test by itself.

After we then create the module, I'm going to try to reach into this container because that's what

34

00:04:57,830 --> 00:04:59,840
Right above that module line.

This is where we are going to create a fake copy of the user's service.

I'm then going to create a new object called fake users service.

After that, I'll then define a create property.

That's better.

89
00:05:40,430 --> 00:05:44,660
And then inside that function, we're going to return promise dot resolve.

90

I'm going to put in an object with an ID of one email and password like so.

91

Okay.

Now to wrap this up, one last step.

00:05:59,730 --> 00:06:01,860
I'm going to find the providers array right here.

I'm going to give myself a little bit of space.

And then as a second element inside that array, I'm going to put in an object with a provide property

96
00:06:10,400 --> 00:06:18,290
of users service and a use value property of fake users service.

Okay.

100
00:06:27,490 --> 00:06:29,560
So we've got find and create.

00:06:29,890 --> 00:06:33,980
They should both be functions that return promise.resolve and promise.resolve.

First one has an array.

00:06:34,960 --> 00:06:37,420
Second one is an object with a couple of different properties.

Then also double check the object down here with provide and use value.

Okay, finally, we're going to save this.

So let's now go back over to our terminal and see how our tests are doing now.

Sure enough, it looks like our tests are passing, which is kind of good and kind of bad.

109
00:06:56,530 --> 00:07:01,270
It's good because, hey, our tests are passing, but it's kind of bad because what in the world is

110
00:07:01,270 --> 00:07:02,950
going on with this code?

111
00:07:03,040 --> 00:07:06,880
Well, like I mentioned, this is where Nest gets just a little bit confusing.
