00:07:06,880 --> 00:07:07,990
So let's take a pause right here.

113
00:07:08,000 --> 00:07:12,430
We're going to come back in just a moment and really understand what this object right here is doing

114
00:07:12,430 --> 00:07:15,010
and what that object right there is doing as well.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,000 --> 00:00:05,030
Let's take a look at some of this weird code we put together in the last video.

2
00:00:05,060 --> 00:00:09,980
We're going to first focus on the providers array inside of our testing module right here.

3
00:00:10,010 --> 00:00:12,740
So what is going on with the providers array in general?

4
00:00:12,980 --> 00:00:17,660
We might recall back when we were first discussing dependency injection, the providers array is a listing

5
00:00:17,660 --> 00:00:21,720
of all the different classes that we might want to inject into our container.

6
00:00:21,740 --> 00:00:26,870
So once we add these providers in or this list of classes, the container can then figure out how to

7
00:00:26,870 --> 00:00:28,700
create any instance we want.

8
00:00:28,730 --> 00:00:33,230
Whenever we create an instance of one of those classes, the container will create instances of all

9
00:00:33,230 --> 00:00:35,400
the dependencies of that class as well.

10
00:00:35,420 --> 00:00:41,180
So essentially the list of providers is a list of classes we want registered inside the container.

11
00:00:44,050 --> 00:00:49,210
So the first line right here is going to behave very similarly to how other classes we've taken a look

12
00:00:49,210 --> 00:00:50,770
at before are going to behave.

13
00:00:51,040 --> 00:00:55,420
Authservice is going to tell the container that at some point in time we are going to want to create

14
00:00:55,420 --> 00:00:57,780
an instance of the auth service.

15
00:00:57,790 --> 00:01:02,710
The container is going to take a look at that class and understand all that classes, dependencies.

16
00:01:02,980 --> 00:01:07,750
When it looks at the auth service, it's going to understand by looking at that constructor argument

17
00:01:07,750 --> 00:01:11,350
that one of the dependencies of this class is the user's service.

18
00:01:11,560 --> 00:01:16,090
So that is business as usual with the first entry in the providers array.

19
00:01:16,210 --> 00:01:19,930
Where things get really interesting is the second element inside that array.

20
00:01:21,160 --> 00:01:26,530
So this is an object that is going to slightly trick or kind of reroute the system.

21
00:01:26,830 --> 00:01:30,250
It's going to change how different classes or different things get resolved.

22
00:01:30,250 --> 00:01:33,850
Whenever we ask for a copy of specifically the auth service.

23
00:01:34,410 --> 00:01:37,700
This object, we can really kind of read it in plain English.

24
00:01:37,710 --> 00:01:44,400
It says, if anyone asks for a copy of the user service, then give them the value.

25
00:01:44,430 --> 00:01:46,400
Fake users service.

26
00:01:46,410 --> 00:01:48,300
So that is what that object means.

27
00:01:48,330 --> 00:01:53,010
The key of providing there is a little bit confusing because the word provide makes it seem like this

28
00:01:53,010 --> 00:01:56,800
is something we're providing to the rest of the classes inside of our container.

29
00:01:56,820 --> 00:01:58,270
That is not the case.

30
00:01:58,290 --> 00:02:02,970
The property name of provide means if anyone asks for this.

31
00:02:02,970 --> 00:02:06,480
So if anyone asks for user service, give them the value.

32
00:02:06,510 --> 00:02:08,130
Fake user service.

33
00:02:09,190 --> 00:02:14,350
So then we can start to think about what happens inside of our container when we ask the container to

34
00:02:14,350 --> 00:02:16,750
create an instance of the auth service.

35
00:02:16,780 --> 00:02:18,610
Here's what goes on behind the scenes.

36
00:02:19,380 --> 00:02:24,210
When we ask for a copy of the art service, the container is going to go find the art service and figure

37
00:02:24,210 --> 00:02:26,490
out all the dependencies of that class.

38
00:02:26,670 --> 00:02:28,830
So naturally at first sees, Oh, okay.

39
00:02:28,830 --> 00:02:33,360
To create a instance of the art service, I first need an instance of the users service.

40
00:02:33,540 --> 00:02:37,640
So the container then says, How do I create a copy of the user service?

41
00:02:37,650 --> 00:02:41,430
And it's going to take a look at a list of all the other rules it has inside the container.

42
00:02:42,850 --> 00:02:44,380
It's going to find this rule right here.

43
00:02:44,380 --> 00:02:50,290
And that rule says if anyone asks for the user service, if anyone asks for that thing, then just give

44
00:02:50,290 --> 00:02:51,340
them this object.

45
00:02:51,340 --> 00:02:54,880
The object we created that has a find and create method on it.

46
00:02:55,660 --> 00:02:57,310
So we can almost imagine.

47
00:02:58,480 --> 00:03:00,760
That these steps kind of fade away.

48
00:03:01,120 --> 00:03:06,970
And whenever we ask for a copy of the auth service, the container is going to automatically create

49
00:03:06,970 --> 00:03:13,570
an instance of the auth service for us using this plain object as the user's service.

50
00:03:14,720 --> 00:03:20,480
So right here, this service object, which is a copy or an instance of our authentication service.

51
00:03:20,510 --> 00:03:25,610
If we took a look at its repo or its user service property because remember, that's what we assign

52
00:03:25,610 --> 00:03:27,230
it to inside the class definition.

53
00:03:27,260 --> 00:03:33,110
If we took a look at its users service property, it would be this object right here.

54
00:03:33,800 --> 00:03:35,210
So what does that mean?

55
00:03:35,240 --> 00:03:40,790
Well, remember, inside of our authentication service, whenever we call, say, sign up right here.

56
00:03:41,380 --> 00:03:45,610
Then the service or the sign up method internally is going to have some amount of logic inside of it.

57
00:03:45,610 --> 00:03:49,640
And one of the things that it does is it calls the user service find method.

58
00:03:49,660 --> 00:03:52,930
It also later on calls the user service create method.

59
00:03:53,050 --> 00:03:58,720
So now, rather than reaching out and calling the real find and the real create, which required the

60
00:03:58,720 --> 00:04:05,260
use of the user's repository and SQLite, we're going to instead run these very fake implemented methods

61
00:04:05,260 --> 00:04:05,990
instead.

62
00:04:06,010 --> 00:04:09,760
So this fake version of find and this fake version of create.

63
00:04:10,120 --> 00:04:13,450
So whenever we call sign up, first line of code right here.

64
00:04:13,450 --> 00:04:17,110
When we call find, this is what actually gets executed.

65
00:04:17,290 --> 00:04:20,830
So we're going to return a promise that resolves with an empty array.

66
00:04:21,160 --> 00:04:23,580
We're then going to run some logic on the result.

67
00:04:23,590 --> 00:04:25,090
Blah, blah blah bunch of stuff.

68
00:04:25,240 --> 00:04:27,730
We eventually then try to create a new user.

69
00:04:28,210 --> 00:04:32,980
What actually gets invoked there is the create method inside of our fake user service.

70
00:04:33,340 --> 00:04:38,890
So this entire technique allows us to create a working copy of the authentication service that can make

71
00:04:38,890 --> 00:04:41,510
use of other classes, other objects.

72
00:04:41,810 --> 00:04:45,290
But we don't have to actually create those other objects or anything like that.

73
00:04:45,290 --> 00:04:49,440
Instead, we create these fake objects and put them into the container.

74
00:04:49,460 --> 00:04:54,620
And because you and I are creating these fake implementations of find and create, we can very easily

75
00:04:54,620 --> 00:04:55,880
control their behavior.

76
00:04:56,090 --> 00:05:01,670
So if you and I wanted to write a test where maybe an email address was in use, because remember,

77
00:05:01,670 --> 00:05:03,770
that was the entire point of this block right here.

78
00:05:04,570 --> 00:05:09,220
So if we want to see what happens when an email address is in use, then you and I could go and tweak

79
00:05:09,220 --> 00:05:12,280
our implementation of find inside of our tests.

80
00:05:12,640 --> 00:05:18,250
So maybe rather than returning an empty array right here, we could return an array with a user inside

81
00:05:18,250 --> 00:05:24,460
of it and say, Oh yeah, there actually is someone who is already using this email address and that

82
00:05:24,460 --> 00:05:27,920
would cause our signup method right here to throw an error.

83
00:05:27,940 --> 00:05:33,970
So again, by changing the implementation of these kind of dependency methods, we can change what happens

84
00:05:33,970 --> 00:05:37,690
inside of our test suite when we call sign up or sign in or anything else.

85
00:05:39,050 --> 00:05:44,690
Okay, so one repeat one more time that this really is the hardest thing to understand in all of nest.

86
00:05:44,720 --> 00:05:48,510
The real hard part, because it's really like 2 or 3 different things going on here.

87
00:05:48,530 --> 00:05:51,710
First, we've got dependency injection, which is already kind of hard to understand.

88
00:05:51,740 --> 00:05:57,380
We've then got the idea that we're kind of going to override the actual dependency of user service with

89
00:05:57,380 --> 00:05:58,660
this fake object.

90
00:05:58,670 --> 00:06:03,380
And then on top of that, we have to understand, well, we have to kind of re-implement these methods

91
00:06:03,380 --> 00:06:08,480
in a very simple and basic way that are going to cause our original methods of sign up and sign in on

92
00:06:08,480 --> 00:06:12,680
the service to do what we want them to do inside of our kind of testing environment.

93
00:06:12,830 --> 00:06:17,300
So once again, if this stuff is feeling pretty crazy or pretty hard, don't sweat it.

94
00:06:17,300 --> 00:06:19,600
This really is tough stuff.

95
00:06:20,250 --> 00:06:22,290
So at this point, let's take a pause right here.

96
00:06:22,320 --> 00:06:23,490
We're going to come back to the next video.

97
00:06:23,490 --> 00:06:28,710
We're going to take a look at why we specifically only implemented, find and Create, even though there

98
00:06:28,710 --> 00:06:31,530
are many other methods defined on our user service.

99
00:06:31,650 --> 00:06:38,430
So remember, the user service itself has other methods like update and remove and stuff like that.

100
00:06:38,560 --> 00:06:41,370
We're going to understand why we only implemented a subset.

101
00:06:41,400 --> 00:06:45,990
We're then going to make a couple changes to this little block right here just for kind of ease of use

102
00:06:45,990 --> 00:06:48,360
and whatnot and just continue on from there.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,560 --> 00:00:03,660
I want to give you a couple more notes on this object.

2
00:00:03,660 --> 00:00:07,350
Some things I'm going to tell you in this video are a repeat of what we discussed in the last video.

3
00:00:07,350 --> 00:00:12,360
But I just want to repeat a couple of really important ideas around this object because it is, again,

4
00:00:12,360 --> 00:00:13,800
just really challenging stuff.

5
00:00:13,980 --> 00:00:19,560
So first thing I want to remind you on is why did we only create the find and create methods on this

6
00:00:19,560 --> 00:00:20,160
object?

7
00:00:20,190 --> 00:00:23,580
The real user service has several other functions tied to it.

8
00:00:23,670 --> 00:00:28,410
So the reason that we are only defining, find and create here is that those are the only methods that

9
00:00:28,410 --> 00:00:31,440
are actually made use of by the authentication service.

10
00:00:31,960 --> 00:00:38,800
So the auth service inside the signup method calls, create and find, and the sign in method only calls

11
00:00:38,800 --> 00:00:39,460
find.

12
00:00:39,630 --> 00:00:43,630
That means that we only have to create those two functions on our fake service.

13
00:00:43,660 --> 00:00:48,100
We could define all the other functions that exist on the real user service, but they're never going

14
00:00:48,100 --> 00:00:48,610
to be used.

15
00:00:48,610 --> 00:00:50,020
They're never going to be called.

16
00:00:51,000 --> 00:00:52,080
Next thing I want to mention.

17
00:00:53,480 --> 00:00:56,570
What is going on with the Promise.resolve calls?

18
00:00:56,600 --> 00:01:00,320
Well, remember, find and create are asynchronous in nature.

19
00:01:00,320 --> 00:01:05,690
It takes some amount of time to read or write data into the SQLite database, so we have to return a

20
00:01:05,690 --> 00:01:11,990
promise from find and create Promise.resolve is a little helper function that creates a promise and

21
00:01:11,990 --> 00:01:15,020
then immediately resolves it with the given value.

22
00:01:15,020 --> 00:01:23,390
So if we now did something like await fake users service dot find, we would get back an empty array.

23
00:01:23,690 --> 00:01:29,210
So that's all Promise.resolve is doing is essentially making sure we get the same exact API that exists

24
00:01:29,210 --> 00:01:31,130
on the real user service.

25
00:01:33,050 --> 00:01:37,960
Finally, one other thing I want to mention here, and this one is a little bit of a gotcha.

26
00:01:37,970 --> 00:01:41,320
I'm going to show you how to fix this up, at least get a better implementation in here.

27
00:01:41,330 --> 00:01:47,420
One thing that you might notice is that we are trying to pass off the fake user service as the user

28
00:01:47,420 --> 00:01:48,710
service, like the real one.

29
00:01:48,740 --> 00:01:53,390
The real user service has a couple of different functions and properties assigned to it.

30
00:01:53,390 --> 00:01:56,480
It has find find one, create update.

31
00:01:56,480 --> 00:01:59,090
It also has a repo property as well.

32
00:01:59,360 --> 00:02:04,490
So we are providing an object and trying to pass it off as a users service.

33
00:02:04,760 --> 00:02:09,950
Right now TypeScript is not helping us and making sure that we are providing the correct type signature

34
00:02:09,980 --> 00:02:12,170
on fake user service in any way.

35
00:02:12,170 --> 00:02:16,730
In other words, when you provide this object right here and you say instead or whenever someone asks

36
00:02:16,730 --> 00:02:22,490
for this, give them this instead you can put in any value that you want to right here for use value.

37
00:02:23,420 --> 00:02:26,140
So, for example, I could put in a number one, two, three.

38
00:02:26,180 --> 00:02:30,740
So now whenever we create an instance of the authentication service, it's going to be given the number

39
00:02:30,740 --> 00:02:34,820
one, two, three in place of an instance of the user service.

40
00:02:35,000 --> 00:02:39,320
Our application would definitely not work as expected because naturally the number one, two, three

41
00:02:39,350 --> 00:02:43,760
doesn't have a find method or a create method and all those other things.

42
00:02:43,850 --> 00:02:48,770
So again, when we use this object right here, TypeScript is not helping us out in any way, shape

43
00:02:48,770 --> 00:02:49,670
or form.

44
00:02:49,910 --> 00:02:54,500
But of course we might want to help TypeScript or have TypeScript give us some help and make sure that

45
00:02:54,500 --> 00:02:57,560
we are putting together our fake user service correctly.

46
00:02:57,890 --> 00:03:00,440
So I'm going to undo that change right there really quickly.

47
00:03:00,650 --> 00:03:05,720
So what I mean by that is you and I are trying to define, find and create right here.

48
00:03:05,840 --> 00:03:10,270
Those two methods are going to be called by our authentication service when our tests run.

49
00:03:10,280 --> 00:03:16,160
So we need to make sure that our fake implementation of find right here returns whatever value or whatever

50
00:03:16,160 --> 00:03:18,700
type of value it is actually supposed to return.

51
00:03:18,710 --> 00:03:24,860
In this case, we know that find is supposed to return an array of users, which is why I'm returning

52
00:03:24,860 --> 00:03:26,270
an empty array right here.

53
00:03:26,360 --> 00:03:31,160
But again, I could just arbitrarily return, say a number or something like that, and that would definitely

54
00:03:31,160 --> 00:03:33,230
make all my code break horribly.

55
00:03:33,230 --> 00:03:38,720
So ideally we would have TypeScript jump in and try to help make sure that we are defining the find

56
00:03:38,720 --> 00:03:40,430
and create methods correctly.

57
00:03:40,580 --> 00:03:45,130
To do so, I'm going to put in a type annotation to fake user service.

58
00:03:45,140 --> 00:03:48,980
So I'll put in a colon and then users service like so.

59
00:03:50,620 --> 00:03:53,020
I'm also going to wrap that with partial.

60
00:03:54,200 --> 00:03:59,180
So by adding this in, TypeScript understands that we are trying to create a partial version of the

61
00:03:59,180 --> 00:04:00,200
user service.

62
00:04:01,060 --> 00:04:05,470
The keyword of partial right there or that type helper is going to make sure that our object, if it

63
00:04:05,470 --> 00:04:09,430
defines any of the properties of the user service, then it defines them correctly.

64
00:04:09,460 --> 00:04:13,990
So by using partial, we're not required to define every method that user service has.

65
00:04:14,020 --> 00:04:16,510
TypeScript is just going to check the ones that we define.

66
00:04:16,780 --> 00:04:23,350
So now if we try to say define the find method, but return an invalid value from it, let's say resolve

67
00:04:23,350 --> 00:04:25,490
with a number, then we're going to get an error.

68
00:04:25,510 --> 00:04:30,460
And TypeScript is going to tell us, Hey, your find method, which you said was supposed to be identical

69
00:04:30,460 --> 00:04:32,860
in nature to the user service find method.

70
00:04:32,890 --> 00:04:38,170
Yours is returning a promise that resolves with the number, but it's supposed to be resolving with

71
00:04:38,170 --> 00:04:39,850
an array of users.

72
00:04:40,000 --> 00:04:43,690
So by adding this type annotation, TypeScript is just going to give us a little bit of help and make

73
00:04:43,690 --> 00:04:46,360
sure we are implementing the fake version correctly.

74
00:04:47,780 --> 00:04:49,520
We can see that right away.

75
00:04:49,520 --> 00:04:51,810
We are also implementing create incorrectly.

76
00:04:51,830 --> 00:04:54,500
Now, in this case, this is a much harder one to fix.

77
00:04:54,680 --> 00:05:00,050
So our create method is supposed to resolve or return with a user entity instance.

78
00:05:00,050 --> 00:05:03,980
And we kind of are right now we've got a object with an ID, an email and a password.

79
00:05:03,980 --> 00:05:09,530
But if we mouse over create, we are told that in order to implement a user, we also have to implement

80
00:05:09,530 --> 00:05:12,230
log, insert, log, update and log remove.

81
00:05:12,260 --> 00:05:16,820
You might recall if you open up your user entity file, here's mine right here.

82
00:05:16,850 --> 00:05:21,920
We had defined those couple of different hook functions just to provide some logging statements.

83
00:05:23,590 --> 00:05:28,620
So I don't really want to have to define all these different functions just for the purposes of my test.

84
00:05:28,630 --> 00:05:33,370
So to fix this up, I'm going to find this object right here, which I want to be treated like a user

85
00:05:33,370 --> 00:05:34,050
entity.

86
00:05:34,060 --> 00:05:40,690
I'm just going to say as user, and then I'm going to import the user entity at the top.

87
00:05:42,880 --> 00:05:47,090
So now we're just telling TypeScript, look, just treat this like it was a user entity.

88
00:05:47,110 --> 00:05:54,100
So now our create function is properly implementing the create function that exists on the user's service.

89
00:05:54,860 --> 00:05:59,150
So again, this step right here is just done to make sure that all these fake methods we are putting

90
00:05:59,150 --> 00:06:01,520
together have the correct type signature.

91
00:06:02,930 --> 00:06:03,320
Okay.

92
00:06:03,320 --> 00:06:07,430
So that's pretty much enough, I think, on this basic testing setup right now.

93
00:06:07,490 --> 00:06:12,290
Think we've gone over some of these more kind of core concepts enough and to actually understand any

94
00:06:12,290 --> 00:06:13,070
more around it.

95
00:06:13,100 --> 00:06:17,030
We need to actually write some tests so you can see a lot of different variations of creating these

96
00:06:17,030 --> 00:06:19,130
kind of fake services and how they work.

97
00:06:19,130 --> 00:06:22,910
So let's continue on with some more actual testing in the next video.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,250 --> 00:00:04,110
In this video, we're going to start to refactor our test file.

2
00:00:04,130 --> 00:00:06,200
Right now, we have a single IT statement.

3
00:00:06,200 --> 00:00:11,240
Usually whenever we set up a testing suite, each it statement is going to try to test one aspect of

4
00:00:11,240 --> 00:00:13,130
our code inside this test.

5
00:00:13,130 --> 00:00:17,900
Right now, we're doing a tremendous amount of setup around creating a fake user service, creating

6
00:00:17,900 --> 00:00:20,880
a module and then getting a copy of our service out of it.

7
00:00:20,900 --> 00:00:25,760
This is all logic that is going to have to be repeated for every test we write inside this file because

8
00:00:25,760 --> 00:00:28,880
every test is going to require a copy of our service.

9
00:00:29,090 --> 00:00:33,920
So to make this file a little bit better organized, we're going to move some code around.

10
00:00:34,220 --> 00:00:40,370
I'm going to first begin by cutting everything from the top of the IT statement, including that comment.

11
00:00:41,580 --> 00:00:44,010
All the way down to const service.

12
00:00:44,010 --> 00:00:45,840
So I'm going to cut all that out.

13
00:00:47,460 --> 00:00:50,790
Then right above the IT statement, I'm going to put in a before each.

14
00:00:52,380 --> 00:00:58,110
I'm going to mark that function as async and then I will put that entire cut block inside of the before

15
00:00:58,110 --> 00:00:58,760
each.

16
00:00:58,770 --> 00:01:00,060
So paste.

17
00:01:01,270 --> 00:01:06,610
So now for every test that we write inside this file, we're going to first create the fake service,

18
00:01:06,640 --> 00:01:11,380
create our testing module, and get an instance of the service.

19
00:01:12,620 --> 00:01:16,460
So now for every single test we have inside of here, we're going to get a fresh, brand new copy of

20
00:01:16,460 --> 00:01:17,660
our authentication service.

21
00:01:17,660 --> 00:01:19,160
That's exactly what we want.

22
00:01:19,250 --> 00:01:21,320
Now, just one other very small problem.

23
00:01:21,320 --> 00:01:24,950
We are defining the service variable inside this before each function.

24
00:01:24,950 --> 00:01:28,470
So it's in a completely different scope than the IT statement down here.

25
00:01:28,490 --> 00:01:31,790
So to fix this up, we're going to use a very common pattern that you're going to see in a lot of different

26
00:01:31,820 --> 00:01:32,930
test files.

27
00:01:33,110 --> 00:01:37,930
I'm going to define a variable called service at the top scope inside this file.

28
00:01:37,940 --> 00:01:43,400
So at the very top of the file, right before the before each, I will say let service is going to be

29
00:01:43,400 --> 00:01:45,470
of type auth service.

30
00:01:47,120 --> 00:01:52,550
So now this variable is at the uppermost or topmost scope of the file so it can be referenced or referred

31
00:01:52,550 --> 00:01:54,710
to inside of any function inside of here.

32
00:01:55,790 --> 00:02:01,790
So then inside the b4 each at the very bottom rather than redeclaring the service variable right here

33
00:02:01,790 --> 00:02:03,470
I'm going to assign.

34
00:02:04,160 --> 00:02:08,750
Module git auth service to the service variable we had already created at the very top.

35
00:02:08,960 --> 00:02:12,590
And now we can reference that variable safely inside of all of our different tests.

36
00:02:13,830 --> 00:02:14,100
All right.

37
00:02:14,100 --> 00:02:15,600
Let's save this really quickly.

38
00:02:15,900 --> 00:02:19,520
I'm going to go back over to my terminal and just make sure everything is still working as expected.

39
00:02:19,530 --> 00:02:19,830
Yep.

40
00:02:19,830 --> 00:02:20,610
Looks good.

41
00:02:21,380 --> 00:02:22,900
The last change I'm going to make here.

42
00:02:22,910 --> 00:02:28,880
I'm going to wrap everything we've done so far except for the import statements inside of a describe

43
00:02:28,880 --> 00:02:29,600
block.

44
00:02:30,540 --> 00:02:33,090
So I'm going to say auth service.

45
00:02:33,760 --> 00:02:38,320
And then put in an arrow function and this one does not need the async keyword on it.

46
00:02:39,120 --> 00:02:43,530
I'm then going to cut everything else inside the file and put it inside of that Describe.

47
00:02:43,680 --> 00:02:45,480
So I'll highlight everything.

48
00:02:46,590 --> 00:02:47,910
I'm going to cut all of it.

49
00:02:49,160 --> 00:02:51,260
And then paste it inside the describe.

50
00:02:53,440 --> 00:02:58,180
The only thing that the describe block does besides creating a new scope inside the file is really just

51
00:02:58,180 --> 00:03:01,000
apply a further description to the text inside of here.

52
00:03:01,030 --> 00:03:06,040
So if we now go back over to our test suite after saving this file, we're just going to see that now

53
00:03:06,040 --> 00:03:07,570
we've got the word auth service right there.

54
00:03:07,570 --> 00:03:09,290
That's all the describe block really does.

55
00:03:09,310 --> 00:03:12,610
It just helps us better kind of organize all of our different tests.

56
00:03:14,200 --> 00:03:14,500
Okay.

57
00:03:14,500 --> 00:03:18,910
So what you see inside of this test file is now going to be just about identical to what you're going

58
00:03:18,910 --> 00:03:23,670
to see inside of every single nest test file or at least for unit testing.

59
00:03:23,680 --> 00:03:29,950
So, for example, if we take a look at how about user controller spec, which you recall was automatically

60
00:03:29,950 --> 00:03:34,600
generated for us, you're going to notice that it pretty much looks identical to what we just put together.

61
00:03:34,630 --> 00:03:35,870
They've got the describe.

62
00:03:35,890 --> 00:03:40,750
They've got a declaration of variable A before each and then a couple of if statements in this case

63
00:03:40,750 --> 00:03:41,350
just one.

64
00:03:41,350 --> 00:03:44,380
But eventually we might have a couple more if statements inside of here as well.

65
00:03:44,770 --> 00:03:48,730
So again, this is a pattern that you're going to see inside of a tremendous number of different test

66
00:03:48,730 --> 00:03:49,480
files.

67
00:03:50,270 --> 00:03:51,480
Okay, so this looks good.

68
00:03:51,500 --> 00:03:54,230
We've got a good refactor, better organization inside of here.

69
00:03:54,230 --> 00:03:56,690
So now we just need to add in some actual tests.

70
00:03:56,720 --> 00:04:00,190
We need to make sure that the authentication service is doing what we expect.

71
00:04:00,200 --> 00:04:02,240
So let's take care of that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,470 --> 00:00:05,150
All right, let's try writing another test around our authentication service.

2
00:00:05,160 --> 00:00:08,640
So in this video I want to try to test our sign up method.

3
00:00:08,820 --> 00:00:12,030
So our sign up method takes in an email and a password.

4
00:00:12,060 --> 00:00:14,160
It's then going to do some logic, blah blah blah.

5
00:00:14,190 --> 00:00:20,580
A lot of stuff generates the salt and the hash and then eventually creates a new user entity with that

6
00:00:20,580 --> 00:00:21,120
password.

7
00:00:21,120 --> 00:00:23,340
So the hashed insulted password.

8
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:27,150
And then after creating that user entity, it's going to return that user.

9
00:00:27,300 --> 00:00:32,820
So in this video, I want to try to write a test that is going to try to call the sign up function with

10
00:00:32,820 --> 00:00:34,290
an email and a password.

11
00:00:34,410 --> 00:00:38,130
We're then going to take a look at the user entity that gets returned back to us, and we're going to

12
00:00:38,130 --> 00:00:41,550
make sure that the password is salted and hashed.

13
00:00:41,550 --> 00:00:45,090
So the password we get out should not be the same as the password that goes in.

14
00:00:45,480 --> 00:00:52,050
A quick reminder the find method right here and the create method right here are implemented by you

15
00:00:52,050 --> 00:00:52,800
and I.

16
00:00:52,830 --> 00:00:57,870
So we need to make sure that our implementation of find and create are compatible with our goals for

17
00:00:57,870 --> 00:00:58,770
this test.

18
00:00:58,770 --> 00:01:02,620
So back inside of our test file, we're going to take a look at find and create.

19
00:01:02,890 --> 00:01:05,650
It looks like Find is going to return an empty array.

20
00:01:05,680 --> 00:01:07,270
That's exactly what we want.

21
00:01:07,300 --> 00:01:11,460
Remember, if it returns an array with anything inside of it, we're going to throw an error.

22
00:01:11,470 --> 00:01:14,530
So that would not allow us to test our code as we want.

23
00:01:14,980 --> 00:01:16,960
And then for the create method.

24
00:01:18,160 --> 00:01:24,340
This will take in an email and a password and then return a promise that resolves with an object with

25
00:01:24,340 --> 00:01:26,590
whatever password that was put inside of here.

26
00:01:26,770 --> 00:01:29,260
So that is exactly what we want as well.

27
00:01:29,290 --> 00:01:35,050
Remember, inside of the signup method, we're going to eventually call create with a given email and

28
00:01:35,050 --> 00:01:37,030
the salted and hashed password.

29
00:01:37,030 --> 00:01:40,420
So we want to make sure that we actually make use of that email and password.

30
00:01:40,420 --> 00:01:41,650
We want to return an object.

31
00:01:41,680 --> 00:01:45,640
We want to return a user right here that has the hashed password on it.

32
00:01:46,210 --> 00:01:49,750
Now, it might seem like I'm making a big deal out of this, but just what I really want you to understand

33
00:01:49,750 --> 00:01:55,470
here was if we put together a create function that completely disregarded the provided email and password,

34
00:01:55,480 --> 00:01:58,840
for example, if we hardcoded in some email.

35
00:02:00,720 --> 00:02:02,970
And hardcoded in some password.

36
00:02:03,000 --> 00:02:08,010
Then our code would probably or our test would not work as expected because we'd be getting an email

37
00:02:08,010 --> 00:02:12,660
and a password inside this function, but just returning some willy nilly totally different email and

38
00:02:12,660 --> 00:02:13,200
password.

39
00:02:13,200 --> 00:02:14,700
So that's not what we want.

40
00:02:14,730 --> 00:02:20,580
We want to implement this create method as close to the original implementation as possible, and the

41
00:02:20,580 --> 00:02:26,070
closest implementation would be to create a new user or creating one with the email and password that

42
00:02:26,070 --> 00:02:26,970
are provided.

43
00:02:28,190 --> 00:02:28,610
Okay.

44
00:02:28,610 --> 00:02:29,420
So.

45
00:02:31,200 --> 00:02:32,640
Down at the bottom of the file.

46
00:02:32,640 --> 00:02:33,920
Let's create our test.

47
00:02:33,930 --> 00:02:41,850
So underneath the existing statement, I'm going to say it creates a new user with a salted and hashed

48
00:02:41,850 --> 00:02:42,690
password.

49
00:02:45,590 --> 00:02:47,540
Make sure that we mark that function as async.

50
00:02:48,130 --> 00:02:55,840
Then inside of here, I'm going to call my service, specifically the sign up function with some email.

51
00:02:56,700 --> 00:02:58,590
And some password.

52
00:03:00,390 --> 00:03:02,160
Calling sign up is asynchronous.

53
00:03:02,160 --> 00:03:03,930
So I'm going to put a wait there.

54
00:03:03,930 --> 00:03:06,260
And then out of it we get back a user.

55
00:03:06,270 --> 00:03:11,250
So in theory, this would be the user that was saved to our database.

56
00:03:12,510 --> 00:03:18,180
This user should have an ID, it should have an email, and it should have the hashed and salted version

57
00:03:18,180 --> 00:03:20,130
of that signup password right there.

58
00:03:20,250 --> 00:03:22,710
Remember, that is the password that is supplied by our users.

59
00:03:22,950 --> 00:03:25,230
So we're going to take a look at the user that was created.

60
00:03:25,260 --> 00:03:27,330
We're just going to look at the password property.

61
00:03:27,360 --> 00:03:30,810
We're going to make sure that it is not identical to the password we put in.

62
00:03:30,850 --> 00:03:34,830
We're going to make sure that it more or less looks like it is salted and hashed.

63
00:03:34,860 --> 00:03:40,530
Remember, we can't really directly or exactly figure out what the value of the password is because

64
00:03:40,530 --> 00:03:42,210
the salt is randomly generated.

65
00:03:42,210 --> 00:03:47,310
So we can't say, Oh, the salted and hashed password that comes out should be X, y, z because we

66
00:03:47,310 --> 00:03:51,900
just plain don't know what the salted and hashed password looks like, but we can at least say whatever

67
00:03:51,900 --> 00:03:56,880
we get back is that string should have maybe a period in it or something like that, something that

68
00:03:56,880 --> 00:03:59,670
says, yes, we did properly salt and hash it.

69
00:04:00,420 --> 00:04:01,260
All right, enough talking.

70
00:04:01,260 --> 00:04:02,160
Let's just write some code.

71
00:04:02,160 --> 00:04:09,030
So what we need to do here is something like expect userpassword not to equal Asdf.

72
00:04:10,580 --> 00:04:14,120
I'm then going to get the salt and hash.

73
00:04:15,470 --> 00:04:24,140
From Userpassword split on period, and I'm going to expect salt to be defined.

74
00:04:25,020 --> 00:04:26,680
And password or something.

75
00:04:26,890 --> 00:04:27,360
Hash.

76
00:04:28,180 --> 00:04:29,680
To be defined.

77
00:04:30,920 --> 00:04:35,720
So, again, this entire block right here, we're just making sure that the password was actually salted

78
00:04:35,720 --> 00:04:37,070
and hashed in some way.

79
00:04:37,310 --> 00:04:38,750
So let's now save this.

80
00:04:39,400 --> 00:04:42,580
Let's go back over to our terminal and see how our tests are doing.

81
00:04:42,850 --> 00:04:44,100
Sure enough, it passes.

82
00:04:44,110 --> 00:04:44,890
Very good.

83
00:04:44,980 --> 00:04:49,780
So, as usual, whenever you run test suites, really nice to make sure that your tests are actually

84
00:04:49,780 --> 00:04:52,290
working by causing them to fail in some way.

85
00:04:52,300 --> 00:04:57,280
So in other words, I want to see this test actually fail, just so I know that the test is running

86
00:04:57,280 --> 00:05:00,610
in its entirety and all the code inside here is running in some way.

87
00:05:01,100 --> 00:05:04,010
So to make sure that it fails, I'm going to try to split on.

88
00:05:04,010 --> 00:05:04,910
How about.

89
00:05:05,830 --> 00:05:07,000
A dollar sign.

90
00:05:07,990 --> 00:05:11,890
So if I try to split on a dollar sign, we're probably not gonna have a dollar sign inside the salted

91
00:05:11,890 --> 00:05:12,900
and hashed password.

92
00:05:12,910 --> 00:05:15,940
So we are probably not going to end up with a value for hash right here.

93
00:05:15,940 --> 00:05:20,350
It will end up being undefined and that would cause the second expectation probably to fail.

94
00:05:20,650 --> 00:05:24,240
So if I save this and flip back over, yep, that's exactly what happened.

95
00:05:24,250 --> 00:05:26,200
So expected hash to be defined.

96
00:05:26,230 --> 00:05:27,190
It was not.

97
00:05:27,310 --> 00:05:30,190
So that means that my test is probably running as I expect.

98
00:05:30,370 --> 00:05:36,580
I'm going to undo that dollar sign and change it back to a period now if I save again test runs.

99
00:05:37,520 --> 00:05:40,190
Okay, So this is a good first test right here.

100
00:05:41,460 --> 00:05:42,700
Let's come back in just a moment.

101
00:05:42,710 --> 00:05:44,830
We're going to add in a test around sign in.

102
00:05:44,840 --> 00:05:48,980
We're also going to take a look at some other ways that we can kind of mess around with our testing

103
00:05:48,980 --> 00:05:55,160
suite and whatnot by massaging or changing how some of the functions on our fake user service behave.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:04,980
The next thing I would like to test inside of our authentication service is to make sure that whenever

2
00:00:04,980 --> 00:00:09,400
we call sign up, if someone is already using that email, we should throw an error.

3
00:00:09,420 --> 00:00:10,260
So I want to test that.

4
00:00:10,260 --> 00:00:13,200
I want to make sure that we correctly throw some kind of error message.

5
00:00:13,320 --> 00:00:15,390
So let's go and write a test that's going to do so.

6
00:00:15,420 --> 00:00:19,470
We're going to very quickly realize that writing a test like this is actually going to kind of conflict

7
00:00:19,470 --> 00:00:20,730
with the one we already put together.

8
00:00:20,730 --> 00:00:22,570
But let's just write out some code first.

9
00:00:22,590 --> 00:00:30,750
So I'm going to say it throws an error if user signs up with email that is in use.

10
00:00:35,540 --> 00:00:35,930
Okay.

11
00:00:36,260 --> 00:00:39,890
So then inside of here, I'm going to try to call.

12
00:00:42,120 --> 00:00:42,620
Service.

13
00:00:42,870 --> 00:00:45,540
Sign up with an email.

14
00:00:45,600 --> 00:00:47,700
Hopefully that is in use.

15
00:00:48,490 --> 00:00:49,390
Now, here's a question.

16
00:00:49,390 --> 00:00:52,510
How do we actually make sure that email is in use?

17
00:00:52,660 --> 00:00:53,080
Okay.

18
00:00:53,080 --> 00:00:56,840
We need to trace through the execution of code inside of our test suite right now.

19
00:00:56,860 --> 00:01:01,480
So whenever you call, sign up that is going to run this function right here.

20
00:01:01,570 --> 00:01:05,980
The first thing we do is make use of the user service, find method.

21
00:01:06,650 --> 00:01:11,090
We're going to call it with the provided email and we're going to get back an array of users.

22
00:01:11,240 --> 00:01:15,990
Then if that array has any entries inside of it, then we're going to throw an error.

23
00:01:16,010 --> 00:01:22,610
So for our test, we want to make sure that the find method returns an array with at least one record

24
00:01:22,610 --> 00:01:28,100
inside of it that will allow us to finish up our test and make sure that an error actually is thrown.

25
00:01:28,950 --> 00:01:34,980
So to change how the find method right here behaves, we need to go back to our fake implementation

26
00:01:34,980 --> 00:01:36,500
of the users service.

27
00:01:36,510 --> 00:01:40,770
Because remember, inside of our test suite, whenever we call this find method on the user service,

28
00:01:40,800 --> 00:01:45,840
what actually gets executed is the find function right here.

29
00:01:47,420 --> 00:01:48,350
Now, here's the problem.

30
00:01:48,350 --> 00:01:49,520
Here's the conflict.

31
00:01:49,550 --> 00:01:53,870
We now have two tests that are going to be calling this find function.

32
00:01:54,140 --> 00:01:57,650
So the first one is the test we wrote out in the last video.

33
00:01:57,800 --> 00:02:00,610
And the second one is the one we just put together a second ago.

34
00:02:00,650 --> 00:02:04,300
These two tests are both going to cause the find method to be called.

35
00:02:04,310 --> 00:02:10,910
And in one case, we want the find method to return an empty array, because if it returns a user inside

36
00:02:10,910 --> 00:02:12,920
that array, then this test is definitely going to fail.

37
00:02:12,920 --> 00:02:16,970
We're going to get an error during this test that says something like Sorry, but that email is in use.

38
00:02:17,840 --> 00:02:22,370
But the current implementation of the find method, which returns an empty array, is not going to allow

39
00:02:22,370 --> 00:02:23,760
for an error to be thrown.

40
00:02:23,780 --> 00:02:25,580
So essentially two tests.

41
00:02:26,440 --> 00:02:30,170
They each have different requirements of this find method.

42
00:02:30,190 --> 00:02:35,440
One has to make sure or in one test, we want the find method to return an empty array and the other

43
00:02:35,440 --> 00:02:39,160
test we want to get back an array with at least one record inside of it.

44
00:02:39,310 --> 00:02:40,810
So how are we going to fix this?

45
00:02:40,840 --> 00:02:44,650
Well, we're going to start to mess around with a fake user's service.

46
00:02:44,680 --> 00:02:45,850
Here's what we're going to do.

47
00:02:46,530 --> 00:02:53,850
At the very top of our describe block, I'm going to declare another variable of fake users service.

48
00:02:53,850 --> 00:02:56,640
And I'm going to say that this is of type partial.

49
00:02:57,830 --> 00:02:59,780
Users service.

50
00:03:02,550 --> 00:03:07,170
Then inside of our beforeeach rather than declaring a new variable right here, I'm going to assign

51
00:03:07,170 --> 00:03:12,960
this entire object to the variable of fake user service, which we declared right above.

52
00:03:13,140 --> 00:03:17,850
So now we can access this object inside of all of our different tests in the same way that we can access

53
00:03:17,850 --> 00:03:19,050
the service variable.

54
00:03:19,580 --> 00:03:24,620
So I'm going to go down to the test where we want the find method to behave slightly differently, where

55
00:03:24,620 --> 00:03:29,840
we want to make sure that find is going to return through a promise, an array with at least one user

56
00:03:29,840 --> 00:03:30,710
inside of it.

57
00:03:30,920 --> 00:03:32,990
So that's the test you and I just put together.

58
00:03:33,840 --> 00:03:40,080
So down here, before we call sign up, we're going to take a look at the fake users service and we're

59
00:03:40,080 --> 00:03:43,090
going to update how the find method on this thing works.

60
00:03:43,110 --> 00:03:46,290
So I'm going to redefine the find function.

61
00:03:46,680 --> 00:03:51,120
I'm going to say that find is now going to return Promise.resolve.

62
00:03:52,080 --> 00:03:59,130
With an array that does have one user inside of it, and this user will have an ID, maybe an email.

63
00:04:00,950 --> 00:04:02,030
And a password.

64
00:04:03,800 --> 00:04:08,540
Once again, we're going to run into some type issues here because this object right here technically

65
00:04:08,540 --> 00:04:13,640
doesn't implement everything that a user object or user entity is supposed to, specifically those on

66
00:04:13,640 --> 00:04:17,030
save on or log insert, log, update, log remove functions.

67
00:04:17,030 --> 00:04:22,280
So I'm going to also after that object put in as user, which again just tells TypeScript, just trust

68
00:04:22,280 --> 00:04:23,810
us, this is a user entity.

69
00:04:24,790 --> 00:04:30,040
So now that we've redefined the find method just for this single test, whenever we call, sign up.

70
00:04:30,730 --> 00:04:31,990
So here's sign up.

71
00:04:32,200 --> 00:04:37,720
Sign up is going to immediately call user service dot find and it's going to run the new tweaked version

72
00:04:37,720 --> 00:04:39,900
of the find method you and I just put together.

73
00:04:39,910 --> 00:04:44,950
So it's going to get back a promise that resolves with an array that has one object inside of it.

74
00:04:45,040 --> 00:04:49,960
And that means that we are going to end up throwing an error and that behavior is going to apply to

75
00:04:49,960 --> 00:04:51,630
only this test right here.

76
00:04:51,640 --> 00:04:56,800
Every other test inside this file is going to use the original defined version of the find method.

77
00:04:57,510 --> 00:05:01,020
Which is that one right there, which returns an empty array.

78
00:05:01,960 --> 00:05:03,100
Okay, so that's it.

79
00:05:03,340 --> 00:05:07,120
So we've now got a modified version of find just for this test.

80
00:05:07,330 --> 00:05:11,890
Now the last thing we need to do is actually run this thing and make sure that an error is thrown when

81
00:05:11,890 --> 00:05:13,540
we run the sign up function.

82
00:05:14,450 --> 00:05:15,290
Now bad news.

83
00:05:15,290 --> 00:05:17,840
The actual testing framework behind the scenes here is jest.

84
00:05:17,840 --> 00:05:23,060
Jest historically doesn't really work very well with asynchronous code that is supposed to throw an

85
00:05:23,060 --> 00:05:23,510
error.

86
00:05:23,540 --> 00:05:28,160
So we're going to make use of just a little bit of a trick here to get just to understand what we're

87
00:05:28,160 --> 00:05:29,060
trying to do.

88
00:05:29,270 --> 00:05:30,110
So.

89
00:05:31,040 --> 00:05:36,590
I'm going to inside of the async function right here wrapping this test, I'm going to add in Done.

90
00:05:37,840 --> 00:05:40,630
That is a function that we're going to call when our tests are complete.

91
00:05:40,930 --> 00:05:43,330
I'm then going to wrap the sign up call.

92
00:05:44,140 --> 00:05:45,310
With a try.

93
00:05:47,600 --> 00:05:49,130
Catch statement.

94
00:05:52,070 --> 00:05:57,260
I'm going to move the await into the try and I'm going to put the catch inside the done.

95
00:05:59,710 --> 00:06:04,420
So now whenever our signup function is called, it's going to hopefully throw an error.

96
00:06:04,450 --> 00:06:05,820
We're going to go into catch.

97
00:06:05,830 --> 00:06:08,470
That's going to call done, which means our test suite is complete.

98
00:06:08,560 --> 00:06:12,790
If signup doesn't throw an error, then we're not going to go into the catch and we're going to end

99
00:06:12,790 --> 00:06:14,620
up with execution down here.

100
00:06:14,950 --> 00:06:19,780
If we do not call the done function at any point in time inside this test, after five seconds, Jest

101
00:06:19,780 --> 00:06:21,620
will assume that our test suite failed.

102
00:06:21,640 --> 00:06:24,730
So if we don't call done, the test is going to just plain fail.

103
00:06:24,760 --> 00:06:30,910
So with this setup, we're guaranteed to make sure that the signup call right here will throw an error.

104
00:06:31,150 --> 00:06:35,080
So to test this out, let's save go back over to our terminal.

105
00:06:36,090 --> 00:06:37,910
And sure enough, it works.

106
00:06:37,920 --> 00:06:42,720
So, as usual, to make sure it works and make sure that, say, this entire mechanic is working as

107
00:06:42,720 --> 00:06:43,330
expected.

108
00:06:43,350 --> 00:06:44,220
I'm going to comment out.

109
00:06:44,250 --> 00:06:44,910
Done.

110
00:06:45,270 --> 00:06:46,340
I'm going to save this.

111
00:06:46,350 --> 00:06:48,990
So now it's going to take a lot longer for our test to run.

112
00:06:48,990 --> 00:06:53,730
After five seconds, that test is going to expire and we're going to be told, hey, you never actually

113
00:06:53,730 --> 00:06:55,050
called that done function.

114
00:06:55,050 --> 00:06:57,420
And that's exactly what this message right here says.

115
00:06:57,600 --> 00:07:02,220
So that means that we are definitely or probably I should say we're definitely throwing an error and

116
00:07:02,220 --> 00:07:04,590
we are definitely correctly calling done.

117
00:07:05,350 --> 00:07:07,540
And that means our test is working as expected.

118
00:07:08,370 --> 00:07:09,030
Okay.

119
00:07:09,420 --> 00:07:14,280
Once again, I got to repeat for probably the fifth time, I know this testing stuff in Nest is pretty

120
00:07:14,280 --> 00:07:14,610
crazy.

121
00:07:14,610 --> 00:07:19,530
It's not just testing, it is also testing with the idea of these fake services and having to understand

122
00:07:19,530 --> 00:07:23,120
how to mock stuff out and really kind of massage your code around.

123
00:07:23,130 --> 00:07:24,540
So I just want to repeat one more time.

124
00:07:24,540 --> 00:07:27,870
I know this stuff is really hard, hopefully kind of getting the hang of it.

125
00:07:27,870 --> 00:07:31,920
So let's take another pause right here and write a couple more tests in the coming videos.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,390 --> 00:00:04,120
We've got two tests around the sign up process.

2
00:00:04,120 --> 00:00:08,840
So let's now start to add in some tests around the sign in method of our authentication service.

3
00:00:08,860 --> 00:00:13,570
So here's the method right here at the very top, we've got one block of logic where we take a look

4
00:00:13,570 --> 00:00:18,010
at our user service and try to find a user with the email matching the one that is provided to this

5
00:00:18,010 --> 00:00:18,670
function.

6
00:00:18,700 --> 00:00:23,950
Now, unlike the sign up method in this case, we do want to find a user because if there's no user

7
00:00:23,950 --> 00:00:28,810
inside of our database with that matching email, and that means that the user has given us an invalid

8
00:00:28,840 --> 00:00:30,000
email address.

9
00:00:30,010 --> 00:00:35,980
So let's put in a test right away that makes sure that if we do not find any users matching that email,

10
00:00:35,980 --> 00:00:38,140
we immediately throw an error message.

11
00:00:38,410 --> 00:00:43,060
So back inside my test file at the very bottom, I'm going to add in another if statement.

12
00:00:43,090 --> 00:00:54,430
We'll say it throws if a or how about it throws if sign in is called with an unused email.

13
00:00:57,650 --> 00:01:01,040
Once again, we're going to put in async and done right there.

14
00:01:02,350 --> 00:01:07,060
So then inside of here, in this case, we don't need to mess around with the find function in any way

15
00:01:07,060 --> 00:01:12,610
as we did with the last test, because in this case, we want to use the default implementation of the

16
00:01:12,610 --> 00:01:16,900
find function where we return an empty array, because that's what we're trying to make sure of here.

17
00:01:16,900 --> 00:01:21,490
We want to get back an empty array so we fail to find the user and we throw an error.

18
00:01:21,700 --> 00:01:28,000
So for this test, all we have to do is the same kind of try catch setup.

19
00:01:29,340 --> 00:01:30,090
Inside the try.

20
00:01:30,120 --> 00:01:31,410
We'll do an await service.

21
00:01:31,650 --> 00:01:33,840
Sign in with any email address.

22
00:01:33,840 --> 00:01:38,670
Doesn't matter any password and then we'll call done inside the catch.

23
00:01:39,740 --> 00:01:42,680
So let's save this back over to our terminal.

24
00:01:43,920 --> 00:01:45,360
And it looks like we are good to go.

25
00:01:45,390 --> 00:01:46,110
Awesome.

26
00:01:47,610 --> 00:01:53,060
Okay, so that's one test down for at least that handling that no email found case.

27

So let's take another quick pause and take a look at how we're going to test out the password comparison

28
00:01:57,960 --> 00:02:01,500
process, because this is actually going to be a little bit more challenging.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,360 --> 00:00:05,180
There are two other tests would like to write around our sine function.

00:00:05,180 --> 00:00:08,760
So we've got one test where we do not find a user with a given email.

3

4
00:00:13,880 --> 00:00:15,410

5
00:00:15,560 --> 00:00:19,670

6
00:00:19,700 --> 00:00:22,280

7
00:00:22,400 --> 00:00:27,920
Back inside my test file at the very bottom, I'm going to add in an if statement and say it throws

8
00:00:27,950 --> 00:00:31,190
if an invalid password is provided.

9
00:00:34,070 --> 00:00:36,950
And once again, I'm going to use that done argument.

10
00:00:39,130 --> 00:00:39,490
Okay.

11
00:00:39,490 --> 00:00:45,760
So then inside of here, this time around, we need to make sure that we do, in fact, return a user

12
00:00:45,760 --> 00:00:48,030
record from calling the find method.

13
00:00:48,040 --> 00:00:49,960
So very similar to what we did earlier.

14
00:00:49,960 --> 00:00:54,730
We need to mess around with the implementation of our find method for just this one particular test.

15
00:00:54,730 --> 00:00:59,230
So we're going to take a look at our fake user service and we're going to reassign the find property.

16
00:00:59,230 --> 00:01:01,930
We're going to make sure that we return a user.

17
00:01:02,760 --> 00:01:05,210
Whenever we run our sign in function.

18
00:01:05,220 --> 00:01:10,860
So inside of our new test, we'll say fake users service dot find.

00:01:11,490 --> 00:01:14,640
I'm going to put in a new line here just so you can very easily read the line.

20
00:01:15,430 --> 00:01:18,430
And then we'll do a resolve or promise.resolve.

21
00:01:19,850 --> 00:01:22,000
An array with an object.

22
00:01:22,010 --> 00:01:27,560
I'll give them an email of whatever and a password of whatever.

23
00:01:29,460 --> 00:01:34,440
And I'm going to specifically tell TypeScript to consider that object as being a user record.

24
00:01:36,020 --> 00:01:36,290
Okay.

25
00:01:36,290 --> 00:01:37,100
That looks good.

26
00:01:38,420 --> 00:01:41,660
So then I'm going to put in my try.

27
00:01:43,900 --> 00:01:44,920
And my catch.

28
00:01:45,860 --> 00:01:47,580
I'll call service.

29
00:01:47,900 --> 00:01:48,950
Sign in.

30
00:01:49,560 --> 00:01:52,140
And we can provide any email here that we want to provide.

31
00:01:52,140 --> 00:01:57,000
Because remember, no matter what email we give, we are always going to return that user object.

32
00:01:57,030 --> 00:01:59,910
So we can put in any email here doesn't really make a difference.

33
00:02:00,930 --> 00:02:07,080
And I'm going to put in a password that will definitely not compare in any way, shape or form against

34
00:02:07,080 --> 00:02:09,120
the password that gets returned.

35
00:02:09,390 --> 00:02:15,240
So remember this user right here inside of our find method is going to show up inside of our sign in

36
00:02:15,240 --> 00:02:17,040
function as that user.

37
00:02:17,130 --> 00:02:21,840
This user, we are assuming, has a password that is salted and hashed.

38
00:02:22,750 --> 00:02:27,610
And we're going to hash the provided password and then compare the two of them.

39
00:02:28,700 --> 00:02:33,290
So without a doubt, if we just put in any random string of characters for the stored password and this

40
00:02:33,290 --> 00:02:36,860
one right here, the password comparison is definitely not going to work as expected.

41
00:02:36,860 --> 00:02:41,030
And without a doubt we will be throwing a bad request exception.

42
00:02:43,460 --> 00:02:45,160
Okay, so all we need is the try.

43
00:02:45,170 --> 00:02:47,810
We can pretty much call it with any password you can possibly imagine.

44
00:02:47,810 --> 00:02:50,270
And the comparison is not going to work as expected.

45
00:02:50,450 --> 00:02:53,150
So then finally, inside of the catch, I'll put in a done.

46

All right, let's save this.

Go back over to our terminal and see how we're doing.

48

Looks like it passed.

49
00:03:02,540 --> 00:03:03,230

00:03:07,910 --> 00:03:11,090
How about by just returning?

52
00:03:11,210 --> 00:03:12,610
How about I'll come out the done?

53
00:03:12,620 --> 00:03:13,390
That'll work.

54
00:03:13,400 --> 00:03:17,960
So I'm not going to call done that will make the test time out again just making sure that the test

55

56

57
00:03:24,950 --> 00:03:25,220

58
00:03:25,220 --> 00:03:25,910

59

Find right there, paste it down here.

67

I'll then get my user.

68
00:04:07,690 --> 00:04:08,440
By calling service.

69
00:04:08,610 --> 00:04:12,970
Sign in and I'll provide once again the email doesn't matter because no matter what, we're always going

70
00:04:12,970 --> 00:04:15,220
to send back exactly that object right there.

71
00:04:15,730 --> 00:04:17,170
So I'll put in any email.

72
00:04:18,930 --> 00:04:20,970
And any password as well.

73
00:04:23,690 --> 00:04:24,110
Okay.

74
00:04:24,110 --> 00:04:29,180
So then after we create that user, we might write out a test and say something like expect user to

75
00:04:29,180 --> 00:04:30,100
be defined.

76
00:04:30,110 --> 00:04:34,490
So we're just going to say, Yeah, we successfully compared the passwords, we found the user and we

77
00:04:34,490 --> 00:04:36,110
are returning them from that function.

78
00:04:36,440 --> 00:04:39,140
So now here's where things start to get really interesting.

79
00:04:39,140 --> 00:04:41,900
So another reminder on the sign in function.

80
00:04:41,900 --> 00:04:44,090
So we are going to supply a password.

81
00:04:45,590 --> 00:04:49,580
We're going to try to find a user with the matching supplied email address.

82
00:04:49,580 --> 00:04:54,920
So our user right here during our test suite running is going to be the user that we return from our

83
00:04:54,920 --> 00:04:56,690
fake find method.

84
00:04:57,200 --> 00:04:59,990
We then take the stored password.

85
00:05:00,690 --> 00:05:01,270
From the user.

86
00:05:01,290 --> 00:05:03,270
User that is coming from the fake find method.

87
00:05:03,270 --> 00:05:06,390
And we pull off that salt and the hash.

88
00:05:07,060 --> 00:05:09,580
We then use the supplied password.

89
00:05:10,570 --> 00:05:11,620
And the salt.

90
00:05:12,530 --> 00:05:18,680
To generate a new hash and we compare that hash against the stored hash, and that is our password comparison

91
00:05:18,680 --> 00:05:19,470
process.

92
00:05:19,490 --> 00:05:22,670
And this is all the logic that we were actually trying to test here.

93
00:05:22,850 --> 00:05:29,330
So to correctly test all this stuff, we have to supply right here for the user coming from the find

94
00:05:29,330 --> 00:05:35,330
method, we need to supply a password that is correctly salted and hashed like a real salted and hash

95
00:05:35,330 --> 00:05:36,080
password.

96
00:05:36,200 --> 00:05:42,320
And whenever we call sign in with it has to be a password that is going to correctly compare against

97
00:05:42,320 --> 00:05:44,240
that salted and hashed password.

98
00:05:44,270 --> 00:05:50,570
So in other words, we need a very real password and the very real salt and hash to go along with it.

99
00:05:50,930 --> 00:05:52,760
So there are two ways we can go about this.

100
00:05:52,770 --> 00:05:57,230
I'm going to first show you the kind of jankier way that is not always going to work.

101
00:05:57,230 --> 00:06:00,950
Well, it's always going to work correctly, but it's really maybe not just the best way to set everything

102
00:06:00,950 --> 00:06:01,280
up.

103
00:06:01,280 --> 00:06:05,210
So I'll show you the kind of quick and dirty, easy way first and we'll take a look at a much more involved

104
00:06:05,210 --> 00:06:07,850
way of making our code work as expected.

105
00:06:08,000 --> 00:06:09,950
So here's what we are going to do.

106
00:06:10,130 --> 00:06:13,350
I'm going to comment out everything inside this test for right now.

107
00:06:13,970 --> 00:06:15,170
I'm then going to.

108
00:06:16,470 --> 00:06:17,340
Create a user.

109
00:06:18,370 --> 00:06:19,960
With our sign up function.

110
00:06:21,710 --> 00:06:23,720
And a password of my password.

111
00:06:23,960 --> 00:06:26,990
I'll then console log out the user that gets returned.

112
00:06:27,020 --> 00:06:32,480
So remember this user right here, they're going to have an ID, an email and a password as well because

113
00:06:32,480 --> 00:06:34,610
we are generating that hash and whatnot.

114
00:06:35,240 --> 00:06:37,790
So I'm going to oops, I'm going to save this.

115
00:06:37,790 --> 00:06:41,300
I'm go back into my terminal and I'm going to take a look at that console log.

116
00:06:42,120 --> 00:06:43,170
So back over here.

117
00:06:44,080 --> 00:06:45,500
Yep, there's the console log.

118
00:06:45,520 --> 00:06:49,210
So here is a actual salt and hashed password.

119
00:06:50,620 --> 00:06:51,970
For my password.

120
00:06:51,970 --> 00:06:55,060
So it is a salt in a hash that works for my password.

121
00:06:55,060 --> 00:06:58,780
Those two things are going to compare successfully inside of our sign in function.

122
00:06:59,460 --> 00:07:00,600
So I'm going to take.

123
00:07:01,390 --> 00:07:03,300
This salted and hashed password.

124
00:07:03,310 --> 00:07:04,600
I'm going to copy it.

125
00:07:06,840 --> 00:07:10,260
I'm going to uncomment everything we just wrote for our test.

126
00:07:11,020 --> 00:07:16,450
And I'm going to take that copy paste I just got and put it in for the password of the stored user.

127
00:07:17,230 --> 00:07:19,900
So there is my salted and hashed password.

128
00:07:21,200 --> 00:07:24,020
I'm then going to clean up the sign up call down here.

129
00:07:25,180 --> 00:07:32,290
And now if we run our test and we try to take my password and salt it and hash it and compare it against

130
00:07:32,290 --> 00:07:37,500
the hash section of our password, that comparison should be done successfully.

131
00:07:37,510 --> 00:07:39,670
So now our test should pass.

132
00:07:39,820 --> 00:07:42,130
So I'm going to save this and go back over to my terminal.

133
00:07:44,310 --> 00:07:46,090
And everything should work now.

134
00:07:46,110 --> 00:07:47,730
Hopefully we'll see what happens.

135
00:07:48,030 --> 00:07:48,560
Yep.

136
00:07:48,570 --> 00:07:49,440
It looks good.

137
00:07:50,140 --> 00:07:55,270
So now the downside to this approach is, of course, we've got a test with a really long password inside

138
00:07:55,270 --> 00:07:55,840
of it.

139
00:07:55,840 --> 00:07:58,650
And this I don't know, just something about this just doesn't feel right.

140
00:07:58,660 --> 00:08:03,490
If someone ever just accidentally changes that, it's no longer going to compare correctly against specifically

141
00:08:03,490 --> 00:08:04,870
the string my password.

142
00:08:04,870 --> 00:08:06,590
And so our test would start to fail.

143
00:08:06,610 --> 00:08:10,840
In addition, there might be other locations inside of our project where we might want to, I don't

144
00:08:10,840 --> 00:08:16,870
know, actually create a user with a real email, real password, hash it and do all this stuff behind

145
00:08:16,870 --> 00:08:18,080
the scenes correctly.

146
00:08:18,100 --> 00:08:21,750
So even though this works, it still doesn't really feel all that great.

147
00:08:21,820 --> 00:08:23,920
So as I mentioned, we're going to take a pause right here.

148
00:08:23,920 --> 00:08:25,120
We're going to come back in just a moment.

149
00:08:25,120 --> 00:08:30,460
And I'm going to show you another way of setting up our fake user service where we should not have to

150
00:08:30,460 --> 00:08:33,970
manually run our tests once, get a password and then paste it in here.

151
00:08:33,970 --> 00:08:34,660
So quick pause.

152
00:08:34,660 --> 00:08:37,150
We'll take a look at that alternate solution in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,610 --> 00:00:04,700
Our password comparison test is definitely working, without a doubt.

2
00:00:04,700 --> 00:00:09,440
And we can leave our code as is, and it would not break anytime soon, so long as we do not actually

3
00:00:09,440 --> 00:00:13,280
change our password hashing implementation or comparison process.

4
00:00:13,490 --> 00:00:19,490
Nonetheless, having a really long string like this inside of any of our tests just really doesn't feel

5
00:00:19,490 --> 00:00:21,170
that right, per se.

6
00:00:21,200 --> 00:00:26,630
So I want to show you another way of setting up our fake user service to allow us to test stuff like

7
00:00:26,630 --> 00:00:30,920
exactly this in a much more convenient and easy and kind of straightforward way.

8
00:00:31,070 --> 00:00:32,760
So here's what we're going to do.

9
00:00:32,780 --> 00:00:34,520
Back up at the very top of the file.

10
00:00:35,530 --> 00:00:38,090
I'm going to find my fake user service.

11
00:00:38,110 --> 00:00:43,060
So remember, this entire service right here really functioned around the idea of having some different

12
00:00:43,060 --> 00:00:46,870
methods that are always going to return some fixed set of data.

13
00:00:46,960 --> 00:00:48,190
So here's the plan.

14
00:00:48,220 --> 00:00:49,330
Here's what we're going to do.

15
00:00:49,360 --> 00:00:55,150
Rather than having find and create be just very static methods that don't change or don't return any

16
00:00:55,150 --> 00:00:59,290
different data, no matter how many times they're called, we're going to actually put together a slightly

17
00:00:59,290 --> 00:01:02,680
more realistic version of the fake user's service.

18
00:01:02,860 --> 00:01:08,410
So whenever we call create, we're going to actually take the supplied email and password and store

19
00:01:08,410 --> 00:01:09,650
it inside of an array.

20
00:01:09,670 --> 00:01:15,100
And then whenever we call find, we're going to search through that array and try to find a list of

21
00:01:15,100 --> 00:01:17,680
users with a matching email address.

22
00:01:18,070 --> 00:01:22,770
So in other words, we're going to put together just a slightly more advanced version of fake user service.

23
00:01:22,780 --> 00:01:24,230
Now, how is that going to help us at all?

24
00:01:24,250 --> 00:01:29,160
Well, it's going to help us avoid having to do this kind of complicated setup down here.

25
00:01:29,170 --> 00:01:33,610
If fake user service actually worked correctly, here's what we could do instead.

26
00:01:33,640 --> 00:01:35,600
We could comment all that out.

27
00:01:35,950 --> 00:01:36,850
And then.

28
00:01:38,020 --> 00:01:39,610
Before trying to sign in.

29
00:01:39,640 --> 00:01:40,870
We could simply.

30
00:01:44,190 --> 00:01:49,320
I'll sign up, pass in some email address and some password.

31
00:01:49,500 --> 00:01:54,990
Now the sign up function is going to work as it usually does, and it's going to make use of a slightly

32
00:01:54,990 --> 00:01:57,270
more realistic version of the user service.

33
00:01:57,270 --> 00:02:03,180
So it's going to kind of temporarily in memory store a user and give us the ability to find that user.

34
00:02:03,270 --> 00:02:10,770
So this will store a user in memory with the given email and a hashed and salted password matching my

35
00:02:10,770 --> 00:02:11,510
password.

36
00:02:11,520 --> 00:02:16,560
So we can then call service dot sign in and assuming that we put together our fake service correctly,

37
00:02:16,560 --> 00:02:21,810
it should just plain work and we won't have to worry about putting together these promise.resolve statements

38
00:02:21,810 --> 00:02:24,740
or updating the implementation of the find method all the time.

39
00:02:24,750 --> 00:02:29,520
So in other words, we can just use our service that we're trying to test in a slightly more realistic

40
00:02:29,520 --> 00:02:33,720
way, which definitely, I think, results in some more useful testing.

41
00:02:34,640 --> 00:02:34,940
Okay.

42
00:02:34,940 --> 00:02:35,630
So that's the plan.

43
00:02:35,630 --> 00:02:36,220
That's the goal.

44
00:02:36,230 --> 00:02:41,300
We're going to put together a slightly more realistic version of our fake user service.

45
00:02:41,450 --> 00:02:43,040
So let's just go ahead and write some code.

46
00:02:43,040 --> 00:02:45,800
I think you'll get an idea of what we're doing here very quickly.

47
00:02:46,040 --> 00:02:51,170
So to get started right above that service, I'm going to create a new array called users, and I'm

48
00:02:51,170 --> 00:02:54,740
going to annotate it as being of type array of user entities.

49
00:02:54,890 --> 00:02:56,630
I'll have it start off as empty.

50
00:02:57,900 --> 00:03:02,940
We're then going to update our find function and the create function to operate and store users inside

51
00:03:02,940 --> 00:03:03,690
that array.

52
00:03:03,780 --> 00:03:05,580
So first off, find.

53
00:03:05,610 --> 00:03:10,620
Now remember, our real find function takes in an argument of an email address.

54
00:03:10,650 --> 00:03:16,110
And since we are actually going to look through a list of real users here or semi real users, we definitely

55
00:03:16,110 --> 00:03:19,380
want to receive that email and use it for our filtering logic.

56
00:03:21,560 --> 00:03:27,500
So inside of here, I'm going to say filtered users will be users dot filter.

57
00:03:27,530 --> 00:03:35,090
We're going to take a look at every user and then return true where user dot email is equal to the supplied

58
00:03:35,090 --> 00:03:35,660
email.

59
00:03:39,550 --> 00:03:43,720
I'll then return promise.resolve filtered users.

60
00:03:43,960 --> 00:03:45,750
So why do we still have the promise.resolve here?

61
00:03:45,760 --> 00:03:47,910
Well, remember, find is supposed to be asynchronous.

62
00:03:47,920 --> 00:03:52,780
We're supposed to return a promise from it that is going to eventually resolve with a list of users.

63
00:03:52,780 --> 00:03:54,880
So we still have to have a promise.resolve.

64
00:03:56,280 --> 00:03:57,190
Yeah, that looks good.

65
00:03:57,210 --> 00:04:00,030
So now we're going to do something very similar for Create as well.

66
00:04:00,150 --> 00:04:02,370
So I'm going to put in a function body.

67
00:04:06,140 --> 00:04:12,290
And inside of here, we're going to just create a new user entity and put it into our array of users.

68
00:04:12,560 --> 00:04:15,590
So we'll say users dot push.

69
00:04:16,250 --> 00:04:20,910
With a given email and password and we should probably supply an ID as well.

70
00:04:20,930 --> 00:04:25,490
So for an ID, I'll just say how about an ID of users?

71
00:04:26,460 --> 00:04:28,380
Dot length.

72
00:04:29,390 --> 00:04:34,100
Now, that's not the best strategy right there, because if we start removing users, the idea is going

73
00:04:34,100 --> 00:04:34,370
to break.

74
00:04:34,370 --> 00:04:37,700
We're going to eventually get some duplicate users, but this is good enough for now.

75
00:04:37,730 --> 00:04:38,780
If you don't like that, well, you know what?

76
00:04:38,810 --> 00:04:40,520
Let's just go ahead and put in a real solution here.

77
00:04:40,520 --> 00:04:50,090
So how about something like math dot random times, a really big number, and then we'll do a math dot

78
00:04:50,090 --> 00:04:51,470
floor on that.

79
00:04:53,260 --> 00:04:55,010
So that will give us our random ID.

80
00:04:56,680 --> 00:04:58,060
Our email and our password.

81
00:04:58,840 --> 00:05:01,900
And we're going to also mark that object as being a user.

82
00:05:04,750 --> 00:05:08,560
Once we have created that new user, we need to return it from the create function.

83
00:05:08,560 --> 00:05:11,560
So guess I'm sorry, I actually kind of made a little mistake here.

84
00:05:11,560 --> 00:05:16,840
Rather than calling users dot push with that whole object right away, let's first assign that object

85
00:05:16,840 --> 00:05:18,220
to a user variable.

86
00:05:18,920 --> 00:05:21,380
So I'll say user is that big object.

87
00:05:21,410 --> 00:05:27,020
I'll then push in that new user and then return the newly created user and we're going to wrap that

88
00:05:27,290 --> 00:05:28,460
with the promise.resolve.

89
00:05:28,490 --> 00:05:29,360
That's better.

90
00:05:32,170 --> 00:05:35,510
Okay, so we now have a slightly more realistic find and create function.

91
00:05:35,530 --> 00:05:39,100
Now, without a doubt, making this change might break some of our existing tests.

92
00:05:39,130 --> 00:05:39,520
Who knows?

93
00:05:39,520 --> 00:05:40,500
We'll see what happens.

94
00:05:40,510 --> 00:05:45,130
But let's now go down to our sign in test that we were just working on and see how much easier it is

95
00:05:45,130 --> 00:05:45,460
to write.

96
00:05:45,490 --> 00:05:51,940
Now, since we have a more realistic fake user service, so no longer do we have to set up the find

97
00:05:51,940 --> 00:05:53,660
function in some very particular way.

98
00:05:53,680 --> 00:05:59,770
So can I can remove that entirely and instead I can first sign up my user.

99
00:06:03,960 --> 00:06:06,210
And then attempt to sign in as them.

100
00:06:07,120 --> 00:06:08,050
And that should be it.

101
00:06:08,050 --> 00:06:09,220
So let's save this.

102
00:06:09,250 --> 00:06:13,390
Even if some tests fail, I'm still going to expect our last test to pass.

103
00:06:13,660 --> 00:06:15,190
So back over at the terminal.

104
00:06:17,250 --> 00:06:17,640
Yep.

105
00:06:17,640 --> 00:06:18,690
Looks like it passed.

106
00:06:19,560 --> 00:06:20,250
Very good.

107
00:06:21,080 --> 00:06:24,890
As usual, let's try to get it to fail, because this is the one time where I do want to make sure everything

108
00:06:24,890 --> 00:06:26,090
is working as expected.

109
00:06:26,090 --> 00:06:27,560
So I'll put in my password one.

110
00:06:27,560 --> 00:06:29,390
So that's definitely an invalid password.

111
00:06:30,390 --> 00:06:31,860
I save that and rerun.

112
00:06:32,010 --> 00:06:34,680
We should now be told that we got a bad password.

113
00:06:37,330 --> 00:06:37,900
Sure enough.

114
00:06:37,900 --> 00:06:38,680
There we go.

115
00:06:38,680 --> 00:06:39,910
So bad Password.

116
00:06:40,840 --> 00:06:42,730
Okay, so I'd say this looks very good.

117
00:06:42,760 --> 00:06:45,520
Now, why did we not just do this from the get go?

118
00:06:45,550 --> 00:06:49,810
Why didn't we not just say, Hey, here's our fake user service, We're going to store an actual list

119
00:06:49,810 --> 00:06:51,730
of users in memory during our test.

120
00:06:51,760 --> 00:06:53,020
The reason is very simple.

121
00:06:53,020 --> 00:06:59,440
In some cases you might be trying to mock or fake out a much more complex service that has functions

122
00:06:59,440 --> 00:07:04,780
that do a lot more than just a simple find and create where you are essentially storing records inside

123
00:07:04,780 --> 00:07:05,560
of an array.

124
00:07:05,770 --> 00:07:11,410
So in some cases it does make sense to put in a method like, say, find one.

125
00:07:12,340 --> 00:07:16,290
That will always just immediately resolve with some fixed value.

126
00:07:16,300 --> 00:07:18,880
In some cases, that's just going to be a lot easier.

127
00:07:18,880 --> 00:07:23,890
But in other cases, if the service that you're trying to mock or fake out is going to be a little bit

128
00:07:23,890 --> 00:07:28,600
more simple in nature, such as just the simple find and create, well then fantastic.

129
00:07:28,600 --> 00:07:32,920
Just do that instead because it might might make some other tests really easy and straightforward.

130
00:07:34,080 --> 00:07:34,350
All right.

131
00:07:34,350 --> 00:07:38,820
So last thing I'd like to do very quickly in the next video is now go around to our different tests.

132
00:07:38,820 --> 00:07:44,940
And rather than customizing the find method and kind of tweaking it to be exactly as we expect, we're

133
00:07:44,940 --> 00:07:49,620
going to instead just make use of our service directly and call different methods on it to simulate

134
00:07:49,620 --> 00:07:52,530
stuff like, say, an email already being in use.

135
00:07:52,650 --> 00:07:56,040
So quick pause right here and we'll do that last little refactor in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,720 --> 00:00:05,980
As mentioned, we're going to go through our existing tests very quickly rather than customizing the

2
00:00:05,980 --> 00:00:07,990
find method on a per test basis.

3
00:00:08,020 --> 00:00:12,590
We're going to just call our service directly and try to set up the different scenarios.

4
00:00:12,610 --> 00:00:15,730
So the first one we're going to take a look at is where we throw an error.

5
00:00:15,730 --> 00:00:18,610
If a user signs up with an email that is already in use.

6
00:00:18,730 --> 00:00:24,250
So to simulate this without changing our find method implementation, all we have to do is call sign

7
00:00:24,250 --> 00:00:26,980
up twice in a row with the same email.

8
00:00:27,280 --> 00:00:29,110
So I'm going to outside the try.

9
00:00:29,930 --> 00:00:30,710
Do a service.

10
00:00:30,950 --> 00:00:31,700
Sign up.

11
00:00:33,670 --> 00:00:35,920
With a password doesn't actually have to match.

12
00:00:35,980 --> 00:00:37,080
That doesn't make a difference.

13
00:00:37,090 --> 00:00:39,820
All that matters is that we make use of the same password.

14
00:00:40,060 --> 00:00:44,140
So now if we save this and run our test again.

15
00:00:46,980 --> 00:00:48,930
They should still be passing at this point.

16
00:00:49,680 --> 00:00:52,190
Oh, I forgot to fix up that last one down at the bottom.

17
00:00:52,200 --> 00:00:52,890
I never changed it.

18
00:00:52,890 --> 00:00:55,910
Back to the correct password.

19
00:00:55,920 --> 00:00:56,760
There we go.

20
00:00:57,000 --> 00:00:58,110
Let me change that.

21
00:00:58,530 --> 00:01:00,330
Go back to the one that we were just working on.

22
00:01:00,360 --> 00:01:01,440
Here it is right here.

23
00:01:01,740 --> 00:01:03,060
Run those tests again.

24
00:01:05,880 --> 00:01:06,540
There we go.

25
00:01:08,040 --> 00:01:12,480
And the reason I put this first one outside of the try statement is because I definitely expect this

26
00:01:12,480 --> 00:01:13,440
call to succeed.

27
00:01:13,440 --> 00:01:15,240
I want that first one to run successfully.

28
00:01:15,240 --> 00:01:20,820
If I put it inside the try and it failed, well then I would have a tough time deciding whether this

29
00:01:20,820 --> 00:01:27,480
test passed because the first call incorrectly threw an error or the second call correctly threw an

30
00:01:27,480 --> 00:01:27,750
error.

31
00:01:27,750 --> 00:01:30,060
So that's why the first one is just going outside.

32
00:01:31,900 --> 00:01:32,860
Hey, that looks good.

33
00:01:33,130 --> 00:01:34,840
So let's keep on going down.

34
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:38,580
Looks like the next one here is throws if an invalid password is provided.

35
00:01:38,590 --> 00:01:43,710
So once again, this would be a very simple one to set up without having to redefine the find method.

36
00:01:43,720 --> 00:01:45,460
So I'm going to first.

37
00:01:46,470 --> 00:01:51,270
Sign up with a real email or the same email.

38
00:01:52,970 --> 00:01:54,710
And a different password.

39
00:01:54,710 --> 00:01:56,660
So I'll put in a very different password right there.

40
00:01:56,660 --> 00:01:59,630
So now the emails are the same, but the passwords are different.

41
00:01:59,630 --> 00:02:03,650
So this will correctly simulate an invalid password being provided.

42
00:02:04,400 --> 00:02:05,420
So let's save this.

43
00:02:05,450 --> 00:02:07,910
We're going to make sure it passes.

44
00:02:12,360 --> 00:02:13,830
And, yep, looks good.

45
00:02:14,220 --> 00:02:17,230
