So then finally, let's try making them making this pass.

46
00:02:17,230 --> 00:02:18,960
So I'm going to give the same password.

47
00:02:21,830 --> 00:02:22,280
Excuse me.

48
00:02:22,280 --> 00:02:23,060
This would not pass.

49
00:02:23,060 --> 00:02:25,870
This is going to fail because we're never going to hit the done statement.

50
00:02:25,880 --> 00:02:30,350
So I'm just going to make sure that we see a timeout of this test after about five seconds or so.

51
00:02:30,970 --> 00:02:31,630
And so.

52
00:02:31,630 --> 00:02:33,070
Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait.

53
00:02:33,100 --> 00:02:34,110
Just is so slow.

54
00:02:34,180 --> 00:02:35,050
Got to be honest with you.

55
00:02:35,050 --> 00:02:36,010
And yeah, there we go.

56
00:02:36,010 --> 00:02:40,180
So that failed because we were expecting to see a error being thrown.

57
00:02:40,180 --> 00:02:43,120
So now we can change the passwords to not be the same anymore.

58
00:02:43,120 --> 00:02:44,800
And it should be working again.

59
00:02:45,760 --> 00:02:46,090
All right.

60
00:02:46,090 --> 00:02:46,750
So that is it.

61
00:02:46,750 --> 00:02:53,170
That is testing of a single service by setting up a mock or fake service as a dependency.

62
00:02:53,380 --> 00:02:58,570
Again, two ways to approach this for the mocking side either set up very simple static functions that

63
00:02:58,570 --> 00:03:02,600
return static data or put together an actual implementation.

64
00:03:02,620 --> 00:03:07,720
It might seem like putting together an actual implementation is a huge pain and takes extra time, but

65
00:03:07,720 --> 00:03:11,440
honestly, at the end of the day, it's not the worst thing in the world and it allows you to pace to

66
00:03:11,440 --> 00:03:14,980
practice some really basic logic or putting together some really basic functions.

67
00:03:14,980 --> 00:03:18,340
So definitely not the worst thing, even though it does take some extra time.

68
00:03:18,880 --> 00:03:19,180
Okay.

69
00:03:19,180 --> 00:03:20,850
So that is it for our first test.

70
00:03:20,860 --> 00:03:23,440
Let's take a pause here and continue in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,860 --> 00:00:04,550
I think we have enough tests around our authentication service, so we're going to move on and take

2
00:00:04,550 --> 00:00:07,140
a look at testing something else inside of our application.

3
00:00:07,160 --> 00:00:11,510
In particular, we're going to write a couple of tests around our users controller.

4
00:00:11,600 --> 00:00:16,380
Now the users controller is going to be very interesting to test for two different reasons.

5
00:00:16,400 --> 00:00:19,530
First off, the user's controller has two different dependencies.

6
00:00:19,550 --> 00:00:25,080
That means we're going to have to write out a fake copy of the user service and the auth service.

7
00:00:25,100 --> 00:00:31,100
So that means twice the amount of mock code, kind of like the same thing we did around the fake user

8
00:00:31,100 --> 00:00:34,490
service just a moment ago when we were testing the authentication service.

9
00:00:34,700 --> 00:00:37,100
So that's the first thing that's going to be kind of interesting.

10
00:00:37,100 --> 00:00:41,420
The second thing that's going to be very interesting is that you're going to very quickly see that we

11
00:00:41,420 --> 00:00:46,340
are not really writing out a lot of code or really, I should say there's not really a lot of test stuff

12
00:00:46,340 --> 00:00:48,090
to test inside this controller.

13
00:00:48,110 --> 00:00:51,770
So, for example, if we take a look at the route handler.

14
00:00:51,800 --> 00:00:56,880
All this thing does is take in an argument of a user and returns that exact argument.

15
00:00:56,900 --> 00:00:59,450
So there's really not a whole lot to test there.

16
00:00:59,600 --> 00:01:04,530
Your immediate response to that might be, well, Steven, what about all these decorators and whatnot?

17
00:01:04,560 --> 00:01:10,020
It turns out that unit testing decorators that are hooked up to a method is actually rather challenging

18
00:01:10,020 --> 00:01:10,710
with Nest.

19
00:01:10,740 --> 00:01:12,370
TypeScript in general as well.

20
00:01:12,390 --> 00:01:17,490
So whenever we unit test a controller, we're not really quite so much going to test out the different

21
00:01:17,490 --> 00:01:19,220
decorators that we're applying to a method.

22
00:01:19,230 --> 00:01:25,110
Instead, we can almost imagine that we're going to test everything except those decorators.

23
00:01:25,110 --> 00:01:27,300
So we're left with just this right here.

24
00:01:27,300 --> 00:01:31,110
And when you look at this function, again, not a whole lot to test.

25
00:01:31,640 --> 00:01:35,030
Nonetheless, we're going to give this a shot and just see how far we can get.

26
00:01:35,060 --> 00:01:38,270
Writing out some tests, even though they aren't super, super useful.

27
00:01:39,520 --> 00:01:45,120
So to get started on testing the user's controller, we're going to open up the user's controller spec.

28
00:01:45,130 --> 00:01:46,140
Here it is right here.

29
00:01:46,150 --> 00:01:48,580
This file was generated for us automatically.

30
00:01:48,700 --> 00:01:52,330
You'll notice that the contents of the file look very similar to the test file we put together just

31
00:01:52,330 --> 00:01:53,200
a moment ago.

32
00:01:53,200 --> 00:01:58,900
So generally, same exact structure to get started inside of here at the very top, we are going to

33
00:01:58,900 --> 00:02:00,850
add in a couple of different imports.

34
00:02:00,970 --> 00:02:03,610
First, the user's service.

35
00:02:06,490 --> 00:02:08,800
Then the off service.

36
00:02:11,270 --> 00:02:14,060
And finally, the user entity.

37
00:02:18,120 --> 00:02:18,480
Okay.

38
00:02:18,480 --> 00:02:21,990
So now inside of the testing module right here that is being created.

39
00:02:21,990 --> 00:02:26,070
And remember, we can really think of this as being an isolated container.

40
00:02:26,100 --> 00:02:31,230
We are trying to create an instance of a users controller, but when we try to create an instance of

41
00:02:31,230 --> 00:02:36,540
that controller, we're definitely going to get an error because inside the container we have not provided

42
00:02:36,540 --> 00:02:39,600
anything to be used for the dependencies of that class.

43
00:02:39,720 --> 00:02:45,750
Again, the controller requires a copy of users and auth service and we are currently not providing

44
00:02:45,750 --> 00:02:47,460
that inside this container.

45
00:02:47,550 --> 00:02:54,150
So you and I have to make a fake or mock copy of these two services and then provide those fakes to

46
00:02:54,150 --> 00:02:55,350
that container.

47
00:02:56,080 --> 00:03:01,480
Then whenever the container tries to create an instance of the user's controller right here, it's going

48
00:03:01,480 --> 00:03:05,130
to use the two mocks or those two fakes that we've put together.

49
00:03:05,140 --> 00:03:09,010
So it's the exact same setup that we did inside of our auth service a moment ago.

50
00:03:09,280 --> 00:03:14,740
So that means essentially same exact code we had before right outside of our before each statement,

51
00:03:14,770 --> 00:03:18,260
we are going to declare two variables with the let keyword.

52
00:03:18,280 --> 00:03:26,430
First will be fake user's service that's going to be of type user's service just like before.

53
00:03:26,440 --> 00:03:30,340
You and I probably don't want to have to implement all the different methods of that user service if

54
00:03:30,340 --> 00:03:31,900
they're not actually being used.

55
00:03:31,930 --> 00:03:34,660
So we are going to annotate this with partial.

56
00:03:35,360 --> 00:03:39,260
Remember, that means that we're only going to implement a couple of different properties or a couple

57
00:03:39,260 --> 00:03:41,150
of different methods on the user service.

58
00:03:41,180 --> 00:03:46,100
If we did not put in the word partial, then TypeScript would want to make sure that we implement every

59
00:03:46,100 --> 00:03:47,690
single method on the service correctly.

60
00:03:47,690 --> 00:03:50,180
And again, we don't want to have to write out all that code.

61
00:03:51,160 --> 00:03:54,430
I'm then going to also create fake auth service.

62
00:04:00,160 --> 00:04:00,520
Okay.

63
00:04:00,550 --> 00:04:05,380
Next up inside of our before each at the very top, that's where we are going to put together a fake

64
00:04:05,380 --> 00:04:07,060
definition for these two things.

65
00:04:07,060 --> 00:04:13,360
So I'll first say fake users service and then fake auth service.

66
00:04:14,590 --> 00:04:19,210
So now inside both these objects, we're going to put together a set of methods that will eventually

67
00:04:19,210 --> 00:04:22,240
be called by our controller as we are testing it.

68
00:04:22,800 --> 00:04:27,240
So we only need to implement the methods in these services that are actually used by the controller.

69
00:04:27,240 --> 00:04:31,440
So at this point, we will go back over to the controller and see what methods add these two services

70
00:04:31,440 --> 00:04:32,550
are actually used.

71
00:04:32,970 --> 00:04:34,350
So inside the controller.

72
00:04:35,330 --> 00:04:41,180
We're going to find every reference or every use of user service in auth service inside of who am I?

73
00:04:41,210 --> 00:04:44,120
I don't see any none inside of sign out.

74
00:04:44,330 --> 00:04:51,170
It looks like inside of create user we are making use of sign up on the service and sign in on the sign

75
00:04:51,170 --> 00:04:51,970
in handler.

76
00:04:51,980 --> 00:04:56,630
So that means that if we want to test that route handler and that route handler, you and I need to

77
00:04:56,630 --> 00:05:02,390
provide a mock implementation for sign up and sign in on our fake auth service.

78
00:05:02,720 --> 00:05:05,960
So right away I'm going to flip back over and just kind of leave myself a note here.

79
00:05:05,960 --> 00:05:08,990
I'm going to say I need to implement sign up.

80
00:05:12,230 --> 00:05:18,380
And sign in and will come back very shortly and provide an implementation for those back inside the

81
00:05:18,380 --> 00:05:18,810
controller.

82
00:05:18,830 --> 00:05:20,180
I'm going to keep going through.

83
00:05:20,630 --> 00:05:20,750
Okay.

84
00:05:20,750 --> 00:05:24,830
It looks like on user service, I'll make use of find one down here on user service.

85
00:05:24,830 --> 00:05:26,010
I'll make use of find.

86
00:05:26,030 --> 00:05:28,850
So I'm going to go put those two method stubs in.

87
00:05:29,060 --> 00:05:30,650
So find one.

88
00:05:32,830 --> 00:05:34,240
And find.

89
00:05:37,130 --> 00:05:37,790
Back over.

90
00:05:37,820 --> 00:05:39,410
Keep on going through.

91
00:05:39,440 --> 00:05:42,980
So finally, it looks like we make use of remove and update.

92
00:05:44,520 --> 00:05:47,850
So on the user service remove.

93
00:05:48,910 --> 00:05:50,350
And update.

94
00:05:51,490 --> 00:05:51,820
Okay.

95
00:05:51,820 --> 00:05:56,200
So just to repeat myself one more time, if we want to test every handler inside of our controller,

96
00:05:56,200 --> 00:06:00,850
we would need to provide some kind of implementation for all these different methods because they're

97
00:06:00,850 --> 00:06:03,490
all being used inside of one of those different handlers.

98
00:06:03,580 --> 00:06:06,550
If we don't want to test every single handler, that's totally fine.

99
00:06:06,550 --> 00:06:08,470
We don't have to implement all these methods.

100
00:06:08,470 --> 00:06:11,620
If we're not going to test the handler that these methods are used in.

101
00:06:11,620 --> 00:06:17,680
So, for example, if we don't want to test sign up or sign in inside the controller, here they are

102
00:06:17,680 --> 00:06:18,070
right here.

103
00:06:18,070 --> 00:06:20,440
So the create user and sign in methods.

104
00:06:20,440 --> 00:06:25,300
If we don't want to test those, that means we do not need to provide a mock implementation of auth

105
00:06:25,310 --> 00:06:27,100
service signup or auth service.

106
00:06:27,100 --> 00:06:27,940
Sign in.

107
00:06:30,090 --> 00:06:32,550
So maybe we will eventually give up and not implement all these.

108
00:06:32,550 --> 00:06:34,480
But for right now, let's just say we're going to do them all.

109
00:06:34,500 --> 00:06:38,070
I think at some point we'll kind of get bored and want to move on to something more interesting.

110
00:06:38,610 --> 00:06:43,080
Okay, so now that we know what kind of fake implementation we need to provide, let's take a pause

111
00:06:43,080 --> 00:06:43,380
right here.

112
00:06:43,380 --> 00:06:47,520
We'll come back in just a moment and start putting in some mock implementation for each of these.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:05,130
All right, let's take a stab at putting together a mock implementation for each of these different

2
00:00:05,130 --> 00:00:05,910
methods.

3
00:00:06,030 --> 00:00:10,170
Remember, right now, we're getting an error from each of them because TypeScript is making sure that

4
00:00:10,170 --> 00:00:15,900
we are correctly implementing the find one method as it is defined by the real user service.

5
00:00:16,260 --> 00:00:20,730
So if we want to correctly implement these different methods, we can hover over each of them and it

6
00:00:20,730 --> 00:00:22,740
will tell us exactly what we need to do.

7
00:00:22,770 --> 00:00:28,200
So on find one, we are being told that we have a function that takes no arguments and doesn't return

8
00:00:28,200 --> 00:00:28,920
anything.

9
00:00:28,950 --> 00:00:35,010
If we want to correctly implement find one, we need to receive an ID that's a number and then return

10
00:00:35,010 --> 00:00:37,680
a promise that eventually resolves with a user.

11
00:00:37,860 --> 00:00:44,370
So to provide a possible mock implementation of find one I would put in that I'm going to receive an

12
00:00:44,370 --> 00:00:51,720
ID that is a number, and then I'm going to return Promise.resolve and I'm going to resolve with something

13
00:00:51,720 --> 00:00:55,500
that satisfies the user entity interface.

14
00:00:55,950 --> 00:01:00,820
So I'm going to put in how about just the ID that was actually passed into the function?

15
00:01:01,210 --> 00:01:02,560
I'll give it an email.

16
00:01:03,640 --> 00:01:04,720
And a password.

17
00:01:05,280 --> 00:01:09,570
Once again, we're going to run into the same error here because our actual user entity has a couple

18
00:01:09,570 --> 00:01:11,360
of methods attached to it as well.

19
00:01:11,450 --> 00:01:14,910
I don't want to have to implement those different methods, so I'm going to tell TypeScript to just

20
00:01:14,910 --> 00:01:21,060
treat this object right here as a real instance of a user entity, and we'll do that by putting in as

21
00:01:21,060 --> 00:01:21,780
user.

22
00:01:22,780 --> 00:01:28,140
So we have now satisfied the find one function and so we don't get any error anymore.

23
00:01:28,150 --> 00:01:30,280
We have correctly implemented that function.

24
00:01:30,640 --> 00:01:34,090
Now, remember, it's totally up to you how you implement the function.

25
00:01:34,090 --> 00:01:39,100
We are taking in an ID right here and presumably from something like a find one function, we would

26
00:01:39,100 --> 00:01:45,340
find a user with exactly that ID, But if you returned a user that had a totally different ID, well

27
00:01:45,340 --> 00:01:49,540
then this function doesn't really make a lot of sense or the implementation doesn't make a lot of sense.

28
00:01:49,540 --> 00:01:53,800
So it's really up to you to take a look at the arguments that are coming in and decide how to return

29
00:01:53,800 --> 00:01:55,060
some kind of value here.

30
00:01:55,060 --> 00:01:57,400
That kind of makes sense in the context of a test.

31
00:01:58,650 --> 00:02:03,700
Okay, let's go through 1 or 2 more really quickly and then start to put a test or two together.

32
00:02:03,720 --> 00:02:05,190
So how about find.

33
00:02:06,660 --> 00:02:07,500
To implement find.

34
00:02:07,530 --> 00:02:12,630
We're going to take in an email that's a string and then return an array of users.

35
00:02:12,930 --> 00:02:15,690
So I'm going to provide a mock implementation.

36
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:17,090
Of an email.

37
00:02:17,090 --> 00:02:19,820
That's a string I'm going to return Promise.resolve.

38
00:02:21,120 --> 00:02:23,100
I'm going to resolve within array.

39
00:02:23,930 --> 00:02:25,820
That has an object in it.

40
00:02:25,820 --> 00:02:27,110
So one single user.

41
00:02:27,140 --> 00:02:29,030
I'm going to give it a made up number.

42
00:02:29,660 --> 00:02:35,240
I'm going to give it the exact same email that was passed into the function and then just some random

43
00:02:35,240 --> 00:02:35,960
password.

44
00:02:39,130 --> 00:02:43,360
And then once again, I'm going to tell TypeScript, hey, treat this thing as though it were a user.

45
00:02:45,890 --> 00:02:48,080
It looks like that's good enough for find.

46
00:02:48,200 --> 00:02:51,770
So at this point in time, hopefully you might be getting the idea of what we're doing inside these

47
00:02:51,770 --> 00:02:53,300
different mock functions.

48
00:02:53,490 --> 00:02:56,030
Right now, however, I think that we've gone through enough of these.

49
00:02:56,060 --> 00:03:02,090
Let's just run with find one and find for right now by providing an implementation for these two methods,

50
00:03:02,090 --> 00:03:05,930
that means that we can successfully test inside of our controller.

51
00:03:06,850 --> 00:03:12,880
Defined user route handler and the find all users route handler So we can at least test these two with

52
00:03:12,880 --> 00:03:18,460
just the partial mocks we have put together, because those two handlers only make use of find one and

53
00:03:18,460 --> 00:03:19,120
find.

54
00:03:20,450 --> 00:03:26,570
So for right now, I'm going to comment out back inside of our test file, remove and update and I'm

55
00:03:26,570 --> 00:03:27,650
going to comment out.

56
00:03:27,650 --> 00:03:29,150
Sign up and sign in.

57
00:03:30,320 --> 00:03:35,480
So now that we've got our mocks set up, we need to add them into our container and we need to make

58
00:03:35,480 --> 00:03:41,060
sure it's really clear to the container that anytime that our users controller tries to find a copy

59
00:03:41,060 --> 00:03:44,090
of the user service, we want it to use this mock.

60
00:03:44,090 --> 00:03:50,690
And anytime our users control, say users control, I meant to say users or user service users controller.

61
00:03:50,690 --> 00:03:55,640
Whenever our users controller tries to find a copy of the user service, we're going to use this mock

62
00:03:55,670 --> 00:03:59,360
whenever the users controller tries to find a copy of the auth service, we're going to have it use

63
00:03:59,360 --> 00:04:00,230
this mock.

64
00:04:00,350 --> 00:04:07,850
So to set all that up inside of our testing module down here, we're going to add in a list of providers.

65
00:04:08,090 --> 00:04:10,670
And remember, we're going to put in a set of objects here.

66
00:04:10,670 --> 00:04:18,800
Each object is going to say whenever someone asks for the users service, give them the value.

67
00:04:18,830 --> 00:04:21,110
Fake users service.

68
00:04:22,130 --> 00:04:23,570
And that should not be provider.

69
00:04:23,570 --> 00:04:24,830
It should be just provide.

70
00:04:26,470 --> 00:04:29,230
So that's a little redirect or the little trick.

71
00:04:29,230 --> 00:04:34,300
Whenever the user's controller is created and we try to pass it, something to use for its user service

72
00:04:34,300 --> 00:04:40,150
dependency instead of giving it an actual copy of user service, give it the object we just put together.

73
00:04:40,540 --> 00:04:42,640
So we'll then repeat the same process.

74
00:04:42,820 --> 00:04:48,640
We'll say provide the auth service and in place of it.

75
00:04:49,390 --> 00:04:52,960
Use the value fake auth service.

76
00:04:54,220 --> 00:04:54,610
Okay.

77
00:04:54,640 --> 00:04:56,340
So that should be good enough.

78
00:04:56,350 --> 00:04:59,420
Good enough at least, to get the first test up and running.

79
00:04:59,440 --> 00:05:03,340
The first test that was created for us automatically just makes sure that the controller actually gets

80
00:05:03,340 --> 00:05:04,490
created successfully.

81
00:05:04,510 --> 00:05:06,310
So let's now save this file.

82
00:05:06,610 --> 00:05:11,740
We'll go back over to our terminal and we're going to try to run just this user's controller spec.

83
00:05:12,100 --> 00:05:18,040
So back over inside my terminal right now, we are doing a filter test, so we're only running files

84
00:05:18,040 --> 00:05:20,280
with the name of auth dot service.

85
00:05:20,290 --> 00:05:28,390
So to change that out, I'm going to press w I'm going to press P to do another filter and I'm going

86
00:05:28,390 --> 00:05:33,130
to run all my different test files with users dot controller in the name.

87
00:05:33,950 --> 00:05:35,950
So that should only find our one single spec.

88
00:05:35,960 --> 00:05:37,940
And sure enough, it looks like it's passing.

89
00:05:38,240 --> 00:05:39,080
Very good.

90
00:05:39,790 --> 00:05:40,150
Okay.

91
00:05:40,150 --> 00:05:45,070
So that means that we are now successfully creating a copy of our controller and it's making use of

92
00:05:45,070 --> 00:05:47,440
these two mock objects we've put together.

93
00:05:47,560 --> 00:05:52,180
So now we can start to put a couple of different tests together inside of here and make sure that at

94
00:05:52,180 --> 00:05:57,640
least our find user and find all users methods are working as expected.

95
00:05:57,760 --> 00:06:00,070
So let's give that a shot in just a moment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,250 --> 00:00:05,690
With our mock implementation of the user service put together, we can now test all the different methods

2
00:00:05,690 --> 00:00:09,850
inside of our controller that only use either find or find one.

3
00:00:09,860 --> 00:00:14,840
So inside of our controller it looks like find all users right here only makes use of the find method,

4
00:00:14,840 --> 00:00:17,540
which means we can now test this thing successfully.

5
00:00:17,780 --> 00:00:19,310
Remember, quick reminder here.

6
00:00:19,310 --> 00:00:23,840
Whenever we are testing methods inside of a controller, we don't get the ability to run or make any

7
00:00:23,840 --> 00:00:25,730
use of the surrounding decorators.

8
00:00:25,730 --> 00:00:30,410
So we do not have the ability to make sure that something comes out of the query string or to make sure

9
00:00:30,410 --> 00:00:32,240
that this is tied to a get request.

10
00:00:32,330 --> 00:00:36,560
We are testing just the method by itself without any decorators present.

11
00:00:36,590 --> 00:00:42,110
If you want to test the decorators, then we have to write out an end to end test, which you'll recall

12
00:00:42,110 --> 00:00:43,760
is a very different kind of test.

13
00:00:44,590 --> 00:00:44,920
All right.

14
00:00:44,920 --> 00:00:49,960
So back inside of our test file at the very bottom, I'm going to add in a new statement.

15
00:00:50,740 --> 00:00:55,420
And I'll say find all users because that's the method we're going to test.

16
00:00:55,870 --> 00:00:59,650
Returns a list of users with the given email.

17
00:01:02,690 --> 00:01:04,910
I'm going to mark that function as a sink.

18
00:01:06,620 --> 00:01:10,460
And then inside of here, all we have to do is call our method on the controller.

19
00:01:10,460 --> 00:01:11,650
So find all users.

20
00:01:11,660 --> 00:01:16,910
We're going to get back a list of users and just make sure that it has one entry inside of it with a

21
00:01:16,910 --> 00:01:17,660
correct email.

22
00:01:17,690 --> 00:01:21,050
Remember, ultimately, all we're really doing here is calling.

23
00:01:21,850 --> 00:01:23,620
The mock function we just put together.

24
00:01:23,620 --> 00:01:26,330
So this really is a very simple test.

25
00:01:26,350 --> 00:01:30,100
All we're doing is saying, Yep, we're going to take that email, pass it off to the user service,

26
00:01:30,100 --> 00:01:32,680
find method and then get back and return the result.

27
00:01:32,710 --> 00:01:36,670
So this is about as basic and simple a test as we can really put together.

28
00:01:37,480 --> 00:01:37,690
Okay.

29
00:01:37,690 --> 00:01:43,060
So down at the bottom inside the test, once again, I'm going to say users is await controller dot

30
00:01:43,060 --> 00:01:49,030
find all users and I'm going to put in a string of asdf@asdf.com.

31
00:01:49,980 --> 00:01:52,140
Now we're going to take a look at the return value right there.

32
00:01:52,140 --> 00:01:55,980
And all we're going to do is say, yep, it's got one user inside of it, whatever.

33
00:01:55,980 --> 00:01:58,140
We're returning from our mock function at the top.

34
00:01:58,140 --> 00:02:08,070
So will expect users dot length to equal one and will expect users at zero dot email to equal.

35
00:02:09,560 --> 00:02:11,090
The email that we were looking for.

36
00:02:12,600 --> 00:02:13,620
Let's save this.

37
00:02:13,650 --> 00:02:15,370
Flip back over to our terminal.

38
00:02:15,390 --> 00:02:16,380
Looks like it's passing.

39
00:02:16,380 --> 00:02:17,160
Very good.

40
00:02:17,790 --> 00:02:24,400
If I make this test fail in some way, let's say maybe I expect to get back to users now and save this.

41
00:02:24,420 --> 00:02:24,780
Yep.

42
00:02:24,780 --> 00:02:26,280
It fails as expected.

43
00:02:27,510 --> 00:02:27,840
All right.

44
00:02:27,870 --> 00:02:31,450
Now, if this task seems pointless, it's because it kind of is.

45
00:02:31,470 --> 00:02:32,910
We're not really doing a whole lot here.

46
00:02:32,940 --> 00:02:38,160
Our controller methods are so simple and basic and straightforward that if we are forgetting all the

47
00:02:38,160 --> 00:02:42,490
decorator related stuff, there simply is not a lot of logic here.

48
00:02:42,510 --> 00:02:44,400
And that is actually very desirable.

49
00:02:44,400 --> 00:02:46,380
That's what we want our controllers to look like.

50
00:02:46,380 --> 00:02:49,330
We don't want to have controllers that are just stuffed with tons of logic.

51
00:02:49,350 --> 00:02:52,470
Instead, we want them to be as simple and basic as possible.

52
00:02:53,840 --> 00:02:56,240
Okay, let's do just one more test inside of here.

53
00:02:56,240 --> 00:03:02,930
So we'll test a method that makes use of the other mocked function we put together, which was find

54
00:03:02,930 --> 00:03:03,620
one.

55
00:03:05,030 --> 00:03:09,260
So if we go back over to our controller, let's find a method on the controller that only makes use

56
00:03:09,260 --> 00:03:10,190
of find one.

57
00:03:10,580 --> 00:03:14,780
So back over here, it looks like find user only makes use of find one.

58
00:03:14,900 --> 00:03:17,810
So we are going to call find user with some ID.

59
00:03:18,170 --> 00:03:21,270
Well then make sure that we just get back whatever user is found.

60
00:03:21,290 --> 00:03:23,390
So this test is going to be very similar in nature.

61
00:03:23,390 --> 00:03:25,490
Not a lot of logic here to test.

62
00:03:25,520 --> 00:03:30,680
We could do a second test, a little variant on it, and make sure that if we update our mock to not

63
00:03:30,680 --> 00:03:33,330
return any user, we could make sure that it error is thrown.

64
00:03:33,350 --> 00:03:35,630
That's definitely a second test we could write out here.

65
00:03:35,720 --> 00:03:40,190
Let's first just begin and make sure that we can call, find one with some ID.

66
00:03:40,400 --> 00:03:43,100
Get back user and ultimately return it from the handler.

67
00:03:44,250 --> 00:03:46,380
So at the very bottom of our test file.

68
00:03:47,930 --> 00:03:49,280
I'll say it.

69
00:03:50,750 --> 00:03:52,030
What do we call that method?

70
00:03:52,040 --> 00:03:53,060
Find user.

71
00:03:54,870 --> 00:03:58,260
Returns a single user with the given ID.

72
00:04:04,320 --> 00:04:11,040
So then in this case, we'll say user is await controller find user.

73
00:04:11,490 --> 00:04:15,390
We're going to put in just any given ID because remember our mock.

74
00:04:15,780 --> 00:04:17,580
That should be a string.

75
00:04:17,580 --> 00:04:17,760
Yep.

76
00:04:17,760 --> 00:04:18,530
There we go.

77
00:04:18,570 --> 00:04:24,210
So our mock is going to just really take that ID and return some fake user with the exact same ID.

78
00:04:25,080 --> 00:04:26,190
So we can.

79
00:04:26,940 --> 00:04:31,560
Really just say that we expect how about user to be defined?

80
00:04:31,560 --> 00:04:32,490
And that's pretty much it.

81
00:04:32,520 --> 00:04:37,170
We're just making sure that find user makes use of the user service to find user and return it really

82
00:04:37,170 --> 00:04:38,130
nothing else.

83
00:04:38,370 --> 00:04:39,330
So we can save this.

84
00:04:39,330 --> 00:04:41,250
Now back over to the terminal.

85
00:04:41,280 --> 00:04:42,410
Yep, that's passing.

86
00:04:42,420 --> 00:04:44,460
So let's now do one variant on this.

87
00:04:44,850 --> 00:04:46,320
I'm going to put together another test.

88
00:04:46,320 --> 00:04:53,040
We'll say find user throws an error if user with given ID is not.

89
00:04:54,930 --> 00:04:55,590
Bound.

90
00:04:58,470 --> 00:05:04,830
So inside of this case, we're going to mess around with our mock implementation of Find One.

91
00:05:05,010 --> 00:05:08,790
By default, our mock is going to always return some user.

92
00:05:08,790 --> 00:05:13,500
So we're going to replace this mock just for this one test and we're going to have our function return

93
00:05:13,500 --> 00:05:16,740
just null to say sorry, but we did not find someone with that ID.

94
00:05:17,980 --> 00:05:20,740
When our controller gets back null for user right there.

95
00:05:20,740 --> 00:05:24,700
We should then enter the If statement and end up throwing a not found exception.

96
00:05:24,940 --> 00:05:29,500
So very similar to what we did back on our auth service spec just a moment ago.

97
00:05:29,530 --> 00:05:33,460
We're going to rewrite the find one implementation for that one test.

98
00:05:33,550 --> 00:05:37,240
Return null and then just make sure an exception is thrown.

99
00:05:38,000 --> 00:05:44,270
So inside of our test, I'm going to first update the implementation of the fake users service.

100
00:05:45,110 --> 00:05:46,130
Or find one.

101
00:05:47,130 --> 00:05:49,740
And now it's going to be a function that returns null.

102
00:05:53,470 --> 00:05:54,220
I'll then.

103
00:05:55,320 --> 00:05:57,960
Put in a try catch block.

104
00:05:59,240 --> 00:06:00,080
Inside the try.

105
00:06:00,110 --> 00:06:05,210
I'll do an await controller dot find user with any given ID doesn't matter because we're always going

106
00:06:05,210 --> 00:06:06,740
to return null no matter what.

107
00:06:07,810 --> 00:06:12,910
I'm going to go to the enclosing function for the IT statement and receive that done callback, which

108
00:06:12,910 --> 00:06:15,280
we call whenever our test is finished running.

109
00:06:15,280 --> 00:06:19,420
And then just like we did back on the auth service, I'll call done inside of the catch.

110
00:06:21,150 --> 00:06:21,420
Okay.

111
00:06:21,420 --> 00:06:24,330
So now when this test runs, we're going to update the implementation.

112
00:06:24,330 --> 00:06:25,530
We're going to call find user.

113
00:06:25,530 --> 00:06:28,510
We should get back null when find one is executed.

114
00:06:28,530 --> 00:06:30,780
That's going to cause find user to throw a method.

115
00:06:30,780 --> 00:06:33,750
We're going to catch that call done and the test should end.

116
00:06:34,170 --> 00:06:36,660
So I'm going to save this flip back over.

117
00:06:36,690 --> 00:06:37,770
Looks good.

118
00:06:39,550 --> 00:06:45,190
And if we comment out that mock implementation or the updated implementation and we actually do return

119
00:06:45,190 --> 00:06:45,730
a user.

120
00:06:45,760 --> 00:06:49,580
Now, in this case, the find user call is not going to throw an error.

121
00:06:49,600 --> 00:06:54,070
So we're never going to get done and our test should time out after about five seconds.

122
00:06:54,070 --> 00:06:56,890
So one, two, three, four, five.

123
00:06:56,890 --> 00:06:59,200
And there we go.

124
00:07:01,280 --> 00:07:02,330
Okay, so that looks good.

125
00:07:04,400 --> 00:07:04,700
All right.

126
00:07:04,700 --> 00:07:10,250
So hopefully in these last two videos, as we wrote some tests around our controller methods, you started

127
00:07:10,250 --> 00:07:14,090
to get the sense of these tests are not super productive per se.

128
00:07:14,120 --> 00:07:19,760
We're not really doing a whole lot of useful testing here, particularly on find all users in the case

129
00:07:19,760 --> 00:07:24,020
of find user definitely effective because we're making sure that we throw an exception anytime a user

130
00:07:24,020 --> 00:07:24,740
is not found.

131
00:07:24,740 --> 00:07:26,360
But certainly on find all users.

132
00:07:26,360 --> 00:07:28,970
Well, we didn't really get a lot of benefit out of that test.

133
00:07:28,970 --> 00:07:32,300
All we really tested was that we call some method and return the result.

134
00:07:32,300 --> 00:07:32,900
That's it.

135
00:07:32,900 --> 00:07:33,950
And nothing else.

136
00:07:35,530 --> 00:07:39,790
Again, want to repeat that this is not necessarily the worst thing in the world when we're making use

137
00:07:39,790 --> 00:07:40,360
of nest.

138
00:07:40,390 --> 00:07:43,540
We do want our controller methods to be very simple in nature.

139
00:07:43,540 --> 00:07:48,880
So not terrible, but at the same time, maybe we don't really need to test this thing all that much.

140
00:07:49,090 --> 00:07:54,400
I would not mind, however, having a test around find user and more complicated methods like say maybe

141
00:07:54,430 --> 00:07:55,750
sign in would be a good one to test.

142
00:07:55,750 --> 00:07:59,650
Make sure that session object gets assigned a user ID and whatnot.

143
00:07:59,740 --> 00:08:01,150
So let's take a pause right here.

144
00:08:01,150 --> 00:08:06,100
We'll either do just one more test around, maybe sign in or create user, maybe we'll call it good.

145
00:08:06,100 --> 00:08:06,670
I don't know.

146
00:08:06,670 --> 00:08:08,410
We'll decide what to do in the next video.

147
00:08:08,440 --> 00:08:08,890
All right.

148
00:08:08,890 --> 00:08:09,700
See you in a minute.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,160 --> 00:00:01,670
You know what?

2
00:00:01,670 --> 00:00:05,320
After all, let's go ahead and write a very quick test for how about sign in?

3
00:00:05,330 --> 00:00:07,970
So this will again be a pretty straightforward test.

4
00:00:08,000 --> 00:00:12,230
We're going to provide some body and some object as our session.

5
00:00:12,260 --> 00:00:15,080
We're then going to provide a mock implementation for sign in.

6
00:00:15,080 --> 00:00:20,090
So you and have to put that together, that mock implementation needs to return an object that has an

7
00:00:20,090 --> 00:00:25,700
ID property, because our expectation is that the ID is going to be assigned to the session object that

8
00:00:25,700 --> 00:00:27,320
we pass into this function.

9
00:00:27,500 --> 00:00:30,800
So our test ultimately can really assert two different things.

10
00:00:30,830 --> 00:00:34,370
Our test can make sure that the found user gets returned, number one.

11
00:00:34,370 --> 00:00:39,560
And number two, we can make sure that the user ID property is set on the session object that you and

12
00:00:39,560 --> 00:00:41,600
I manually pass into this handler.

13
00:00:41,840 --> 00:00:46,970
So this test is at least a little bit more effective than, say, the find all users one we put together

14
00:00:46,970 --> 00:00:47,990
just a moment ago.

15
00:00:48,630 --> 00:00:48,900
All right.

16
00:00:48,900 --> 00:00:50,180
So back over to our test file.

17
00:00:50,190 --> 00:00:55,260
We're going to first put together a mock implementation for the auth service sign in method.

18
00:00:55,850 --> 00:01:00,830
So back inside the controller test file, I'm going to find my fake auth service.

19
00:01:01,040 --> 00:01:03,080
I'm going to uncomment sign in.

20
00:01:03,440 --> 00:01:05,840
So this will receive an email and a password.

21
00:01:05,870 --> 00:01:08,570
We don't actually make use of that email and password.

22
00:01:08,960 --> 00:01:09,540
But you know what?

23
00:01:09,590 --> 00:01:11,120
Let's just receive it anyways.

24
00:01:13,190 --> 00:01:14,450
Just to be complete.

25
00:01:14,660 --> 00:01:18,470
And then inside of here, we're going to make sure that we return a promise that's going to resolve

26
00:01:18,470 --> 00:01:19,480
with the user.

27
00:01:19,490 --> 00:01:22,040
So will return promise.resolve.

28
00:01:23,080 --> 00:01:28,740
I'm going to resolve with an object that's going to have an ID and I'll hardcode it to be an ID of one

29
00:01:28,750 --> 00:01:30,310
that's going to be relevant in a little bit.

30
00:01:30,340 --> 00:01:35,020
When we make sure that our session object gets assigned that user ID property.

31
00:01:35,500 --> 00:01:38,320
I'll then just directly pass through the email and password.

32
00:01:38,470 --> 00:01:43,210
And then once again, we're going to help TypeScript understand that, yes, this is a user entity by

33
00:01:43,210 --> 00:01:44,770
putting in as user.

34
00:01:46,090 --> 00:01:46,930
Okay, so that looks good.

35
00:01:46,930 --> 00:01:50,860
We've now got a working implementation for sign in.

36
00:01:51,720 --> 00:01:55,770
So let's go back down to the very bottom of our test file and put in new test together.

37
00:01:58,590 --> 00:01:59,940
So I'll say it.

38
00:02:00,240 --> 00:02:01,200
Updates.

39
00:02:01,230 --> 00:02:02,630
Actually, let's say it.

40
00:02:02,640 --> 00:02:03,960
Sign in.

41
00:02:04,860 --> 00:02:08,940
Updates session object and returns user.

42
00:02:10,330 --> 00:02:13,600
So we're going to check those two things inside of one single test.

43
00:02:14,840 --> 00:02:15,110
All right.

44
00:02:15,110 --> 00:02:18,590
So then inside of here, I'm going to first create a session object.

45
00:02:18,590 --> 00:02:19,760
So just a plain object.

46
00:02:19,790 --> 00:02:20,960
Nothing special about it.

47
00:02:22,650 --> 00:02:28,830
I'm then going to call our controller method and assign the result to user because remember, our method

48
00:02:28,830 --> 00:02:30,910
does eventually return the user that is found.

49
00:02:30,930 --> 00:02:39,090
So we'll do an await controller dot sign in and we will pass in something to serve as the body.

50
00:02:40,180 --> 00:02:42,760
And then something to serve as the session.

51
00:02:43,680 --> 00:02:48,000
So for the body, we're going to put in an object that has any given email.

52
00:02:51,820 --> 00:02:54,010
And any random password.

53
00:02:55,420 --> 00:02:59,710
Then as the second argument, I'm going to put a new line in here just so you can read this entire line

54
00:02:59,950 --> 00:03:01,360
as a second argument.

55
00:03:02,170 --> 00:03:04,930
We'll put in our session object.

56
00:03:06,000 --> 00:03:10,530
Now, the reason I said any old email and any old password is remember, at no point in time the email

57
00:03:10,530 --> 00:03:14,490
and password are actually being used back inside of our controller method.

58
00:03:14,490 --> 00:03:15,770
So here's what we are testing.

59
00:03:15,780 --> 00:03:22,230
Although we pass off that email and password to the sign in method, we are calling our mocked version

60
00:03:22,260 --> 00:03:23,100
of sign in.

61
00:03:23,100 --> 00:03:27,780
And our mocked version of sign in doesn't really operate on the email and password in any way.

62
00:03:27,780 --> 00:03:30,060
It returns them eventually inside of a user object.

63
00:03:30,060 --> 00:03:32,250
But we are not operating on those values.

64
00:03:32,250 --> 00:03:34,800
We don't parse them, compare them, nothing like that.

65
00:03:37,200 --> 00:03:43,110
So back over inside of our test, we should now have a user and we should also have an updated session

66
00:03:43,110 --> 00:03:46,010
object so we can write out to expectations around that.

67
00:03:46,020 --> 00:03:52,110
First, I'm going to say that I expect user.id to equal one.

68
00:03:52,110 --> 00:03:57,300
So it's specifically one right here because we hardcoded our mock at the very top of this file.

69
00:03:58,390 --> 00:04:01,560
To return a user entity that has an ID of one.

70
00:04:01,570 --> 00:04:04,180
So this is what is being returned from our method right here.

71
00:04:04,390 --> 00:04:06,910
So I'm going to make sure that we get back the correct user.

72
00:04:09,290 --> 00:04:14,930
And then will also make sure that we set the user ID property on that session object.

73
00:04:18,600 --> 00:04:22,290
We're going to get a type error here because we had said that session is going to be an object without

74
00:04:22,290 --> 00:04:23,280
any properties.

75
00:04:23,280 --> 00:04:29,790
So I'm going to initialize user ID to maybe just have a starting value of negative ten or something

76
00:04:29,790 --> 00:04:30,480
like that.

77
00:04:30,600 --> 00:04:35,040
So if we ever get negative ten, that means that we did not update the session property correctly or

78
00:04:35,100 --> 00:04:36,960
maybe the user ID property correctly.

79
00:04:37,110 --> 00:04:41,190
If we did update it correctly, it should have been changed to user ID of one.

80
00:04:42,250 --> 00:04:43,480
All right, So let's run this.

81
00:04:44,850 --> 00:04:46,620
And it looks like we are good to go.

82
00:04:46,950 --> 00:04:48,450
As usual, quick test here.

83
00:04:48,450 --> 00:04:50,340
So how about we try to.

84
00:04:51,620 --> 00:04:54,060
I don't know what would be a good way to break this thing.

85
00:04:54,080 --> 00:04:56,600
How about let's go back up to sign in?

86
00:04:58,560 --> 00:05:03,090
So here's our mock implementation rather than returning a promise that resolves.

87
00:05:03,210 --> 00:05:05,250
Let's just, I don't know, change the ID.

88
00:05:06,480 --> 00:05:08,130
So I'll change the ID to five.

89
00:05:08,400 --> 00:05:09,750
Comparison should fail.

90
00:05:09,780 --> 00:05:11,010
Yep, that works.

91
00:05:12,660 --> 00:05:14,310
Okay, so that is good.

92
00:05:14,320 --> 00:05:18,960
I think that we've now got kind of a reasonable idea of how to put tests together around a controller.

93
00:05:18,960 --> 00:05:23,730
We could keep going, but all the other methods inside of here are extremely basic in nature and kind

94
00:05:23,730 --> 00:05:27,240
of repetitive and repeats of the stuff we already put together.

95
00:05:27,240 --> 00:05:29,550
So if we wanted to test who am I?

96
00:05:29,580 --> 00:05:31,200
Okay, we're going to call Who am I?

97
00:05:31,410 --> 00:05:35,960
Pass in some fake user and just make sure we get back that exact same object.

98
00:05:35,970 --> 00:05:41,370
If you want to test sign out, you're going to pass in some object with a user ID property and just

99
00:05:41,370 --> 00:05:44,010
make sure that the user ID property gets set to null.

100
00:05:44,370 --> 00:05:48,730
Create user just about really effectively identical to sign in.

101
00:05:48,750 --> 00:05:50,370
Exact same thing going on there.

102
00:05:51,480 --> 00:05:55,020
We did find user, we did find all users remove user.

103
00:05:55,230 --> 00:05:57,740
All we're doing is making sure that remove gets called.

104
00:05:57,750 --> 00:05:59,340
Same thing with update as well.

105
00:06:00,280 --> 00:06:02,260
Okay, so that's pretty much it.

106
00:06:02,290 --> 00:06:07,120
I just want to repeat one more time that as you start to test your own controllers, it will seem like,

107
00:06:07,120 --> 00:06:09,460
gee, these tests are very basic and not super useful.

108
00:06:09,490 --> 00:06:11,110
Yes, that is expected.

109
00:06:11,110 --> 00:06:16,600
I expect them to be very basic and not super complex or anything like that because usually our controllers

110
00:06:16,600 --> 00:06:18,760
have pretty simple logic inside them.

111
00:06:19,610 --> 00:06:19,910
All right.

112
00:06:19,910 --> 00:06:23,690
So we've now got examples of testing a controller and testing a.

113
00:06:24,530 --> 00:06:25,490
Service as well.

114
00:06:25,490 --> 00:06:28,340
So let's take a pause right here and move on in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# sectin 13: Integration Testing

1
00:00:01,530 --> 00:00:03,330
I think we've seen enough around unit testing.

2
00:00:03,330 --> 00:00:07,530
So in this video we're going to start to move on to the other form of testing in Nest, which is referred

3
00:00:07,530 --> 00:00:09,750
to as end to end testing.

4
00:00:09,990 --> 00:00:15,600
You can find an automatically generated end to end test inside the test directory of our root project

5
00:00:15,600 --> 00:00:16,200
folder.

6
00:00:16,230 --> 00:00:19,300
So inside of here is one end to end test file.

7
00:00:19,320 --> 00:00:23,550
If you open that file up, you're going to notice that the structure of this file looks very similar

8
00:00:23,550 --> 00:00:25,950
to the unit test files we already put together.

9
00:00:26,070 --> 00:00:28,350
So inside of here is a describe statement.

10
00:00:28,380 --> 00:00:33,900
There's a beforeeach where some kind of testing module is created and then some tests at the very bottom.

11
00:00:35,160 --> 00:00:39,360
So let me show you a couple diagrams and understand what we are really doing with end to end tests and

12
00:00:39,360 --> 00:00:42,840
how they differ from a traditional unit test.

13
00:00:44,170 --> 00:00:44,530
Okay.

14
00:00:44,530 --> 00:00:50,320
So with an end to end test, we are attempting to make sure that a wide swath or essentially a lot of

15
00:00:50,320 --> 00:00:54,580
different pieces of our application are working together and working as expected.

16
00:00:54,610 --> 00:00:59,050
So rather than trying to call one single method and make sure that that method does the right thing,

17
00:00:59,050 --> 00:01:03,820
we're going to instead create an entire copy or an entire instance of our application.

18
00:01:04,030 --> 00:01:09,910
We're then going to assign that instance to listen to traffic on some random port on your computer.

19
00:01:10,210 --> 00:01:15,940
Then inside of our test, we're going to make requests to that very temporary server that is listening

20
00:01:15,970 --> 00:01:16,810
to traffic.

21
00:01:16,930 --> 00:01:21,370
We can make one request, we can make five, We can make as many requests to that running application

22
00:01:21,370 --> 00:01:24,790
as we want by writing out some code inside of our test.

23
00:01:25,030 --> 00:01:29,710
So if we had a test where we are trying to make sure that we were able to sign up successfully, we

24
00:01:29,710 --> 00:01:32,850
might make first one request to just sign up.

25
00:01:32,860 --> 00:01:37,510
Then maybe we could also attempt to make another request to sign out and then maybe another to sign

26
00:01:37,510 --> 00:01:40,780
back in to make sure that the account was actually created correctly.

27
00:01:42,070 --> 00:01:46,670
A brand new copy of our server is going to be created for every separate test we write out.

28
00:01:46,690 --> 00:01:51,100
So if we end up with, say, five different tests, we are going to create.

29
00:01:52,350 --> 00:01:58,520
Over time, five or maybe in this case, just three different copies of our application.

30
00:01:58,530 --> 00:02:00,360
So each of these are going to be created.

31
00:02:00,360 --> 00:02:04,250
We're going to run a test on that server and then shut the entire server down.

32
00:02:04,260 --> 00:02:07,590
We're then going to repeat the entire process during the next test.

33
00:02:09,090 --> 00:02:10,200
To run these tests.

34
00:02:10,230 --> 00:02:13,140
We're going to run yet another command from our CLI.

35
00:02:14,430 --> 00:02:14,820
Or not our.

36
00:02:15,120 --> 00:02:15,960
But our terminal.

37
00:02:16,110 --> 00:02:18,720
The command is listed out just in case you forget it.

38
00:02:18,720 --> 00:02:20,560
Inside the Package.json file.

39
00:02:20,580 --> 00:02:23,250
So if you open up that file and find the scripts section.

40
00:02:23,940 --> 00:02:28,290
Go on down and you'll notice there is a test colon e2e script.

41
00:02:28,320 --> 00:02:32,490
So this is going to run all the end to end tests inside that test directory.

42
00:02:32,520 --> 00:02:36,270
It's not going to run any of the spec files inside of your SRC folder.

43
00:02:36,330 --> 00:02:40,500
Let's try running that command right now and seeing if the existing test passes.

44
00:02:41,400 --> 00:02:45,260
Back over at my terminal, I've stopped my running test suite.

45
00:02:45,270 --> 00:02:50,040
I'm going to do a NPM run test, Colon E to E.

46
00:02:53,210 --> 00:02:54,530
And I'll very quickly see that.

47
00:02:54,530 --> 00:02:54,760
Yep.

48
00:02:54,770 --> 00:02:56,960
Looks like everything is working as expected.

49
00:02:57,200 --> 00:03:02,570
So this means that we are testing out the built in app controller, which has just a very small single

50
00:03:02,570 --> 00:03:03,860
route attached to it.

51
00:03:03,890 --> 00:03:09,110
Just make sure that we've got a root route and then that root route is going to return a string of something

52
00:03:09,110 --> 00:03:09,840
like Hi there.

53
00:03:09,860 --> 00:03:11,450
That's what the test actually does.

54
00:03:11,840 --> 00:03:16,460
So as you can imagine, pretty straightforward and pretty useless test that we probably don't really

55
00:03:16,460 --> 00:03:17,060
need.

56
00:03:17,150 --> 00:03:18,420
So let's take a pause right here.

57
00:03:18,440 --> 00:03:22,610
We're going to come back in the next video and we're going to start putting together our own end to

58
00:03:22,610 --> 00:03:28,910
end tests and make sure that our authentication setup, all of our authentication setup inside the user's

59
00:03:28,910 --> 00:03:31,160
controller is working as expected.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,170 --> 00:00:04,170
Let's try putting together our own custom end to end test.

2
00:00:04,200 --> 00:00:08,970
We're going to test out our authentication system and make sure that we can successfully sign up, sign

3
00:00:08,970 --> 00:00:10,430
out and sign back in.

4
00:00:10,440 --> 00:00:19,020
To get started inside my test directory, I'm going to make a new file called Auth dot E2E expects.

5
00:00:19,970 --> 00:00:23,120
Then inside of here, we need to write out a whole lot of boilerplate.

6
00:00:23,150 --> 00:00:28,640
Rather than writing this all out from scratch, let's just go back over to the app end to end file copy

7
00:00:28,640 --> 00:00:33,590
everything inside of here and then paste it into the new auth E2e spec file.

8
00:00:35,190 --> 00:00:37,400
Okay, so here's the auth e2e spec file.

9
00:00:37,410 --> 00:00:39,690
I've now got everything copied over into it.

10
00:00:40,020 --> 00:00:42,900
I'm going to close the app end to end spec file.

11
00:00:43,630 --> 00:00:45,700
So I only have the auth file open now.

12
00:00:46,390 --> 00:00:48,880
Then inside of here, we're going to go on through this file line by line.

13
00:00:48,910 --> 00:00:52,720
Maybe not line by line just yet, but want to change out a couple of things inside of here.

14
00:00:52,750 --> 00:00:55,980
The first and most obvious one is the description at the very top.

15
00:00:55,990 --> 00:00:59,590
So I don't want to have a description of app controller because that's not what we are testing.

16
00:00:59,590 --> 00:01:01,660
I'm going to change it to off.

17
00:01:02,390 --> 00:01:04,700
Authentication system.

18
00:01:05,910 --> 00:01:08,280
So that's what we were testing inside this file.

19
00:01:09,300 --> 00:01:11,850
I'm then going to go down to the IT statement right here.

20
00:01:11,880 --> 00:01:15,720
You'll notice that they are using a format right here where they list out the route that they're going

21
00:01:15,720 --> 00:01:18,330
to make a request to and the method of the request.

22
00:01:18,360 --> 00:01:21,330
You can use this format if you want to for your test descriptions.

23
00:01:21,330 --> 00:01:27,540
But personally I like to use something a little bit more human readable, so I'm going to say it handles

24
00:01:27,570 --> 00:01:30,180
a sign up request.

25
00:01:31,090 --> 00:01:35,650
So now it's very clear that this test right here is all about attempting to sign up to our application.

26
00:01:36,760 --> 00:01:41,800
So then inside the test, you're going to see some code that for right now might look a little bit mysterious.

27
00:01:41,830 --> 00:01:45,520
We're going to understand exactly how to interact and make request over time.

28
00:01:45,520 --> 00:01:47,920
But for right now, let me just tell you what you need to know.

29
00:01:48,310 --> 00:01:53,650
The first line inside this test is always going to be us attempting to make a request off to our Http

30
00:01:53,650 --> 00:01:54,370
server.

31
00:01:54,400 --> 00:01:58,660
We're then going to chain on some different method calls that are going to customize that request and

32
00:01:58,660 --> 00:02:02,380
then eventually make an expectation around the response that comes back.

33
00:02:02,470 --> 00:02:06,250
So in this case, we're first forming up a request.

34
00:02:07,620 --> 00:02:13,110
Then making a Git method request to the root route and then expecting that we get back a status code

35
00:02:13,110 --> 00:02:14,070
of 200.

36
00:02:14,070 --> 00:02:17,970
And in the body of the response, there should be a string of Hello world.

37
00:02:18,150 --> 00:02:23,610
So in order to test our sign up feature, we should probably just go very quickly review how we make

38
00:02:23,610 --> 00:02:27,030
a request to sign up and what kind of data we expect to get back.

39
00:02:27,330 --> 00:02:29,820
So I'm going to open up my client very quickly.

40
00:02:31,340 --> 00:02:32,330
Here it is right here.

41
00:02:33,670 --> 00:02:39,070
So you might recall that to sign up to our application, we need to make a post request to a root of

42
00:02:39,100 --> 00:02:40,900
auth slash sign up.

43
00:02:41,110 --> 00:02:44,410
We then need to include an email and a password.

44
00:02:44,740 --> 00:02:52,090
And if we sign up with some unique email and password, we then would expect to get back a user entity

45
00:02:52,090 --> 00:02:53,350
without a password.

46
00:02:53,350 --> 00:02:58,810
So there should be an ID and an email that comes back so we can write out a test to make sure this is

47
00:02:58,810 --> 00:03:00,460
all working as expected.

48
00:03:00,460 --> 00:03:05,770
Inside of our test, we can make a request that is a post request to auth slash, sign up with an email

49
00:03:05,770 --> 00:03:09,310
and a password and then we can make sure.

50
00:03:11,150 --> 00:03:12,500
That inside the response.

51
00:03:12,500 --> 00:03:15,830
We have a status code of maybe 201 created.

52
00:03:16,220 --> 00:03:19,550
And the response body has an email and an ID.

53
00:03:19,850 --> 00:03:22,280
So let's try that out, see what that would look like.

54
00:03:22,520 --> 00:03:24,230
Back over inside my test file.

55
00:03:24,380 --> 00:03:29,120
I'm going to delete everything that's chained on and we're going to replace it with our own customization

56
00:03:29,120 --> 00:03:31,030
and our own expectation.

57
00:03:31,040 --> 00:03:36,350
So I'm going to first say that I want to make a post request to auth slash signup.

58
00:03:38,110 --> 00:03:39,550
Inside the body that request.

59
00:03:39,550 --> 00:03:42,010
I'm then going to send along some amount of information.

60
00:03:42,010 --> 00:03:47,110
So to send a post request with a body, we're going to first call post and then call send right after

61
00:03:47,110 --> 00:03:51,660
and inside of send, we're going to list out what we want to send inside the post request.

62
00:03:51,670 --> 00:03:55,870
So in this case, I want to send along an email that is unique.

63
00:03:56,290 --> 00:03:58,360
So I'll put in some unique string right here.

64
00:04:00,330 --> 00:04:01,320
And a password.

65
00:04:05,830 --> 00:04:08,290
We're then going to chain on a set of expectations.

66
00:04:08,290 --> 00:04:12,490
So this is where we are going to write out some code to make sure that the request worked as expected.

67
00:04:12,970 --> 00:04:16,780
I'm going to expect that we get back a status code of 201.

68
00:04:17,500 --> 00:04:20,650
Then to make sure that we got back an actual user entity.

69
00:04:20,680 --> 00:04:27,280
So an object with an ID and a password rather than putting on another statement, we're going to instead

70
00:04:27,280 --> 00:04:29,140
chain on a dot then.

71
00:04:31,330 --> 00:04:36,100
This is going to be called with a response object, which I'm going to abbreviate simply as res.

72
00:04:36,280 --> 00:04:41,860
This response object has a body property, and that body property contains the data that is being sent

73
00:04:41,860 --> 00:04:44,230
back to our test from our running server.

74
00:04:44,440 --> 00:04:51,610
So in theory, hopefully the res body property should have an email and an ID property, so I'm going

75
00:04:51,610 --> 00:04:53,470
to try to destructure those off.

76
00:04:57,950 --> 00:04:59,690
And then I'll write out an expectation.

77
00:04:59,690 --> 00:05:07,050
I'm going to expect the ID just to be defined because remember that ID is somewhat randomly generated.

78
00:05:07,070 --> 00:05:12,290
It is in sequence, but we can't necessarily always predict exactly what the ID is going to be going

79
00:05:12,290 --> 00:05:13,790
to actually be when it gets sent back.

80
00:05:13,790 --> 00:05:16,460
So I'm just going to say that I expect the ID to exist.

81
00:05:16,730 --> 00:05:21,470
But then in the case of the email, I definitely expect the email to be exactly whatever we sent to

82
00:05:21,470 --> 00:05:23,350
the original signup route handler.

83
00:05:23,360 --> 00:05:28,420
So would expect email to equal that string right there.

84
00:05:28,430 --> 00:05:31,430
So I will copy that string and put it in right there.

85
00:05:32,830 --> 00:05:34,300
Duplicating the email like this.

86
00:05:34,300 --> 00:05:35,830
Not super good practice.

87
00:05:35,830 --> 00:05:36,460
So rather than.

88
00:05:36,490 --> 00:05:42,610
Duplicating that string, I'm going to instead create a new variable at the top of the test called email

89
00:05:42,610 --> 00:05:44,890
and assign it that email.

90
00:05:45,250 --> 00:05:50,170
I'll then make use of that variable in both locations, so I'll remove that string right there.

91
00:05:50,880 --> 00:05:53,760
And I'll change that string to just email.

92
00:05:55,800 --> 00:05:56,130
Okay.

93
00:05:56,160 --> 00:05:57,210
So that should be it.

94
00:05:57,210 --> 00:05:59,370
This should be enough to actually run a test.

95
00:05:59,610 --> 00:06:03,000
Now, unfortunately, hey, you know how life is.

96
00:06:03,030 --> 00:06:04,290
We're going to run this test.

97
00:06:04,290 --> 00:06:05,880
It's not going to work as expected.

98
00:06:05,910 --> 00:06:09,510
We have two very big issues around our test setup right now.

99
00:06:09,510 --> 00:06:13,770
Nonetheless, want to run this test as is, and we're going to figure out what these two issues are

100
00:06:13,770 --> 00:06:15,600
and then solve them in the coming videos.

101
00:06:15,600 --> 00:06:16,470
So let's get to it.

102
00:06:16,470 --> 00:06:17,790
Let's go back over to our terminal.

103
00:06:18,940 --> 00:06:23,260
I'm going to run npm run test colon E to e.

104
00:06:26,090 --> 00:06:28,700
Okay, So it looks like it's going to run our test file.

105
00:06:28,940 --> 00:06:33,140
And sure enough, yep, we get a failure with a nice big error message.

106
00:06:33,290 --> 00:06:34,730
So let's take a pause right here.

107
00:06:34,730 --> 00:06:38,450
We're going to come back in just a moment and figure out exactly why this test is failing.

108
00:06:38,480 --> 00:06:39,560
Two reasons for it.

109
00:06:39,560 --> 00:06:41,000
We're going to fix those both up.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,530 --> 00:00:04,800
We just ran our tests and it looks like they are already failing.

2
00:00:04,800 --> 00:00:06,810
So if we scroll up inside of our terminal, we'll see.

3
00:00:06,810 --> 00:00:11,370
The reason that the test failed was that we expected to get back a status of 201, but instead we got

4
00:00:11,370 --> 00:00:13,080
back a status of 500.

5
00:00:13,110 --> 00:00:15,840
Now, that is just the reason that the test failed.

6
00:00:15,930 --> 00:00:17,400
We were expecting 2A1.

7
00:00:17,400 --> 00:00:18,270
We got back 500.

8
00:00:18,270 --> 00:00:20,940
But that doesn't really describe why we got a 500.

9
00:00:20,970 --> 00:00:25,800
To understand why we got back a status code of 500, we need to scroll up a little bit further and we're

10
00:00:25,800 --> 00:00:29,000
going to see the actual error that occurred when we made the request.

11
00:00:29,010 --> 00:00:30,600
So here's the actual error.

12
00:00:30,600 --> 00:00:34,080
It says cannot set property user ID of undefined.

13
00:00:34,380 --> 00:00:37,530
Now we're going to take a look at exactly what that era means in just a moment.

14
00:00:37,530 --> 00:00:43,230
But first, I want to give you kind of a quick reminder on how our application usually runs during development,

15
00:00:43,260 --> 00:00:45,270
how our application runs during testing.

16
00:00:45,270 --> 00:00:49,500
And then we're going to very quickly notice that, well, there's a little piece missing when we are

17
00:00:49,500 --> 00:00:53,490
running our app in this testing environment that is causing this error to be thrown.

18
00:00:53,490 --> 00:00:57,990
So in other words, a couple of quick diagrams and a quick reminder inside of our test file.

19
00:00:59,060 --> 00:01:00,890
Okay, so first diagram right here.

20
00:01:00,920 --> 00:01:04,880
This is what happens whenever we run our application directly from the command line.

21
00:01:04,910 --> 00:01:09,320
In other words, when we are doing active development on it, remember we've got our users module,

22
00:01:09,320 --> 00:01:13,220
the reports module, both those get imported into the app module.

23
00:01:13,430 --> 00:01:18,980
We then import the app module into the Main.ts file and we have a bootstrap function inside there.

24
00:01:19,010 --> 00:01:24,170
The bootstrap function is going to create a new nest application out of the app module and then we manually

25
00:01:24,170 --> 00:01:27,070
wire up cookie session and the validation pipe.

26
00:01:27,080 --> 00:01:30,800
And then after that we start listening for traffic on Port 3000.

27
00:01:30,950 --> 00:01:37,430
So in short, when we are doing active development on our application inside of our Main.ts file, we

28
00:01:37,430 --> 00:01:41,990
are creating an instance of our application and then wiring up these two Middlewares really one middleware

29
00:01:41,990 --> 00:01:42,770
and the pipe.

30
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:47,920
So now let's take a look at another diagram that's going to explain what is going on with our application

31
00:01:47,920 --> 00:01:49,180
during testing.

32
00:01:49,850 --> 00:01:54,620
So during testing we completely skip over the main.ts file.

33
00:01:54,620 --> 00:01:58,490
No code inside the Main.ts file is executed in any way.

34
00:01:58,520 --> 00:02:04,880
Instead, our end to end test is importing the app module directly and then creating an app out of the

35
00:02:04,880 --> 00:02:05,960
app module.

36
00:02:06,140 --> 00:02:09,610
We can see that very easily back inside of our before each statement.

37
00:02:09,620 --> 00:02:11,660
So if you go back over to your code editor.

38
00:02:12,950 --> 00:02:14,180
Here's the test file.

39
00:02:14,970 --> 00:02:16,800
I'm going to go to the before each.

40
00:02:16,890 --> 00:02:19,860
So right here is where we make use of the app module.

41
00:02:19,950 --> 00:02:25,290
We then create a nest application out of that app module and then eventually tell that application to

42
00:02:25,290 --> 00:02:27,390
start up and start listening for traffic.

43
00:02:28,060 --> 00:02:33,700
So during testing we completely skip over the main.ts file and the configuration inside there that sets

44
00:02:33,700 --> 00:02:36,280
up cookie session and the validation pipe.

45
00:02:36,460 --> 00:02:43,300
So at present during testing, we have no concept of sessions and we have no concept of validating incoming

46
00:02:43,300 --> 00:02:47,080
requests through the validation pipe just because that stuff does not get set up.

47
00:02:47,760 --> 00:02:51,280
So that kind of explains the error message we are seeing back over at our terminal.

48
00:02:51,300 --> 00:02:54,690
It says cannot set property user of undefined.

49
00:02:54,780 --> 00:03:02,070
This error is being thrown whenever we try to set the user ID property on the user's session object

50
00:03:02,100 --> 00:03:06,630
because the cookie session middleware is not running the session, object just plain does not exist

51
00:03:06,630 --> 00:03:08,950
and instead we get undefined.

52
00:03:08,970 --> 00:03:13,890
So when we try to set a property on undefined, this is a very classic error message that we get back.

53
00:03:14,600 --> 00:03:16,380
So that's what's going on behind the scenes.

54
00:03:16,400 --> 00:03:20,690
We are getting this error simply because we are not doing setup of cookies, session and validation

55
00:03:20,690 --> 00:03:23,060
pipe when we are running our end to end tests.

56
00:03:23,450 --> 00:03:26,900
So to fix this, we have two possible solutions.

57
00:03:27,080 --> 00:03:29,720
The first solution is going to be very quick and easy.

58
00:03:29,750 --> 00:03:35,540
The second solution is much more involved, but it's also the kind of more nest appropriate way of solving

59
00:03:35,540 --> 00:03:36,260
this stuff.

60
00:03:36,350 --> 00:03:40,130
So let's first take a look at the quick and easy way of solving this.

61
00:03:41,780 --> 00:03:43,310
Back inside my code editor.

62
00:03:44,380 --> 00:03:46,440
I'm going to find my Main.ts file.

63
00:03:46,450 --> 00:03:47,020
So here it is.

64
00:03:47,020 --> 00:03:47,740
Right here.

65
00:03:47,860 --> 00:03:50,710
Inside this main.ts file, we've got that bootstrap function.

66
00:03:50,710 --> 00:03:55,370
And here's where we wire up the cookie session middleware and apply our global validation pipe as well.

67
00:03:55,390 --> 00:03:59,530
So for our first solution, we're going to take a look at that's going to be very simple, very straightforward,

68
00:03:59,530 --> 00:04:02,050
but not really the appropriate way of doing things.

69
00:04:02,080 --> 00:04:06,700
We're just going to take all this setup around applying these middlewares and whatnot and move it out

70
00:04:06,700 --> 00:04:11,590
to a separate helper function that we can then apply equally well whenever our application is running

71
00:04:11,590 --> 00:04:14,770
in a development environment or during the testing process.

72
00:04:14,980 --> 00:04:18,800
Now, we are not going to use this solution, so I'm going to show you how to implement this, but I

73
00:04:18,820 --> 00:04:22,940
encourage you, you to not write out this code because I'm going to undo it very quickly.

74
00:04:22,960 --> 00:04:26,170
So as a very quick example, let's run through this as fast as possible.

75
00:04:26,290 --> 00:04:33,100
First, I'm going to create a new file inside of my SRC directory called setup App.ts.

76
00:04:33,770 --> 00:04:39,740
Then inside of here, I'm going to export a function called setup app as an argument to this function,

77
00:04:39,740 --> 00:04:42,950
I'm going to expect to receive my application.

78
00:04:42,950 --> 00:04:45,860
So an instance of our app module more or less.

79
00:04:46,130 --> 00:04:48,830
And then for right now I'm just going to annotate its type as any.

80
00:04:49,280 --> 00:04:55,640
Then inside of here I'm going to go back over to my Main.ts file and I'm going to cut all that application

81
00:04:55,640 --> 00:04:56,360
setup code.

82
00:04:56,360 --> 00:05:00,260
So where we wire up the cookie session middleware and the global pipe stuff as well.

83
00:05:00,350 --> 00:05:02,210
So I'm going to cut all that out.

84
00:05:02,950 --> 00:05:05,500
I'm going to paste it into the setup app function.

85
00:05:06,240 --> 00:05:11,340
I'll then go back over to Main.ts and I'm going to cut and paste over the import statements for cookie

86
00:05:11,340 --> 00:05:13,230
session and validation pipe as well.

87
00:05:13,880 --> 00:05:17,330
So back inside a validation pipe, I'll get import number one.

88
00:05:19,520 --> 00:05:21,080
And import number two.

89
00:05:23,550 --> 00:05:27,390
So now we've got this function right here that we can use in any location inside of our project.

90
00:05:27,420 --> 00:05:32,280
We can import that function back into the main.ts file and apply all of our different middleware stuff

91
00:05:32,280 --> 00:05:33,790
in exactly the same way.

92
00:05:33,840 --> 00:05:35,220
So that would be very straightforward.

93
00:05:35,220 --> 00:05:44,040
We would go back over to Main.ts at the very top, I would import setup app from setup app, and then

94
00:05:44,040 --> 00:05:49,680
after creating the application inside the bootstrap function, we would call setup app with the app

95
00:05:49,860 --> 00:05:50,960
and that's it.

96
00:05:50,970 --> 00:05:55,500
So now our application is working just as it was before in the development mode.

97
00:05:55,680 --> 00:06:01,380
But now since we have this nice setup application function, we can also import it into our testing

98
00:06:01,380 --> 00:06:02,550
file as well.

99
00:06:03,230 --> 00:06:05,120
So I'm going to go back over to my test file.

100
00:06:05,150 --> 00:06:07,370
Here it is right here at the very top.

101
00:06:07,370 --> 00:06:08,510
I will import.

102
00:06:09,840 --> 00:06:10,860
Set up app.

103
00:06:14,260 --> 00:06:20,940
And now I can make use of this function after we create an instance of our nest application right there.

104
00:06:20,950 --> 00:06:23,080
So I'm going to pass in setup app.

105
00:06:23,770 --> 00:06:26,650
Or call setup app and pass in the application that was created.

106
00:06:27,390 --> 00:06:32,370
So now our application, whether is is being created for our development environment or our test environment,

107
00:06:32,370 --> 00:06:35,850
is going to have all the appropriate middlewares attached to it no matter what.

108
00:06:36,530 --> 00:06:39,830
It's not going to save this file and I'm going to try to rerun my test.

109
00:06:39,860 --> 00:06:43,970
Before I do, I'm going to change the email right here, because otherwise we're going to get an error

110
00:06:43,970 --> 00:06:45,670
from this email already being in use.

111
00:06:45,680 --> 00:06:48,170
So I'm going to put in just some different email.

112
00:06:48,590 --> 00:06:50,540
I'll then run my tests again.

113
00:06:52,160 --> 00:06:56,240
And sure enough, they're going to, as you'll see, very quickly, pass just fine.

114
00:06:56,270 --> 00:06:57,560
Yeah, there we go.

115
00:06:58,310 --> 00:06:59,990
So this definitely appears to work.

116
00:06:59,990 --> 00:07:04,640
But as mentioned, it's not really the best appropriate way of fixing everything up.

117
00:07:04,730 --> 00:07:06,980
Now, why did I show you all this solution then?

118
00:07:06,980 --> 00:07:10,760
Well, I just want you to know that we're going to go through the more nest appropriate solution.

119
00:07:10,760 --> 00:07:13,610
It is going to be a lot more effort than what we just did.

120
00:07:13,640 --> 00:07:17,990
And I just want you to know that you don't always have to go with a nest appropriate solution if you

121
00:07:17,990 --> 00:07:18,740
don't want to.

122
00:07:18,770 --> 00:07:22,520
We could very easily say, you know what, this works for our purposes.

123
00:07:22,520 --> 00:07:24,560
Let's call it there, we're done, we're good.

124
00:07:24,560 --> 00:07:27,170
And just move on to the next thing inside of our project.

125
00:07:27,230 --> 00:07:32,000
The only reason really the only reason I'm going to show you this more complicated solution is that

126
00:07:32,000 --> 00:07:33,410
this is a course about nest.

127
00:07:33,410 --> 00:07:38,930
And so I want you to understand the nest appropriate or kind of the official way of handling problems

128
00:07:38,930 --> 00:07:39,470
like this.

129
00:07:39,470 --> 00:07:40,610
That's the only reason.

130
00:07:40,610 --> 00:07:45,680
If I was working on my own project, I would definitely go with this very simple setup app function.

131
00:07:46,670 --> 00:07:47,090
Okay.

132
00:07:47,090 --> 00:07:53,030
So I'm going to undo everything I just did in this video, so I'm going to delete the setup app function.

133
00:07:54,630 --> 00:07:57,990
I'm going to undo all the changes I just made inside of Main.ts.

134
00:07:59,030 --> 00:08:00,650
So a bunch of undos here.

135
00:08:00,680 --> 00:08:01,640
There we go.

136
00:08:01,760 --> 00:08:06,770
And then back inside my spec file, I'm going to remove the import for setup app at the top.

137
00:08:07,850 --> 00:08:10,700
And remove the call to setup app right there as well.

138
00:08:11,850 --> 00:08:14,400
Okay, so now I'm back to how I was before.

139
00:08:14,400 --> 00:08:17,550
Now, if I tried to run my tests, I would probably get the exact same error.

140
00:08:18,200 --> 00:08:19,220
So let's take a pause right here.

141
00:08:19,220 --> 00:08:24,230
We'll come back in just a moment and understand what the once again, kind of nest official way to fix

142
00:08:24,230 --> 00:08:25,130
this is.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:02,000 --> 00:00:02,540
In this video.

2
00:00:02,540 --> 00:00:07,490
We're going to take a look at the second way of solving this issue of having some setup only run during

3
00:00:07,490 --> 00:00:11,630
the bootstrap function, which does not get executed when our tests are ran.

4
00:00:11,870 --> 00:00:16,340
So the second solution we're going to take a look at is, at first glance, going to seem very similar

5
00:00:16,340 --> 00:00:17,990
to what I showed you in the last video.

6
00:00:17,990 --> 00:00:22,160
But as soon as we start actually writing some code, you're going to see that this is, in fact just

7
00:00:22,160 --> 00:00:23,240
a little bit different.

8
00:00:23,780 --> 00:00:29,060
Okay, So we are still going to apply our validation pipe and this cookie session middleware as a global

9
00:00:29,060 --> 00:00:31,170
pipe and a global middleware.

10
00:00:31,190 --> 00:00:35,960
That means that these two things are going to run on every single request that comes into our application.

11
00:00:35,990 --> 00:00:41,540
The only difference here is that we're going to set these things up globally from within the app module

12
00:00:41,540 --> 00:00:42,380
itself.

13
00:00:43,050 --> 00:00:48,240
So when the app module is executed, when we create an application out of it, the app module is going

14
00:00:48,240 --> 00:00:52,130
to automatically set up this validation pipe and the cookies session middleware.

15
00:00:52,170 --> 00:00:58,830
So this is a very small and very subtle difference between doing it inside Main.ts and inside the app

16
00:00:58,830 --> 00:00:59,830
module directly.

17
00:00:59,850 --> 00:01:02,850
Effectively, it's kind of like almost the same thing.

18
00:01:02,850 --> 00:01:06,570
Either way, these things are going to run on every single incoming request.

19
00:01:06,600 --> 00:01:09,160
The only difference is where we are setting them up.

20
00:01:09,180 --> 00:01:13,590
So again, this might seem very similar to what I showed you in the last video, but there are some

21
00:01:13,590 --> 00:01:15,990
very, very subtle differences around it.

22
00:01:16,140 --> 00:01:21,090
First, the big, subtle, first, big, subtle difference is that setting these things up from directly

23
00:01:21,090 --> 00:01:25,620
inside the app module is going to be a little bit more confusing, a little bit more complicated.

24
00:01:25,800 --> 00:01:31,230
The other big difference is that by using this approach, we are now going to be able to use dependency

25
00:01:31,230 --> 00:01:35,790
injection on the setup of that pipe and the setup of the cookies session middleware itself.

26
00:01:35,820 --> 00:01:40,290
Now that is a very small secondary concern, so don't worry about that fact just yet.

27
00:01:40,290 --> 00:01:45,160
I only mention it as one reason that you might set up these things from inside the app module itself

28
00:01:45,160 --> 00:01:48,310
as opposed to from directly inside the Main.ts file.

29
00:01:49,160 --> 00:01:50,500
They might be a little bit curious.

30
00:01:50,510 --> 00:01:52,430
Why did we not just do this from the get go?

31
00:01:52,460 --> 00:01:56,180
Why did we not just wire up the pipe and the middleware from inside the app module?

32
00:01:56,360 --> 00:02:00,650
Well, the reason for that is that if you look at the official documentation, they're going to make

33
00:02:00,650 --> 00:02:06,200
it seem like wiring up stuff from inside the main.ts file is just totally fine and there's no issues

34
00:02:06,200 --> 00:02:07,490
with it whatsoever.

35
00:02:07,580 --> 00:02:10,190
And as we've seen, that's not really true.

36
00:02:10,220 --> 00:02:15,470
If you set up stuff from inside the main file, these things are not going to be set up for you when

37
00:02:15,470 --> 00:02:17,330
you are running end to end tests.

38
00:02:17,420 --> 00:02:21,680
So that's why I want to show you setting everything up inside the main file, because that's what the

39
00:02:21,680 --> 00:02:22,910
documentation says.

40
00:02:22,910 --> 00:02:27,170
And I want you to see why we would move it over to inside the app module.

41
00:02:27,200 --> 00:02:30,260
Again, it's to make sure that we can run our tests successfully.

42
00:02:32,030 --> 00:02:32,360
Okay.

43
00:02:32,360 --> 00:02:35,570
So we're going to first set up our validation pipe from inside the app module.

44
00:02:35,570 --> 00:02:37,770
That's going to be pretty easy and straightforward.

45
00:02:37,790 --> 00:02:42,080
We'll then come back in the next video and start to set up the cookies session globally, which is going

46
00:02:42,080 --> 00:02:43,460
to be a little bit more involved.

47
00:02:43,460 --> 00:02:44,420
