So let's get to it.

48
00:02:44,420 --> 00:02:45,890
First off, the validation pipe.

49
00:02:47,420 --> 00:02:47,780
All right.

50
00:02:47,780 --> 00:02:52,160
So to get started, I'm going to go back over to my editor and I'm going to find my app module right

51
00:02:52,160 --> 00:02:52,670
here.

52
00:02:53,820 --> 00:02:55,560
Inside the app module at the very top.

53
00:02:55,560 --> 00:02:57,060
I'm then going to import.

54
00:02:58,010 --> 00:03:00,890
That exact same validation pipe thing right there.

55
00:03:00,890 --> 00:03:05,540
And as you'll recall, we're going to get the validation pipe from Nestjs Common.

56
00:03:05,750 --> 00:03:07,820
So I'll put in validation.

57
00:03:09,160 --> 00:03:09,640
Pipe.

58
00:03:10,210 --> 00:03:11,650
I'm also going to import.

59
00:03:12,270 --> 00:03:18,990
Something called app pipe from Nestjs core notis core right there.

60
00:03:18,990 --> 00:03:19,650
It's not common.

61
00:03:19,650 --> 00:03:20,880
It's core instead.

62
00:03:22,130 --> 00:03:25,340
And then going to go down to my list of providers.

63
00:03:26,560 --> 00:03:28,690
So here's our list of providers right here.

64
00:03:28,960 --> 00:03:32,290
And inside of this array, I'm going to add in a brand new object.

65
00:03:34,410 --> 00:03:37,950
We're going to say provide app pipe.

66
00:03:38,560 --> 00:03:40,090
And then use.

67
00:03:41,750 --> 00:03:42,740
Value.

68
00:03:43,750 --> 00:03:46,230
And we're going to put in our entire custom pipe right there.

69
00:03:46,240 --> 00:03:47,200
We're not really custom pipe.

70
00:03:47,320 --> 00:03:48,790
We're going to put our pipe in right there.

71
00:03:48,790 --> 00:03:52,120
So to just save a little bit of time, I'll go back over to my main file.

72
00:03:52,300 --> 00:03:54,520
I'm going to cut that pipe right there.

73
00:03:55,500 --> 00:03:58,260
And then paste it in for use value like so.

74
00:03:58,850 --> 00:03:59,660
And that's it.

75
00:03:59,660 --> 00:04:03,830
That's how we set up a global pipe from inside of our app module.

76
00:04:04,130 --> 00:04:08,210
Now, you'll notice right away that this kind of looks very similar to some code that we wrote out inside

77
00:04:08,210 --> 00:04:09,810
of a test a little bit ago.

78
00:04:09,830 --> 00:04:11,300
So what does this really do?

79
00:04:11,330 --> 00:04:17,420
Well, it says whenever we create an instance of our app module, automatically make use of this thing

80
00:04:17,420 --> 00:04:23,180
or take this value right here and apply it to every incoming request that flows into our application.

81
00:04:23,210 --> 00:04:25,070
So that's kind of what's going on behind the scenes.

82
00:04:25,070 --> 00:04:30,530
We're saying specifically every single request that comes into the application, run it through this

83
00:04:30,530 --> 00:04:33,140
class right here or this instance of a class right here.

84
00:04:34,690 --> 00:04:36,860
That's how we set up a global pipe.

85
00:04:37,700 --> 00:04:42,380
Once again kind of seems vaguely similar to what we did back inside the Main.ts file, because either

86
00:04:42,380 --> 00:04:47,030
way, we're just creating an instance of validation pipe and well, telling the application to use it

87
00:04:47,030 --> 00:04:48,050
in some fashion.

88
00:04:48,720 --> 00:04:50,700
So this is kind of straightforward.

89
00:04:50,700 --> 00:04:54,110
But like I said, setting up the middleware is going to be a little bit more involved.

90
00:04:54,120 --> 00:04:59,580
So let's take a pause right here and then see how we turn our cookies session middleware into a global

91
00:04:59,580 --> 00:05:01,110
middleware in the next video as well.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,210 --> 00:00:05,680
At the end of the last video, I did not clean up this app, use global pipe statement inside of our

2
00:00:05,680 --> 00:00:06,550
main.ts file.

3
00:00:06,550 --> 00:00:08,200
So I'm going to clean that up right away.

4
00:00:08,900 --> 00:00:13,580
Okay, so now our next step is to take our cookies session middleware, and we want to apply this as

5
00:00:13,580 --> 00:00:15,130
a global middleware as well.

6
00:00:15,140 --> 00:00:19,430
We want to make sure that it gets applied to every single request that comes into our application.

7
00:00:19,670 --> 00:00:23,390
So to do so, we're going to once again go back over to our app module.

8
00:00:25,090 --> 00:00:31,420
And now inside of our app module class, we're going to add in a function to it called configure.

9
00:00:33,470 --> 00:00:39,530
The arguments to this are going to be something called consumer and its type will be middleware.

10
00:00:39,530 --> 00:00:40,640
Consumer.

11
00:00:41,950 --> 00:00:47,620
I'm going to then immediately import that type at the very top from Nestjs Common.

12
00:00:47,890 --> 00:00:50,110
So I'll add in that import right there.

13
00:00:52,440 --> 00:00:56,910
Okay, so this configure function is going to be called automatically whenever our application starts

14
00:00:56,910 --> 00:00:58,350
listening for incoming traffic.

15
00:00:58,350 --> 00:01:03,450
So inside of here we can set up some middleware that will run on every single incoming request.

16
00:01:03,750 --> 00:01:07,370
To do so, we're going to call our reference consumer.

17
00:01:07,380 --> 00:01:11,730
We're going to call apply, and then we're going to pass in the middleware that we want to run right

18
00:01:11,730 --> 00:01:12,320
here.

19
00:01:12,330 --> 00:01:16,080
So in our case, we want to make sure we run that cookie session middleware.

20
00:01:16,080 --> 00:01:20,460
So just like we did a moment ago, I'm going to come back over here to my Main.ts file.

21
00:01:20,490 --> 00:01:23,490
I'm going to cut cookie session from right there.

22
00:01:25,000 --> 00:01:27,370
And then paste it into that API call.

23
00:01:28,580 --> 00:01:32,480
I then need to make sure I add in our require statement for cookie session at the very top.

24
00:01:32,570 --> 00:01:34,520
So at the very top I will add in.

25
00:01:35,290 --> 00:01:36,430
Cookie session.

26
00:01:37,560 --> 00:01:40,320
Is require cookie session.

27
00:01:41,760 --> 00:01:47,970
Then back inside my main.ts file, I'm going to clean up the app.use call and our two unused require

28
00:01:47,970 --> 00:01:49,260
and import statements.

29
00:01:53,620 --> 00:01:53,890
All right.

30
00:01:53,920 --> 00:01:56,260
Finally back inside of the app module.

31
00:01:57,230 --> 00:01:58,730
Down at the very bottom.

32
00:02:00,810 --> 00:02:02,580
Right after calling applied right here.

33
00:02:02,580 --> 00:02:05,170
We're then going to chain on one more method call.

34
00:02:05,190 --> 00:02:11,430
So we're going to chain on four roots and we're going to put in a star like so.

35
00:02:11,730 --> 00:02:16,740
So that means that we want to make use of this middleware on every single incoming request that flows

36
00:02:16,740 --> 00:02:18,390
into our entire application.

37
00:02:18,570 --> 00:02:21,060
So that means it's essentially a global middleware.

38
00:02:22,120 --> 00:02:26,740
Okay, So we've now got a globally scoped middleware that's going to be applied whenever we start up

39
00:02:26,740 --> 00:02:30,160
our application and we've got a globally scoped pipe as well.

40
00:02:30,490 --> 00:02:34,330
So you'll notice now the downside to this approach is that even though we are wiring stuff up and kind

41
00:02:34,330 --> 00:02:39,790
of the official nest way is that it's really a lot more challenging to take a glance in one location

42
00:02:39,790 --> 00:02:45,010
and understand what pipes or what middlewares we are applying to every single request that flows into

43
00:02:45,010 --> 00:02:45,580
our app.

44
00:02:45,610 --> 00:02:49,850
Previously, we could see all that stuff very easily from inside of our main.ts file.

45
00:02:49,870 --> 00:02:54,190
We could take a look at the bootstrap function and just immediately recognize that we are going to apply

46
00:02:54,190 --> 00:02:57,610
the validation pipe and cookie session to every incoming request.

47
00:02:57,820 --> 00:03:02,380
But now that code is tucked away inside the app module in a pretty confusing fashion.

48
00:03:02,380 --> 00:03:07,120
It's a lot more challenging to now take a look at this file and understand, okay, every request is

49
00:03:07,120 --> 00:03:12,280
going to have the validation applied to it or the validation pipe applied to it and the cookie session

50
00:03:12,280 --> 00:03:13,420
middleware as well.

51
00:03:13,630 --> 00:03:18,280
I know you can see these two things on these few lines right here, but there's just a lot more noise

52
00:03:18,280 --> 00:03:18,790
around it.

53
00:03:18,790 --> 00:03:21,040
So it's just a little bit harder to actually see it.

54
00:03:22,680 --> 00:03:22,980
Okay.

55
00:03:22,980 --> 00:03:24,000
So that should be it.

56
00:03:24,030 --> 00:03:29,290
We should now still be applying our validation pipe and the cookie session middleware to every request.

57
00:03:29,310 --> 00:03:33,180
So it's essentially equivalent to what we had before when we were doing all the setup inside the main

58
00:03:33,570 --> 00:03:34,140
file.

59
00:03:34,170 --> 00:03:38,670
The only big difference is that now these things are going to be wired up whenever we create our application

60
00:03:38,670 --> 00:03:40,140
using the app module.

61
00:03:40,440 --> 00:03:42,780
So let's now go back over to our test file.

62
00:03:43,800 --> 00:03:44,700
Here's my spec file.

63
00:03:44,700 --> 00:03:45,740
The end to end test.

64
00:03:45,750 --> 00:03:47,010
I'm going to go down to the bottom.

65
00:03:47,010 --> 00:03:51,000
I'm going to change out the email once again because remember, we always need a unique email address

66
00:03:51,000 --> 00:03:51,540
right now.

67
00:03:51,540 --> 00:03:53,160
We're going to fix that up in just a moment.

68
00:03:53,400 --> 00:03:55,500
So I'm going to make a change to that email.

69
00:03:55,560 --> 00:03:59,040
I'll then go back over to my terminal and run my tests again.

70
00:04:00,650 --> 00:04:03,050
And after a small pause.

71
00:04:04,260 --> 00:04:04,620
Yep.

72
00:04:04,620 --> 00:04:05,160
There we go.

73
00:04:05,190 --> 00:04:06,780
They're both now passing.

74
00:04:06,810 --> 00:04:07,740
Fantastic.

75
00:04:08,220 --> 00:04:09,510
Okay, so this looks good.

76
00:04:09,720 --> 00:04:12,180
There are some obvious trade offs here.

77
00:04:12,210 --> 00:04:16,210
Our code is more complicated to understand, but we don't have to deal with that separate setup app

78
00:04:16,230 --> 00:04:16,890
function.

79
00:04:16,890 --> 00:04:19,590
So it's really up to you which solution you want to take.

80
00:04:19,890 --> 00:04:24,480
Now that we've got this working, we do have another small issue, although we can run our tests one

81
00:04:24,480 --> 00:04:25,980
time and have them pass.

82
00:04:26,010 --> 00:04:28,350
If I try to run this test suite again.

83
00:04:28,950 --> 00:04:31,520
The second time I run it, I am going to see an error.

84
00:04:31,530 --> 00:04:36,500
And the reason for that is that I'm trying to sign up a user with an email that is already in use.

85
00:04:36,510 --> 00:04:39,600
So that's the next thing we're going to fix up in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,160 --> 00:00:05,390
We were just able to run our tests successfully one time, but then the second time we ended up with

2
00:00:05,390 --> 00:00:06,350
another failure.

3
00:00:06,350 --> 00:00:09,440
And the failure in this case says got 400 bad request.

4
00:00:09,440 --> 00:00:11,450
So let's figure out what is going wrong here.

5
00:00:12,350 --> 00:00:12,710
All right.

6
00:00:12,710 --> 00:00:16,700
So once I describe this issue, I think you're going to say very quickly, oh, of course, that's what's

7
00:00:16,700 --> 00:00:17,360
happening.

8
00:00:17,360 --> 00:00:19,700
So here's what's going on in our test suite right now.

9
00:00:19,700 --> 00:00:24,380
We have a test where we are attempting to sign up the very first time we try to run this test.

10
00:00:24,410 --> 00:00:29,090
We are sending sending a request off to our application, which is going to write some information into

11
00:00:29,090 --> 00:00:30,380
our SQLite database.

12
00:00:30,380 --> 00:00:33,710
And remember that SQLite database is the.

13
00:00:34,390 --> 00:00:37,780
DB SQLite file inside of our root project directory.

14
00:00:38,140 --> 00:00:44,110
So we're going to end up creating a new record inside of here with some email and some password like

15
00:00:44,110 --> 00:00:44,560
so.

16
00:00:45,630 --> 00:00:51,810
Now that information is persisted inside of that SQLite file and it does not automatically get deleted

17
00:00:51,810 --> 00:00:54,450
or anything like that during our test run.

18
00:00:54,450 --> 00:01:00,330
So the net result is that the next time we run these exact same tests, we are going to create a new

19
00:01:00,330 --> 00:01:04,500
instance of our application because that is what occurs inside of our Beforeeach statement.

20
00:01:04,500 --> 00:01:09,480
But we're going to rerun the exact same test with the same email and the same password.

21
00:01:09,480 --> 00:01:16,230
So we're going to try to add in a brand new user with the exact same email to the exact same database,

22
00:01:16,230 --> 00:01:18,920
and naturally that email is already in use.

23
00:01:18,930 --> 00:01:22,980
So we're going to end up getting an error message saying, Sorry, but this email is in use.

24
00:01:22,980 --> 00:01:26,340
You cannot sign up to users with the exact same email.

25
00:01:26,460 --> 00:01:27,540
So that's the issue.

26
00:01:27,570 --> 00:01:32,520
We can run our test successfully one time with a unique email, but then after that the tests are going

27
00:01:32,520 --> 00:01:33,120
to fail.

28
00:01:33,860 --> 00:01:35,450
So how are we going to fix this?

29
00:01:35,480 --> 00:01:39,620
Well, one obvious way might be to just randomize the email we try to sign up with.

30
00:01:39,730 --> 00:01:41,080
But that's not super.

31
00:01:41,090 --> 00:01:42,320
Maybe not the best approach.

32
00:01:42,330 --> 00:01:44,900
Let's take a look at a slightly different way of fixing this.

33
00:01:46,430 --> 00:01:47,810
Okay, so here's the idea.

34
00:01:47,990 --> 00:01:53,480
Every time that we run any single test inside of our test suite, so every one of those, it functions

35
00:01:53,480 --> 00:01:58,550
right before that inside of our before each statement or something like it, We are going to both create

36
00:01:58,550 --> 00:02:03,680
a new instance of our application, which is already happening, but we are also going to either wipe

37
00:02:03,710 --> 00:02:07,010
our database or just create a brand new one.

38
00:02:07,160 --> 00:02:12,380
So that's going to allow each of our tests to run in total isolation so we can run one request right

39
00:02:12,380 --> 00:02:16,700
here that's going to handle a request to sign up, that's going to make a request to our app, which

40
00:02:16,700 --> 00:02:19,400
is still going to write that information into our database.

41
00:02:21,620 --> 00:02:27,890
Then immediately after that test succeeds or fails, we're then going to create another new instance

42
00:02:27,890 --> 00:02:28,370
of our app.

43
00:02:28,370 --> 00:02:32,530
And again, either wipe the existing database or just create a brand new one.

44
00:02:32,540 --> 00:02:37,370
So then we might have another test called something like handles the request to maybe sign in.

45
00:02:37,370 --> 00:02:40,790
And in this case, we are now working with a totally empty database.

46
00:02:40,790 --> 00:02:46,340
So we would have to first maybe sign up a user and then attempt to sign in as them.

47
00:02:46,700 --> 00:02:51,770
So this is going to prevent us from leaking data between our different tests, which might cause one

48
00:02:51,770 --> 00:02:57,170
to pass and the other to fail, or might cause a test one time to pass, but then every subsequent time

49
00:02:57,170 --> 00:02:58,220
cause it to fail.

50
00:02:58,520 --> 00:02:59,720
So that's pretty much it.

51
00:02:59,720 --> 00:03:03,920
In addition to creating a new instance of our app, which is already occurring inside that before each

52
00:03:03,920 --> 00:03:07,580
statement, we're also going to either wipe or recreate the database.

53
00:03:08,560 --> 00:03:10,620
Now, even though that might sound kind of simple.

54
00:03:10,660 --> 00:03:12,820
Turns out there are a couple of things we need to be aware of.

55
00:03:12,850 --> 00:03:13,750
First one.

56
00:03:15,560 --> 00:03:21,610
I want you to be aware of is that it might really be worth us trying to have two copies of our database,

57
00:03:21,620 --> 00:03:26,870
one for our development mode, or, as you and I are trying to manually test our application and then

58
00:03:26,870 --> 00:03:32,030
maybe a completely separate database which is only used when we are running our application in testing

59
00:03:32,030 --> 00:03:32,600
mode.

60
00:03:32,810 --> 00:03:37,760
The reason we might want to do this is that as you and I are actively testing our application using

61
00:03:37,760 --> 00:03:43,520
our client, we would probably want to have 1 or 2 user accounts sitting around that we can very easily

62
00:03:43,520 --> 00:03:47,210
log in as and then make requests using those credentials.

63
00:03:47,420 --> 00:03:53,030
If we were to only be using one single database that got wiped entirely with every single time we ran

64
00:03:53,030 --> 00:03:57,950
our tests, doing any active development of our application would get really tedious really quickly

65
00:03:57,950 --> 00:04:01,460
because every single time we run our tests, we would wipe all of our data.

66
00:04:01,460 --> 00:04:06,320
We would then have to re sign up to our application every single time we wanted to manually test our

67
00:04:06,320 --> 00:04:06,650
app.

68
00:04:07,390 --> 00:04:12,640
So we're going to solve that by just having these two separate databases, one that will be used for

69
00:04:12,640 --> 00:04:17,320
us just actively running our application from the terminal, and then a totally separate database that

70
00:04:17,320 --> 00:04:22,780
will be used when we are testing our application using the end to end test suite so we can delete this

71
00:04:22,780 --> 00:04:27,820
testing one over here, wipe it as many times as we want, but we're not going to do the same for our

72
00:04:27,820 --> 00:04:28,990
development database.

73
00:04:29,870 --> 00:04:30,200
Okay.

74
00:04:30,200 --> 00:04:31,070
So that's the general idea.

75
00:04:31,070 --> 00:04:32,540
That's how we're going to fix up this problem.

76
00:04:32,540 --> 00:04:38,180
We're going to first make sure we wipe or recreate our database right before every single test we run.

77
00:04:38,180 --> 00:04:42,350
We're also going to make sure that we have two separate databases, one for development and one for

78
00:04:42,350 --> 00:04:43,010
testing.

79
00:04:43,190 --> 00:04:45,980
So let's start to implement all this in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:02,660
We have two big challenges ahead of us.

2
00:00:02,670 --> 00:00:06,810
The first big challenge is to make sure that we use a separate database between development and testing.

3
00:00:06,840 --> 00:00:11,160
The second challenge after that is to make sure that we wipe out all the data inside of our testing

4
00:00:11,160 --> 00:00:13,530
database in between each test.

5
00:00:13,560 --> 00:00:17,430
So we're going to first focus on just making sure that we have two different databases.

6
00:00:17,430 --> 00:00:18,330
That's it.

7
00:00:18,600 --> 00:00:24,120
Now, to start off this project, I first want to give you a very quick reminder on how we decide what

8
00:00:24,120 --> 00:00:25,710
database we are going to use right now.

9
00:00:25,740 --> 00:00:32,070
So if you open up your app module once again inside of here, you will find that type module right here.

10
00:00:32,160 --> 00:00:35,370
One of the properties we passed off to it was this database string.

11
00:00:35,400 --> 00:00:40,170
That string determines what database we are going to use when we are running our application.

12
00:00:40,260 --> 00:00:45,630
So in order to use a different database during development and testing, all we really have to do is

13
00:00:45,630 --> 00:00:50,130
change out that string depending upon whether we are running in development or test mode.

14
00:00:50,890 --> 00:00:55,630
A very simple way to do this would be to read in an environment variable into our application.

15
00:00:55,630 --> 00:00:59,710
And depending upon whether that environment variable says that we are running in test or development,

16
00:00:59,740 --> 00:01:01,270
we can change out that string.

17
00:01:02,780 --> 00:01:08,320
Now, usually reading in environment variables into a node application is very easy and very straightforward.

18
00:01:08,330 --> 00:01:13,040
So our issue right now could be solved with some very simple code.

19
00:01:13,070 --> 00:01:17,940
Here's what we could potentially write to solve this problem and have two separate databases.

20
00:01:17,960 --> 00:01:25,310
We could say something like if node or process env node env is.

21
00:01:26,070 --> 00:01:26,790
Test.

22
00:01:27,120 --> 00:01:30,120
Then I want to use a database called test.

23
00:01:30,150 --> 00:01:31,170
SQLite.

24
00:01:32,230 --> 00:01:32,500
Oop.

25
00:01:32,500 --> 00:01:33,760
No colon, Right.

26
00:01:33,760 --> 00:01:35,380
There should be a question mark.

27
00:01:35,380 --> 00:01:39,490
And then otherwise, I want to use a database called DB SQLite.

28
00:01:39,490 --> 00:01:42,370
So we could use a very simple ternary expression like this.

29
00:01:42,370 --> 00:01:43,480
So if we're running a test.

30
00:01:43,510 --> 00:01:44,200
Great.

31
00:01:44,200 --> 00:01:44,680
Right.

32
00:01:44,680 --> 00:01:46,840
Every everything inside of this file.

33
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:47,380
Otherwise.

34
00:01:47,380 --> 00:01:47,590
Right.

35
00:01:47,590 --> 00:01:48,910
Everything inside that file.

36
00:01:49,060 --> 00:01:52,990
Now, of course, this doesn't really handle the production case, but the point still stands.

37
00:01:52,990 --> 00:01:54,640
We could have some logic like this.

38
00:01:54,640 --> 00:01:57,700
Very simple and very basic, but unfortunately.

39
00:01:57,700 --> 00:02:00,910
Well, we're going to do something a little bit more complicated.

40
00:02:00,910 --> 00:02:02,380
And let me tell you why.

41
00:02:03,750 --> 00:02:04,290
Okay.

42
00:02:04,910 --> 00:02:09,920
So at the end of the day, what we really need to do here is read in some environment variable configuration

43
00:02:09,920 --> 00:02:14,810
and then change out that DB name property based upon what environment we are in.

44
00:02:14,810 --> 00:02:20,980
In order to read in environment configuration, we are going to use recommended way of doing this.

45
00:02:20,990 --> 00:02:24,020
Now, in my personal opinion, this is just my opinion.

46
00:02:24,020 --> 00:02:24,530
Ness.

47
00:02:24,530 --> 00:02:28,760
Handling of environment variables is really way too complicated.

48
00:02:28,760 --> 00:02:30,380
It is very much over the top.

49
00:02:31,180 --> 00:02:34,930
Now, normally I would not put in a disclaimer like this inside the course because I want you to remain

50
00:02:34,930 --> 00:02:36,850
like excited about the stuff we're learning.

51
00:02:36,850 --> 00:02:41,470
But in this particular case, handling environment configuration is just so complicated.

52
00:02:41,470 --> 00:02:44,530
And as soon as we start it, you're going to say, this seems ridiculous.

53
00:02:44,530 --> 00:02:46,140
And I just want you to understand.

54
00:02:46,150 --> 00:02:47,530
Yes, I agree with you.

55
00:02:47,530 --> 00:02:49,510
It is a little bit over the top.

56
00:02:49,630 --> 00:02:52,830
So why are we still going to do it the recommended way?

57
00:02:52,840 --> 00:02:58,390
Well, as I mentioned just a little bit ago in a previous video, I want you to learn nest as Nest recommends

58
00:02:58,390 --> 00:02:58,630
it.

59
00:02:58,630 --> 00:03:03,580
So I want you to understand what the documentation says you should do when you are looking to handle

60
00:03:03,580 --> 00:03:04,720
environment variables.

61
00:03:04,720 --> 00:03:09,400
So that's why we are going to follow Nest recommendation, even though, again, in my opinion, it's

62
00:03:09,400 --> 00:03:11,530
a little bit overly complicated.

63
00:03:11,830 --> 00:03:17,290
So let me tell you how we're going to read in some environment variables that are going to specify what

64
00:03:17,290 --> 00:03:18,670
database we should use.

65
00:03:20,340 --> 00:03:21,990
Okay, so here's the general idea.

66
00:03:22,720 --> 00:03:24,160
So we've got our app module.

67
00:03:24,160 --> 00:03:30,040
And remember, whenever we create a module that creates a container and a container allows us to list

68
00:03:30,040 --> 00:03:33,370
out some different classes and what their dependencies are.

69
00:03:33,550 --> 00:03:37,390
So we are going to create a new service called the config service.

70
00:03:37,420 --> 00:03:42,190
The only goal, the config service is to read in some environment variables and make those environment

71
00:03:42,190 --> 00:03:44,710
variables available to the rest of our application.

72
00:03:44,920 --> 00:03:50,020
Then to make use of that service rather than just directly referencing it or something like that, we

73
00:03:50,020 --> 00:03:54,520
are going to use dependency injection to read all these different environment variables.

74
00:03:54,670 --> 00:03:58,150
So we are going to slightly change our type module code.

75
00:03:58,150 --> 00:04:03,460
So really this block right here, we're going to change that block and we're going to say that in order

76
00:04:03,460 --> 00:04:09,070
to make use of the type module, it needs to have a copy of the config service.

77
00:04:09,600 --> 00:04:14,700
So then the type or module is going to make use of that config service to read in some environment variables

78
00:04:14,700 --> 00:04:18,840
and set up some properties such as this database field right here.

79
00:04:19,630 --> 00:04:25,030
So once again, this is really, really over the top, considering how easy environment variables can

80
00:04:25,030 --> 00:04:26,380
be to read in.

81
00:04:26,680 --> 00:04:29,490
There are reasons that Nest chooses to do things like this.

82
00:04:29,500 --> 00:04:32,870
Personally, I just I'm not going to say any more about it.

83
00:04:32,890 --> 00:04:34,060
I do think it's over the top.

84
00:04:34,060 --> 00:04:35,290
I'm just going to leave it there.

85
00:04:36,050 --> 00:04:37,180
Let's just pause right here.

86
00:04:37,190 --> 00:04:41,420
Come back in just a moment, and we're going to start to set up this config service and provide some

87
00:04:41,420 --> 00:04:43,550
configuration to that type or module.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# section 14: Managing App Configuration

1
00:00:00,880 --> 00:00:03,630
Let's start the setup of this config service thing.

2
00:00:03,640 --> 00:00:07,420
So the config service, fortunately you and I do not have to create ourselves.

3
00:00:07,420 --> 00:00:11,530
Instead we are going to install a package that is going to create the config service for us.

4
00:00:11,530 --> 00:00:16,000
So let's go over to our terminal right away and install that back over at my terminal.

5
00:00:17,040 --> 00:00:20,490
I'm going to stop my running server and then do an NPM install.

6
00:00:21,320 --> 00:00:22,370
At nest.

7
00:00:23,450 --> 00:00:25,370
JS config.

8
00:00:25,790 --> 00:00:30,410
Once that is installed, I'm then going to start my server back up with an NPM run start colon dev.

9
00:00:31,480 --> 00:00:31,780
All right.

10
00:00:31,780 --> 00:00:34,220
So let's take a look at what is actually inside that package.

11
00:00:34,240 --> 00:00:37,510
I'm going to show you a couple of different diagrams and help you understand what it really does for

12
00:00:37,510 --> 00:00:38,020
us.

13
00:00:39,060 --> 00:00:43,710
So internally that package includes another package called dot env.

14
00:00:44,040 --> 00:00:49,380
This is a plain JavaScript library that is very commonly used with a lot of different backend JavaScript

15
00:00:49,380 --> 00:00:51,000
and TypeScript projects.

16
00:00:51,180 --> 00:00:55,710
The only goal of this library is to put together a list of different environment variables that exist

17
00:00:55,710 --> 00:00:56,650
on your machine.

18
00:00:56,670 --> 00:01:01,860
It's going to do so by taking a look at all the different normal environment variables that are defined,

19
00:01:01,860 --> 00:01:07,020
and it's also optionally going to read in the contents of some file inside of your project as well,

20
00:01:07,020 --> 00:01:10,470
and try to pull some environment configuration from that file too.

21
00:01:10,560 --> 00:01:14,940
Usually by default we call this file the dot env file.

22
00:01:16,200 --> 00:01:21,270
So this library is going to read in configuration information from both locations, assemble all that

23
00:01:21,270 --> 00:01:24,540
information into a single object and then give it back to you.

24
00:01:24,870 --> 00:01:29,820
If there's ever any conflict between the environment variables between these two locations.

25
00:01:29,820 --> 00:01:35,670
So, for example, if they both define a DB name and variable, then the normal environment variable

26
00:01:35,700 --> 00:01:37,950
on your machine is going to take precedence.

27
00:01:37,950 --> 00:01:40,920
So in this case, our final DB name.

28
00:01:41,900 --> 00:01:43,190
Would be Asdf.

29
00:01:45,320 --> 00:01:51,020
So this object that is created by the Env library is then going to be exposed to the rest of your application

30
00:01:51,020 --> 00:01:52,830
through the config service.

31
00:01:52,850 --> 00:01:57,620
So we're going to use dependency injection to get access to that config service and then use that service

32
00:01:57,620 --> 00:02:02,060
to read values out of this object right here.

33
00:02:02,450 --> 00:02:07,220
So here's the first kind of little hang up I have with this entire config service thing.

34
00:02:07,220 --> 00:02:09,140
Why don't we just use directly?

35
00:02:09,140 --> 00:02:10,250
That's pretty much it.

36
00:02:10,280 --> 00:02:15,680
By making use of this extra library of nestjs slash config, it's just an extra layer of complexity

37
00:02:15,680 --> 00:02:20,660
and it's kind of hard to say that it really gives us any defined extra benefit to this entire system.

38
00:02:21,790 --> 00:02:22,150
Okay.

39
00:02:22,150 --> 00:02:26,830
So now there are a couple of other issues I want you to be aware of right away, specifically around

40
00:02:26,830 --> 00:02:29,480
the treatment of this dot env file.

41
00:02:29,500 --> 00:02:35,800
So if you go and read the official dot env library documentation on that file and if you read the nestjs

42
00:02:35,800 --> 00:02:39,850
documentation on this file, they're going to tell you two very different things.

43
00:02:41,400 --> 00:02:47,190
So the Nestjs documentation says that it is totally fine to have multiple different files naturally,

44
00:02:47,190 --> 00:02:48,630
as long as they have different names.

45
00:02:49,220 --> 00:02:56,270
But the library itself, their documentation says never, ever under any circumstance ever create more

46
00:02:56,270 --> 00:02:58,430
than one dot env file.

47
00:02:58,520 --> 00:03:03,230
So the documentation between these two libraries are giving you a very different opinions.

48
00:03:03,950 --> 00:03:09,710
The other thing that is really not addressed here is how a file would ever get created in a production

49
00:03:09,710 --> 00:03:10,580
environment.

50
00:03:10,610 --> 00:03:12,350
Let me help you understand what I mean by that.

51
00:03:13,050 --> 00:03:18,930
The people who put together this library strongly recommend that you never commit that file to version

52
00:03:18,930 --> 00:03:19,660
control.

53
00:03:19,680 --> 00:03:23,130
So we are not going to commit that file with git or anything like it.

54
00:03:23,160 --> 00:03:28,980
That means that when you go to deploy your application, presumably using a Git based workflow in your

55
00:03:28,980 --> 00:03:32,610
production environment, that env file will not exist.

56
00:03:32,610 --> 00:03:37,170
So you still need to get all the information that would normally be inside that file into your production

57
00:03:37,170 --> 00:03:37,980
environment.

58
00:03:37,980 --> 00:03:41,700
But that's not really super well addressed by the people.

59
00:03:42,470 --> 00:03:47,330
The other thing that's kind of mysterious and not really addressed by the dot env setup is it doesn't

60
00:03:47,330 --> 00:03:52,340
really give you any good way of having different configuration in just one single environment.

61
00:03:52,380 --> 00:03:57,110
So remember the entire idea behind this config module stuff you and I are putting together is that we

62
00:03:57,110 --> 00:04:04,040
want to change that value right there based upon whether on our computer we are running in a development

63
00:04:04,040 --> 00:04:06,230
environment or a test environment.

64
00:04:06,950 --> 00:04:08,870
And we're going to read that config information.

65
00:04:08,870 --> 00:04:10,550
So the value for that string right there.

66
00:04:10,580 --> 00:04:13,640
Hopefully out of some kind of env file.

67
00:04:13,790 --> 00:04:20,240
But if we can only have one of those files in any given environment, how can we have some information

68
00:04:20,240 --> 00:04:23,780
inside the file to be used during testing and different during development?

69
00:04:23,990 --> 00:04:26,690
So all this stuff, it's just nasty.

70
00:04:26,720 --> 00:04:28,010
It's just not great.

71
00:04:28,040 --> 00:04:29,870
So let me tell you what you and I are going to do.

72
00:04:31,120 --> 00:04:35,090
We're going to kind of forget the rules set up by the people.

73
00:04:35,110 --> 00:04:37,030
We're not going to very strictly follow their rules.

74
00:04:37,060 --> 00:04:39,650
Instead, we're going to take the Nest recommendation.

75
00:04:39,670 --> 00:04:43,420
We are going to have more than one different dot env file.

76
00:04:43,450 --> 00:04:47,980
We're going to have one specifically to be used during development of our application and one during

77
00:04:48,010 --> 00:04:49,530
testing of our application.

78
00:04:49,540 --> 00:04:55,360
So these different files are going to provide some different database names for this database property

79
00:04:55,360 --> 00:04:55,900
right here.

80
00:04:55,930 --> 00:05:00,430
We'll have one file that says use a database name of DB Dot SQLite and then the other file will say,

81
00:05:00,430 --> 00:05:04,600
use a database name of something like Test dot SQLite or something like that.

82
00:05:04,810 --> 00:05:11,140
So again, we are not strictly following the recommendations of dot env because I don't know, I'm just

83
00:05:11,140 --> 00:05:14,740
not a really big fan of that library myself, as you can tell with all this environment configuration

84
00:05:14,740 --> 00:05:15,370
stuff.

85
00:05:15,640 --> 00:05:17,080
So let's take a pause right here.

86
00:05:17,080 --> 00:05:20,920
We're going to come back and put a lot of this stuff into practice, start creating these two different

87
00:05:21,430 --> 00:05:24,130
files and then read information from both of them.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,570 --> 00:00:07,370
Back at my code editor inside of my root project directory, I'm going to create two different env files.

2
00:00:07,390 --> 00:00:08,860
So I'm going to create one.

3
00:00:10,390 --> 00:00:11,560
That is called.

4
00:00:12,330 --> 00:00:15,690
Dot env dot development.

5
00:00:16,790 --> 00:00:20,120
I'll then create another one called dot env dot test.

6
00:00:20,740 --> 00:00:26,740
So again, I want to repeat, we are not strictly following rules set out by the people, but this is

7
00:00:26,740 --> 00:00:29,530
a solution that's going to work out reasonably well for you and I.

8
00:00:29,560 --> 00:00:34,060
So then inside of each of these different files, we're going to specify some different database name

9
00:00:34,060 --> 00:00:37,170
to be used inside of the development file.

10
00:00:37,180 --> 00:00:42,310
I'm going to put in a DB name of DB dot SQLite.

11
00:00:43,970 --> 00:00:49,790
And then inside the test file, I'm going to put in a name of test dot SQLite.

12
00:00:52,430 --> 00:00:58,310
Okay, so now back inside of our app module, we're going to wire up the config module or the config

13
00:00:58,310 --> 00:01:02,930
library we installed just a moment ago and we're going to specify which these two different files to

14
00:01:02,930 --> 00:01:06,080
be used based upon our current environment.

15
00:01:06,380 --> 00:01:07,610
So at the very top.

16
00:01:08,440 --> 00:01:17,020
I'm going to add in an import statement for config module and config service from nestjs.

17
00:01:18,840 --> 00:01:20,100
Slash config.

18
00:01:21,530 --> 00:01:24,270
At this point I didn't really discuss the config module at all.

19
00:01:24,290 --> 00:01:28,130
The config module is what we're going to use to specify which of these two different files we actually

20
00:01:28,130 --> 00:01:28,910
want to read.

21
00:01:28,940 --> 00:01:33,350
The config service is what is going to expose the information inside those files to the rest of our

22
00:01:33,350 --> 00:01:34,160
application.

23
00:01:35,210 --> 00:01:39,890
So we're going to first set up the config module by adding it into our list of imports.

24
00:01:40,040 --> 00:01:43,280
So at the very top of our list of imports, I'll say config.

25
00:01:44,390 --> 00:01:45,320
Module.

26
00:01:47,970 --> 00:01:54,690
We're going to add on a four route to it and we're going to pass in a configuration object to the configuration

27
00:01:54,690 --> 00:01:55,170
object.

28
00:01:55,170 --> 00:01:59,490
We're going to first say that we only want to set up this config module one time and then after we set

29
00:01:59,490 --> 00:02:03,210
it up, we want to use this config module throughout the rest of our application.

30
00:02:03,210 --> 00:02:06,840
So anywhere else where we need any kind of environment information.

31
00:02:07,170 --> 00:02:09,380
So essentially we want to use this thing globally.

32
00:02:09,390 --> 00:02:13,140
So to do so we're going to add in a is global of true.

33
00:02:13,630 --> 00:02:19,030
So this setting right here just means that we do not have to re-import the config module all over the

34
00:02:19,030 --> 00:02:23,200
place into other modules inside of our project whenever we want to get some config information.

35
00:02:24,380 --> 00:02:29,540
Then as a second property, we're going to put in exactly which of these two files we want to use.

36
00:02:29,750 --> 00:02:35,750
So we're going to say env file path and I'm going to put in a template string right here.

37
00:02:36,140 --> 00:02:38,690
So we're going to read in dot env.

38
00:02:39,140 --> 00:02:44,310
Then another dot and then we're going to put in whatever our current environment is.

39
00:02:44,330 --> 00:02:47,450
So I'll put in process env node env.

40
00:02:48,080 --> 00:02:52,670
So if we are in a development environment, hopefully that environment variable right there will be

41
00:02:52,670 --> 00:02:56,480
set to development and that will cause us to read in the development file.

42
00:02:56,510 --> 00:03:02,390
Otherwise if we are in a test environment, hopefully the node env environment variable will be set

43
00:03:02,390 --> 00:03:05,810
to test and we should read in the env test file instead.

44
00:03:07,230 --> 00:03:08,400
Okay, so that's step one.

45
00:03:08,400 --> 00:03:13,200
We are now reading in hopefully the correct file and all the information from it.

46
00:03:13,380 --> 00:03:18,510
So now step two, we need to get that information which is shared to the rest of our app through this

47
00:03:18,510 --> 00:03:22,560
config service into our type module.

48
00:03:22,830 --> 00:03:28,410
Now, unfortunately, this is one of the parts where I said, yeah, configuration stuff with nest is

49
00:03:28,410 --> 00:03:28,920
kind of nasty.

50
00:03:28,950 --> 00:03:33,990
Unfortunately we cannot just put in here something like config service dot.

51
00:03:34,940 --> 00:03:36,880
Dbname or what have you.

52
00:03:36,890 --> 00:03:40,160
Unfortunately, we have to do something a little bit more complicated.

53
00:03:40,160 --> 00:03:42,890
So this is where dependency injection is going to come into play.

54
00:03:43,100 --> 00:03:47,030
So we're going to do a slight refactor to this entire block of code right here.

55
00:03:47,060 --> 00:03:53,840
The entire goal of the refactor is so that we can get access to this config service through the dependency

56
00:03:53,840 --> 00:03:55,130
injection system.

57
00:03:55,950 --> 00:04:00,150
So I'm going to comment out the entire type or module block that we currently have.

58
00:04:02,230 --> 00:04:05,950
And then right above it, I'm going to put in a very similar block with just a couple of changes to

59
00:04:05,950 --> 00:04:06,440
it.

60
00:04:06,460 --> 00:04:12,430
So I'm going to say type or module dot for route async.

61
00:04:15,620 --> 00:04:19,040
I'm then going to inject the config service.

62
00:04:19,040 --> 00:04:21,310
So that right there, that's the magic part.

63
00:04:21,320 --> 00:04:26,540
That's what tells the dependency injection system that we want to go and find the configuration service

64
00:04:26,540 --> 00:04:31,130
which should have all of our config info inside of it from our chosen file.

65
00:04:31,130 --> 00:04:37,130
And we want to get access to that instance of the config service during the setup of our type module.

66
00:04:38,890 --> 00:04:43,720
Then as a second argument or a second property inside of here, we're going to put in use factory.

67
00:04:44,170 --> 00:04:51,080
This is going to be a function that is going to receive our instance of the config service.

68
00:04:51,100 --> 00:04:53,320
So this is the dependency injection part.

69
00:04:53,350 --> 00:04:57,610
This is where we are going to get our copy or the instance of the config service that again should have

70
00:04:57,610 --> 00:05:00,790
all the information from our env file inside of it.

71
00:05:01,870 --> 00:05:06,640
Then from this function, we're going to return the same kind of object that we were previously passing

72
00:05:06,640 --> 00:05:08,740
directly into for root down here.

73
00:05:08,860 --> 00:05:13,810
So I'm going to return an object that has a type of SQLite.

74
00:05:14,850 --> 00:05:16,290
A database property.

75
00:05:16,290 --> 00:05:20,640
And remember, that was the property that we wanted to change based upon our environment.

76
00:05:20,790 --> 00:05:25,100
So for database right here, we're going to read the value from our config service.

77
00:05:25,110 --> 00:05:30,930
So I'm going to put in config dot get, I'm going to put in angle brackets with string.

78
00:05:31,700 --> 00:05:37,130
And I'm going to specify exactly what environment variable we want to read in from these two files or

79
00:05:37,130 --> 00:05:38,570
one of these two files.

80
00:05:38,960 --> 00:05:41,690
So it's going to be DB underscore name.

81
00:05:43,600 --> 00:05:51,910
Well then put in same options we had before of synchronize true and for entities I'll put in user and

82
00:05:51,910 --> 00:05:52,720
report.

83
00:05:53,790 --> 00:05:55,470
And that's it, hopefully.

84
00:05:55,500 --> 00:05:57,540
So that hopefully should be it.

85
00:05:58,020 --> 00:06:02,160
So now the last thing we need to do is we need to make sure that whenever we start up our project,

86
00:06:02,160 --> 00:06:08,880
either in development mode or in test mode, we need to make sure that the node env environment variable

87
00:06:08,880 --> 00:06:09,870
is actually set.

88
00:06:09,870 --> 00:06:14,340
Because if this is not set, if it is something like undefined or something unexpected, we're going

89
00:06:14,340 --> 00:06:20,400
to try to read in the env undefined environment variable or environment file, which clearly does not

90
00:06:20,400 --> 00:06:21,090
exist.

91
00:06:21,210 --> 00:06:24,360
When we try to read that in, we're going to end up not failing.

92
00:06:24,360 --> 00:06:25,770
An error is not thrown from it.

93
00:06:25,770 --> 00:06:30,360
So it's only going to be later on down the road when we try to read in config information and then we

94
00:06:30,360 --> 00:06:34,350
eventually get something like undefined back then we're going to notice that something went wrong and

95
00:06:34,350 --> 00:06:36,390
that just makes that a little bit hard to troubleshoot.

96
00:06:36,540 --> 00:06:41,460
So again, we need to make sure that whenever we run our project now this environment variable is set

97
00:06:41,460 --> 00:06:44,370
so we know exactly which of these two files we are going to use.

98
00:06:45,280 --> 00:06:47,650
So let's take care of that last step in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,180 --> 00:00:04,600
As I mentioned at the end of the last video, last thing we have to do here is make sure that the node

2
00:00:04,600 --> 00:00:09,940
underscore env environment variable gets correctly set to either development or test whenever we are

3
00:00:09,940 --> 00:00:14,800
just running our application normally or whenever we are running our application in a test environment.

4
00:00:15,190 --> 00:00:20,020
So to set this environment variable correctly, we're going to open up our package.json file.

5
00:00:20,050 --> 00:00:24,280
We're then going to find our different Start scripts and the different test scripts.

6
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:28,970
So in front of each of these different scripts, we're going to set up that environment variable.

7
00:00:28,990 --> 00:00:33,700
If you are on anything besides Windows right now, you would normally be able to write out something

8
00:00:33,700 --> 00:00:37,540
like node env equals development.

9
00:00:38,700 --> 00:00:42,990
And that would just take care of it, but want to accommodate for anyone who is on Windows, of course.

10
00:00:42,990 --> 00:00:47,280
So rather than setting our environment variables like this, we're going to make use of a very small

11
00:00:47,280 --> 00:00:51,690
command line tool that's just going to help us out with this process and work equally well across different

12
00:00:51,690 --> 00:00:54,480
environments either Windows, Mac or whatever else.

13
00:00:54,720 --> 00:01:01,350
So back over at my terminal, I'm going to stop my running server and do a quick NPM install for a library

14
00:01:01,350 --> 00:01:03,000
called cross env.

15
00:01:03,690 --> 00:01:08,700
This library is all about allowing us to set up different environment variables very easily inside that

16
00:01:08,700 --> 00:01:12,180
script section that are going to work across Windows or Mac or something else.

17
00:01:12,990 --> 00:01:15,150
I'll then start my actually, let's not start it back up.

18
00:01:15,150 --> 00:01:16,800
Let's update our script first.

19
00:01:17,100 --> 00:01:20,010
So now back over inside of our script section.

20
00:01:20,100 --> 00:01:25,920
We're going to go to start start dev and debug and on each of those three.

21
00:01:25,950 --> 00:01:31,230
So just those three not start production, we are going to add in cross env.

22
00:01:32,520 --> 00:01:38,820
Node underscore env equals development and then the rest of that entire command.

23
00:01:39,620 --> 00:01:45,890
I'm then going to duplicate that little prefix right there or I guess big prefix down to the dev command.

24
00:01:47,450 --> 00:01:49,070
And the debug command as well.

25
00:01:52,060 --> 00:01:56,800
Then down on all of our different test scripts we will put in cross env.

26
00:01:57,860 --> 00:01:59,860
Node env test.

27
00:02:00,930 --> 00:02:03,780
And I'm going to duplicate that down to test watch.

28
00:02:05,720 --> 00:02:07,010
Test coverage.

29
00:02:07,830 --> 00:02:10,680
Test, debug and test end to end.

30
00:02:11,480 --> 00:02:12,770
And that should be it.

31
00:02:14,010 --> 00:02:15,300
So let's now save this.

32
00:02:15,330 --> 00:02:16,750
We're going to go back over to our terminal.

33
00:02:16,770 --> 00:02:21,180
We're going to start up our application in its development mode and just make sure that everything runs

34
00:02:21,180 --> 00:02:22,200
as expected.

35
00:02:22,410 --> 00:02:26,610
So back over at my terminal, I'll do a NPM run start dev.

36
00:02:30,060 --> 00:02:32,490
And it looks like everything is good just to make sure it is.

37
00:02:32,490 --> 00:02:37,020
I'm going to try to make a request to my application to maybe just sign in or sign up or something like

38
00:02:37,020 --> 00:02:37,580
that.

39
00:02:37,590 --> 00:02:40,170
So I will very quickly open up my client.

40
00:02:42,150 --> 00:02:45,140
And just send off any request that has to read from the database.

41
00:02:45,150 --> 00:02:45,840
In this case.

42
00:02:45,900 --> 00:02:47,910
Got an error saying that the email is is in use.

43
00:02:47,910 --> 00:02:48,900
But that's actually good.

44
00:02:48,900 --> 00:02:52,350
That's what we want because that implies that we successfully read from our database.

45
00:02:53,230 --> 00:02:54,630
Okay, so that definitely looks good.

46
00:02:54,640 --> 00:02:58,690
So now I'm going to also attempt to run my tests inside of a second terminal window.

47
00:02:59,320 --> 00:03:01,630
So in this case, I'll do a NPM run test.

48
00:03:01,660 --> 00:03:02,350
E e2e.

49
00:03:04,680 --> 00:03:08,280
And we should still see the tests very first time.

50
00:03:08,280 --> 00:03:10,200
Hopefully should work as expected.

51
00:03:10,770 --> 00:03:12,760
It looks like it actually did.

52
00:03:12,780 --> 00:03:14,190
I did get an error up here.

53
00:03:15,120 --> 00:03:16,170
Database is locked.

54
00:03:16,200 --> 00:03:16,590
Okay.

55
00:03:16,590 --> 00:03:17,460
That's actually good.

56
00:03:17,460 --> 00:03:19,860
We kind of want that error right now, so that's totally okay.

57
00:03:20,570 --> 00:03:20,870
Okay.

58
00:03:20,870 --> 00:03:23,270
So overall, I'd say that this looks pretty good.

59
00:03:23,360 --> 00:03:25,730
We do, of course, have this database is locked here.

60
00:03:25,760 --> 00:03:26,360
Don't sweat it.

61
00:03:26,360 --> 00:03:28,040
We're going to fix that up in just a moment.

62
00:03:28,160 --> 00:03:33,770
So now we have two different sets of of environment configuration, one for development and one for

63
00:03:33,770 --> 00:03:34,280
test.

64
00:03:34,310 --> 00:03:38,600
We are, of course, going to have to do something very similar when we move our application to production

65
00:03:38,600 --> 00:03:40,970
and eventually deploy it as well for production.

66
00:03:40,970 --> 00:03:42,710
We're not going to actually create a file.

67
00:03:42,710 --> 00:03:45,920
We're going to instead just manually create some different environment variables.

68
00:03:46,280 --> 00:03:51,560
Now the very last thing I want to do I do not want to commit the development or the test environment

69
00:03:51,560 --> 00:03:52,180
files.

70
00:03:52,190 --> 00:03:53,630
I don't want to commit those to git.

71
00:03:53,630 --> 00:03:58,820
I don't want to accidentally expose any information or secrets I might have around my development environment

72
00:03:58,820 --> 00:04:02,210
to the outside world, just in case I ever post this thing up on GitHub.

73
00:04:02,570 --> 00:04:07,550
So to make sure I do not commit those two files inside of my gitignore file.

74
00:04:08,190 --> 00:04:10,700
We're going to list out those two file names.

75
00:04:10,710 --> 00:04:17,160
So I'm going to put in dot env development and dot env test like so.

76
00:04:19,060 --> 00:04:22,060
So now I can commit everything inside my project as much as I want.

77
00:04:22,060 --> 00:04:24,010
And I will not commit those two files.

78
00:04:25,360 --> 00:04:26,290
Okay, so this looks good.

79
00:04:26,290 --> 00:04:32,440
We've now got different databases for our test, whereas our diagram, here we go, we got different

80
00:04:32,440 --> 00:04:34,850
databases for development and for testing.

81
00:04:34,870 --> 00:04:36,190
Definitely a step forward.

82
00:04:36,220 --> 00:04:40,180
We do still have that error we just saw a moment ago around the locking of the database.

83
00:04:40,180 --> 00:04:43,690
So let's take a quick pause right here and then fix that one up in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,490 --> 00:00:05,100
In the last video, we were almost able to successfully run our tests.

2
00:00:05,120 --> 00:00:09,740
However, you might have gotten an error like this one, something that says SQLite busy database is

3
00:00:09,740 --> 00:00:10,250
locked.

4
00:00:10,250 --> 00:00:13,430
So I want to very quickly explain what that means and how we can solve it.

5
00:00:13,460 --> 00:00:16,070
If you did not get that error, no problem whatsoever.

6
00:00:16,070 --> 00:00:18,080
Still, stick around and watch this video anyways.

7
00:00:18,470 --> 00:00:22,730
So a little bit of backstory here on what goes on when we run our end to end tests.

8
00:00:22,820 --> 00:00:25,280
By default, our test runner is jest.

9
00:00:25,280 --> 00:00:30,140
So Jest is going to read our two different spec files because remember we have that app spec file and

10
00:00:30,140 --> 00:00:35,640
the auth spec file jest tries to run all of your different tests at the exact same time.

11
00:00:35,660 --> 00:00:40,880
So that means that we have one test running that's going to create an instance of our application and

12
00:00:40,880 --> 00:00:43,850
try to access the Test SQLite database.

13
00:00:43,970 --> 00:00:49,430
At the same time, we are also going to be running the app spec file and that is also going to be creating

14
00:00:49,430 --> 00:00:53,120
an instance of our application and try to connect to that exact same database.

15
00:00:53,270 --> 00:00:57,710
In general, SQLite does not like to see multiple different connections to it.

16
00:00:57,710 --> 00:01:02,730
It really just wants to see one single connection to that database that gets shared among all the different

17
00:01:02,730 --> 00:01:04,140
things inside of our application.

18
00:01:04,140 --> 00:01:08,610
So the whole problem right now is just that Jest is trying to run multiple different tests at the exact

19
00:01:08,610 --> 00:01:09,390
same time.

20
00:01:11,030 --> 00:01:12,020
To solve this problem.

21
00:01:12,020 --> 00:01:14,620
We're going to tell just don't try to run our tests in parallel.

22
00:01:14,630 --> 00:01:16,420
Just run one test at a time.

23
00:01:16,430 --> 00:01:19,600
Now, this might sound like it's going to significantly slow down our tests.

24
00:01:19,610 --> 00:01:21,380
In fact, it's actually the opposite.

25
00:01:21,410 --> 00:01:26,210
It turns out that when you're making use of TypeScript with jest together, having tests run in parallel,

26
00:01:26,240 --> 00:01:29,830
usually you get actually far, far worse performance.

27
00:01:29,900 --> 00:01:34,730
So when we tell jest to only run one test at a time, it's actually going to make our tests usually

28
00:01:34,730 --> 00:01:36,030
run a lot faster.

29
00:01:36,050 --> 00:01:38,120
You can actually see this effect very quickly.

30
00:01:38,450 --> 00:01:42,770
So to change jest behavior, we're going to go back to our Package.json file.

31
00:01:44,110 --> 00:01:47,350
I'm going to go to the test E2e script right there.

32
00:01:47,350 --> 00:01:53,170
And at the very end I'm going to add on an option of dash, dash max workers.

33
00:01:54,050 --> 00:01:55,340
Equals one.

34
00:01:55,820 --> 00:01:59,270
So that means only try to run one spec file at a time.

35
00:02:00,360 --> 00:02:05,070
So now to test this out again, I'm going to delete the test SQLite file that was created inside of

36
00:02:05,070 --> 00:02:06,210
our project directory.

37
00:02:06,300 --> 00:02:07,680
So I'm going to delete that.

38
00:02:09,460 --> 00:02:12,160
I'll then go and run my tests again at the terminal.

39
00:02:12,160 --> 00:02:13,930
So another end to end test.

40
00:02:15,930 --> 00:02:17,010
And now this time.

41
00:02:18,170 --> 00:02:21,590
All my tests are going to pass and I don't see any errors coming from SQLite.

42
00:02:21,620 --> 00:02:25,730
Naturally, if I try to run the tests again, I will get an error this time.

43
00:02:25,730 --> 00:02:30,050
But that's just because remember we've already got a user making use of the email that we tried to sign

44
00:02:30,050 --> 00:02:34,490
up as I didn't get any errors coming from SQLite or anything like that.

45
00:02:34,940 --> 00:02:36,440
Okay, so this is a good improvement.

46
00:02:36,590 --> 00:02:39,560
So now we've got our two separate databases.

47
00:02:39,560 --> 00:02:40,010
Very good.

48
00:02:40,010 --> 00:02:43,280
We've got one for development and one for testing.

49
00:02:43,310 --> 00:02:45,050
Now remember our original goal.

50
00:02:45,050 --> 00:02:49,460
We want to make sure that right before we run each of our different tests, we wipe out that entire

51
00:02:49,460 --> 00:02:50,190
database.

52
00:02:50,210 --> 00:02:52,580
So let's take care of that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,500 --> 00:00:05,670
The last thing we have to take care of is to make sure that we wipe out or somehow delete all the data

2
00:00:05,670 --> 00:00:09,270
inside of our test database right before every single test runs.

3
00:00:09,300 --> 00:00:12,090
So that's the last thing we're going to take care of inside this video.

4
00:00:12,420 --> 00:00:15,650
Now, as far as clean up our data, we have two different options available.

5
00:00:15,660 --> 00:00:21,030
Either go into our database, find all the different tables, and delete all the rows in each table.

6
00:00:21,030 --> 00:00:22,230
That's option number one.

7
00:00:22,260 --> 00:00:27,570
Option number two is to just delete the entire database and then right before the next test starts,

8
00:00:27,570 --> 00:00:31,500
rely upon type ORM to automatically recreate the database for us.

9
00:00:31,860 --> 00:00:33,330
So we're going to go with the latter.

10
00:00:33,330 --> 00:00:38,700
We're going to delete the entire database in between every test type or IM once again is going to jump

11
00:00:38,700 --> 00:00:41,310
in and recreate the database automatically for us.

12
00:00:42,240 --> 00:00:43,560
Now to delete the database.

13
00:00:43,560 --> 00:00:48,020
All we really have to do is, well, delete the test SQLite file.

14
00:00:48,030 --> 00:00:49,020
That's pretty much it.

15
00:00:49,470 --> 00:00:53,940
So we could write out some code for this inside of our different before each statements inside of all

16
00:00:53,940 --> 00:00:56,310
of our different end to end test files.

17
00:00:56,700 --> 00:01:01,290
But putting all this repetitive code inside of these before each is would well, it'd be repetitive.

18
00:01:01,470 --> 00:01:06,330
So rather than writing out all this database deleting logic inside of every single before each, we're

19
00:01:06,330 --> 00:01:09,150
going to instead define a global before each.

20
00:01:09,270 --> 00:01:15,030
So this will be one single before each statement that is going to run before every single test in every

21
00:01:15,030 --> 00:01:17,670
file of our end to end test suite.

22
00:01:19,270 --> 00:01:25,480
So to build a global before each, we're going to first begin by opening up the just e to e JSON file

23
00:01:25,480 --> 00:01:26,740
inside the test directory.

24
00:01:27,040 --> 00:01:30,100
Inside of here, we're going to add in a new option at the very bottom.

25
00:01:30,550 --> 00:01:33,820
This is called setup files after E and V.

26
00:01:34,780 --> 00:01:38,230
But then going to put in an array with root dir.

27
00:01:39,300 --> 00:01:41,400
And set up dates.

28
00:01:42,260 --> 00:01:48,200
This defines a file that's going to be executed after all of our tests, or I should say, right before

29
00:01:48,200 --> 00:01:50,270
all of our tests are just about to be executed.

30
00:01:50,810 --> 00:01:54,800
So we are going to place this global before each inside the setup file.

31
00:01:55,370 --> 00:01:59,000
The root dir right here is pretty much a reference to our test directory.

32
00:01:59,000 --> 00:02:00,680
That's what the root dir is.

33
00:02:01,070 --> 00:02:06,470
So inside of the test folder, I'm going to make a new file called setup dots.

34
00:02:07,850 --> 00:02:10,940
Then inside of Europe to define a global B for each function.

35
00:02:10,940 --> 00:02:14,000
I will add in global dot before each.

36
00:02:16,380 --> 00:02:18,540
And mark that function as async.

37
00:02:19,540 --> 00:02:24,880
So now this function right here is going to be executed before every single test across all of our different

38
00:02:24,880 --> 00:02:25,720
spec files.

39
00:02:26,140 --> 00:02:31,180
So once again, inside there, all we really have to do is go take a look at that test SQLite file inside

40
00:02:31,180 --> 00:02:32,980
of our root directory and delete it.

41
00:02:33,370 --> 00:02:37,510
So to do so, we'll just use a couple of different functions from the node standard library.

42
00:02:37,750 --> 00:02:42,580
At the very top I'm going to import our M from FS promises.

43
00:02:43,330 --> 00:02:45,640
And join from path.

44
00:02:46,600 --> 00:02:53,170
Then inside of here we'll say a weight are m short for remove and we're going to join together.

45
00:02:54,130 --> 00:02:54,880
Dir name.

46
00:02:54,880 --> 00:02:56,950
So that's underscore underscore der.

47
00:02:58,130 --> 00:03:02,570
That's going to be a reference to the test folder that this setup file is in.

48
00:03:02,900 --> 00:03:06,650
We're then going to go up one directory.

49
00:03:07,370 --> 00:03:10,610
And then inside their find the test that SQLite file.

50
00:03:11,640 --> 00:03:12,510
And that's it.

51
00:03:13,180 --> 00:03:14,830
Now, one little gotcha here.

52
00:03:14,830 --> 00:03:19,240
If we try to remove a file that does not exist, this line of code is going to throw an error.

53
00:03:19,600 --> 00:03:24,190
There might be some scenarios where, let's say maybe the very first time we run our test suite that

54
00:03:24,190 --> 00:03:27,610
SQL light file, the test SQL might not exist.

55
00:03:27,700 --> 00:03:31,300
So if it doesn't exist and we try to run this code right here, it's going to throw an error before

56
00:03:31,300 --> 00:03:32,680
we ever run any test.

57
00:03:32,890 --> 00:03:36,730
So if the file doesn't exist, I don't really care if an error is thrown, because that was the entire

58
00:03:36,730 --> 00:03:37,090
point.

59
00:03:37,090 --> 00:03:39,310
I wanted to delete the file and get rid of it.

60
00:03:39,670 --> 00:03:44,440
So to make sure that we don't get an error coming from this, if the file doesn't exist, I'm just going

61
00:03:44,440 --> 00:03:45,760
to wrap it with a try.

62
00:03:46,030 --> 00:03:47,440
Catch statement.

63
00:03:49,010 --> 00:03:53,210
And I'm not going to handle the air in any way because if an error does occur, totally fine by me.

64
00:03:53,210 --> 00:03:54,530
No problem whatsoever.

65
00:03:55,380 --> 00:03:56,940
And that should be it.

66
00:03:57,300 --> 00:03:59,050
So now let's go back over to our terminal.

67
00:03:59,070 --> 00:04:01,200
We're going to try to run our tests again.

68
00:04:03,950 --> 00:04:07,010
So back over here, I'm going to run my test e to e.

69
00:04:08,450 --> 00:04:11,530
And I should be able to run these tests one time, see everything pass.

70
00:04:11,540 --> 00:04:12,890
And then here's the real question.

71
00:04:12,890 --> 00:04:13,820
Here's the real test.

72
00:04:13,820 --> 00:04:18,290
I should be able to run the tests again and have them execute successfully again.

73
00:04:18,290 --> 00:04:19,040
Perfect.

74
00:04:19,040 --> 00:04:20,300
Exactly what we wanted.

75
00:04:20,660 --> 00:04:24,530
So now we are deleting that entire database in between every single test.

76
00:04:25,360 --> 00:04:26,920
Well, this is fantastic.

77
00:04:27,670 --> 00:04:28,000
All right.

78
00:04:28,000 --> 00:04:30,730
So that's it for doing our end to end test setup.

79
00:04:30,730 --> 00:04:35,230
So let's take a pause right here and add in a couple more tests to our spec file in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,010 --> 00:00:03,050
We spent a very long time talking about testing.

2
00:00:03,050 --> 00:00:07,550
So in this video, we're going to wrap up testing with just one more test around our authentication

3
00:00:07,550 --> 00:00:08,210
logic.

4
00:00:08,210 --> 00:00:11,900
So again, I'm inside of our auth E2e spec file at the very bottom.

5
00:00:11,900 --> 00:00:18,350
I'm going to add in a test where we first attempt to log in or how about sign up, I should say sign

6
00:00:18,350 --> 00:00:21,290
up as a new user, then get the.

7
00:00:22,030 --> 00:00:24,100
Currently logged in user.

8
00:00:27,150 --> 00:00:27,480
Okay.

9
00:00:27,480 --> 00:00:31,440
So as you can kind of tell from the description on this thing, I want to first sign up as a brand new

10
00:00:31,470 --> 00:00:36,760
user and then I want to make a get request to the Who am I route that we have inside of our app?

11
00:00:36,780 --> 00:00:41,370
Remember the Who am I route that we had implemented inside of our users controller.

12
00:00:42,280 --> 00:00:43,810
Right here.

13
00:00:44,990 --> 00:00:50,180
So here is who am I that's going to return whoever is currently signed in or really whoever is making

14
00:00:50,180 --> 00:00:51,850
the request to our application.

15
00:00:51,860 --> 00:00:56,930
So the goal is to simply sign up, then make a request to the who my route and we should get back our

16
00:00:56,930 --> 00:00:57,560
user.

17
00:00:58,320 --> 00:00:59,460
So let's go ahead and give this a shot.

18
00:00:59,490 --> 00:01:03,750
It will be just a little bit challenging because of, well, one very small hang up.

19
00:01:03,960 --> 00:01:09,510
So inside of this test, we are going to first make a request to our application in the same way we

20
00:01:09,510 --> 00:01:10,900
did right back up here.

21
00:01:10,920 --> 00:01:13,350
So I'm going to create a new email address.

22
00:01:16,590 --> 00:01:19,950
I'm then going to make a request to app.get.

23
00:01:20,010 --> 00:01:22,470
Http server.

24
00:01:23,480 --> 00:01:29,840
We're going to make a post request to auth slash signup and we're going to send along that given email

25
00:01:29,840 --> 00:01:32,450
right there along with a password.

26
00:01:33,260 --> 00:01:35,540
And the password can be pretty much anything we want.

27
00:01:36,830 --> 00:01:39,830
I'm then going to expect that this results in a 201.

28
00:01:40,840 --> 00:01:41,860
Now here's the gotcha.

29
00:01:41,860 --> 00:01:46,150
Whenever we sign up to the application, as you and I well know, because we put together the authentication

30
00:01:46,150 --> 00:01:49,660
system, this request is going to respond with a cookie.

31
00:01:49,660 --> 00:01:53,800
And that cookie is what is going to prove that we are signed in when we make a follow up request to

32
00:01:53,800 --> 00:01:54,730
our application.

33
00:01:55,610 --> 00:01:59,550
The library we are using to actually make the request is referred to as super agent.

34
00:01:59,570 --> 00:02:03,470
That is the name of the library we are using here, and that's this request object that we're dealing

35
00:02:03,470 --> 00:02:04,010
with.

36
00:02:04,040 --> 00:02:08,480
Unfortunately, super agent by default does not handle cookies for us.

37
00:02:08,480 --> 00:02:13,190
So that means that when we get a cookie back from making this request, we need to temporarily store

38
00:02:13,190 --> 00:02:18,260
that cookie inside this task right here and then send that cookie along with our follow up request to

39
00:02:18,260 --> 00:02:21,770
figure out who is currently logged in or who is currently making that request.

40
00:02:22,070 --> 00:02:27,680
So to get access to that cookie, we're going to take the response that comes back from this request.

41
00:02:27,710 --> 00:02:33,140
We can do that by saying res is await request, dot blah, blah blah.

42
00:02:33,380 --> 00:02:38,930
So now res right here is the response that we get back from making this entire request to get access

43
00:02:38,930 --> 00:02:40,430
to the cookie inside there.

44
00:02:40,700 --> 00:02:44,720
We can do cookie is res dot.

45
00:02:44,720 --> 00:02:46,460
Get set.

46
00:02:46,460 --> 00:02:47,210
Cookie.

47
00:02:48,850 --> 00:02:54,370
So that is pulling out the Set-cookie header, which is where a cookie is usually sent back to us inside

48
00:02:54,370 --> 00:02:55,480
of a response.

49
00:02:55,870 --> 00:03:00,160
So this cookie should be a string and inside of it is the information that proves that we are signed

50
00:03:00,160 --> 00:03:01,330
into the application.

51
00:03:01,480 --> 00:03:04,510
So now we're going to make a follow up request to the route handler.

52
00:03:04,510 --> 00:03:05,830
We were just looking at the Who am I?

53
00:03:05,860 --> 00:03:06,310
One.

54
00:03:06,310 --> 00:03:10,090
And we're going to send along this cookie as a header inside that request as well.

55
00:03:11,090 --> 00:03:13,510
So we'll say await request.

56
00:03:13,970 --> 00:03:17,420
App.get http server once again.

57
00:03:18,840 --> 00:03:21,000
I'll then do a git to auth slash.

58
00:03:21,000 --> 00:03:21,840
Who am I?

59
00:03:22,500 --> 00:03:27,270
And along with that request I'm going to send the cookie like so.

60
00:03:27,600 --> 00:03:33,000
So calling set with a string of cookie sets the cookie header on the outgoing request.

61
00:03:33,180 --> 00:03:36,000
We then provide the cookie that we want to send inside of it.

62
00:03:37,440 --> 00:03:40,440
I'm then going to expect that I get back a 200 from that.

63
00:03:42,090 --> 00:03:47,040
And to make sure that we actually are truly signed in beyond just expecting that we get a 200 inside

64
00:03:47,040 --> 00:03:47,700
the response.

65
00:03:47,700 --> 00:03:52,140
I'm also going to receive the response right here in structure off the body property.

66
00:03:52,900 --> 00:03:55,000
So I'm going to pull out just the body, like so.

67
00:03:55,360 --> 00:03:59,090
So the body is going to be whatever gets sent back to us inside the response.

68
00:03:59,110 --> 00:04:04,210
So inside that body, remember, that should be our user entity, which means it should have an ID and

69
00:04:04,210 --> 00:04:05,400
an email as well.

70
00:04:05,410 --> 00:04:10,060
So let's just take a look at the email property and make sure that the email is identical to the email

71
00:04:10,060 --> 00:04:11,170
that we signed up with.

72
00:04:11,990 --> 00:04:13,190
So we can do.

73
00:04:13,430 --> 00:04:20,150
Expect body email to equal the email we had signed up with at the top of the test.

74
00:04:20,540 --> 00:04:21,890
And that should be it.

75
00:04:22,790 --> 00:04:23,810
So let's save this.

76
00:04:23,840 --> 00:04:26,900
Go back over to our terminal and rerun our tests.

77
00:04:29,280 --> 00:04:30,480
So back over here.

78
00:04:31,190 --> 00:04:31,610
Yep.

79
00:04:31,610 --> 00:04:32,300
Looks good to me.

80
00:04:32,330 --> 00:04:33,290
Test is passing.

81
00:04:33,290 --> 00:04:33,830
Naturally.

82
00:04:33,830 --> 00:04:36,420
We might want to make sure that we can get the test to fail.

83
00:04:36,440 --> 00:04:41,490
So if I say that maybe the body dot email is equal to just some random string.

84
00:04:41,510 --> 00:04:42,350
How about that?

85
00:04:43,840 --> 00:04:46,090
I'm gonna run my test again and just make sure they fail.

86
00:04:47,770 --> 00:04:48,520
Very good.

87
00:04:49,250 --> 00:04:51,090
Okay, Well, that is pretty much it.

88
00:04:51,110 --> 00:04:55,640
So based on this test right here, if you want to add in more tests around authentication, you are

89
00:04:55,640 --> 00:04:57,230
absolutely free to do so.

90
00:04:57,260 --> 00:05:02,420
All you have to do inside of each test is first sign up, get access to the cookie, and then if you

91
00:05:02,420 --> 00:05:06,980
want to make a follow up request, you're going to attach that cookie to the outgoing follow up request

92
00:05:06,980 --> 00:05:09,350
using that line of code right there.

93
00:05:10,510 --> 00:05:10,930
Okay.

94
00:05:10,930 --> 00:05:14,560
Well, my friends, we have spent, like I said, a very good amount of time on testing.

95
00:05:14,560 --> 00:05:17,740
So at this point, I think we're going to say, yeah, we're pretty good on testing.

96
00:05:17,740 --> 00:05:21,910
So let's pause right here and we're going to move on inside of our application in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# section 15: Relations with TypeORM

1
00:00:00,770 --> 00:00:05,510
We have now spent a tremendous amount of time working on the authentication and user systems inside

2
00:00:05,510 --> 00:00:06,400
of our application.

3
00:00:06,410 --> 00:00:08,990
We're pretty much all done with the user stuff for now.

4
00:00:08,990 --> 00:00:13,910
So at this point we're going to go back over to the reports functionality around our application.

5
00:00:13,940 --> 00:00:19,040
As a quick reminder, this report stuff is going to allow a user to submit information about a vehicle

6
00:00:19,040 --> 00:00:24,650
that they have sold and then eventually other users will be able to try to make a query and get an estimate

7
00:00:24,650 --> 00:00:26,270
for the value of their car.

8
00:00:26,480 --> 00:00:32,600
So whenever a user submits a report, they're going to give us the make model year mileage, longitude,

9
00:00:32,600 --> 00:00:35,030
latitude and price that their cars sold for.

10
00:00:35,060 --> 00:00:39,410
We're going to record that and then we're going to use that data whenever someone tries to get their

11
00:00:39,410 --> 00:00:40,100
estimate.

12
00:00:40,700 --> 00:00:45,290
You might recall that we already generated some stuff around our reports feature, so we've already

13
00:00:45,290 --> 00:00:49,430
got the reports module controller service and we didn't create the repository.

14
00:00:49,430 --> 00:00:53,690
We created the entity which behind the scenes remember creates the repository for us.

15
00:00:54,590 --> 00:00:56,480
So with that in mind, let's take a pause right here.

16
00:00:56,480 --> 00:01:00,200
When we come back in the next video, we're going to start to work on this report's functionality.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:05,180
All right, let's get started on adding in some code to our reports module.

2
00:00:05,220 --> 00:00:09,460
Now, we've already generated that controller service and the entity as well, so we've already got

3
00:00:09,460 --> 00:00:10,840
a little bit of code in here.

4
00:00:10,840 --> 00:00:14,680
So for the most part, we're just going to be going back through these files we have already created

5
00:00:14,680 --> 00:00:18,730
and adding in some different route handlers properties to the entity and so on.

6
00:00:18,760 --> 00:00:23,590
A lot of the steps we have to go through, we already did in great detail on our users feature.

7
00:00:23,590 --> 00:00:28,270
So just about everything we need to do around reports is going to be, for the most part, really straightforward

8
00:00:28,270 --> 00:00:31,240
because it's just a repeat of all things we've done already.

9
00:00:31,600 --> 00:00:35,740
We're going to first begin by opening up our report entity and we're going to add in a couple of different

10
00:00:35,740 --> 00:00:36,820
properties to it.

11
00:00:37,210 --> 00:00:43,750
So we need to store for every report, the make the model, the year mileage, longitude and latitude

12
00:00:43,780 --> 00:00:45,310
of a vehicle sale.

13
00:00:45,310 --> 00:00:49,960
So let's go open up our report entity file and add in all these different properties to it.

14
00:00:50,970 --> 00:00:52,020
Back inside my editor.

15
00:00:52,020 --> 00:00:53,900
I'll find the reports directory inside there.

16
00:00:53,910 --> 00:00:55,920
The report entity file.

17
00:00:56,490 --> 00:01:01,330
So when we previously created this file, we had added in an ID and a price already.

18
00:01:01,350 --> 00:01:04,050
So we're going to add in some additional properties to this.

19
00:01:04,440 --> 00:01:09,690
We're going to first add in a column that's going to store the make of the vehicle.

20
00:01:09,720 --> 00:01:13,140
So remember, the make is the company who made a vehicle.

21
00:01:13,140 --> 00:01:18,390
So that would be like a Honda or a Hyundai or a Toyota and so on.

22
00:01:19,640 --> 00:01:21,740
We're also going to store the model.

23
00:01:21,890 --> 00:01:24,490
So that's going to be the actual model of the vehicle.

24
00:01:24,500 --> 00:01:28,610
So it might be a mustang or a Corolla or what have you.

25
00:01:29,990 --> 00:01:35,120
We're going to store the year that the vehicle was manufactured.

26
00:01:38,270 --> 00:01:39,890
Then we're going to also store.

27
00:01:41,060 --> 00:01:41,930
Oh, come on.

28
00:01:42,230 --> 00:01:43,220
There we go.

29
00:01:43,550 --> 00:01:46,850
The longitude and latitude of the sale of the vehicle.

30
00:01:46,850 --> 00:01:51,890
So essentially where the vehicle was sold because a vehicle that's sold in one country might have a

31
00:01:51,890 --> 00:01:55,760
totally different price expectation than a vehicle sold in a different country.

32
00:01:55,880 --> 00:02:00,320
We're going to abbreviate the longitude and latitude properties as simply LNG.

33
00:02:01,950 --> 00:02:05,250
And lat for latitude.

34
00:02:07,550 --> 00:02:13,070
And then finally, we're going to also store the mileage or the number of miles or kilometers that the

35
00:02:13,070 --> 00:02:18,470
vehicle was driven when it was actually sold because more miles or more kilometers on a vehicle decreases

36
00:02:18,470 --> 00:02:19,400
its value.

37
00:02:19,640 --> 00:02:22,880
So I'm going to call this mileage, and that's going to be a number as well.

38
00:02:24,210 --> 00:02:26,400
Okay, so there's all of our different properties.

39
00:02:26,520 --> 00:02:27,690
Simple enough.

40
00:02:27,820 --> 00:02:32,010
