So now that we've got our entity put together, we're going to start to work on our.

41
00:02:32,750 --> 00:02:34,480
Service and the controller.

42
00:02:34,490 --> 00:02:36,940
We're going to make sure that we receive requests into the controller.

43
00:02:36,950 --> 00:02:40,150
We're going to use a DTO to extract and validate some information.

44
00:02:40,160 --> 00:02:45,230
We're then going to use our service to make use of our repository and through that the entity we just

45
00:02:45,230 --> 00:02:48,830
modified to actually store these different reports inside of our database.

46
00:02:48,950 --> 00:02:52,100
So let's get started on the controller and the service in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,950 --> 00:00:05,630
When we were working on our users feature just a little bit ago, we implemented everything around the

2
00:00:05,630 --> 00:00:07,900
service and then everything around the controller.

3
00:00:07,910 --> 00:00:12,080
This time around, we're going to take a slightly different approach rather than implementing the entire

4
00:00:12,080 --> 00:00:16,730
controller and then the entire service, we're going to implement one line of functionality at a time.

5
00:00:16,730 --> 00:00:21,830
So for example, we're going to first worry about just having the entire ability to create a brand new

6
00:00:21,830 --> 00:00:22,550
report.

7
00:00:22,560 --> 00:00:27,050
So we're going to put together a route handler on our controller and then implement some supporting

8
00:00:27,050 --> 00:00:28,430
method inside the service.

9
00:00:28,430 --> 00:00:32,780
So we're going to implement everything around creating a report and then move on to our next route.

10
00:00:32,930 --> 00:00:36,020
So with that in mind, let's first open up our reports controller.

11
00:00:36,020 --> 00:00:40,610
We're going to add in a route handler that's going to receive a request that intends to create a brand

12
00:00:40,610 --> 00:00:41,630
new report.

13
00:00:41,840 --> 00:00:45,890
We're then going to implement the appropriate method on the report service.

14
00:00:46,550 --> 00:00:46,820
Okay.

And I think that's probably all we need for right now.

I'm then going to define a new route.

21

With post and we'll call this How about create report?

22
00:01:13,070 --> 00:01:16,850
Now, remember, the controller decorator already has a string right there of reports.

23
00:01:16,850 --> 00:01:22,520
So if we wanted to make a request to this handler, we would make a post request to slash reports.

24
00:01:23,900 --> 00:01:28,730
So then inside of here, we need to extract some information from the body of the request, and we want

25
00:01:28,730 --> 00:01:34,250
to validate that body against some DTO, make sure that it has all the appropriate different values.

26
00:01:34,400 --> 00:01:38,330
So let's immediately add in our body decorator.

27
00:01:38,900 --> 00:01:43,340
I'm going to say that I want to extract some information out of the body of the request.

28
00:01:44,170 --> 00:01:49,120
I'm going to call that simply body and I'm going to give this a name or a type, I should say.

29
00:01:49,120 --> 00:01:52,420
So a type annotation of Create report.

30
00:01:53,360 --> 00:01:54,170
DTO.

31
00:01:54,440 --> 00:02:01,310
So we need to create this DTO something that describes exactly what a create report body or the body

32

of a create report request actually looks like.

33
00:02:03,650 --> 00:02:06,780
So as you can guess, it's going to have a lot of very familiar properties.

34

It's going to have probably the make model year, mileage, longitude, latitude and price.

35

We're going to require all those different properties to be present inside the body and we'll also make

36

sure that they are, say, strings and numbers and so on.

37
00:02:20,030 --> 00:02:22,880
So let's open up our folder Explorer over here.

38

I'm going to create a new directory inside of reports called DTOs.

39
00:02:26,630 --> 00:02:30,890
And then inside there I'm going to make a new file called Create Report.

40
00:02:30,920 --> 00:02:31,760
DTO.

41
00:02:31,800 --> 00:02:32,780
It's.

42

find the Dto's folder inside there and then look up.

44

Maybe create user DTO.

45
00:02:45,110 --> 00:02:49,580
So again, remember a DTO, all we do is define a class with a couple of different validation rules

46
00:02:49,580 --> 00:02:50,270
on it.

47
00:02:52,210 --> 00:03:00,040
Okay, so inside of Create Report, DTO, I'm going to export a class called Create Report, DTO, and

48
00:03:00,040 --> 00:03:03,970
then we're going to list out all the different properties that we want this incoming request to have.

49
00:03:04,090 --> 00:03:09,160
So it's going to be pretty much what we just added into our report entity a moment ago.

50
00:03:09,190 --> 00:03:10,750
We should have a make.

51
00:03:11,440 --> 00:03:12,340
That's a string.

52
00:03:13,210 --> 00:03:13,720
Model.

53
00:03:13,720 --> 00:03:15,250
That's a string a year.

54
00:03:15,250 --> 00:03:16,240
That's a number.

55
00:03:16,600 --> 00:03:17,380
Mileage.

56
00:03:17,380 --> 00:03:18,430
That's a number.

57
00:03:19,480 --> 00:03:20,500
That's a number.

58
00:03:20,980 --> 00:03:22,330
And a latitude.

59
00:03:22,330 --> 00:03:23,170
That's a number.

60
00:03:23,200 --> 00:03:26,640
And then finally, we also want to have a price that this vehicle was sold for.

61
00:03:26,650 --> 00:03:28,600
And that's going to be a number as well.

62
00:03:29,430 --> 00:03:33,600
So now we're going to add in a couple of different decorators to validate each of these different properties.

63

The validation that we're going to do here is going to be a little bit more complicated or we're going

64

65

So let's take a quick pause right here, because adding in all these different validators is going to

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:04,310
Let's add in a couple of different validators around all these different properties.

2
00:00:04,310 --> 00:00:11,420
So at the very top, I'm going to import a couple of different decorators from the class validator library.

3
00:00:11,600 --> 00:00:16,210
So as I mentioned, we're going to add in many more validators than our last DTOs had.

4
00:00:16,220 --> 00:00:20,360
So in this case, for make and model, we're just going to check and make sure that those things are

5
00:00:20,360 --> 00:00:21,020
strings.

6
00:00:21,020 --> 00:00:23,630
So I'm going to get the is string decorator.

7
00:00:24,360 --> 00:00:24,990
For a year.

8
00:00:24,990 --> 00:00:27,840
I'm going to make sure that that is a number.

9
00:00:28,110 --> 00:00:32,200
And we also probably want to make sure that the user gave us a reasonable value for year.

10
00:00:32,220 --> 00:00:37,620
So a user should probably not give us a year of something like 5000 because chances are they don't have

11
00:00:37,620 --> 00:00:39,900
a vehicle that was made in the year 5000.

12
00:00:39,930 --> 00:00:44,820
We also should probably not get a year earlier than maybe 1930 or something like that because if you

13
00:00:44,820 --> 00:00:47,640
have a car from 1800, well, I kind of doubt that.

14
00:00:47,850 --> 00:00:52,980
So to make sure that the user gives us some appropriate value for year, we're going to get a validating

15
00:00:52,980 --> 00:00:56,460
decorator of Min and Max.

16
00:00:56,580 --> 00:01:02,160
These are going to allow us to make sure that the value provided for year is within some minimum range

17
00:01:02,160 --> 00:01:03,420
and maximum range.

18
00:01:05,480 --> 00:01:07,660
Then for mileage, we're going to do the exact same thing.

19
00:01:07,670 --> 00:01:09,770
We're going to make sure the mileage is a number.

20
00:01:09,800 --> 00:01:14,270
We are going to make sure that it's probably greater than zero and maybe less than something like a

21
00:01:14,270 --> 00:01:18,950
million, because chances are you don't have a vehicle that has more than 1,000,000km or a million miles

22
00:01:18,950 --> 00:01:19,670
on it.

23
00:01:20,380 --> 00:01:24,490
Then for longitude and latitude, we're going to make sure that those things are actually valid.

24
00:01:24,490 --> 00:01:26,350
Longitude and latitude values.

25
00:01:27,100 --> 00:01:27,700
For that.

26
00:01:27,700 --> 00:01:36,460
There is actually a built in decorator or two built in decorators called is longitude and is latitude.

27
00:01:36,490 --> 00:01:41,050
So those will make sure that the provided longitude and latitude are within an appropriate range.

28
00:01:41,850 --> 00:01:43,050
And then finally for the price.

29
00:01:43,050 --> 00:01:48,570
Well, once again, we probably just want to make sure that it has it is at least $0 or something like

30
00:01:48,570 --> 00:01:52,980
that, and at most probably nothing more than $1 million because chances are people are not going to

31
00:01:52,980 --> 00:01:56,610
be using our platform for vehicles that cost more than $1 million.

32
00:01:57,410 --> 00:01:57,680
All right.

33
00:01:57,680 --> 00:02:00,100
So I think that's all the different validating decorators we need.

34
00:02:00,110 --> 00:02:02,990
So let's now apply them to all of our different properties.

35
00:02:03,530 --> 00:02:07,760
So first off, make and model, we are just going to make sure that these are strings.

36
00:02:10,080 --> 00:02:10,830
For year.

37
00:02:10,860 --> 00:02:16,900
I'm going to make sure this is a number and I want to have a minimum year of how about 1930?

38
00:02:16,920 --> 00:02:19,890
So no vehicles older than 1930.

39
00:02:21,120 --> 00:02:26,040
And I'm going to put in a year here, a maximum year of something like we could just put in 2050 or

40
00:02:26,040 --> 00:02:27,000
something like that.

41
00:02:27,210 --> 00:02:32,850
Obviously, no one right now is going to have a vehicle much newer than that or anywhere near that new.

42
00:02:32,850 --> 00:02:34,890
But we'll just do some future proofing.

43
00:02:35,820 --> 00:02:36,870
For mileage.

44
00:02:37,440 --> 00:02:38,730
I want to have a number.

45
00:02:39,420 --> 00:02:44,130
Must be at least zero and at most will say 1 million.

46
00:02:44,880 --> 00:02:49,290
So people might have a vehicle with more than a million miles or kilometers on it.

47
00:02:49,290 --> 00:02:51,360
So if you want to, you can increase that value right there.

48
00:02:51,360 --> 00:02:56,100
But for me, for the purposes of this application, 1 million miles or kilometers, totally fine.

49
00:02:56,930 --> 00:03:05,870
Then for longitude and latitude, we'll use the is longitude and is latitude decorators.

50
00:03:07,540 --> 00:03:10,480
Then finally, for price, we'll make sure it's a number.

51
00:03:11,660 --> 00:03:12,800
At least zero.

52
00:03:12,980 --> 00:03:18,740
And chances are you are not selling your vehicle on our platform for anything more than maybe $1 million.

53
00:03:19,510 --> 00:03:20,740
And that should be it.

54
00:03:22,380 --> 00:03:24,060
So remember how this is going to work.

55
00:03:24,060 --> 00:03:29,100
Anytime someone makes a request to us, the validation pipe that we had set up globally as we were working

56
00:03:29,100 --> 00:03:34,530
on our tests is going to apply this DTO to the body of the incoming request and validate all these different

57

annotation right there.

So at the top I'm going to import create report DTO.

Service.

18
00:00:47,320 --> 00:00:51,970
We then make use of the private keyword we call the repository simply repo.

And then we put in a type annotation of repository with a generic type of our user entity.

20
00:00:58,870 --> 00:01:01,210
Inject repository is coming from Nestjs.

21
00:01:01,240 --> 00:01:02,200
Type ORM.

22
00:01:02,440 --> 00:01:08,440
The repository type comes from type ORM itself, and then our entity comes from our entity file.

So we need to essentially write that exact same line of code right there, just replacing the user entity

24

with our report entity.

00:01:15,130 --> 00:01:19,960
So let's go back over to our report service at the very top I'm going to import.

26
00:01:22,430 --> 00:01:24,260
Inject repository.

27
00:01:25,260 --> 00:01:26,610
From Nestjs.

28
00:01:26,640 --> 00:01:27,810
Type ORM.

29
00:01:29,270 --> 00:01:30,380
Repository.

30
00:01:31,840 --> 00:01:33,050
From type ORM.

31

And then finally report from report entity.

00:01:40,980 --> 00:01:43,800
Then inside of our service itself, we'll define our constructor.

So we'll say report is this repo create.

62
00:03:39,890 --> 00:03:41,510
We're going to pass in the report.

63
00:03:42,550 --> 00:03:43,290
DTO.

64
00:03:44,440 --> 00:03:49,000
And then return this dot repo dot save report.

65
00:03:49,690 --> 00:03:50,890
And that should be it.

66
00:03:52,940 --> 00:03:53,390
All right.

67
00:03:53,390 --> 00:03:58,380
Well, that should be enough to at least allow us to test out everything we've written so far.

68
00:03:58,400 --> 00:04:03,260
So we should be able to log into our application and then make a request to create a brand new report.

69
00:04:03,260 --> 00:04:05,550
And we should see it persisted into the database.

70
00:04:05,570 --> 00:04:08,200
So let's do a very quick test in the next video.

71
00:04:08,210 --> 00:04:10,670
So quick pause right here and we'll continue in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,980 --> 00:00:02,540
Let's test out this new route.

2
00:00:02,540 --> 00:00:07,280
Handler So to actually test this, we need to create a new client file.

3
00:00:07,610 --> 00:00:13,730
Remember our old client file if you are using the rest http extension inside of VS code like I am,

4
00:00:13,730 --> 00:00:19,400
we created the request dot http file inside of the users directory and the intent was to put only requests

5
00:00:19,400 --> 00:00:21,680
related to users inside that file.

6
00:00:21,710 --> 00:00:27,920
So because now we are working on reports, I'm going to create a new request dot http file inside of

7
00:00:27,920 --> 00:00:29,090
the reports folder.

8
00:00:29,120 --> 00:00:30,170
Now quick note here.

9
00:00:30,170 --> 00:00:33,110
This is not really an established pattern that you're going to see anywhere online.

10
00:00:33,110 --> 00:00:37,250
In other words, creating this request file inside of some associated directory.

11
00:00:37,250 --> 00:00:41,750
So if you don't like this, if you don't want to put these kind of client documenting files inside of

12
00:00:41,750 --> 00:00:44,150
our related module folders, totally fine.

13
00:00:44,150 --> 00:00:48,020
You can always create one single request file inside of our root project directory.

14
00:00:48,020 --> 00:00:49,070
That's totally fine as well.

15
00:00:49,100 --> 00:00:49,910
Totally up to you.

16
00:00:50,770 --> 00:00:54,250
So inside of this file, we're going to make a post request.

17
00:00:55,650 --> 00:00:58,710
To localhost 3000 slash reports.

18
00:00:59,100 --> 00:01:01,680
We need to apply that header of content.

19
00:01:02,560 --> 00:01:05,650
Type application Json.

20
00:01:06,450 --> 00:01:09,990
I'll then put in that new line right there and we'll put in the body of the request.

21
00:01:10,440 --> 00:01:13,470
So first let's try to just make an invalid request.

22
00:01:13,500 --> 00:01:18,000
I'm going to leave an empty body in here and I'm going to make the request as is, and let's just see

23
00:01:18,000 --> 00:01:18,840
what happens.

24
00:01:19,200 --> 00:01:22,770
Oh, let me also make sure my server is actually running because I might have stopped it.

25
00:01:22,770 --> 00:01:23,010
Yep.

26
00:01:23,010 --> 00:01:27,150
I stopped it when I was running some of those different tests, so I'll also start my server back up

27
00:01:27,150 --> 00:01:28,230
with start dev.

28
00:01:30,770 --> 00:01:31,030
Okay.

29
00:01:31,070 --> 00:01:31,500
Looks good.

30
00:01:31,520 --> 00:01:33,440
So now I'm going to make that request.

31
00:01:34,170 --> 00:01:35,310
So I'm going to send it off.

32
00:01:35,400 --> 00:01:36,330
And sure enough.

33
00:01:36,370 --> 00:01:36,630
Yep.

34
00:01:36,630 --> 00:01:38,860
Looks like our validation is kicking in.

35
00:01:38,880 --> 00:01:43,110
We're getting a lot of errors here telling us exactly how we must modify that body.

36
00:01:43,880 --> 00:01:47,670
You might also notice that it looks like there are some duplicate validation rules here.

37
00:01:47,690 --> 00:01:50,390
That's just because we are making use of multiple different decorators.

38
00:01:50,390 --> 00:01:54,890
So we're seeing all three different validations that we applied to the mileage property.

39
00:01:55,890 --> 00:01:57,360
Okay, So let's update this request.

40
00:01:57,360 --> 00:01:59,630
We're going to add in all these different required properties.

41
00:01:59,640 --> 00:02:03,210
So I'm going to put in a make of Toyota.

42
00:02:04,680 --> 00:02:06,990
A model of Corolla.

43
00:02:08,540 --> 00:02:10,370
I'm going to have a year of.

44
00:02:10,370 --> 00:02:11,480
How about 1980?

45
00:02:11,540 --> 00:02:12,740
Very old car.

46
00:02:14,610 --> 00:02:16,200
See, we also need mileage.

47
00:02:18,260 --> 00:02:21,680
We'll say I've got, how about 100,000 miles?

48
00:02:21,680 --> 00:02:24,410
And we're not really going to distinguish here between kilometers or miles.

49
00:02:24,410 --> 00:02:27,980
So if you want to just pretend that this is kilometers, totally fine, go for it.

50
00:02:28,870 --> 00:02:30,370
And we need a longitude.

51
00:02:32,370 --> 00:02:37,910
I'm going to put in a longitude of zero and a latitude of zero, and I think that should be it.

52
00:02:37,920 --> 00:02:38,610
Oh, price.

53
00:02:38,610 --> 00:02:39,870
I need a price as well.

54
00:02:40,020 --> 00:02:45,330
So I'm going to say that I sold my car for a very optimistic half $1 million.

55
00:02:46,170 --> 00:02:48,240
I don't think anyone's going to pay that much money, but who knows?

56
00:02:48,240 --> 00:02:51,900
Maybe a 1980s Corolla is a collector's item and I just don't know it.

57
00:02:52,320 --> 00:02:54,420
So now if we make this follow up request.

58
00:02:55,330 --> 00:02:58,690
Let's send it off and yep, looks like it was successful.

59
00:02:58,940 --> 00:03:02,980
Now remember, your request might fail if you are not currently signed into the application.

60
00:03:02,980 --> 00:03:09,010
So if you are not signed in, then you need to go back over to your client and make a request to first

61
00:03:09,010 --> 00:03:10,600
sign in to our application.

62
00:03:10,600 --> 00:03:15,820
So for me, I would go back over to requests inside the users directory and I would make a request to

63
00:03:15,820 --> 00:03:19,750
either sign up as a brand new user or sign in as an existing user.

64
00:03:20,970 --> 00:03:24,410
But overall, I'd say that our request handler is definitely working as expected.

65
00:03:24,420 --> 00:03:26,440
So got back a record right here.

66
00:03:26,460 --> 00:03:30,180
It looks like I've got all my different properties along with the ID as well.

67
00:03:31,690 --> 00:03:32,560
Now, one quick follow up.

68
00:03:32,560 --> 00:03:37,390
I want to do I want to try to create a report while I am not signed into the application.

69
00:03:37,390 --> 00:03:39,730
So I'm going to sign out.

70
00:03:40,620 --> 00:03:43,800
By making a post request to auth slash sign out.

71
00:03:44,110 --> 00:03:45,390
So I'm going to send that.

72
00:03:45,640 --> 00:03:45,920
Okay.

73
00:03:45,930 --> 00:03:47,640
I'm now signed out in theory.

74
00:03:48,890 --> 00:03:51,200
And now if I try to create another report.

75
00:03:52,160 --> 00:03:55,940
I'm going to get a 403 forbidden, which is exactly what we want.

76
00:03:56,030 --> 00:03:57,680
So this looks pretty good.

77
00:03:58,310 --> 00:03:58,790
All right.

78
00:03:58,820 --> 00:03:59,590
Not bad.

79
00:03:59,600 --> 00:04:00,770
So let's take a pause right here.

80
00:04:00,770 --> 00:04:04,190
We've now got the ability to create a brand new report.

81
00:04:04,400 --> 00:04:06,890
Let's take a pause and continue in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,060 --> 00:00:06,280
We are now able to create a report, which is fantastic, of course, but there is one extra little

2
00:00:06,280 --> 00:00:10,450
kind of surprise feature I would like to add into our application, something that's going to make some

3
00:00:10,450 --> 00:00:13,470
additional features down the line a little bit easier to put together.

4
00:00:13,480 --> 00:00:18,460
So here's something I want to add in very quickly to this Create report route we just assembled.

5
00:00:18,460 --> 00:00:23,740
I want to make sure that whenever we create a report, we associate the user who created the report

6
00:00:23,740 --> 00:00:25,240
with the report itself.

7
00:00:25,240 --> 00:00:29,800
So in other words, I just want a tie between a user and the reports they create.

8
00:00:29,890 --> 00:00:34,240
So then eventually, at some point in time in the future, we can take a look at all these different

9
00:00:34,240 --> 00:00:38,830
reports that have been created and we can very easily say, okay, these reports were created by the

10
00:00:38,830 --> 00:00:42,790
user with ID one and these were created with the user with ID two.

11
00:00:43,000 --> 00:00:47,890
So we need to form what is called an association between these different records.

12
00:00:47,890 --> 00:00:51,370
We need to associate a user with some reports.

13
00:00:51,610 --> 00:00:55,060
Now let me first give you a couple of different notes on associations.

14
00:00:55,830 --> 00:01:00,690
So the term association refers to relating one kind of record to another.

15
00:01:01,120 --> 00:01:02,220
And I gotta be honest with you.

16
00:01:02,250 --> 00:01:06,340
A lot of stuff we've covered in Nest so far has been, well, rather confusing.

17
00:01:06,360 --> 00:01:08,430
Nest is definitely not easy.

18
00:01:08,520 --> 00:01:12,660
And unfortunately, associations is no exception to that.

19
00:01:12,690 --> 00:01:17,850
So understanding associations and how they work and really having mastery over them requires knowledge

20
00:01:17,850 --> 00:01:23,160
of just about every topic we've covered so far inside this course, plus some more topics on top of

21
00:01:23,160 --> 00:01:28,620
that for in particular, security and understanding some rest conventions as well.

22
00:01:28,770 --> 00:01:33,750
So I'm not going to lie to you going through this association stuff and truly understanding it and having

23
00:01:33,750 --> 00:01:36,270
mastery over it is not going to be easy.

24
00:01:36,300 --> 00:01:41,010
So we're going to take this one step at a time and understand every step along the way.

25
00:01:41,010 --> 00:01:45,750
Whenever a request comes in to create a new report, exactly how we are going to associate a user with

26
00:01:45,750 --> 00:01:46,230
it.

27
00:01:46,560 --> 00:01:49,960
So for right now, all I want to tell you is our end goal.

28
00:01:49,980 --> 00:01:54,960
I want to tell you very directly how we are going to associate a user with a report.

29
00:01:55,780 --> 00:02:00,700
Okay, So here's what we need to do to actually associate a report with a user.

30
00:02:00,730 --> 00:02:03,010
We need to make a change to our database.

31
00:02:03,010 --> 00:02:07,150
So at present we've got our users table and we have a reports table.

32
00:02:07,180 --> 00:02:12,760
Right now, our reports table only has those columns of ID, the make the model, the price, latitude,

33
00:02:12,760 --> 00:02:13,660
longitude and so on.

34
00:02:13,660 --> 00:02:15,610
I'm just not showing them on this diagram.

35
00:02:15,940 --> 00:02:21,550
In order to associate a report with the user who created it, we are going to add in a new column to

36
00:02:21,550 --> 00:02:22,590
our reports table.

37
00:02:22,600 --> 00:02:26,380
We're going to call this column something like user underscore ID.

38
00:02:27,040 --> 00:02:31,350
This is going to store the ID of the user who created this report.

39
00:02:31,360 --> 00:02:37,450
So, for example, if we log in as user with ID one and we create a report, I would expect to see a

40
00:02:37,450 --> 00:02:38,920
new report added in here.

41
00:02:41,070 --> 00:02:43,230
And my user ID should be one.

42
00:02:43,230 --> 00:02:47,190
So that would indicate that this person created this report.

43
00:02:48,530 --> 00:02:52,040
If this person then created another report, it would get of two.

44
00:02:52,070 --> 00:02:54,290
Maybe this time around it is a Ford.

45
00:02:55,320 --> 00:02:57,600
And we would still have a user with ID one.

46
00:02:57,600 --> 00:03:00,510
So that is pointing back at this person right here.

47
00:03:00,810 --> 00:03:06,600
So with this column alone, we can now very easily identify exactly who created every single report

48
00:03:06,600 --> 00:03:07,860
inside of our application.

49
00:03:08,220 --> 00:03:09,090
So this is it.

50
00:03:09,090 --> 00:03:10,050
This is the end goal.

51
00:03:10,050 --> 00:03:13,980
This is what we need to make sure actually exists inside of our database.

52
00:03:14,250 --> 00:03:18,900
The reason this is going to be so challenging is that getting this column in here and then eventually

53
00:03:18,900 --> 00:03:22,520
extracting the information from it and using it in some interesting way.

54
00:03:22,530 --> 00:03:25,920
Well, that is where some big complexity is going to come in.

55
00:03:26,510 --> 00:03:28,390
So let's take a pause right here.

56
00:03:28,400 --> 00:03:32,120
We're going to come back in the next video and we're going to start to understand this entire process

57
00:03:32,120 --> 00:03:34,010
and implement it step by step.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,070 --> 00:00:05,420
All right, my friends, let's get started on building up this relationship between our users and our

2
00:00:05,420 --> 00:00:06,200
reports.

3
00:00:06,270 --> 00:00:10,010
I'll put together a little series of steps we're going to go through in the coming videos.

4
00:00:10,010 --> 00:00:13,010
So this is the series of steps we're going to go through every single time.

5
00:00:13,010 --> 00:00:17,360
We want to build up an association between different kinds of records using nest and type.

6
00:00:17,900 --> 00:00:23,270
Step number one we're going to focus on in this video is figuring out what kind of association we are

7
00:00:23,270 --> 00:00:24,290
actually modeling.

8
00:00:24,290 --> 00:00:30,050
So in the world of SQL databases, there are three kinds of associations that you need to be aware of.

9
00:00:30,080 --> 00:00:33,800
Let me show you what these associations are and give you examples of each of them.

10
00:00:33,800 --> 00:00:39,110
And I think you'll get an idea of what this term association and specifically what kind of association

11
00:00:39,110 --> 00:00:40,250
is referring to.

12
00:00:41,270 --> 00:00:41,540
Okay.

13
00:00:41,540 --> 00:00:45,890
So we're going to investigate three different kinds of relationships between different records.

14
00:00:45,920 --> 00:00:50,580
The first kind of relationship we're going to take a look at is 1 to 1 relationships.

15
00:00:50,660 --> 00:00:54,980
So this is where we have one kind of record or one kind of entity.

16
00:00:54,980 --> 00:01:02,360
And one instance of that record or entity is directly related to one other single instance of a record.

17
00:01:02,450 --> 00:01:08,130
So examples of a 1 to 1 relationship would be the relationship between a country and a capital.

18
00:01:08,150 --> 00:01:14,180
If we had a list of countries and we wanted to relate them to a list of capital cities, then every

19
00:01:14,180 --> 00:01:20,260
country would have exactly one capital city, and every capital city would have exactly one country.

20
00:01:20,270 --> 00:01:24,380
So there is a 1 to 1 relationship between a country and its capital.

21
00:01:25,320 --> 00:01:28,130
The same thing can be said about a car and its engine.

22
00:01:28,140 --> 00:01:34,230
Every single instance of a car is always going to have exactly one instance of an engine, and every

23
00:01:34,230 --> 00:01:36,600
engine has exactly one car.

24
00:01:37,450 --> 00:01:41,680
Same thing for passports and people and people and cell phones.

25
00:01:41,860 --> 00:01:46,720
Now, one important thing to note right away is that you might be able to think of examples where some

26
00:01:46,720 --> 00:01:48,770
of these different relationships are violated.

27
00:01:48,790 --> 00:01:52,270
For example, you personally might have more than one cell phone.

28
00:01:52,300 --> 00:01:56,170
You might have one phone for personal stuff and one phone for work.

29
00:01:56,500 --> 00:02:01,180
Likewise, a single person absolutely probably can have more than one passport.

30
00:02:01,210 --> 00:02:06,400
So these relationships are not set in stone and you might eventually be able to think of rules or situations

31
00:02:06,400 --> 00:02:07,550
that violate them.

32
00:02:07,570 --> 00:02:12,490
The idea behind all these different relationships comes down to how we want to model data inside of

33
00:02:12,490 --> 00:02:13,480
our application.

34
00:02:13,570 --> 00:02:19,960
So inside of our app, we might enforce rules saying that a single person can only ever have exactly

35
00:02:19,960 --> 00:02:24,220
one cell phone and every cell phone belongs to one single person in the real world.

36
00:02:24,220 --> 00:02:25,540
That might not be true.

37
00:02:25,570 --> 00:02:28,900
This is all about the context of the application we are building.

38
00:02:29,900 --> 00:02:34,250
Now we are not building a 1 to 1 relationship, so let's move on to the next kind of relationship.

39
00:02:35,510 --> 00:02:39,800
Okay, so the next kind of relationship is a one to many or a many to one.

40
00:02:39,830 --> 00:02:45,830
This is where we have one instance of one kind of record that is directly related to many other instances

41
00:02:45,830 --> 00:02:47,300
of another kind of record.

42
00:02:47,420 --> 00:02:51,500
So an example of this might be a customer and orders.

43
00:02:51,770 --> 00:02:57,320
If you are a customer of Amazon, you might have many different orders for products at any given time.

44
00:02:57,320 --> 00:03:02,750
You might make one order today and set that to be shipped out on, say, Monday and then tomorrow you

45
00:03:02,750 --> 00:03:07,130
might create a completely different order that you expect to be shipped on Tuesday or Wednesday.

46
00:03:08,360 --> 00:03:10,760
A car has many different parts.

47
00:03:10,760 --> 00:03:15,950
So one single instance of a car that might be sitting in your garage or driveway right now has many

48
00:03:15,950 --> 00:03:17,540
different parts inside of it.

49
00:03:17,660 --> 00:03:23,750
All those different parts belong to one single car, and those parts don't necessarily belong at the

50
00:03:23,750 --> 00:03:25,790
same time to multiple different cars.

51
00:03:27,000 --> 00:03:31,360
One single country is going to have many different cities inside of it.

52
00:03:31,380 --> 00:03:35,250
All the cities inside of a country belong to that one country.

53
00:03:35,910 --> 00:03:41,070
And finally, one continent on the planet might have many different mountains inside of it.

54
00:03:41,100 --> 00:03:46,260
All these different mountains belong to exactly one single continent at any given time, and there's

55
00:03:46,260 --> 00:03:50,700
probably no mountain that ever exists between distinctly different continents.

56
00:03:50,970 --> 00:03:55,980
Now, once again, you might be able to find some examples of real world things that violate these rules.

57
00:03:56,010 --> 00:04:00,240
It all comes down to how we want to model these relationships inside of our application.

58
00:04:00,360 --> 00:04:05,760
So there might be some mountain out there that actually straddles, say, Europe and Asia.

59
00:04:05,790 --> 00:04:11,550
It might be split right in the middle and some on Wikipedia, let's say, or some encyclopedia might

60
00:04:11,550 --> 00:04:15,400
say, Oh yeah, well, this mountain exists in both Europe and Asia at the same time.

61
00:04:15,420 --> 00:04:21,330
Absolutely might exist, but for the purposes of applications that we build, we would want to say that

62
00:04:21,330 --> 00:04:26,400
maybe that given mountain exists in specifically Europe or specifically Asia, one or the other.

63
00:04:28,080 --> 00:04:28,410
All right.

64
00:04:28,410 --> 00:04:29,730
Last kind of relationship.

65
00:04:30,400 --> 00:04:31,530
Many too many.

66
00:04:31,540 --> 00:04:38,080
So this is where we have a couple of instances of one kind of record that are related to multiple different

67
00:04:38,080 --> 00:04:40,030
instances of another kind of record.

68
00:04:40,030 --> 00:04:41,920
And the reverse is true as well.

69
00:04:42,100 --> 00:04:45,820
So an example of this might be trains with riders.

70
00:04:45,910 --> 00:04:50,950
On any given day on a train network, there will be many different trains and every different train

71
00:04:50,950 --> 00:04:53,110
has a collection of different riders.

72
00:04:53,410 --> 00:04:59,260
A rider likewise might have many different trains that they need to take to get to their destination.

73
00:04:59,260 --> 00:05:02,680
So this would be a classic case of a many to many relationship.

74
00:05:03,130 --> 00:05:05,920
Another good example would be classes with students.

75
00:05:06,250 --> 00:05:11,830
There are many classes going on on maybe a university campus at any given time, and every class has

76
00:05:11,830 --> 00:05:13,840
multiple different students inside of it.

77
00:05:13,990 --> 00:05:18,610
But every student in turn has many different classes that they go to throughout the day.

78
00:05:18,640 --> 00:05:21,580
So another good example of a many to many relationship.

79
00:05:22,390 --> 00:05:26,860
So now out of these three different kinds of relationships, let's think about what kind of relationship

80
00:05:26,860 --> 00:05:28,450
we have in our application.

81
00:05:28,450 --> 00:05:32,140
What is the relationship between a user and a report?

82
00:05:32,380 --> 00:05:38,410
Well, I would say that we have a many to one or alternatively a one to many relationship.

83
00:05:38,900 --> 00:05:44,090
So depending upon how we kind of look at this relationship or what kind of side of the relationship

84
00:05:44,090 --> 00:05:47,660
you consider, we refer to this either as a one to many or many to one.

85
00:05:47,930 --> 00:05:53,330
So for us, from the perspective of a user, a user has many reports.

86
00:05:53,330 --> 00:05:56,270
So that would be a one to many relationship.

87
00:05:56,540 --> 00:06:01,630
From the perspective of a report, however, one single report belongs to exactly one user.

88
00:06:01,640 --> 00:06:08,720
So from the perspective of a report, many reports belong to or have one user.

89
00:06:09,470 --> 00:06:12,740
So we fall into this kind of second case right here, one too many.

90
00:06:12,740 --> 00:06:16,430
Or alternatively, depending upon how we look at the relationship, many to one.

91
00:06:16,700 --> 00:06:22,850
So we need to set up some rules inside of our application that say specifically that exactly one user

92
00:06:22,880 --> 00:06:25,670
can have multiple different reports associated with them.

93
00:06:26,700 --> 00:06:31,180
Okay, so now that we've established what kind of relationship we have, we're now going to come back

94
00:06:31,180 --> 00:06:36,830
in the next video and we're going to add in some decorators to our user entity and our report entity,

95
00:06:36,840 --> 00:06:39,030
and we're going to set up this association.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,970 --> 00:00:05,590
Now that we've established what kind of association we have between a user and report, we're now going

2
00:00:05,590 --> 00:00:09,690
to open up our entity files and we're going to add in some appropriate decorators.

3
00:00:09,700 --> 00:00:16,240
So these are going to be some decorators coming from the type library that tell type how these two things

4
00:00:16,240 --> 00:00:18,850
or these two entities are somehow related together.

5
00:00:19,570 --> 00:00:24,050
The decorators we are going to use are called one to many and many to one.

6
00:00:24,070 --> 00:00:27,280
So those are the decorators we are going to apply to these entities.

7
00:00:27,340 --> 00:00:34,000
We are going to make use of the one to many decorator on our user because one user has many reports

8
00:00:34,450 --> 00:00:35,740
on our report entity.

9
00:00:35,770 --> 00:00:37,830
We're going to use the exact opposite decorator.

10
00:00:37,840 --> 00:00:40,610
We're going to use a separate decorator called many to one.

11
00:00:40,630 --> 00:00:45,640
We are making use of many to one because many reports have one user.

12
00:00:46,000 --> 00:00:50,830
So let's now open up these different entity files, so user and report and we're going to apply these

13
00:00:50,830 --> 00:00:51,850
decorators to them.

14
00:00:52,770 --> 00:00:53,070
Okay.

15
00:00:53,070 --> 00:00:57,720
So back inside my editor, I'm going to first go to my user entity file.

16
00:00:57,960 --> 00:00:59,160
Here it is right here.

17
00:00:59,830 --> 00:01:03,940
Then at the very top from type, I'm going to import the decorator.

18
00:01:04,060 --> 00:01:05,590
One to many.

19
00:01:06,560 --> 00:01:09,560
So one too many.

20
00:01:10,460 --> 00:01:15,830
Then inside my user entity class, I'm going to add in a new property that's going to tell type that

21
00:01:15,830 --> 00:01:19,580
we are forming a relationship between a user and a list of reports.

22
00:01:19,760 --> 00:01:22,880
So I'm going to go down right after our password property.

23
00:01:22,970 --> 00:01:26,390
I'm going to add in one to many.

24
00:01:27,970 --> 00:01:31,120
I'm going to define a property right after it called reports.

25
00:01:31,120 --> 00:01:35,260
And this is going to be of type array of report.

26
00:01:35,380 --> 00:01:39,040
So make sure you get in, report and then those square brackets right after it.

27
00:01:39,430 --> 00:01:42,490
We do have to add in an import statement for report right away.

28
00:01:42,490 --> 00:01:44,170
So that is the report entity.

29
00:01:44,200 --> 00:01:45,550
So I'm going to go up to the top.

30
00:01:46,220 --> 00:01:47,540
And import.

31
00:01:48,210 --> 00:01:52,830
The report entity from up one directory reports report dot entity.

32
00:01:55,590 --> 00:01:55,920
Okay.

33
00:01:55,920 --> 00:01:59,010
So after adding in that decorator, we're then getting a little TypeScript error here.

34
00:01:59,010 --> 00:02:03,300
So we have to provide some different arguments to it to customize how this decorator behaves.

35
00:02:03,570 --> 00:02:07,770
Right now I'm going to add these arguments in and we'll come back in just a moment and describe exactly

36
00:02:07,770 --> 00:02:09,470
what the arguments do for us.

37
00:02:09,480 --> 00:02:13,080
So for right now, these arguments are going to look a little bit mysterious, but I've got good news

38
00:02:13,080 --> 00:02:13,490
for you.

39
00:02:13,500 --> 00:02:17,670
You really just need to memorize these arguments one time and then just pretty much repeat them whenever

40
00:02:17,670 --> 00:02:19,500
you want to build an association.

41
00:02:19,520 --> 00:02:25,350
So we're going to write in here a function that returns the report entity class.

42
00:02:26,080 --> 00:02:31,270
Then as the second argument, we're going to put in a function that takes an instance of a report.

43
00:02:31,270 --> 00:02:37,150
So I'm going to put that down with the lowercase R, and then we're going to return report dot user.

44
00:02:38,200 --> 00:02:42,310
We're going to get an air from that for right now, but that air is going to go away just a moment once

45
00:02:42,310 --> 00:02:44,170
we update our report entity.

46
00:02:45,400 --> 00:02:47,880
Okay, so that's it inside this file for right now.

47
00:02:47,890 --> 00:02:50,810
Like I said, we're going to go over these arguments in great detail.

48
00:02:50,830 --> 00:02:54,610
I'm also going to explain what this additional property is all about in just a little bit.

49
00:02:55,000 --> 00:03:00,550
Right now, let's go back over to our Report Entity file and we're going to set up the many to one decorator

50
00:03:00,550 --> 00:03:01,180
in there.

51
00:03:02,270 --> 00:03:04,970
So inside of our report entity file.

52
00:03:06,270 --> 00:03:09,060
I'm going to find the import statement for type ORM.

53
00:03:11,100 --> 00:03:14,250
And we are going to import the exact opposite decorator.

54
00:03:14,250 --> 00:03:19,650
So in this case we need many to one because many reports have one user.

55
00:03:20,390 --> 00:03:22,970
So I'm going to import many to one.

56
00:03:23,300 --> 00:03:26,390
I'm also going to import the user entity.

57
00:03:28,580 --> 00:03:29,360
As well.

58
00:03:32,220 --> 00:03:37,860
Then at the bottom of our class and go down to the very bottom and we're going to make use of this decorator

59
00:03:37,860 --> 00:03:41,290
just about identical to how we used it inside of our user entity.

60
00:03:41,310 --> 00:03:43,860
So we'll say many to one.

61
00:03:45,560 --> 00:03:48,800
I'm going to put in user is going to be an instance of a user.

62
00:03:48,800 --> 00:03:52,580
And you'll notice that in this case there is no array on here, just one single user.

63
00:03:52,880 --> 00:03:56,120
We'll then put in some very similar looking arguments to the decorator.

64
00:03:56,120 --> 00:03:59,810
So first a function that returns the user entity class.

65
00:04:00,050 --> 00:04:05,300
Then another function that takes an instance of a user excuse me, an instance of a report.

66
00:04:07,190 --> 00:04:08,090
No instance of a user.

67
00:04:08,090 --> 00:04:08,600
My mistake.

68
00:04:08,600 --> 00:04:08,900
Sorry.

69
00:04:08,900 --> 00:04:11,330
I'm reading my notes here, got my notes reversed.

70
00:04:11,360 --> 00:04:13,400
We're going to say user dot reports.

71
00:04:13,400 --> 00:04:16,940
So the exact opposite of what we had inside the other entity file.

72
00:04:18,010 --> 00:04:20,260
I apologize for that little hesitation there.

73
00:04:20,710 --> 00:04:21,580
Okay, So that's it.

74
00:04:21,610 --> 00:04:23,500
We have now set up this relationship.

75
00:04:23,500 --> 00:04:27,310
We've told type that these two entities are going to be related.

76
00:04:27,340 --> 00:04:33,250
Now, as soon as we add these decorators in, one extremely important change happens behind the scenes

77
00:04:33,280 --> 00:04:40,330
type, ORM is going to notice that we made use of this many to one decorator inside of our report entity.

78
00:04:41,410 --> 00:04:44,920
The many to one decorator is going to cause a change inside of our database.

79
00:04:44,950 --> 00:04:48,820
The opposite decorator, one to many does not cause a change.

80
00:04:49,120 --> 00:04:50,800
So this decorator right here.

81
00:04:50,830 --> 00:04:52,180
No, No change.

82
00:04:52,180 --> 00:04:53,440
Cause to our database.

83
00:04:53,740 --> 00:04:56,320
Many to one cause is changed.

84
00:04:56,500 --> 00:04:58,540
The change that is going to be made to our database.

85
00:04:58,570 --> 00:05:00,580
Is that on the report table?

86
00:05:00,580 --> 00:05:06,280
So our listing of all of our different reports type is going to add in a new column to it automatically,

87
00:05:06,280 --> 00:05:08,890
and that column is going to be the one that we need.

88
00:05:08,920 --> 00:05:13,840
It's going to be a new column that's going to record the user ID or the ID of every user.

89
00:05:14,800 --> 00:05:20,950
So by just adding in that decorator, specifically the many to one decorator, a change has been made

90
00:05:20,950 --> 00:05:26,560
to our database and that change is what's going to allow us to associate a report with a user.

91
00:05:27,780 --> 00:05:30,480
Okay, so now we've got at least a little bit more setup.

92
00:05:30,480 --> 00:05:32,490
We're going to take another quick pause right here.

93
00:05:32,520 --> 00:05:36,120
We're going to come back in just a moment, understand a little bit more about these decorators and

94
00:05:36,120 --> 00:05:42,120
what they're doing for us and really understand what the reports property on a user and what the user

95
00:05:42,120 --> 00:05:45,180
property on a report are doing for us.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,240 --> 00:00:02,590
We've got our decorators hooked up.

2
00:00:02,590 --> 00:00:06,940
So let's now understand what the arguments to those decorators are and what the decorators themselves

3
00:00:06,940 --> 00:00:08,230
are really doing for us.

4
00:00:08,590 --> 00:00:08,890
Okay.

5
00:00:08,890 --> 00:00:10,080
So quick diagram here.

6
00:00:10,090 --> 00:00:13,770
So at the top we've got our class user and then down here class report.

7
00:00:13,780 --> 00:00:20,410
So on the user we made use of the one to many decorator by adding in that decorator alone no change

8
00:00:20,410 --> 00:00:22,060
has been made to the users table.

9
00:00:22,150 --> 00:00:27,010
However, on class report down here where we made use of many to one, a change has been made to the

10
00:00:27,010 --> 00:00:27,870
reports table.

11
00:00:27,880 --> 00:00:31,300
As I mentioned at the end of the last video by making use of many to one.

12
00:00:31,330 --> 00:00:35,080
That causes a change to be made to the reports table.

13
00:00:35,080 --> 00:00:39,410
We're now going to have that user ID column added to it automatically by type ORM.

14
00:00:42,280 --> 00:00:43,570
Right after we apply the decorator.

15
00:00:43,570 --> 00:00:46,360
In both case, we then list out a property on the user.

16
00:00:46,360 --> 00:00:50,680
We called it reports and we said that that was going to be of type array of report.

17
00:00:50,800 --> 00:00:55,750
So now at some point in time in the future, if we ever get an instance of a user, so if we fetch a

18
00:00:55,750 --> 00:01:00,520
user out of the database or something like that, we can get an access to all the different reports

19
00:01:00,520 --> 00:01:04,600
tied to this user by referring to user dot reports.

20
00:01:04,600 --> 00:01:09,790
That will be an array of all the different reports that is tied or associated to this user.

21
00:01:10,360 --> 00:01:15,580
One very important thing to note is that that association, so that array of all those reports is not

22
00:01:15,580 --> 00:01:18,770
going to be fetched automatically whenever you fetch a user.

23
00:01:18,790 --> 00:01:23,440
So right now, if we went into our database and we set up an association between a user and a couple

24
00:01:23,440 --> 00:01:29,860
of different reports and then fetch that user alone, that query by itself by default is not going to

25
00:01:29,860 --> 00:01:32,320
fetch all the associated reports.

26
00:01:32,910 --> 00:01:38,070
We have to add in some additional options to our query when we fetch that user to also get these associated

27
00:01:38,070 --> 00:01:38,870
records.

28
00:01:38,940 --> 00:01:43,740
So if you start to add in your own code to fetch a user and find all the reports associated with them,

29
00:01:43,740 --> 00:01:46,230
don't be surprised if you get back an empty array.

30
00:01:46,260 --> 00:01:51,900
We will of course understand in great detail how to fetch a user along with all their associated records.

31
00:01:53,830 --> 00:01:54,760
On class report.

32
00:01:54,760 --> 00:01:55,990
The same thing is true.

33
00:01:56,020 --> 00:01:59,110
So on report we now have a user property.

34
00:01:59,350 --> 00:02:04,000
If we ever want to figure out who created a report or who is associated with a report, we're going

35
00:02:04,000 --> 00:02:07,390
to access something like report dot user.

36
00:02:08,240 --> 00:02:14,840
A very similar to what we just said about users that property of user is not going to be automatically

37
00:02:14,840 --> 00:02:15,480
fetched.

38
00:02:15,500 --> 00:02:22,250
So if you go into your database and attempt to pull out a report using typo by default, the user is

39
00:02:22,250 --> 00:02:23,750
not going to be loaded up with it.

40
00:02:23,780 --> 00:02:29,090
We have to make a change to our query and we have to specifically say fetch report and the user associated

41
00:02:29,090 --> 00:02:29,750
with it.

42
00:02:31,060 --> 00:02:31,390
Okay.

43
00:02:31,390 --> 00:02:36,240
So now I want to tell you a little bit more around the arguments to these two decorators.

44
00:02:36,250 --> 00:02:41,530
So we're going to first begin inside of our user entity where we made use of one to many.

45
00:02:42,270 --> 00:02:43,920
So we put in a function.

46
00:02:44,730 --> 00:02:47,940
Let's returning our report entity class.

47
00:02:49,070 --> 00:02:52,860
Now, the really confusing part here is the fact that we're wrapping this up with a function.

48
00:02:52,880 --> 00:02:57,200
The fact that we're returning the entity class might not be too strange because, as you might guess,

49
00:02:57,200 --> 00:03:02,840
at some point in time, we have to tell type what kind of record our user is going to be associated

50
00:03:02,840 --> 00:03:03,230
with.

51
00:03:03,260 --> 00:03:05,450
So that's really the goal of the first argument.

52
00:03:05,480 --> 00:03:09,920
We're saying our user is going to be associated with something of type report.

53
00:03:11,030 --> 00:03:14,540
The part that is strange is the fact that it's wrapped up inside of a function.

54
00:03:14,540 --> 00:03:18,150
And we did the same thing over inside of our report entity as well.

55
00:03:18,170 --> 00:03:23,360
That first argument is returning a user entity class wrapped up inside of a function.

56
00:03:23,720 --> 00:03:29,720
So the function is only there due to a little issue that's very common in the world of TypeScript and

57
00:03:29,720 --> 00:03:31,190
many other languages.

58
00:03:31,280 --> 00:03:35,960
Let me show you exactly with a very quick demonstration why we are wrapping that thing up with a function

59
00:03:35,960 --> 00:03:39,440
at the top of class report or my report entity file.

60
00:03:39,560 --> 00:03:42,050
I'm going to add in a console log of user.

61
00:03:43,090 --> 00:03:48,670
Then over inside my user entity file, I'm going to add in a console log of report.

62
00:03:49,500 --> 00:03:55,680
So want you to just notice that inside the user entity file we are importing report and inside of the

63
00:03:55,680 --> 00:03:58,710
report entity file, we are importing user.

64
00:03:58,800 --> 00:04:03,060
That means we have a circular dependency between these two different files.

65
00:04:03,240 --> 00:04:07,080
So now let's save these two files and see what happens with these console logs.

66
00:04:08,880 --> 00:04:13,260
So back over at my terminal, I'm going to scroll up a little bit and I will find to console logs right

67
00:04:13,260 --> 00:04:13,560
here.

68
00:04:13,590 --> 00:04:17,270
The first one says undefined and the second says class report.

69
00:04:17,280 --> 00:04:18,570
So undefined right there.

70
00:04:18,600 --> 00:04:24,090
That is my console log of class user that I put together inside the report entity file.

71
00:04:24,180 --> 00:04:28,620
So in my code executed this console log right here reported undefined.

72
00:04:28,620 --> 00:04:31,600
That is the result of having a circular dependency.

73
00:04:31,620 --> 00:04:37,490
So this means that my report entity file is being executed first inside my project.

74
00:04:37,500 --> 00:04:39,330
All the code inside here is loaded up.

75
00:04:39,330 --> 00:04:40,830
It all gets executed.

76
00:04:40,830 --> 00:04:45,090
And at that point in time, the user entity file has not been executed.

77
00:04:45,120 --> 00:04:50,520
That means that the user entity class is undefined just because the code inside that file has not been

78
00:04:50,520 --> 00:04:51,330
executed.

79
00:04:51,980 --> 00:04:57,470
So thanks to the circular dependency, we can't directly make reference to the user entity inside of

80
00:04:57,470 --> 00:04:59,270
our report entity file.

81
00:04:59,570 --> 00:05:01,340
I'm going to delete that console.log, by the way.

82
00:05:01,340 --> 00:05:05,150
And the same is true inside of our report entity or our user entity file.

83
00:05:05,150 --> 00:05:07,430
We can't directly reference the report.

84
00:05:08,430 --> 00:05:15,270
To get around this issue, we wrap the entity inside of a function and that's going to solve or fix

85
00:05:15,270 --> 00:05:19,110
up this problem of the report or the user not yet being defined.

86
00:05:19,650 --> 00:05:24,960
Once both these classes or once both these files get executed only after that, at some point in time

87
00:05:24,960 --> 00:05:29,010
in the future when our code is actually running and we need to actually figure out what is meant by

88
00:05:29,010 --> 00:05:29,910
this relationship.

89
00:05:29,910 --> 00:05:31,590
Does this function get executed?

90
00:05:31,590 --> 00:05:37,300
And at that point in time, in the future, report and user in the other file will actually be defined.

91
00:05:37,320 --> 00:05:41,190
So again, the function is just here to solve this circular dependency issue.

92
00:05:42,310 --> 00:05:45,430
Okay, so now what's going on with the second argument?

93
00:05:45,610 --> 00:05:50,860
So with the second argument, I got to be honest with you, this is a very specialized thing right here.

94
00:05:50,860 --> 00:05:53,200
It's not really something you need to worry about too much.

95
00:05:53,230 --> 00:05:59,980
The use case of the second argument is very, very particular comes down to how type internally models,

96
00:05:59,980 --> 00:06:04,570
relationships between different entities and does validation of how all these relationships are set

97
00:06:04,570 --> 00:06:05,050
up.

98
00:06:05,320 --> 00:06:10,630
I will give you a very, very fast and quick example of where this second argument starts to be really

99
00:06:10,630 --> 00:06:11,290
critical.

100
00:06:13,280 --> 00:06:15,130
Okay, So very fast example here.

101
00:06:15,140 --> 00:06:20,150
Let's say that at some point in time in the future, remember we want to allow a user to approve a report

102
00:06:20,150 --> 00:06:25,190
or really an administrator to approve a report before it's ever used inside of any car estimates.

103
00:06:25,190 --> 00:06:31,940
So we might want to eventually record which user approved a report versus which user created a report.

104
00:06:31,940 --> 00:06:36,740
So the person who created a report might be referred to as the owner and the other user who approved

105
00:06:36,740 --> 00:06:39,020
it might be referred to as the approver.

106
00:06:39,690 --> 00:06:42,660
So this is where that second argument starts to become more necessary.

107
00:06:42,660 --> 00:06:47,120
It's when we've got two different entities that are related in more than one way.

108
00:06:47,130 --> 00:06:51,770
So that's when you start want to wanting to research what that second argument is actually used for.

109
00:06:51,780 --> 00:06:55,050
But in our case, we're not going to worry about dividing up a relationship like this.

110
00:06:55,050 --> 00:06:58,050
So for you and I, we're not going to worry about that second argument too much.

111
00:06:58,080 --> 00:07:01,740
All you need to understand about the second argument is that it's going to be a function that's going

112
00:07:01,740 --> 00:07:05,910
to take an instance of the entity that you're trying to relate to.

113
00:07:05,940 --> 00:07:12,090
So in the user file, it would take an instance of a report and then we're going to return how to go

114
00:07:12,090 --> 00:07:18,120
from that target entity instance back to the entity that we are currently defining, which is in our

115
00:07:18,120 --> 00:07:21,540
case right now, the user, because I'm in the user file.

116
00:07:22,140 --> 00:07:25,400
Naturally inside of our report entity, it's the opposite.

117
00:07:25,410 --> 00:07:30,780
So inside the report entity, we go from a user back to a list of reports.

118
00:07:31,980 --> 00:07:32,490
Okay.

119
00:07:33,150 --> 00:07:33,390
All right.

120
00:07:33,390 --> 00:07:37,050
So that's enough for right now on what these decorators mean.

121
00:07:37,050 --> 00:07:41,790
And we've also got a little bit of information on what doing or setting up this relationship has now

122
00:07:41,790 --> 00:07:42,900
enabled us to do.

123
00:07:42,930 --> 00:07:48,450
If we now make a query that is shaped or formed in the correct way to make sure that we do in fact load

124
00:07:48,450 --> 00:07:54,060
up associated records, then if we fetch an instance of a user with that special query, the user will

125
00:07:54,060 --> 00:07:58,500
have a reports property and that will be an array of all the reports tied to this user.

126
00:07:58,620 --> 00:08:00,180
Likewise for reports.

127
00:08:00,180 --> 00:08:05,580
If we try to fetch a report and we shape the query in a very specific way, that's also going to fetch

128
00:08:05,580 --> 00:08:09,510
the user that created this report and set them on the user property.

129
00:08:10,560 --> 00:08:10,830
All right.

130
00:08:10,830 --> 00:08:14,130
So now that we've got this far, let's take a pause right here, Come back in the next video, and we're

131
00:08:14,130 --> 00:08:17,070
going to move on to the next step of our flow.

132
00:08:17,980 --> 00:08:23,050
Which is where we are going to start to associate our records together whenever one is created.

133
00:08:23,050 --> 00:08:25,930
So in this case, we're going to associate a user with a report.

134
00:08:25,960 --> 00:08:27,790
When a report is created.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,420 --> 00:00:02,990
Let's move on to step number three.

2
00:00:03,010 --> 00:00:05,940
So this is where we are going to associate our two records together.

3
00:00:05,950 --> 00:00:10,150
We need to make sure that whenever we create a new report, we associate it with a user who is making

4
00:00:10,150 --> 00:00:11,090
a request to us.

5
00:00:11,110 --> 00:00:13,060
So let me walk you through this process.

6
00:00:13,850 --> 00:00:14,390
Okay.

7
00:00:15,070 --> 00:00:18,100
So you and I are going to receive a post request to slash reports.

8
00:00:18,100 --> 00:00:22,030
We've already got a route handler for this route put together inside that route handler.

9
00:00:22,030 --> 00:00:26,530
We are currently receiving our body and that is being validated by the Create report.

10
00:00:26,530 --> 00:00:31,600
DTO So we need to take that DTO right there, which we're going to use to create a new report and we

11
00:00:31,600 --> 00:00:35,110
need to associate it with some user to get our current user.

12
00:00:35,110 --> 00:00:37,780
We're going to make use of the current user decorator.

13
00:00:37,780 --> 00:00:41,680
So that will extract some information from the cookie off that incoming request.

14
00:00:41,980 --> 00:00:47,320
So inside of our request handler, we are going to eventually have our user entity instance and the

15
00:00:47,320 --> 00:00:49,690
validated create report DTO.

16
00:00:50,630 --> 00:00:52,160
So now we've got these two things.

17
00:00:52,160 --> 00:00:58,150
We're going to take that report, DTO, and turn it into a report entity instance.

18
00:00:58,160 --> 00:01:01,610
So this is our report entity instance.

19
00:01:02,530 --> 00:01:06,910
So all these properties are going to be copied over to this report entity instance such as longitude,

20
00:01:06,910 --> 00:01:08,200
latitude and so on.

21
00:01:08,380 --> 00:01:14,590
And then to associate our user with this report that we're about to create, we're going to assign the

22
00:01:14,590 --> 00:01:19,570
entire user entity instance to the user property of our report.

23
00:01:19,750 --> 00:01:24,250
So we're going to take this entire entity instance and assign it to that user property.

24
00:01:25,640 --> 00:01:30,740
Once we've created this report entity instance, we're then going to pass it off to the save method

25
00:01:30,740 --> 00:01:32,490
of our reports repository.

26
00:01:32,510 --> 00:01:37,310
And then behind the scenes, the reports repository is going to take a look at that user property.

27
00:01:37,310 --> 00:01:40,640
It's going to see that we assigned to it some user entity.

28
00:01:40,640 --> 00:01:46,130
So it might be some object with an ID and an email and whatever other properties.

29
00:01:47,840 --> 00:01:54,680
Behind the scenes, the repository is going to extract just the ID of that entire entity and it's going

30
00:01:54,680 --> 00:01:59,360
to save just the ID as the user field inside of our database.

31
00:01:59,510 --> 00:02:02,090
So you might recall that we had said a little bit ago.

32
00:02:02,850 --> 00:02:06,510
But eventually we want to save the user ID on our reports table.

33
00:02:06,600 --> 00:02:09,570
So the repository is going to take care of that for us.

34
00:02:09,600 --> 00:02:14,940
It's going to extract the idea of the user entity and save just the ID inside the reports table.

35
00:02:14,970 --> 00:02:20,520
All you and I have to do is make sure that we assign our chosen user entity so the user we want to associate

36
00:02:20,520 --> 00:02:22,630
with this report to that user property.

37
00:02:22,650 --> 00:02:23,460
That's it.

38
00:02:23,490 --> 00:02:28,680
We just need to make sure that we put that user entity instance right there before we hand this entire

39
00:02:28,680 --> 00:02:30,510
thing off to our save method.

40
00:02:31,520 --> 00:02:33,350
Okay, so now we've got the general idea.

41
00:02:33,380 --> 00:02:35,060
Let's go back over to our editor.

42
00:02:35,090 --> 00:02:37,580
We're going to first begin by opening up our controller.

43
00:02:37,610 --> 00:02:42,950
We're going to make sure that whenever we receive a request to post slash reports, we not only get

44
00:02:42,950 --> 00:02:47,870
the body, but we also get our current user as well through the use of the current user decorator.

45
00:02:48,680 --> 00:02:52,370
So back inside my editor, I'm going to find my reports controller.

46
00:02:54,940 --> 00:02:55,600
At the top.

47
00:02:55,600 --> 00:02:58,540
I'm going to import current user.

48
00:02:59,250 --> 00:02:59,760
From.

49
00:02:59,760 --> 00:03:05,340
And you'll recall we put together our current user decorator inside the Users Decorators folder.

50
00:03:05,640 --> 00:03:12,510
So we're going to go up one directory into users decorators, current user dot decorator.

51
00:03:12,540 --> 00:03:13,980
Let me show you that entire line.

52
00:03:15,060 --> 00:03:16,200
Because it's pretty long.

53
00:03:19,900 --> 00:03:20,260
Okay.

54
00:03:20,260 --> 00:03:26,890
So then on our Create report route handler right there, I'm going to make use of the current user decorator.

55
00:03:27,310 --> 00:03:30,670
I can receive this argument as a variable that I will call user.

56
00:03:30,670 --> 00:03:32,920
And then we need to annotate its type as well.

57
00:03:32,920 --> 00:03:36,010
So for that we need to also import our user entity.

58
00:03:36,190 --> 00:03:38,800
So at the top we'll add in another import.

59
00:03:39,970 --> 00:03:41,680
Or user from.

60
00:03:42,850 --> 00:03:43,870
Users.

61
00:03:44,110 --> 00:03:45,760
User entity.

62
00:03:47,500 --> 00:03:47,860
All right.

63
00:03:47,860 --> 00:03:48,550
Very good.

64
00:03:49,720 --> 00:03:52,090
So I'm going to add in that type annotation.

65
00:03:52,310 --> 00:03:53,880
Oh, not body, but user.

66
00:03:53,890 --> 00:03:54,610
There we go.

67
00:03:56,260 --> 00:04:00,310
So now inside of our route handler, we've got both a body and our user.

68
00:04:01,170 --> 00:04:03,150
So now we need to take both these things.

69
00:04:03,180 --> 00:04:09,720
We need to turn that into an actual report entity instance and then associate the user with it.

70
00:04:09,840 --> 00:04:15,300
So for that, I'm going to decide to do all that association directly inside of our report service,

71
00:04:15,300 --> 00:04:16,210
Create method.

72
00:04:16,230 --> 00:04:21,540
So I'm going to say that I'm going to pass off to create not only this entire DTO, but as a second

73
00:04:21,540 --> 00:04:21,960
argument.

74
00:04:21,960 --> 00:04:26,910
I'm also going to pass off the user that I want to associate with this newly created report.

75
00:04:29,580 --> 00:04:31,290
Now we'll go over to our service.

76
00:04:32,910 --> 00:04:34,440
I'm going to find the create method.

77
00:04:34,620 --> 00:04:35,760
Here it is right here.

78
00:04:36,520 --> 00:04:40,330
And I'm going to also receive that user as the second argument.

79
00:04:44,220 --> 00:04:45,810
I'm going to make sure I import user.

80
00:04:49,970 --> 00:04:53,930
And now it's time for us right after we create our report entity instance.

81
00:04:53,930 --> 00:04:55,630
So here's the instance right here.

82
00:04:55,640 --> 00:05:00,440
We're going to assign the user entity to that user property, and that's what's going to set up this

83
00:05:00,440 --> 00:05:01,850
entire association.

84
00:05:01,940 --> 00:05:07,340
So we'll say right after we create the report entity instance report user is user.

85
00:05:07,950 --> 00:05:08,850
And that's it.

86
00:05:09,360 --> 00:05:11,640
So that's how we set up our association.

87
00:05:11,790 --> 00:05:17,760
When we now call Save on the report, behind the scenes, the repository is going to extract just the

88
00:05:17,760 --> 00:05:23,730
user ID from the entire user entity instance, and it's going to save that ID for us automatically inside

89
00:05:23,730 --> 00:05:24,810
the reports table.

90
00:05:25,690 --> 00:05:28,360
So we should be able to now test out all this code.

91
00:05:28,510 --> 00:05:30,640
So I'm going to save all these different files.

92
00:05:31,770 --> 00:05:36,170
I'm then going to test this out by first just making sure that I'm signed in to the application.

93
00:05:36,180 --> 00:05:40,860
So I'm going to go back to my client around our authentication stuff and I'm going to make sure that

94
00:05:40,860 --> 00:05:43,200
I sign in as an existing user.

95
00:05:44,000 --> 00:05:48,710
Once I'm signed in, I'll then pull up my client for reports.

96
00:05:50,330 --> 00:05:56,660
And I'm going to try to make a request to create a brand new report with whatever information.

97
00:05:56,660 --> 00:05:57,920
So I'll send that off.

98
00:05:58,220 --> 00:06:03,380
And now inside the response I get back, I'm going to see our price, blah, blah, blah, all that

99
00:06:03,380 --> 00:06:04,280
different stuff.

100
00:06:04,280 --> 00:06:06,290
And then a user property as well.

101
00:06:07,230 --> 00:06:11,610
So this is the user who is associated or created this report.

102
00:06:11,610 --> 00:06:14,860
So we've now got all the information about them tied to this report.

103
00:06:14,880 --> 00:06:15,840
Perfect.

104
00:06:16,520 --> 00:06:22,760
Now, the one downside here is you will notice we are unfortunately including the entire password property

105
00:06:22,760 --> 00:06:26,030
in there, which is almost definitely not what we want.

106
00:06:26,240 --> 00:06:28,190
So let's take a pause right here in the next video.

107
00:06:28,190 --> 00:06:28,820
We're going to come back.

108
00:06:28,870 --> 00:06:34,070
We're going to discuss a couple of issues around the setup we currently have, and we'll also make sure

109
00:06:34,070 --> 00:06:36,800
that we do not show that entire password property.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,290 --> 00:00:03,060
We are now on to step number four.

2
00:00:03,060 --> 00:00:07,260
So we are creating a report and we are associating it with a user, but we are currently sending back

3
00:00:07,260 --> 00:00:08,700
way too much information.

4
00:00:08,700 --> 00:00:12,510
So we are going to apply a serializer to limit the amount of information shared.

5
00:00:13,020 --> 00:00:16,950
So inside my editor, here's the response we get back from creating a brand new report.

6
00:00:16,980 --> 00:00:20,220
Here's the user property and again, we're sending back the entire password.

7
00:00:20,220 --> 00:00:23,370
So we don't want to send that entire password back, obviously.

8
00:00:24,150 --> 00:00:28,260
In order to limit the amount of information we send back, we're going to reuse something we created

9
00:00:28,260 --> 00:00:29,760
earlier on inside the course.

10
00:00:30,060 --> 00:00:36,240
Our interceptor called the serialize interceptor, so that's inside of our interceptors directory.

11
00:00:36,270 --> 00:00:40,920
You might recall that along with the interceptor, we also created a decorator to make use of it very

12
00:00:40,920 --> 00:00:41,890
easily as well.

13
00:00:41,910 --> 00:00:47,640
So we are going to create a new DTO that is going to describe how we want to format that outgoing response

14
00:00:47,640 --> 00:00:49,080
with a report inside of it.

15
00:00:49,080 --> 00:00:53,310
And we're going to specifically say that we do not want to include the user's password.

16
00:00:54,020 --> 00:00:57,170
But before we do that, I want to have a very quick side discussion.

17
00:00:57,410 --> 00:01:00,800
I'm going to take another look at the response that we're sending back whenever we create a brand new

18
00:01:00,800 --> 00:01:01,550
report.

19
00:01:01,880 --> 00:01:06,590
I'm going to copy it into a file, Empty one just really quickly so I can edit this thing very easily.

20
00:01:07,840 --> 00:01:10,960
Okay, So here is our response from creating a brand new report.

21
00:01:10,990 --> 00:01:14,690
Now I want to show you something that a lot of API's choose to do.

22
00:01:14,710 --> 00:01:19,120
This is not strictly required, but it's a pattern that you're going to see very, very frequently.

23
00:01:19,420 --> 00:01:24,640
So we have a report right here, and we've got an association with some user ID.

24
00:01:24,640 --> 00:01:29,100
In this case, we have decided to embed the entire user inside the response.

25
00:01:29,110 --> 00:01:33,520
So we have a user property and lo and behold, there's the entire user object.

26
00:01:33,820 --> 00:01:38,680
Presumably if this user had some additional properties associated with it, such as a first name, last

27
00:01:38,680 --> 00:01:44,440
name, favorite color, age name, whatever else, those would also be present in here.

28
00:01:44,650 --> 00:01:51,160
That's a lot of information to include around a request that was really intended to just create a report.

29
00:01:52,300 --> 00:01:57,340
So a pattern that you're going to see in a lot of different APIs is to say whenever we have an association

30
00:01:57,340 --> 00:02:03,730
rather than embedding the entire association, instead put in just a property called something like

31
00:02:03,730 --> 00:02:10,330
user ID and have that be the ID of the record that this report is associated with.

32
00:02:11,020 --> 00:02:12,700
By just putting in the user ID right there.

33
00:02:12,700 --> 00:02:18,190
We now give the person who is making use of our API the option to get more information about this user

34
00:02:18,190 --> 00:02:19,250
if they want.

35
00:02:19,270 --> 00:02:24,460
For example, someone making a request to make this report could decide to make a follow up request

36
00:02:24,460 --> 00:02:28,150
to get some information about the user with ID of one.

37
00:02:28,330 --> 00:02:33,820
So this is a pattern that you and I are going to use inside of our project, rather than always embedding

38
00:02:34,060 --> 00:02:37,180
the entire associated record inside of a response.

39
00:02:37,210 --> 00:02:40,170
We're just going to have kind of a pointer of sorts.

40
00:02:40,180 --> 00:02:46,330
We're going to say, if you have any interest in this associated record, then make a follow up request

41
00:02:46,330 --> 00:02:50,080
to its own dedicated endpoint and try to get some information about this user.

42
00:02:51,130 --> 00:02:55,090
So this is going to make our just a little bit easier to manage because we're not going to have to worry

43
00:02:55,090 --> 00:03:00,610
about always fetching a ton of associated data for every single request or every single record a user

44
00:03:00,610 --> 00:03:01,810
ever tries to fetch.

45
00:03:02,600 --> 00:03:06,170
So in total, what I'm trying to say here is we're going to build out a serializer.

46
00:03:06,680 --> 00:03:09,080
We're going to write out a custom DTO for it.

47
00:03:09,680 --> 00:03:15,980
And rather than just trying to subtract or remove that password property, we're going to instead add

48
00:03:15,980 --> 00:03:21,800
a new property called user ID, and it's going to be whatever the ID of the user is.

49
00:03:22,100 --> 00:03:26,340
We're then going to make sure we delete that entire original user property.

50
00:03:26,360 --> 00:03:27,170
So that's the goal.

51
00:03:27,170 --> 00:03:28,130
That's what we're going to do.

52
00:03:29,140 --> 00:03:29,440
Okay.

53
00:03:29,440 --> 00:03:34,660
So with all that in mind, let's take a quick pause right here and then get started working on this

54
00:03:34,660 --> 00:03:38,350
new DTO to format the outgoing report in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:05,920
All right, my friends, in this video, we're going to make a new DTO that is going to format the outgoing

2
00:00:05,950 --> 00:00:09,350
response that has the report and the user inside of it.

3
00:00:09,370 --> 00:00:15,970
So inside of my reports DTO directory, I'm going to make a new file called report DTO dot ts.

4
00:00:16,700 --> 00:00:23,300
Then inside of here, we're going to create a very similar to the user we had created previously.

5
00:00:23,480 --> 00:00:27,920
So we are going to export a class called Report DTO.

6
00:00:29,160 --> 00:00:31,290
I'm going to import two directives or two.

7
00:00:31,290 --> 00:00:31,620
Excuse me.

8
00:00:31,620 --> 00:00:32,490
Decorators.

9
00:00:33,890 --> 00:00:39,710
Called expose, and we're going to make use of a new one called Transform as well from class transformer.

10
00:00:40,840 --> 00:00:44,680
I'm then going to also add in an import statement for our user entity as well.

11
00:00:48,450 --> 00:00:53,250
So inside of our outgoing response, whenever we create a brand new report, we're going to include

12
00:00:53,250 --> 00:00:56,080
all the different properties that a report normally has.

13
00:00:56,100 --> 00:00:58,470
So let's just write them all out immediately.

14
00:00:58,500 --> 00:01:03,510
So we'll have an ID that's going to be a number price year.

15
00:01:04,950 --> 00:01:05,850
Longitude.

16
00:01:06,590 --> 00:01:07,580
Latitude.

17
00:01:08,460 --> 00:01:09,150
A make.

18
00:01:09,860 --> 00:01:10,640
A model.

19
00:01:12,520 --> 00:01:14,170
And a mileage.

20
00:01:16,860 --> 00:01:20,880
We're then going to annotate each of these with the expose decorator to make sure that they explicitly

21
00:01:20,880 --> 00:01:22,740
get shared in the outgoing response.

22
00:01:22,860 --> 00:01:24,390
So we'll add an expose right here.

23
00:01:24,390 --> 00:01:28,200
And I'm just going to copy paste that down for all these different properties.

24
00:01:36,090 --> 00:01:38,070
Now, let's save the file just right here.

25
00:01:38,070 --> 00:01:42,990
We're going to try to test out a response or making a brand new report and just make sure that the outgoing

26
00:01:43,020 --> 00:01:45,570
response has only these properties listed.

27
00:01:45,600 --> 00:01:49,560
Well, then worry about actually making sure that we get that user ID inside there as well.

28
00:01:49,680 --> 00:01:55,200
So to make use of this report, DTO, we'll go back over to our reports controller.

29
00:01:57,130 --> 00:01:57,730
At the very top.

30
00:01:57,730 --> 00:01:59,330
I'm going to import that DTO.

31
00:02:06,100 --> 00:02:09,520
And the serialized decorator that we put together as well.

32
00:02:16,940 --> 00:02:20,780
I'm then going to apply that decorator to our Create report route handler.

33
00:02:22,230 --> 00:02:27,480
And say that I want to serialize the outgoing response following the rules we set up inside of the report.

34
00:02:27,480 --> 00:02:28,230
DTO.

35
00:02:29,530 --> 00:02:30,850
And that should be it.

36
00:02:30,880 --> 00:02:36,100
So I'm going to save this, open up my client again and try to create another report.

37
00:02:37,530 --> 00:02:38,540
Okay, so that looks good.

38
00:02:38,550 --> 00:02:41,430
We are no longer sharing that entire user property.

39
00:02:41,670 --> 00:02:46,800
So now we're going to make sure that we do just pluck off the user ID property by itself and include

40
00:02:46,800 --> 00:02:48,480
it on the outgoing response.

41
00:02:49,910 --> 00:02:51,920
So to do so, I'm going to go back over to my report.

42
00:02:52,610 --> 00:02:56,540
And at the very bottom, I'm going to add in another property that we're going to send in the outgoing

43
00:02:56,540 --> 00:02:57,320
response.

44
00:02:57,350 --> 00:03:02,330
We're going to call this user ID, It's going to be a number, and I will immediately mark it with the

45
00:03:02,330 --> 00:03:03,560
exposed directive.

46
00:03:03,680 --> 00:03:05,870
So let me help you understand what's going on here.

47
00:03:05,900 --> 00:03:12,470
Remember the entire purpose of the report, DTO is to take a report entity instance and convert it into

48
00:03:12,470 --> 00:03:15,080
an object that has all these different properties.

49
00:03:15,620 --> 00:03:20,690
During that conversion, we can choose to obviously exclude certain properties and we just do that by

50
00:03:20,690 --> 00:03:22,940
not marking those properties with the expose directive.

51
00:03:22,970 --> 00:03:28,820
But we can also add in brand new properties as well to generate a new property that is going to pull

52
00:03:28,820 --> 00:03:31,460
some information from the original report entity.

53
00:03:31,490 --> 00:03:34,430
We're going to use the transform decorator.

54
00:03:35,370 --> 00:03:38,610
The transform decorator is going to be called with a function.

55
00:03:39,000 --> 00:03:41,590
We're going to destructure off the argument list.

56
00:03:41,610 --> 00:03:42,990
So I'm going to put in curly braces.

57
00:03:43,020 --> 00:03:44,850
We're going to pull out something called obj.

58
00:03:45,810 --> 00:03:49,660
OBJ is a reference to the original report entity.

59
00:03:49,680 --> 00:03:51,660
So we're going to pull in that original report.

60
00:03:51,670 --> 00:03:52,020
Entity.

61
00:03:52,030 --> 00:03:52,830
Entity.

62
00:03:53,740 --> 00:03:56,130
We're going to take a look at its user property.

63
00:03:56,140 --> 00:03:58,660
And from the user, we're going to pull out the ID.

64
00:04:00,520 --> 00:04:06,370
So this entire line right here says, take the original report entity that we're currently trying to

65
