00:04:06,370 --> 00:04:07,980
format using this DTO.

66
00:04:09,460 --> 00:04:13,210
Look at its user property and look at that user's ID.

67
00:04:13,690 --> 00:04:19,720
So take that value and assign it to this brand new property that you and I are adding in called user

68
00:04:19,720 --> 00:04:20,020
ID.

69
00:04:20,590 --> 00:04:22,960
So that's how we're going to get the user's ID inside of here.

70
00:04:24,080 --> 00:04:25,760
Okay, so let's save this.

71
00:04:26,300 --> 00:04:28,070
I'm going to go make my request again.

72
00:04:28,580 --> 00:04:32,580
And now inside of our response, sure enough, I do have my user ID right there.

73
00:04:32,600 --> 00:04:33,530
Perfect.

74
00:04:34,380 --> 00:04:35,910
So now we are creating a report.

75
00:04:35,940 --> 00:04:40,950
We are associating a user with a report and in the outgoing response we are including just the bare

76
00:04:40,950 --> 00:04:43,830
minimum amount of information around the report itself.

77
00:04:43,830 --> 00:04:46,410
So its ID price, blah blah, blah, all that stuff.

78
00:04:46,410 --> 00:04:50,580
And then for the association, just the ID of that association.

79
00:04:50,580 --> 00:04:55,740
So we are not embedding a ton of additional information inside of here that is not necessarily required.

80
00:04:55,860 --> 00:04:57,420
So let's say this looks pretty good.

81
00:04:58,450 --> 00:04:59,970
Okay, So let's take a pause right here.

82
00:05:00,020 --> 00:05:03,730
Now that we have wired up this association and we'll continue in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# section 16: A Basic Permissions System

1
00:00:01,030 --> 00:00:05,920
Let's move on to our next route handler So we're going to implement the idea of approving or rejecting

2
00:00:05,920 --> 00:00:08,330
a report that has been submitted by a user.

3
00:00:08,350 --> 00:00:11,350
So that's going to be this patch route handler down here at the bottom.

4
00:00:11,530 --> 00:00:16,990
Remember, we eventually want to allow a report to be created in an unapproved state, and we're going

5
00:00:16,990 --> 00:00:21,100
to require some kind of administrator to review this report before it gets approved.

6
00:00:21,100 --> 00:00:26,050
And only then will it be used inside of our car value estimate calculations.

7
00:00:26,080 --> 00:00:31,930
The entire idea behind having a report start off as unapproved is to just prevent abuse of our platform.

8
00:00:31,930 --> 00:00:36,490
We want to make sure that some malicious user does not create a bunch of reports where they either say

9
00:00:36,490 --> 00:00:39,640
that their car is worth way more than it should be or way less.

10
00:00:39,670 --> 00:00:42,850
In order to manipulate all the different estimates that we spit out.

11
00:00:43,530 --> 00:00:48,600
So we're going to eventually have the concept of administrative users and these admin users are going

12
00:00:48,600 --> 00:00:51,930
to be responsible for reviewing all these different reports and approving them.

13
00:00:52,170 --> 00:00:57,090
We're not going to worry about these administrative users for right now, for right now, for the first

14
00:00:57,090 --> 00:01:01,740
kind of take through this little feature, we're going to say that anyone can approve any report.

15
00:01:01,770 --> 00:01:05,760
Once we get the base functionality put together, we'll then come back through and add in the idea of

16
00:01:05,760 --> 00:01:08,340
authorization and administrative users.

17
00:01:08,580 --> 00:01:12,480
So for right now, all we need to do is open up our report entity.

18
00:01:12,510 --> 00:01:16,230
We're going to add in a new property that we'll call approved or something like that.

19
00:01:16,230 --> 00:01:19,920
By default, every report is going to be unapproved.

20
00:01:20,620 --> 00:01:25,450
We're then going to make sure that whenever someone makes a request to patch reports slash ID, we're

21
00:01:25,450 --> 00:01:29,530
going to find that report with a given ID and we're going to change its approved property to.

22
00:01:29,560 --> 00:01:30,140
True.

23
00:01:30,160 --> 00:01:31,210
And that's it.

24
00:01:31,880 --> 00:01:33,230
So let's get to it.

25
00:01:33,440 --> 00:01:34,970
Back over inside my editor.

26
00:01:34,970 --> 00:01:38,420
I'm going to first begin by opening up my report entity file.

27
00:01:39,890 --> 00:01:44,960
Then inside the report entity, I'm going to add in a new property that I'll call approved.

28
00:01:45,590 --> 00:01:47,390
So that's going to be a Boolean.

29
00:01:47,750 --> 00:01:49,820
I'll add in my column Decorator.

30
00:01:50,790 --> 00:01:54,630
And whenever a report is first created, I really do not want it to be approved.

31
00:01:54,630 --> 00:01:57,880
I want a approved value of false or something like that.

32
00:01:57,900 --> 00:02:02,340
So to make sure that's always the case, I'm going to add in an option to the column decorator.

33
00:02:02,430 --> 00:02:06,660
I'm going to put in an object with a default property of false.

34
00:02:07,080 --> 00:02:12,360
So that means that by default, if we do not set approved to some value when we create a brand new report

35
00:02:12,390 --> 00:02:15,690
approved, will be given a default value of false.

36
00:02:18,120 --> 00:02:18,190
Yeah.

37
00:02:18,280 --> 00:02:19,120
So that looks good.

38
00:02:19,660 --> 00:02:21,120
So now we're going to go over to our controller.

39
00:02:21,130 --> 00:02:23,830
We're going to put together our new patch route handler.

40
00:02:23,860 --> 00:02:26,980
We're going to find the ID that the request is looking for.

41
00:02:26,980 --> 00:02:32,650
So that wild card of ID right there, we're going to fetch that report and change the approved property

42
00:02:32,650 --> 00:02:32,800
to.

43
00:02:32,830 --> 00:02:33,430
True.

44
00:02:34,910 --> 00:02:36,500
So back inside of my controller.

45
00:02:37,800 --> 00:02:38,460
At the very top.

46
00:02:38,460 --> 00:02:40,950
I'm going to find my import statement for Nestjs Common.

47
00:02:40,980 --> 00:02:43,110
We're going to add in a couple more imports.

48
00:02:43,620 --> 00:02:45,540
I'm going to first put in a couple new lines here.

49
00:02:47,560 --> 00:02:55,390
And then I'm going to add in an import for the patch decorator and the param decorator as well.

50
00:02:55,660 --> 00:03:00,520
We're using the param decorator, remember, to extract this kind of wild card ID right there.

51
00:03:01,640 --> 00:03:04,970
So now let's put together the route handler itself with these two decorators.

52
00:03:07,750 --> 00:03:11,290
Down at the bottom of the controller, I'm going to add in a new new route handler called something

53
00:03:11,290 --> 00:03:13,600
like Approve report.

54
00:03:15,080 --> 00:03:17,390
Mark it with the patch decorator.

55
00:03:17,390 --> 00:03:21,890
And I'm going to say that this is going to receive some wild card ID inside of the URL.

56
00:03:22,280 --> 00:03:27,200
So now in total, our route is going to be reports slash wild card ID.

57
00:03:28,790 --> 00:03:33,650
Then inside the route handler itself, I'm going to read in that parameter called ID.

58
00:03:36,770 --> 00:03:41,860
I'm also going to receive the body of the request, which is going to have this approved property.

59
00:03:41,900 --> 00:03:46,430
So I've said that, yeah, we're going to allow someone to make a request to approve a report, but

60
00:03:46,430 --> 00:03:50,370
we're also going to allow this route handler to approve a report as well.

61
00:03:50,390 --> 00:03:54,260
So in the body of the request, we're going to have this object that's going to have an approved property

62
00:03:54,260 --> 00:03:57,010
and it's going to be either true or false.

63
00:03:57,020 --> 00:04:01,940
So we'll be able to use this route handler to either approve or UN approve a report.

64
00:04:03,150 --> 00:04:06,220
In order to receive that body, we're going to have to create a DTO.

65
00:04:06,240 --> 00:04:09,960
So for right now, let's just add in this body.

66
00:04:10,260 --> 00:04:16,800
We'll call it body and we'll say that it is of type about approve report.

67
00:04:18,070 --> 00:04:18,710
DTO.

68
00:04:20,400 --> 00:04:22,110
So let's go and define this DTO.

69
00:04:22,140 --> 00:04:23,880
We'll then import it into this file.

70
00:04:25,440 --> 00:04:31,770
All right, So inside of our directory, I'm going to make a new file called Approve Reports.

71
00:04:33,610 --> 00:04:34,450
Inside of here.

72
00:04:34,450 --> 00:04:36,130
I'm going to import.

73
00:04:38,900 --> 00:04:39,200
Let's see.

74
00:04:39,230 --> 00:04:41,260
What validator should we make use of here?

75
00:04:41,270 --> 00:04:45,590
We really just want to make sure that we have a approved property that is gonna be either true or false.

76
00:04:45,590 --> 00:04:48,740
So I'm going to use the is boolean.

77
00:04:49,830 --> 00:04:51,600
Validator from class.

78
00:04:52,320 --> 00:04:53,130
Validator.

79
00:04:54,450 --> 00:04:57,920
I'll then export a class called approve report DTO.

80
00:04:58,880 --> 00:05:02,930
So the body of the incoming request, we're going to say this has an approved property that's going

81
00:05:02,930 --> 00:05:08,540
to be a Boolean and want to validate that and make sure that it is actually a Boolean.

82
00:05:11,580 --> 00:05:14,490
Now we can import this back inside of our controller.

83
00:05:14,670 --> 00:05:17,040
So back over inside the controller at the very top.

84
00:05:18,310 --> 00:05:19,690
I'm going to import.

85
00:05:20,670 --> 00:05:21,960
Approve report.

86
00:05:28,810 --> 00:05:29,950
And now our route handler.

87
00:05:29,950 --> 00:05:34,150
We should not have an error around that type annotation right there anymore.

88
00:05:34,660 --> 00:05:35,820
Okay, so this looks good.

89
00:05:35,830 --> 00:05:37,520
We've got the route handler put together.

90
00:05:37,540 --> 00:05:43,250
So now we just need to take in that ID, take in the body and pull off just the approved property.

91
00:05:43,270 --> 00:05:47,740
We're then going to pass that information off to the report service, which will be responsible for

92
00:05:47,740 --> 00:05:52,090
actually finding the appropriate report, changing the approved property over to whatever it should

93
00:05:52,090 --> 00:05:53,980
be, and then saving the updated record.

94
00:05:54,010 --> 00:05:57,400
So let's take a pause right here and continue on in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,240 --> 00:00:02,440
Inside of our route handler.

2
00:00:02,440 --> 00:00:07,540
We're now going to take that ID and that body, specifically the approved property off the body.

3
00:00:07,540 --> 00:00:11,000
And we're going to pass this information off to some method inside of our service.

4
00:00:11,020 --> 00:00:15,700
The report service will then be in charge of finding the appropriate report and updating the approved

5
00:00:15,700 --> 00:00:16,450
property.

6
00:00:17,100 --> 00:00:24,330
Okay, so inside the route handler, I'm going to return this dot reports service and I'm going to just

7
00:00:24,330 --> 00:00:29,250
imagine or assume that we're going to add in a new method to the service called something like change

8
00:00:29,250 --> 00:00:30,150
approval.

9
00:00:30,880 --> 00:00:36,430
So this method is going to be 100% dedicated to just finding a particular report and changing its approval

10
00:00:36,430 --> 00:00:37,150
status.

11
00:00:37,810 --> 00:00:43,570
So I'm going to pass in the ID and then off the body property or the body argument we're going to pass

12
00:00:43,570 --> 00:00:46,450
in whatever the value of approved is.

13
00:00:48,720 --> 00:00:52,370
So let's go over to our service now and we're going to add in this new method that's going to receive

14
00:00:52,370 --> 00:00:57,470
the ID and either the true or false value for whether this thing should be approved or not.

15
00:00:58,290 --> 00:00:59,940
So over inside of our service.

16
00:01:02,020 --> 00:01:06,280
I'm going to define the new method we'll call change approval.

17
00:01:07,570 --> 00:01:10,000
We're going to receive that, which will be a string.

18
00:01:10,930 --> 00:01:13,630
And approved, which will be a boolean.

19
00:01:14,890 --> 00:01:19,420
So now, once again, inside of here, all we have to do is find the appropriate report.

20
00:01:19,540 --> 00:01:20,890
Change the approved property.

21
00:01:20,890 --> 00:01:22,060
And that's pretty much it.

22
00:01:22,830 --> 00:01:26,160
So inside of here, I'm going to first mark this function as async.

23
00:01:26,610 --> 00:01:30,490
I'm then going to find the appropriate report by using our repository.

24
00:01:30,540 --> 00:01:34,470
So I'll say report is await this dot repo.

25
00:01:34,500 --> 00:01:36,930
Find one and I'll pass in the ID.

26
00:01:39,770 --> 00:01:44,000
Now, whenever we try to find a single record by ID, it is entirely possible that we might fail to

27
00:01:44,000 --> 00:01:44,840
find that record.

28
00:01:44,840 --> 00:01:47,210
So we're going to make sure that report actually exists.

29
00:01:47,240 --> 00:01:49,730
We're going to make sure that we actually found a report with a given ID.

30
00:01:50,180 --> 00:01:55,610
So we'll say if there is not a report, then we need to throw an error and tell whoever's make the request.

31
00:01:55,610 --> 00:01:57,650
Sorry, but we were not able to find that record.

32
00:01:57,680 --> 00:02:02,900
So once again, to handle this case, we're going to throw a not found exception.

33
00:02:03,180 --> 00:02:05,660
I'll give it the custom message of something like report.

34
00:02:07,140 --> 00:02:08,190
Not found.

35
00:02:10,850 --> 00:02:13,880
I'm then going to make sure that I import that exception at the top of the file.

36
00:02:14,450 --> 00:02:19,700
So at the very top I will import not found exception from nestjs common.

37
00:02:25,700 --> 00:02:27,500
I'm still getting an error around that.

38
00:02:28,770 --> 00:02:30,180
Oh, Throat knew my mistake.

39
00:02:30,210 --> 00:02:30,770
That's better.

40
00:02:30,780 --> 00:02:32,520
Throw knew and not found exception.

41
00:02:34,240 --> 00:02:34,450
Okay.

42
00:02:34,450 --> 00:02:38,140
So now that we've got the reports, we're going to update the approved property on it and then we're

43
00:02:38,140 --> 00:02:41,210
just going to save it, just as we did back up here inside of Create.

44
00:02:41,230 --> 00:02:49,000
So we'll say report dot approved is the new value for approved that was passed into this function and

45
00:02:49,000 --> 00:02:55,810
then we will return this dot repo dot save and we're going to save the updated report.

46
00:02:56,860 --> 00:02:58,090
And that should be it.

47
00:02:59,680 --> 00:03:02,970
Let's now save all the changes we have made inside this file.

48
00:03:03,150 --> 00:03:04,870
We're going to go back over to our controller.

49
00:03:04,890 --> 00:03:06,750
Everything over here looks pretty good as well.

50
00:03:06,750 --> 00:03:08,970
So I think we're ready to test this stuff out.

51
00:03:09,120 --> 00:03:10,950
I'm going to open up my client.

52
00:03:12,540 --> 00:03:13,260
Here we go.

53
00:03:14,890 --> 00:03:17,580
I'm going to first attempt to make a brand new report.

54
00:03:17,590 --> 00:03:22,120
And when I create the report, I should get back a response that say that this thing is by default,

55
00:03:22,150 --> 00:03:23,320
not approved.

56
00:03:23,350 --> 00:03:25,000
So I'm going to send the request off.

57
00:03:25,270 --> 00:03:26,380
There we go.

58
00:03:26,380 --> 00:03:31,480
And I am matter of fact, I'm not told at all about the approval state because we did not mention the

59
00:03:31,480 --> 00:03:33,520
approved property inside of our report.

60
00:03:34,150 --> 00:03:35,570
So let's go fix that up real quick.

61
00:03:35,590 --> 00:03:37,250
My mistake on forgetting that step.

62
00:03:37,270 --> 00:03:43,720
Let's make sure that inside of our DTOs report file, we're going to say that in the response whenever

63
00:03:43,720 --> 00:03:46,720
we send out a report, we are also going to mention.

64
00:03:47,680 --> 00:03:49,510
Whether or not this thing is approved.

65
00:03:52,650 --> 00:03:54,090
Okay, that should be better.

66
00:03:54,420 --> 00:03:58,590
So now if we try to make a report with our client.

67
00:04:00,010 --> 00:04:04,390
By default, the import report is not approved, so we get approved a false.

68
00:04:04,420 --> 00:04:06,010
That's definitely what we want.

69
00:04:06,250 --> 00:04:09,880
So now inside my client, I'm going to write out a brand new request.

70
00:04:10,030 --> 00:04:13,900
So I'm going to say approve an existing report.

71
00:04:15,260 --> 00:04:17,750
I'm going to do a patch request to.

72
00:04:18,750 --> 00:04:25,080
Localhost 3000 slash reports and we need to put in the ID of an actual report right here.

73
00:04:25,080 --> 00:04:28,980
So I'm just going to make another report very quickly and just take note of the ID.

74
00:04:29,010 --> 00:04:35,340
Looks like I've got a report of eight right here, so I'm going to try to set the report with ID eight.

75
00:04:35,370 --> 00:04:37,680
I'm going to try to change its approved property to.

76
00:04:37,680 --> 00:04:38,190
True.

77
00:04:39,590 --> 00:04:45,440
I'm then going to send along a header of content type application Json.

78
00:04:46,080 --> 00:04:52,200
And then finally, I'm going to say that I want to change the approved property to true.

79
00:04:52,650 --> 00:04:53,970
And that should be it.

80
00:04:55,010 --> 00:04:59,990
So I'll now send off this request and sure enough, get a response back with approved of True.

81
00:05:00,020 --> 00:05:04,790
So that means that this report is now approved and in theory, it will be used inside of all of our

82
00:05:05,240 --> 00:05:10,460
estimate reports or our estimate values we eventually generate and put together.

83
00:05:10,730 --> 00:05:14,250
Let's also just make sure that we can also unapproved report as well.

84
00:05:14,270 --> 00:05:17,510
So I'm going to try changing approved over to false.

85
00:05:17,660 --> 00:05:18,950
I'm going to send this off.

86
00:05:19,010 --> 00:05:20,870
Yep, that definitely works as well.

87
00:05:21,620 --> 00:05:23,510
Okay, so this definitely looks good.

88
00:05:23,600 --> 00:05:30,410
So we've got some basic functionality now around having a report in an approved or unapproved state.

89
00:05:30,440 --> 00:05:35,180
So now we just need to add in this idea of administrative users, because remember, we only want to

90
00:05:35,180 --> 00:05:40,370
allow an administrator to take a look at all the different reports and eventually approve a report.

91
00:05:40,400 --> 00:05:42,470
Common users should not be able to do so.

92
00:05:42,650 --> 00:05:47,150
So let's start adding in some authorization to our application in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,480 --> 00:00:05,790
Now that we can approve or approve an existing report, we're going to add in some authorization around

2
00:00:05,790 --> 00:00:06,860
this request handler.

3
00:00:06,870 --> 00:00:11,010
We're going to make sure that the idea of an administrative user exists inside of our application,

4
00:00:11,010 --> 00:00:15,270
and we're going to make sure that only admin users should be able to make a request to this route.

5
00:00:15,420 --> 00:00:19,470
So let me show you a quick diagram or two just to help you understand what we really need to do here.

6
00:00:19,710 --> 00:00:21,630
So first thing, a little bit of terminology.

7
00:00:21,660 --> 00:00:24,690
We have already implemented authentication inside of our app.

8
00:00:24,720 --> 00:00:29,340
Authentication systems are all about figuring out who is making a request to our application.

9
00:00:29,340 --> 00:00:31,620
So we have already put all this stuff together.

10
00:00:31,620 --> 00:00:36,180
Whenever someone makes a request to our app, we are extracting some information out of the incoming

11
00:00:36,180 --> 00:00:41,040
cookie of the request and we're using that information to figure out who is making a request to us.

12
00:00:41,160 --> 00:00:43,650
So now we're going to start to work on authorization.

13
00:00:43,680 --> 00:00:49,200
Authorization is a reference to a system that helps us figure out whether or not this particular user

14
00:00:49,200 --> 00:00:51,870
is allowed to do something inside of our app.

15
00:00:51,870 --> 00:00:57,420
So we are going to build out a very simple authorization system that we can use to forbid certain users

16
00:00:57,420 --> 00:00:59,970
from making requests to some different route handlers.

17
00:01:01,110 --> 00:01:05,730
Now, as a quick reminder, before I show you the next diagram, I want to show you two things that

18
00:01:05,730 --> 00:01:08,070
we have already put together inside of our application.

19
00:01:08,310 --> 00:01:13,630
So the first thing I want to give you a quick reminder on is our users interceptors.

20
00:01:13,650 --> 00:01:15,570
Current user interceptor.

21
00:01:15,930 --> 00:01:21,450
So remember, this is an interceptor we put together to intercept incoming requests into our application

22
00:01:21,660 --> 00:01:23,160
for any incoming request.

23
00:01:23,190 --> 00:01:29,130
We take a look at that request cookie through the request dot session property and we use that cookie

24
00:01:29,130 --> 00:01:34,710
or that session to figure out who the current user is or who the person that's making this request is.

25
00:01:35,220 --> 00:01:42,360
We then fetch that person using our user service and assign that user to the request dot current user

26
00:01:42,360 --> 00:01:43,120
property.

27
00:01:43,150 --> 00:01:47,460
So that means that inside of all of our different request handlers, we can very easily get access to

28
00:01:47,460 --> 00:01:52,290
the entire user entity that represents the person who's making a request to our application.

29
00:01:52,380 --> 00:01:54,120
So that's reminder number one.

30
00:01:54,300 --> 00:01:55,800
Reminder number two.

31
00:01:57,050 --> 00:02:00,200
Recall that we also put together something called an auth guard.

32
00:02:00,230 --> 00:02:04,400
That's inside of our guards auth guard file.

33
00:02:05,160 --> 00:02:10,710
The auth guard or authentication guard makes sure that someone is signed into your application before

34
00:02:10,740 --> 00:02:16,110
they are allowed to access a given route Handler So in general, we make use of guards to forbid access

35
00:02:16,110 --> 00:02:17,670
to certain route handlers.

36
00:02:18,180 --> 00:02:18,510
Okay.

37
00:02:18,510 --> 00:02:22,920
So now that we got this reminder, let's understand how we're going to implement authorization inside

38
00:02:22,920 --> 00:02:23,580
of our app.

39
00:02:25,410 --> 00:02:25,740
All right.

40
00:02:25,740 --> 00:02:27,000
So here's the general idea.

41
00:02:27,000 --> 00:02:31,740
It's going to be pretty simple and straightforward, very similar to the auth guard we already put together.

42
00:02:31,890 --> 00:02:37,920
Whenever someone makes a request to patch reports, slash wild card ID, we're going to make sure that

43
00:02:37,920 --> 00:02:40,830
this route is guarded by a guard.

44
00:02:40,860 --> 00:02:44,340
We're going to call this something like admin guard or something very similar.

45
00:02:44,370 --> 00:02:47,100
This guard is going to have a very simple purpose.

46
00:02:47,130 --> 00:02:52,440
It's going to look at the request dot current user property and figure out whether or not that person

47
00:02:52,440 --> 00:02:54,690
or that user is an administrator.

48
00:02:54,690 --> 00:02:56,040
And that's pretty much it.

49
00:02:56,250 --> 00:03:00,900
If this person is an administrator, we're going to return true from the auth guard, which is going

50
00:03:00,900 --> 00:03:02,250
to allow the request through.

51
00:03:02,430 --> 00:03:07,350
Otherwise, if they are not an administrator, we're going to return false, which is going to reject

52
00:03:07,350 --> 00:03:09,780
the request and send back an error immediately.

53
00:03:10,020 --> 00:03:11,370
So that's pretty much it.

54
00:03:11,370 --> 00:03:12,600
That's all we really have to do here.

55
00:03:12,600 --> 00:03:14,070
We have to put together a new guard.

56
00:03:14,100 --> 00:03:19,080
We also have to open up our user entity file and we need to make sure that all users also have some

57
00:03:19,080 --> 00:03:21,120
kind of admin property tied to them.

58
00:03:21,120 --> 00:03:26,740
So that can be a very simple boolean that says, yes, this person is an admin or no they are not.

59
00:03:27,070 --> 00:03:30,130
So adding in this feature should be pretty straightforward.

60
00:03:31,060 --> 00:03:35,650
Now, having said that, you are going to very quickly see that as soon as we put together our guard,

61
00:03:35,650 --> 00:03:38,260
it's not quite going to work as expected.

62
00:03:38,380 --> 00:03:42,700
So this is going to be another scenario where I've kind of introduced an error into our application,

63
00:03:42,730 --> 00:03:47,920
a very, very small and subtle error that we added into our application a while back that we're only

64
00:03:47,920 --> 00:03:51,340
going to start to see the result of now as we put this guard together.

65
00:03:51,340 --> 00:03:56,140
And I specifically want you to see this error that we introduced on purpose, because this is going

66
00:03:56,140 --> 00:04:01,390
to be an error that you are absolutely going to run into as you start working on your own applications.

67
00:04:01,390 --> 00:04:02,770
So just keep that in mind.

68
00:04:02,800 --> 00:04:08,320
On the first take of our admin guard, it's not going to quite work as expected, but that is totally

69
00:04:08,320 --> 00:04:10,660
fine and we're going to fix it up very quickly after that.

70
00:04:11,170 --> 00:04:11,560
Okay.

71
00:04:11,560 --> 00:04:16,000
So with all this in mind, let's take a pause right here and get started on this admin guard in just

72
00:04:16,000 --> 00:04:16,720
a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:03,450
Let's get started on this admin guard.

2
00:00:03,480 --> 00:00:07,080
The first thing we're going to do is open up our user entity file.

3
00:00:07,110 --> 00:00:08,850
So I'm going to go and find my users.

4
00:00:08,850 --> 00:00:10,950
User dot entity, dot ts file.

5
00:00:11,190 --> 00:00:15,060
So inside of here, very similar to what we did a moment ago around our report.

6
00:00:15,090 --> 00:00:19,650
We're going to say that in addition to all the properties that a user user usually has, we're also

7
00:00:19,650 --> 00:00:22,200
going to give them an admin property as well.

8
00:00:23,130 --> 00:00:27,570
So this admin property, which is going to be either true or false, indicates whether or not this is

9
00:00:27,570 --> 00:00:29,070
an administrative user.

10
00:00:29,190 --> 00:00:30,720
I'm going to mark this with column.

11
00:00:30,930 --> 00:00:36,090
And then for just right now, just for testing purposes, just to save a little bit of time, we're

12
00:00:36,090 --> 00:00:40,350
going to say that by default, everyone is an administrator.

13
00:00:40,440 --> 00:00:45,300
So now if we sign up as a brand new user, they would have an admin of true value.

14
00:00:45,690 --> 00:00:49,350
Obviously that's not something we want to have in the final version of our application, so we will

15
00:00:49,350 --> 00:00:51,450
come back eventually and fix this up.

16
00:00:51,450 --> 00:00:54,510
But for right now, for testing purposes, this is good enough.

17
00:00:54,630 --> 00:00:59,700
So if we went and created a brand new user, we would be an administrator and in theory, we should

18
00:00:59,730 --> 00:01:01,830
be able to get past this admin guard.

19
00:01:03,320 --> 00:01:07,220
Inside the admin guard itself to decide whether or not to allow a request through.

20
00:01:07,250 --> 00:01:12,020
We're going to take a look at this admin property and if admin is true, naturally they are going to

21
00:01:12,020 --> 00:01:13,760
be able to get through the admin guard.

22
00:01:13,790 --> 00:01:17,600
Otherwise, if admin is false, we're going to reject the incoming request.

23
00:01:18,840 --> 00:01:19,140
Okay.

24
00:01:19,140 --> 00:01:20,870
So keep in mind, we do need to come back.

25
00:01:20,880 --> 00:01:25,470
We need to make sure that not everyone is an admin by default, but we will take care of that in a little

26
00:01:25,470 --> 00:01:26,070
bit.

27
00:01:26,610 --> 00:01:34,350
So now I'm going to find my SRC guards folder and inside there I'm going to make a new guard called

28
00:01:34,350 --> 00:01:36,700
admin Guards.

29
00:01:38,410 --> 00:01:42,790
So then inside of here, we're going to put together a guard that looks very similar to the auth guard

30
00:01:42,820 --> 00:01:44,140
that we already assembled.

31
00:01:44,530 --> 00:01:55,210
So inside of here, I'm going to import can activate and execution context from nestjs Common.

32
00:01:57,880 --> 00:02:05,080
I'm then going to define and export a class called admin Guard and that's going to implement can activate.

33
00:02:07,510 --> 00:02:14,080
I'll define the can activate method, which will be called with some execution context.

34
00:02:14,080 --> 00:02:18,520
And remember, this execution context is like a wrapper around the incoming request.

35
00:02:18,830 --> 00:02:23,860
So now inside of here, we need to either return true or false to state simply whether or not the user

36
00:02:23,860 --> 00:02:28,810
is authenticated or is an administrator and should be allowed to actually access a given route.

37
00:02:29,200 --> 00:02:31,690
So I'm going to pull out the request.

38
00:02:38,770 --> 00:02:43,090
We're then going to take a look at the request current user property.

39
00:02:43,120 --> 00:02:46,110
Remember just a moment ago I gave you a very fast reminder.

40
00:02:46,120 --> 00:02:49,690
I told you that inside of our where do we put that thing?

41
00:02:49,720 --> 00:02:52,540
Users interceptors, current user interceptor.

42
00:02:52,570 --> 00:02:57,790
This class or this interceptor right here is going to make sure that we define our current user property

43
00:02:57,790 --> 00:03:01,120
on the request object with the user who is signed in to the application.

44
00:03:01,330 --> 00:03:05,010
So we are going to first make sure that request dot current user is defined.

45
00:03:05,020 --> 00:03:07,300
We're going to make sure that there actually is a user.

46
00:03:07,300 --> 00:03:09,790
So if there is not.

47
00:03:11,290 --> 00:03:14,160
A current user, then we're going to immediately return False.

48
00:03:14,170 --> 00:03:18,700
So that means if you're not signed in, obviously you are not an administrator, so we're going to reject

49
00:03:18,700 --> 00:03:19,510
your request.

50
00:03:19,930 --> 00:03:23,950
If we get past that check, we'll then take a look and see if.

51
00:03:25,980 --> 00:03:27,810
Request current user.

52
00:03:28,970 --> 00:03:30,260
Admin is true.

53
00:03:30,560 --> 00:03:35,500
So if there is a current user property and they are an administrator, fantastic.

54
00:03:35,510 --> 00:03:37,310
Let's go ahead and return.

55
00:03:37,310 --> 00:03:37,640
True.

56
00:03:37,670 --> 00:03:39,080
We're going to allow the request.

57
00:03:39,440 --> 00:03:41,210
Otherwise we will return.

58
00:03:41,240 --> 00:03:42,050
False.

59
00:03:42,840 --> 00:03:47,550
Now the second if statement can be very easily simplified rather than doing this complicated if statement,

60
00:03:47,580 --> 00:03:54,360
we can simply return request dot current user dot admin and that would be equivalent to that really

61
00:03:54,360 --> 00:03:55,530
long if statement.

62
00:03:56,940 --> 00:03:58,020
And that's pretty much it.

63
00:03:58,020 --> 00:03:59,340
That's all we really need.

64
00:03:59,430 --> 00:04:02,970
So if there is not a current user, definitely return false.

65
00:04:03,000 --> 00:04:05,640
Otherwise we can just return whatever the admin property is.

66
00:04:05,640 --> 00:04:08,240
If admin is true, great, the user is going to be allowed through.

67
00:04:08,250 --> 00:04:11,610
Otherwise if admin is false, then the request will be rejected.

68
00:04:12,350 --> 00:04:15,110
Okay, so that is it for the Guard itself.

69
00:04:15,110 --> 00:04:16,820
That is pretty much the final implementation.

70
00:04:16,820 --> 00:04:18,170
That's all we have to put together.

71
00:04:18,170 --> 00:04:22,460
But as I mentioned, we are going to run into a very small bug around this.

72
00:04:23,000 --> 00:04:24,070
So let's take a pause right here.

73
00:04:24,080 --> 00:04:25,190
We're going to come back to the next video.

74
00:04:25,190 --> 00:04:26,570
We're going to test this thing out.

75
00:04:26,600 --> 00:04:28,670
It's not going to quite work as expected.

76
00:04:28,760 --> 00:04:30,530
We're going to figure out what the error is.

77
00:04:30,560 --> 00:04:34,700
And along the way, we're going to learn a lot around the internals of Nest itself.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,740 --> 00:00:05,250
Let's now wire up our admin guard to the reports controller.

2
00:00:05,250 --> 00:00:10,140
We're going to make sure that only users who are administrators are allowed to make requests to approve

3
00:00:10,140 --> 00:00:11,010
a report.

4
00:00:11,130 --> 00:00:14,820
So I'm going to go back over to my reports controller at the very top.

5
00:00:14,820 --> 00:00:16,200
I'm going to import.

6
00:00:17,350 --> 00:00:18,790
The guard we just assembled.

7
00:00:18,790 --> 00:00:21,430
So admin guard from up one directory.

8
00:00:21,460 --> 00:00:22,240
Guards.

9
00:00:22,270 --> 00:00:22,900
Admin.

10
00:00:23,170 --> 00:00:23,800
Guard.

11
00:00:24,590 --> 00:00:30,420
And then as a quick reminder on how we wire up a guard, all we have to do is make use of the use guards

12
00:00:30,440 --> 00:00:31,040
decorator.

13
00:00:31,040 --> 00:00:34,340
So we've already done something very similar on create report right here.

14
00:00:35,760 --> 00:00:36,060
Okay.

15
00:00:36,060 --> 00:00:37,140
So I'm going to add in.

16
00:00:37,830 --> 00:00:38,730
Use.

17
00:00:39,210 --> 00:00:40,310
Guards.

18
00:00:40,350 --> 00:00:41,090
Admin.

19
00:00:41,100 --> 00:00:41,810
Guard.

20
00:00:41,820 --> 00:00:42,980
And that should be it.

21
00:00:42,990 --> 00:00:47,250
So now only an administrative user will be able to approve a report.

22
00:00:48,890 --> 00:00:53,570
To test this out, we do need to first create a brand new user because remember, we added in that default

23
00:00:53,570 --> 00:00:56,210
of admin true into our user entity.

24
00:00:56,210 --> 00:00:58,660
So we need to create a brand new user by default.

25
00:00:58,670 --> 00:01:00,500
They will be marked as an administrator.

26
00:01:00,500 --> 00:01:02,870
Once we do that, we can then test out this route.

27
00:01:02,900 --> 00:01:05,990
Handler So I'm going to open up my client.

28
00:01:07,610 --> 00:01:09,920
Around all of our users related stuff.

29
00:01:10,220 --> 00:01:15,200
I'm going to create a brand new user with some unique email address, so I'm going to send that request

30
00:01:15,200 --> 00:01:15,730
off.

31
00:01:15,740 --> 00:01:16,290
Okay.

32
00:01:16,310 --> 00:01:17,630
I'm now signed in.

33
00:01:17,630 --> 00:01:22,670
I've signed up to the application and in theory, this user that I just created should be an administrator.

34
00:01:23,380 --> 00:01:27,040
So now I'm going to go to my client around reports.

35
00:01:28,480 --> 00:01:33,010
So here's the patch request that we just put together to approve a report.

36
00:01:33,400 --> 00:01:37,000
I'm going to first, how about first make a new report just to make sure I'm working with a fresh one.

37
00:01:37,180 --> 00:01:38,950
That is definitely not approved.

38
00:01:38,950 --> 00:01:40,480
So I'm going to create a new one really quickly.

39
00:01:40,480 --> 00:01:42,220
This one is definitely not approved.

40
00:01:42,460 --> 00:01:47,050
So now if I attempt to approve that one, make sure I put in the correct ID right there.

41
00:01:47,080 --> 00:01:53,230
I'm going to send this off and unfortunately, I end up with an error that's a little bit unexpected.

42
00:01:53,230 --> 00:01:57,040
I really kind of expected that everything we put together would have worked by this point in time.

43
00:01:57,040 --> 00:02:00,310
So this is the little issue I mentioned a little bit ago.

44
00:02:00,310 --> 00:02:04,270
And as I mentioned, this is going to allow us to get a better idea of what's going on internally with

45
00:02:04,270 --> 00:02:04,770
Nest.

46
00:02:05,480 --> 00:02:08,000
Giving you an explanation around why this thing is failing.

47
00:02:08,000 --> 00:02:10,670
So the admin guard is going to be just a little bit involved.

48
00:02:10,670 --> 00:02:14,360
So let's take a quick pause right here, come back the next video and take a look at a series of diagrams

49
00:02:14,360 --> 00:02:18,170
and understand why the guard is not quite working as expected.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,480 --> 00:00:06,340
We just put our admin guard together and tested it, but unfortunately we were not able to update a

2
00:00:06,340 --> 00:00:07,000
report.

3
00:00:07,000 --> 00:00:10,360
So in this video we're going to take a look at a couple of different diagrams and understand why that

4
00:00:10,360 --> 00:00:12,510
is and eventually fix things up.

5
00:00:12,520 --> 00:00:18,220
So as I mentioned, this error that we are seeing is related to a very fundamental or core concept in

6
00:00:18,220 --> 00:00:19,240
the world of Nest.

7
00:00:19,870 --> 00:00:20,170
All right.

8
00:00:20,170 --> 00:00:20,920
So a couple diagrams.

9
00:00:20,920 --> 00:00:21,580
Here we go.

10
00:00:21,700 --> 00:00:26,050
So you might recall that over this course, we have worked with many different tools that are going

11
00:00:26,050 --> 00:00:30,760
to operate on a request before it gets handled inside of a request handler.

12
00:00:30,880 --> 00:00:32,640
So we have an incoming request.

13
00:00:32,650 --> 00:00:36,940
That request is then going to be processed by any middlewares that we've hooked up to our application,

14
00:00:36,940 --> 00:00:40,840
then by any guards, and then finally by any interceptors.

15
00:00:40,840 --> 00:00:46,990
And remember, an interceptor is very special in that it can work on a request before or after or both

16
00:00:46,990 --> 00:00:47,740
for that matter.

17
00:00:47,740 --> 00:00:49,960
It is handled by a request handler.

18
00:00:50,260 --> 00:00:52,660
So here's the issue that we are running into.

19
00:00:53,610 --> 00:00:56,460
At present you and I have the cookies session middleware.

20
00:00:56,460 --> 00:01:00,150
We have our admin guard and we have our current user interceptor.

21
00:01:00,390 --> 00:01:05,100
So the problem is that our incoming request is going to flow through the cookies session middleware

22
00:01:05,100 --> 00:01:08,760
which will place the session object on that incoming request.

23
00:01:09,550 --> 00:01:12,280
The request then goes directly off to the admin guard.

24
00:01:12,280 --> 00:01:13,330
And here's the problem.

25
00:01:13,330 --> 00:01:19,390
It is processed by the admin guard before the current user interceptor ever runs.

26
00:01:19,720 --> 00:01:24,730
So we are trying to look at the current user property on that request before we have ever set the current

27
00:01:24,730 --> 00:01:26,920
user property on the request itself.

28
00:01:27,590 --> 00:01:30,860
So inside the admin guard we take a look at current user.

29
00:01:30,860 --> 00:01:32,180
So right here.

30
00:01:33,200 --> 00:01:34,940
We check to see if that thing is defined.

31
00:01:34,940 --> 00:01:40,670
And in every scenario, in every case, current user will not be defined because we never set that property

32
00:01:40,670 --> 00:01:44,000
until the request goes into our current user interceptor.

33
00:01:44,090 --> 00:01:46,070
So that's the issue we are running into now.

34
00:01:46,100 --> 00:01:51,260
We have made use of middlewares guards and interceptor interceptors, but we haven't really considered

35
00:01:51,260 --> 00:01:53,270
the order in which they execute.

36
00:01:53,420 --> 00:01:58,850
So to fix this up, we need to take our current user interceptor and we need to turn it into something

37
00:01:58,850 --> 00:01:59,570
else.

38
00:01:59,600 --> 00:02:03,290
We need to turn this thing into a middleware instead.

39
00:02:05,010 --> 00:02:10,110
If we turn this into a middleware, that's going to guarantee that it runs before our request ever goes

40
00:02:10,110 --> 00:02:11,800
off into the admin guard.

41
00:02:11,820 --> 00:02:17,370
So by the time the request is inside the admin guard, we will have already set the current user property

42
00:02:17,370 --> 00:02:18,990
on the request object.

43
00:02:19,670 --> 00:02:24,530
Now, the reason that I wanted you to see this error is that this is something you are almost definitely

44
00:02:24,530 --> 00:02:28,720
going to run into on your own projects if you start making use of interceptors.

45
00:02:28,730 --> 00:02:31,040
So if you want to use an interceptor, fantastic.

46
00:02:31,040 --> 00:02:32,330
I highly recommend you do.

47
00:02:32,330 --> 00:02:38,570
But you have to be aware of when interceptors run, they are always going to execute after any middlewares

48
00:02:38,570 --> 00:02:40,130
and after any guards.

49
00:02:40,130 --> 00:02:45,470
So your middlewares and your guards cannot rely upon any information that is created or assigned to

50
00:02:45,470 --> 00:02:48,110
a request object inside of the interceptor.

51
00:02:48,410 --> 00:02:49,520
So that's pretty much it.

52
00:02:49,520 --> 00:02:52,570
That's why we were running into this issue and that's how we were going to fix it up.

53
00:02:52,580 --> 00:02:55,640
We're going to go back to our current user interceptor.

54
00:02:56,590 --> 00:03:00,430
Which is inside of our users interceptors folder.

55
00:03:00,910 --> 00:03:05,680
We're going to turn this thing into a middleware and we're going to wire it up as a global middleware

56
00:03:05,680 --> 00:03:06,940
inside of our project.

57
00:03:07,030 --> 00:03:12,670
You might recall that we've already created a global middleware before when we created the What Do We

58
00:03:12,700 --> 00:03:13,330
Create?

59
00:03:13,330 --> 00:03:16,660
I don't remember off the top of my head was that the current user decorate?

60
00:03:16,690 --> 00:03:17,260
No.

61
00:03:17,530 --> 00:03:18,580
Or maybe it was.

62
00:03:18,670 --> 00:03:21,140
Well, we've definitely created a global middleware.

63
00:03:21,160 --> 00:03:22,060
Oh, cookie session.

64
00:03:22,060 --> 00:03:22,720
That's where we did it.

65
00:03:22,720 --> 00:03:23,450
That's right.

66
00:03:23,470 --> 00:03:26,110
So I think we wired that up inside of our app module.

67
00:03:26,230 --> 00:03:27,550
Right here.

68
00:03:27,550 --> 00:03:28,660
Somewhere inside of here.

69
00:03:30,180 --> 00:03:31,030
There's our global.

70
00:03:31,350 --> 00:03:32,100
There we are.

71
00:03:32,130 --> 00:03:32,940
There it is.

72
00:03:32,970 --> 00:03:34,320
Okay, so there's our global middleware.

73
00:03:34,320 --> 00:03:38,480
I knew we had done that once before, so we essentially just have to repeat the exact same process.

74
00:03:38,490 --> 00:03:42,630
So we're going to take our current user interceptor, we're going to turn it into a middleware and then

75
00:03:42,630 --> 00:03:46,640
wire it up globally across all the different route handlers inside of our project.

76
00:03:46,650 --> 00:03:49,050
So let's get started on this in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,800 --> 00:00:06,510
All right, let's start to refactor our current user interceptor into a middleware instead.

2
00:00:06,540 --> 00:00:10,050
The middleware version of this thing is going to be very similar in nature.

3
00:00:10,050 --> 00:00:13,380
So inside of the middleware version, we're going to receive an incoming request.

4
00:00:13,410 --> 00:00:18,240
We're going to take a look at the request dot session object, look up the user ID using the user service,

5
00:00:18,240 --> 00:00:21,740
and then assign the current user to the request dot current user property.

6
00:00:21,750 --> 00:00:23,220
So very similar in nature.

7
00:00:23,250 --> 00:00:28,020
The only difference is that now we are putting this together inside of a middleware rather than directly

8
00:00:28,020 --> 00:00:29,720
refactoring our interceptor.

9
00:00:29,730 --> 00:00:34,500
I would like to instead create a brand new file, so I want to basically just start from scratch as

10
00:00:34,500 --> 00:00:37,380
opposed to trying to change out the current interceptor.

11
00:00:37,470 --> 00:00:42,090
So inside of my users directory, I'm going to make a new folder called Middlewares.

12
00:00:42,090 --> 00:00:47,820
And then inside there I'll make a new file called Current User Middleware dot ts.

13
00:00:48,870 --> 00:00:53,490
Then inside of here at the very top, I'm going to first add in a couple of different imports from the

14
00:00:53,850 --> 00:00:55,230
common package.

15
00:00:55,890 --> 00:01:03,570
So we're going to get injectable and nest middleware from Nestjs Common.

16
00:01:04,840 --> 00:01:09,280
I'm then going to get request response and next function.

17
00:01:10,020 --> 00:01:11,310
From Express.

18
00:01:14,070 --> 00:01:18,840
And then finally, I'm going to also get our users service.

19
00:01:19,600 --> 00:01:23,380
From up one directory users service.

20
00:01:24,630 --> 00:01:25,890
Then to define the middleware.

21
00:01:25,890 --> 00:01:30,240
I'm going to export class current user middleware.

22
00:01:31,860 --> 00:01:35,580
This is going to implement Nest middleware.

23
00:01:36,880 --> 00:01:40,840
So once again we are only putting in the implements keyword just so TypeScript will jump in and help

24
00:01:40,840 --> 00:01:42,110
us define this middleware.

25
00:01:42,130 --> 00:01:47,110
So to define a middleware, we must define a use function inside of our class.

26
00:01:47,200 --> 00:01:49,120
So I'll define that function right away.

27
00:01:49,540 --> 00:01:55,210
This use function can be async, and in this case we do want it to be async and it's going to receive

28
00:01:55,210 --> 00:01:56,450
a couple of different arguments.

29
00:01:56,470 --> 00:01:59,140
It's going to receive the incoming request object.

30
00:02:02,020 --> 00:02:04,090
The outgoing response object.

31
00:02:05,000 --> 00:02:06,980
And the next function.

32
00:02:08,550 --> 00:02:12,990
Next is a reference to the next middleware that we might have in the chain of middleware.

33
00:02:13,170 --> 00:02:17,310
So we need to eventually call next when our middleware is all done running.

34
00:02:17,550 --> 00:02:21,510
So then inside of here, we're going to do pretty much exactly what we did back inside of our interceptor.

35
00:02:21,780 --> 00:02:24,360
There's one other thing we need to add into our middleware, however.

36
00:02:24,390 --> 00:02:30,390
We need to get access to our users service because we need to eventually reach into our users repository

37
00:02:30,390 --> 00:02:34,680
and find a user with a given ID coming out of our request session object.

38
00:02:34,710 --> 00:02:39,270
So as usual, to get access to our user service, we are going to use dependency injection.

39
00:02:39,300 --> 00:02:41,220
So remember how we use dependency injection.

40
00:02:41,220 --> 00:02:44,490
We're going to mark this class with the injectable decorator.

41
00:02:47,180 --> 00:02:49,010
We'll then add in our constructor.

42
00:02:50,560 --> 00:02:52,390
And on the constructor argument list.

43
00:02:53,580 --> 00:03:01,020
I'm going to add in private users, service is of type users service.

44
00:03:02,090 --> 00:03:03,170
It's now inside of us.

45
00:03:03,170 --> 00:03:08,300
We should be able to freely get access to the user service and use it to look up some particular user.

46
00:03:09,350 --> 00:03:13,970
So now inside of us itself, we're going to take a look at the incoming request object.

47
00:03:14,000 --> 00:03:19,520
We're going to access the session, object from it, and pull off the user ID property.

48
00:03:19,520 --> 00:03:21,590
So req dot session.

49
00:03:21,680 --> 00:03:26,930
And just in case the user does not yet have a session object, I'm going to put in an or empty object

50
00:03:26,930 --> 00:03:27,710
like so.

51
00:03:27,890 --> 00:03:32,390
So if there is no session object, no problem, just create an empty object that's going to make sure

52
00:03:32,390 --> 00:03:37,010
that we do not try to destructure user ID from what might be an undefined value.

53
00:03:38,750 --> 00:03:45,490
Then we'll say if user ID is defined, let's go ahead and look up that user inside of our user service.

54
00:03:45,500 --> 00:03:47,690
So we'll say user.

55
00:03:48,680 --> 00:03:55,790
Is await this user's service, find one and pass in the user ID.

56
00:04:00,000 --> 00:04:01,260
I'll then take that user.

57
00:04:02,010 --> 00:04:09,360
And assign it to the request current user property so req dot current user is user.

58
00:04:10,230 --> 00:04:15,450
We're going to get an air from that for right now, right above, I'm going to put in a comment that

59
00:04:15,450 --> 00:04:17,580
says at Ignore.

60
00:04:17,610 --> 00:04:19,620
So we're going to come back to that in address in just a little bit.

61
00:04:19,620 --> 00:04:20,670
For right now, totally fine.

62
00:04:20,670 --> 00:04:21,750
We're going to ignore it.

63
00:04:23,510 --> 00:04:28,700
So then after doing all that current user stuff, we've executed everything we need to inside of our

64
00:04:28,700 --> 00:04:29,230
middleware.

65
00:04:29,240 --> 00:04:33,080
So we're going to tell our next middleware that it needs to now run.

66
00:04:33,080 --> 00:04:37,190
So finally at the very bottom, we're going to call next, which means, okay, that's it for this use

67
00:04:37,190 --> 00:04:37,520
function.

68
00:04:37,520 --> 00:04:38,510
That's it for this middleware.

69
00:04:38,540 --> 00:04:41,690
Go ahead and run whatever other middleware might exist.

70
00:04:42,520 --> 00:04:43,720
And that should be it.

71
00:04:44,440 --> 00:04:48,430
So now we need to actually wire up our middleware and make sure that it gets executed at some point

72
00:04:48,430 --> 00:04:49,170
in time.

73
00:04:49,240 --> 00:04:53,440
So for that, we're going to use the exact same strategy that we used back inside of our app module.

74
00:04:53,470 --> 00:04:58,480
So you might recall that back inside of our app module, we defined the configure function configure

75
00:04:58,510 --> 00:05:02,980
gets called automatically, and that's where we can wire up some global middleware to be applied to

76
00:05:02,980 --> 00:05:04,780
all the different routes of our project.

77
00:05:04,930 --> 00:05:08,230
So we're going to do the same thing but back inside of our users module.

78
00:05:09,860 --> 00:05:11,780
So inside of our users module.

79
00:05:12,880 --> 00:05:14,830
At the very top I'm going to import.

80
00:05:17,080 --> 00:05:19,000
Current user middleware.

81
00:05:21,020 --> 00:05:21,770
From.

82
00:05:22,860 --> 00:05:24,030
Middlewares.

83
00:05:24,060 --> 00:05:27,090
Current user dot middleware.

84
00:05:29,840 --> 00:05:33,950
We will delete the current user interceptor in just a moment, but I'm going to leave it there for right

85
00:05:33,950 --> 00:05:34,520
now.

86
00:05:36,160 --> 00:05:41,500
Then from Nestjs common I'm going to import middleware consumer.

87
00:05:43,710 --> 00:05:46,800
And then finally, inside of our module itself, I'm going to define.

88
00:05:48,150 --> 00:05:54,360
The can configure function, which is going to be called with our consumer.

89
00:05:57,600 --> 00:06:01,590
And then we will wire up the middleware in the exact same way that we did back inside of our app module.

90
00:06:01,590 --> 00:06:03,930
So we'll say consumer dot apply.

91
00:06:04,750 --> 00:06:11,170
Our current user middleware dot four routes and we want to run this for all the different routes inside

92
00:06:11,170 --> 00:06:15,610
of our application because for every single route, if the current user exists or the user is signed

93
00:06:15,610 --> 00:06:19,430
in, we want to figure out who that user is and assign them to our request object.

94
00:06:19,450 --> 00:06:21,760
So I'll put in a star like so.

95
00:06:23,680 --> 00:06:24,540
Okay, so that looks good.

96
00:06:24,550 --> 00:06:26,130
So we've now wired up our middleware.

97
00:06:26,140 --> 00:06:30,030
I'm now going to go back through and delete all the stuff related to our interceptor.

98
00:06:30,040 --> 00:06:35,380
So right above inside of our list of providers right here, I'm going to delete that interceptor.

99
00:06:37,940 --> 00:06:41,000
And I'll also delete the interceptor import at the top.

100
00:06:43,020 --> 00:06:47,940
And then finally, we will delete the app Interceptor global from Nestjs core.

101
00:06:49,280 --> 00:06:49,610
Okay.

102
00:06:49,610 --> 00:06:51,200
So we should be good to go.

103
00:06:51,230 --> 00:06:55,430
So now, whenever any request comes into our entire application, we're going to run our current user

104
00:06:55,430 --> 00:06:56,090
middleware.

105
00:06:56,210 --> 00:07:00,350
Hopefully this is going to run after the cookies session middleware will.

106
00:07:00,350 --> 00:07:01,910
And to be honest with you, it is.

107
00:07:01,910 --> 00:07:03,610
It's going to run after cookie session.

108
00:07:03,620 --> 00:07:05,420
So cookie session is going to run first.

109
00:07:05,420 --> 00:07:08,750
It's going to set that session object on the request.

110
00:07:08,900 --> 00:07:13,040
We're then going to look up the current user, assign them to our request object, and then hopefully

111
00:07:13,040 --> 00:07:15,560
our admin guard should run after that.

112
00:07:15,920 --> 00:07:19,850
So let's now save everything, then go back over to our request.

113
00:07:21,140 --> 00:07:26,410
Client around reports, and I'm going to attempt to approve an existing report once again.

114
00:07:26,420 --> 00:07:27,830
So I'll send the request off.

115
00:07:27,830 --> 00:07:31,040
And it looks like this time it actually worked perfect.

116
00:07:31,620 --> 00:07:33,450
So I have approved a false right here.

117
00:07:33,450 --> 00:07:36,610
That's just because I put in and approved of false on the outgoing request.

118
00:07:36,630 --> 00:07:38,730
I'm going to change it to approved of true.

119
00:07:39,030 --> 00:07:40,350
Send the request off again.

120
00:07:40,350 --> 00:07:40,950
And now I see.

121
00:07:40,950 --> 00:07:41,370
Approved.

122
00:07:41,400 --> 00:07:42,000
True.

123
00:07:42,830 --> 00:07:44,250
All right, So this looks great.

124
00:07:44,270 --> 00:07:48,170
So we've now refactored that interceptor to be a middleware instead.

125
00:07:48,320 --> 00:07:53,810
So like I said, this is a little gotcha around middleware guards and interceptors, something that

126
00:07:53,810 --> 00:07:58,880
you might not quite expect because the documentation really does not make the order in which these things

127
00:07:58,880 --> 00:08:00,620
get executed super clear.

128
00:08:01,260 --> 00:08:04,050
So now that we've got all this stuff put together, we're going to go back through.

129
00:08:04,050 --> 00:08:06,210
There's a couple of things we kind of brushed over.

130
00:08:06,210 --> 00:08:10,920
For example, you might have noticed that on the middleware itself, we put in that ignore comment right

131
00:08:10,920 --> 00:08:11,250
there.

132
00:08:11,250 --> 00:08:14,190
So just a couple of very small things I want to very quickly fix up.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,110 --> 00:00:02,550
Back inside of our current user middleware.

2
00:00:02,580 --> 00:00:07,000
We're going to fix up this little error around TypeScript, where we fixed it just a moment ago by adding

3
00:00:07,000 --> 00:00:09,690
in this comment, telling TypeScript to just ignore the problem.

4
00:00:09,690 --> 00:00:13,500
So I'm going to remove that comment and now we get the error back.

5
00:00:13,500 --> 00:00:17,940
So the reason we're seeing this error is that we are dealing with some request object that is defined

6
00:00:17,940 --> 00:00:19,040
by express.

7
00:00:19,050 --> 00:00:23,970
So Express exports, an interface that describes exactly what a request object is and the different

8
00:00:23,970 --> 00:00:25,290
properties it has.

9
00:00:25,380 --> 00:00:29,040
A request object by default does not have a current user property.

10
00:00:29,040 --> 00:00:33,540
So we are trying to assign some value to a property that just does not exist.

11
00:00:33,720 --> 00:00:38,310
So to fix this up, we're going to modify the definition of the request interface.

12
00:00:38,340 --> 00:00:43,080
We're going to tell TypeScript that the request interface, as it is defined by Express, now has an

13
00:00:43,080 --> 00:00:49,860
additional property of current user, and it's going to possibly refer to some kind of user entity object.

14
00:00:50,250 --> 00:00:55,200
So to fix all this stuff up at the top of the file, I'm going to import user.

15
00:00:56,030 --> 00:00:58,870
From up one directory user entity.

16
00:00:58,880 --> 00:01:01,430
So that is our definition of what a user is.

17
00:01:01,430 --> 00:01:02,900
That's our user entity.

18
00:01:03,080 --> 00:01:07,910
We're then going to add in a little bit of code right above the middleware to tell TypeScript that this

19
00:01:07,910 --> 00:01:13,210
request might have a current user property that is going to be an instance of a user.

20
00:01:13,220 --> 00:01:15,770
So we're going to add in declare global.

21
00:01:17,210 --> 00:01:22,100
Then name Space Express with a capital E.

22
00:01:22,790 --> 00:01:25,640
Inside there, we'll say interface request.

23
00:01:25,940 --> 00:01:27,860
And then finally, current user.

24
00:01:28,250 --> 00:01:29,270
Question mark.

25
00:01:29,300 --> 00:01:30,140
User.

26
00:01:30,860 --> 00:01:35,220
So this is going to update or add an additional property to an existing interface.

27
00:01:35,240 --> 00:01:40,880
So we're saying go and find the Express library, find the interface called request inside there, and

28
00:01:40,880 --> 00:01:44,240
we're going to add in one more property to that interface.

29
00:01:44,450 --> 00:01:47,420
So we're going to say that in request object might have.

30
00:01:47,450 --> 00:01:53,000
So it's an optional a current user property and if it is defined, it's going to be set to a user entity

31
00:01:53,000 --> 00:01:53,900
instance.

32
00:01:55,400 --> 00:01:58,970
Once we add that in, we can then scroll back down and we'll see that the error down here has now gone

33
00:01:58,970 --> 00:01:59,580
away.

34
00:01:59,600 --> 00:02:01,100
So that looks pretty good.

35
00:02:01,860 --> 00:02:02,250
Okay.

36
00:02:02,250 --> 00:02:05,250
So I think that we've got everything around the middleware set up.

37
00:02:06,240 --> 00:02:10,440
The last thing that is still a little bit funky inside of our application is the fact that inside of

38
00:02:10,440 --> 00:02:11,910
our user entity.

39
00:02:12,060 --> 00:02:13,760
So here's the user entity file.

40
00:02:13,770 --> 00:02:17,790
We are still saying that everyone who signs up for our application is going to be an administrator by

41
00:02:17,790 --> 00:02:20,100
default, so we will eventually change that.

42
00:02:20,100 --> 00:02:23,790
But for right now, I'm just going to leave it in here just to make testing our application a little

43
00:02:23,790 --> 00:02:24,570
bit easier.

44
00:02:25,260 --> 00:02:25,520
Okay.

45
00:02:25,530 --> 00:02:28,310
So that's pretty much it around authorization for right now.

46
00:02:28,320 --> 00:02:32,880
So we're going to take a quick pause and then move on to our next route handler in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,460 --> 00:00:03,080
Time to move on to our very last route.

2
00:00:03,080 --> 00:00:06,950
Handler So in this video, we're going to focus on putting together the jet reports route.

3
00:00:06,980 --> 00:00:09,500
Handler And remember, in this request.

4
00:00:09,500 --> 00:00:13,250
Handler we're going to receive some information inside the query string of the request.

5
00:00:13,250 --> 00:00:16,070
So that will include include the make model year and so on.

6
00:00:16,100 --> 00:00:20,770
The goal of this handler is to generate an estimate for the value of a car.

7
00:00:20,780 --> 00:00:24,050
So the car that is specified inside that query string.

8
00:00:24,470 --> 00:00:28,040
So we're going to need to validate all the information inside that query string.

9
00:00:28,040 --> 00:00:32,780
We're going to do this validation by creating a DTO in the exact same way that we have previously validated

10
00:00:32,780 --> 00:00:36,000
the bodies of incoming post or patch requests.

11
00:00:36,050 --> 00:00:41,540
So let's first get started by creating a DTO that's going to validate the query string on this incoming

12
00:00:41,540 --> 00:00:42,200
request.

13
00:00:42,970 --> 00:00:45,590
So back inside my editor, I'm going to find the reports directory.

14
00:00:45,610 --> 00:00:53,840
Inside there, I'll find DTOs and I'm going to make a new file called git estimate dot dto ts.

15
00:00:57,060 --> 00:01:01,440
Now, one thing you might notice is that for get estimate, all these different properties right here

16
00:01:01,470 --> 00:01:06,570
are just about identical in nature to our request where we expect to receive a new report.

17
00:01:06,570 --> 00:01:09,350
So we still want to have make model year mileage and so on.

18
00:01:09,360 --> 00:01:13,490
So we've already created a DTO that is going to be very similar to the one that we need.

19
00:01:13,500 --> 00:01:20,070
So just to save a little bit of time, let's copy the Create report DTO over into this get estimate

20
00:01:20,070 --> 00:01:20,820
DTO.

21
00:01:22,060 --> 00:01:28,930
So I'm going to find the Create report DTO I'm going to copy everything inside there and paste it inside

22
00:01:28,930 --> 00:01:31,720
this new get estimate DTO file.

23
00:01:33,170 --> 00:01:36,830
We'll then go through this thing and remove 1 or 2 properties that we don't need.

24
00:01:37,730 --> 00:01:42,170
So at the very top, I'm going to find the class with a name of Create report, DTO we should probably

25
00:01:42,170 --> 00:01:42,770
change that.

26
00:01:42,770 --> 00:01:45,170
So I'll change it to Git estimate.

27
00:01:47,500 --> 00:01:48,190
DTO.

28
00:01:48,900 --> 00:01:54,090
So then whenever we are trying to get an estimate for a vehicle, we definitely need to know the make

29
00:01:54,120 --> 00:01:58,670
the model the year mileage, longitude and latitude.

30
00:01:58,680 --> 00:02:02,280
But we definitely do not need to know the price because that's what we are trying to return.

31
00:02:02,280 --> 00:02:07,440
We are trying to estimate the value of a car so we are not going to receive a price property.

32
00:02:07,470 --> 00:02:09,120
So I'm going to delete that entire thing.

33
00:02:10,410 --> 00:02:11,820
Okay, so that looks good.

34
00:02:12,210 --> 00:02:15,600
So now we can go back over to our controller and define our route handler.

35
00:02:15,630 --> 00:02:19,740
We're going to make sure that whenever we receive a request, we validate all the information inside

36
00:02:19,740 --> 00:02:22,740
the query string using the get estimate DTO.

37
00:02:23,960 --> 00:02:25,010
So I'm going to close this.

38
00:02:25,010 --> 00:02:27,770
I'm going to go back over to my reports controller.

39
00:02:30,420 --> 00:02:31,290
At the very top.

40
00:02:31,290 --> 00:02:38,100
I'm going to find my import from Nestjs Common and I'm going to get the git decorator and the query

41
00:02:38,130 --> 00:02:39,450
decorator as well.

42
00:02:39,810 --> 00:02:45,240
So the git, as we are very well established, we know we make use of that to declare a Git request

43
00:02:45,240 --> 00:02:49,920
route handler and then the query decorator is used to extract information out of a query string of a

44
00:02:49,920 --> 00:02:50,670
request.

45
00:02:51,670 --> 00:02:53,680
I'm also going to make sure that I import.

46
00:02:55,670 --> 00:02:57,560
The Jit estimate.

47
00:02:59,110 --> 00:03:00,700
DTO as well.

48
00:03:06,080 --> 00:03:10,760
Okay, so now down inside of our controller, I'm going to define a new route handler and I'll call

49
00:03:10,760 --> 00:03:10,940
this.

50
00:03:10,940 --> 00:03:13,280
How about create estimate?

51
00:03:13,370 --> 00:03:15,620
Maybe a better name would be get estimate.

52
00:03:17,900 --> 00:03:20,180
I'll mark it with the Jit decorator.

53
00:03:20,720 --> 00:03:25,940
I'm then going to say that I want to get some information out of the query string of the incoming request.

54
00:03:26,650 --> 00:03:28,300
I'm going to call this query.

55
00:03:28,940 --> 00:03:33,140
And I'm going to apply our type annotation, which is also going to be used as the set of validation

56
00:03:33,140 --> 00:03:36,670
rules of get estimate DTO.

57
00:03:39,110 --> 00:03:39,500
Okay.

58
00:03:39,590 --> 00:03:42,040
Would like to test out our code as it stands right now.

59
00:03:42,050 --> 00:03:46,430
I want to try to make a request that has a query string and want to make sure that we get some actual

60
00:03:46,430 --> 00:03:47,780
validation done against that.

61
00:03:47,790 --> 00:03:51,770
DTO, we're going to very quickly see that we run into a very small issue that we're going to need to

62
00:03:51,770 --> 00:03:52,420
fix.

63
00:03:52,430 --> 00:03:53,960
So I'm going to save this file.

64
00:03:54,050 --> 00:03:56,300
I'm then going to open up my client.

65
00:03:57,740 --> 00:04:00,350
At the very bottom, I'm going to add in a new section.

66
00:04:01,350 --> 00:04:05,880
And I'll say git an estimate for an existing.

67
00:04:07,160 --> 00:04:07,760
Vehicle.

68
00:04:08,180 --> 00:04:10,070
So this will be a get request.

69
00:04:11,760 --> 00:04:14,670
To localhost 3000 slash reports.

70
00:04:14,670 --> 00:04:19,500
And then I'm going to add on a query string with a question mark and we'll add on all the different

71
00:04:19,500 --> 00:04:23,420
properties we need, such as make model year mileage and so on.

72
00:04:23,430 --> 00:04:27,060
Each of these different properties are going to be a set of key value pairs and we're going to separate

73
00:04:27,060 --> 00:04:28,440
each of them with an ampersand.

74
00:04:28,440 --> 00:04:30,180
So let me just give you an example.

75
00:04:30,510 --> 00:04:33,690
I'm going to put in a make of Toyota.

76
00:04:34,550 --> 00:04:36,680
And so that's the ampersand right there.

77
00:04:36,710 --> 00:04:37,820
Don't miss the end.

78
00:04:38,660 --> 00:04:40,850
A model of Corolla.

79
00:04:41,690 --> 00:04:45,040
I'll then repeat the same pattern to add in the longitude and latitude.

80
00:04:45,050 --> 00:04:46,820
So I'll use a longitude of zero.

81
00:04:47,120 --> 00:04:48,530
A latitude of zero.

82
00:04:49,750 --> 00:04:52,420
A mileage of about 20,000.

83
00:04:54,900 --> 00:04:58,170
And a year of 1980.

84
00:05:00,000 --> 00:05:01,530
So there's my entire query string.

85
00:05:01,530 --> 00:05:05,910
And as far as I can tell, all the values I have provided definitely look valid.

86
00:05:05,940 --> 00:05:09,860
They definitely satisfy all the different validation rules we put together inside the DTO.

87
00:05:09,870 --> 00:05:13,890
But as I mentioned, as soon as we send off this request, we are going to run it into a very small

88
00:05:13,890 --> 00:05:14,460
error.

89
00:05:14,550 --> 00:05:15,850
So I'm going to save this.

90
00:05:15,870 --> 00:05:18,900
I'm going to send off the request and let's just see what happens.

91
00:05:19,080 --> 00:05:22,410
Sure enough, we get back a response with a couple of errors inside of it.

92
00:05:22,440 --> 00:05:27,780
So these errors are saying that I need to provide a year and it needs to be a number.

93
00:05:28,550 --> 00:05:31,130
It sure seems to me like I provided a valid year right here.

94
00:05:31,130 --> 00:05:33,690
But definitely there's some disagreement by our validator.

95
00:05:33,710 --> 00:05:35,320
Same thing for mileage as well.

96
00:05:35,330 --> 00:05:40,280
So definitely provided mileage, but unfortunately, I'm getting some validation errors around that

97
00:05:40,280 --> 00:05:41,210
mileage property.

98
00:05:41,210 --> 00:05:42,470
So what's going on here?

99
00:05:42,560 --> 00:05:47,690
Well, just as a quick reminder, whenever you send a query string inside of a request, all the information

100
00:05:47,690 --> 00:05:52,400
inside of here is treated as a string and it is received by our server as a string.

101
00:05:52,400 --> 00:05:57,050
Even though this really looks like a number to you and Nest doesn't necessarily know that it really

102
00:05:57,050 --> 00:05:57,950
is a number.

103
00:05:57,950 --> 00:06:00,290
So it's not going to automatically parse it for you.

104
00:06:00,290 --> 00:06:02,630
It's not going to turn it from a string into a number.

105
00:06:02,720 --> 00:06:08,030
So you and I, at some point in time during our request lifecycle, we need to take a look at this query

106
00:06:08,030 --> 00:06:08,510
string.

107
00:06:08,510 --> 00:06:14,060
We need to look up very certain properties inside the query string and parse whatever values are given

108
00:06:14,060 --> 00:06:15,800
from a string into a number.

109
00:06:16,160 --> 00:06:20,960
You will notice that this validation requirement or this parsing requirement doesn't really seem to

110
00:06:20,960 --> 00:06:23,540
apply to longitude and latitude.

111
00:06:23,570 --> 00:06:27,440
The reason for that is that the validation decorators we are using.

112
00:06:29,300 --> 00:06:29,960
Back inside of our.

113
00:06:31,250 --> 00:06:35,270
We made use of a is longitude and is latitude decorator.

114
00:06:35,270 --> 00:06:40,370
So these two decorators are going to correctly handle any time that they receive a string.

115
00:06:40,550 --> 00:06:46,070
Nonetheless, you will notice that inside of our DTO, we annotated longitude as being a number and

116
00:06:46,070 --> 00:06:50,510
latitude is being a number, even though we are definitely receiving them through the query string as

117
00:06:50,510 --> 00:06:51,480
a string.

118
00:06:51,500 --> 00:06:56,120
So even though we are not getting any errors around longitude and latitude right now, we still might

119
00:06:56,120 --> 00:07:01,160
want to parse these values as well because, well, the rest of our application, because of TypeScript

120
00:07:01,160 --> 00:07:04,610
and because we have told TypeScript that these are going to be numbers, everything else inside of our

121
00:07:04,610 --> 00:07:09,350
app is going to assume that these are numbers, even though when our application is actually running

122
00:07:09,350 --> 00:07:12,650
and we receive a request, we are going to receive them as strings.

123
00:07:13,010 --> 00:07:17,050
So long story short here, we need to do a little bit of a parsing step.

124
00:07:17,060 --> 00:07:21,620
We need to make sure that a bunch of different properties inside of here are turned from a string as

125
00:07:21,620 --> 00:07:24,070
they are inside the query string into a number.

126
00:07:24,080 --> 00:07:26,240
So let's get on that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,240 --> 00:00:06,490
I bet you can guess how we're going to make sure that the longitude, latitude, mileage and year all

2
00:00:06,490 --> 00:00:09,430
get turned into numbers before they flow into our application.

3
00:00:09,460 --> 00:00:12,670
Yep, you guessed it, we're going to use yet another decorator.

4
00:00:12,850 --> 00:00:15,860
So we're going to go back over to our git estimate DTO.

5
00:00:18,080 --> 00:00:24,080
And then at the top, in addition to importing class validator, we are also going to import the transform

6
00:00:24,080 --> 00:00:25,070
decorator.

7
00:00:25,720 --> 00:00:27,760
From class transformer.

8
00:00:29,360 --> 00:00:34,730
This transform decorator is going to allow us to receive an incoming value, such as, say, a string

9
00:00:34,730 --> 00:00:40,160
from the incoming request, and then do some processing on it or transformation on it and allow us to

10
00:00:40,160 --> 00:00:45,990
turn that value into some other kind of value before it ever gets assigned and validated on our DTO.

11
00:00:46,750 --> 00:00:49,000
So let's go to our first property.

12
00:00:49,030 --> 00:00:54,460
So here, right here, right above all the existing decorators, I'm going to apply.

13
00:00:54,850 --> 00:00:56,020
Transform.

14
00:00:57,100 --> 00:01:01,120
We're then going to pass in a function that is going to be called with an object that has a couple of

15
00:01:01,120 --> 00:01:02,110
different properties.

16
00:01:02,140 --> 00:01:06,820
Now, you might recall that we made use of the transform decorator just a little bit ago, the last

17
00:01:06,820 --> 00:01:07,720
time we made use of this.

18
00:01:07,750 --> 00:01:11,380
We received the entire object or essentially the entire DTO.

19
00:01:11,410 --> 00:01:14,890
This time around, we're going to receive just the year property.

20
00:01:15,040 --> 00:01:20,440
So to receive just the property that we are applying the transform decorator to, we are going to destructure

21
00:01:20,440 --> 00:01:22,330
off value.

22
00:01:23,790 --> 00:01:27,960
So value is going to be the incoming year in the string form.

23
00:01:28,050 --> 00:01:32,340
So we're going to take that year and we're going to parse it into an integer and then return it from

24
00:01:32,340 --> 00:01:33,210
this function.

25
00:01:33,210 --> 00:01:36,880
That parsed integer will then be assigned to the year property.

26
00:01:36,900 --> 00:01:42,510
So I'm going to call inside of here, parse int and pass in value and that's it.

27
00:01:43,330 --> 00:01:47,770
So if I now save this, go back over to my client and make the request again.

28
00:01:47,770 --> 00:01:53,050
I'm no longer going to see any validation errors around here because I've now correctly parsed it into

29
00:01:53,050 --> 00:01:53,950
an integer.

30
00:01:54,310 --> 00:01:59,620
So let's now do the same thing to mileage and to the longitude and latitude as well.

31
00:02:00,470 --> 00:02:06,710
So back inside the DTO again, I'm going to copy that entire decorator and apply it to mileage.

32
00:02:08,090 --> 00:02:10,640
I'll then go down to longitude and latitude.

33
00:02:10,670 --> 00:02:15,440
Now, as I mentioned, we probably want to turn these things into numbers just because that is how the

34
00:02:15,440 --> 00:02:18,560
entire rest of our application is set up in every location.

35
00:02:18,560 --> 00:02:22,260
We are always assuming that longitude and latitude are going to be numbers.

36
00:02:22,310 --> 00:02:27,080
But remember, a longitude and latitude can easily be a number with a decimal on it.

37
00:02:27,950 --> 00:02:30,740
Up here we are parsing these values using parse int.

38
00:02:30,770 --> 00:02:35,570
That's going to give us back an integer, which is going to be a whole number, either positive or negative.

39
00:02:35,600 --> 00:02:37,700
In other words, a number without a decimal.

40
00:02:38,030 --> 00:02:43,200
So for latitude and longitude, we're going to slightly change this transform function.

41
00:02:43,250 --> 00:02:50,720
Instead of calling parse int, we are going to parse float and I'm then going to duplicate that down

42
00:02:50,810 --> 00:02:53,030
onto our latitude property as well.

43
00:02:54,060 --> 00:02:55,440
Okay, that should be it.

44
00:02:55,620 --> 00:02:58,590
So now I'm going to go back over to my controller very quickly.

45
00:02:58,980 --> 00:03:04,020
Inside my controller, I'm going to find our request handler and I'm going to now console log out,

46
00:03:04,050 --> 00:03:06,120
whatever that query object is.

47
00:03:07,340 --> 00:03:09,170
I'll then go back over to my client.

48
00:03:09,680 --> 00:03:13,460
I'm going to send off the request again, and this time we're not going to get any kind of error sent

49
00:03:13,460 --> 00:03:14,060
back to us.

50
00:03:14,090 --> 00:03:16,120
We get a entirely empty response.

51
00:03:16,130 --> 00:03:20,240
That's why I wanted to add on that console log just to make sure that we're now receiving all the correct

52
00:03:20,240 --> 00:03:20,780
data.

53
00:03:20,870 --> 00:03:28,320
So I'm going to go back to my terminal and just make sure that I'm receiving that entire query string.

54
00:03:28,340 --> 00:03:33,950
So sure enough, I've got my make and model longitude and latitude, mileage and year and the four latter

55
00:03:33,950 --> 00:03:36,130
properties on there are all numbers.

56
00:03:36,140 --> 00:03:36,980
Perfect.

57
00:03:37,830 --> 00:03:38,100
Okay.

58
00:03:38,100 --> 00:03:42,510
So now that we are receiving all this information, we're going to use it to somehow generate an estimate

59
00:03:42,510 --> 00:03:44,600
for a car with these attributes.

60
00:03:44,610 --> 00:03:47,730
So let's figure out how we're going to generate that estimate in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,160 --> 00:00:05,300
Now that we've got our search criteria inside of our request handler, we need to actually take this

2
00:00:05,300 --> 00:00:10,220
criteria and generate some kind of estimate for the value of a user's car and then eventually return

3
00:00:10,220 --> 00:00:10,670
it.

4
00:00:10,790 --> 00:00:15,100
All the logic for this is going to be placed inside of our reports service.

5
00:00:15,110 --> 00:00:19,580
So let's take a look at a diagram and just understand exactly how we're going to execute the logic that's

6
00:00:19,580 --> 00:00:21,020
going to give us an estimate.

7
00:00:21,720 --> 00:00:23,280
Okay, so here's the general idea.

8
00:00:23,550 --> 00:00:27,180
The first thing I want you to understand here is that in generating an estimate, we're going to use

9
00:00:27,180 --> 00:00:29,880
a methodology that is not super precise.

10
00:00:29,910 --> 00:00:34,020
In other words, our estimates are not going to be the most accurate thing in the world.

11
00:00:34,050 --> 00:00:38,490
The real goal here is, of course, not really to help you estimate the value of a car, but I want

12
00:00:38,490 --> 00:00:42,700
to show you some inner workings of type and how to make interesting queries with it.

13
00:00:42,720 --> 00:00:45,870
