So I'm much more interested in how we're going to actually execute this query.

14
00:00:45,900 --> 00:00:49,350
Then the validity of the query itself in the real world.

15
00:00:49,800 --> 00:00:50,100
Okay.

16
00:00:50,100 --> 00:00:52,330
So with that in mind, here's what we are going to do.

17
00:00:52,350 --> 00:00:55,070
We are going to take the estimate criteria.

18
00:00:55,080 --> 00:00:58,910
So that is essentially the information that we just pulled out of our query string.

19
00:00:58,920 --> 00:01:02,610
We've got make model longitude, latitude, year and mileage.

20
00:01:03,820 --> 00:01:05,080
Using that information.

21
00:01:05,080 --> 00:01:09,490
We're then going to do a search over all of our different reports we have inside of our database.

22
00:01:09,640 --> 00:01:12,220
Our search is going to go through the following steps.

23
00:01:12,250 --> 00:01:16,500
We're going to find all the reports with the exact same make and model.

24
00:01:16,510 --> 00:01:20,740
So we want to find all existing reports for, say, a Toyota Corolla.

25
00:01:21,630 --> 00:01:27,960
We're then going to find all the reports that were made within plus or minus five degrees of the estimate,

26
00:01:27,990 --> 00:01:29,650
longitude and latitude.

27
00:01:29,670 --> 00:01:34,860
So in other words, if a user is looking to sell their car at these coordinates on the planet, we want

28
00:01:34,860 --> 00:01:40,980
to find all reports that are within plus or minus five degrees of that point, which is roughly about

29
00:01:40,980 --> 00:01:42,870
600 miles or so.

30
00:01:43,640 --> 00:01:45,110
Or see me think it's 300 miles?

31
00:01:45,110 --> 00:01:45,280
Yeah.

32
00:01:45,300 --> 00:01:47,120
Think it's close to 300 miles of.

33
00:01:47,150 --> 00:01:48,080
That location.

34
00:01:49,100 --> 00:01:53,930
We're then going to find all the reports that were made on vehicles within three years of manufacture.

35
00:01:53,930 --> 00:01:59,330
So in other words, we're going to consider if the value that is provided to us where we're trying to

36
00:01:59,330 --> 00:02:05,000
generate the estimate for is 1980, we're going to find all reports for Toyota Corollas that were created

37
00:02:05,000 --> 00:02:07,700
between 1977 and 1983.

38
00:02:08,630 --> 00:02:14,570
Finally, we're then going to subtract the difference of each of the report's mileage against our estimate

39
00:02:14,570 --> 00:02:17,690
mileage, and then we're going to order by that difference.

40
00:02:17,720 --> 00:02:21,650
So we're going to find all the vehicles with the closest mileage possible.

41
00:02:22,130 --> 00:02:24,500
We're going to order by that closest mileage.

42
00:02:24,680 --> 00:02:29,900
And then eventually, after this entire query, we're going to be hopefully left with a series of different

43
00:02:29,900 --> 00:02:30,800
reports.

44
00:02:31,040 --> 00:02:36,470
So these should be reports that are very similar in nature to the car that the user is trying to sell.

45
00:02:37,390 --> 00:02:41,880
We're going to take the top three closest reports and we're going to average their value.

46
00:02:41,890 --> 00:02:47,560
So how much ever these cars sold for, we're then going to take that average value and send it back

47
00:02:47,560 --> 00:02:49,210
to whoever made the request to us.

48
00:02:49,330 --> 00:02:51,880
And that's how we're going to generate our estimate.

49
00:02:52,060 --> 00:02:58,510
So again, not super great way of estimating the value of a car because we are really assuming that

50
00:02:58,510 --> 00:03:01,840
we've got a tremendous amount of data already inside of our database.

51
00:03:01,870 --> 00:03:08,230
We would need to have a tremendous number of cars in order to always be able to apply this search criteria

52
00:03:08,230 --> 00:03:10,570
and get back at least three reports.

53
00:03:10,570 --> 00:03:12,940
So perhaps not the most realistic thing in the world.

54
00:03:12,940 --> 00:03:17,290
But again, the real goal here is to show you how to write an interesting query with all these different

55
00:03:17,290 --> 00:03:19,330
filters and sorting logic on it.

56
00:03:20,360 --> 00:03:22,850
Okay, so with all this in mind, let's take a pause right here.

57
00:03:22,850 --> 00:03:24,110
We're gonna come back in the next video.

58
00:03:24,110 --> 00:03:29,180
We're going to get started on our reports service, and we're going to add in a new method that is going

59
00:03:29,180 --> 00:03:30,500
to generate this estimate.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# section 17: Query Builders with TypeORM

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:05,910
Now that we understand our estimate criteria of sorts and how we're going to execute this query, let's

2
00:00:05,910 --> 00:00:09,960
talk about how we're going to actually take this information and find some records from the database

3
00:00:09,960 --> 00:00:11,520
by using type ORM.

4
00:00:11,850 --> 00:00:12,360
Okay.

5
00:00:12,360 --> 00:00:16,240
So at this point in time, we've made use of the type ORM repository several times.

6
00:00:16,260 --> 00:00:19,800
Remember, this repository is created for us automatically behind the scenes.

7
00:00:20,070 --> 00:00:25,210
A repository has a couple of different methods tied to it, such as Create, save, find and find one.

8
00:00:25,230 --> 00:00:30,150
Now we can use, find and find one to execute very basic filtering steps.

9
00:00:30,150 --> 00:00:35,370
For example, we can find all the different records with a particular, say, email address or find

10
00:00:35,370 --> 00:00:37,860
one record record with a particular ID.

11
00:00:38,370 --> 00:00:42,270
But in our case, we need to execute a far more complicated query.

12
00:00:42,300 --> 00:00:47,010
We need to execute all these different filtering steps and then we eventually also need to do some kind

13
00:00:47,010 --> 00:00:48,750
of order by logic as well.

14
00:00:49,670 --> 00:00:55,010
So you and I are going to be using a different method on the repository referred to as Create Query

15
00:00:55,010 --> 00:00:55,730
Builder.

16
00:00:55,760 --> 00:01:00,770
This method is going to give us back an object that we can use to generate a very complex query.

17
00:01:00,980 --> 00:01:07,100
Now, got to be honest with you, the query builder object inside of type is rather complex and making

18
00:01:07,100 --> 00:01:10,490
use of it is really challenging if you don't have a background in SQL.

19
00:01:10,700 --> 00:01:14,260
If you're in this course, I'm not really assuming that you know very well.

20
00:01:14,270 --> 00:01:18,770
So in general, you and I are going to make use in this of this create query builder thing, but we're

21
00:01:18,770 --> 00:01:23,300
not going to go into too much detail around it again, just because it's really much more in the world

22
00:01:23,300 --> 00:01:25,340
of SQL than it is around Nest.

23
00:01:26,890 --> 00:01:28,440
So let's go back over to our editor.

24
00:01:28,450 --> 00:01:32,570
We're going to create a query builder inside of our reports service.

25
00:01:32,590 --> 00:01:37,300
This query builder will then have a lot of logic tied to it to execute all these different filtering

26
00:01:37,300 --> 00:01:37,960
steps.

27
00:01:38,680 --> 00:01:39,040
Okay.

28
00:01:39,040 --> 00:01:44,380
So back inside my editor, I'm still inside my controller, inside of our git estimate method, I'm

29
00:01:44,380 --> 00:01:45,970
going to call and return.

30
00:01:47,930 --> 00:01:50,240
This dot reports.

31
00:01:52,870 --> 00:01:53,470
Service.

32
00:01:53,500 --> 00:01:54,460
If can type there we go.

33
00:01:54,460 --> 00:01:59,410
So this dot report service and we're going to write out this entire query with a query builder thing

34
00:01:59,410 --> 00:02:02,860
inside of a method that we'll call create estimate.

35
00:02:02,860 --> 00:02:06,250
And I'm going to pass in our query string object into that thing.

36
00:02:06,520 --> 00:02:07,950
Let's now go into our service.

37
00:02:07,960 --> 00:02:11,920
We're going to define this method and then make use of a query builder inside of it.

38
00:02:13,370 --> 00:02:13,640
All right.

39
00:02:13,640 --> 00:02:17,330
So back over inside of our service at the very top, I'm going to import.

40
00:02:18,420 --> 00:02:21,180
The Jit estimate dto.

41
00:02:28,110 --> 00:02:32,040
We'll then define our new method inside of here of create estimate.

42
00:02:32,760 --> 00:02:36,740
I'm going to assume that this is going to receive an instance of that DTO.

43
00:02:36,810 --> 00:02:40,260
So I'm going to call it simply estimate DTO.

44
00:02:42,580 --> 00:02:45,940
And then inside of here, we are going to create and return.

45
00:02:47,050 --> 00:02:50,190
A query builder that we're going to generate by using our repository.

46
00:02:50,200 --> 00:02:53,800
So I'm going to call this dot repo dot create query builder.

47
00:02:54,960 --> 00:02:56,070
Now to use a query builder.

48
00:02:56,070 --> 00:03:00,300
We're going to chain on a couple of different method calls to it, and these different method calls

49
00:03:00,300 --> 00:03:02,950
are going to customize the query that we eventually make.

50
00:03:02,970 --> 00:03:06,720
So let me show you the most simple and basic query we can execute.

51
00:03:06,900 --> 00:03:13,380
I'm going to chain on Dot Select with a star and then git raw many.

52
00:03:14,680 --> 00:03:18,100
So this query is going to take a look at our reports table.

53
00:03:18,100 --> 00:03:22,240
It's going to return all the different reports and all the data that we have around all those different

54
00:03:22,240 --> 00:03:22,990
reports.

55
00:03:23,380 --> 00:03:24,650
I'm going to save this.

56
00:03:24,670 --> 00:03:28,840
I'm then going to go back over to my client and send off a request.

57
00:03:29,740 --> 00:03:30,880
I'll then see the response.

58
00:03:30,880 --> 00:03:33,340
Is all the data out of our reports table?

59
00:03:35,690 --> 00:03:37,070
Okay, so this looks good.

60
00:03:37,430 --> 00:03:42,260
So now we're going to start to chain on a couple of different method calls to create Query Builder.

61
00:03:42,260 --> 00:03:47,600
And each of these different chained on methods are going to apply either some filtering logic or some

62
00:03:47,600 --> 00:03:48,800
sorting criteria.

63
00:03:48,890 --> 00:03:54,050
So we need to do a little bit of filtering on those steps right there and then eventually do some sorting

64
00:03:54,050 --> 00:03:55,340
on that step right there.

65
00:03:56,010 --> 00:04:01,200
So we can apply a filtering step by chaining on a where statement.

66
00:04:01,960 --> 00:04:03,610
Inside of a dot where call.

67
00:04:03,610 --> 00:04:08,230
We can provide a string that's going to specify some kind of filter that we want to apply.

68
00:04:08,500 --> 00:04:15,010
So in this case, the first filter we're going to apply is to make sure that we only consider cars of

69
00:04:15,010 --> 00:04:16,360
a very specific make.

70
00:04:16,510 --> 00:04:21,130
So I'm going to say make equal to colon make.

71
00:04:22,620 --> 00:04:23,880
As a second argument.

72
00:04:23,910 --> 00:04:29,820
I'm then going to pass in an object with a make property of estimate dto dot make.

73
00:04:31,270 --> 00:04:34,030
Now, let's just forget the syntax inside of here for just a moment.

74
00:04:34,030 --> 00:04:35,290
I'm going to save this.

75
00:04:35,470 --> 00:04:41,110
Go back over to my client once again and make the request a second time.

76
00:04:41,500 --> 00:04:45,790
So now I'm going to only find vehicles with a make of Toyota.

77
00:04:46,790 --> 00:04:50,750
And as I look at my result set, I should only see Toyota listed inside of here.

78
00:04:52,470 --> 00:04:58,080
If I change the make property of my query string to something else, like, say, Ford, I don't have

79
00:04:58,080 --> 00:04:59,430
any Ford cars created yet.

80
00:04:59,430 --> 00:05:02,910
So now if I send off that request, I'm going to get back an empty result set.

81
00:05:03,680 --> 00:05:07,250
So it appears to me that we are definitely doing some filtering logic now.

82
00:05:07,310 --> 00:05:11,600
Let's take a pause right here, come back in just a moment and understand what the syntax around this

83
00:05:11,630 --> 00:05:13,730
where statement is really doing for us.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,130 --> 00:00:05,390
We just made use of the create query builder function inside of type ORM.

2
00:00:05,420 --> 00:00:07,730
So remember, this is tied to the repository.

3
00:00:07,760 --> 00:00:13,040
We make use of a query builder any time we want to run a more complex query than what we can run with,

4
00:00:13,070 --> 00:00:15,230
say the find or find one methods.

5
00:00:15,350 --> 00:00:17,990
So let's take a look at the code that we have written out here so far.

6
00:00:18,020 --> 00:00:20,960
The first statement I want to focus on is the where function.

7
00:00:20,990 --> 00:00:25,310
This is going to apply a filter over all the different rows of our reports table.

8
00:00:25,340 --> 00:00:31,310
So in this case, we are trying to find all the different reports where the make column or the make

9
00:00:31,310 --> 00:00:37,220
value for a given row is equal to some value that we want to use as our search criteria.

10
00:00:38,180 --> 00:00:44,330
So in our case, if I execute that query statement with a make of, say, Toyota, it's going to find

11
00:00:44,330 --> 00:00:49,700
all the rows inside of my reports table where I have a make of Toyota and it will filter out all the

12
00:00:49,700 --> 00:00:51,890
other rows that don't have a make of Toyota.

13
00:00:54,180 --> 00:00:58,590
You'll notice that in order to specify that filter, we're using some kind of weird syntax here.

14
00:00:58,590 --> 00:01:00,970
So first off, what's going on with the string?

15
00:01:00,990 --> 00:01:05,880
Well, on the left hand side, that specifically says take a look at the make column of every single

16
00:01:05,880 --> 00:01:12,450
row and we want to find all the columns where the make column is equal to some value that we're going

17
00:01:12,450 --> 00:01:13,980
to stick in to this string.

18
00:01:15,430 --> 00:01:19,660
So the value of colon make is going to come from this object.

19
00:01:20,540 --> 00:01:23,210
So behind the scenes type arm is going to take a look at this object.

20
00:01:23,210 --> 00:01:27,290
It's going to find the make property inside the object specifically because we have colon make right

21
00:01:27,290 --> 00:01:27,920
here.

22
00:01:28,010 --> 00:01:33,020
It's going to see that we have provided estimate dot make and it's going to take that value and we can

23
00:01:33,020 --> 00:01:36,620
kind of imagine that value gets inserted into the string right there.

24
00:01:38,410 --> 00:01:43,660
Now, the entire reason that we use this colon syntax in order to kind of substitute in a search parameter

25
00:01:43,660 --> 00:01:45,760
is just for security concerns.

26
00:01:45,790 --> 00:01:51,580
If you've ever heard of a SQL injection exploit, well, this is exactly how we address a SQL injection

27
00:01:51,580 --> 00:01:52,270
exploit.

28
00:01:52,300 --> 00:01:57,370
We're making sure that we are not sticking in a raw string that is coming from an incoming request directly

29
00:01:57,370 --> 00:01:59,080
into one of our SQL queries.

30
00:01:59,960 --> 00:02:05,180
In order to kind of condense this object right here, we can destructure off the make property from

31
00:02:05,180 --> 00:02:06,970
estimate ahead of time.

32
00:02:07,010 --> 00:02:10,370
So for example, I could just destructure out make.

33
00:02:11,030 --> 00:02:15,110
And now on this object right here, I can provide just curly braces.

34
00:02:15,110 --> 00:02:16,280
Braces, make.

35
00:02:18,480 --> 00:02:22,150
Okay, So now let's start to add on some additional filtering logic.

36
00:02:22,170 --> 00:02:26,430
In addition to getting cars with a certain make, I want to make sure I also get cars with a very certain

37
00:02:26,430 --> 00:02:27,580
model as well.

38
00:02:27,600 --> 00:02:30,630
So we need to add on a second where statement.

39
00:02:30,900 --> 00:02:34,190
Now this is where the query builder starts to get a little bit complex.

40
00:02:34,200 --> 00:02:40,200
If you call where a second time, the second where is going to override the first one.

41
00:02:40,290 --> 00:02:49,050
So if we add on something like where model is colon model and then model inside of a object and we also

42
00:02:49,050 --> 00:02:54,090
destructure off model that is going to override the initial where where we were trying to find cars

43
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:55,740
with a very specific make.

44
00:02:55,860 --> 00:03:01,230
So to chain on some additional where filtering statements, we use a slightly different statement or

45
00:03:01,230 --> 00:03:04,170
a slightly different method called and where.

46
00:03:06,210 --> 00:03:11,090
So if we ever need to call where more than once the first time, we're going to call simply where,

47
00:03:11,100 --> 00:03:14,580
and then each additional time we will invoke and where.

48
00:03:16,120 --> 00:03:21,700
So now we can do something very similar for our other search criteria as well, specifically for longitude,

49
00:03:21,700 --> 00:03:23,230
latitude and the year.

50
00:03:23,620 --> 00:03:29,890
Keep in mind that for these three different filtering statements, we really want to do not just a direct

51
00:03:29,890 --> 00:03:30,520
comparison.

52
00:03:30,520 --> 00:03:36,790
Instead, we want to find reports at a longitude and latitude within plus or minus five degrees.

53
00:03:37,060 --> 00:03:39,650
So our where statement is going to be slightly different for those.

54
00:03:39,670 --> 00:03:41,080
Let me show you what we're going to do.

55
00:03:41,410 --> 00:03:43,870
I'm going to chain on another and where?

56
00:03:45,060 --> 00:03:49,080
I'm then going to look at the longitude of every single row.

57
00:03:49,530 --> 00:03:53,460
I'm going to subtract from that colon longitude.

58
00:03:54,410 --> 00:03:57,840
I'm then going to provide that longitude parameter from our git estimate DTO.

59
00:03:59,370 --> 00:04:01,530
So that's going to result in some number.

60
00:04:01,530 --> 00:04:07,740
So it might be something like five, ten, 15, negative five or just taking the longitude of some report

61
00:04:07,740 --> 00:04:10,350
and then subtracting some other longitude from it.

62
00:04:10,560 --> 00:04:14,850
So I want to make sure that this is plus or minus five degrees.

63
00:04:14,880 --> 00:04:22,400
So to get that kind of variance in there, I'm going to add in between -5 and 5.

64
00:04:25,510 --> 00:04:28,600
I'm then going to do the same thing for Latitude as well.

65
00:04:28,960 --> 00:04:34,270
So I'm going to say copy that line down and then change it to latitude and latitude.

66
00:04:34,420 --> 00:04:37,270
And then on the object over here, latitude.

67
00:04:37,950 --> 00:04:38,670
As well.

68
00:04:39,360 --> 00:04:43,770
I'll also make sure that I destructure off the latitude property off our DTO.

69
00:04:46,480 --> 00:04:46,840
Okay.

70
00:04:46,840 --> 00:04:47,980
So then finally.

71
00:04:48,900 --> 00:04:52,530
We're going to add on very similar line for the year as well.

72
00:04:53,800 --> 00:04:55,960
So I'm going to paste that in once again.

73
00:04:55,960 --> 00:04:57,250
Change it to yea.

74
00:04:57,520 --> 00:05:01,210
Yea, I want to find plus or minus three years.

75
00:05:01,210 --> 00:05:04,690
So in this case it's going to be between -3 and 3.

76
00:05:05,020 --> 00:05:09,130
I'm going to change that object to year and destructure off the year property as well.

77
00:05:10,800 --> 00:05:14,400
And then the very last step is going to be our order by logic.

78
00:05:14,400 --> 00:05:20,640
So I want to find all the vehicles or reports or sort them by whichever vehicle has the closest mileage

79
00:05:20,640 --> 00:05:23,280
to the one that our user is trying to get an estimate for.

80
00:05:23,700 --> 00:05:24,870
So for this.

81
00:05:25,950 --> 00:05:29,070
I'm going to chain on an order by.

82
00:05:30,290 --> 00:05:36,500
We're going to take a look at the mileage of a given report and subtract the supplied mileage from it.

83
00:05:38,130 --> 00:05:43,320
Now, unfortunately, we want to use a parameter right here once again for security reasons, but unfortunately,

84
00:05:43,320 --> 00:05:46,080
a type API is not super consistent here.

85
00:05:46,080 --> 00:05:50,340
And the second argument does not allow us to pass in the mileage.

86
00:05:50,340 --> 00:05:54,720
So in this case, we're going to have to provide that mileage parameter in a slightly different way.

87
00:05:55,140 --> 00:05:58,380
I'm going to destructure off mileage at the top.

88
00:05:59,590 --> 00:06:04,750
And then to set that mileage parameter, I'm going to chain on set parameters.

89
00:06:05,750 --> 00:06:06,740
Mileage.

90
00:06:08,050 --> 00:06:12,970
And again that's just because order by does not take a parameter object as a second argument.

91
00:06:13,780 --> 00:06:17,590
I'm going to make sure that I sort the mileage in descending order.

92
00:06:18,440 --> 00:06:23,030
And then to also handle the case in which we have either a mileage that is greater or less than the

93
00:06:23,030 --> 00:06:24,290
one that we're looking for.

94
00:06:24,320 --> 00:06:29,090
I'm going to take the absolute value of the difference between the two.

95
00:06:31,100 --> 00:06:31,460
Okay.

96
00:06:31,460 --> 00:06:34,130
I know that I kind of sped through this query very quickly.

97
00:06:34,130 --> 00:06:35,540
This is everything we actually need right here.

98
00:06:35,540 --> 00:06:39,230
I know I sped through it very fast and didn't really explain a lot of stuff that's going on here.

99
00:06:39,230 --> 00:06:44,120
But as I mentioned just a little bit ago, all this query builder stuff is really built upon knowledge

100
00:06:44,120 --> 00:06:45,020
of SQL.

101
00:06:45,020 --> 00:06:48,530
And in this course we're not really super focused on per se.

102
00:06:48,560 --> 00:06:53,960
So I didn't want to have what would probably turn into like a 1 or 2 hour aside in order to very clearly

103
00:06:53,960 --> 00:06:56,210
understand every line of code right here.

104
00:06:56,210 --> 00:06:59,480
So we're pretty much just going to say all this stuff has to do with SQL.

105
00:06:59,480 --> 00:07:03,380
If you want to understand more about what's going on here, take a look at the documentation inside

106
00:07:03,380 --> 00:07:05,540
of type around the query builder.

107
00:07:08,650 --> 00:07:10,960
Now, the last thing we need to take care of here.

108
00:07:11,880 --> 00:07:15,780
We have taken care of all this filtering logic and done the order by step.

109
00:07:15,780 --> 00:07:20,490
So the very last thing we need to deal with is to make sure that we get back at most three different

110
00:07:20,490 --> 00:07:23,910
reports and find the average value of those three.

111
00:07:24,000 --> 00:07:25,860
So that's going to be the last thing we do.

112
00:07:26,580 --> 00:07:32,340
So to make sure that we get back only three reports right after the order by and set parameters, I'm

113
00:07:32,340 --> 00:07:37,860
going to chain on a limit three, which is going to make sure that we only take a look at the top three

114
00:07:37,860 --> 00:07:38,760
reports.

115
00:07:39,180 --> 00:07:44,280
I'm then going to modify the select statement up here to actually find the average price among these

116
00:07:44,280 --> 00:07:44,880
three.

117
00:07:45,000 --> 00:07:48,600
So I'm going to change the string to avg price.

118
00:07:49,210 --> 00:07:52,510
And then as the second argument, I'm going to put in a string of price as well.

119
00:07:55,470 --> 00:08:01,230
Finally, very last step we make use of git raw many if we expect to get back many different records.

120
00:08:01,260 --> 00:08:06,480
In this case, we are really condensing everything down to just one single row thanks to the average

121
00:08:06,480 --> 00:08:07,460
call right there.

122
00:08:07,470 --> 00:08:11,850
So rather than making use of git raw many, I'm going to change it to git raw one.

123
00:08:13,240 --> 00:08:14,530
Okay, so that's it?

124
00:08:14,530 --> 00:08:15,790
That's the entire query.

125
00:08:15,820 --> 00:08:17,060
Let's take a pause right here.

126
00:08:17,080 --> 00:08:18,760
We're going to come back in just a moment.

127
00:08:18,790 --> 00:08:23,360
We are going to create a couple of different reports where we know the actual values.

128
00:08:23,380 --> 00:08:27,180
So we're going to make some Toyota Corollas or just some kind of fixed car.

129
00:08:27,190 --> 00:08:31,900
Well, then run a couple of different queries and make sure that our query is working as expected.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:04,240
All right, time to test out our git estimate query.

2
00:00:04,270 --> 00:00:08,740
So the first thing we need to do is create a couple of different reports that are of the exact same

3
00:00:08,740 --> 00:00:14,950
make and model inside the correct longitude and latitude with the same year, or at least a very similar

4
00:00:14,950 --> 00:00:15,430
year.

5
00:00:15,460 --> 00:00:20,590
We'll then be able to test out our new Git estimate route handler down here and make sure that everything

6
00:00:20,590 --> 00:00:22,270
works as expected.

7
00:00:22,390 --> 00:00:26,230
So I'm going to first begin by changing the make to some other make vehicle.

8
00:00:26,230 --> 00:00:30,100
In this case, we'll go with a Ford and I'm going to change the model to Mustang.

9
00:00:30,960 --> 00:00:33,000
I'm going to stick with the year of 1980.

10
00:00:33,030 --> 00:00:38,190
A mileage of how about we'll stick with about 50,000?

11
00:00:39,740 --> 00:00:45,170
I'll change the longitude to 45, the latitude to 45, and then for the price, I'm going to put in

12
00:00:45,170 --> 00:00:46,130
10,000.

13
00:00:48,190 --> 00:00:51,370
I'm now going to create exactly one car with these attributes.

14
00:00:52,340 --> 00:00:53,630
Okay, so there's the one car.

15
00:00:55,440 --> 00:00:59,480
I'm then going to make a very small change to a couple of these parameters.

16
00:00:59,490 --> 00:01:05,040
So I'm going to change the year to 1981 and the price to 15,000.

17
00:01:07,390 --> 00:01:13,630
And then finally, I'll change the year to 1982 and I'll change the price to 20,000.

18
00:01:16,260 --> 00:01:23,040
So now if my query is working as expected, I would if I make a or try to get an estimate for a Ford

19
00:01:23,070 --> 00:01:29,730
Mustang with a year of, say, 1981 with the same mileage or really any mileage around that longitude

20
00:01:29,730 --> 00:01:36,060
and latitude, I would expect to get back a price estimate of 15,000 because my first Ford Mustang had

21
00:01:36,060 --> 00:01:38,960
a price of 10,000, then 15 and then 20.

22
00:01:38,970 --> 00:01:43,410
So if I average those three vehicles together, the result would be a price of 15,000.

23
00:01:43,740 --> 00:01:47,190
So I'm going to go down to the bottom and update my query string a little bit.

24
00:01:47,640 --> 00:01:49,620
I'm going to change the make to Ford.

25
00:01:50,730 --> 00:01:52,170
The Model two Mustang.

26
00:01:53,160 --> 00:01:57,000
I'll change the longitude to 45 and the latitude to 45.

27
00:01:57,180 --> 00:02:02,040
The mileage should not make a big difference because right now we only have three cars anyways.

28
00:02:02,250 --> 00:02:05,190
I will change the year to say 1981.

29
00:02:06,790 --> 00:02:12,220
So now if I send off this request, it should build me an estimate for my vehicle based upon the three

30
00:02:12,220 --> 00:02:13,930
different reports that I just submitted.

31
00:02:14,950 --> 00:02:16,270
Okay, so we're going to send this off.

32
00:02:16,690 --> 00:02:18,250
And sure enough, it worked.

33
00:02:19,120 --> 00:02:19,870
Awesome.

34
00:02:19,870 --> 00:02:21,460
So that's exactly what we wanted.

35
00:02:22,360 --> 00:02:22,660
Okay.

36
00:02:22,660 --> 00:02:27,760
So I'd say that our request to make or to get an estimate is working as we expect.

37
00:02:28,120 --> 00:02:32,740
So now that we've done this test, the very last thing would like to take care of is to add in one last

38
00:02:32,740 --> 00:02:34,420
condition around our estimate.

39
00:02:34,540 --> 00:02:40,870
Right now, our estimate is not actually only considering a report that has been approved by an admin.

40
00:02:41,050 --> 00:02:46,120
So we can very easily add in this case or add in a check for that by.

41
00:02:47,010 --> 00:02:50,400
Putting in one additional and we're here.

42
00:02:50,790 --> 00:02:53,160
So we're going to put in an and we're of.

43
00:02:54,760 --> 00:02:55,630
Approved.

44
00:02:56,820 --> 00:02:58,110
Is true.

45
00:02:59,310 --> 00:03:03,540
That will make sure that we only consider reports where they have been approved.

46
00:03:04,460 --> 00:03:05,690
So I'm going to save this.

47
00:03:06,020 --> 00:03:10,640
And now if I try to run that same request again, I have not approved the three different reports I

48
00:03:10,640 --> 00:03:11,450
just created.

49
00:03:11,450 --> 00:03:14,990
So now I would expect to probably get back a price of null or something like that.

50
00:03:15,020 --> 00:03:15,650
Sure enough.

51
00:03:15,680 --> 00:03:16,610
Yep, it's null.

52
00:03:16,850 --> 00:03:22,040
So now if I wanted to get a actual estimate, I would have to go back through and approve the three

53
00:03:22,040 --> 00:03:23,660
different reports that I just submitted.

54
00:03:25,040 --> 00:03:25,430
Okay.

55
00:03:25,430 --> 00:03:29,630
Well, that is pretty much it for the estimate feature inside of our application.

56
00:03:29,630 --> 00:03:34,340
And in general, I think that's kind of it for the entire app overall, at least for code, we have

57
00:03:34,340 --> 00:03:36,230
to write around our application logic.

58
00:03:36,530 --> 00:03:42,320
So now that we've wrapped up our authentication logic and the reports logic, we're going to start to

59
00:03:42,320 --> 00:03:44,450
think about deploying our application.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# section 18: Production Deployment

1
00:00:01,180 --> 00:00:03,610
Our application is now pretty much feature complete.

2
00:00:03,610 --> 00:00:08,380
So moving forward, we're going to take this app and start to deploy it into a production environment.

3
00:00:08,650 --> 00:00:13,360
So preparing our app for production is going to be a little bit of an ordeal because we're going to

4
00:00:13,360 --> 00:00:19,060
very quickly realize that some of the recommendations made to us by the documentation itself don't always

5
00:00:19,060 --> 00:00:20,590
work out quite so nicely.

6
00:00:20,740 --> 00:00:24,910
So let me first begin by just telling you exactly what we really need to be concerned with here.

7
00:00:24,940 --> 00:00:29,770
So inside of our development and test environments, we are connecting to a SQLite database.

8
00:00:29,770 --> 00:00:31,120
In our production environment.

9
00:00:31,120 --> 00:00:35,560
We're going to be connecting to a slightly different database, a copy of Postgres instead.

10
00:00:35,590 --> 00:00:40,420
The only reason we've made use of SQLite is just because it's so easy to get started with and you don't

11
00:00:40,420 --> 00:00:42,880
have to install any additional software on your machine.

12
00:00:44,320 --> 00:00:48,970
When we move from the dev environment over to production, we're going to have to change out some of

13
00:00:48,970 --> 00:00:50,300
our configuration as well.

14
00:00:50,320 --> 00:00:56,170
So you might recall that we went through all that environment config file stuff a little bit ago specifically

15
00:00:56,170 --> 00:01:00,250
to make sure that we connected to a different database during development versus test.

16
00:01:00,280 --> 00:01:04,050
We're going to have to do something very similar when we move over to production as well.

17
00:01:04,060 --> 00:01:08,740
So we need to load up some environment variables telling our application how to connect to a Postgres

18
00:01:08,740 --> 00:01:09,490
database.

19
00:01:10,230 --> 00:01:12,280
Only very small thing over here.

20
00:01:12,300 --> 00:01:16,450
You might recall that we set up a cookie key for cookie session a while ago.

21
00:01:16,470 --> 00:01:19,950
This was a very special string that was used for encrypting our cookie.

22
00:01:20,610 --> 00:01:26,700
Right now we have that cookie or that that key itself in plain text inside of our project, which is

23
00:01:26,700 --> 00:01:29,670
generally pretty poor form, not something we really want to do.

24
00:01:29,700 --> 00:01:34,020
So we're going to make sure that we extract that cookie key to be an environment variable instead.

25
00:01:34,020 --> 00:01:38,640
And we need to set that environment variable inside of our dev and test environments and inside of our

26
00:01:38,640 --> 00:01:40,170
production environment as well.

27
00:01:41,110 --> 00:01:41,500
Okay.

28
00:01:41,500 --> 00:01:43,450
So that's kind of what's coming up.

29
00:01:43,450 --> 00:01:45,310
We need to do a database swap.

30
00:01:45,310 --> 00:01:48,340
When we go to production, we need to extract some configuration.

31
00:01:48,340 --> 00:01:51,670
We need to update the cookie key and then eventually do the deployment.

32
00:01:51,700 --> 00:01:56,110
Like I said, along the way, we're going to run into a lot of kind of nasty problems.

33
00:01:56,110 --> 00:02:00,220
I'm not going to lie some stuff that's going to be not quite so nice, but we'll get through it step

34
00:02:00,220 --> 00:02:00,910
by step.

35
00:02:01,060 --> 00:02:02,410
So let's take a pause right here.

36
00:02:02,410 --> 00:02:06,310
We're going to come back in just a moment and we're going to work on the easiest step first, this cookie

37
00:02:06,310 --> 00:02:07,360
key issue.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,340 --> 00:00:04,550
The first issue we're going to take care of is our cookie key.

2
00:00:04,580 --> 00:00:10,010
So as a quick reminder, back inside of our app module file, I'm going to scroll down all the way to

3
00:00:10,010 --> 00:00:10,730
the bottom.

4
00:00:11,680 --> 00:00:16,180
And you might recall that inside of here we set up that global middleware of cookie session.

5
00:00:16,300 --> 00:00:18,190
So we provided that keys property.

6
00:00:18,190 --> 00:00:21,980
And this string right here was used to encrypt our outgoing cookie.

7
00:00:22,000 --> 00:00:26,740
It's really important that this string does not fall into our user's hands or any kind of malicious

8
00:00:26,740 --> 00:00:31,690
user because if anyone got access to this string, they would be able to modify their cookie and pretend

9
00:00:31,690 --> 00:00:32,800
to be someone else.

10
00:00:32,920 --> 00:00:38,410
So we need to make sure that in development and in production, we issue this string right here as an

11
00:00:38,410 --> 00:00:43,690
environment variable so that it is not tied to our actual application code and make sure that we never

12
00:00:43,690 --> 00:00:47,440
accidentally commit this key to Git or GitHub or anything like that.

13
00:00:48,190 --> 00:00:55,150
So to extract the key right there, we're going to open up our env development file and our env test

14
00:00:55,150 --> 00:00:55,810
file.

15
00:00:55,840 --> 00:01:00,130
Recall that both these files house some different environment variables that we want to load up at certain

16
00:01:00,130 --> 00:01:01,120
points in time.

17
00:01:01,970 --> 00:01:03,590
Inside of the development file.

18
00:01:03,620 --> 00:01:08,360
We're going to add in a new property called Cookie Key.

19
00:01:08,810 --> 00:01:11,150
And I'm going to put in just some random string inside there.

20
00:01:12,080 --> 00:01:15,640
I'll then do something very similar inside the environment test file as well.

21
00:01:15,650 --> 00:01:18,830
So cookie key is a random string.

22
00:01:20,380 --> 00:01:25,030
So we're going to use this cookie key environment variable inside of our app module during the setup

23
00:01:25,030 --> 00:01:26,080
of this middleware.

24
00:01:27,060 --> 00:01:31,170
To get access to these different environment variables inside these two files, we need to use that

25
00:01:31,170 --> 00:01:33,930
config service that we had put together previously.

26
00:01:34,080 --> 00:01:37,140
Remember back inside of our app module towards the top.

27
00:01:38,290 --> 00:01:38,980
Right here.

28
00:01:38,980 --> 00:01:40,480
We set up that config module.

29
00:01:40,480 --> 00:01:45,640
And remember, this module gives us access to something called the config service which we can use to

30
00:01:45,640 --> 00:01:49,360
read out configuration from these two files.

31
00:01:50,220 --> 00:01:56,070
So we need to get a copy of the config service inside of our app module class.

32
00:01:56,100 --> 00:02:00,600
Right there, that's where we want to stick that key that we just wrote inside the file.

33
00:02:00,960 --> 00:02:06,540
So in order to get access to the config service inside of our app module, we're going to use, you

34
00:02:06,570 --> 00:02:08,850
guessed it, dependency injection.

35
00:02:09,270 --> 00:02:12,720
So on the app module itself, I'm going to add in a constructor.

36
00:02:15,660 --> 00:02:16,920
As an argument to the constructor.

37
00:02:16,920 --> 00:02:22,410
I'll add in private config service will be of type config service.

38
00:02:23,880 --> 00:02:29,100
And now at Keys right here, I'm going to delete the hardcoded string and replace it with config service

39
00:02:29,100 --> 00:02:30,320
dot git.

40
00:02:30,330 --> 00:02:36,660
And then I want to look up cookie key and that should be this dot config service.

41
00:02:37,360 --> 00:02:38,690
And that's pretty much it.

42
00:02:38,710 --> 00:02:44,410
So now if we save this and go back over to our terminal, we should not see any errors over here or

43
00:02:44,410 --> 00:02:45,550
anything like that.

44
00:02:45,980 --> 00:02:50,530
So now whenever our application runs in a development or test environment, our cookie key is going

45
00:02:50,530 --> 00:02:53,290
to be coming from one of these two different files.

46
00:02:53,480 --> 00:02:55,390
So now we can repeat a very similar pattern.

47
00:02:55,390 --> 00:03:01,720
When our application starts to move over to production, we can either somehow get a production file

48
00:03:01,720 --> 00:03:07,150
into our production deployment or we can just set an environment variable directly during our deployment.

49
00:03:07,180 --> 00:03:08,610
We're going to take the latter approach.

50
00:03:08,620 --> 00:03:13,270
We're going to eventually set an actual environment config or an actual environment variable as opposed

51
00:03:13,270 --> 00:03:16,570
to writing out some kind of key inside of a env file.

52
00:03:17,170 --> 00:03:21,850
So we have to do is remember that when we go to deploy our application, we need to set up this cookie

53
00:03:21,850 --> 00:03:23,170
key environment variable.

54
00:03:24,040 --> 00:03:24,310
Okay.

55
00:03:24,310 --> 00:03:25,320
So that's step number one.

56
00:03:25,330 --> 00:03:27,310
Now, fortunately, that was kind of the easy step.

57
00:03:27,310 --> 00:03:28,660
So let's come back in just a moment.

58
00:03:28,660 --> 00:03:31,900
We're going to start taking a look at all the database replacement stuff.

59
00:03:31,900 --> 00:03:35,200
And unfortunately, that's where life is going to be a little bit more challenging.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,510 --> 00:00:05,700
Next, we're going to work on replacing our SQLite database with Postgres instead.

2
00:00:05,710 --> 00:00:09,460
Just to be clear, we are only going to use Postgres in our production environment.

3
00:00:09,490 --> 00:00:12,430
We're still going to use SQLite in development and test.

4
00:00:13,330 --> 00:00:18,430
Okay, so making use of Postgres in production is going to have, like I said a little bit ago, a lot

5
00:00:18,430 --> 00:00:21,410
of really interesting issues are going to come up along the way.

6
00:00:21,430 --> 00:00:24,760
A lot of these issues are not really tied to Nestjs per se.

7
00:00:24,790 --> 00:00:27,910
Instead, it's going to be a lot of issues with Type ORM itself.

8
00:00:28,090 --> 00:00:33,730
So I want to give you a quick reminder on something that we discussed very briefly a while ago inside

9
00:00:33,730 --> 00:00:38,980
of our app module down at our Type ORM module right here.

10
00:00:39,010 --> 00:00:44,170
You might recall that we set up all this configuration stuff and we eventually returned an object that

11
00:00:44,170 --> 00:00:45,610
configured our database.

12
00:00:45,610 --> 00:00:48,370
And one property we put on there was synchronize.

13
00:00:48,370 --> 00:00:49,060
True.

14
00:00:49,090 --> 00:00:55,330
This is an incredibly important property and I can't tell you enough how important it is for you to

15
00:00:55,330 --> 00:00:58,090
understand exactly what this property does for you.

16
00:00:59,090 --> 00:01:03,980
So let's take a look at a couple diagrams and understand what synchronize is really doing for us.

17
00:01:04,680 --> 00:01:05,340
Okay.

18
00:01:07,340 --> 00:01:10,640
So in this diagram, we've got our database here on the right hand side.

19
00:01:10,820 --> 00:01:15,320
I'm only going to consider our table of users for right now, even though we also have a table of reports

20
00:01:15,320 --> 00:01:16,040
as well.

21
00:01:16,710 --> 00:01:22,020
Now, inside of our application code, we defined a class called User, and that was supposed to be

22
00:01:22,020 --> 00:01:23,240
our user entity.

23
00:01:23,250 --> 00:01:27,930
On that class, we added a couple of different properties along with some type annotations as well.

24
00:01:27,930 --> 00:01:33,290
So for example, we had an ID of number email that was a string and password that was a string.

25
00:01:33,300 --> 00:01:36,360
We also had a reference to reports that belong to this user.

26
00:01:36,360 --> 00:01:39,300
But for right now, let's just consider these three properties.

27
00:01:40,020 --> 00:01:46,320
So because of that synchronized true flag, specifically because this is set to true, whenever our

28
00:01:46,320 --> 00:01:50,370
application starts up, type ORM is going to look at the user entity.

29
00:01:50,400 --> 00:01:54,150
It's going to look at all the different properties that it has and their type.

30
00:01:54,600 --> 00:02:00,080
Type is then going to take a look at the current status of our table of users inside the database,

31
00:02:00,090 --> 00:02:06,000
and type is going to make sure that this table has all the exact same properties with more or less the

32
00:02:06,000 --> 00:02:09,560
exact same type as the user entity itself.

33
00:02:10,040 --> 00:02:17,000
So, for example, if you and I went into our user entity file right now and deleted that password property

34
00:02:17,000 --> 00:02:23,090
and then restarted our application when type read our user entity, the next time it would notice that

35
00:02:23,090 --> 00:02:28,700
there was no longer a password field, so it would go into the database, find the table of users and

36
00:02:28,700 --> 00:02:31,880
automatically remove that password column.

37
00:02:32,490 --> 00:02:36,240
So it delete the entire column along with all the data inside of it.

38
00:02:36,630 --> 00:02:38,670
If we were to add another property.

39
00:02:38,700 --> 00:02:43,770
So let's say we also put in something here like name that is a string as well.

40
00:02:43,950 --> 00:02:49,380
Then the next time that typo starts up, it's going to notice that there is a column here of name.

41
00:02:49,380 --> 00:02:54,120
It's going to take a look at the table of users and notice that the table does not have a name column.

42
00:02:54,830 --> 00:02:57,920
So type is going to modify that table.

43
00:02:58,660 --> 00:03:02,440
And add in a new column that's going to record the user's name.

44
00:03:04,810 --> 00:03:09,580
So the entire purpose of that synchronized flag is to make sure that whenever type ORM starts up, it's

45
00:03:09,580 --> 00:03:11,590
going to take a look at our different entities.

46
00:03:11,620 --> 00:03:17,230
It's going to look at the structure of them and then modify our database to suit exactly the structure

47
00:03:17,230 --> 00:03:18,760
of your entities.

48
00:03:19,330 --> 00:03:21,100
Now, I can't tell you enough.

49
00:03:21,100 --> 00:03:24,010
This is extremely uncommon behavior.

50
00:03:24,040 --> 00:03:29,140
There are very few SQL frameworks out there, not only for TypeScript and JavaScript, but for other

51
00:03:29,140 --> 00:03:31,960
languages as well that behave in this manner.

52
00:03:32,290 --> 00:03:33,560
So let's take a pause right here.

53
00:03:33,580 --> 00:03:34,720
We're going to come back in the next video.

54
00:03:34,720 --> 00:03:36,700
I'm going to tell you exactly why that is.

55
00:03:36,730 --> 00:03:41,260
I'm going to make sure it's really clear why this can be a very dangerous setting to have enabled inside

56
00:03:41,260 --> 00:03:42,370
of your application.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,270 --> 00:00:03,840
We just spoke about what that synchronize flag does.

2
00:00:03,850 --> 00:00:08,350
So synchronize is going to cause type to take a look at your different entities and then modify your

3
00:00:08,350 --> 00:00:09,090
database.

4
00:00:09,100 --> 00:00:10,540
So what's the issue with that?

5
00:00:10,540 --> 00:00:12,730
What kind of problem could ever possibly arise?

6
00:00:12,730 --> 00:00:18,190
Well, I want you to imagine a scenario where maybe you are making some changes to your code and maybe

7
00:00:18,190 --> 00:00:22,370
by mistake, you delete some property off your user entity.

8
00:00:22,390 --> 00:00:27,880
Let's imagine that you then saved your entire project and deployed it off to a production environment.

9
00:00:28,000 --> 00:00:32,860
When your application starts up on that production server, it's going to connect to your production

10
00:00:32,860 --> 00:00:33,760
database.

11
00:00:33,760 --> 00:00:39,220
Type is then going to see your updated user entity and it's going to notice that it no longer has a

12
00:00:39,220 --> 00:00:40,360
password property.

13
00:00:40,450 --> 00:00:46,870
So type is going to automatically go into your production database and delete that entire password column

14
00:00:47,140 --> 00:00:53,320
When that delete occurs, you have now lost data inside of your application that is obviously really,

15
00:00:53,320 --> 00:00:56,920
really bad and definitely not something we would ever want to do.

16
00:00:57,250 --> 00:01:03,560
So the downside to using this synchronize flag of True is that you can very easily accidentally lose

17
00:01:03,560 --> 00:01:07,370
data inside of your production database the next time you deploy your app.

18
00:01:07,610 --> 00:01:11,990
So even though it can be really handy to use during development because we can very quickly make changes

19
00:01:11,990 --> 00:01:17,630
to our database just by changing our different entity files, I highly recommend that you do not make

20
00:01:17,630 --> 00:01:19,130
use of synchronize True.

21
00:01:19,130 --> 00:01:22,840
As soon as you start deploying your application to a production environment.

22
00:01:22,850 --> 00:01:27,380
So just to make sure that's super crystal clear, I'm going to put a diagram on the screen that repeats

23
00:01:27,380 --> 00:01:28,800
exactly what I just said.

24
00:01:28,820 --> 00:01:33,950
So when we are actively developing our application, using Synchronize of True is fantastic because

25
00:01:33,950 --> 00:01:39,380
we can just very easily make a change to our entity and see the updated structure inside of our database.

26
00:01:39,780 --> 00:01:45,240
But as soon as you start deploying your application, I really very highly recommend you change, synchronize

27
00:01:45,240 --> 00:01:48,340
to false and never ever change it back to true.

28
00:01:48,360 --> 00:01:52,860
So at a certain point in time, you really want to lock down your database structure and not allow a

29
00:01:52,860 --> 00:01:57,390
very simple property change inside of an entity file to modify your database?

30
00:01:58,420 --> 00:02:03,030
So now, with this having been said, how do we actually change our database structure?

31
00:02:03,040 --> 00:02:08,200
Without a doubt, at some point in time, after we first make our database and eventually decide upon

32
00:02:08,200 --> 00:02:12,190
its structure and all the different tables and columns, we might want to change our structure in some

33
00:02:12,190 --> 00:02:12,600
way.

34
00:02:12,610 --> 00:02:16,090
We might want to add a column or remove a column or even rename one.

35
00:02:16,090 --> 00:02:17,560
So how are we going to do that?

36
00:02:17,590 --> 00:02:21,910
We're going to use a tool called migrations, and that's what we're going to start to discuss in just

37
00:02:21,910 --> 00:02:22,570
a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:05,850
So if we are not going to use the synchronize flag, we still need some way of updating the structure

2
00:00:05,850 --> 00:00:06,850
of our database.

3
00:00:06,870 --> 00:00:09,760
And to do so, we're going to use something called migrations.

4
00:00:09,780 --> 00:00:13,800
So let's take a look at a couple of different diagrams and understand what a migration is.

5
00:00:14,370 --> 00:00:18,180
A migration is a file that has two functions defined inside of it.

6
00:00:18,210 --> 00:00:22,530
Usually we call one function something like up and the other something like down.

7
00:00:22,560 --> 00:00:27,480
These two functions are going to describe a change that we want to make to the structure of our database.

8
00:00:27,780 --> 00:00:32,010
The up function is going to describe some kind of update that we actually want to apply.

9
00:00:32,040 --> 00:00:37,470
So this might be creating a table, deleting a table, adding a column, renaming a column, any kind

10
00:00:37,470 --> 00:00:40,140
of change to the structure of our database we want to make.

11
00:00:40,440 --> 00:00:46,500
Then inside of the down function, we're going to describe exactly how to undo the steps that we committed

12
00:00:46,500 --> 00:00:52,270
during up by having both the up and down file or up and down functions inside of one file.

13
00:00:52,290 --> 00:00:56,400
We can then safely apply this migration to a database and change its structure.

14
00:00:56,400 --> 00:01:00,810
And then if something goes wrong during that process, we could immediately undo that change if we want

15
00:01:00,910 --> 00:01:01,630
to as well.

16
00:01:02,910 --> 00:01:08,100
So, for example, in our application for our users table, we might have a migration file that looks

17
00:01:08,100 --> 00:01:08,730
like this.

18
00:01:08,760 --> 00:01:14,790
We might have one function called up that describes adding in a new table called users and then saying

19
00:01:14,790 --> 00:01:19,530
that on that table we want to add in a column called email and a column called Password.

20
00:01:20,550 --> 00:01:24,510
So all those steps describe some kind of changes that we want to make to the database.

21
00:01:24,810 --> 00:01:31,440
Then inside of our down function, we would put in a step that exactly undoes whatever we did during

22
00:01:31,440 --> 00:01:31,980
up.

23
00:01:32,310 --> 00:01:37,920
So in this case, to undo what we did during the up step, we could just delete the entire table called

24
00:01:37,920 --> 00:01:41,070
users and that would undo everything we did during up.

25
00:01:41,550 --> 00:01:46,200
So now if we apply the up function to our database, that would create a table with two columns.

26
00:01:46,200 --> 00:01:51,090
And if we applied the down function that would delete the entire table and the two columns we had created

27
00:01:51,090 --> 00:01:51,960
inside of it.

28
00:01:52,970 --> 00:01:58,310
In order to eventually generate the entire structure of our database, we can create multiple different

29
00:01:58,310 --> 00:02:01,410
migration files and then run them all in a row.

30
00:02:01,430 --> 00:02:05,960
So, for example, to build up the structure of our current database where we have a table of users

31
00:02:05,960 --> 00:02:11,810
and a table of reports, we might create one migration file with an up function that creates our table

32
00:02:11,810 --> 00:02:18,110
of users and then a second migration file with another up function that creates a table of reports and

33
00:02:18,110 --> 00:02:20,630
gives it all the different appropriate columns as well.

34
00:02:21,490 --> 00:02:26,920
Naturally, inside the second migration file, we would also write out a done down function that perfectly

35
00:02:26,920 --> 00:02:30,620
undoes whatever or undoes whatever we did during up.

36
00:02:30,640 --> 00:02:33,760
So in this case we would delete the table called reports.

37
00:02:35,030 --> 00:02:40,490
So this is how we are going to change the structure of our database moving forward rather than using

38
00:02:40,490 --> 00:02:44,240
that synchronized flag, because that might lead to some data loss.

39
00:02:44,240 --> 00:02:48,500
When we start to deploy our application to production, we're going to instead write out these different

40
00:02:48,500 --> 00:02:49,610
migration files.

41
00:02:49,610 --> 00:02:54,500
And these migration files are going to perfectly describe the structure of our database or some changes

42
00:02:54,500 --> 00:02:55,730
that we want to make to it.

43
00:02:56,990 --> 00:03:00,740
So now that we understand this idea of a migration file where things start to get a little bit more

44
00:03:00,740 --> 00:03:06,380
complicated is how we actually execute these and where things start to get really complicated in our

45
00:03:06,380 --> 00:03:12,830
application in particular is how we are going to run these migrations during our production deployment.

46
00:03:13,220 --> 00:03:14,690
So let's take another pause right here.

47
00:03:14,690 --> 00:03:19,250
Come back in just a moment, and I'm going to tell you exactly how we execute migrations using type

48
00:03:19,280 --> 00:03:19,980
ORM.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,480 --> 00:00:05,230
All right, my friends, now that we understand the idea behind migration files, we're going to take

2
00:00:05,230 --> 00:00:10,150
a look at some well, a little bit of difficulty in getting these migrations to work.

3
00:00:10,240 --> 00:00:15,460
I cannot understate how kind of I'll be honest with you, kind of frustrating the coming videos are

4
00:00:15,460 --> 00:00:20,560
going to be because it turns out that after all this time, unfortunately, nest and type, although

5
00:00:20,560 --> 00:00:25,210
they seem to integrate together so darn well it turns out that nest and type, when it really comes

6
00:00:25,210 --> 00:00:29,050
down to these migrations, not the best thing in the world.

7
00:00:29,140 --> 00:00:30,760
So let me tell you why this is going to be.

8
00:00:30,760 --> 00:00:32,620
We're in for a wild ride, trust me.

9
00:00:32,620 --> 00:00:34,180
Are you going to eventually have something that works?

10
00:00:34,180 --> 00:00:36,400
But it's going to be challenging.

11
00:00:36,400 --> 00:00:37,780
Okay, let's just dive in.

12
00:00:39,140 --> 00:00:43,050
Okay, so first off, how do we actually create and run a migration?

13
00:00:43,070 --> 00:00:47,240
Well, when we are doing active development and let's say we want to change the structure of our database,

14
00:00:47,240 --> 00:00:48,680
here's what we would normally do.

15
00:00:48,710 --> 00:00:50,450
We would stop our development server.

16
00:00:50,480 --> 00:00:52,550
We'd use the type ORM cli.

17
00:00:52,910 --> 00:00:55,100
Now we have not used the type ORM yet.

18
00:00:55,100 --> 00:00:57,470
So this is a new tool we're going to use.

19
00:00:57,500 --> 00:01:00,500
We're going to use it to generate an empty migration file.

20
00:01:00,530 --> 00:01:04,520
This migration file is going to have inside of it an up function and a down function.

21
00:01:04,520 --> 00:01:09,140
And inside there we're going to add in some code that describes a change we want to make to our database.

22
00:01:09,880 --> 00:01:14,980
Once we've created that migration, we'll then execute it by using the type ORM again.

23
00:01:15,100 --> 00:01:17,830
And then at that point, in theory database is updated.

24
00:01:17,830 --> 00:01:20,950
We can now restart the development server and we are good to go.

25
00:01:21,340 --> 00:01:28,300
Now it turns out that this process in actuality is really, really challenging and especially not only

26
00:01:28,300 --> 00:01:34,030
during development, but remember we are also running our application in a test environment and eventually

27
00:01:34,060 --> 00:01:36,100
a production environment as well.

28
00:01:36,430 --> 00:01:40,690
So that is what is going to make the setup of this entire flow rather challenging.

29
00:01:40,690 --> 00:01:41,800
Really frustrating.

30
00:01:41,800 --> 00:01:46,210
I can't tell you how frustrating it is for me personally, having gone through this entire process more

31
00:01:46,210 --> 00:01:48,160
than one time on my own personal projects.

32
00:01:48,160 --> 00:01:49,510
But again, we'll get through it.

33
00:01:49,510 --> 00:01:52,690
So let me tell you why this entire flow is a little bit strange.

34
00:01:54,290 --> 00:01:54,530
Okay.

35
00:01:54,530 --> 00:02:01,190
So at some point in time, we're going to use the type to open up our database or connect to our database

36
00:02:01,190 --> 00:02:03,080
and make some changes to it.

37
00:02:03,110 --> 00:02:04,610
That is the key.

38
00:02:04,640 --> 00:02:10,910
The Typeform CLI needs to somehow connect to our database and somehow change the structure of it.

39
00:02:11,360 --> 00:02:16,610
Now remember, where did we set up all of our different database configuration variables?

40
00:02:16,640 --> 00:02:19,820
We set up all that stuff back inside of our app module.

41
00:02:19,820 --> 00:02:21,080
So here's the app module right here.

42
00:02:21,110 --> 00:02:26,150
Remember, we went through that entire process with type or module for route async.

43
00:02:26,180 --> 00:02:29,480
We made use of the config module and the config service.

44
00:02:29,480 --> 00:02:34,640
So we are building up an object right here that describes how to connect to our database.

45
00:02:35,250 --> 00:02:41,610
We then created those two env files to say we either want to connect to a database called SQLite during

46
00:02:41,610 --> 00:02:46,830
development or one called test dot SQLite when we are running our application in a test mode.

47
00:02:47,600 --> 00:02:54,020
So the problem is that the type or whenever we run it to generate a migration file or to apply a migration

48
00:02:54,020 --> 00:03:00,580
file, the CLI only will run the code inside of our entity files and the migration files.

49
00:03:00,590 --> 00:03:01,550
That's it.

50
00:03:01,580 --> 00:03:09,680
The type has no idea what is the type or has no idea what the config module is.

51
00:03:09,710 --> 00:03:11,930
It doesn't know what the config service is.

52
00:03:11,960 --> 00:03:17,390
It has no idea what we have going on inside of the app module.

53
00:03:17,480 --> 00:03:23,750
But the app module itself is what defines exactly how to connect to our database.

54
00:03:25,480 --> 00:03:31,960
The tapeworm just plain has no idea how to get that object right there and how to use it to connect

55
00:03:31,960 --> 00:03:34,420
to our database and apply the migration.

56
00:03:34,660 --> 00:03:36,780
That is the core issue here.

57
00:03:36,790 --> 00:03:42,010
We have encoded all of our different connection settings directly into the nest side of our application,

58
00:03:42,010 --> 00:03:46,930
and now we cannot share these connection settings very easily with a type side of things.

59
00:03:47,170 --> 00:03:53,140
Now a very easy way to address this would be to simply copy paste more or less that connection object

60
00:03:53,140 --> 00:03:58,000
right there and somehow hand that off to type and say, Hey, here you go, here's how you connect to

61
00:03:58,000 --> 00:04:01,140
our database and go ahead and apply the migration to it.

62
00:04:01,150 --> 00:04:07,080
But obviously copy pasting configuration settings like this around our application is a recipe for disaster.

63
00:04:07,090 --> 00:04:08,860
Definitely not something we want to do.

64
00:04:09,430 --> 00:04:15,160
So what we really have to figure out here is how to define all of our configuration for connecting to

65
00:04:15,160 --> 00:04:22,090
our database in one location so that it can be used easily or equally well by either nest or the type.

66
00:04:23,140 --> 00:04:24,910
That is the challenge.

67
00:04:25,240 --> 00:04:31,660
And then on top of that, the additional challenge is how our application is executed slightly differently

68
00:04:31,660 --> 00:04:36,520
when we are running it in production versus development versus test mode.

69
00:04:37,340 --> 00:04:40,010
Okay, So now that I've kind of prepared you, we're going to take a pause right here.

70
00:04:40,010 --> 00:04:43,970
We're going to come back in the next video and I'm going to really, truly we're going to really dive

71
00:04:43,970 --> 00:04:47,270
into why exactly this is so challenging.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,750 --> 00:00:06,520
In the last video we established that when we are running the type, we need to somehow figure out some

2
00:00:06,520 --> 00:00:07,670
connection options.

3
00:00:07,690 --> 00:00:12,340
In other words, we need to tell the how to connect to our database and eventually run some migrations.

4
00:00:12,670 --> 00:00:17,650
And we establish that right now that's a big issue because all of our connection settings are embedded

5
00:00:17,650 --> 00:00:19,160
inside of our app module.

6
00:00:19,180 --> 00:00:24,790
When the type executes, it does not execute anything related to Nest itself.

7
00:00:24,790 --> 00:00:31,210
So we cannot right now tell typo how to connect to our database using options we wrote out inside of

8
00:00:31,210 --> 00:00:32,270
our app module.

9
00:00:32,290 --> 00:00:33,490
That's the issue.

10
00:00:34,360 --> 00:00:36,970
Okay, Now, before I go any further, I want to take a very quick pause.

11
00:00:37,000 --> 00:00:40,200
I want to tell you that this video is 100% optional.

12
00:00:40,210 --> 00:00:41,860
This is going to be a very long video.

13
00:00:41,860 --> 00:00:48,490
And in this video, I'm going to show you why Configuring Nest and type together is an absolute nightmare.

14
00:00:48,730 --> 00:00:51,310
This is an optional video because it's going to be so long.

15
00:00:51,310 --> 00:00:56,200
So if you don't want to hear about a rather long rant coming from me, that's going to help you understand

16
00:00:56,200 --> 00:01:00,280
some problems you might run into in the future on your own, then just pause the video right here and

17
00:01:00,280 --> 00:01:04,930
go on to the next one where I'll give you a lot of code that we're going to use to properly configure,

18
00:01:04,930 --> 00:01:06,700
type and nest together.

19
00:01:07,820 --> 00:01:09,470
Okay, So if you're still here, fantastic.

20
00:01:09,470 --> 00:01:10,450
Let's get through this.

21
00:01:10,460 --> 00:01:13,910
I can try to make it as quickly as I can, or so.

22
00:01:13,910 --> 00:01:18,130
First thing I want to do is show you a little bit of documentation around type itself.

23
00:01:18,140 --> 00:01:23,690
So we're going to take a look at a page that tells us how we can load up some connection options into

24
00:01:23,690 --> 00:01:24,620
type ORM.

25
00:01:25,040 --> 00:01:26,480
Okay, so here's that page right here.

26
00:01:26,510 --> 00:01:31,880
This page will tell you that there are several different ways to get some connection settings into type

27
00:01:31,910 --> 00:01:37,040
ORM, either when it is running inside of our Nest project or when we are running type earned by itself

28
00:01:37,040 --> 00:01:37,880
through the CLI.

29
00:01:39,000 --> 00:01:40,830
So here are all the different options.

30
00:01:40,860 --> 00:01:48,150
We can define a config.json file inside of our root project directory that tells Typeform exactly how

31
00:01:48,150 --> 00:01:49,260
to connect to our database.

32
00:01:49,260 --> 00:01:54,780
So if you create this file with some information like this inside of it, then type is going to automatically

33
00:01:54,780 --> 00:01:57,390
load it up whenever our project starts.

34
00:01:57,390 --> 00:02:01,500
So whenever it starts up, type REM is going to automatically look for this file.

35
00:02:03,330 --> 00:02:07,420
Whenever we run the type as well, it will also look for this file.

36
00:02:07,440 --> 00:02:12,330
So in other words, if we make a file like this, it will be automatically loaded up by type and this

37
00:02:12,330 --> 00:02:15,150
file will tell type how to connect to our database.

38
00:02:16,410 --> 00:02:19,290
Now, there are other ways to encode the exact same information.

39
00:02:19,290 --> 00:02:23,970
For example, if we scroll down, we can also define a config.js file.

40
00:02:24,150 --> 00:02:29,490
We can also define these connection settings using plain environment variables like the ones you see

41
00:02:29,520 --> 00:02:30,210
right here.

42
00:02:30,240 --> 00:02:36,300
We could either define those as normal environment variables or we can define them inside of a env file.

43
00:02:36,600 --> 00:02:37,980
We can also.

44
00:02:39,390 --> 00:02:44,070
Encode all these settings in a Yaml file or an XML file.

45
00:02:44,070 --> 00:02:48,630
And elsewhere on this page you'll also see it says we can put all this stuff inside of a TypeScript

46
00:02:48,630 --> 00:02:49,650
file as well.

47
00:02:51,180 --> 00:02:56,200
So again, this page tells you all the different ways you can feed connection settings into type ORM,

48
00:02:56,340 --> 00:03:01,290
be it as it is running inside of our Nest application or when it is running by itself through the CLI.

49
00:03:02,310 --> 00:03:07,740
The one thing that this page does not show you or does not tell you how to do is how to load connection

50
00:03:07,740 --> 00:03:11,190
settings directly into Typeform itself through your code.

51
00:03:11,220 --> 00:03:13,350
In other words, as your application is running.

52
00:03:13,530 --> 00:03:18,510
So the last way in which you can really configure type is to give it some connection settings directly

53
00:03:18,510 --> 00:03:19,560
when you make use of it.

54
00:03:19,560 --> 00:03:22,770
So that's what's really going on right here inside of our app module.

55
00:03:23,010 --> 00:03:28,200
So this is where we set up all that stuff around for route async and then return this configuration

56
00:03:28,200 --> 00:03:28,950
object.

57
00:03:29,920 --> 00:03:31,570
Okay, so now we've established that.

58
00:03:31,600 --> 00:03:32,760
Let's take a look at a diagram.

59
00:03:32,770 --> 00:03:35,950
It's going to show you why things are going to be a little bit crazy here.

60
00:03:37,450 --> 00:03:43,090
So in this diagram at the very bottom, I've got all the different ways in which we can load up some

61
00:03:43,090 --> 00:03:44,590
connection settings into type.

62
00:03:45,190 --> 00:03:49,150
So these are pretty much all the different options that were listed on that documentation page.

63
00:03:49,210 --> 00:03:51,460
We can set some plain environment variables.

64
00:03:52,070 --> 00:03:54,600
Set environment variables inside of a env file.

65
00:03:54,620 --> 00:03:56,210
Make a json file.

66
00:03:56,420 --> 00:03:57,140
JS file.

67
00:03:57,170 --> 00:03:58,070
TypeScript file.

68
00:03:58,100 --> 00:03:58,910
Yaml file.

69
00:03:58,910 --> 00:03:59,840
And also an XML.

70
00:03:59,870 --> 00:04:00,860
But I'm just not going to show.

71
00:04:00,890 --> 00:04:01,520
XML.

72
00:04:03,120 --> 00:04:08,310
Now right from the get go, we need to somehow figure out some way to define some connection settings

73
00:04:08,310 --> 00:04:09,920
that will be fed into type O.

74
00:04:10,320 --> 00:04:14,830
And remember, Type ORM is being executed in a variety of different environments.

75
00:04:14,850 --> 00:04:20,730
We need to figure to put in some configuration options to type when we are running it inside of our

76
00:04:20,730 --> 00:04:22,500
application during development.

77
00:04:22,650 --> 00:04:27,840
When we are running inside of our application during testing and when we run type run by itself through

78
00:04:27,840 --> 00:04:28,710
the CLI.

79
00:04:29,340 --> 00:04:35,430
So we need to pick one of these options so we have all of our configuration in one place and all that

80
00:04:35,430 --> 00:04:40,140
configuration needs to be fed into type ORM in these three different scenarios.

81
00:04:40,930 --> 00:04:43,990
At present our application is doing this.

82
00:04:44,110 --> 00:04:49,990
We are running our application either in development or testing, and when our app runs, we generate

83
00:04:49,990 --> 00:04:53,380
some connection options and feed it directly into type ORM.

84
00:04:53,410 --> 00:04:59,150
So this green box right here really corresponds to this object back inside of our app module.

85
00:04:59,170 --> 00:05:03,550
We are generating these connection options and feeding them directly into type ORM.

86
00:05:03,700 --> 00:05:08,680
So it's very clear that this is not going to work anymore because we cannot get access to those connection

87
00:05:08,680 --> 00:05:10,360
options from the type ORM.

88
00:05:10,960 --> 00:05:12,200
So not feasible.

89
00:05:12,220 --> 00:05:14,080
So I'm just going to delete that right away.

90
00:05:15,970 --> 00:05:22,810
So we need to now pick one of these other different ways of feeding in all this config and that exactly

91
00:05:22,810 --> 00:05:24,350
all these different options right here.

92
00:05:24,370 --> 00:05:31,040
This is why getting type and nest to work together is a real nightmare for one reason or another.

93
00:05:31,060 --> 00:05:37,660
It turns out that just about all these different methods are does not work very well to get to nest

94
00:05:37,660 --> 00:05:39,100
and type room to work.

95
00:05:39,340 --> 00:05:43,060
So we're going to go through these one by one and we're going to disqualify each of them.

96
00:05:43,060 --> 00:05:46,330
And that, again, is why this video is going to end up being really long.

97
00:05:46,960 --> 00:05:51,490
So the first thing we need to keep in mind is that during development and testing and production for

98
00:05:51,520 --> 00:05:57,040
that matter, we need to be able to to feed in very different connection settings to type.

99
00:05:58,310 --> 00:06:04,010
So that almost immediately starts to disqualify something like loading up environment variables from

100
00:06:04,010 --> 00:06:09,200
a fixed file, like a config.json file inside of a plain Json file.

101
00:06:09,200 --> 00:06:10,840
We have no scripting abilities.

102
00:06:10,850 --> 00:06:17,270
We don't have any way to change what database we connect to or what username or what password or what

103
00:06:17,270 --> 00:06:24,490
database name we make use of inside of a plain static file like a Json file or a yaml file.

104
00:06:24,500 --> 00:06:31,670
So right off the bat these things are not options because we cannot configure type in a slightly different

105
00:06:31,670 --> 00:06:34,310
way during development testing or production.

106
00:06:35,580 --> 00:06:37,290
So that leaves these other options.

107
00:06:37,530 --> 00:06:43,800
So first off, to for these remaining ones, can we make use of environment variables to feed in some

108
00:06:43,800 --> 00:06:44,850
connection settings to type?

109
00:06:46,440 --> 00:06:51,420
During us running the or us running it directly in development or testing?

110
00:06:51,450 --> 00:06:53,340
Unfortunately not really.

111
00:06:53,370 --> 00:06:58,800
We can make use of environment variables to set up type room correctly during dev and testing, but

112
00:06:58,800 --> 00:07:03,090
for some reasons you're going to see very shortly we cannot really make use of environment variables

113
00:07:03,090 --> 00:07:08,020
directly to feed in connection settings to type when we deploy our application to production.

114
00:07:08,040 --> 00:07:12,470
The reason for that, just to be really clear here, I'm going to tell you why just immediately.

115
00:07:12,480 --> 00:07:19,260
The reason is that type room expects us to feed in some environment variables with very specific names.

116
00:07:19,410 --> 00:07:26,580
So if we go back over to the documentation type wants to see environment variables with explicitly these

117
00:07:26,580 --> 00:07:30,150
names, but the hosting provider that you and I are going to be using.

118
00:07:30,180 --> 00:07:34,740
So the place we're actually going to deploy our app to gives us our connection settings.

119
00:07:34,740 --> 00:07:37,590
We're going to be getting a database through them, just to be clear.

120
00:07:37,590 --> 00:07:41,790
So our hosting provider will give us a database and to tell us how to connect to that database.

121
00:07:41,790 --> 00:07:46,810
They're going to give us an environment variable immediately set inside of our environment automatically.

122
00:07:46,810 --> 00:07:50,680
And that environment variable is going to have a very different name than any of these.

123
00:07:50,680 --> 00:07:56,470
And there's no easy way to kind of redirect type until it, hey, use this different environment variable

124
00:07:56,500 --> 00:07:58,600
to figure out how to connect to the database.

125
00:08:00,010 --> 00:08:04,510
So even though we can make use of environment variables to correctly tell Typekit how to behave during

126
00:08:04,510 --> 00:08:10,210
development testing and with the CLI in either environment, it's not going to work very well in production.

127
00:08:10,210 --> 00:08:13,030
So unfortunately those options are out as well.

128
00:08:14,150 --> 00:08:22,370
That leaves the config.js file or the config file as our only options to very easily load up configuration

129
00:08:22,370 --> 00:08:26,930
settings into type in all these different environments, including production as well.

130
00:08:27,170 --> 00:08:32,360
And this is where things get really weird and really frustrating.

131
00:08:33,160 --> 00:08:37,390
So you might think, hey, no problem, let's define a file called config.

132
00:08:38,290 --> 00:08:40,620
We'll put all of our configuration settings inside there.

133
00:08:40,630 --> 00:08:46,760
We can change very easily depending upon whether we are running our application in dev testing or production.

134
00:08:46,780 --> 00:08:48,100
You might think that.

135
00:08:48,280 --> 00:08:49,120
I'm sorry.

136
00:08:49,150 --> 00:08:50,410
You thought wrong.

137
00:08:51,090 --> 00:08:54,990
So we're now at a point where let's go back over to our code editor and we're going to try to create

138
00:08:54,990 --> 00:08:56,910
this config file.

139
00:08:56,910 --> 00:09:00,840
And I'm going to show you, unfortunately, this thing does not work as expected.

140
00:09:02,310 --> 00:09:02,670
Okay.

141
00:09:02,670 --> 00:09:05,730
Now, I would encourage you not to make these changes to your project.

142
00:09:05,730 --> 00:09:09,480
I'm going to make the changes very quickly and then undo them because like I said, unfortunately,

143
00:09:09,480 --> 00:09:12,360
making this file is not going to work as expected.

144
00:09:13,170 --> 00:09:13,380
All right.

145
00:09:13,380 --> 00:09:15,000
So I'm going to run through the changes very quickly.

146
00:09:15,030 --> 00:09:20,910
First off, inside my app module, I'm going to comment out all that stuff around for root async.

147
00:09:22,720 --> 00:09:25,400
And replace it with a single for route call.

148
00:09:25,420 --> 00:09:29,860
So by putting this in here, it tells type as it is running inside of our project that we're not going

149
00:09:29,860 --> 00:09:32,300
to give it any configuration settings anymore.

150
00:09:32,320 --> 00:09:38,200
Instead, it needs to figure out how to connect to our database by opening up a config file or reading

151
00:09:38,200 --> 00:09:39,400
environment variables.

152
00:09:40,030 --> 00:09:45,220
So if I now go back over to my terminal and I try to run my project, I'll very quickly get some error

153
00:09:45,220 --> 00:09:51,760
messages that say no connection options because there are no configuration sources available for type

154
00:09:52,030 --> 00:09:52,740
anymore.

155
00:09:52,800 --> 00:09:54,610
I didn't give it any environment variables.

156
00:09:54,610 --> 00:09:56,920
I didn't give it any direct connection settings.

157
00:09:56,920 --> 00:09:59,230
I didn't give it any config file.

158
00:09:59,440 --> 00:10:03,760
So I'm now going to fix this by making an config dot ts file.

159
00:10:06,610 --> 00:10:11,020
So instead my root project directory, I'll make a file called config dot ts.

160
00:10:11,380 --> 00:10:19,150
And then inside of here, in order to give or provide some configuration settings, we need to export.

161
00:10:20,010 --> 00:10:24,600
A plain object that has some familiar properties, like the type of database.

162
00:10:25,390 --> 00:10:26,590
The database name.

163
00:10:28,950 --> 00:10:31,920
And then we also have to provide a list of entities.

164
00:10:33,260 --> 00:10:37,430
Or at least the where they are located inside of our project.

165
00:10:37,430 --> 00:10:39,350
So we're not going to import these into this file.

166
00:10:39,350 --> 00:10:44,420
Instead, we're going to provide a path to find all the different entities, such as our user entity

167
00:10:44,420 --> 00:10:45,720
and the report entity.

168
00:10:45,740 --> 00:10:50,540
So I'm going to put in here star, star, star, entity, dot ts.

169
00:10:50,660 --> 00:10:52,520
So just go and find those files.

170
00:10:53,440 --> 00:10:55,900
And then finally, I'm going to put in a synchronize.

171
00:10:58,480 --> 00:10:59,320
A false.

172
00:11:00,400 --> 00:11:00,700
Okay.

173
00:11:00,700 --> 00:11:05,800
So now, in theory, whenever our project starts up, type is going to say, I need to figure out how

174
00:11:05,800 --> 00:11:06,940
to connect to the database.

175
00:11:06,970 --> 00:11:09,510
It was not given any connection settings directly.

176
