00:11:09,520 --> 00:11:14,470
It's going to start looking for all these different variations of the config file and hopefully it's

177
00:11:14,470 --> 00:11:16,810
going to find awesome config dot ts.

178
00:11:17,050 --> 00:11:21,790
So with that in mind, I'm going to go back over to my terminal after saving both of these files and

179
00:11:21,790 --> 00:11:24,190
let's see if our project is now running.

180
00:11:24,810 --> 00:11:27,840
And sure enough, I get an error.

181
00:11:28,020 --> 00:11:29,710
This is bizarre.

182
00:11:29,730 --> 00:11:33,390
I can't tell you how unexpected an error this is.

183
00:11:34,390 --> 00:11:34,750
Okay.

184
00:11:34,750 --> 00:11:36,730
So let's walk through what is going on here.

185
00:11:37,580 --> 00:11:41,960
Once again, whenever our project starts up during development type room is going to start looking for

186
00:11:41,960 --> 00:11:43,460
some configuration sources.

187
00:11:43,490 --> 00:11:48,860
It's going to find that config file and so it's going to load it up and try to parse everything inside

188
00:11:48,860 --> 00:11:51,260
there and use that file to get some connection settings.

189
00:11:51,500 --> 00:11:55,400
It looks like we are getting an unexpected, unexpected token error.

190
00:11:55,610 --> 00:11:59,290
Well, you know, we did write out that export statement a little bit strange.

191
00:11:59,300 --> 00:12:01,480
Maybe there's something wrong with the syntax of this file.

192
00:12:01,490 --> 00:12:02,480
So, you know what?

193
00:12:02,540 --> 00:12:07,490
Let's just comment out this entire thing and I'm going to put in some very basic code that should definitely

194
00:12:07,490 --> 00:12:08,510
work no matter what.

195
00:12:08,540 --> 00:12:12,350
I'm also going to put in just a little bit of TypeScript code just for TypeScript sake.

196
00:12:12,350 --> 00:12:16,910
So I'll say let some variable is type string.

197
00:12:18,830 --> 00:12:21,200
So this is definitely some valid TypeScript right here.

198
00:12:21,200 --> 00:12:22,400
So I'm going to save this.

199
00:12:22,640 --> 00:12:24,320
Go back over to my terminal.

200
00:12:26,180 --> 00:12:29,060
And sure enough, I end up with another error.

201
00:12:29,090 --> 00:12:30,680
So what's going on here?

202
00:12:30,710 --> 00:12:34,160
Why do I keep on getting errors coming from the TypeScript file?

203
00:12:34,400 --> 00:12:36,170
The reason is simple.

204
00:12:37,210 --> 00:12:38,770
Inside of our project directory.

205
00:12:38,770 --> 00:12:44,140
We need to think about how our project is actually being executed in the development environment.

206
00:12:44,380 --> 00:12:47,920
Whenever you run a project, here's what happens behind the scenes.

207
00:12:47,950 --> 00:12:52,460
Nest is going to take all of the TypeScript files inside of your SRC directory.

208
00:12:52,480 --> 00:12:57,550
It's going to transpile them into JavaScript and then place the output into the dist directory.

209
00:12:57,730 --> 00:13:03,430
Nest is then going to start up node and run the main.js file inside of here.

210
00:13:03,670 --> 00:13:08,560
So when we are running our application, we are running it as plain JavaScript.

211
00:13:08,920 --> 00:13:15,670
That means that when type starts up and starts looking around for all these config files, it's going

212
00:13:15,670 --> 00:13:17,740
to find config dot ts.

213
00:13:17,800 --> 00:13:23,140
But we are already past the point of trying to turn TypeScript into JavaScript.

214
00:13:23,350 --> 00:13:24,310
So.

215
00:13:25,610 --> 00:13:31,880
Type is going to load up the config file and try to execute everything inside of here as though it were

216
00:13:31,880 --> 00:13:32,760
JavaScript.

217
00:13:32,780 --> 00:13:36,260
And lo and behold, we have some amount of TypeScript.

218
00:13:36,290 --> 00:13:43,070
So execution is going to fail because type erm expected to find JavaScript code inside of here, even

219
00:13:43,070 --> 00:13:47,210
though we are really sure that we made a dot ts file.

220
00:13:48,490 --> 00:13:51,700
So, again, this is bizarre, very unexpected.

221
00:13:51,820 --> 00:13:58,330
So in short, using nest and type room together, we cannot really make an config file.

222
00:13:58,330 --> 00:14:03,550
It's not an option because when our application starts up during development, the expectation is that

223
00:14:03,550 --> 00:14:06,280
we are only going to be running JavaScript code.

224
00:14:06,430 --> 00:14:08,830
So unfortunately not an option.

225
00:14:09,510 --> 00:14:15,750
So that leaves out of all these supposed different ways of feeding in configuration to type that leaves

226
00:14:15,750 --> 00:14:18,890
nothing but the config.js file.

227
00:14:18,900 --> 00:14:21,550
But even that has its own set of issues.

228
00:14:21,570 --> 00:14:24,010
So here's where things get really, really fun.

229
00:14:24,030 --> 00:14:27,690
This is an easier problem to solve, but I just want to walk you through it very quickly.

230
00:14:28,470 --> 00:14:33,900
Okay, so without a doubt we cannot make an config file, so let's try turning it into a JavaScript

231
00:14:33,900 --> 00:14:34,770
file instead.

232
00:14:37,010 --> 00:14:39,200
So I'll change the extension to JS.

233
00:14:39,880 --> 00:14:41,050
I'm going to.

234
00:14:42,240 --> 00:14:43,470
Uncomment that code.

235
00:14:43,470 --> 00:14:48,180
And because this is a plain JavaScript file that is not going to be parsed by Babel or anything like

236
00:14:48,180 --> 00:14:48,690
that.

237
00:14:48,720 --> 00:14:51,060
We have to use Commonjs exports.

238
00:14:51,060 --> 00:14:54,630
So have to change this to a module exports like so.

239
00:14:55,930 --> 00:14:58,600
Okay, so now, in theory, this should work.

240
00:14:58,600 --> 00:15:05,300
Whenever our application starts up in development mode, it should try to load up the config.js file.

241
00:15:05,320 --> 00:15:10,630
This is after transpiling everything into JavaScript, so a JavaScript file should load up successfully.

242
00:15:10,630 --> 00:15:11,860
There should not be any issues.

243
00:15:11,860 --> 00:15:12,520
Right?

244
00:15:12,640 --> 00:15:14,620
Well, naturally something is going to go wrong.

245
00:15:14,650 --> 00:15:15,550
Of course it will.

246
00:15:15,880 --> 00:15:17,320
So we'll go back over here now.

247
00:15:17,320 --> 00:15:19,270
And now we see a really bizarre error.

248
00:15:19,300 --> 00:15:24,310
Now we're told cannot use import statement outside of module and it's pointing at our report entity

249
00:15:24,340 --> 00:15:25,030
file.

250
00:15:25,240 --> 00:15:27,160
So what's this one about?

251
00:15:27,460 --> 00:15:30,160
Okay, so here's where it's just so great.

252
00:15:30,190 --> 00:15:30,940
Unbelievable.

253
00:15:30,940 --> 00:15:34,180
So in inside of here, we've got our list of entities.

254
00:15:34,180 --> 00:15:38,550
So we have to provide here are all the different entities that are going to exist inside of our application.

255
00:15:38,560 --> 00:15:45,100
Remember, this file is being executed after we have transpiled everything into plain JavaScript.

256
00:15:45,890 --> 00:15:48,390
So type is going to look at this list of entities.

257
00:15:48,410 --> 00:15:52,490
It's going to try to find all the different files that match this pattern right here.

258
00:15:52,640 --> 00:15:57,650
In this case, it's going to find some different TypeScript files that we have written out inside of

259
00:15:57,650 --> 00:15:58,800
our SRC directory.

260
00:15:58,820 --> 00:16:01,930
And once again, we run into the same issue type.

261
00:16:01,970 --> 00:16:07,700
ORM tries to load up these files, but they are TypeScript, so we're going to fail to load and execute

262
00:16:07,700 --> 00:16:12,020
these entity files and that's why we're seeing this error over here.

263
00:16:12,380 --> 00:16:16,280
So to solve this, we're going to tell type, Hey, okay, we get it.

264
00:16:16,280 --> 00:16:21,130
You can't read TypeScript files even though your name of type would make you think differently.

265
00:16:21,140 --> 00:16:22,130
And we're going to tell type.

266
00:16:22,580 --> 00:16:27,920
Okay, Tell you what, Just go inside the dist directory and find the transpiled version of all of our

267
00:16:27,920 --> 00:16:28,990
entity files.

268
00:16:29,000 --> 00:16:32,510
So I'm going to change this to JS instead.

269
00:16:33,750 --> 00:16:34,860
I'm going to save that.

270
00:16:35,460 --> 00:16:36,450
Pull it back over.

271
00:16:37,230 --> 00:16:41,010
And now if I do a hard restart just to save some time.

272
00:16:42,090 --> 00:16:42,960
Finally.

273
00:16:44,030 --> 00:16:45,160
Stuff is going to work.

274
00:16:45,170 --> 00:16:46,340
Hey, it actually worked.

275
00:16:46,370 --> 00:16:48,410
We got some configuration settings inside there.

276
00:16:49,120 --> 00:16:51,520
So now at this point, you're probably thinking, okay, hey, we get it.

277
00:16:51,550 --> 00:16:53,590
We need to make an config.js file.

278
00:16:53,590 --> 00:16:57,250
And there's an oddity or two around the file extension on there.

279
00:16:57,250 --> 00:16:58,720
And I know you think that's it.

280
00:16:58,750 --> 00:16:59,830
No, of course not.

281
00:16:59,830 --> 00:17:00,700
That's not it.

282
00:17:00,730 --> 00:17:01,980
No, we're not done yet.

283
00:17:01,990 --> 00:17:07,600
Remember, our application is running in multiple different environments in a development environment

284
00:17:07,630 --> 00:17:12,700
using the type CLI directly and in a test environment as well.

285
00:17:13,690 --> 00:17:16,450
So here's where things get just really fantastic.

286
00:17:16,450 --> 00:17:17,410
Just really good.

287
00:17:17,410 --> 00:17:18,130
Okay.

288
00:17:18,130 --> 00:17:19,690
I'm going to go back over to my terminal.

289
00:17:21,710 --> 00:17:26,150
I'm going to stop my running server and I'm going to try to run my end to end test suite.

290
00:17:26,270 --> 00:17:29,120
Remember, we can run that with an NPM run test.

291
00:17:29,120 --> 00:17:30,290
Colon E2e.

292
00:17:32,370 --> 00:17:36,990
So this is going to presumably run all the different tests inside of a project, specifically the end

293
00:17:36,990 --> 00:17:37,840
to end ones.

294
00:17:37,860 --> 00:17:39,510
And sure enough, they all fail.

295
00:17:39,540 --> 00:17:41,260
Well, why are they failing?

296
00:17:41,280 --> 00:17:44,940
We can scroll back up and we're going to notice some errors up here.

297
00:17:44,970 --> 00:17:50,000
These errors say that we are trying to load a JavaScript file.

298
00:17:50,010 --> 00:17:52,710
So what's going on now?

299
00:17:53,010 --> 00:17:56,490
Okay, so here's where things get really, really strange.

300
00:17:57,060 --> 00:18:01,920
When we are running our application in development mode, Nest is going to first transpile all of all

301
00:18:01,920 --> 00:18:07,980
of our code into TypeScript using TypeScript itself, and then it runs the resulting JavaScript.

302
00:18:08,010 --> 00:18:10,720
Just say Transpile into TypeScript is a long video.

303
00:18:10,740 --> 00:18:15,030
Nest is going to transpile all of our code into JavaScript and then execute it.

304
00:18:15,300 --> 00:18:21,510
But how our applications executed during testing is a little bit different when we are running our application

305
00:18:21,510 --> 00:18:23,220
in a test environment.

306
00:18:23,880 --> 00:18:27,450
Nest is going to use a tool called TTS Jest.

307
00:18:27,630 --> 00:18:33,900
This is a tool that allows us to kind of somewhat directly execute a TypeScript file.

308
00:18:33,910 --> 00:18:34,810
Not really.

309
00:18:34,810 --> 00:18:39,530
It loads up the TypeScript file, immediately converts into JavaScript and then executes the result.

310
00:18:39,550 --> 00:18:44,350
But the point is that with jest running we can directly load up TypeScript files.

311
00:18:44,350 --> 00:18:50,200
So that means that we could safely when our tests are running only, only when our tests are running,

312
00:18:50,200 --> 00:18:57,370
we actually could use a config file, but that does not work when we are running our application in

313
00:18:57,370 --> 00:18:58,810
a development environment.

314
00:18:59,910 --> 00:19:04,030
And that's why we initially went over to saying, okay, we'll do a config.js file.

315
00:19:04,050 --> 00:19:09,960
But now the problem is that when we are running our application in the test environment, Jest does

316
00:19:09,960 --> 00:19:13,640
not expect to receive any plain JavaScript files.

317
00:19:13,650 --> 00:19:16,500
So at this point it kind of seems like we're in a catch 22.

318
00:19:16,530 --> 00:19:21,930
We can't make a config.js file because it can't be used in a testing environment, but we also cannot

319
00:19:21,930 --> 00:19:26,340
use a TS file because that cannot be used in a dev environment.

320
00:19:26,580 --> 00:19:30,440
Well, to get around this, here's a fix we're going to tell jest.

321
00:19:30,480 --> 00:19:31,620
Look, don't worry about it.

322
00:19:31,620 --> 00:19:32,160
It's okay.

323
00:19:32,160 --> 00:19:35,280
If you get a JavaScript file, just go ahead and execute it.

324
00:19:35,340 --> 00:19:38,040
We're going to do that using the exact note they put right here.

325
00:19:38,160 --> 00:19:40,470
So I'm going to open up my config file.

326
00:19:41,600 --> 00:19:44,990
At the bottom, I'm going to put in a setting of allow DJs.

327
00:19:46,080 --> 00:19:46,980
Of true.

328
00:19:48,010 --> 00:19:49,120
I'm going to save this.

329
00:19:50,110 --> 00:19:51,550
And then restart my tests.

330
00:19:52,730 --> 00:19:55,060
And I bet you think, Hey, we're all done now, right?

331
00:19:55,070 --> 00:19:56,720
Everything should work as expected.

332
00:19:58,250 --> 00:19:59,570
Let's see what happens.

333
00:19:59,810 --> 00:20:00,740
Wrong.

334
00:20:00,950 --> 00:20:01,850
Wrong?

335
00:20:02,750 --> 00:20:03,350
Nope.

336
00:20:03,350 --> 00:20:04,400
Not done yet.

337
00:20:04,610 --> 00:20:06,040
Okay, so what's going on now?

338
00:20:06,050 --> 00:20:09,830
So we are now getting an error message that says no repository for user.

339
00:20:09,920 --> 00:20:14,620
So that seems like we are for some reason not loading up our entities correctly.

340
00:20:14,630 --> 00:20:16,430
So what could be going on now?

341
00:20:16,760 --> 00:20:18,590
Okay, trust me, this is the very last step.

342
00:20:18,590 --> 00:20:19,460
So here's the issue.

343
00:20:19,460 --> 00:20:25,820
Back inside of our Config.js file, we just had to fix an issue around running our project in a development

344
00:20:25,820 --> 00:20:28,940
mode by putting in entity JS right here.

345
00:20:29,210 --> 00:20:32,150
So now we are in the exact opposite scenario.

346
00:20:32,150 --> 00:20:37,520
When our tests are running, we can only run TypeScript files.

347
00:20:37,880 --> 00:20:40,520
We're not going to find any JavaScript files.

348
00:20:41,580 --> 00:20:47,670
So now the issue is that in our test environment, the test runner is not going to find all the JavaScript

349
00:20:47,670 --> 00:20:50,070
files that were placed inside the Dist directory.

350
00:20:50,640 --> 00:20:55,770
It's only going to find different files or any kind of TypeScript file that's placed inside the SRC

351
00:20:55,800 --> 00:20:56,600
directory.

352
00:20:56,610 --> 00:21:02,490
So in other words, depending upon our environment, we need to either load up entities from JavaScript

353
00:21:02,490 --> 00:21:04,850
files or TypeScript files.

354
00:21:04,860 --> 00:21:06,870
So to fix this last error, here's what I'm going to do.

355
00:21:06,870 --> 00:21:09,150
I'm going to say if.

356
00:21:10,540 --> 00:21:11,080
Process.

357
00:21:11,110 --> 00:21:11,500
Env.

358
00:21:11,820 --> 00:21:16,690
Node env is equal to development.

359
00:21:17,290 --> 00:21:18,970
I'll put in a ternary right here.

360
00:21:19,060 --> 00:21:23,380
So if we are running in development mode, try to load up the JavaScript version of all of our different

361
00:21:23,380 --> 00:21:24,220
entities.

362
00:21:24,250 --> 00:21:27,440
Otherwise we must be in a test environment.

363
00:21:27,460 --> 00:21:30,790
In that case, load up the TypeScript version instead.

364
00:21:32,320 --> 00:21:34,570
Now, this is not going to work when we eventually go to production.

365
00:21:34,570 --> 00:21:36,490
So we're going to put in something a little bit more robust.

366
00:21:36,490 --> 00:21:39,760
But for right now, this is good enough, so I'm not going to save this file.

367
00:21:39,960 --> 00:21:41,980
Flip back over, restart my tests.

368
00:21:43,230 --> 00:21:45,510
And now, finally, hopefully.

369
00:21:45,510 --> 00:21:48,690
Anyways, everything should finally run.

370
00:21:49,950 --> 00:21:52,170
Hey, it actually worked, believe it or not.

371
00:21:52,590 --> 00:21:58,380
So now, at long last, after a little bit of troubleshooting, we can run our tests and we can also

372
00:21:58,380 --> 00:22:00,510
run our application normally as well.

373
00:22:04,180 --> 00:22:08,920
So I'm just going to start up the application and yep, looks like the application actually started

374
00:22:08,920 --> 00:22:09,310
up.

375
00:22:10,490 --> 00:22:11,750
So is that a lot of headache?

376
00:22:11,780 --> 00:22:13,010
Yes, it was.

377
00:22:13,340 --> 00:22:19,760
And finally, at the very end, the result is we have to use a config dot js file to load in connection

378
00:22:19,760 --> 00:22:24,530
settings properly to a nest application during development or during testing.

379
00:22:24,530 --> 00:22:28,780
And this config file is also going to work correctly when we are using the type as well.

380
00:22:28,790 --> 00:22:33,170
And it's also going to work correctly when we are running our application in production as well.

381
00:22:34,110 --> 00:22:34,440
Huh?

382
00:22:35,100 --> 00:22:35,610
Okay.

383
00:22:35,640 --> 00:22:36,390
Lung video.

384
00:22:36,390 --> 00:22:38,490
If you watch this whole thing, I'm very impressed.

385
00:22:38,520 --> 00:22:40,620
Hopefully you've got an idea of.

386
00:22:40,740 --> 00:22:43,140
Yes, this stuff is kind of a nightmare.

387
00:22:43,170 --> 00:22:47,910
Kind of hard to put all these configuration settings again and hopefully now earlier on inside the course

388
00:22:47,910 --> 00:22:53,280
when I was kind of complaining about this configuration setting management stuff, this is pretty much

389
00:22:53,280 --> 00:22:58,020
why because Nest put a lot of effort into making that config module and that config service and it turns

390
00:22:58,020 --> 00:23:03,480
out it really doesn't work that well because we can't take any settings that exist inside of our Nest

391
00:23:03,480 --> 00:23:06,090
application and pass them off to the type.

392
00:23:08,880 --> 00:23:09,210
Okay.

393
00:23:09,210 --> 00:23:10,170
So I think that's good enough.

394
00:23:10,170 --> 00:23:16,590
So at the very end here, just to be very clear, the result is we need to make an config.js file inside

395
00:23:16,590 --> 00:23:16,860
there.

396
00:23:16,860 --> 00:23:22,320
We need to return multiple different versions of our config object depending upon what environment we

397
00:23:22,320 --> 00:23:22,890
are in.

398
00:23:23,820 --> 00:23:28,140
Now, the last thing I'm going to do is undo everything I just did in this video because, of course,

399
00:23:28,140 --> 00:23:29,280
this was an optional video.

400
00:23:29,280 --> 00:23:32,100
And I want to make sure anyone who skipped it doesn't miss out on this code.

401
00:23:32,100 --> 00:23:34,500
So I do want you to see me undo all these changes.

402
00:23:34,500 --> 00:23:38,220
I'm going to undo the or delete the config.js file.

403
00:23:38,640 --> 00:23:45,000
I'm going to remove the allow JS from config and then finally back inside of the app module.

404
00:23:45,000 --> 00:23:50,640
I'm going to delete that for root right there and uncomment all the stuff we had before.

405
00:23:51,580 --> 00:23:51,940
Okay.

406
00:23:51,940 --> 00:23:52,630
There we go.

407
00:23:52,660 --> 00:23:54,340
So now, finally, pause right here.

408
00:23:54,340 --> 00:23:55,270
Come back in just a moment.

409
00:23:55,270 --> 00:23:58,540
We're going to redo those exact settings for everyone who is not watching.

410
00:23:58,540 --> 00:24:02,500
And we're going to just dress them up a little bit more to handle these different environments slightly

411
00:24:02,500 --> 00:24:03,010
better.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,910 --> 00:00:05,080
In the last video we established that we need to define all of our database configuration inside of

2
00:00:05,080 --> 00:00:07,250
an config.js file.

3
00:00:07,270 --> 00:00:12,850
This will allow us to share some database configuration for our application when it's running in development

4
00:00:12,850 --> 00:00:18,580
or testing, or when we are running the type by itself which is going to occur whenever we need to run

5
00:00:18,580 --> 00:00:20,650
some migrations or anything like that.

6
00:00:21,010 --> 00:00:25,360
So we're going to extract a little bit of configuration out of our app module and we're going to put

7
00:00:25,360 --> 00:00:28,150
it into this config dot JS file instead.

8
00:00:28,750 --> 00:00:29,110
Okay.

9
00:00:29,110 --> 00:00:32,110
So back inside my editor, I'm going to find the app module.

10
00:00:32,380 --> 00:00:36,430
I'm going to go down to where we set up the type module right here.

11
00:00:36,850 --> 00:00:44,920
We're going to delete that entire section and replace it with type module for root instead.

12
00:00:47,330 --> 00:00:53,270
Then inside of our root project directory, I'm going to make a new file called Config.js.

13
00:00:53,480 --> 00:00:57,140
Now, once again, make sure that you create this file inside of your root project directory.

14
00:00:57,140 --> 00:00:59,330
It's not going to be inside the SRC folder.

15
00:00:59,860 --> 00:01:03,550
Then inside of here, we're going to define a variable we'll call.

16
00:01:03,550 --> 00:01:04,390
How about.

17
00:01:06,000 --> 00:01:07,170
DV config.

18
00:01:08,600 --> 00:01:11,150
We're going to put in a couple of options on it.

19
00:01:11,180 --> 00:01:15,770
We're then going to adjust this option or this object in a couple of different ways, depending upon

20
00:01:15,770 --> 00:01:17,140
what environment we are running in.

21
00:01:17,150 --> 00:01:19,280
So development test or production.

22
00:01:19,400 --> 00:01:23,870
You might notice also right away that we are getting an error from using the keyword const right here.

23
00:01:23,900 --> 00:01:26,000
It's coming from Eslint yet again.

24
00:01:26,030 --> 00:01:27,830
Eslint is causing a little bit of issues here.

25
00:01:27,830 --> 00:01:30,180
If we want to make that word go away or that error go away.

26
00:01:30,200 --> 00:01:32,180
We can change it out to var instead.

27
00:01:32,540 --> 00:01:36,710
Again, Eslint is kind of hampering our creative abilities here, but doesn't matter.

28
00:01:37,410 --> 00:01:37,800
Okay.

29
00:01:37,800 --> 00:01:41,970
So then after that, we're going to take a look or figure out what environment we are running our application

30
00:01:41,970 --> 00:01:42,330
in.

31
00:01:42,360 --> 00:01:44,370
So I'm going to set up a switch statement.

32
00:01:44,730 --> 00:01:48,720
We're going to switch over process env node env.

33
00:01:50,130 --> 00:01:53,340
And then we're going to handle the case in which we are in development.

34
00:01:54,060 --> 00:01:56,160
The case in which we are in test.

35
00:01:57,510 --> 00:01:59,400
The case in which we are in production.

36
00:02:00,750 --> 00:02:03,060
And we'll also have a default case as well.

37
00:02:04,330 --> 00:02:07,030
Then at the very bottom we're going to export.

38
00:02:07,030 --> 00:02:10,450
So module.exports that config object.

39
00:02:11,460 --> 00:02:15,840
So again, we're going to put in a little bit of configuration right here that is going to be true for

40
00:02:15,840 --> 00:02:17,250
all these different cases.

41
00:02:17,400 --> 00:02:21,840
We're then going to modify that config object depending upon our different environment.

42
00:02:22,540 --> 00:02:27,460
So on the object for right now, we're going to add in no properties right away, but we are going to

43
00:02:27,460 --> 00:02:31,870
come back very shortly and add in a couple of default properties to be used right now.

44
00:02:31,870 --> 00:02:35,710
Instead, we're just going to worry about updating that object in these three different cases.

45
00:02:36,370 --> 00:02:40,000
So if we are in development, I'm going to.

46
00:02:41,090 --> 00:02:43,160
Do an object assign.

47
00:02:44,370 --> 00:02:48,890
On top of config and we're going to add in a couple of different properties inside of here.

48
00:02:48,930 --> 00:02:54,330
So if we are in development, then I'm going to specify my type of database as being SQLite.

49
00:02:54,660 --> 00:02:58,710
The database that we're going to use is going to be DB dot SQLite.

50
00:03:00,020 --> 00:03:02,690
The location of all of our different entities.

51
00:03:03,490 --> 00:03:09,610
Is going to be star star star dot entity Dot JS.

52
00:03:12,130 --> 00:03:14,970
And then we are not going to do any synchronization anymore.

53
00:03:14,980 --> 00:03:17,050
So I'm going to put in a synchronize.

54
00:03:18,040 --> 00:03:18,550
Pulse.

55
00:03:18,550 --> 00:03:19,090
And you know what?

56
00:03:19,090 --> 00:03:21,610
We can actually that is one property we can extract to the very top.

57
00:03:21,610 --> 00:03:23,070
So let's just do that right away.

58
00:03:23,080 --> 00:03:27,160
So I'm going to put that one up at the very top, because in no scenario do we ever want to run that

59
00:03:27,160 --> 00:03:28,930
synchronize flag anymore.

60
00:03:30,210 --> 00:03:34,380
After doing the object assign, I'm then going to break out of the case statement.

61
00:03:35,710 --> 00:03:39,850
Okay, now I'm going to copy that block down and put it in the test case.

62
00:03:40,700 --> 00:03:44,660
When we are running our application in test, we still want to use a SQLite database.

63
00:03:44,660 --> 00:03:49,490
But rather than saving all of our data into a file called DB dot SQLite, we're going to instead write

64
00:03:49,490 --> 00:03:51,650
it into test dot SQLite.

65
00:03:52,380 --> 00:03:56,970
And then for reasons that we saw in the last video, we're going to change entities right here to look

66
00:03:56,970 --> 00:04:01,140
for any file ending with entity dot ts instead.

67
00:04:03,800 --> 00:04:05,840
We're going to deal with the production case in a little bit.

68
00:04:05,900 --> 00:04:10,670
Right now, I'll just put in a break and then finally for default right here, we're only going to fall

69
00:04:10,670 --> 00:04:17,410
into the default case if process node env is not going to match development test or production.

70
00:04:17,420 --> 00:04:22,430
So if we do not have a defined environment or if the environment is something we don't expect well,

71
00:04:22,430 --> 00:04:24,880
we don't really know how to handle a configuration for that.

72
00:04:24,890 --> 00:04:30,050
So if we fall into the default case, I'm going to throw a new error and say unknown.

73
00:04:31,670 --> 00:04:32,780
Environment.

74
00:04:34,140 --> 00:04:34,740
Like so.

75
00:04:36,060 --> 00:04:37,560
Okay, so that should be it.

76
00:04:37,680 --> 00:04:42,780
So now to test this out, I first I'm going to go again into my root project directory and I'm going

77
00:04:42,780 --> 00:04:45,420
to delete my SQLite file.

78
00:04:47,790 --> 00:04:50,970
I'm also going to make sure I delete the test SQLite file as well.

79
00:04:50,970 --> 00:04:53,610
So want to start entirely from scratch right now.

80
00:04:53,610 --> 00:04:57,120
So don't have any more SQLite files inside of my root project directory.

81
00:04:57,240 --> 00:05:01,020
Once I've ensured that, I'm then going to go back over to my terminal and I'm going to start up my

82
00:05:01,020 --> 00:05:05,970
application in development mode by running the command NPM run dev.

83
00:05:06,910 --> 00:05:07,090
Okay.

84
00:05:07,090 --> 00:05:08,230
So back over at the terminal.

85
00:05:10,270 --> 00:05:12,670
I'll do a NPM run, start colon dev.

86
00:05:12,670 --> 00:05:16,900
Now, just so you know, our application is not going to work as expected simply because we are not

87
00:05:16,900 --> 00:05:20,830
doing the synchronized step anymore, but at least want to see some kind of message up here.

88
00:05:20,830 --> 00:05:26,080
Something that says, hey, we do, or we did at least somewhat try or we at least got to some point

89
00:05:26,080 --> 00:05:29,350
where a database we attempted to create a database or something like that.

90
00:05:30,080 --> 00:05:31,370
So I'm going to start up the server.

91
00:05:32,680 --> 00:05:34,690
And it might start up, we might get an error.

92
00:05:34,720 --> 00:05:37,780
It's probably only going to get an error when we start trying to make a request.

93
00:05:37,780 --> 00:05:39,610
So it looks like we are able to start up.

94
00:05:39,610 --> 00:05:40,520
That's good.

95
00:05:40,540 --> 00:05:45,460
That also means that we probably are creating a SQLite database right there, so that's good as well.

96
00:05:45,460 --> 00:05:49,810
So again, it's probably only going to be once we start making requests and trying to access data inside

97
00:05:49,810 --> 00:05:54,700
that database because right now, because we turned off synchronize, there is no structure inside that

98
00:05:54,700 --> 00:05:55,330
database.

99
00:05:55,360 --> 00:05:58,870
There is no users table, there is no reports table or anything like that.

100
00:05:59,490 --> 00:06:05,610
So if I go into my client, let's say I go and try to sign up to the application.

101
00:06:05,850 --> 00:06:08,100
So I'll do a sign up request.

102
00:06:08,430 --> 00:06:09,780
I probably will get an error.

103
00:06:09,810 --> 00:06:10,160
Yep.

104
00:06:10,170 --> 00:06:14,190
Looks like I get an error right there and if I flip back over to my terminal, I'll probably see an

105
00:06:14,190 --> 00:06:16,410
error that says no such table user.

106
00:06:16,500 --> 00:06:17,790
So that's totally fine.

107
00:06:17,790 --> 00:06:18,360
That's good.

108
00:06:18,360 --> 00:06:22,500
That is expected because our database no longer has any structure inside of it.

109
00:06:22,950 --> 00:06:28,260
So now we've got this config file put together and we can at least kind of connect to our application.

110
00:06:28,260 --> 00:06:29,340
Let's take a pause right here.

111
00:06:29,340 --> 00:06:33,390
We're going to come back to the next video and start putting together some logic around our database

112
00:06:33,390 --> 00:06:38,040
migration, and that will allow us to actually put some structure into our database.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,010 --> 00:00:05,840
Now that we've got this database config file put together, we can start to focus on using the type

2
00:00:06,350 --> 00:00:08,690
to run a migration inside of our project.

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:12,500
Remember, these migrations are what are going to add some structure to our database.

4
00:00:12,500 --> 00:00:18,500
So they will make sure that we get a table of users inside the SQLite file and a table of reports as

5
00:00:18,500 --> 00:00:19,080
well.

6
00:00:19,100 --> 00:00:25,010
In order to create and run a migration, we need to use the type CLI to use the CLI.

7
00:00:25,100 --> 00:00:28,710
We're going to do a little bit of setup and installation inside this video.

8
00:00:28,730 --> 00:00:34,790
Now unfortunately installing and setting up and making use of the CLI is just a little bit confusing.

9
00:00:34,790 --> 00:00:38,150
So let's take a look at the documentation and understand why that is.

10
00:00:38,270 --> 00:00:41,690
I'm going to open up a new browser tab and navigate to that address right there.

11
00:00:42,760 --> 00:00:44,140
And here it is.

12
00:00:45,310 --> 00:00:47,560
Okay, so I'm at the using page.

13
00:00:47,860 --> 00:00:51,970
We're going to scroll down a little bit and right away we are told that there are two ways to set up

14
00:00:51,970 --> 00:00:56,710
the CLI depending upon whether our entity files are in JavaScript or in TypeScript.

15
00:00:56,740 --> 00:00:59,470
Well, we of course fall in the case of TypeScript.

16
00:00:59,500 --> 00:01:03,370
We wrote out all of our different entities inside of TypeScript files.

17
00:01:03,370 --> 00:01:07,060
So we have to go through a slightly more complicated setup process.

18
00:01:07,180 --> 00:01:14,020
The reason this is complicated is that type ORM itself and its CLI has no idea how to interpret TypeScript

19
00:01:14,020 --> 00:01:14,730
files.

20
00:01:14,740 --> 00:01:19,660
So we have to put together an extra little step to make sure that all of our different entity files

21
00:01:19,690 --> 00:01:23,110
are first turned into JavaScript and then executed.

22
00:01:23,140 --> 00:01:25,660
That's why this is a little bit of a pain.

23
00:01:26,950 --> 00:01:28,720
Now to actually set all this stuff up.

24
00:01:28,720 --> 00:01:31,780
We're going to first install a module called Node.

25
00:01:32,260 --> 00:01:36,100
Node is what is going to allow us to eventually run some TypeScript files directly.

26
00:01:36,130 --> 00:01:42,970
We're then going to make sure that whenever we try to run type, we're going to first wire up Node.

27
00:01:43,240 --> 00:01:45,720
We're then going to execute the type CLI.

28
00:01:46,600 --> 00:01:51,130
So at the end of the day, this all comes down to just adding in a script to our package.json file.

29
00:01:51,130 --> 00:01:56,230
And then whenever we want to run the type or in any way, we're going to execute a command like NPM

30
00:01:56,230 --> 00:01:59,500
run type and then the command we actually want to run.

31
00:01:59,770 --> 00:02:05,980
One nasty consequences of this is that if we ever want to pass some command line arguments to this run

32
00:02:05,980 --> 00:02:06,700
command.

33
00:02:07,830 --> 00:02:11,370
After putting in run type and then the command we want to execute.

34
00:02:11,400 --> 00:02:16,500
We have to put in a dash dash and then any additional flags we have to add in after that.

35
00:02:16,620 --> 00:02:23,070
So again, a little bit confusing, a little bit nasty stuff all comes down to the fact that type unfortunately

36
00:02:23,070 --> 00:02:27,030
doesn't really mesh super well with native TypeScript.

37
00:02:27,790 --> 00:02:28,900
All right, enough lecturing.

38
00:02:28,900 --> 00:02:30,400
Let's get started on the setup.

39
00:02:30,400 --> 00:02:34,840
So we're pretty much just going to take that script right there and toss it into our package.json file.

40
00:02:35,730 --> 00:02:38,910
Back inside my editor, I will find my Package.json file.

41
00:02:38,940 --> 00:02:43,530
I'm going to go down to the scripts section and at the very bottom of the script section, I'm going

42
00:02:43,530 --> 00:02:44,970
to add in a new script.

43
00:02:45,240 --> 00:02:50,730
We're going to call this type ORM, and whenever we run this script, we're going to actually execute.

44
00:02:52,200 --> 00:02:54,510
Node dash dash require.

45
00:02:55,400 --> 00:03:01,430
TSA dash node slash register, then dot slash node modules.

46
00:03:02,510 --> 00:03:07,520
Slash type orm cli JS like so.

47
00:03:10,020 --> 00:03:14,970
We're also going to make sure that whenever we run the type in any way, shape or form, we tell it

48
00:03:14,970 --> 00:03:19,860
that we are in a development mode because we're only going to run the type whenever we are connecting

49
00:03:19,890 --> 00:03:21,660
to our application in development.

50
00:03:22,730 --> 00:03:28,220
So to make sure that is the case, I'm also going to copy one of these cross lines up here.

51
00:03:29,450 --> 00:03:32,000
And put it right at the very start of that command.

52
00:03:32,090 --> 00:03:40,190
So now in total I've got type cross envy node, blah blah blah development node require then node modules

53
00:03:40,190 --> 00:03:41,990
type js.

54
00:03:44,130 --> 00:03:47,880
If you're looking at the official documentation a moment ago, you might have also noticed that it said

55
00:03:47,880 --> 00:03:50,310
that we need to globally install Node.

56
00:03:50,340 --> 00:03:52,560
That is actually not the case for you and I.

57
00:03:52,650 --> 00:03:58,470
When we installed all of our dependencies initially when we generated our project Nest itself actually

58
00:03:58,470 --> 00:04:00,180
depends upon node.

59
00:04:00,180 --> 00:04:02,550
So if we go down to our dev dependencies section.

60
00:04:02,760 --> 00:04:04,410
So here's dev dependencies.

61
00:04:05,200 --> 00:04:05,920
Inside this list.

62
00:04:05,920 --> 00:04:07,630
We should see Node right there.

63
00:04:07,630 --> 00:04:11,290
So it is actually already installed into our project and we don't have to install it globally.

64
00:04:11,290 --> 00:04:13,870
That's kind of an oversight on the documentation itself.

65
00:04:15,830 --> 00:04:16,680
That should be it.

66
00:04:16,710 --> 00:04:21,690
So now if we want to ever generate a new migration or run a migration, we're going to run a command

67
00:04:21,690 --> 00:04:23,580
just like what it shows in the documentation.

68
00:04:23,580 --> 00:04:28,680
So something like NPM run type migration generate and then whatever other arguments we want to pass

69
00:04:28,680 --> 00:04:29,250
along.

70
00:04:30,190 --> 00:04:31,750
Okay, so now we got this put together.

71
00:04:31,750 --> 00:04:32,800
We're going to take a pause right here.

72
00:04:32,800 --> 00:04:33,970
We're going to come back in just a moment.

73
00:04:33,970 --> 00:04:37,750
And we're going to use this new command to generate and run a migration.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,010 --> 00:00:05,690
With a type all set up, we can now use it to generate a new migration and run it.

2
00:00:05,720 --> 00:00:09,770
Remember, the entire purpose of a migration is to give our database some structure.

3
00:00:09,980 --> 00:00:14,340
So to generate and run a migration, we are going to take a look at the official documentation.

4
00:00:14,360 --> 00:00:18,380
This entire process is a little bit involved, so I really want you to have a resource you can look

5
00:00:18,380 --> 00:00:21,620
at at some point in time in the future, at the official documentation.

6
00:00:21,620 --> 00:00:27,260
I'm going to scroll down the left hand side and find the advanced topics section and then go to migrations.

7
00:00:28,530 --> 00:00:29,970
So under the heading right here.

8
00:00:29,970 --> 00:00:30,080
Hi.

9
00:00:30,090 --> 00:00:30,870
Migrations work.

10
00:00:30,870 --> 00:00:34,380
They're pretty much going to tell you everything I told you over the last couple of videos.

11
00:00:35,250 --> 00:00:40,230
We're going to scroll down past that and we are going to be told how to create a new migration.

12
00:00:40,350 --> 00:00:46,560
So step number one, we need to add in some additional options to our config dot JS file.

13
00:00:46,590 --> 00:00:52,920
These options are going to tell TypeScript or type the CLI and type itself as it's running cyber application

14
00:00:52,920 --> 00:00:55,380
where all of our different migration files exist.

15
00:00:55,500 --> 00:01:01,500
So we're going to add in just about exactly those two pieces of configuration into our config dot JS

16
00:01:01,500 --> 00:01:02,160
file.

17
00:01:02,250 --> 00:01:03,930
So we'll go back over to my editor.

18
00:01:05,190 --> 00:01:11,460
I'm going to find Config.js and this is some default configuration, so we're going to add it to the

19
00:01:11,820 --> 00:01:13,620
config object up here at the top.

20
00:01:14,330 --> 00:01:16,730
I'm going to add in migrations.

21
00:01:16,910 --> 00:01:18,800
An array inside there.

22
00:01:18,800 --> 00:01:21,260
I'll put a string with migrations.

23
00:01:21,260 --> 00:01:23,300
Slash sartaj's.

24
00:01:24,790 --> 00:01:29,080
And then cli migrations dir.

25
00:01:30,080 --> 00:01:31,160
Migrations.

26
00:01:34,020 --> 00:01:38,130
Now, with that put together, we can now start to actually generate our different migration files.

27
00:01:38,130 --> 00:01:39,270
But first, one more.

28
00:01:39,270 --> 00:01:39,660
Gotcha.

29
00:01:39,660 --> 00:01:42,870
And again, this is why you really need to look at the official documentation.

30
00:01:43,050 --> 00:01:45,960
So there's a section here creating a new migration.

31
00:01:45,960 --> 00:01:48,330
We're going to scroll on down past that a little bit.

32
00:01:49,720 --> 00:01:54,270
And we're going to look for a section that says running and reverting migrations.

33
00:01:54,280 --> 00:01:59,290
So here's a very key thing and goes back to this same topic we've been harping on over the last couple

34
00:01:59,290 --> 00:01:59,950
of videos.

35
00:01:59,950 --> 00:02:04,540
Type itself doesn't really know how to interpret TypeScript files.

36
00:02:04,540 --> 00:02:10,360
And so it says right here that if you make use of the type CLI to generate a migration in any way,

37
00:02:10,360 --> 00:02:12,910
it's going to create a TS file.

38
00:02:13,180 --> 00:02:19,690
However, in order to actually run these things, to actually run a migration or to roll one back,

39
00:02:19,960 --> 00:02:27,460
the type can only work on JavaScript files, so something has to transpile the TypeScript migrations

40
00:02:27,460 --> 00:02:29,920
before we actually try to run the migration.

41
00:02:30,310 --> 00:02:34,440
A very easy way to get around this is to just make use of this o flag.

42
00:02:34,450 --> 00:02:40,150
So the O flag will create a migration inside of a plain JavaScript file and thus remove the requirement

43
00:02:40,150 --> 00:02:44,650
for us to have to transpile this at any point along the way or have to worry about setting up any additional

44
00:02:44,650 --> 00:02:45,790
strange commands.

45
00:02:47,420 --> 00:02:50,790
If you take a look at the generating migration section right there.

46
00:02:50,810 --> 00:02:53,280
So this is the section at the top that we just scrolled past.

47
00:02:53,300 --> 00:02:56,810
Scroll down just a little bit and it will tell you what the O flag does.

48
00:02:56,840 --> 00:03:02,750
So O is short for dash dash output and it's going to generate all your migrations as plain JavaScript

49
00:03:02,750 --> 00:03:03,290
files.

50
00:03:03,290 --> 00:03:04,850
So that's what you and I are going to do.

51
00:03:05,060 --> 00:03:09,130
So at long last, having said all this, let's go over to our terminal.

52
00:03:09,140 --> 00:03:14,090
We're going to generate a new migration and then make use of it to apply some structure to our database.

53
00:03:14,810 --> 00:03:19,790
So back over at my terminal, I've stopped my running server and inside my project directory I'm going

54
00:03:19,790 --> 00:03:23,330
to run NPM run type ORM.

55
00:03:24,210 --> 00:03:26,790
Migration colon generate.

56
00:03:27,550 --> 00:03:28,090
Then.

57
00:03:28,090 --> 00:03:28,510
Dash.

58
00:03:28,510 --> 00:03:29,200
Dash.

59
00:03:29,800 --> 00:03:36,370
Then dash n We're going to give this migration a name of initial dash schema.

60
00:03:38,000 --> 00:03:42,350
And then finally a dash o at the very end to make sure we generate a JavaScript file.

61
00:03:43,300 --> 00:03:44,560
So I'm going to run that command.

62
00:03:45,690 --> 00:03:50,670
And sure enough, I'm told that I've now generated a schema migration file.

63
00:03:50,970 --> 00:03:54,180
So let's now open up that file and see what is contained inside of it.

64
00:03:55,680 --> 00:04:00,540
Okay, So inside the migrations directory, which was just created, I now have a file inside there.

65
00:04:00,690 --> 00:04:02,880
The long number you see right there is a timestamp.

66
00:04:02,880 --> 00:04:04,980
So it defines when this file was created.

67
00:04:05,100 --> 00:04:10,080
As soon as we open the file up, you are going to get some errors coming from once again Eslint.

68
00:04:10,110 --> 00:04:11,790
You can completely ignore these errors.

69
00:04:11,820 --> 00:04:13,740
Eslint is very much in the wrong here.

70
00:04:14,390 --> 00:04:16,280
So we just created a migration.

71
00:04:16,280 --> 00:04:20,480
And inside of here, even though I told you a little bit ago that we frequently have to write out migrations

72
00:04:20,480 --> 00:04:24,830
from scratch by using the migration colon generate command at the terminal.

73
00:04:26,530 --> 00:04:30,820
Type has automatically taken a look at all of our different entities inside of our project.

74
00:04:30,820 --> 00:04:36,100
And then it generated a migration file for us automatically to suit those different migrations.

75
00:04:36,130 --> 00:04:40,750
So you'll notice that I've got some different tables here automatically being created for me.

76
00:04:41,690 --> 00:04:43,130
And that's exactly what we want.

77
00:04:43,690 --> 00:04:44,980
So inside the migration file.

78
00:04:45,010 --> 00:04:49,330
Beyond that, we've also got the up function, which is going to advance the structure of our database

79
00:04:49,330 --> 00:04:54,760
and the down function, which is going to remember, revert the migration or undo it completely.

80
00:04:55,520 --> 00:05:03,230
So now to run our migration and give our SQLite file some structure, we can go back over to our terminal.

81
00:05:03,610 --> 00:05:07,940
Let me just show you the documentation on where it says how to do this very quickly, just so you understand.

82
00:05:09,090 --> 00:05:11,190
So we want to go to.

83
00:05:12,210 --> 00:05:13,230
Let me just go to the top.

84
00:05:13,440 --> 00:05:18,600
I'm going to go to running and reverting migrations, and all we should have to do is run NPM run type

85
00:05:19,080 --> 00:05:20,460
migration, colon run.

86
00:05:20,460 --> 00:05:22,230
So let's try that out right away.

87
00:05:23,980 --> 00:05:27,250
So NPM run type migration.

88
00:05:27,250 --> 00:05:28,450
Colon run.

89
00:05:30,680 --> 00:05:33,320
We're then going to get back a lot of output like this right here.

90
00:05:33,320 --> 00:05:38,640
And eventually at the very bottom, it should say something has been executed successfully.

91
00:05:38,660 --> 00:05:42,830
If you do not see a message like that, if you see something that says database not found, make sure

92
00:05:42,830 --> 00:05:47,630
you start up your application at least one time to get the SQLite file created.

93
00:05:48,310 --> 00:05:53,920
Now, once you have ran that migration, now our SQLite file should have all the necessary tables created

94
00:05:53,920 --> 00:05:57,350
inside of it for storing our users and storing our reports.

95
00:05:57,400 --> 00:06:02,530
So now we should be able to start up our application once again and everything should work as expected.

96
00:06:03,060 --> 00:06:07,050
So to test that out, I'll do an NPM run start colon dev.

97
00:06:10,010 --> 00:06:11,270
The server is going to start up.

98
00:06:11,660 --> 00:06:13,730
I'm then going to open up my client.

99
00:06:13,880 --> 00:06:18,380
Remember that we have now completely deleted our database, which means we are definitely not signed

100
00:06:18,380 --> 00:06:21,470
into our application anymore because we don't have any users whatsoever.

101
00:06:21,650 --> 00:06:27,920
So I'm going to go to my client for manipulating users and I'm going to attempt to sign up to the application.

102
00:06:28,610 --> 00:06:33,740
I should be able to make the sign up request and get back a response saying that I have signed up successfully.

103
00:06:34,070 --> 00:06:35,090
Perfect.

104
00:06:35,600 --> 00:06:36,680
So that's it.

105
00:06:36,680 --> 00:06:39,800
That is how we generate and then execute a migration.

106
00:06:39,830 --> 00:06:42,970
As you can tell, yes, this is very advanced stuff.

107
00:06:42,980 --> 00:06:45,170
A lot of topics going on at the same time.

108
00:06:45,170 --> 00:06:49,580
And I really encourage you to take a look at the documentation and understand the full scope of what's

109
00:06:49,580 --> 00:06:51,290
going on with these migrations.

110
00:06:51,750 --> 00:06:52,850
We're not done yet.

111
00:06:53,030 --> 00:06:57,080
We still have a little bit to do here, so we need to make sure that we can apply migrations like the

112
00:06:57,080 --> 00:07:01,910
one we just put together in our test environment and eventually go over to production and run a migration

113
00:07:01,910 --> 00:07:03,210
over there as well.

114
00:07:03,230 --> 00:07:05,050
So still a little bit to get through.

115
00:07:05,060 --> 00:07:07,100
Let's start to tackle that in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,020 --> 00:00:04,710
Although our application works in the development environment, we need to also make sure that works

2
00:00:04,710 --> 00:00:06,450
in the test environment as well.

3
00:00:06,480 --> 00:00:13,260
Right now, if I stop my server and execute NPM run test colon e2e, my test will execute.

4
00:00:13,260 --> 00:00:18,120
But I'm going to get a lot of different failures around some tables not existing.

5
00:00:18,420 --> 00:00:22,230
So get table or errors right here such as no such table user.

6
00:00:22,230 --> 00:00:25,890
And if I go up even further, you might have seen at the very start when I first ran that command,

7
00:00:25,920 --> 00:00:30,270
I also got some errors around JavaScript files not being allowed or something like that.

8
00:00:30,420 --> 00:00:37,380
We have to do a little bit of work, number one, to make sure that our config.js file can be safely

9
00:00:37,380 --> 00:00:39,430
read when our tests are actually running.

10
00:00:39,450 --> 00:00:43,650
We also need to add in a little bit more configuration to make sure that our migrations are executed

11
00:00:43,650 --> 00:00:46,110
when we start up our test suite as well.

12
00:00:46,140 --> 00:00:50,070
Because remember, every single test we run, we completely delete our database.

13
00:00:50,070 --> 00:00:53,580
So for every single test, we need to re migrate the database.

14
00:00:54,680 --> 00:00:54,950
All right.

15
00:00:54,950 --> 00:00:59,360
So step number one, we're going to make sure that we do not get that initial warning, which you might

16
00:00:59,360 --> 00:01:00,690
have seen Very briefly here.

17
00:01:00,710 --> 00:01:02,240
I'll run this very quick.

18
00:01:02,270 --> 00:01:05,120
We're going to see some text right here for just a second.

19
00:01:05,810 --> 00:01:07,400
Oh, I'm not getting it now.

20
00:01:07,400 --> 00:01:11,870
But there is some text that eventually pops up and it says something like, Hey, you need to allow

21
00:01:11,870 --> 00:01:13,640
JavaScript files or something like that.

22
00:01:13,640 --> 00:01:19,970
So to make sure that error goes away, I'm going to open up my tsconfig.json file and inside the compiler

23
00:01:20,000 --> 00:01:25,550
options object, I'm going to add in one property down here at the very bottom of Allow JS.

24
00:01:26,590 --> 00:01:27,270
True.

25
00:01:27,280 --> 00:01:29,620
And notice it is a lowercase s.

26
00:01:30,890 --> 00:01:33,530
So that's going to fix the little warning that we sometimes get.

27
00:01:35,030 --> 00:01:39,590
Then next up, to make sure that our migrations are ran before every single test that we execute.

28
00:01:39,710 --> 00:01:42,620
I'm going to go back to the Config.js file.

29
00:01:42,890 --> 00:01:47,480
I'm going to find the section where we are putting together configuration for the test environment,

30
00:01:47,840 --> 00:01:52,610
and we're going to add in a new property here of migrations Colon.

31
00:01:53,330 --> 00:01:56,020
Assuming migrations run colon True.

32
00:01:57,080 --> 00:02:01,790
So this property is going to make sure that all of our different migrations get ran when we are starting

33
00:02:01,790 --> 00:02:04,610
up our database for each individual test.

34
00:02:04,820 --> 00:02:08,660
So that's going to give our database some structure as soon as it gets created.

35
00:02:09,940 --> 00:02:10,090
Okay.

36
00:02:10,090 --> 00:02:11,560
So let's now save this change.

37
00:02:12,440 --> 00:02:14,450
I'm going to go and try to run my tests again.

38
00:02:15,200 --> 00:02:20,330
So now when I run my tests, I should see them passing now because we are creating the database and

39
00:02:20,330 --> 00:02:24,410
then applying our migration to it, which creates the users table and the reports table.

40
00:02:24,410 --> 00:02:27,080
And so now everything is passing as it was before.

41
00:02:27,560 --> 00:02:28,970
Definitely good improvement.

42
00:02:29,390 --> 00:02:29,900
Okay.

43
00:02:29,900 --> 00:02:35,480
So at this point in time, it appears we've just about got everything working in the development and

44
00:02:35,480 --> 00:02:36,810
the test environments.

45
00:02:36,830 --> 00:02:41,570
So now finally, at long last, we're going to start thinking about taking all of our application code

46
00:02:41,570 --> 00:02:45,130
and deploying it somewhere online and making it actually available somewhere.

47
00:02:45,140 --> 00:02:48,710
So let's start with the actual deployment process in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:03,630
Finally time to start deploying our application.

2
00:00:03,630 --> 00:00:05,280
So here's the general idea.

3
00:00:05,310 --> 00:00:08,040
We're going to deploy our app to a service called Heroku.

4
00:00:08,100 --> 00:00:11,650
Heroku is a free hosting provider and is extremely easy to use.

5
00:00:11,670 --> 00:00:17,230
So to deploy our application, we are going to wrap up our Nest application inside of a Git repository.

6
00:00:17,250 --> 00:00:21,560
It actually turns out that when we first generated our project, a git repo was created for us.

7
00:00:21,570 --> 00:00:23,640
So all we have to do is commit our code.

8
00:00:23,670 --> 00:00:29,370
We're then going to push this entire git repository with all of our applications code off to Heroku.

9
00:00:30,040 --> 00:00:35,680
Once our application is there, Heroku or Nest is going to automatically build itself from the TypeScript

10
00:00:35,680 --> 00:00:38,050
code that it is into plain JavaScript.

11
00:00:38,200 --> 00:00:41,980
Our server is then going to start itself up and listen for incoming traffic.

12
00:00:43,070 --> 00:00:45,110
To actually get access to a database.

13
00:00:45,110 --> 00:00:48,950
We're going to also ask Heroku to create a copy of Postgres for us.

14
00:00:48,980 --> 00:00:53,720
The Postgres copy or instance that is created is going to have some authentication credentials associated

15
00:00:53,720 --> 00:00:54,170
with it.

16
00:00:54,170 --> 00:00:59,870
So a username, a password and so on to get those authentication credentials over to our application.

17
00:01:00,080 --> 00:01:04,430
Heroku assigns a environment variable to our application as it is running.

18
00:01:04,430 --> 00:01:09,220
This is called the environment variable database underscore URL.

19
00:01:09,230 --> 00:01:13,070
So this will be set up for us automatically by Heroku.

20
00:01:13,860 --> 00:01:18,030
So we need to make sure that we take that environment variable and make use of it inside of our type

21
00:01:18,300 --> 00:01:22,110
config to tell type ORM how to connect to that Postgres database.

22
00:01:23,060 --> 00:01:23,360
All right.

23
00:01:23,360 --> 00:01:28,190
So there's a couple of things we need to do to get all this entire process wired up and put together.

24
00:01:28,280 --> 00:01:30,290
We have to commit all of our code with git.

25
00:01:30,320 --> 00:01:36,620
We have to make sure we read in this database URL parameter inside of our config dot JS file.

26
00:01:36,650 --> 00:01:39,020
We need to create a new project on Heroku.

27
00:01:39,050 --> 00:01:41,790
We need to create a database inside that project as well.

28
00:01:41,810 --> 00:01:43,490
So a couple different things to go.

29
00:01:44,710 --> 00:01:48,430
The first thing we're going to take care of is making sure that we connect to the database correctly

30
00:01:48,430 --> 00:01:49,820
in this production environment.

31
00:01:49,840 --> 00:01:53,160
So we're going to open up our Config.js file again.

32
00:01:53,170 --> 00:01:55,600
We're going to put in some production configuration.

33
00:01:55,600 --> 00:01:56,800
So let's get to it.

34
00:01:57,540 --> 00:01:57,990
Back inside.

35
00:01:57,990 --> 00:02:01,050
My editor will find the Config.js file.

36
00:02:01,770 --> 00:02:04,290
I'm going to go down to the production section.

37
00:02:05,500 --> 00:02:11,350
Under case production, we'll put in another object.assign db config.

38
00:02:12,580 --> 00:02:16,210
And we're going to put in some very different configuration this time around because we are no longer

39
00:02:16,210 --> 00:02:17,630
connecting to SQLite.

40
00:02:17,650 --> 00:02:20,710
So I'm going to instead put in type of postgres.

41
00:02:22,180 --> 00:02:29,290
We're going to put in a URL property that's going to refer to process Env and we're going to refer to

42
00:02:29,320 --> 00:02:34,270
that environment variable that is set for us by Heroku, which is database.

43
00:02:35,400 --> 00:02:36,300
Earl.

44
00:02:36,980 --> 00:02:43,400
This database is going to have the name of the database, the username to connect to it, and the password

45
00:02:43,400 --> 00:02:44,570
to connect to it as well.

46
00:02:44,600 --> 00:02:49,550
All encoded inside that URL that's going to very much simplify this configuration object.

47
00:02:50,060 --> 00:02:56,510
I'm going to also add in migrations run of true to make sure that we run our migrations whenever we

48
00:02:56,510 --> 00:02:59,120
are about to launch our application in production.

49
00:02:59,330 --> 00:03:02,660
We're going to tell type where all of our different entities are.

50
00:03:04,270 --> 00:03:07,480
So we'll put in star star star dot entity.

51
00:03:07,510 --> 00:03:13,270
Dot JS Because when we are in production, we are running plain JavaScript files as opposed to TypeScript

52
00:03:13,270 --> 00:03:14,710
as we were during test.

53
00:03:16,600 --> 00:03:22,030
And think the last property that we really need for right now is one property that's specifically tailored

54
00:03:22,030 --> 00:03:22,840
to Heroku.

55
00:03:22,870 --> 00:03:31,030
So we're going to put in SSL, assign it an object and say reject unauthorized false.

56
00:03:31,450 --> 00:03:35,140
Do double check your spelling on the word unauthorized right there as well.

57
00:03:36,900 --> 00:03:41,210
Next up, we need to make sure that we install the Postgres driver that is going to be used by Type

58
00:03:41,250 --> 00:03:42,720
ORM into our project.

59
00:03:42,750 --> 00:03:47,160
So back over at our terminal, I'm going to run NPM install.

60
00:03:48,350 --> 00:03:48,560
G.

61
00:03:50,130 --> 00:03:51,420
Standing for Postgres.

62
00:03:51,780 --> 00:03:53,250
And that's pretty much it.

63
00:03:53,550 --> 00:03:57,270
Okay, so the database side of our application is now production ready.

64
00:03:57,270 --> 00:04:01,260
So we are going to go through a couple of other very small changes in the next video.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:05,910
In the lecture right before this one, you should found some directions on how to install the Heroku

2
00:00:05,910 --> 00:00:06,330
CLI.

3
00:00:06,540 --> 00:00:11,010
So make sure you get that signed up and make sure you log into Roku as well.

4
00:00:11,040 --> 00:00:15,390
You might have to create a Heroku account if you have not created one before to make sure everything

5
00:00:15,390 --> 00:00:16,450
is set up correctly.

6
00:00:16,470 --> 00:00:19,170
Run Heroku off Colon.

7
00:00:19,170 --> 00:00:23,940
Who am I at your terminal and you should see your login email appear right there.

8
00:00:24,490 --> 00:00:26,470
Once you got the CLI installed.

9
00:00:26,500 --> 00:00:31,330
We're then going to do a couple of last minute configuration changes inside of our project just to make

10
00:00:31,330 --> 00:00:36,670
sure that our app builds itself correctly and runs correctly once we deploy it off to Heroku.

11
00:00:37,150 --> 00:00:37,660
Okay.

12
00:00:37,660 --> 00:00:39,530
So a couple of very quick changes.

13
00:00:39,550 --> 00:00:45,010
The first change we're going to make is inside of our Main.ts file inside of here, our application

14
00:00:45,010 --> 00:00:48,850
is always trying to listen by default to Port 3000 on Heroku.

15
00:00:48,880 --> 00:00:52,960
We're going to be given a port to run our application on or a port to listen to.

16
00:00:52,990 --> 00:00:57,970
So to make sure that we listen to the provided port, we're going to change that 3000 right there to

17
00:00:57,970 --> 00:01:02,260
process dot env dot port in all capitals.

18
00:01:02,840 --> 00:01:07,640
Then if that environment variable is not provided, we will default to port 3000 instead.

19
00:01:09,320 --> 00:01:11,450
Once they make that change, I'll then save the file.

20
00:01:12,150 --> 00:01:13,820
Okay, so that's the first change.

21
00:01:13,970 --> 00:01:18,290
The next change we're going to make inside of our root project directory, we are going to make a brand

22
00:01:18,290 --> 00:01:21,500
new file called proc file.

23
00:01:21,770 --> 00:01:26,610
So this file has a capital P on it and no extension whatsoever inside of here.

24
00:01:26,630 --> 00:01:30,700
We're going to list out the command to tell Heroku how to actually run our application.

25
00:01:30,710 --> 00:01:38,540
So I'm going to put in Web colon and then to actually run our application inside of a production environment,

26
00:01:39,050 --> 00:01:44,510
we can actually take a look at the Package.json file and there's a command called Start Colon Prod to

27
00:01:44,510 --> 00:01:46,880
run our application before running that command.

28
00:01:46,880 --> 00:01:49,040
However, we first have to build our app.

29
00:01:49,800 --> 00:01:52,080
Roku is going to run that build command for us automatically.

30
00:01:52,080 --> 00:01:55,980
So all we have to do is say start colon prod, and that's pretty much it.

31
00:01:56,850 --> 00:02:02,490
So back inside that proc file, I will put in npm run start colon prod.

32
00:02:04,840 --> 00:02:06,720
Only inside of our root project directory.

33
00:02:06,730 --> 00:02:10,750
We're going to find the config dot build dot Json file.

34
00:02:11,020 --> 00:02:15,250
So make sure you get the build version inside this list of exclude right here.

35
00:02:15,250 --> 00:02:20,710
We're going to make sure that TypeScript does not attempt to compile the config dot js file or any of

36
00:02:20,710 --> 00:02:22,780
the files inside that migrations directory.

37
00:02:22,780 --> 00:02:30,670
So on the exclude array I'm going to add in or config.js and migrations like so.

38
00:02:32,660 --> 00:02:33,890
Okay, so that looks good.

39
00:02:33,940 --> 00:02:37,100
Well, that's just about all the changes we need to make to our project itself.

40
00:02:37,100 --> 00:02:41,900
So quick pause right here and we'll do a little bit of setup around Heroku itself in just a moment.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,390 --> 00:00:01,840
All right.

2
00:00:01,870 --> 00:00:06,370
Time to actually deploy our application, so to do so, we're going to run a couple of different commands

3
00:00:06,370 --> 00:00:11,500
at our terminal and interact with the Roku Seelye step number one inside of our project directory.

4
00:00:11,650 --> 00:00:16,090
We're going to commit all of our work that we have done to our project, to our git repository that

5
00:00:16,090 --> 00:00:18,580
was created inside this project when we first generated it.

6
00:00:19,030 --> 00:00:21,400
So I'm going to run Git add dots.

7
00:00:22,300 --> 00:00:24,850
I'll then commit that with the message of.

8
00:00:26,270 --> 00:00:27,830
Free production.

9
00:00:28,280 --> 00:00:28,730
Commit.

10
00:00:31,560 --> 00:00:37,380
Once I've committed all these changes and I'm going to create a new Heroku project by running Heroku

11
00:00:37,590 --> 00:00:38,100
Create.

12
00:00:41,270 --> 00:00:45,650
After a very short pause will then be given the URL of our project, so that's where we are going to

13
00:00:45,650 --> 00:00:50,300
eventually access our project at and before we push any of our code off.

14
00:00:50,540 --> 00:00:54,950
We're going to make sure that we create a post-credit database inside this project as well.

15
00:00:55,280 --> 00:01:00,590
So to create the database, we will run Heroku add ons colon creates.

16
00:01:01,600 --> 00:01:05,080
Heroku, Dash, PostgreSQL.

17
00:01:06,140 --> 00:01:09,320
Then a colon, Hobie Dash Dev.

18
00:01:10,190 --> 00:01:15,380
Now this database is entirely free to run, so we are specifically using the hobby tier, which costs

19
00:01:15,380 --> 00:01:16,820
no money whatsoever.

20
00:01:18,690 --> 00:01:19,530
So run that commands.

21
00:01:21,240 --> 00:01:26,000
And then after a little bit, we will be told, OK, we are now we've now carried the database.

22
00:01:26,070 --> 00:01:27,570
And right now it is completely empty.

23
00:01:28,790 --> 00:01:33,760
Then a environment variable is going to set be set up inside of our project of database underscore URL.

24
00:01:33,800 --> 00:01:38,030
So remember, we already wired that up back inside of our own config file.

25
00:01:41,860 --> 00:01:46,000
Finally, very last step before we do the deployment, we're going to set up some different environment

26
00:01:46,000 --> 00:01:46,480
variables.

27
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:50,830
The first one we're going to set up is going to be our cookie key to be used by that cookie session

28
00:01:50,830 --> 00:01:51,370
middleware.

29
00:01:51,850 --> 00:01:59,530
So I'm going to set up that environment variable with Heroku config colon set cookie underscore cookie

30
00:02:00,130 --> 00:02:03,730
equals and then we can put in any random string of characters who want to write here.

31
00:02:05,270 --> 00:02:06,530
So I'll set that investment variable.

32
00:02:07,580 --> 00:02:09,800
And they're going to set up one other environment variable.

33
00:02:11,430 --> 00:02:16,360
Which will be A. underscore E and V equals production, just be a hundred percent clear.

34
00:02:16,380 --> 00:02:18,450
We are running our application in production mode.

35
00:02:21,040 --> 00:02:21,880
And that should be it.

36
00:02:22,030 --> 00:02:25,630
So now it's time to actually deploy our application to deploy our app.

37
00:02:25,870 --> 00:02:29,920
All we have to do is run, get, push, Heroku Master.

38
00:02:30,370 --> 00:02:35,050
That's going to take all the changes inside of our local repository and push it off to the git repository

39
00:02:35,050 --> 00:02:36,430
that now exists on Heroku.

40
00:02:37,120 --> 00:02:37,990
So run that command.

41
00:02:38,530 --> 00:02:41,560
And we'll see a lot of information about stuff being pushed off.

42
00:02:41,950 --> 00:02:45,850
And we'll see some scripts or some feedback right here from our project being built.
